blocks:
Ayahs

الٓمٓ (١)
Alif, Lam, Mim. (1)
ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ لَا رَيْبَ ۛ فِيهِ ۛ هُدًى لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (٢)
This is the Book, there is no doubt in it, a guidance to the Godwary, (2)

Commentary

Research regarding the Disjointed Letters (al-Huruf al-Muqatta'ah) of the Quran

At the beginning of twenty-nine surahs of the Quran, we encounter the "Disjointed Letters" (al-Huruf al-Muqatta'ah), and as the name suggests, these letters appear to be severed from one another and do not seemingly form a meaningful word.

The Disjointed Letters of the Quran have always been considered among the mysterious elements of the Quran, and commentators have offered numerous interpretations for them. With the passage of time and new research by scholars, fresh interpretations are being discovered.

Interestingly, we do not find any instance in history where the Arabs of the Jahiliyyah (Age of Ignorance) or the polytheists criticized the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله for the existence of these Disjointed Letters at the beginning of many surahs, or used them as a means for mockery and ridicule. This itself suggests that they, too, were perhaps not entirely unaware of the secrets behind the existence of these Disjointed Letters.

In any case, among these interpretations, a few seem more important and credible, aligning with the latest research conducted in this field. We will gradually explain these interpretations at the beginning of this surah, Surah Al-Imran, and Surah Al-A'raf, God willing. For now, we will address the most important of them here:

These letters indicate that this Heavenly Book—with all its grandeur and significance that has bewildered all Arab and non-Arab orators and rendered scholars incapable of challenging it—is composed of the very same letters that are available to everyone. Despite the fact that the Quran is composed of these same "Alif, Ba" and ordinary words, its words are so balanced and carry such profound meanings that they penetrate the depths of the human heart and soul, filling the spirit with wonder and admiration, and compelling thoughts and intellects to bow in reverence before it. Its ordered sentence structures and words are at the highest level, casting lofty meanings into the mold of the most beautiful expressions, which have no equal or parallel.

The eloquence and rhetoric of the Quran are hidden from no one. This statement is not a mere claim, for the Creator of the universe—the One who revealed this Book to the Prophet—has invited all human beings to a challenge of emulation (tahaddi) and has asked them to bring something like it, or at least one surah like it. He has invited all the inhabitants of the world (Jinn and mankind) to cooperate and think together to bring its like if they can, yet all have remained helpless and incapable. This shows that it is not the product of human thought.

Just as the Great Allah creates beings such as man—with that amazing structure—and various beautiful birds, diverse animals, and colorful plants and flowers from soil, while we make bowls and pots and the like from it; similarly, Allah has cast lofty subjects and meanings from the alphabet and ordinary words into the mold of beautiful expressions and balanced words, employing a unique style that has left everyone biting their fingers in amazement. Yes, these same letters are at the disposal of humans as well, yet they lack the power to innovate compositions and sentence structures like those of the Quran.

The Golden Age of Arabic Literature

It is noteworthy that the era of Jahiliyyah was a golden age in terms of literature. Those same desert-dwelling Arabs, those barefoot and semi-savage people, despite all their economic and social deprivations, possessed hearts full of literary taste and eloquence. So much so that today, the poems remaining from that golden era are considered among the most authentic and rich of Arabic poetry, serving as precious treasures for enthusiasts of authentic Arabic literature. This itself is the best evidence for the literary genius and eloquence of the Arabs during that period.

During the Jahiliyyah, the Arabs had a large annual market called the "Market of 'Ukaz," which was simultaneously considered an "important literary assembly" and a political and judicial congress.

In this market, in addition to economic activities, the finest examples of Arabic poetry and prose were presented by capable poets and orators at this great congress. The best among them was selected as the "Poem of the Year," seven (or ten) pieces of which are famous as the "Mu'allaqat al-Sab'ah" (The Seven Hanging Odes) or "al-'Asharah" (The Ten). Success in this great literary competition was, of course, a great honor for the poet and his tribe.

In such an era, the Quran challenged them to produce its like, and all expressed their inability to bring anything like it and knelt before it (you will read more details regarding the challenge of the Quran and the inability to bring its like under verse 23 of this same surah).

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 1, p. 64.

*

A Clear Witness

A living witness for this interpretation of the Disjointed Letters is a hadith narrated from Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, Imam Sajjad عليه السلام, where he says:

كذب قريش و اليهود بالقرآن و قالوا هذا سحر مبين، تقوله، فقال اللَّه:"الم ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ ...":

اى يا محمد هذا الكتاب الذى انزلته اليك هو الحروف المقطعة التي منها الف و لام و م و هو بلغتكم و حروف هجائكم فاتوا بمثله ان كنتم صادقين ...:

"The Quraysh and the Jews belied the Quran and said: 'The Quran is clear magic; he has fabricated it himself and attributed it to Allah.' So Allah announced to them: 'Alif, Lam, Mim. This is the Book...' meaning: O Muhammad, this Book that We have sent down to you is [composed of] these same Disjointed Letters (Alif-Lam-Mim) and their like, which are the same letters of your alphabet." [^1]

Another witness: A hadith narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Ridha عليه السلام where he says:

" ... ثم قال ان اللَّه تبارك و تعالى انزل هذا القرآن بهذه الحروف التي يتداولها جميع العرب ثم قال:قُلْ لَئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنْسُ وَ الْجِنُّ عَلى‌ أَنْ يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هذَا الْقُرْآنِ ...

"... Then he said: Indeed, Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, revealed this Quran with these very letters that all Arabs use in their speech. Then He said: Say, if mankind and the Jinn were to gather to bring the like of this Quran, they could not do so..." [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Volume 1, page 54. [^2]: Tawhid al-Saduq, page 162, 1375 AH edition.

Another point that supports this theory regarding the meaning of the Disjointed Letters of the Quran is that in 24 instances at the beginning of the surahs that start with these letters, the greatness of the Quran is immediately mentioned. This itself shows that there is a connection between these two (the Disjointed Letters and the greatness of the Quran).

Here we present a few examples:

1- الر كِتابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آياتُهُ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِنْ لَدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ (Hud: 1-2) 2- طس تِلْكَ آياتُ الْقُرْآنِ وَ كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ (An-Naml: 1-2) 3- الم تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْحَكِيمِ (Luqman: 1-2) 4- المص كِتابٌ أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ (Al-A'raf: 1-2)

In all these cases and many other instances at the beginning of the surahs of the Quran, after mentioning the Disjointed Letters, the Quran is mentioned, and its greatness is discussed.

After stating the Disjointed Letters of the Quran, it points to the greatness of this Heavenly Book, saying: "This is that great Book about which there is no doubt" (ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ). This expression may refer to the fact that Allah had promised His Prophet to reveal a Book to him for the guidance of mankind, which would be a source of guidance for all truth-seekers and leave no room for doubt for those searching for the truth, and now He has fulfilled His promise.

As for the statement that there is no doubt or hesitation in it, this is not a mere claim; rather, it means that the content of the Quran is such that it itself bears witness to its own truthfulness. It is "like a perfumer's tray, silent yet displaying its excellence." In other words: the signs of truth, greatness, consistency, firmness, depth of meaning, sweetness, and eloquence of the words and expressions are so apparent in it that they repel any kind of whisper or doubt, being an instance of "that which is manifest requires no explanation."

Interestingly, the passage of time not only does not diminish its freshness, but with the advancement of science and the lifting of the veil from the secrets of the universe, the truths of the Quran become clearer. The more science advances toward perfection, the more the brilliance of these verses increases.

This is not a claim; it is a reality that, by the will of Allah, we will realize throughout this book of commentary.

Key Points

1- Why the use of a distant demonstrative pronoun?

We know that the word "Dhalika" (ذلِكَ) in the Arabic language is a demonstrative pronoun for the distant (that). Therefore, "Dhalikal Kitab" (ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ) literally means "That Book," whereas here a near demonstrative pronoun should have been used, saying "Hadhal Kitab" (this Book), since the Quran was accessible to the people.

This is because sometimes a distant demonstrative pronoun is used to express the greatness of a thing or a person; meaning that its status is so high that it is as if it is located at a distant point at the peak of the heavens. We have similar expressions in Persian as well; for example, in the presence of a great individual, we say: "If that master permits, we shall do such a task," whereas "this master" should be said. This is solely to express greatness and the loftiness of status.

In some other verses of the Quran, the expression "Tilka" (تِلْكَ) is used, which is also a distant demonstrative, such as: "تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْحَكِيمِ" (These are the verses of the Wise Book) (Luqman, verse 2).

2- The Meaning of "Book" (Kitab)

"Kitab" means something written. Undoubtedly, the intended meaning of "Book" in the verse under discussion is the Holy Quran.

Here the question arises: was the entire Quran written on that day?

In response to this question, we say that it is not necessary for the entire Quran to have been written, because "Quran" is applied both to the whole of this Book and to its parts.

Furthermore, "Kitab" is sometimes used in a broader sense to mean subjects that are worthy of being written and will come out in written form, even if they had not been written until that time. In Surah Sad, verse 29, we read: كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبارَكٌ لِيَدَّبَّرُوا آياتِهِ: "This is a Book which We have sent down to you, full of blessings, that they may ponder over its verses." It is certain that the Quran did not exist as a writing among humans before its revelation.

There is also the possibility that the expression "Book" refers to its being written in the "Preserved Tablet" (al-Lauh al-Mahfuz). (We will discuss the Preserved Tablet in its proper place). [^1]

3- What is Guidance?

The word "Guidance" (Hidayah) has been used in many instances in the Quran, but the root and basis of all of them return to two meanings:

1- "Generative Guidance" (al-Hidayat al-Takwiniyyah), which exists in all beings of the world. (By generative guidance is meant the leading of beings by the Lord under the cover of the system of creation and the calculated laws of the universe).

In this regard, the Holy Quran says from the tongue of Musa عليه السلام: رَبُّنَا الَّذِي أَعْطى‌ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى‌: "Our Lord is He who gave to everything its creation and then guided it" (Ta-Ha: 50).

2- "Legislative Guidance" (al-Hidayat al-Tashri'iyyah), which is carried out by the prophets and the Heavenly Books.

[^1]: Refer to Volume 10 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 241 (under verse 39 of Surah Ar-Ra'd).

Through their teaching and training, humans advance on the path of perfection. Evidence for this is also abundant in the Quran, including where we read: وَ جَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا: "And We made them leaders guiding by Our command" (Al-Anbiya: 73).

4- Why is the Quran's guidance specific to the Pious?

Certainly, the Quran was revealed for the guidance of all the worlds, but why in the above verse is the Quran's guidance introduced as being specific to the pious (muttaqin)?

The reason is that unless there is a level of piety (taqwa) within a human being—the stage of submission to the truth and acceptance of what is consistent with intellect and fitrah (innate nature)—it is impossible for a person to benefit from the guidance of the Heavenly Books and the call of the prophets.

In other words: individuals lacking faith are of two groups: one group consists of those who are in search of the truth, and this amount of piety exists in their hearts such that wherever they see the truth, they accept it.

The other group consists of stubborn, prejudiced, and ego-worshipping individuals who are not only not in search of the truth but, wherever they find it, they strive to extinguish it.

Certainly, the Quran and any other Heavenly Book have been and are beneficial only to the first group, and the second group will not benefit from its guidance.

And to use another expression: in addition to the "efficacy of the actor" (fa'iliyyat al-fa'il), the "receptivity of the receiver" (qabiliyyat al-qabil) is also a condition, both in generative guidance and in legislative guidance.

Salty soil will never produce hyacinths, even if it rains upon it a thousand times; rather, the ground must be ready to benefit from the life-giving drops of rain.

The territory of the human being likewise will not accept the seed of guidance until it is cleansed of stubbornness, enmity, and prejudice. Therefore, Allah says: "The Quran is a guide and leader for the pious."

Ayahs

ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَيُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (٣)
who believe in the Unseen, maintain the prayer, and spend out of what We have provided for them; (3)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلِكَ وَبِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ (٤)
and who believe in what has been sent down to you and what was sent down before you, and are certain of the Hereafter. (4)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَىٰ هُدًى مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٥)
Those follow their Lord’s guidance and it is they who are the felicitous. (5)

Commentary

The Effects of Piety (Taqwa) on the Human Soul and Body

At the beginning of this Surah, the Quran divides people into three distinct groups regarding the program and creed of Islam:

  1. "The Pious" (Muttaqin): Those who have accepted Islam in all its dimensions.

  2. "The Disbelievers" (Kafirin): Those who stand in direct opposition to the first group, confessing their disbelief and not hesitating to engage in hostile speech and behavior against Islam.

  3. "The Hypocrites" (Munafiqin): Those who are double-faced; they appear as Muslims when with Muslims, but with the opposing group, they are against Islam. Of course, their true face is that of disbelief, though they maintain Islamic pretenses.

Undoubtedly, the harm of this group to Islam is greater than that of the second group, and for this reason, we will see that the Quran confronts them with greater severity.

Of course, this subject is not exclusive to Islam; all world ideologies face these three groups: either believers in that ideology, open opponents, or conservative hypocrites. Furthermore, this issue is not restricted to a specific time but has existed throughout all eras of the world.

In the verses under discussion, the talk is of the first group. Their characteristics in terms of faith and action are presented under five headings: (Faith in the Unseen, establishing prayer, spending from all blessings, faith in the call of all prophets, and faith in the Resurrection).

1. Faith in the Unseen

First, It says: "They are those who believe in the Unseen" (الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْغَيْبِ).

"The Unseen" (Ghayb) and "the Manifest" (Shuhud) are two opposites. The world of the manifest is the world of the senses, and the world of the unseen is that which is beyond the senses. This is because "Ghayb" originally means something that is covered and hidden, and since the world beyond the senses is hidden from our perception, it is called the Unseen. We read in the Holy Quran: عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَ الشَّهَادَةِ هُوَ الرَّحْمَنُ الرَّحِيمُ: "The Knower of the Unseen and the witnessed, the hidden and the manifest; He is the Beneficent, the Merciful" (Al-Hashr: 22).

Faith in the Unseen is precisely the first point that separates believers from others and places the followers of heavenly religions against the deniers of God, revelation, and the Day of Judgment. For this reason, the first characteristic of the pious is mentioned as faith in the Unseen.

Believers have breached the boundaries of the material world and moved beyond its four walls. With this broad vision, they are connected to an extraordinarily larger universe, while their opponents insist on limiting man, like animals, within the four walls of the material world, calling this regressive path "civilization," "progress," and "advancement"!

In comparing the "perception and vision" of these two, we find that "believers in the Unseen" believe the universe is much larger and vaster than what we perceive with our senses. The Creator of this world possesses infinite knowledge and power, and boundless majesty and perception; He is Eternal and Everlasting. He has founded the world according to a highly calculated and precise plan. In the world of humans, the human soul has created a vast distance between them and animals. Death does not mean annihilation and non-existence, but rather one of the developmental stages of man and a gateway to a vaster and more expansive world.

In contrast, a materialist believes that existence is limited to what we see and what natural sciences have proven for us: the laws of nature are a series of deterministic laws that brought this world into being without any plan or program. The creative force of the world does not possess even as much intellect and consciousness as a small child. Man is a part of nature, and after death, everything ends; his body decomposes, and its components rejoin natural materials once more. There is no survival for man, and there is not much distance between him and an animal. [1]

Are these two humans, with these two ways of thinking, comparable? Is their life path and behavior in society the same?

The first cannot ignore truth, justice, benevolence, and helping others, while the second sees no reason for any of these matters unless they affect his material life for today or tomorrow. For this reason, in the world of true believers, there is brotherhood and understanding, purity and cooperation; whereas in a world ruled by materialism, there is colonialism and exploitation, bloodshed, and plunder. If we see that the Quran considers the starting point of piety in the above verses to be faith in the Unseen, this is the reason.

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether faith in the Unseen here refers only to faith in the Pure Essence of the Lord, or if "Unseen" has a broad meaning that includes the realm of revelation, the Resurrection, the world of angels, and generally everything beyond the senses.

From what we said above—that faith in the world beyond the senses is the first point of separation between believers and disbelievers—it becomes clear that "Unseen" here carries that same broad meaning of the word. Furthermore, the expression in the verse is absolute, and there is no restriction to limit it to a specific meaning.

And if we see in some narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام [1] that "Unseen" in the above verse is interpreted as "the Hidden Imam, Hazrat Mahdi سلام الله عليه"—who, according to our belief, is currently alive and hidden from sight—it does not contradict what was said above. This is because narrations regarding the interpretation of verses (many examples of which you will see later) often state specific instances without being limited to those instances. These narrations actually want to illustrate the breadth of the meaning of faith in the Unseen and its inclusion even of the Hidden Imam عليه السلام. It can even be said that faith in the Unseen has a vast meaning that may find new instances as time passes.

2. Connection with God

Another characteristic of the pious is that: "They establish prayer" (وَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ).

"Prayer," which is the secret of connection with God, keeps the believers who have found their way to the world beyond nature in a permanent and constant relationship with that Great Origin of creation. They bow their heads in humility only before God and submit only to the Great Creator of the universe. For this reason, bowing before idols or submitting to tyrants and oppressors has no place in their program.

Such a person feels that he has transcended all other creatures and has gained the worthiness to speak with God, and this is his greatest educational factor.

One who stands before God at least five times a day and night, engaging in intimate supplication with Him—his thoughts, his actions, and his speech all become Godly. How is it possible for such a person to step against His will? (Provided that his supplication at the threshold of Truth stems from his soul and heart, and he turns to His threshold with all his heart). [1]

3. Connection with Humans

In addition to a permanent connection with the Lord, they have a close and continuous relationship with God's creation. For this reason, the Quran describes their third characteristic as follows: "And they spend out of what We have provided them" (وَ مِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ يُنْفِقُونَ).

It is noteworthy that the Quran does not say: "they spend from their wealth" (min amwalihim yunfiqun), but rather says "from what We have provided them" (mimma razaqnahum). In this way, it generalizes the issue of "spending" (infaq) such that it encompasses all material and spiritual blessings.

Therefore, the pious people are those who give not only from their wealth but also from their knowledge, intellect, physical strength, social status, and in short, from all their assets to those in need, without expecting any reward.

Another point is that spending is a general law in the world of creation, especially in the bodily organization of every living being. The human heart does not work only for itself; rather, it spends from what it has to all cells. The brain, lungs, and other systems of the human body all constantly spend from the results of their work. Fundamentally, collective life has no meaning without spending. [1]

Connection with humans is, in fact, the result of the connection and bond with God. A person who is connected to God and, by the decree of the phrase "from what We have provided them," considers all provisions and blessings to be from God and not from himself, views them as the gift of a Great God who has placed this trust with him for a few days. He is not only not upset by spending and giving in His way but is happy, for he has given God's property to His servants, yet has purchased the material and spiritual results and blessings for himself. This way of thinking purifies the human soul from stinginess and envy and transforms the world of "struggle for survival" into a "world of cooperation"—a world where everyone feels indebted to place the blessings they have at the disposal of all the needy, shining like the sun without expecting a reward.

Interestingly, in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read regarding the interpretation of the phrase "and they spend out of what We have provided them": "Its meaning is that they disseminate the sciences and knowledge We have taught them and teach them to those in need." [1] Obviously, the meaning of this statement is not that spending is exclusive to knowledge; rather, since views on the issue of spending often turn toward financial spending, the Imam, by mentioning this type of spiritual spending, intends to clarify the breadth of the concept of infaq.

Furthermore, it becomes clearly evident from here that "spending" in the verse under discussion is not specifically the obligatory Zakat, nor is it limited to obligatory and recommended Zakat; rather, it has a broad meaning that encompasses any kind of unconditional assistance.

  1. Another characteristic of the pious is faith in all prophets and divine programs. The Quran says: "And those who believe in what has been sent down to you and what was sent down before you" (وَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَ ما أُنْزِلَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ).

In this way, they not only see no difference in terms of principles and foundations in the call of the prophets, but they consider them harmonious teachers and educators who, one after another, step into this great school of the world of humanity to advance humans in their evolutionary journey. They do not consider heavenly religions a source of division and hypocrisy; rather, noting their fundamental unity, they see them as a means for connection and bonding among humans.

Those who possess this understanding and vision purify their souls from prejudice and gain faith in what all divine prophets brought for the guidance and perfection of humans. They respect all guides and leaders of the path of Monotheism (Tawhid).

Of course, faith in the instructions of previous prophets will not prevent them from aligning their thoughts and actions with the creed of the Last Prophet, who is the final link in the evolutionary chain of religions. For if they do otherwise, they have taken a step backward in their path of perfection.

  1. Faith in the Resurrection is the last attribute mentioned in this series of attributes for the pious: "And they are certain of the Hereafter" (وَ بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ).

They are certain that man was not created neglected, in vain, or without a purpose. Creation has determined a path for him that never ends with death; for if everything ended right here, surely all this commotion for these few days of life would be futile and useless.

He confesses that the absolute justice of the Lord awaits everyone, and it is not the case that our actions in this world will be without account or reward.

This belief grants him peace. He not only does not suffer from the pressures exerted on him in the path of fulfilling responsibilities but welcomes them. He stands like a mountain against events, does not submit to injustices, and is certain that the smallest good or bad deed has a reward or punishment. After death, he transitions to a vaster world free from any oppression and benefits from the vast mercy and favors of the Great Lord.

Faith in the Hereafter means breaching the wall of the material world and entering a higher and loftier environment for which this world is considered a farm, and a school for the greatest possible preparation. The life of this world is not the ultimate goal but has a preparatory aspect and is a period of construction for the other world.

Life in this world is like life in the embryonic stage, which was never the goal of human creation, but rather an evolutionary period for another life. However, unless one is born healthy and free from any defect in this way, he will not be happy and prosperous in the life after it.

Faith in the Resurrection has a profound effect on the education of humans. It grants them bravery and courage, because based on it, the peak of honor in the life of this world is "martyrdom" in the way of a sacred divine goal, which is the most beloved of things for a person of faith and a beginning for an eternal and everlasting life.

Faith in the Day of Judgment controls man against sin; in other words, our sins have an inverse relationship with faith in God and the Hereafter. To whatever extent faith is stronger, sin is less. In Surah Sad, verse 26, we read that God says to David:

وَ لا تَتَّبِعِ الْهَوى‌ فَيُضِلَّكَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ: "And do not follow desire, for it will lead you astray from the path of Allah. Indeed, those who go astray from the path of Allah will have a severe punishment because they forgot the Day of Account." Yes, this forgetting of the Day of Recompense is the source of all kinds of rebellion, oppression, and sins, and those in turn are the source of severe punishment.

The last verse under discussion refers to the result and end of the affair of the believers who have gathered these five attributes within themselves. It says: "Those are upon guidance from their Lord" (أُولئِكَ عَلى‌ هُدىً مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ).

"And those are the successful" (وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ).

In reality, their guidance as well as their success is guaranteed by God, and the expression "from their Lord" (مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ) refers to this very fact.

Interestingly, it says: "upon guidance from their Lord" (عَلى‌ هُدىً مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ), indicating that divine guidance is like a swift mount upon which they are riding, and with the help of this mount, they advance toward success and happiness (for we know the word "ala" [upon] is usually used where the concept of mastery, height, and dominance is conveyed).

Furthermore, the expression "huda" (guidance) in the indefinite form refers to the greatness of the guidance that reaches them from God; that is, they possess a very great guidance.

Also, the expression "they are the successful" (هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ), considering what is stated in the science of rhetoric, indicates exclusivity; that is, the only path to success is the path of this group who, by acquiring five specific attributes, have become subjects of divine guidance. [1]

Key Points

1. Continuity in the Path of Faith and Action

In the above verses, the present/future tense (mudari'), which usually denotes continuity, is used throughout (believe in the Unseen—establish prayer—spend—they are certain of the Hereafter). This shows that the pious and true believers are those who have stability and continuity in their programs. The ups and downs of life do not affect their soul and thought and do not create a flaw in their constructive programs.

At the beginning, they possess a spirit of seeking the truth, and that same thing causes them to follow the call of the Quran, and then the call of the Quran creates these five characteristics in them.

2. What is the Reality of Piety (Taqwa)?

"Taqwa" originally comes from the root "wiqayah," meaning "protection" or "self-restraint." [1] In other words, it is an internal control force that preserves man against the rebellion of desires. In fact, it plays the role of a powerful brake that protects the machine of human existence at precipices and restrains it from dangerous speeding.

For this reason, the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, has counted "Taqwa" as a powerful fortress against the dangers of sin, where he says:

اعلموا عباد الله ان التقوى دار حصن عزيز: "Know, O servants of Allah, that piety is a strong and impenetrable fortress." [2]

In Islamic Hadiths and also the words of scholars, many metaphors have been stated to illustrate the state of Taqwa. The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says:

الا و ان التقوى مطايا ذلل، حمل عليها اهلها و اعطوا ازمتها، فاوردتهم الجنة: "Behold, indeed piety is like submissive mounts; their owners are seated upon them and given their reins, and they lead them into Paradise!" [1]

Some have likened Taqwa to the state of someone passing through a land full of thorns; he tries to pull up his skirts completely and walk with caution lest the tip of a thorn pierce his foot or catch his garment.

"Abdullah Mu'tazz" has put this saying into poetry and says: "Leave sins, the small of them and the large; that is Taqwa." "And act like one walking upon a land of thorns, wary of what he sees." "Do not belittle a small sin; indeed, mountains are made of pebbles." [2]

Furthermore, it is well-derived from this metaphor that Taqwa does not consist of a person choosing isolation and seclusion; rather, one must be in the heart of society and, if society is polluted, preserve oneself.

In any case, this state of Taqwa and powerful spiritual control is the clearest effect of faith in the "Origin" and the "Return"—that is, God and the Resurrection. It is considered the criterion of human virtue and honor and the scale for measuring his personality in Islam, to the extent that the phrase "Indeed, the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most pious of you" has become an eternal slogan of Islam (Al-Hujurat, verse 14).

Ali عليه السلام says:

ان تقوى الله مفتاح سداد و ذخيرة معاد و عتق من كل ملكة و نجاة من كل هلكة: "Indeed, the fear of Allah is the key to righteousness, the provision for the Hereafter, freedom from every slavery [to desire], and salvation from every destruction." (Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 230).

Additionally, it should be noted that Taqwa has branches and divisions: financial and economic Taqwa, sexual and social Taqwa, political Taqwa, and the like.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٦)
As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith. (6)
خَتَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلَىٰ سَمْعِهِمْ ۖ وَعَلَىٰٓ أَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ غِشَـٰوَةٌ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (٧)
Allah has set a seal on their hearts and their hearing, and there is a blindfold on their sight, and there is a great punishment for them. (7)

Commentary

The Second Group, the Stubborn and Persistent Disbelievers

This group stands in direct contrast to the Muttaqin (the God-fearing), and their characteristics are concisely described in the two aforementioned verses.

In the first verse, it says: "Indeed, those who disbelieve (and are stubborn and persistent in their disbelief), it is all the same for them whether you warn them or do not warn them—they will not believe" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَ أَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

The first group [the God-fearing] was prepared in every sense, with all their senses and perceptions ready to accept the truth once they received it and to follow it.

However, this second group is so stubborn in its deviation that even if the truth becomes clear to them, they are unwilling to accept it. The Quran, which was a guide and leader for the Muttaqin, is entirely ineffective for these people. Whether you speak or remain silent, whether you warn or do not warn, whether you give glad tidings or not—it has no effect. Fundamentally, they lack the spiritual readiness to follow the truth and submit to it.

The second verse points to the reason for this prejudice and stubbornness, stating: They are so deeply immersed in disbelief and enmity that they have lost their sense of discernment. "Allah has set a seal upon their hearts and upon their hearing, and over their vision is a veil" (خَتَمَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَ عَلى‌ سَمْعِهِمْ وَ عَلى‌ أَبْصارِهِمْ غِشاوَةٌ). For this reason, the result of their conduct is that "for them is a great punishment" (وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ‌).

Thus, the eye with which the God-fearing saw the signs of Allah, the ear with which they heard the words of truth, and the heart through which they understood realities have ceased to function in these people. They possess intellect, eyes, and ears, but they lack the power of "understanding," "vision," and "hearing"! This is because their evil deeds, stubbornness, and enmity have become a veil before these instruments of cognition.

Certainly, as long as a human has not reached this stage, he is capable of being guided, even if he is astray. But once he loses his sense of discernment due to his own evil actions, there is no longer a way of salvation for him, because he lacks the tools of cognition. It is only natural that a great punishment awaits him.

Key Points

1- Does the deprivation of the power of discernment imply predestination (Jabr)?

The first question that arises here is: if, according to the above verse, Allah has set a seal on the hearts and ears of this group and placed a veil over their eyes, are they not forced to remain in disbelief? Is this not predestination? Similar verses appear elsewhere in the Quran; if so, what is the meaning of their punishment?

The Quran itself provides the answer to this question: their persistence and stubbornness against the truth, and their continuation in oppression, injustice, and disbelief, cause a veil to fall over their sense of discernment. In Surah An-Nisa, verse 155, we read: "Rather, Allah has set a seal upon them because of their disbelief" (بَلْ طَبَعَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْها بِكُفْرِهِمْ‌). In Surah Ghafir (Al-Mu'min), verse 35, we read: "Thus does Allah seal over every heart [that is] proud and tyrannical" (كَذلِكَ يَطْبَعُ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ قَلْبِ مُتَكَبِّرٍ جَبَّارٍ). And in Surah Al-Jathiyah, verse 23, it is stated: "Have you seen he who has taken as his god his [own] desire, and Allah has sent him astray due to knowledge and has set a seal upon his hearing and his heart and put over his vision a veil?" (أَ فَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ وَ أَضَلَّهُ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ عِلْمٍ وَ خَتَمَ عَلى‌ سَمْعِهِ وَ قَلْبِهِ وَ جَعَلَ عَلى‌ بَصَرِهِ غِشاوَةً).

You observe that the deprivation of the sense of discernment and the disabling of the instruments of cognition in man are described in these verses as being caused by specific factors: disbelief, arrogance, oppression, following rebellious desires, and stubbornness against the truth. In reality, this state is the reaction and reflection of the human's own actions, nothing else.

Fundamentally, it is a natural phenomenon that if a person continues a wrong and deviant path, he gradually becomes accustomed to it. At first, it is a "state," then it becomes a "habit," then it transforms into a "fixed trait" (malakah) and becomes part of the fabric of the human soul. Sometimes it reaches a point where return is impossible for him. However, since he consciously chose this path, he is responsible for all its consequences without any necessity of predestination—just like someone who consciously uses a tool to blind and deafen himself so that he sees and hears nothing.

And if we see these effects attributed to Allah, it is because Allah has placed this property within such actions (reflect on this).

The opposite of this is also clearly visible in the laws of creation: one who adopts purity, piety, integrity, and truthfulness, Allah strengthens his sense of discernment and grants him a specific understanding, vision, and insight. As we read in Surah Al-Anfal, verse 29: "O you who have believed, if you fear Allah, He will grant you a criterion [to discern between right and wrong]" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ تَتَّقُوا اللَّهَ يَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ فُرْقاناً).

We have also tested this reality in our daily lives. There are individuals who begin a wrong action; at the start, they admit they are 100% wrong and sinful, and for this reason, they feel uneasy about their work. But gradually, as they become accustomed to it, this unease disappears. In higher stages, it sometimes reaches a point where not only are they not uneasy, but they are happy and consider it their human or religious duty! In the accounts of Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, that arch-criminal of his era, we read that to justify his horrific crimes, he would say: "These people are sinners; I must dominate them and oppress them because they deserve it." It was as if he perceived all those killings, bloodshed, and crimes as a mission from God! It is also said that one of Genghis Khan's soldiers gave a speech in one of the border cities of Iran, saying: "Do you not believe that Allah sends punishment upon sinners? We are that divine punishment, so do not resist at all!"

2- If they cannot be guided, why the persistence of the Prophets?

This is another question that comes to mind regarding the above verses. However, paying attention to one point clarifies the answer: divine punishments and penalties are always linked to human actions and behavior. One cannot be punished merely for being a bad person at heart; rather, it is necessary first to invite them toward the truth. If they do not follow and reflect their inner impurity in their actions, then they deserve punishment. Otherwise, it would be a case of "retribution before the crime."

This is what we call "establishing the proof" (itmam al-hujjah).

In short: the recompense and reward for an action must necessarily follow the performance of the action; mere intention or readiness and spiritual or intellectual predispositions are not sufficient for this.

Furthermore, the Prophets did not come only to guide these individuals; these are in the minority. The majority of the misguided masses are those who, under correct teaching and training, are capable of being guided.

3- Setting a Seal on the Hearts

In the above verses and many other verses of the Quran, the term "seal" (khatm) is used to describe the deprivation of the sense of discernment and true understanding in individuals; occasionally, the terms "stamping" (tab') and "rust" (rayn) are also used.

This meaning is derived from a custom among people: when they placed objects in special bags or containers, or put important letters in envelopes, they would tie and knot them and then place a seal on the knot so that no one would open or touch them. Today, it is also common to bind official property deeds with a special string, place a piece of lead over it, and stamp the lead so that if anything is added or removed from the pages, it becomes evident.

In history, many instances are seen where heads of government would seal bags of gold with their own signet and send them to specific individuals. This was to ensure no tampering occurred until it reached the recipient, as tampering was impossible without breaking the seal. Today, postal bags are similarly wax-sealed.

In the Arabic language, the word khatm is used for this meaning. Of course, this expression is used for stubborn, faithless individuals who, due to many sins, have become impenetrable to the factors of guidance. Stubbornness and enmity toward men of truth have become so deeply rooted in their hearts that they are exactly like that sealed package or bag which can no longer be altered; colloquially, their hearts have been "waxed and sealed."

Tab' also carries this same meaning in the language, and tabi' (on the pattern of khatam) both mean the same thing: the object with which one seals.

As for rayn, it means rust, dust, or a dirty layer that settles on precious objects. This expression is also used in the Quran for those whose hearts have become impenetrable due to excessive obstinacy and sin. In Surah Al-Mutaffifin, verse 14, we read: "No! Rather, the stain of what they were earning has covered their hearts" (كَلَّا بَلْ رانَ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ ما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ‌).

The important thing is for a person to be careful: if, God forbid, a sin is committed, he should wash it away shortly after with the water of repentance and righteous deeds, lest it become a permanent color for the heart and set a seal upon it.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "There is no believing servant except that in his heart there is a white (broad) spot. When he commits a sin, a black spot appears in that white area. If he repents, that blackness departs, but if he persists in sins, that blackness increases until it covers the whiteness. Once the whiteness is covered, the owner of such a heart will never return to goodness, and that is the meaning of the words of Allah, the Almighty: 'No! Rather, the stain of what they were earning has covered their hearts.'" [^1]

4- The Meaning of "Heart" in the Quran

Why is the understanding of realities attributed to the "heart" in the Quran, while we know the heart is not the center of perceptions but a pump for circulating blood in the body? In response, we say:

"Heart" (qalb) appears in the Quran with various meanings, including:

  1. Meaning intellect and understanding, as we read in verse 37 of Surah Qaf: "Indeed in that is a reminder for whoever has a heart" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَذِكْرى‌ لِمَنْ كانَ لَهُ قَلْبٌ‌)—meaning those who possess the power of intellect and understanding.

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Sins, Hadith 20, p. 209.

  1. Meaning the soul and life, as stated in Surah Al-Ahzab, verse 10: "And when the eyes swerved [in fear] and the hearts reached the throats" (وَ إِذْ زاغَتِ الْأَبْصارُ وَ بَلَغَتِ الْقُلُوبُ الْحَناجِرَ).

  2. Meaning the center of emotions; verse 12 of Surah Al-Anfal testifies to this: "I will cast terror into the hearts of those who disbelieved" (سَأُلْقِي فِي قُلُوبِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الرُّعْبَ‌). And elsewhere in Surah Ali 'Imran, verse 159, we read: "...So by mercy from Allah, you were lenient with them. And if you had been rude [in speech] and harsh in heart, they would have disbanded from about you" (فَبِما رَحْمَةٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ لِنْتَ لَهُمْ وَ لَوْ كُنْتَ فَظًّا غَلِيظَ الْقَلْبِ لَانْفَضُّوا مِنْ حَوْلِكَ‌).

To explain: Two powerful centers are observed in the human existence:

  1. The center of perceptions, which is the "brain and the nervous system." Thus, when an intellectual matter arises for us, we feel that we analyze it with our brain (although the brain and nervous system are actually tools and instruments for the soul).

  2. The center of emotions, which is the physical heart located in the left part of the chest. Emotional issues affect this center in the first instance; the first spark begins from the heart.

Intuitively, when we face a tragedy, we feel its pressure on this physical heart, and similarly, when we encounter something joyful, we feel delight and expansion in this same center (reflect on this).

It is true that the primary center of both "perceptions" and "emotions" is the human psyche and soul, but their physical manifestations and reactions differ. The reaction of understanding and comprehension first appears in the brain apparatus, but the reaction to emotional matters such as love, enmity, fear, tranquility, joy, and grief appears in the human heart, such that when these things occur, we clearly feel their effect in our hearts.

The conclusion is that if emotional matters are attributed to the heart (this specific organ) and intellectual matters are attributed to the heart (meaning the intellect or brain) in the Quran, the reason is what has been stated, and it is not an exaggeration.

Beyond all this, the heart, as a specific organ, plays a vital role in human life and survival, such that its cessation for a single moment is accompanied by destruction. Therefore, what prevents intellectual and emotional activities from being attributed to it?

5- Why are "Heart" and "Vision" mentioned in the plural, while "Hearing" is mentioned in the singular?

In the above verse, as in many other verses of the Quran, "heart" and "vision" appear in the plural form (qulub and absar), but "hearing" (sam') is mentioned in the singular form everywhere in the Quran. This difference must have a point; what is it?

Answer: It is true that the word sam' appears everywhere in the Quran in the singular and never in the plural (asma'), whereas qalb and basar sometimes appear in the plural as in the above verse, and sometimes in the singular as in verse 23 of Surah Al-Jathiyah: (وَ خَتَمَ عَلى‌ سَمْعِهِ وَ قَلْبِهِ وَ جَعَلَ عَلى‌ بَصَرِهِ غِشاوَةً).

The noble scholar, the late Sheikh Tusi, in the commentary Al-Tibyan, quotes one of the famous linguists stating that the reason for sam' being singular might be one of two things:

First: Sam' is sometimes used as a collective noun, and we know that a collective noun contains the meaning of plurality and does not need to be pluralized.

Second: Sam' can have an infinitive (masdar) meaning, and we know that an infinitive denotes both little and much and does not require pluralization.

Furthermore, another aesthetic and scientific reason can be given for this difference: the variety of cardiac perceptions and visual observations is far greater than that of "audible things." Because of this difference, qulub and absar are mentioned in the plural, while sam' appears in the singular.

In modern physics, we also read that the number of audible sound waves is relatively limited and does not exceed several tens of thousands, whereas the waves of light and colors that are visible number in the millions (reflect on this).

Ayahs

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَبِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَمَا هُم بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (٨)
Among the people are those who say, ‘We have faith in Allah and the Last Day,’ but they have no faith. (8)
يُخَـٰدِعُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَمَا يَخْدَعُونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٩)
They seek to deceive Allah and those who have faith, yet they deceive no one but themselves, but they are not aware. (9)
فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ فَزَادَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مَرَضًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْذِبُونَ (١٠)
There is a sickness in their hearts; then Allah increased their sickness, and there is a painful punishment for them because of the lies they used to tell. (10)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ لَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ مُصْلِحُونَ (١١)
When they are told, ‘Do not cause corruption on the earth,’ they say, ‘We are only reformers!’ (11)
أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْسِدُونَ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا يَشْعُرُونَ (١٢)
Behold! They are themselves the agents of corruption, but they are not aware. (12)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ ءَامِنُوا۟ كَمَآ ءَامَنَ ٱلنَّاسُ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنُؤْمِنُ كَمَآ ءَامَنَ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٣)
And when they are told, ‘Believe like the people who have believed,’ they say, ‘Shall we believe like the fools who have believed?’ Behold! They are themselves the fools, but they do not know. (13)
وَإِذَا لَقُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ شَيَـٰطِينِهِمْ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مَعَكُمْ إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ مُسْتَهْزِءُونَ (١٤)
When they meet the faithful, they say, ‘We believe,’ but when they are alone with their devils, they say, ‘We are with you; we were only deriding [them].’ (14)
ٱللَّهُ يَسْتَهْزِئُ بِهِمْ وَيَمُدُّهُمْ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (١٥)
It is Allah who derides them, and leaves them bewildered in their rebellion. (15)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةَ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ فَمَا رَبِحَت تِّجَـٰرَتُهُمْ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُهْتَدِينَ (١٦)
They are the ones who bought error for guidance, so their trade did not profit them, nor were they guided. (16)

Commentary

The Third Group (The Hypocrites)

The above verses provide a concise and highly profound description of the hypocrites, their psychological traits, and their actions.

To elaborate: At a specific historical juncture, Islam encountered a group that possessed neither the sincerity and courage to embrace faith nor the power and audacity for overt opposition.

This group, whom the Quran refers to as the "Munafiqin" (hypocrites)—and whom we describe in Persian as "two-faced"—had infiltrated the ranks of the true Muslims. They were considered a great danger to Islam and the Muslims, and since they maintained an Islamic outward appearance, identifying them was often difficult. However, the Quran provides precise and vivid signs for them that distinguish their inner path, offering a model in this regard for Muslims of all centuries and ages.

First, it offers a definition of hypocrisy itself, stating: "And of the people are some who say, 'We believe in Allah and the Last Day,' while they are not believers" (وَ مِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَقُولُ آمَنَّا بِاللَّهِ وَ بِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَ ما هُمْ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

They consider this act a kind of cleverness or a so-called "interesting tactic": "They seek to deceive Allah and those who believe" (يُخادِعُونَ اللَّهَ وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا).

"While they deceive none but themselves, but they perceive [it] not" (وَ ما يَخْدَعُونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَ ما يَشْعُرُونَ‌).

By deviating from the correct path and the Straight Path, they spend a lifetime in error, wasting all their energies and resources, gaining nothing but failure, defeat, infamy, and Divine punishment.

Then, in the following verse, the Quran points to the reality that hypocrisy is, in fact, a type of disease. A healthy human being has only one face; complete harmony reigns between his soul and body, for the outward and inward, the spirit and the physical form, all complement one another. If he is a believer, his entire being cries out with faith, and if he deviates, both his outward and inward reflect that deviation. This duality of body and soul is a new pain and an additional illness; it is a type of contradiction, disharmony, and fragmentation that dominates a person's existence.

It says: "In their hearts is a disease" (فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ‌).

However, since in the system of creation, whoever sets out on a path and provides its means will move forward on that same path—or in other words, the accumulation of a person's actions and thoughts in one direction makes it more vivid and deep-rooted—the Quran adds: "So Allah has increased their disease" (فَزادَهُمُ اللَّهُ مَرَضاً).

And since the primary capital of the hypocrites is lying—using it as much as they can to justify the contradictions seen in their lives—at the end of the verse, He says: "And for them is a painful punishment because they used to lie" (وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ بِما كانُوا يَكْذِبُونَ‌).

Then it points to their characteristics, the first of which is their claim to be reformers, while the true corrupters are none but them: "And when it is said to them, 'Do not cause corruption on the earth,' they say, 'We are only reformers!'" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ لا تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ قالُوا إِنَّما نَحْنُ مُصْلِحُونَ‌). We have had no program throughout our lives except reform!

In the next verse, the Quran says: "Unquestionably, it is they who are the corrupters, and they have no program but corruption, but they perceive [it] not!" (أَلا إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ الْمُفْسِدُونَ وَ لكِنْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ‌).

Rather, their insistence and persistence in the way of hypocrisy and becoming accustomed to these ugly and shameful programs have caused them to gradually imagine that these programs are beneficial, constructive, and reformative. As we have previously noted, if sin exceeds its limit, it strips a person of the sense of discernment; rather, it turns his discernment upside down, and impurity and pollution become his "second nature."

Another sign is that they consider themselves wise and alert, while viewing the believers as foolish, simple-minded, and gullible. As the Quran says: "And when it is said to them, 'Believe as the people have believed,' they say, 'Should we believe as the fools have believed?'" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ آمِنُوا كَما آمَنَ النَّاسُ قالُوا أَ نُؤْمِنُ كَما آمَنَ السُّفَهاءُ).

In this way, they accuse the pure-hearted, truth-seeking individuals—who bowed their heads in submission upon seeing the signs of truth in the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call and the content of his teachings—of foolishness. They count devilry, double-dealing, and hypocrisy as evidence of intelligence, wisdom, and sagacity. Yes, in their logic, wisdom has swapped places with foolishness.

Therefore, the Quran says in response to them: "Unquestionably, it is they who are the fools, but they know [it] not" (أَلا إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ السُّفَهاءُ وَ لكِنْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

Is it not foolishness for a person not to define his path in life, to take on the color of every group he is among, and to accept duality and multiplicity instead of focus and unity of personality? To employ his talent and strength in the way of devilry, conspiracy, and destruction, and yet consider himself wise?!

Their third sign is that they change colors every day and harmonize with every group they are among. As the Quran says: "And when they meet those who believe, they say, 'We believe'" (وَ إِذا لَقُوا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا قالُوا آمَنَّا). We are of you and follow the same school of thought; we have accepted Islam with heart and soul and are no different from you! "But when they are alone with their evil ones (satanic friends), they say, 'Indeed, we are with you'" (وَ إِذا خَلَوْا إِلى‌ شَياطِينِهِمْ قالُوا إِنَّا مَعَكُمْ‌).

"And if you see us expressing faith before the believers, we were only mocking them!" (إِنَّما نَحْنُ مُسْتَهْزِؤُنَ‌).

In our hearts, we laugh at their thoughts and actions; we want to pull the wool over their eyes; you are our friends, our confidants, and our everything!

Then the Quran says in a crushing and decisive tone: "Allah mocks them" (اللَّهُ يَسْتَهْزِئُ بِهِمْ‌). "And He leaves them in their transgression, wandering blindly" (وَ يَمُدُّهُمْ فِي طُغْيانِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ‌) [1]

The final verse under discussion describes their ultimate fate—which is a very tragic, ominous, and dark fate—as follows:

"Those are the ones who have purchased error in exchange for guidance in the marketplace of this world" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ اشْتَرَوُا الضَّلالَةَ بِالْهُدى‌).

And for this reason, "their transaction has brought no profit," rather they have even lost their capital (فَما رَبِحَتْ تِجارَتُهُمْ‌).

"And they were never guided" (وَ ما كانُوا مُهْتَدِينَ‌).

Key Points

1- The Emergence of Hypocrisy and Its Roots

When a revolution occurs in an environment—especially a revolution like the Islamic Revolution which was based on truth and justice—the interests of a group of plunderers, oppressors, and autocrats are inevitably endangered. They first try to break the revolution through mockery and ridicule, and then by using armed force, economic pressure, and continuous social propaganda.

However, when the signs of the revolution's victory over all the powers of the environment become apparent, a group of opponents changes their tactics and practical methods. They outwardly surrender but, in reality, form an underground opposition group.

These people, who are called "Munafiq" (hypocrites) because of having two different faces (Munafiq is from the root "nafaq," meaning the tunnels and burrows dug underground to be used for concealment or escape), are the most dangerous enemies of a revolution. This is because their position is not clearly defined for the revolutionary people to recognize and expel them; rather, they infiltrate the ranks of the pure and truthful people, and sometimes even sensitive posts.

The Islamic Revolution also faced such a group. That is, until the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله migrated from Mecca to Medina, the Muslims had not formed a government.

But after the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله arrival in Medina, the first foundation of the Islamic government was laid, and after the victory in the Battle of Badr, this matter became more evident; that is, a small but growing government and state was officially formed.

It was here that the interests of many of the leaders of Medina, especially the Jews who were respected by the Arabs at that time, were endangered. The respect for the Jews at that time was mostly because they were People of the Book, relatively literate, and economically advanced; it was they who, before the appearance of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, used to give glad tidings of such an appearance.

There were other individuals in Medina who claimed leadership and headship over the people, but with the migration of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, their calculations were disrupted. The oppressive leaders and their plundering entourages saw that the masses of people were quickly believing in the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, even their own relatives. After a period of resistance, they saw no choice but to outwardly become Muslims, because sounding the drum of opposition and standing in the opposing front, in addition to the problems of war and economic damage, carried the risk of their destruction—especially since an Arab's entire power was his tribe, and their tribes had mostly separated from them.

Based on this, they chose a third way: to outwardly become Muslims and secretly plan the destruction of Islam.

In short, the emergence of "hypocrisy" in a society is usually caused by one of two things: first, the victory and power of the existing revolutionary creed and its dominance over society; and second, a weakness of spirit and a lack of sufficient character and courage to face difficult events.

2- The Necessity of Identifying Hypocrites in Every Society

Undoubtedly, hypocrisy and the hypocrite were not exclusive to the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; this program and group exist in every society. However, they must be identified based on the calculated criteria that the Quran provides for them, so they cannot cause harm or danger. In the preceding verses, as well as in Surah Al-Munafiqun and Islamic narrations, various signs have been mentioned for them, including:

1- Great clamor and big claims; in short, much talk and little, inconsistent action. 2- Taking on the color of every environment and speaking to every group according to their taste; saying "We believe" to the believers and "Indeed, we are with you" to the opponents! 3- Separating their account from the people and forming secret and mysterious societies with calculated plans. 4- Deception, trickery, fraud, lying, flattery, sycophancy, breaking covenants, and betrayal. 5- Self-superiority, regarding the people as unaware, foolish, and simpletons, while considering themselves wise and alert.

In summary, the duality of personality and the contradiction between the outward and inward—which is the prominent trait of hypocrites—has various manifestations in their individual and social actions, speech, and behavior that can be easily recognized.

What a beautiful expression the Quran uses in the verses we read: فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ‌: "In their hearts is a disease." What disease is worse than the duality of outward and inward? And what disease is more painful than self-superiority or lacking the courage to face events?

But just as a heart disease, however hidden, cannot be completely concealed—rather its signs appear in a person's face and all members of the body—the disease of hypocrisy is the same, identifiable through various manifestations.

In Tafsir Nemouneh, under verses 141 to 143 of Surah An-Nisa, we have also discussed the "attributes of the hypocrites" (Volume 4, pages 174 to 178). We also have a detailed discussion on this subject under verses 49 to 57 of Surah At-Tawbah (Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 7, pages 438 to 450). In Surah At-Tawbah, under verses 62 to 85, you will also read many discussions in this regard (Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 8, pages 19 to 72).

3- The Breadth of the Meaning of Hypocrisy

Although hypocrisy in its specific sense is a trait of faithless individuals who are outwardly in the ranks of Muslims but inwardly committed to disbelief, hypocrisy has a broad meaning that includes any kind of duality between outward and inward, speech and action, even if it is in believing individuals—which we refer to as "streaks of hypocrisy."

For example, we read in a hadith: "There are three traits which, if found in anyone, make him a hypocrite, even if he fasts, prays, and claims to be a Muslim: he who betrays a trust, he who lies when he speaks, and he who breaks his promise when he makes one." [1] Certainly, such individuals are not hypocrites in the specific sense, but there are streaks of hypocrisy in their existence. Especially regarding the ostentatious (show-offs), we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "Ostentation is a tree that bears no fruit except hidden polytheism (shirk), and its root is hypocrisy!" [1]

Here, we draw your attention to a statement by the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, regarding the hypocrites: "O servants of Allah! I advise you to have Taqwa (piety) and warn you against the hypocrites, for they are the strayed and the straying, the erring and the ones who lead into error. They take on various colors and show themselves in multiple guises and tongues. They use every means to deceive and break you, and they lie in wait for you at every ambush. They are evil inwardly and fair outwardly, and they walk secretly to deceive people. They move through bypasses, and their speech is seemingly a cure, but their actions are an incurable disease. They envy the people's ease and comfort and are happy if a calamity befalls someone. They make the hopeful lose hope and have a victim on every path. They have a way into every heart and shed fake tears for every misfortune. They lend praise and eulogy to one another and expect reward and recompense. If they ask for something, they persist, and if they blame, they strip away all veils of decency." [2]

4- The Sabotage of the Hypocrites

Not only for Islam but for any revolutionary and progressive creed, hypocrites are the most dangerous group. They infiltrate the ranks of the Muslims and use every opportunity for sabotage.

Sometimes they would mock the true believers who, with complete sincerity, spent their modest wealth in the way of Allah, as the Quran says: "Those who criticize the contributors among the believers concerning [their] charities and [criticize] those who find nothing except their effort, so they ridicule them - Allah will ridicule them, and they will have a painful punishment" (At-Tawbah: 79).

And sometimes in their secret meetings, they would decide to completely cut off financial aid from the companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله so that they would disperse from around him, as stated in Surah Al-Munafiqun: "They are the ones who say, 'Do not spend on those who are with the Messenger of Allah until they disband.' And to Allah belong the depositories of the heavens and the earth, but the hypocrites do not understand" (Surah Al-Munafiqun: 7).

Sometimes they decided that after returning from war to Medina, they would join hands and, using a suitable opportunity, expel the believers from Medina, saying: "If we return to Medina, the more honored will surely expel therefrom the more humble"! (Al-Munafiqun: 8).

And at times, under various pretexts such as gathering agricultural harvests, they would refrain from participating in Jihad programs and would leave the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله alone at the most critical moments, while simultaneously fearing that the veil would be lifted from their secret and they would be disgraced.

Because of these very hostile stances, they were targeted by the most severe attacks in many verses of the Quran, and a whole surah in the Quran named "Al-Munafiqun" was revealed concerning their situation.

In Surahs At-Tawbah, Al-Hashr, and some other surahs of the Quran, they have also been heavily rebuked; including thirteen verses of this very Surah Al-Baqarah which speak of their traits and their ominous consequences.

5- Deceiving the Conscience

A major problem that Muslims had in connection with the hypocrites was that, on one hand, they were commanded to welcome with open arms anyone who declared Islam and to refrain from investigating people's beliefs; on the other hand, they had to be wary of the conspiracies of the hypocrites—hypocrites whose speech was accepted by people because they appeared righteous and called themselves Muslims, while inwardly they were barriers to the path of Islam and sworn enemies.

By taking this path, this group thought they could deceive Allah and the believers forever, while they were deceiving themselves without realizing it.

The expression يُخادِعُونَ اللَّهَ وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (considering the meaning of mukhad'ah which implies deception and trickery from two sides) depicts a precise concept: that on one hand, due to their blindness of heart, they believed that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was a deceiver who had brought up religion and prophethood to rule over the people, and that simple-minded individuals had gathered around him; therefore, they must rise to deceive him! Thus, on one hand, the work of these hypocrites was deception and trickery, and on the other hand, they held such a false belief about the great Prophet of God.

But the next sentence (وَ ما يَخْدَعُونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَ ما يَشْعُرُونَ‌) crushes both of their imaginings. On one hand, it proves that deception and trickery are only from their side, and on the other hand, it says that this deception and trickery also returns to themselves and they do not understand; for they waste the original capitals that Allah created in their existence to achieve happiness in the path of deception, fraud, and trickery, and they leave the world empty-handed of any good or virtue, with a heavy burden of sin.

Of course, no one can deceive Allah, for He is aware of the open and the hidden. Therefore, the expression "they seek to deceive Allah" is either from the perspective that deceiving the Prophet and the believers is like deceiving Allah (it is seen in other places in the Quran that Allah places Himself in their rank to honor the Prophet and the believers), or that due to their lack of knowledge of Allah's attributes, they truly thought with their short and deficient thoughts that it was possible for something to remain hidden from Allah; similar instances are seen in some other verses of the Quran.

In any case, the above verse clearly points to the issue of "deceiving the conscience" and that it often happens that a deviated and polluted person, to escape the reproach and punishment of the conscience for ugly and deviant acts, resorts to deceiving his own conscience. Gradually, he creates this belief for himself that "this act of mine is not only not ugly and deviant, but it is reform and fighting against corruption" (إِنَّما نَحْنُ مُصْلِحُونَ‌), so that by deceiving the conscience, he may continue his wrongful acts with a peaceful mind.

It is said that one of the leaders of America, in response to why he ordered the atomic bombing of two large cities in Japan (Hiroshima and Nagasaki) and the destruction or maiming of about 200,000 children, elderly, and young people, said: "We gave this order for the sake of peace! For if we had not done this, the war would have lasted longer and we would have had to kill more than this!!" Yes, the hypocrites of our age also have many such sayings and such deeds to deceive the people or deceive their own consciences, while there was a clear third way instead of continuing the war or the atomic bombing of defenseless cities: to stop their aggression and leave the nations free with their countries' resources.

Therefore, hypocrisy is in reality a means to deceive the conscience, and how painful it is for a person to suffocate this internal preacher, this always-awake policeman, and this Divine representative within himself, or to cast such a veil over it that its voice cannot be heard.

6- A Highly Loss-Making Trade

In the Holy Quran, human activities in this world are repeatedly likened to a type of trade. In reality, all of us in this world are traders who enter this great marketplace with vast God-given capitals: the capital of intellect, fitrah (innate nature), emotions, various physical powers, the blessings of the natural world, and finally the leadership of the Prophets. One group profits, succeeds, and becomes happy; another group not only makes no profit but also loses the original capital and becomes bankrupt in every sense. The perfect example of the first group are the mujahideen in the way of Allah, as the Quran says about them: "O you who have believed, shall I guide you to a transaction that will save you from a painful punishment? [It is that] you believe in Allah and His Messenger and strive in the cause of Allah with your wealth and your lives" (As-Saff: 10-11).

And the clear example of the second group are the hypocrites, about whom the Quran says in the above verses, after mentioning their destructive works carried out in the garb of reform and wisdom: "Those are the ones who have purchased error in exchange for guidance, so their transaction has brought no profit, nor were they guided." This group was in a position where they could have chosen the best path; they were beside the clear spring of revelation, in an environment full of purity, honesty, and faith.

But instead of taking the highest advantage of this specific situation—which throughout the centuries has been granted to only a small group—they gave away guidance and bought error. The guidance that was within their fitrah, the guidance that was surging in the environment of revelation—they lost all these possibilities thinking that by doing so, they could crush the Muslims and realize the ominous dreams they nurtured in their brains.

This wrong exchange and choice brought two great losses: First, they lost their material and spiritual capitals without gaining any profit in return. And second, they did not even reach their intended ominous result, for Islam progressed rapidly and soon covered the face of the world, and these hypocrites were also disgraced.

[1]: "Ya'mahun" is from the root "amah" (on the weight of hamah), meaning hesitation and bewilderment in a task; it has also been interpreted as blindness of heart and darkness of insight, the effect of which is wandering (refer to Raghib's Mufradat, Tafsir al-Manar, and Qamus al-Lughah).

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 605. [^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, under the root "ra-a-ya". [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 194.

Ayahs

مَثَلُهُمْ كَمَثَلِ ٱلَّذِى ٱسْتَوْقَدَ نَارًا فَلَمَّآ أَضَآءَتْ مَا حَوْلَهُۥ ذَهَبَ ٱللَّهُ بِنُورِهِمْ وَتَرَكَهُمْ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتٍ لَّا يُبْصِرُونَ (١٧)
Their parable is that of one who lighted a torch, and when it had lit up all around him, Allah took away their light, and left them sightless in a manifold darkness. (17)
صُمٌّۢ بُكْمٌ عُمْىٌ فَهُمْ لَا يَرْجِعُونَ (١٨)
Deaf, dumb, and blind, they will not come back. (18)
أَوْ كَصَيِّبٍ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فِيهِ ظُلُمَـٰتٌ وَرَعْدٌ وَبَرْقٌ يَجْعَلُونَ أَصَـٰبِعَهُمْ فِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِم مِّنَ ٱلصَّوَٰعِقِ حَذَرَ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ مُحِيطٌۢ بِٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٩)
Or that of a rainstorm from the sky, wherein is darkness, thunder and lightning: they put their fingers in their ears due to the thunderclaps, apprehensive of death; and Allah besieges the faithless. (19)
يَكَادُ ٱلْبَرْقُ يَخْطَفُ أَبْصَـٰرَهُمْ ۖ كُلَّمَآ أَضَآءَ لَهُم مَّشَوْا۟ فِيهِ وَإِذَآ أَظْلَمَ عَلَيْهِمْ قَامُوا۟ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَذَهَبَ بِسَمْعِهِمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢٠)
The lightning almost snatches away their sight: whenever it shines for them, they walk in it, and when the darkness falls upon them, they stand. Had Allah willed, He would have taken away their hearing and sight. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (20)

Commentary

Two Striking Parables Depicting the State of the Hypocrites

After describing the attributes and characteristics of the hypocrites, the Holy Quran presents two vivid parables in the above verses to visualize their condition:

1- In the first example, It says: "Their likeness is as the likeness of one who kindled a fire (in a dark night, so that by its light he might distinguish the path from the wilderness and reach his destination) (مَثَلُهُمْ كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي اسْتَوْقَدَ ناراً)."

"But as soon as this flame lit up what was around them, Allah took away their light and left them in darkness, so they could not see" (فَلَمَّا أَضاءَتْ ما حَوْلَهُ ذَهَبَ اللَّهُ بِنُورِهِمْ وَ تَرَكَهُمْ فِي ظُلُماتٍ لا يُبْصِرُونَ‌).

They thought that with this meager fire and its light, they could combat the darkness. However, suddenly a fierce wind rises, or a heavy rain falls, or the fuel runs out, causing the fire to grow cold and die out, leaving them once again wandering in terrifying darkness.

Then It adds: "They are deaf, dumb, and blind, so they do not return (to the right path), because they lack the primary means of perceiving realities" (صُمٌّ بُكْمٌ عُمْيٌ فَهُمْ لا يَرْجِعُونَ‌).

What a precise and expressive example: In human life, there are many deviations, but the straight line leading to the ultimate destination is only one, while the lines of deviation are infinite. Furthermore, veils of darkness, terrifying storms, and various incidents are frequent along this path. A bright lamp is needed—one that is immune to these incidents—to pierce through these veils of darkness and withstand the storms. This is nothing other than the lamp of intellect and faith, and the sun of Revelation.

What can a meager flame, lit temporarily by a human, achieve on such a long and storm-filled journey?! By choosing the path of hypocrisy, the "hypocrites" imagined they could maintain their position in all circumstances and remain safe from any potential danger. They sought to benefit from both sides, identifying with whichever group prevailed. If the believers were victorious, they were in the ranks of the believers; if the disbelievers gained the upper hand, they were with them.

They considered themselves clever and intelligent, and in the glow of this weak and unstable flame, they wanted to continue their way of life and gain some advantage.

But the Quran pulled back the curtain on their schemes and exposed their lies, as we read: إِذا جاءَكَ الْمُنافِقُونَ قالُوا نَشْهَدُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُ اللَّهِ وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُهُ وَ اللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ لَكاذِبُونَ‌: "When the hypocrites come to you, they say, 'We bear witness that you are indeed the Messenger of Allah.' Allah knows that you are indeed His Messenger, and Allah bears witness that the hypocrites are surely liars (in their declarations)" (Surah Al-Munafiqun, Verses 1 and 2).

The Quran even announces to the disbelievers that the hypocrites are not truly with them and will not fulfill any promises they make:

أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ نافَقُوا يَقُولُونَ لِإِخْوانِهِمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ لَئِنْ أُخْرِجْتُمْ لَنَخْرُجَنَّ مَعَكُمْ وَ لا نُطِيعُ فِيكُمْ أَحَداً أَبَداً وَ إِنْ قُوتِلْتُمْ لَنَنْصُرَنَّكُمْ وَ اللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ- لَئِنْ أُخْرِجُوا لا يَخْرُجُونَ مَعَهُمْ وَ لَئِنْ قُوتِلُوا لا يَنْصُرُونَهُمْ وَ لَئِنْ نَصَرُوهُمْ لَيُوَلُّنَّ الْأَدْبارَ ثُمَّ لا يُنْصَرُونَ‌:

"Have you not seen those who are hypocrites? They say to their brothers who disbelieve among the People of the Book, 'If you are expelled, we will surely leave with you, and we will never obey anyone against you; and if you are fought against, we will surely help you.' But Allah bears witness that they are liars. If they are expelled, they will not leave with them; and if they are fought against, they will not help them. And even if they were to help them (in the heat of battle), they would surely turn their backs and flee" (and they will not show steadfastness) (Al-Hashr, 11-12).

It is noteworthy that the Quran here uses the phrase "استوقد نارا" (kindled a fire); that is, they use "fire" (nar) to reach "light" (nur)—a fire that produces smoke, ash, and burning—whereas the believers benefit from the pure light and the bright, radiant lamp of faith.

Although the hypocrites pretend to have the light of faith, their inner reality is fire, and even if there is a light, it is weak and short-lived.
