This meager light refers either to the glow of conscience and the primordial nature of monotheism (fitrah), or to their initial faith which later became covered by veils of darkness due to blind imitation, false prejudice, stubbornness, and enmity—not just one darkness, but as the Quran describes, "darknesses" (ظلمات).

It is these factors that eventually strip them of their seeing eyes, hearing ears, and speaking tongues. As we mentioned before, continuing on a wrong path gradually weakens a person's power of discernment and understanding, to the point where they sometimes see realities in reverse: good appears bad to them, and an angel appears as a demon.

In any case, this parable clarifies a reality regarding hypocrisy: that hypocrisy and double-dealing cannot be effective for a long time. The hypocrites might enjoy the benefits of Islam and the protections of the believers for a short period, and also benefit from their secret friendship with the disbelievers.

But this, like a weak and fleeting flame exposed to storms in a dark and gloomy desert, does not last long. Eventually, their true face is revealed, and instead of gaining success and popularity, they become loathed and rejected, left wandering like someone who has lost their way in the desert and whose lamp has gone out.

It is also noteworthy that in the commentary of the verse: هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ الشَّمْسَ ضِياءً وَ الْقَمَرَ نُوراً: "It is He who made the sun a radiance and the moon a light" (Yunus, 5), it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that he said:

اضائت الارض بنور محمد (ص) كما تضي‌ء الشمس فضرب اللَّه مثل محمد (ص) الشمس و مثل الوصى القمر:

"Allah illuminated the surface of the earth with the light of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله just as the sun illuminates; thus Allah likened Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله to the sun and his successor (Wasi) Ali عليه السلام to the moon." [1] This means the light of faith and Revelation is world-embracing, whereas hypocrisy, if it has any glow at all, only illuminates a small circle around itself and only for a short time (ما حَوْلَهُ‌).

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 36.

In the second parable, the Quran depicts the scene of their lives in another way:

It is a dark and gloomy night, full of fear and danger. Rain is pouring heavily. From the edges of the horizon, a bright flash of lightning strikes. The terrifying and massive roar of thunder is about to burst the eardrums. A defenseless person is left bewildered and wandering in the heart of this vast, dark, and dangerous plain. The heavy rain has soaked his body; there is no reliable shelter to take refuge in, and the darkness does not allow him to take a single step toward his destination.

The Quran, in a short phrase, recounts the state of such a bewildered traveler:

"Or like a rainstorm from the sky, in which is darkness, thunder, and lightning (falling upon wayfarers)" (أَوْ كَصَيِّبٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ فِيهِ ظُلُماتٌ وَ رَعْدٌ وَ بَرْقٌ‌).

Then It adds: "They put their fingers in their ears against the thunderclaps in dread of death" (يَجْعَلُونَ أَصابِعَهُمْ فِي آذانِهِمْ مِنَ الصَّواعِقِ حَذَرَ الْمَوْتِ‌).

And at the end of the verse, It says: "And Allah encompasses the disbelievers" (and wherever they go, they are within the grip of His power) (وَ اللَّهُ مُحِيطٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ‌).

Lightning flashes repeatedly across the dark sky: "The light of the lightning is so dazzling that it almost snatches away their sight" (يَكادُ الْبَرْقُ يَخْطَفُ أَبْصارَهُمْ‌).

"Whenever it flashes for them and the dark desert surface is lit, they walk therein; but when darkness covers them, they stand still" (كُلَّما أَضاءَ لَهُمْ مَشَوْا فِيهِ وَ إِذا أَظْلَمَ عَلَيْهِمْ قامُوا).

Every moment they feel danger before them, for in the heart of this desert, neither a mountain is seen nor a tree to prevent the danger of thunder and lightning. At any moment, they might be targeted by a thunderbolt and turned to ashes in an instant!

We know that thunderbolts strike any protrusion from the ground, and in the heart of the desert, there is no protrusion other than them for the thunderbolt to target. Therefore, the danger is serious and certain (considering that the danger of thunderbolts in flat deserts like those of Hijaz is degrees higher than in mountainous regions, the significance of this example becomes clearer to the people of that environment).

In short, he does not know what to do; anxious and distressed, bewildered and wandering, he stands in his place. Neither is a path visible among the desert sands, nor is there a guide by whose light he might walk.

There is even the danger that the roar of thunder might burst their ears and the dazzling light of lightning might blind their eyes. Indeed, "If Allah willed, He could take away their hearing and their sight; for Allah has power over all things" (وَ لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ لَذَهَبَ بِسَمْعِهِمْ وَ أَبْصارَهِمْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

Yes, the hypocrites are exactly like such a traveler. They are placed among the ever-increasing believers who move in every direction like a roaring flood and heavy rain. Alas, they have not taken refuge in the secure shelter of faith to be saved from the evil of the deadly thunderbolts of divine punishment.

The armed Jihad of the Muslims against the enemies descended upon them like the roar of thunder and lightning. Occasionally, opportunities to find the path of truth were granted to them and their thoughts were awakened, but alas, this awakening, like the lightning in the sky, did not last long. Just as they wanted to take a few steps, it would go out, and the darkness of negligence, followed by stopping and bewilderment, would take its place.

The rapid progress of Islam, like lightning, dazzled their eyes, and the verses of the Quran that pulled back the curtain on their hidden secrets targeted them like thunderbolts. Every moment they expected a verse to be revealed that would expose other secrets and further disgrace them.

As the Quran says in Verse 64 of Surah At-Tawbah: يَحْذَرُ الْمُنافِقُونَ أَنْ تُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُورَةٌ تُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ قُلِ اسْتَهْزِؤُا إِنَّ اللَّهَ مُخْرِجٌ ما تَحْذَرُونَ‌:

"The hypocrites fear lest a Surah should be revealed against them, informing them of what is in their hearts. Say: 'Mock as you will; surely Allah will bring to light what you fear.'"

The hypocrites also feared that with their secrets becoming public, a command for war would be issued by Allah against these treacherous internal enemies, and the Muslims, who were strong and powerful at that time, would attack them. As the Quran says: لَئِنْ لَمْ يَنْتَهِ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَ الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ وَ الْمُرْجِفُونَ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ لَنُغْرِيَنَّكَ بِهِمْ ثُمَّ لا يُجاوِرُونَكَ فِيها إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مَلْعُونِينَ أَيْنَما ثُقِفُوا أُخِذُوا وَ قُتِّلُوا تَقْتِيلًا:

"If the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a disease and the alarmists in Medina do not cease, We will surely incite you against them; then they will not remain your neighbors therein except for a little while. Accursed, wherever they are found, they shall be seized and slain with a (terrible) slaughter" (Al-Ahzab, 60-61).

From these verses, it is clearly understood that the hypocrites were in a state of severe terror and bewilderment in Medina. Verses with a sharp and decisive tone were revealed one after another against them like thunder and lightning, and at any moment there was the possibility that the order for their punishment or at least their expulsion from Medina would be issued.

Although the occasion of revelation for these verses concerns the hypocrites of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله time, considering that the line of hypocrisy has existed and exists in every age and time against the line of true revolutions, it extends to the hypocrites of all ages and centuries. We see all these signs one by one and point by point in the hypocrites of our own time: their bewilderment, their terror and anxiety, and in short, their lack of refuge, their misery, their dark days, and their disgrace—exactly like the traveler whose state the Quran has depicted in the clearest way.

Regarding the difference between the first and second parables in the above verses, there are two interpretations:

First: The first verse (مَثَلُهُمْ كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي ...) refers to the hypocrites who initially entered the ranks of the true believers and truly believed, but this faith was not stable and established, and they turned to hypocrisy.

However, the second parable (أَوْ كَصَيِّبٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ ...) recounts the state of the hypocrites who were in the ranks of hypocrisy from the beginning and did not believe even for a single moment.

Second: The first parable describes the state of individuals, while the second parable visualizes the state of the environments. Therefore, in the first, It says: مَثَلُهُمْ كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي ... (Their likeness is as the likeness of one who...), and in the second parable It says: "أَوْ كَصَيِّبٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ فِيهِ ظُلُماتٌ وَ رَعْدٌ وَ بَرْقٌ‌" (Or like a heavy rain falling from the sky, in which is darkness, thunder, and lightning), referring to the terrifying and dangerous environment in which the hypocrites lived.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (٢١)
O mankind! Worship your Lord, who created you and those who were before you, so that you may be Godwary (21)
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ فِرَٰشًا وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بِنَآءً وَأَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجَ بِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ رِزْقًا لَّكُمْ ۖ فَلَا تَجْعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ أَندَادًا وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٢)
—He who made the earth a place of repose for you, and the sky a canopy, and He sends down water from the sky and with it brings forth crops for your sustenance. So do not set up equals to Allah, while you know. (22)

Commentary

Worship Such a God

In the preceding verses, Allah described the states of three groups (the God-fearing, the disbelievers, and the hypocrites). He explained that the God-fearing are encompassed by Divine guidance and the Quran is their guide, whereas the hearts of the disbelievers are sealed with the stamp of ignorance, and due to their actions, a veil of neglect has been cast over their eyes, stripping them of the faculty of discernment.

The hypocrites are those with diseased hearts, whose sickness increases as a result of their evil deeds.

However, in the verses under discussion, following this clear comparison, the path of felicity and salvation—which is joining the first group—is identified. It says: "O mankind, worship your Lord, who created you and those before you, that you may become God-fearing" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اعْبُدُوا رَبَّكُمُ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ).

Here, several points must be noted

  1. The address "O mankind" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ), which is repeated about twenty times in the Quran, is a comprehensive and universal address. It shows that the Quran is not exclusive to a specific race, tribe, clan, or class; rather, it includes everyone in this general invitation. It calls all to worship the One God and to struggle against every form of polytheism and deviation from the path of Monotheism (Tawhid).

  2. To awaken the sense of gratitude in people and to draw them toward the worship of the Lord, He begins with the most significant blessing: the blessing of creation and the origination of all human beings. This is a blessing that is a sign of God's power, His knowledge and wisdom, and His general and specific mercy. For in the creation of man—this crown of the universe—the signs of God's infinite knowledge and power and His vast blessings are fully visible.

Those who do not humble themselves before God and do not worship Him are often in this state because they do not reflect upon their own creation and that of those before them. They fail to realize that this great creation cannot be attributed to blind and deaf natural factors, and these calculated and unique blessings manifest in the human body and soul cannot be attributed to anything other than a Source of infinite knowledge and power.

Therefore, reminding them of these blessings is both a proof for the recognition of God (Ma'rifah) and a motive for gratitude and worship.

  1. The result of this worship is piety and God-fearingness (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ).

Thus, our worship and prayers add nothing to the majesty and glory of God, just as abandoning them subtracts nothing from the greatness of His station. These acts of worship are educational classes for teaching Taqwa (piety). Taqwa is that sense of responsibility and inner spontaneity that serves as the criterion for human value and the measure of one's personality.

  1. The emphasis on "those before you" (الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ) perhaps hints that if you justify the worship of idols by citing the tradition of your ancestors, God is both your Creator and the Creator of your ancestors. He is your Owner and Nourisher, as well as theirs. Therefore, the worship of idols, whether by you or by them, is nothing but deviation.

The Blessing of the Earth and the Sky

In the following verse, He points to another part of God's great blessings that can serve as a motive for gratitude. First, He speaks of the creation of the earth: "The One who made the earth a resting place for you" (الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ فِراشاً).

This steady mount that has carried you on its back, continuing its various movements in this space at a dizzying speed without causing the slightest tremor to your being, is one of His great blessings.

Its gravitational force, which allows you to move, rest, build houses and nests, and prepare gardens, farms, and various means of life, is another blessing. Have you ever thought that if there were no gravity, in the blink of an eye, all of us and all our homes and belongings would be flung into space and wander aimlessly due to the earth's rotational movement?! The expression "firash" (resting place/bed) is a beautiful term. Firash implies not only the concept of peace, tranquility, and rest but also suggests being warm, soft, and in a state of moderation.

Interestingly, the fourth leader of the world's Shiites, Imam Sajjad Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, explained this reality in his eloquent words while interpreting this very verse:

"He made it suitable for your nature and compatible with your bodies. He did not make it intensely hot so as to burn you, nor intensely cold so as to freeze you. He did not make the scent of the wind so sharp as to cause you headaches, nor so foul as to perish you. He did not make it as soft as water so that you would drown, nor so hard that you could not build your houses, buildings, and graves for your dead... Therefore, He made the earth a firash for you!" [1]

Then He addresses the blessing of the sky and says: "And the sky a canopy [built] over you" (وَ السَّماءَ بِناءً).

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 41.

The word "bina" (structure/building), in conjunction with the word "alaykum" (over you), indicates that the sky is built above your heads, naturally like a ceiling. This meaning is stated more explicitly elsewhere in the Quran: "And We made the sky a protected ceiling" (وَ جَعَلْنَا السَّماءَ سَقْفاً مَحْفُوظاً) (Al-Anbiya, 32).

Perhaps this expression might seem strange to some who are familiar with the structure of the heavens and the earth according to modern astronomy—what is this ceiling and where is it? Does this expression evoke the Ptolemaic hypothesis regarding the celestial spheres being layered like the skins of an onion? However, with the following explanation, the matter becomes perfectly clear:

The word "sama" (sky/heaven) is used in the Quran with various meanings, the common denominator of which is something located in the upward direction. One of these meanings, referred to in this verse, is the earth's atmosphere—that layer of dense air that surrounds the globe and, according to scientists, is several hundred kilometers thick.

If we reflect on the fundamental and vital role of this thick layer of air surrounding the earth from all sides, we will realize to what extent this "ceiling" is firm and effective for the protection of human beings.

This specific layer of air, which surrounds us like a crystal ceiling, while not preventing the rays of the sun—those life-giving and life-creating rays—is so strong and resistant that it is firmer than a steel dam several meters thick! If this ceiling did not exist, the earth would be constantly subjected to a barrage of scattered celestial stones, and peace would practically be stripped from the people of the world. But this dense layer of several hundred kilometers [1] burns and destroys almost all celestial stones before they fall to the earth's surface, and only a very small number can pass through and be thrown into a corner as a warning to earth-dwellers; and this small number has never been able to disrupt peace.

Among the evidences showing that one of the meanings of "sky" is this "earth's atmosphere" is a tradition narrated from our great leader Imam Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the color of the sky, where he says to Mufaddal:

"O Mufaddal, reflect on the color of the sky, which God created this way in blue, as it is the most suitable color for the human eye, and even looking at it strengthens the sight." [2]

Today, we all know that the blue color of the sky is nothing but the color of the condensed air surrounding the earth. Therefore, the meaning of "sky" in this tradition is the earth's atmosphere.

In Surah An-Nahl, verse 79, we read: "Do they not see the birds controlled in the atmosphere of the sky?" (أَ لَمْ يَرَوْا إِلَى الطَّيْرِ مُسَخَّراتٍ فِي جَوِّ السَّماءِ).

Regarding other meanings of "sky," you will read a more detailed discussion under verse 29 of this same Surah.

After that, He addresses the blessing of rain, saying: "And sent down water from the sky" (وَ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً).

[1]: In many books, the thickness of the air layer surrounding the earth is written as one hundred kilometers, but apparently, they mean where the air molecules are relatively close to each other. However, modern science has proven that even at a distance of several hundred kilometers, air molecules exist in a scattered and extremely thin form. [2]: Tawhid al-Mufaddal.

But what water? Life-giving, life-creating, the source of all prosperity, and the foundation of all material blessings.

The phrase "and sent down water from the sky" once again emphasizes the fact that "sky" here refers to the earth's atmosphere, for we know that rain comes from clouds, and clouds are formed from the condensation of vapors scattered in the atmosphere.

Imam Sajjad Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام provided an interesting explanation regarding the descent of rain from the sky in his commentary on this verse:

"God sends down rain from the sky so that it reaches all the peaks of your mountains, hills, mounds, and hollows—in short, all high and level points (so that all are quenched without exception). He made it drop by drop, gentle and continuous—sometimes as large drops and sometimes as smaller droplets—so that it may fully penetrate the earth and quench it. He did not send it as a flood to wash away and destroy your lands, trees, fields, and fruits." [1]

The Quran then points to the various types of fruits that are granted to humans through the blessing of rain and the sustenance provided: "And brought forth thereby fruits as provision for you" (فَأَخْرَجَ بِهِ مِنَ الثَّمَراتِ رِزْقاً لَكُمْ).

This Divine program, which on one hand highlights God's vast and extensive mercy upon all servants, and on the other hand expresses His power—how He creates from colorless water hundreds of thousands of colors of fruits and food grains with different properties for humans and other living creatures—is one of the most vivid proofs of His existence. Therefore, He immediately adds: "So do not set up rivals to Allah while you know" (فَلا تَجْعَلُوا لِلَّهِ أَنْداداً وَ أَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ).

Yes, you all know that these idols and fabricated partners neither created you nor provide for you, and not even the smallest of your blessings comes from them. So how can you consider them similar to God?

"Andad" is the plural of "nidd" (rhyming with zidd), meaning partner and peer. It is obvious that this similarity and partnership existed only in the imagination of the idolaters, not as a reality.

Or, in more precise terms, as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "nidd" and "nadid" refer to something that is a partner or peer to something else in terms of essence and nature. Therefore, it refers to a specific type of likeness, namely likeness in the core essence.

Key Point: Idolatry in Various Forms

It is necessary here to pay attention to the fact that setting up a partner for God is not limited solely to making stone and wooden idols, or beyond that, considering a human like Christ عليه السلام as one of three gods. Rather, it has a broad meaning that includes more hidden and subtle forms. Generally, considering anything alongside God as effective in life is a type of polytheism (shirk).

Ibn Abbas has an interesting expression here; he says: "Al-Andad is polytheism that is more hidden than the crawling of an ant on a black stone in the darkness of the night. It includes a person saying: 'By Allah and by your life, O so-and-so, and by my life!'... and saying: 'If it weren't for this dog, the thieves would have come to us last night!'... and a man saying to his companion: 'Whatever Allah wills and you will'... all of this has the scent of polytheism." [1]

In a tradition, we read that a man said this very phrase to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Whatever Allah wills and you will." The Prophet replied: "Have you made me a rival (nidd) to Allah?"

In common everyday expressions, many say: "First God, second you!" It must be accepted that such expressions are also not suitable for a person of perfect Monotheism.

In a narration regarding the interpretation of verse 106 of Surah Yusuf, "And most of them do not believe in Allah except while they associate others with Him," we read from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "(This refers to hidden polytheism), such as a person saying to another: 'If it weren't for you, I would have been destroyed' or 'my life would have gone to waste.'" [2] You will read more explanation in this regard in Volume 10, page 88, under verse 106 of Surah Yusuf onwards.

[1]: Fi Zilal al-Quran, Sayyid Qutb, Vol. 1, p. 53. [2]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 697.

Ayahs

وَإِن كُنتُمْ فِى رَيْبٍ مِّمَّا نَزَّلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا فَأْتُوا۟ بِسُورَةٍ مِّن مِّثْلِهِۦ وَٱدْعُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٢٣)
And if you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a surah like it, and invoke your helpers besides Allah, if you are truthful. (23)
فَإِن لَّمْ تَفْعَلُوا۟ وَلَن تَفْعَلُوا۟ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلنَّارَ ٱلَّتِى وَقُودُهَا ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱلْحِجَارَةُ ۖ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢٤)
But if you do not—and you will not—then beware the Fire whose fuel will be humans and stones, prepared for the faithless. (24)

Commentary

The Quran, the Eternal Miracle

Since hypocrisy and disbelief—the subjects of the previous verses—sometimes stem from a lack of understanding regarding the nature of prophethood and the miracles of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the verses under discussion address this issue. Specifically, they point to the eternal miracle, the "Quran," to eliminate any doubt or hesitation concerning the mission of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. It says:

"And if you are in doubt about what We have sent down upon Our servant, then produce a surah the like thereof (وَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فِي رَيْبٍ مِمَّا نَزَّلْنا عَلى‌ عَبْدِنا فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِنْ مِثْلِهِ‌) [1]." In this way, the Quran challenges all deniers to contend with it and produce even a single chapter like it, so that their inability to do so may serve as clear evidence of the divine origin of this revelation and the truth of the mission of the one who brought it.

Then, for further emphasis, it says: do not attempt this task alone, but rather "call your witnesses besides Allah (to help you in this task) if you are truthful in your claim that this Quran is not from God" (وَ ادْعُوا شُهَداءَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ‌).

The word "shuhada" (witnesses) here refers to the helpers and supporters who assisted them in denying the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mission. The phrase "besides Allah" (مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ‌) indicates that even if all of humanity—excluding Allah—were to join hands to produce a single surah like those of the Quran, they would never be able to do so.

The phrase "if you are truthful" (إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ‌) is, in reality, a provocation to incite them to accept this challenge. Its meaning is: if you are incapable of this, it is proof of your falsehood; therefore, to prove your truthfulness, rise up and get to work.

A "Tahaddi" (challenge) and a call to combat must be as decisive as possible, provoking the enemy to the greatest extent and, so to speak, stirring their sense of honor so that they employ all their power. Thus, after their inevitable failure and exhaustion, they will know with certainty that the phenomenon they face is not a human one, but a Divine matter.

Therefore, in the next verse, the Quran addresses this vital point with various expressions, saying: "But if you do not—and you will never be able to—then fear the Fire whose fuel is men (the faithless) and stones!" (فَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا وَ لَنْ تَفْعَلُوا فَاتَّقُوا النَّارَ الَّتِي وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ وَ الْحِجارَةُ).

"A Fire that has already been prepared for the disbelievers," and is not a matter of the distant future! (أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكافِرِينَ‌).

"Waqud" means "fuel"—that is, flammable material like wood (it does not mean "igniter" like a match or a spark produced by specific stones).

Some commentators have said that "stones" (حجارة) refers to the idols they had carved from stone, citing verse 98 of Surah Al-Anbiya as evidence: "Indeed, you and what you worship other than Allah are the fuel of Hell" (إِنَّكُمْ وَ ما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ‌).

Others say "stones" refers to sulfurous rocks, which possess greater heat than other stones.

However, some commentators believe this expression is intended to draw attention to the intensity of the heat of Hell; meaning its heat and burning power are such that stones and humans are set ablaze like firewood.

What seems most consistent with the literal meaning of the above verses is that the fire of Hell is ignited from within the humans and stones themselves. Given the fact proven today that all physical bodies contain a massive fire within them (or in other words, energies that can be converted into fire), understanding this meaning is not difficult. There is no necessity to consider that scorching fire as being identical to the ordinary fires of this world.

In Surah Al-Humazah, verses 6 and 7, we read: "The Fire of Allah, kindled, which leaps up over the hearts" (نارُ اللَّهِ الْمُوقَدَةُ الَّتِي تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى الْأَفْئِدَةِ). This is a fire that originates from within the hearts and casts its shadow over them, spreading from the inside out (unlike the fires of this world, which reach the inside from the outside)!

[1]: Some commentators believe the pronoun in "mithlihi" (the like thereof) refers to the Prophet, who was mentioned in the previous phrase as "Our servant" (abdina). That is: if you doubt the authenticity of this divine revelation, find someone like Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله—who never studied and never learned to read or write—who can produce the like of it. However, this possibility seems unlikely because elsewhere in the Quran we read: "Then let them produce a discourse like it" (فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ - Tur: 34) and "Then produce a surah like it" (فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ - Yunus: 38), which shows that "mithlihi" is an attribute of the Quran, not a description of the Prophet.

Key Points

1- Why do Prophets need miracles?

We know that the office of prophethood is the greatest position bestowed upon a group of pure individuals. While other positions and ranks usually govern the bodies of individuals, the office of prophethood governs the souls and hearts of societies. Therefore, in proportion to its great value, more false claimants and charlatans attempt to seize this office for themselves and exploit it.

In this situation, people must either accept the claim of every claimant or reject the call of everyone. If they accept everyone, it is obvious what chaos would ensue and what form the religion of God would take. If they accept no one, the result would be misguidance and backwardness.

Therefore, the same reasoning that makes the sending of prophets mandatory dictates that true prophets must carry a sign that distinguishes them from false claimants and serves as a document of their truthfulness.

Based on this principle, it is necessary for every prophet to bring a miracle that testifies to the truth of his mission. As is evident from the word "mu'jizah" (miracle), the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله must have the power to perform extraordinary acts that others are "incapable" (ajiz) of performing.

A prophet who possesses a miracle must challenge the people to "produce the like of it." He must introduce the sign of the truthfulness of his words as his miracle, so that if others can produce the same, they may do so. This is technically called "Tahaddi" (challenge).

The Quran: The Eternal Miracle of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله

Among the miracles and extraordinary acts that proceeded from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, the Quran is the supreme living document of his truthfulness.

The Quran is a book beyond human thought; no one to date has been able to produce a book like it. This book is a great heavenly miracle.

The reason the Quran was chosen as the living document of the truthfulness of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and his greatest miracle among all his miracles is that the Quran is a miracle that is "eloquent," "eternal," "universal," and "spiritual."

Previous prophets had to be present alongside their miracles and challenge opponents to produce the like of them to prove their miraculous nature. In reality, their miracles did not have a voice of their own; the words of the prophets completed them. This is also true for the other miracles of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله besides the Quran.

But the Quran is an eloquent miracle; it needs no introduction. It invites toward itself, challenges opponents, convicts them, and emerges victorious from the field of battle. Thus, centuries after the passing of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it continues its call just as it did during his lifetime. It is both a religion and a miracle; both a law and the document of the law.

Eternal and Universal Nature

The Quran has shattered the boundaries of "time and space" and stands above them. This is because the miracles of past prophets—and even the miracles of the Prophet of Islam himself other than the Quran—occurred on a specific timeline, at a specific point in space, and before a specific group of people. The speech of the infant Maryam عليه السلام, the reviving of the dead, and the like by Masih عليه السلام occurred at a specific time and place and before specific individuals. As we know, matters that take on the color of time and space become fainter the further we move away from them; this is a property of temporal matters.

But the Quran is not dependent on time and space. It manifests to us today in the same form it manifested 1400 years ago in the dark environment of the Hijaz. In fact, the passage of time and the progress of science and knowledge have provided us with possibilities to benefit from it even more than the people of past ages. It is evident that whatever does not take on the color of time and space will advance forever and throughout the world. It is obvious that a universal and eternal religion must have a universal and eternal document of truthfulness at its disposal.

As for its spiritual nature:

The extraordinary matters seen from previous prophets as evidence of the truth of their words usually had a physical aspect: healing incurable diseases, reviving the dead, an infant speaking in the cradle, etc. All these have a physical dimension and captivate the human eye and ear. However, the words of the Quran—composed of these same ordinary letters and words—penetrate the depths of the human heart and soul, filling the spirit with wonder and admiration, and forcing thoughts and intellects to bow before it. It is a miracle that deals only with the brains, thoughts, and spirits of humans. The superiority of such a miracle over physical miracles requires no explanation.

Has the Quran challenged others to a confrontation?

The Quran has challenged others to produce the like of it in several surahs, including:

1- In Surah Al-Isra, verse 88—this surah was revealed in Makkah—we read: "Say: If the whole of mankind and Jinns were to gather together to produce the like of this Quran, they could not produce the like thereof, even if they backed up each other with help and support." (قُلْ لَئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنْسُ وَ الْجِنُّ عَلى‌ أَنْ يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هذَا الْقُرْآنِ لا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَ لَوْ كانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيراً).

2- In Surah Hud—this surah was also revealed in Makkah—in verses 13 and 14, we read: "Or they say, 'He forged it.' Say: 'Bring you then ten surahs forged like unto it, and call whomsoever you can, other than Allah, if you speak the truth! If then they answer not your (call), know you that this revelation is sent down with the knowledge of Allah...'" (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ وَ ادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما أُنْزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللَّهِ ...).

3- In Surah Yunus—which was also revealed in Makkah—verse 38 states: "Or do they say: 'He forged it'? Say: 'Bring then a surah like unto it, and call upon whomsoever you can besides Allah, if you are truthful!'" (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ وَ ادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ‌).

4- The verse under discussion, which was revealed in Madinah.

As observed, the Quran has challenged others with unparalleled clarity and decisiveness—a clarity and decisiveness that is a living sign of truthfulness.

With very decisive and explicit language, the Quran has invited the entire world and all those who doubted its connection to the Origin of the universe to produce the like of it. Not only has it invited them, but it has also encouraged and provoked them to fight. It has used words in these verses that, so to speak, "sting their pride." These words include:

"If you are truthful" (إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ‌). "Bring ten forged surahs like it" (فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ‌). "Say: Bring a surah like it... if you are truthful" (قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ‌ ...إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ‌). "Call whomsoever you can besides Allah" (وَ ادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ‌). "If all mankind and Jinn were to gather together..." (قُلْ لَئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنْسُ وَ الْجِنُ‌). "They cannot produce the like of it" (لا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ‌). "Fear the Fire whose fuel is men and stones" (فَاتَّقُوا النَّارَ الَّتِي وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ وَ الْحِجارَةُ). "If you do not—and you will never be able to..." (فَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا وَ لَنْ تَفْعَلُوا).

With these provocations and encouragements, and knowing that this struggle was not merely a literary or religious one, but a "political," "economic," and "social" struggle—a struggle where everything, even their "existence," was at stake; in other words, it was considered a vital struggle that determined the path and fate of their life and death. If they were victorious, they would have everything; if they were defeated, they would have to give up everything.

Despite this, when we see that they knelt before the Quran and were unable to produce the like of it, the miraculous nature of the Quran becomes even clearer.

It is noteworthy that these verses are not limited to a specific time or place; they invite the entire world and the scientific centers of the globe to this challenge, and there is no exception. Even now, it continues its challenge.

How do we know they haven't produced the like of it?!

A look at Islamic history clarifies the answer to this question. Within Islamic lands during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and after him, even in Makkah and Madinah themselves, there lived stubborn and fanatical Christians and Jews who used every opportunity to weaken the Muslims. Furthermore, among the Muslims lived a group of "pseudo-Muslims" whom the Holy Quran named "hypocrites," who were tasked with spying for foreigners (such as what is narrated in history regarding "Abu 'Amir" the monk and his hypocrite associates in Madinah and their connection with the Roman Emperor, which led to the building of "Masjid al-Dirar" in Madinah and created that strange scene mentioned by the Quran in Surah At-Tawbah).

Certainly, this group of hypocrites and those fanatical and stubborn enemies who closely monitored the affairs of the Muslims and welcomed any event detrimental to them, would have published such a book as widely as possible to crush them if they had gained access to it, or at the very least, they would have strived to preserve and maintain it.

Therefore, we see that history has recorded the names of even those individuals who might have had a weak possibility of rising to challenge the Quran, including:

The name of "Abdullah ibn al-Muqaffa'" has been mentioned, claiming he wrote the book "Al-Durrat al-Yatimah" for this purpose. However, the said book is currently in our possession and has been printed several times, and there is not the slightest reference to this matter in that book. We do not know how this was attributed to him.

The name of "Mutanabbi," Ahmad ibn Husayn al-Kufi the poet, has also been mentioned in this category, claiming he asserted prophethood. However, many indications show that his claim was more about the ambition resulting from family deprivations and a sense of power-seeking.

"Abu al-'Ala al-Ma'arri" has also been accused of this. Although biting remarks about Islam have been narrated from him, he never claimed to challenge the Quran; rather, he spoke interesting sentences about the greatness of the Quran, some of which will be mentioned.

But "Musaylimah the Liar" from the people of Yamamah was certainly among those who rose to challenge the Quran and produced so-called verses that are more of a source of amusement. It is not bad to mention a few sentences of them here:

1- In opposition to Surah Adh-Dhariyat, he produced these sentences: "By the sowers sowing, and the harvesters harvesting, and the winnowers winnowing the wheat, and the grinders grinding, and the kneaders kneading, and the bakers baking, and the soppers sopping! And those who take large, oily morsels!!" [1]

2- "O frog, daughter of a frog! Croak what you wish! Half of you is in the water and half of you is in the mud. You do not muddy the water, nor do you prevent the drinker!" [1]

The Testimony of Others Regarding the Quran

Here, we deem it necessary to quote a few sentences from the words of great figures—and even those accused of challenging the Quran—regarding the greatness of the Quran:

1- Abu al-'Ala al-Ma'arri (accused of challenging the Quran) says: "It is agreed upon by all people—Muslims and non-Muslims alike—that the book brought by Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله has overwhelmed intellects, and to date, no one has been able to produce the like of it. The style of this book resembles none of the styles common among the Arabs, whether or notatory, rajaz poetry, standard poetry, or the rhymed prose of soothsayers. The distinction and attraction of this book are such that if a single verse of it is placed among the words of others, it shines like a radiant star in a dark night!"

2- Walid ibn al-Mughirah al-Makhzumi—a man famous among the Arabs for his sound judgment, whose thought and strategy were used to solve social problems during the Age of Ignorance, and for this reason, he was called the "Fragrance of the Quraysh" (their cream of the crop!). After hearing a few verses from the beginning of Surah Ghafir from the Prophet, he appeared in a gathering of the Banu Makhzumi tribe and said: "By God, I have heard from Muhammad a speech that resembles neither the speech of humans nor that of Jinn. It has a sweetness and a grace; its top is fruitful and its bottom is overflowing. It is a speech that surpasses everything and nothing shall surpass it." [1]

3- Carlyle—the famous English historian and scholar—says about the Quran: "If we look at this holy book once, prominent truths and the characteristics of the secrets of existence are nurtured in its essential contents in such a way that the greatness and truth of the Quran are clearly visible from them. This itself is a great merit exclusive to the Quran and is not seen in any other scientific, political, or economic book. Yes, reading some books leaves deep impressions on the human mind, but it is never comparable to the impact of the Quran. For this reason, it must be said: the primary merits of the Quran and its fundamental pillars relate to the truth, pure feelings, and the prominent titles of its issues and important contents, in which no doubt has found a way, and which contain and clearly show the end of all virtues that create human perfection and happiness." [2]

4- "John Davenport," author of the book "An Apology for Mohammed and the Koran," writes: "The Quran is so free and pure from defects that it does not need the slightest correction or reform, and it may be read from beginning to end without a person feeling the least bit of boredom." [1] And he also writes: "And everyone accepts this meaning: that the Quran was revealed in the most eloquent and fluent tongue and in the dialect of the Quraysh tribe, who are the noblest and most polite of the Arabs... and it is full of the most brilliant forms and the firmest metaphors..." [2]

5- "Goethe," the German poet and scholar, says: "The Quran is a work that (occasionally) due to the weight of its expression, the reader is initially repelled, then becomes enchanted by its attraction, and finally is involuntarily captivated by its numerous beauties." [3] And elsewhere he writes: "For many years, the godless priests kept us away from discovering the truths of the Holy Quran and the greatness of its bringer, Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله. However, the more we step onto the path of science and knowledge, the veils of ignorance and misplaced prejudice disappear, and soon this indescribable book (the Quran) will attract the world to itself and have a deep impact on the science and knowledge of the world, eventually becoming the axis of the thoughts of the people of the world!" He also says: "We were initially turned away from the Quran, but it was not long before this book attracted our attention and left us in wonder, to the point that we bowed in submission before its great scientific principles and laws!"

6- "Will Durant," the famous historian, says: "The Quran has created such self-respect, justice, and piety in Muslims that it has had no peer or parallel in any region of the world..."

7- "Jules La Beaume," the French thinker and writer, says in the book "Le Koran Analysé": "Knowledge and science were obtained for the world by the Muslims, and the Muslims took the sciences from the 'Quran,' which is an ocean of knowledge, and from it, they made rivers flow for humanity in the world..."

8- "Davenport," another orientalist, writes: "It is mandatory to confess that the natural and astronomical sciences, philosophy, and mathematics that became prevalent in Europe are generally from the blessings of Quranic teachings, and we are indebted to the Muslims; rather, Europe is, in this respect, a city of Islam!" [1]

9- Dr. "Laura Veccia Vaglieri," professor at the University of Naples, writes in the book "Apologia dell'Islamismo": "The heavenly book of Islam is an example of a miracle... The 'Quran' is a book that cannot be imitated; the example of the style and manner of the Quran has no precedent in literature. The impact this style creates in the human soul stems from its distinctions and superiorities... How is it possible for 'this miraculous book' to be the work of 'Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله' when he was an unlettered Arab... In this book, we see treasures and stores of sciences that are beyond the talent and capacity of the most intelligent persons, the greatest philosophers, and the strongest men of politics and law. For these reasons, the Quran cannot be the work of an educated and scholarly man." [2]

[^2]: Apologia dell'Islamismo—in the above discussions regarding the miracle of the Quran, the book "The Quran and the Last Prophet" has been utilized.

Ayah

وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ كُلَّمَا رُزِقُوا۟ مِنْهَا مِن ثَمَرَةٍ رِّزْقًا ۙ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى رُزِقْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَأُتُوا۟ بِهِۦ مُتَشَـٰبِهًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِيهَآ أَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ ۖ وَهُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٥)
And give good news to those who have faith and do righteous deeds, that for them shall be gardens with streams running in them: whenever they are provided with their fruit for nourishment, they will say, ‘This is what we were provided before,’ and they were given something resembling it. There will be chaste mates for them, and they will remain therein [forever]. (25)

Commentary

Characteristics of Heavenly Bounties

Since the final verse of the previous discussion threatened the disbelievers and deniers of the Quran with a painful punishment, the verse under discussion describes the destiny of the believers so that—as is the method of the Quran—by contrasting the two, the truth becomes clearer.

First, It says: "And give glad tidings to those who believe and do righteous deeds that for them are gardens [in Paradise] beneath which rivers flow" (وَبَشِّرِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ).

We know that gardens which do not have a permanent water source and must occasionally have water brought to them from outside will not possess great freshness. True freshness belongs to a garden that always has water at its disposal—waters that belong to it, never cease, and are never threatened by drought or water shortage. Such are the gardens of Paradise.

Then, while referring to the various fruits of these gardens, It says: "Every time they are provided with a fruit therefrom as provision, they say: 'This is what we were provided with before'" (كُلَّمَا رُزِقُوا مِنْهَا مِنْ ثَمَرَةٍ رِزْقاً قَالُوا هَذَا الَّذِي رُزِقْنَا مِنْ قَبْلُ).

Commentators have mentioned various interpretations for this sentence:

Some have said: The meaning is that these blessings are due to the deeds we previously performed in the world, and the groundwork for them was laid beforehand.

Others have said: When heavenly fruits are brought to them for the second time, they say this is the same fruit we ate before, but when they eat it, they find it has a new taste and a fresh pleasure! In other words, for example, the apples and grapes we eat in this world taste the same every time, but the fruits of Paradise—even if they appear to be of the same kind—possess a new flavor every time. This is one of the distinctions of that world, where it is as if there is no repetition!

Others have said: The meaning is that when they see the heavenly fruits, they find them similar to the fruits of the world so that they do not feel unfamiliar; however, when they eat them, they perceive a completely fresh and superior taste.

There is no obstacle to the above sentence referring to all these concepts and interpretations, as the words of the Quran sometimes possess multiple meanings [1].

[1]: In the discussion of "the use of a word in more than one meaning," we have proven that this matter presents no problem whatsoever.

Then It adds: "and they are given things in resemblance" (وَأُتُوا بِهِ مُتَشَابِهاً).

That is, they are all similar in terms of goodness and beauty; they are at such a supreme level that one cannot be preferred over another. This is contrary to the fruits of this world, where some may be unripe, some overripe, some lacking color and scent, and some fragrant. But the fruits of the gardens of Paradise are each more fragrant than the other, each sweeter than the other, and each more attractive and beautiful than the other! Finally, the last heavenly blessing mentioned in this verse is pure and chaste spouses; It says: "and they will have therein purified spouses" (وَلَهُمْ فِيهَا أَزْوَاجٌ مُطَهَّرَةٌ).

Purified from all the impurities they might have had in this world; pure in terms of soul and heart, and pure in terms of body and physical form.

One of the drawbacks of worldly blessings is that while a person possesses a blessing, he thinks about its cessation and his mind becomes troubled. For this reason, these blessings can never create true peace. However, because heavenly blessings are eternal and there is no end or decline for them, they are perfect and tranquilizing in every respect. Therefore, at the end of the verse, It says: "and they will abide therein forever" (وَهُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ).

Key Points

1- "Faith" and "Action"

In many verses of the Quran, faith and righteous deeds are placed side by side in a way that shows these two are inseparable. Indeed, this is the case because faith and action complement one another.

If faith penetrates the depths of the soul, its radiance will certainly shine through a person's actions, making his deeds righteous. Just like a bright lamp lit inside a room, its rays shine out through all the windows and openings; such is the brilliant lamp of faith lit in a person's heart—its radiance becomes manifest through his eyes, ears, tongue, hands, and feet! In Surah At-Talaq, verse 11, we read: وَمَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِاللَّهِ وَيَعْمَلْ صَالِحاً يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهَارُ خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا أَبَداً: "And whoever believes in Allah and does righteous deeds, He will admit him into gardens beneath which rivers flow, to abide therein forever."

And in Surah An-Nur, verse 55, we read: وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ: "Allah has promised those who have believed among you and done righteous deeds that He will surely grant them succession [to authority] upon the earth"! Fundamentally, faith is like the root and righteous action is its fruit; the existence of sweet fruit is proof of the health of the root, and the existence of a healthy root is the cause for nurturing beneficial fruits.

Faithless individuals may occasionally perform a righteous deed, but it will certainly not be permanent. What guarantees righteous action is faith that has taken root in the depths of a person's being, through which he feels responsibility.

2- Purified Spouses

It is interesting that the only description given for heavenly spouses in this verse is the term "Mutahharah" (purified). This is an indication that the first and most important condition for a spouse is purity and cleanliness, and everything else is overshadowed by it. A famous Hadith narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله also clarifies this truth:

"Beware of the greenery of the dung-heap!" It was asked: "O Messenger of Allah! What is the greenery of the dung-heap?" He replied: "A beautiful woman who has been raised in a corrupt family!" [1]

3- Material and Spiritual Blessings in Paradise

Although many verses of the Quran speak of the material blessings of Paradise: gardens with flowing rivers beneath the trees, palaces, purified spouses, colorful fruits, like-minded companions, and the like.

However, alongside these blessings, mention is also made of even more important spiritual blessings, the greatness of which cannot be measured by our standards. For example, in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 72, we read:

وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِنَاتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهَارُ خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا وَمَسَاكِنَ طَيِّبَةً فِي جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ وَرِضْوَانٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ ذَلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ: "Allah has promised the believing men and believing women gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they abide eternally, and pleasant dwellings in gardens of perpetual residence; but approval from Allah is greater. It is that which is the great attainment."

And in Surah Al-Bayyinah, verse 8, after mentioning the material blessings of Paradise, we read:

رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا عَنْهُ: "Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him."

[1]: Wasail al-Shia, Vol. 14, p. 19.

Truly, if someone reaches that station where he feels God is pleased with him and he is pleased with God, he will forget all other pleasures. He will attach his heart only to Him and think of none but Him. This is a spiritual pleasure that cannot be described by any language or expression.

In short, since the Resurrection has both a "spiritual" and a "physical" aspect, the heavenly blessings also possess both aspects, so that their comprehensiveness is achieved, and everyone can benefit from them according to their capacity and merit.

Ayah

۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَسْتَحْىِۦٓ أَن يَضْرِبَ مَثَلًا مَّا بَعُوضَةً فَمَا فَوْقَهَا ۚ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَيَقُولُونَ مَاذَآ أَرَادَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَـٰذَا مَثَلًا ۘ يُضِلُّ بِهِۦ كَثِيرًا وَيَهْدِى بِهِۦ كَثِيرًا ۚ وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِۦٓ إِلَّا ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٢٦)
Indeed Allah is not ashamed to draw a parable whether it is that of a gnat or something above it. As for those who have faith, they know it is the truth from their Lord; and as for the faithless, they say, ‘What did Allah mean by this parable?’ Thereby He leads many astray, and thereby He guides many; and He leads no one astray thereby except the transgressors (26)

Circumstances of Revelation

A group of commentators, quoting Ibn Abbas regarding the revelation of the first verse above, have narrated: When Allah presented two parables for the hypocrites in the preceding verses (مَثَلُهُمْ كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي اسْتَوْقَدَ ناراً ... and أَوْ كَصَيِّبٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ ...), the hypocrites said, "Allah is far above and more exalted than to strike such parables," and through this, they expressed doubt regarding the Quran being Divine revelation. At this point, the above verse was revealed to answer them.

Others have said: When parables regarding the "fly" (ذباب) and the "spider" (عنكبوت) were revealed in the verses of the Quran, a group of polytheists used this as an excuse to open their tongues in criticism and mockery, asking, "What kind of heavenly revelation is this that speaks of spiders and flies?" The above verse was revealed and answered them with vivid expressions.

Commentary

Does Allah also strike parables?!

The first verse says: "Indeed, Allah is not ashamed to strike a parable of a gnat or even something beyond it" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَسْتَحْيِي أَنْ يَضْرِبَ مَثَلًا ما بَعُوضَةً فَما فَوْقَها).

This is because a parable must be consistent with the intended purpose; in other words, a parable is a means to visualize a reality. Sometimes, when the speaker is in a position to belittle and demonstrate the weakness of claimants, the eloquence of speech requires that He choose a weak creature as a parable to show their frailty.

For example, in verse 73 of Surah Al-Hajj, we read: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ لَنْ يَخْلُقُوا ذُباباً وَ لَوِ اجْتَمَعُوا لَهُ وَ إِنْ يَسْلُبْهُمُ الذُّبابُ شَيْئاً لا يَسْتَنْقِذُوهُ مِنْهُ ضَعُفَ الطَّالِبُ وَ الْمَطْلُوبُ: "Indeed, those you invoke besides Allah will never create a fly, even if they gathered together for that purpose. And if the fly should steal anything from them, they could not recover it from him. Weak are the seeker and the sought."

You see that here, no parable is better than a fly or the like to visualize their weakness and inability.

Similarly, in Surah Al-Ankabut, verse 41, when He wishes to visualize the inability of the idolaters in the supports they have chosen for themselves, He likens them to a spider that has chosen for itself that flimsy web, for the flimsiest of houses in the world is the house of the spider (مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ أَوْلِياءَ كَمَثَلِ الْعَنْكَبُوتِ اتَّخَذَتْ بَيْتاً وَ إِنْ أَوهَنَ الْبُيُوتِ لَبَيْتُ الْعَنْكَبُوتِ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ).

Certainly, if in such cases, instead of these small parables, great parables of the creation of stars and the vast heavens were placed, it would be highly inappropriate and would never be consistent with the principles of eloquence and rhetoric.

This is where Allah says: We do not refrain from striking a parable of a gnat or something beyond it, so that We may pour intellectual realities into the garment of sensory parables and place them at the disposal of the servants.

In short, the goal is to convey the message; the parables must also be a cloak perfectly tailored to the stature of the subject matter.

Regarding what is meant by "فَما فَوْقَها" (a gnat or what is beyond it), commentators have offered two interpretations:

One group has said it means "beyond it in smallness," because the context is one of expressing the smallness of the parable, and the "superiority" is from this perspective. This is exactly like when we say to someone, "Why do you work so hard for one Toman? Are you not ashamed?" and he replies, "There is no shame; I work for even beyond that—even for one Rial!" Others have said it means "beyond it in terms of greatness," meaning Allah presents both small parables and large parables, exactly according to the requirements of the situation.

However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

Then, following this statement, He says: "As for those who have believed, they know that it is the truth from their Lord" (فأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ).

In the light of faith and piety, they are far from stubbornness, obstinacy, and malice toward the truth. They can see the face of truth clearly and understand the logic of Allah's parables.

"But as for those who disbelieve, they say, 'What did Allah intend by this as a parable?'—whereby He misleads many and guides many" (وَ أَمَّا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَيَقُولُونَ ما ذا أَرادَ اللَّهُ بِهذا مَثَلًا يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيراً وَ يَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيراً).

This itself is their argument that these parables are not from Allah, for if they were from Him, everyone would accept them!! But Allah answers them with a short and decisive reply: "He misleads thereby only the defiantly disobedient (the transgressors) who are enemies of the truth" (وَ ما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ).

Therefore, all these words are the words of Allah and are light and guidance; it requires a seeing eye to benefit from them. If these blind-hearted people rise in opposition and stubbornness, it is due to their own deficiency and lack; otherwise, there is no defect in these Divine verses. [^1]

Key Points

1- The Importance of Parables in Expressing Realities

Appropriate parables play an exceptionally sensitive and undeniable role in clarifying realities and making various subjects appealing:

Sometimes, mentioning an appropriate parable makes the path so short and provides such a "shortcut" that it removes the burden of many philosophical arguments from the shoulders of the speaker and the listener.

More importantly: for the generalization and expansion of complex scientific subjects to the public level, there is no way other than using appropriate parables.

The role of parables in silencing stubborn and excuse-seeking individuals also cannot be denied.

In any case, likening the "intellectual" to the "sensory" is one of the effective ways of explaining rational issues.

(Of course, as we said, the parable must be appropriate; otherwise, it will be misleading and equally dangerous and distancing from the destination).

For these reasons, we encounter many parables in the Quran, each more interesting, sweet, and effective than the other, because the Quran is a book for all humans at every level and every stage of thought and knowledge—a book of ultimate eloquence and rhetoric. [^1]

2- Why a Parable of a Gnat?

Although the excuse-seekers used the smallness and insignificance of the gnat or fly as a means of mockery and objection to the verses of the Quran, if they had a bit of fairness, understanding, and intellect, and had they reflected on the structure of this very small animal, they would have realized that a world of precision and delicacy has been employed in its structure, such that the intellect remains bewildered by it.

[^1]: A group of commentators have considered the possibility that the phrase "يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيراً ..." is the word of Allah, not the speech of the disbelievers. In this case, the meaning would be that Allah, in response to those who asked what the purpose of these parables was, says: The purpose is to guide a large group and mislead another group, but none are misled except the defiantly disobedient (however, the first interpretation seems more correct).

[^1]: Regarding the role of parables in human life, a detailed discussion appears under verse 17 of Surah Ar-Ra'd (refer to Volume 10 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 172).

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says regarding the creation of this small animal: "Allah has struck a parable with a gnat despite its very small body, yet in terms of structure, it possesses the same organs that the largest (land) animals, namely the elephant, possess, and in addition, two other members (antennae and wings) are in the gnat which the elephant lacks." Allah wants to explain the delicacy of creation to the believers through this parable; reflecting on this seemingly weak creature that Allah created similar to an elephant makes man aware of the greatness of the Creator.

Specifically, its proboscis is hollow like the trunk of an elephant and absorbs blood with a special force; this tube is among the finest syringes in the world, and the hole inside it is exceptionally narrow.

Allah has given it the power of absorption, expulsion, and digestion, as well as appropriate limbs and ears, and has granted it wings to fly in search of food. These wings move up and down so rapidly that their movement is not visible to the eye. This insect is so sensitive that as soon as something moves, it senses danger and quickly distances itself from the danger zone. The wonder is that despite its weakness, it frustrates the largest of animals.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, has a remarkable statement in Nahj al-Balagha in this regard:

"If all the living beings of the world... were to gather and join hands, they would never have the power to create a gnat; rather, their intellects would be bewildered in finding a way to the secrets of the creation of this animal, and their powers would become weak and exhausted and come to an end. Finally, after effort, they would be defeated and confess that they are helpless before the creation of a gnat and acknowledge their inability, and even their inability to destroy it." [^1]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 186.

3- Divine Guidance and Misguidance

The apparent expression of the above verse might create the illusion that guidance and misguidance are compulsory and depend solely on Allah's will, whereas the final phrase of this verse reveals the truth and considers the source of guidance and misguidance to be the actions of man himself.

To explain: the actions and deeds of man always have specific results, fruits, and reflections. Among them is that if the deed is good, its result is insight, success (tawfiq), and further guidance toward Allah and the performance of better deeds.

The witness to this statement is verse 29 of Surah Al-Anfal, which says: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ تَتَّقُوا اللَّهَ يَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ فُرْقاناً: "...If you fear Allah, He will grant you a criterion [to judge between right and wrong] and bestow insight upon you."

And if he follows evils, the darkness and gloom of his heart increase, and he is driven toward more sin, sometimes reaching the point of denying Allah. The witness to this statement is verse 10 of Surah Ar-Rum, which says: ثُمَّ كانَ عاقِبَةَ الَّذِينَ أَساؤُا السُّواى أَنْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِ اللَّهِ وَ كانُوا بِها يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ: "Then the end of those who did evil was that they denied the signs of Allah and used to mock them!" And in another verse we read: فَلَمَّا زاغُوا أَزاغَ اللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ: "So when they deviated, Allah caused their hearts to deviate" (Surah As-Saff, verse 5).

In the verse under discussion, the witness to this statement also appears where He says: وَ ما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ: "And He misleads thereby only the defiantly disobedient."

Therefore, the choice of the good or bad path is initially in our own hands—a reality that the conscience of every human accepts—then one must expect its inevitable consequences.

In short: guidance and misguidance in the Quran do not mean compulsion in choosing the right or wrong path; rather, by the testimony of numerous verses from the Quran itself, "guidance" means providing the means of felicity and "misguidance" means removing the favorable grounds, without taking on a compulsory aspect.

And this providing of means (which we call tawfiq) or the disruption of means (which we call the withdrawal of tawfiq) is the result of the actions of the humans themselves which entail these matters. So if Allah gives the success of guidance to some or withdraws success from others, it is the direct result of their own actions.

This reality can be identified through a simple example: when a person passes by a precipice or a dangerous river, the closer he brings himself to it, the more slippery his foothold becomes, the probability of his fall increases, and the probability of his salvation decreases. And the further he distances himself from it, the firmer and more secure his foothold becomes, and the probability of his fall decreases. This one is called guidance and the other is called misguidance.

From the sum of this discussion, the answer to those who have criticized the verses of guidance and misguidance becomes clearly evident.

4- By "Fasiqin" (the defiantly disobedient) is meant those who have stepped out of the path and custom of servitude and worship, because "fisq" in its linguistic root means the seed emerging from within a date. Then this meaning was expanded and applied to those who step out of the path of servitude to Allah.

Ayah

ٱلَّذِينَ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَ ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مِيثَـٰقِهِۦ وَيَقْطَعُونَ مَآ أَمَرَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦٓ أَن يُوصَلَ وَيُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (٢٧)
—those who break the covenant made with Allah after having pledged it solemnly, and sever what Allah has commanded to be joined, and cause corruption on the earth—it is they who are the losers. (27)

Commentary

The Real Losers

Since the final verse of the previous section spoke of the misguidance of the defiant (the fasiqun), this verse clearly identifies and introduces them by mentioning three of their characteristics:

1- "The defiant are those who break the covenant of Allah after it has been ratified" (الَّذِينَ يَنْقُضُونَ عَهْدَ اللَّهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ مِيثاقِهِ).

Human beings have, in fact, entered into various covenants with Allah: the covenant of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the recognition of God, and the covenant of not following Satan and carnal desires. The defiant break all these covenants, turn away from the command of Truth, and follow the whims of the heart and Satan.

Where and how was this covenant made? Here, a question arises: a covenant is a bilateral matter, yet we do not recall ever making a covenant with our Lord in the past regarding these matters.

However, by considering one point, the answer to this question becomes clear: Allah has placed within the depths of the human soul and the inner nature (fitrah) a specific consciousness and unique powers through which, by following their guidance, one can find the right path, avoid following Satan and carnal desires, respond positively to the call of divine leaders, and harmonize oneself with them.

The Quran refers to this specific primordial nature as the "Covenant of Allah" and the "Divine Pledge." In reality, this is a "creational covenant" (takwini) rather than a legislative or legal one. The Quran says:

أَ لَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَ أَنِ اعْبُدُونِي هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ "Did I not take a covenant from you, O children of Adam, that you should not worship Satan—for he is your sworn enemy—and that you should worship Me? This is the straight path." [^1] It is evident that this verse refers to that primordial nature of Monotheism, the recognition of God, and the innate love for traversing the path of perfection.

Another piece of evidence for this is a statement we read in the first sermon of Nahj al-Balagha from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام:

“So He sent among them His messengers and dispatched His prophets to them in succession, to ask them to fulfill the pledge of His creation (the covenant of their nature).”

To put it more clearly, every blessing Allah bestows upon man is accompanied by a practical covenant taken through the language of creation. He gives him eyes, meaning: "See the truths with these eyes"; He gives him ears, meaning: "Hear the voice of Truth"... and thus, whenever man fails to utilize what is within his primordial nature or uses God-given powers in the path of error, he has broken the covenant of Allah.

Yes, the defiant trample upon all or part of these primordial divine covenants.

2- Then, referring to their second sign, it says: "And they sever what Allah has ordered to be joined" (وَ يَقْطَعُونَ ما أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَنْ يُوصَلَ).

Although many commentators have considered this verse to refer specifically to the severing of kinship ties (qat' al-rahim), a careful examination of the verse's concept shows that it has a broader and more general meaning, of which the issue of "severing kinship ties" is but one instance.

This is because the verse says: the defiant sever the bonds that Allah has commanded to remain established. These bonds include kinship ties, bonds of friendship, social bonds, the bond and connection with divine leaders, and the bond and relationship with Allah. Thus, the meaning of the verse should not be restricted solely to severing kinship ties and disregarding family relations.

Therefore, some commentators have interpreted it as severing the relationship with the Prophets and the believers, or severing the relationship with other Prophets and their Heavenly Books, all of which Allah has commanded to be connected with. It is clear that these interpretations also express part of the verse's general concept.

In some narrations, the phrase "ما أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَنْ يُوصَلَ" (what Allah has ordered to be joined) has been interpreted as the relationship with the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام. [^1]

3- Another sign of the defiant is spreading corruption on earth, which is mentioned in the final stage: "And they spread corruption on the earth" (وَ يُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ).

Of course, this is a self-evident matter. Those who have forgotten Allah and turned away from His obedience, and who lack mercy and compassion even toward their own kin—it is clear how they will deal with others. They pursue their own pleasures, lusts, and personal interests. It makes no difference to them where society is headed; their goal is more profit and greater indulgence. To reach this goal, they do not hesitate to commit any wrongdoing. It is obvious what kind of corruption such a way of thinking and acting creates in society.

The Holy Quran says at the end of the verse: "It is they who are the losers" (أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ).

Is this not truly the case? What loss is greater than for a person to use all his material and spiritual capital—which could create the greatest honors and happiness for him—in the path of his own destruction, non-existence, misery, and wretchedness?! Those who, according to the meaning of "defiance" (fisq), have stepped outside the sphere of Allah's obedience—what fate could they have other than this?

Key Points

1- The Importance of Maintaining Kinship Ties in Islam

Although the above verse spoke of respecting all divine bonds, the bond of kinship is undoubtedly a clear instance of it.

Islam places extraordinary importance on maintaining kinship ties (silat al-rahim) and providing help, support, and affection to relatives, and it strictly forbids severing kinship ties and cutting off relations with family and kin.

The importance of maintaining kinship ties is such that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله says: "Maintaining kinship ties prospers the lands and increases lifespans, even if the people doing so are not among the righteous." [^1]

In the words of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Maintain your kinship ties even with a drink of water, and the best way to maintain ties is to (at least) withhold harm from them!" [^2]

The ugliness and sin of severing kinship ties are to such an extent that Imam al-Sajjad عليه السلام advised his son to avoid the company of five groups, one of which is those who have severed kinship ties: "...And beware of the company of one who has severed kinship ties, for I found him cursed in the Book of Allah." [^1]

In Surah Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, verse 22, it says: فَهَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ أَنْ تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ تُقَطِّعُوا أَرْحامَكُمْ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ اللَّهُ "...Is it then to be expected of you, if you were put in authority, that you will do mischief in the land, and sever your ties of kinship? Such are the men whom Allah has cursed..."

In short: the Quran uses severe expressions regarding those who sever kinship and break family bonds, and Islamic hadiths have also harshly condemned them.

The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was asked: "Which deed is most hated in the sight of Allah?" He replied: "Associating partners with Allah (Shirk)." They asked: "And after that?" He replied: "Severing kinship ties." [^2]

The reason Islam insists so much on maintaining and preserving kinship ties is that to reform, strengthen, advance, and bring greatness to a large society—whether economically, militarily, or in terms of spiritual and moral aspects—one must always start with its small units. With the progress and strengthening of all small units, the great society will automatically be reformed.

Islam has utilized this method in a more perfect way for the greatness of Muslims; it has commanded the reform of units that individuals are usually not reluctant to help, support, and bring greatness to.

This is because it recommends strengthening the foundation of individuals whose blood circulates in each other's veins and skin, who are members of one family. It is clear that when small kinship communities become powerful, their great society also gains greatness and becomes strong in every respect. Perhaps the hadith stating that "maintaining kinship ties leads to the prosperity of lands" refers to this very meaning.

2- Severing Instead of Joining

Interestingly, the expression used in the verse was: the defiant sever what Allah has ordered to be joined.

Here, the question arises: is it possible to sever before joining?

In response, we say: the goal of "joining" is the continuation of the relationships that Allah has placed naturally and primordially between Himself and His servants, or between the servants themselves. In other words, Allah has commanded that these primordial and natural relationships be protected and guarded, but the sinners sever them (take note).

Ayahs

كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَكُنتُمْ أَمْوَٰتًا فَأَحْيَـٰكُمْ ۖ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٢٨)
How can you be unfaithful to Allah, [seeing that] you were lifeless and He gave you life, then He will make you die and then bring you to life, and then you will be brought back to Him? (28)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَسَوَّىٰهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ ۚ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٢٩)
It is He who created for you all that is in the earth, then He turned to the heaven and fashioned it into seven heavens, and He has knowledge of all things. (29)

Commentary

The Mysterious Blessing of Life

In the two aforementioned verses, the Quran directs humanity’s attention toward the Lord and His grandeur by mentioning a series of Divine blessings and wondrous phenomena of creation. It completes the arguments previously mentioned (verses 21 and 22 of this same Surah) regarding the recognition of God.

Here, to prove the existence of God, the Quran begins from a point that leaves no room for denial for anyone: the complex issue of life.

First, It says: "How can you disbelieve in Allah when you were lifeless and He gave you life, then He will cause you to die, then He will bring you [back] to life, and then to Him you will be returned?" (كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَ كُنْتُمْ أَمْواتاً فَأَحْياكُمْ).

The Quran reminds us all that before this, you were dead like stones, wood, and inanimate objects, and the breeze of life had not blown in your direction at all.

But now you possess the blessing of life and existence; various organs and systems, senses, and perception have been given to you. Who bestowed this existence and life upon you? Did you give it to yourselves?

It is evident that every fair-minded person will admit without hesitation that this blessing is not from himself, but rather has reached him from an All-Knowing and All-Powerful Source—One who knew all the secrets of life and its complex laws and had the power to organize them. Thus, the question arises: why then do you disbelieve in the God who is the Bestower of life and existence?

Today, it has become certain for all scientists that we have nothing more complex in this world than the issue of life. Despite all the remarkable progress humanity has achieved in the fields of natural sciences, the enigma of life remains unsolved. This matter is so mysterious that the thoughts and efforts of millions of scientists have thus far remained incapable of comprehending it. It is possible that in the future, in light of persistent efforts, man may gradually become aware of the secrets of life; however, the point is: can anyone attribute such an extraordinarily precise, delicate, and secret-filled matter—which requires extraordinary knowledge and power—to mindless nature, which itself lacked life?
