This is where we say the phenomenon of life in the world of nature is the greatest evidence for proving the existence of God, about which volumes have been written. In the above verse, the Quran specifically emphasizes this very issue, which we shall pass over for now with this brief mention.

After reminding of this blessing, It mentions another clear proof, which is the issue of "death." It says: "Then He will cause you to die" (ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ).

Man sees relatives, kin, and acquaintances dying one after another, their lifeless bodies buried under the soil. Here too is a place for reflection: Who took existence away from them? If their existence were from themselves, it should be eternal. The fact that it is taken from them is proof that Another bestowed it upon them.

Yes, the Creator of life is the same as the Creator of death, as we read in verse 2 of Surah Al-Mulk: الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْمَوْتَ وَ الْحَياةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا: "He is the One who created death and life to test you [as to] which of you is best in deed."

After mentioning these two clear proofs for the existence of God and preparing the human soul for other matters, the Quran proceeds in the continuation of this discussion to mention the issue of the Resurrection and being brought back to life after death, saying: "Then He will bring you [back] to life" (ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ).

Of course, this life after death is by no means a cause for wonder, for man was in such a state previously. Considering the first proof—namely, the granting of life to an inanimate being—accepting the granting of life after the body has decomposed is not only not difficult but is easier than the first time (though "easy" and "difficult" have no meaning for a Being whose power is infinite!).

It is strange that there were, and are, groups who doubt the second life of humans, while they acknowledge the first life which originated from inanimate matter.

Interestingly, in the above verse, the Quran has opened the file of life from beginning to end before the eyes of man, and in a brief statement, has visualized the beginning and end of life, and then the issue of the Resurrection.

And at the end of this verse, It says: "Then to Him you will be returned" (ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ).

The meaning of returning to the Lord is the return toward the blessings of Allah; that is, on the Day of Judgment and the Day of Resurrection, you return to the blessings of Allah. A witness to this statement is verse 36 of Surah Al-An'am, which says: وَ الْمَوْتى‌ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللَّهُ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ: "Allah will raise the dead, then to Him they will be returned."

It is possible that the meaning of returning to the Lord is a reality more precise and subtle than this: that all beings, in the path of perfection, start from the point of non-existence—which is the "zero" point—and move toward "Infinity," which is the Pure Essence of the Lord. Therefore, with death, perfection does not cease, and once again in the Resurrection, man returns to life on a higher level, and his evolutionary journey continues.

After mentioning the blessing of life and referring to the issue of the Origin and the Resurrection, It points to another of the vast blessings of Allah, saying: "He is the One who created for you all that is on earth" (هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ لَكُمْ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً).

In this way, It specifies the existential value of humans and their mastery over all earthly creatures. From this, we realize that God created this human for a very valuable and grand purpose; He created everything for him—for what then did He create him? Yes, he is the highest being in this vast arena and more valuable than all of them.

It is not only this verse that reminds us of the high status of man; rather, we find many verses in the Quran that introduce man as the ultimate goal of the creation of all beings in the world, as stated in verse 13 of Surah Al-Jathiyah: وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الارض: "And He has subjected to you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth."

And elsewhere, we read in more detail: وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْفُلْكَ ... [1] وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْأَنْهارَ ... [2] وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ وَ النَّهارَ ... [3] سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْبَحْرَ ... [4] وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الشَّمْسَ وَ الْقَمَرَ ... [5]:

"He subjected the ships to you... He subjected the rivers to you... He subjected the night and the day to your command... He gave you mastery over the seas and oceans... He also made the sun and the moon subservient and in your service..."

(You may study more discussion on this subject in Volume 10, page 120, under verse 2 of Surah Ar-Ra'd, and also in the same volume under verses 32 and 33 of Surah Ibrahim, page 349).

Returning once again to the proofs of Monotheism, It says: "Then He turned to the heaven and fashioned them into seven heavens, and He is All-Knowing of all things" (ثُمَّ اسْتَوى‌ إِلَى السَّماءِ فَسَوَّاهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ وَ هُوَ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيمٌ).

The phrase "istawa" is derived from the root "istiwa," which linguistically means mastery, complete encompassment, and the power over creation and management. Additionally, the word "thumma" (then) in the phrase "ثُمَّ اسْتَوى‌ إِلَى السَّماءِ" does not necessarily imply a chronological delay, but can mean a delay in the mention and narration of facts one after another.

[1] and [2] and [3]: Surah Ibrahim, verses 32 and 33. [4]: Surah Al-Jathiyah, verse 12. [5]: Surah Ibrahim, verse 32.

Key Points

1- Reincarnation and the Return of Souls

The above verse is among several verses that explicitly negate the belief in reincarnation (tanasukh). Believers in reincarnation imagine that after death, man returns to this same life, except that his soul enters another body (and another embryo) and begins a new life in this world, and this process may repeat many times. This repetitive life in this world is called "reincarnation" or "the return of souls."

The above verse explicitly states: after death, there is only one life, and naturally, this life is the one in the Resurrection and Judgment. In other words, the verse says: you collectively had and have two lives and deaths: first you were dead (in the realm of inanimate objects), Allah gave you life, then He causes you to die, and once again gives you life. If reincarnation were true, the number of a person's lives and deaths would be more than two.

The same theme is seen in various other verses of the Quran, which will be mentioned in their proper places [1]. Therefore, the belief in reincarnation—which is sometimes renamed "the return of souls"—is void and baseless according to the Quran.

[1]: The subject of the "Return" (Raj'ah) cannot create a problem here, because Raj'ah is only for a specific group and is not general or universal, whereas the verse above states a general rule.

Furthermore, we have clear intellectual arguments that negate this belief and prove it to be a type of regression and backward movement in the law of evolution, which we have discussed in its proper place [1]. It is also necessary to mention that some might consider the above verse as a reference to the life of Barzakh (the intermediate realm), while the verse has no such indication. It only says: you were previously a lifeless body, Allah gave you life, He causes you to die again (referring to death at the end of this worldly life), then He gives life (referring to the life of the Hereafter), then you continue your evolutionary journey toward Him.

2- The Seven Heavens

The word "sama" (heaven/sky) linguistically means the upper side, and this is a comprehensive concept that has various instances. Thus, we see it used in the Quran in different contexts:

  1. Sometimes it refers to the "upward direction" in the area adjacent to the earth, as He says: أَ لَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ ضَرَبَ اللَّهُ مَثَلًا كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ أَصْلُها ثابِتٌ وَ فَرْعُها فِي السَّماءِ "Have you not seen how Allah sets forth a parable? A good word is like a good tree, whose root is firm and whose branches are in the sky" (Ibrahim: 24).

  2. Sometimes it refers to a region further from the earth's surface (the place of clouds), as we read: وَ نَزَّلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً مُبارَكاً: "And We sent down blessed water from the sky" (Surah Qaf, verse 9).

  3. Sometimes it refers to the "dense layer of air surrounding the earth": وَ جَعَلْنَا السَّماءَ سَقْفاً مَحْفُوظاً: "And We made the sky a protected ceiling" (Al-Anbiya: 32).

[1]: Refer to the book "The Return of Souls and Communication with Spirits."

This is because we know the earth's atmosphere, which sits like a ceiling above our heads, possesses such strength that it protects the planet against falling meteorites. These stones, which are constantly caught in the earth's gravitational field day and night, would constantly strike us if not for this dense layer of air. However, the existence of this layer causes the stones to ignite and turn to ash upon colliding with the atmosphere.

  1. And sometimes it means the "upper celestial bodies": ثُمَّ اسْتَوى‌ إِلَى السَّماءِ وَ هي دُخانٌ: "Then He turned to the heaven while it was smoke" (and from the primary gas, He created the celestial bodies) (Fussilat: 11).

Now let us return to the main point: what is meant by the "seven heavens"? Interpreters and Islamic scholars have provided various explanations and interpretations:

  1. Some consider the seven heavens to be the "seven planets" (Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, and the Moon and Sun) which, according to ancient astronomers, were part of the planets [1].

  2. Others believe it refers to the dense layers of air surrounding the earth and the various strata placed atop one another.

  3. Others say: the number seven here is not a numerical count (a specific number), but rather a number of multiplicity, meaning a large and abundant quantity. This has significant parallels in Arabic speech and even in the Quran; for example, in verse 27 of Surah Luqman we read: وَ لَوْ أَنَّ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ شَجَرَةٍ أَقْلامٌ وَ الْبَحْرُ يَمُدُّهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ سَبْعَةُ أَبْحُرٍ ما نَفِدَتْ كَلِماتُ اللَّهِ: "And if all the trees on earth were pens and the ocean [were ink], with seven oceans added to it, the words of Allah would not be exhausted."

It is clearly evident that the word "seven" (sab'ah) in this verse does not mean the specific number seven; rather, even if thousands upon thousands of oceans became ink, the infinite knowledge of God could not be written with them.

Therefore, "seven heavens" refers to the numerous heavens and the many celestial bodies of the upper world without a specific number being intended.

  1. What seems more correct is that "seven heavens" refers to the actual meaning of seven heavens. The repetition of this phrase in various verses of the Quran indicates that the number "seven" here is not for multiplicity but refers to that specific number.

However, it is understood from the verses of the Quran that all the celestial bodies, fixed stars, and planets that we see are all part of the first heaven, and there exist six other worlds that are beyond the reach of our vision and today's scientific instruments, and together they constitute the seven heavens.

The evidence for this is that the Quran says: وَ زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِمَصابِيحَ: "And We adorned the nearest heaven with lamps [stars]" (Fussilat: 12).

Elsewhere we read: إِنَّا زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِزِينَةٍ الْكَواكِبِ: "Indeed, We have adorned the nearest heaven with the adornment of stars" (As-Saffat: 6).

From these verses, it is well understood that all that we see and which constitutes the world of stars is part of the first heaven, and beyond it lie six other heavens of which we currently have no precise information regarding their details.

As for our statement that the other six heavens are unknown to us and that science may unveil them in the future, it is because the limited knowledge of man, as it progresses, discovers new wonders of creation. For instance, astronomy has now reached a point where telescopes lose their power of vision; what great observatories have discovered is a distance of one thousand million (one billion) light-years, and they themselves admit that this is only the beginning of the universe, not its end. So what is to prevent the discovery of other heavens, galaxies, and worlds in the future with the advancement of astronomy?

It is best to hear this from the words of one of the world's great observatories.

[1]: Some have also divided the bodies of the solar system—which are ten (the 9 known planets plus the planet that was between Mars and Jupiter and then disintegrated, its remains still orbiting in that same path)—into two groups: those below the earth's orbit (Mercury and Venus) and those outside and above the earth's orbit, which are seven other planets. In this way, they perhaps wish to interpret the seven heavens as the seven outer planets (note carefully).

3- The Grandeur of the Universe

The Palomar Observatory describes the grandeur of the upper world as follows:

"...Until the Palomar Observatory telescope was built, the extent of the world visible to us was no more than five hundred light-years. However, this telescope extended the extent of our world to one thousand million light-years, and as a result, millions of new galaxies were discovered, some of which are one thousand million light-years away from us. But beyond the distance of one thousand million light-years, a vast, terrifying, and dark space is seen in which nothing is visible; that is, light does not pass through there to affect the photographic plate of the observatory's telescope.

But undoubtedly, in that terrifying and dark space, there are hundreds of millions of galaxies, and the world on our side is held by gravity to those galaxies.

All this vast world that is visible and contains hundreds of thousands of millions of galaxies is but a small and insignificant valley of a much larger world, and we are not yet certain that beyond that second world there is not another world." [1]

From this statement, it is clearly evident that science, despite its amazing progress, considers its discoveries in the realm of the heavens to be the beginning of the universe, not its end; rather, it considers it a tiny particle in the face of a truly magnificent universe.

[1]: Quoted from "Faza" (Space) Magazine, Issue 56, Farvardin 1351 (April 1972).

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنِّى جَاعِلٌ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَتَجْعَلُ فِيهَا مَن يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ ٱلدِّمَآءَ وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ ۖ قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٣٠)
When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth,’ they said, ‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood, while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your sanctity?’ He said, ‘Indeed I know what you do not know.’ (30)
وَعَلَّمَ ءَادَمَ ٱلْأَسْمَآءَ كُلَّهَا ثُمَّ عَرَضَهُمْ عَلَى ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ فَقَالَ أَنۢبِـُٔونِى بِأَسْمَآءِ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٣١)
And He taught Adam the Names, all of them; then presented them to the angels and said, ‘Tell me the names of these, if you are truthful.’ (31)
قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَآ إِلَّا مَا عَلَّمْتَنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٣٢)
They said, ‘Immaculate are You! We have no knowledge except what You have taught us. Indeed You are the All-knowing, the All-wise.’ (32)
قَالَ يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ أَنۢبِئْهُم بِأَسْمَآئِهِمْ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَنۢبَأَهُم بِأَسْمَآئِهِمْ قَالَ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ غَيْبَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَأَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ (٣٣)
He said, ‘O Adam, inform them of their names,’ and when he had informed them of their names, He said, ‘Did I not tell you that I know the Unseen of the heavens and the earth, and that I know whatever you disclose and whatever you conceal?’ (33)

Commentary

Man: God's Representative on Earth

In the preceding verses, we read that Allah created all the bounties of the earth for man. In these verses, He formally explains the issue of man's leadership and vicegerency, clarifying his spiritual status which makes him worthy of all these blessings.

These verses refer to the manner of the creation of Adam (the first human). In this series of verses, which begins from verse 30 and ends at verse 39, three fundamental topics are presented:

  1. The Lord informing the angels regarding the vicegerency and stewardship of man on earth, and the dialogue they had with Allah.
  2. The command for the angels to show humility and veneration before the first human, which is mentioned in various verses of the Quran in different contexts.
  3. Explaining the state of Adam and his life in Paradise, the events that led to his departure from Paradise, and subsequently Adam's repentance and the life of him and his children on earth.

The verses under discussion speak of the first stage. Allah's will was such that He would create a being on earth who would be His representative; his attributes would be a reflection of the Lord's attributes, and his status and personality would be superior to the angels. His will was to place the entire earth and its blessings—its forces, treasures, minerals, and all its possibilities—at the disposal of such a human.

Such a being had to possess an ample share of intellect, consciousness, perception, and a special talent to be able to undertake the leadership and guidance of earthly creatures.

Therefore, the first verse says: "And [remember] when your Lord said to the angels, 'Indeed, I will make upon the earth a successive authority [Khalifa]'" (وَ إِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي جاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً).

"Khalifa" means a successor or deputy, but commentators have offered various possibilities regarding whose successor or what's successor is intended here.

Some have said it means a successor to the angels who previously lived on earth. Others have said it means a successor to other humans or other beings who lived on earth before. Some have considered it a reference to human generations succeeding one another.

However, the truth is—as many researchers have accepted—that it refers to Divine Vicegerency and representing Allah on earth. This is because the question the angels ask afterward, saying that Adam's progeny might become the source of corruption and bloodshed while "we celebrate Your praise and sanctify You," is consistent with this meaning; for representing Allah on earth is incompatible with such actions.

Furthermore, the issue of "teaching the names" to Adam, the explanation of which will come in the following verses, is another clear piece of evidence for this claim. Also, the humility and prostration of the angels before Adam bears witness to this purpose! In any case, Allah wanted to create a being who would be the "flower of the pinnacle of existence" and worthy of the status of Divine Vicegerency and being the representative of "Allah" on earth.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of these verses, this same meaning is pointed out: that after becoming aware of Adam's status, the angels realized that he and his children were more deserving of being Divine Vicegerents on earth and His proofs over creation [^1]. Then, in the verse under discussion, it adds: "The angels—as a question to understand the truth and not as an objection—said: 'Will You place upon it one who causes corruption therein and sheds blood?' (قالُوا أَ تَجْعَلُ فِيها مَنْ يُفْسِدُ فِيها وَ يَسْفِكُ الدِّماءَ) 'while we declare Your praise and sanctify You?' (وَ نَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَ نُقَدِّسُ لَكَ)."

But Allah gave them a brief answer here, the explanation of which became clear in the subsequent stages: "He said, 'Indeed, I know that which you do not know'" (قالَ إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ).

The angels, as is evident from their words, had realized that this human would not be a completely submissive individual; he would cause corruption, shed blood, and bring about destruction. But how did they know?! Sometimes it is said that Allah had previously described the future of man to them in general terms, while some have suggested that the angels themselves deduced this from the word "on the earth" (فِي الْأَرْضِ). This is because they knew man would be created from soil, and matter, due to its limitations, is naturally a center of conflict and friction. The limited material world cannot satisfy the naturally acquisitive nature of humans; even if the whole world were given to one individual, he might still not be satiated. This situation, especially when not accompanied by a sufficient sense of responsibility, leads to corruption and bloodshed.

Other commentators believe the angels' prediction was because Adam was not the first creature on earth; rather, there were other creatures before him who engaged in conflict and bloodshed. Their "prior criminal record" caused the angels' suspicion toward Adam's race! These three interpretations are not necessarily contradictory; it is possible that all these factors drew the angels' attention to this point. Incidentally, what they stated was a reality, and therefore Allah never denied it in His response. Instead, He pointed out that alongside this reality, there are more important realities regarding man and his status of which the angels were unaware! They thought that if the goal was servitude and worship, they were its perfect manifestation, constantly immersed in worship and more deserving of vicegerency than anyone! They were unaware that their worship—given that lust, anger, and various desires have no way into their being—differs greatly from the worship and servitude of this human who is surrounded by inclinations and lusts, and whom Satan tempts from every side. Where is the obedience of this storm-tossed being compared to the worship of those peaceful and light-burdened shore-dwellers?! What did they know that from the loins of this Adam, prophets like Muhammad, Abraham, Noah, Moses, and Jesus عليهم السلام, and Imams like the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, and righteous servants, devoted martyrs, and men and women who lovingly give their entire existence in the way of Allah, would step into the arena of existence? Individuals whose single hour of reflection is sometimes equal to years of the angels' worship!

It is noteworthy that the angels emphasized three things regarding their own attributes: Tasbih (glorification), Hamd (praise), and Taqdis (sanctification). Undoubtedly, Tasbih and Hamd mean considering Allah free from any defect and possessing every perfection. But as for what is meant by "Taqdis"?

Some have considered it as declaring the Lord pure from any shortcoming, which in fact becomes an emphasis on the same meaning as Tasbih.

However, others believe that "Taqdis," which comes from the root Quds, means purifying the earth from the corrupt and corrupters, or purifying oneself from all ugly and blameworthy attributes, and cleansing body and soul for Allah. They consider the word "Laka" (for You) in the phrase "we sanctify for You" (نُقَدِّسُ لَكَ) as evidence for this purpose, because the angels did not say "Nuqaddisuka" (we sanctify You), but said "Nuqaddisulaka" (we purify the society for You).

In fact, they wanted to say: if the goal is obedience and servitude, we are at Your command; if it is worship, we are always engaged in it; and if it is the purification of oneself or the face of the earth, we do so; whereas this material human is both corrupt himself and fills the face of the earth with corruption.

But in order for the truths to be clarified in detail for the angels, Allah proceeded to test them, so they themselves would confess that between them and Adam, there is "a difference as vast as the distance between the earth and the sky"!

The Angels in the Crucible of Testing

By the grace of the Lord, Adam possessed an extraordinary talent for perceiving the realities of existence. Allah brought this talent of his into fruition and, as the Quran says, "And He taught Adam the names—all of them" (وَ عَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْماءَ كُلَّها). Although commentators have various explanations for the "knowledge of the names," it is certain that the intention was not teaching words and names without meaning to Adam, as this would not be considered an honor. Rather, the intention was giving the meanings of these names, the concepts, and the things named.

Of course, this awareness of the sciences related to the world of creation and the secrets and various properties of the beings of the universe was a great honor for Adam.

We have a Hadith where Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام was asked about this verse; he said: "It refers to the lands, the mountains, the valleys, and the riverbeds (and in short, all beings)." Then the Imam عليه السلام looked at the carpet spread beneath him and said, "Even this carpet was among the things Allah taught Adam!" [^1] Therefore, the knowledge of the names was not something like "lexicography," but rather related to their philosophy, secrets, qualities, and properties. Allah taught this knowledge to Adam so he could utilize the material and spiritual bounties of this world in the path of his perfection.

He also granted him the talent for naming objects so he could name things and call them by mentioning their names when needed, so it would not be necessary to show the object itself. This is a great blessing. We realize the importance of this subject when we see that everything modern man possesses is by means of books and writing, and all the scientific treasures of the predecessors are gathered in his writings; and this is due to the naming of objects and their properties. Otherwise, it would never have been possible for the sciences of the past to be transferred to future generations.

"Then He showed them to the angels and said, 'Inform Me of the names of these, if you are truthful'" (ثُمَّ عَرَضَهُمْ عَلَى الْمَلائِكَةِ فَقالَ أَنْبِئُونِي بِأَسْماءِ هؤُلاءِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ).

But the angels, who did not possess such scientific encompassment, failed in the face of this test. Therefore, in response, "They said, 'Exalted are You; we have no knowledge except what You have taught us'" (قالُوا سُبْحانَكَ لا عِلْمَ لَنا إِلَّا ما عَلَّمْتَنا). "Indeed, it is You who is the Knowing, the Wise" (إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ).

If we asked a question in this regard, it was out of our lack of awareness; we had not studied this matter and were unaware of this talent and marvelous power of Adam, which is his great distinction over us. Truly, he is worthy of Your vicegerency, and the earth and the universe would have a deficiency without his existence.

At this point, it was Adam's turn to explain the names of the beings and their secrets in the presence of the angels. "He said, 'O Adam, inform them of their names'" (قالَ يا آدَمُ أَنْبِئُهمْ بِأَسْمائِهِمْ).

"And when he had informed them of their names, He said, 'Did I not tell you that I know the unseen [aspects] of the heavens and the earth? And I know what you reveal and what you have been concealing'" (فَلَمَّا أَنْبَأَهُمْ بِأَسْمائِهِمْ قالَ أَ لَمْ أَقُلْ لَكُمْ إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ غَيْبَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ أَعْلَمُ ما تُبْدُونَ وَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ).

Here, the angels bowed in submission before the vast information and abundant knowledge of this human, and it became clear to them that he alone is worthy of the vicegerency of the earth!

The phrase "what you have been concealing" (ما كُنْتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ) refers to the fact that the angels had something in their hearts other than what they expressed. Some have said this refers to the state of arrogance of Iblis, who was in the ranks of the angels that day and was addressed by the command given to them. He had decided within himself that he would never show humility before Adam.

However, the possibility also exists that it meant the angels truly considered themselves more worthy than anyone else for Divine Vicegerency on earth; although they made an allusion to this matter, they did not reveal it explicitly.

Answer to Two Questions [How did Allah teach these sciences to Adam?]

Two questions remain here: first, how did Allah teach these sciences to Adam?

Furthermore, if He had taught these sciences to the angels as well, they too would have acquired this same virtue of Adam; what honor is this for Adam that is not for the angels?

In response, one must note that "teaching" here had a generative (takwini) aspect; meaning Allah placed this awareness in the nature and essence of Adam and brought it to fruition in a short period.

The use of the word "teaching" in the Quran for "generative teaching" has appeared elsewhere as well; in Surah ar-Rahman, verse 4, we read: "He taught him speech" (علمه البيان). It is clear that Allah gave this teaching to man in the school of creation, and its meaning is that very innate talent and characteristic placed in the essence of humans so they can speak.

In response to the second question, it must be noted that the angels had a specific creation that did not have the capacity for learning all these sciences. They were created for another purpose, not for this one. For this reason, the angels realized and accepted the reality after this test. But perhaps they themselves initially thought they were ready for this purpose as well, but Allah clarified the difference in their talent compared to Adam through the test of the knowledge of the names.

Again, another question arises here: if the knowledge of the names means the knowledge of the secrets of creation and understanding the properties of all beings, then why are the pronoun "hum" (them) in the phrase "then He showed them" (ثم عرضهم) and "their names" (اسمائهم), and the word "ha'ula'i" (these), which are usually used for rational beings, used in this case?

In response, we say:

It is not the case that the pronoun "hum" and the word "ha'ula'i" are used exclusively for rational beings; rather, they are sometimes used for a collection of rational and non-rational beings, or even for a collection of non-rational beings. Just as Joseph عليه السلام said regarding the stars, the sun, and the moon: "I saw them prostrating to me" (رَأَيْتُهُمْ لِي ساجِدِينَ) (Surah Yusuf, verse 4).

[^1]: Quoted from the book Ma'ani al-Akhbar (as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 1, p. 121). Although this Hadith mostly highlights the greatness of the status of the Prophets and Imams, it is clear that it is not exclusive to them; they are the most complete and perfect instances of this subject.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰ وَٱسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٣٤)
And when We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis: he refused and acted arrogantly, and he was one of the faithless. (34)
وَقُلْنَا يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ ٱسْكُنْ أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَكُلَا مِنْهَا رَغَدًا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٣٥)
We said, ‘O Adam, dwell with your mate in paradise and eat thereof freely whencesoever you wish, but do not approach this tree, lest you should be among the wrongdoers.’ (35)
فَأَزَلَّهُمَا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ عَنْهَا فَأَخْرَجَهُمَا مِمَّا كَانَا فِيهِ ۖ وَقُلْنَا ٱهْبِطُوا۟ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ ۖ وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَـٰعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (٣٦)
Then Satan caused them to stumble from it, and he dislodged them from what [state] they were in; and We said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time.’ (36)

Commentary

Adam in Paradise

Following the previous discussions regarding the status and greatness of man, the Quran turns to another chapter of this discourse, first stating: "And remember when We said to the angels, 'Prostrate before Adam'" (وَ إِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ).

"They all prostrated except Iblis, who refused and was arrogant" (فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰ وَ اسْتَكْبَرَ).

Yes, he showed arrogance, "and because of this arrogance and disobedience, he became one of the disbelievers" (وَ كَانَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ).

Although it initially appears that the matter of prostrating to Adam occurred after the testing of the angels and the teaching of the Names, a careful examination of other Quranic verses shows that this event took place immediately after the creation of man and the completion of his form, and before the testing of the angels.

In Surah Al-Hijr, verse 29, we read: "So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My soul (a noble soul created by Me), then fall down to him in prostration" (فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَ نَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي فَقَعُوا لَهُ سَاجِدِينَ).

The same meaning appears in Surah Sad, verse 72 [^1]. Another piece of evidence for this is that if the command to prostrate had come after Adam's status had been clarified, it would not have been considered much of an honor for the angels, as by then, Adam's status

[^1]: Alusi in the tafsir "Ruh al-Ma'ani" and Fakhr Razi in "Tafsir al-Kabir" have also pointed to this meaning.

had become apparent to everyone.

In any case, the above verse is a living document and clear evidence of the nobility of man and the greatness of his station, such that after the completion of his creation, all angels were commanded to bow in humility before this great creation. Truly, one who is worthy of the station of Divine Vicegerency and His representation on earth, and possesses the potential for such perfection and the nurturing of high-ranking descendants such as the Prophets—especially the Prophet of Islam and his successors—is worthy of every kind of respect.

If we show such deference to a human who knows a few scientific formulas, then what of the first human with that abundant knowledge of the universe!

Key Points

1. Why Did Iblis Oppose?

We know that "Shaytan" (Satan) is a common noun encompassing the first Satan and all satans, but "Iblis" is a proper noun referring to that specific Satan who seduced Adam. According to the explicit verses of the Quran, he was not of the race of angels; rather, he stood in their ranks. He was from the race of Jinn, which is a material creation. In Surah Al-Kahf, verse 50, we read: "So they prostrated except Iblis; he was of the Jinn" (فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ كَانَ مِنَ الْجِنِّ).

His motive for this opposition was pride, vanity, and a specific prejudice that dominated his thinking. He imagined himself superior to Adam and felt that the command to prostrate to Adam should not have been given to him; rather, he should have been the one prostrated to, and Adam should have prostrated to him. The details of this will come under the commentary of verse 12 of Surah Al-A'raf [^1]. The cause of his disbelief was also this: he considered the wise command of the Lord to be incorrect.

[^1]: Refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 6, page 98 onwards.

He not only rebelled in action but also objected in belief. In this way, self-centeredness and egoism blew away the harvest of a lifetime of faith and worship, setting fire to the stack of his existence. Pride and arrogance have many such effects!

The expression "he was of the disbelievers" (كَانَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ) indicates that even before this command, he had separated his path from that of the angels and obedience to God's command, harboring thoughts of arrogance in his head. Perhaps he said to himself, "If I am commanded to show humility and prostrate, I will certainly not obey." It is possible that the phrase "what you were concealing" (مَا كُنْتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ) is an allusion to this meaning. This same meaning is mentioned in a Hadith narrated in Tafsir al-Qummi from Imam al-Askari عليه السلام [^1].

2. Was the Prostration for God or Adam?

There is no doubt that "prostration" in the sense of "worship" is for God alone, for there is no deity in the universe except God, and the meaning of Monotheism in Worship (Tawhid al-Ibadah) is that we worship none but God.

Therefore, there is no room for doubt that the angels did not perform a "prostration of worship" for Adam. Rather, the prostration was for God but on the occasion of the creation of such a wondrous being; or they prostrated to Adam, but prostration in the sense of "humility" (khudu'), not worship.

In the book "Uyun Akhbar al-Rida," we read from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام: "Their prostration was for Allah the Almighty as worship, and for Adam as honor and obedience, because we were in his loins!" [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 1, page 126. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, page 58.

In any case, after this event and the event of testing the angels, Adam was commanded that he and his spouse dwell in Paradise, as the Quran says: "And We said, 'O Adam, dwell, you and your wife, in Paradise and eat therefrom in abundance from wherever you will'" (وَ قُلْنَا يَا آدَمُ اسْكُنْ أَنْتَ وَ زَوْجُكَ الْجَنَّةَ وَ كُلَا مِنْهَا رَغَدًا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا) [^1]. "But do not approach this tree, lest you be among the wrongdoers" (وَ لَا تَقْرَبَا هَٰذِهِ الشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونَا مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ).

It is understood from the Quranic verses that Adam was created for life on earth—this very ordinary earth—but in the beginning, Allah settled him in Paradise, which was one of the lush, bountiful gardens of this world; an environment where there was no discomfort for Adam.

Perhaps the reason for this process was that Adam had no familiarity with living on earth, and enduring its hardships without preparation would have been difficult for him. He needed to gain more information about how to act and behave on earth. Therefore, he had to receive the necessary training for a short period in the environment of Paradise and know that life on earth is coupled with programs, duties, and responsibilities, the correct fulfillment of which leads to happiness, perfection, and the permanence of blessings, while turning away from them leads to pain and discomfort.

He also needed to know that although he was created free, this freedom is not absolute and unlimited such that he could do whatever he wished; he had to refrain from certain things on earth.

It was also necessary for him to know that if a mistake or slip should befall him,

[^1]: "Raghad" (رغد) means abundant, extensive, and pleasant. The phrase "wherever you will" (حيث شئتما) indicates that they could use any part of Paradise they wished, or any type of fruit they desired.

the doors of happiness would not be closed to him forever; he could return and make a covenant that he would not act against God's command, so that he might return to Divine blessings.

In this environment, he had to become somewhat seasoned, recognize his friend and foe, and learn the way of life on earth. Yes, this was a series of necessary lessons he had to learn, and with this preparation, he would step onto the earth.

These were matters that both Adam and his children would need in their future lives. Therefore, perhaps the reason why Adam, despite being created for the vicegerency of the earth, stayed in Paradise for a while and was given certain commands, was for the purpose of practice and training.

Here, Adam found himself facing the Divine command regarding refraining from the forbidden tree. However, the seductive Satan, who had sworn not to cease misleading Adam and his children, began his whispering. As understood from other Quranic verses, he assured Adam that if he ate from this tree, he and his wife would become angels and live eternally in Paradise; he even swore, "Indeed, I am to you among the sincere advisors" (Surah Al-A'raf, verses 20-21). "Finally, Satan caused them to slip and removed them from that [condition] in which they were" (فَأَزَلَّهُمَا الشَّيْطَانُ عَنْهَا فَأَخْرَجَهُمَا مِمَّا كَانَا فِيهِ) [^1].

[^1]: Regarding the referent of the pronoun "anha" (عنها), two possibilities are given: First, that it refers to "Jannah" (Paradise), in which case the phrase "from that in which they were" refers to their status and rank. Thus, the overall meaning is: Satan caused them to slip from Paradise and removed them from the status they held. The second possibility is that it refers to "shajarah" (the tree), meaning Satan caused them to slip by means of that forbidden tree and removed them from the Paradise they were in.

Yes, from the Paradise that was a center of peace and comfort, far from pain and suffering, they were expelled due to the deception of Satan.

And as the Quran says: "We said, 'Go down, [all of you], as enemies to one another'"—Adam and Eve on one side and Satan on the other (وَ قُلْنَا اهْبِطُوا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ).

"And for you on the earth is a place of settlement and enjoyment for a time" (وَ لَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَ مَتَاعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ).

It was here that Adam realized he had truly wronged himself and had been driven out of the peaceful and bountiful environment of Paradise for submitting to the whisperings of Satan, and would be placed in an environment full of toil and hardship. It is true that Adam was a Prophet and infallible (ma'sum) from sin, but as we shall explain, whenever a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla) occurs by a Prophet, Allah deals strictly with him, similar to a sin committed by ordinary people, and this was the heavy penalty Adam paid for that disobedience.

Key Points

1. Which Paradise was Adam's Paradise?

In response to this question, one must note that although some consider it the promised Paradise of the righteous, the apparent reality is that it was not that Paradise. Rather, it was one of the bountiful and soul-refreshing gardens of one of the lush regions of the earth.

Because, firstly, the promised Paradise of the Resurrection is an eternal blessing, and its eternity is mentioned in many verses of the Quran, and leaving it is impossible. Secondly, the impure and faithless Iblis would have no way into that Paradise; there are neither satanic whisperings nor disobedience to God there.

Thirdly, this matter is explicitly stated in the narrations that have reached us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام. One of the narrators says: I asked Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام about Adam's Paradise. The Imam عليه السلام replied: "It was a garden from the gardens of the world upon which the sun and moon shone, and if it were from the gardens of the Hereafter, Adam would never have been expelled from it" [^1]. From this, it becomes clear that the "descent" (hubut) of Adam to the earth was a descent in status, not location; meaning he came down from his lofty station and from that lush garden.

The possibility has also been raised that this Paradise was on one of the celestial bodies, even if it was not the eternal Paradise. Some Islamic narrations also refer to this Paradise being in the "heaven" (sama'), but it is possible that the word "sama'" in such narrations refers to a "high status" rather than a "high location."

In any case, numerous evidences show that this Paradise is different from the Paradise of the next world, for that is the end of man's journey, while this was the beginning of his journey; this was a prelude to his actions and programs, while that is the result of his actions and programs.

2. What was Adam's Sin?

It is clear that Adam, with the status Allah described for him in the previous verses, held a high rank in terms of knowledge and piety. He was God's representative on earth, he was the teacher of the angels, and he was the one to whom God's great angels prostrated. Such an Adam, with these distinctions, certainly does not commit sin. Furthermore, we know he was a Prophet, and every Prophet is infallible.

[^1]: Kitab al-Kafi (as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, page 62).

Therefore, the question arises: what was it that Adam committed?

There are three interpretations here that complement one another:

  1. What Adam committed was a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla) or, in other words, a "relative sin," not an "absolute sin." Absolute sins are those that are sins regardless of who commits them and deserve punishment (like polytheism, disbelief, injustice, and transgression). A relative sin is when certain permissible or even recommended acts are not befitting the status of great individuals; they should refrain from these acts and attend to more important matters. Otherwise, they have "left the better." For example, the prayer we perform—part of which is with presence of heart and part without—is befitting our status, but such a prayer is never befitting the status of a person like the Prophet or Ali عليه السلام. He must be entirely immersed in presence before God throughout his prayer, and if he does otherwise, he has not committed a forbidden act, but he has "left the better." It was likewise befitting for Adam not to eat from that tree, even though it was not forbidden for him but rather "disliked" (makruh).

  2. God's prohibition here was an "advisory prohibition" (nahi irshadi), similar to a doctor's instruction saying, "Do not eat such-and-such food or you will become ill." God also said to Adam, "If you eat from the forbidden tree, you will leave Paradise and fall into pain and suffering." Therefore, Adam did not oppose a [legislative] command of God, but rather opposed an advisory prohibition.

  3. Fundamentally, Paradise was not a place of religious obligation (taklif); rather, it was a period for testing and preparing Adam for coming to earth, and this prohibition was merely for the purpose of testing [^1].

[^1]: For more explanation in this regard, refer to Vol. 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 122, under verses 19 to 22 of Surah Al-A'raf, and Vol. 13, under verses 121 onwards of Surah Taha.

3. Comparison of Quranic Teachings with the Torah

According to the above verses, the greatest honor and point of strength in Adam's existence—which allowed him to be introduced as a chosen one of creation and for which reason he became the one to whom angels prostrated—was his awareness of the "Knowledge of the Names" and his information regarding the "realities and secrets of creation and the universe."

It is evident that Adam was created for the sake of these sciences, and if the children of Adam wish to reach perfection, they must benefit as much as possible from these sciences. The greater perfection of each of them is in direct proportion to their knowledge of the secrets of creation.

Yes, the Quran with total clarity considers the greatness of Adam's station to lie in these things. But in the Torah, as we read, the reason for Adam's expulsion from Paradise and his great sin is considered to be his attention to science, knowledge, and knowing good and evil!

In the second chapter of "Genesis" in the Torah, it says: "And the Lord God formed man of the dust of the ground, and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and man became a living soul... And out of the ground made the Lord God to grow every tree that is pleasant to the sight, and good for food; the tree of life also in the midst of the garden, and the tree of knowledge of good and evil... And the Lord God commanded the man, saying, Of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat: But of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it: for in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die."

And in the third chapter, it says: "And they heard the voice of the Lord God walking in the garden in the cool of the day: and Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the Lord God amongst the trees of the garden!! And the Lord God called unto Adam, and said unto him, Where art thou? And he said, I heard thy voice in the garden, and I was afraid, because I was naked; and I hid myself! And he said, Who told thee that thou wast naked? Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou shouldest not eat? And the man said, The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat!... And the Lord God said, Behold, the man is become as one of us, to know good and evil: and now, lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever: Therefore the Lord God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, to till the ground from whence he was taken!..."

As you observed, this "offensive legend" which appears in the current Torah as a historical reality, considers the primary cause of Adam's expulsion from Paradise and his great sin to be his attention to science, knowledge, and knowing good and evil.

And had Adam not stretched his hand toward the "tree of good and evil," he would have remained in ignorance forever, to the extent that he would not even know that being naked is shameful and unseemly, and he would have remained in Paradise forever.

In this way, Adam certainly should not have been regretful of his action, for losing a Paradise where the condition for staying is not knowing good and evil, in exchange for gaining science and knowledge, is considered a very profitable trade. Why should Adam be worried or regretful about this trade?

Therefore, the legend of the Torah stands in direct opposition to the Quran, which introduces the value of man's station and the secret of his creation as being in the "Knowledge of the Names."

Beyond this, in the aforementioned legend, strange and offensive matters are seen regarding God or

His creatures, each more astonishing than the other, which are:

  1. Attributing a lie to God (as in verse 17 of chapter 2, it says: God said do not eat of that tree or you will die, whereas they did not die but became knowledgeable).
  2. Attributing stinginess to God (as in verse 22 of chapter 3, it says: God did not want Adam and Eve to eat from the tree of knowledge and life and become knowledgeable and gain eternal life).
  3. The possibility of a partner for God (as in the same verse, it says: Adam, after eating from the tree of good and evil, has become "as one of us" [gods]).
  4. Attributing envy to God (as understood from the same verse, that God envied this science and knowledge that Adam had acquired!).
  5. Attributing a body to God (as understood from chapter 3, that God was walking in the streets of Paradise in the morning)!
  6. God is unaware of events happening near Him! (as in verse 9, it says He called out, "Adam, where art thou?" and they had hidden themselves from God's eyes among the trees) [^1]. (Of course, it must not be forgotten that these false legends were not in the Torah from the beginning and were added to it later).

4. What is Meant by "Shaytan" in the Quran?

The word "Shaytan" is derived from the root "shatana" (شطن), and "shatin" means "wicked and base." Shaytan is applied to any rebellious and defiant being, whether human

[^1]: The section comparing the teachings of the Quran and the Torah is adapted from the book "The Quran and the Final Prophet," pages 127-132.

or jinn or other creatures, and it has also been used to mean an evil spirit far from the truth. In reality, all of these return to a common denominator.

It should be known that "Shaytan" is a common noun (generic name), while "Iblis" is a proper noun. In other words, "Shaytan" refers to any harmful, misleading, rebellious, and defiant being, whether human or non-human, and Iblis is the name of that specific Satan who deceived Adam and is now lying in wait for humans with his army and hosts.

From the instances of this word's usage in the Quran, it also appears that Shaytan is applied to a harmful and injurious being—a being that has turned away from the right path and intends to harm others, a being that tries to create division and stir up discord and corruption, as we read: "Satan only wants to cause animosity and hatred between you..." (إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ الشَّيْطَانُ أَنْ يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ الْعَدَاوَةَ وَ الْبَغْضَاءَ) [^1]. Given that the word "yuridu" (wants) is a present-tense verb indicating continuity, it reflects the meaning that this is the perpetual will of Satan.

On the other hand, we see that in the Quran, Shaytan is not applied only to a specific being, but even to wicked and corrupt humans. Where we read: "And thus We have made for every prophet an enemy—devils (shayatīn) from mankind and jinn" (وَ كَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَا لِكُلِّ نَبِيٍّ عَدُوًّا شَيَاطِينَ الْإِنْسِ وَ الْجِنِّ) [^2]. And the reason Iblis is also called Shaytan is because of the corruption and wickedness that exists in him.

[^1]: Al-Ma'idah: 91. [^2]: Al-An'am: 112.

In addition to these, the word Shaytan is sometimes applied to "microbes": As an example, the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام says: "Do not drink water from the broken part of the vessel nor from its handle, for Satan sits upon the handle and the broken part" [^1]. And also Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: "And do not drink from the handle of the jug, nor from its broken part if there is one, for it is the drinking place of the satans" [^2]. We read from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: "Do not let the hair of your mustache grow long, for Satan makes it a safe environment for his life and hides there" [^3]! In this way, it became clear that one of the meanings of Shaytan is harmful and injurious microbes.

However, it is obvious that the intention is not that Shaytan means this in every context; rather, the intention is that Shaytan has various meanings, one of the clear instances of which is "Iblis" and his armies and helpers, and another instance is corrupt and misleading humans, and occasionally in some cases, it has been used to mean harmful microbes (note carefully).

5. Why Did God Create Satan?

Many ask: since Satan is a seductive being, why was he created at all, and what is the philosophy of his existence?! In response, we say:

First: God did not create Satan as a "Satan." For years he was a companion

[^1], [^2], [^3]: Al-Kafi, Vol. 6, Book of Foods and Drinks, Chapter on Vessels.

of the angels and was upon a pure nature (fitrah), but later he abused his freedom and began rebellion and defiance. Thus, he was created pure in the beginning, and his deviation was due to his own will.

Second: From the perspective of the system of creation, the existence of Satan is not harmful to people of faith and those who wish to tread the path of truth; rather, it is a means for their progress and perfection. This is because progress, elevation, and perfection always take place amidst contradictions.

To put it more clearly: man never mobilizes and employs his powers and genius until he is faced with a powerful enemy. The very existence of a powerful enemy causes greater movement and activity in man, and consequently, his elevation and perfection.

One of the great philosophers of contemporary history, Toynbee, says: "No brilliant civilization appeared in the world unless a nation was attacked by an external force, and as a result of this invasion, it employed its genius and talent and laid the foundation for such a brilliant civilization."

Ayahs

فَتَلَقَّىٰٓ ءَادَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ كَلِمَـٰتٍ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٣٧)
Then Adam received certain words from his Lord, and He turned to him clemently. Indeed He is the All-clement, the All-merciful. (37)
قُلْنَا ٱهْبِطُوا۟ مِنْهَا جَمِيعًا ۖ فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّى هُدًى فَمَن تَبِعَ هُدَاىَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٣٨)
We said, ‘Get down from it, all together! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve. (38)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٣٩)
But those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]. (39)

Commentary

Adam’s Return to Allah

Following the event of Iblis’s temptation and the command for Adam’s departure from Paradise, Adam realized that he had truly wronged himself. He understood that due to Satan’s deception, he was being expelled from that peaceful and bountiful environment and would be placed in the laborious and arduous environment of the Earth. At this point, Adam thought of compensating for his error and turned toward his Lord with all his soul and heart—a turning characterized by a mountain of regret and longing.

At this moment, Allah’s grace rushed to his aid, and as the Quran states in the above verses: "Adam received certain words from his Lord, effective and transformative words, and through them, he repented, and Allah accepted his repentance" (فَتَلَقَّى آدَمُ مِنْ رَبِّهِ كَلِماتٍ فَتابَ عَلَيْهِ‌).

For He is the Oft-Returning, the Merciful. "إِنَّهُ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ‌". "Tawbah" (repentance) originally means "return." In the language of the Quran, it means returning from sin when attributed to a sinful person. However, sometimes this word is attributed to Allah, where it means a return to mercy. That is, the mercy He had withheld from the servant due to the commission of sin is returned to him after the servant returns to the path of obedience and servitude. For this reason, the term "Tawwab" (The One who returns to mercy frequently) is used for Allah.

In other words, "Tawbah" is a shared term between Allah and His servants. When servants are described by it, it means they have returned to Allah, for every sinner has, in reality, fled from his Lord; when he repents, he returns to Him.

Similarly, when servants are in a state of disobedience, it is as if Allah turns away from them. When Allah is described with "Tawbah," it means He returns His gaze of grace, mercy, and love toward them. [^1] It is true that Adam did not actually commit a prohibited (Haram) act, but this very "leaving the better" (Tark al-Awla) was considered a form of disobedience for him. He quickly realized his situation and returned to his Lord.

Regarding what is meant by the "words" (Kalimat), we will discuss this at the end of this section.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 1, p. 197

In any case, what was not supposed to happen—or was destined to happen—occurred. Although Adam’s repentance was accepted, the consequential effect of his action, which was the descent (Hubut) to Earth, did not change. As the above verses state: "We said: Get down from here, all of you (Adam and Eve). Then, whenever guidance comes to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve" (قُلْنَا اهْبِطُوا مِنْها جَمِيعاً فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ مِنِّي هُدىً فَمَنْ تَبِعَ هُدايَ فَلا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ‌).

"But those who disbelieve and deny Our signs, they are the inhabitants of the Fire; they shall abide therein forever" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ‌).

Key Points

1- What were the words Allah inspired to Adam?

There is discussion among commentators regarding what "words" and speech Allah taught Adam for his repentance.

The well-known view is that they were the same sentences found in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 23: "قالا رَبَّنا ظَلَمْنا أَنْفُسَنا وَ إِنْ لَمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنا وَ تَرْحَمْنا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ‌": "They said: Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers."

Some have said the "words" refer to this supplication and prayer:

"O Allah, there is no god but You, Glory be to You and praise be to You. My Lord, I have wronged myself, so forgive me, for You are the best of forgivers. O Allah, there is no god but You, Glory be to You and praise be to You. My Lord, I have wronged myself, so have mercy on me, for You are the best of those who show mercy. O Allah, there is no god but You, Glory be to You and praise be to You. My Lord, I have wronged myself, so turn to me (accept my repentance), for You are the Oft-Returning, the Merciful."

This matter has been narrated in a tradition from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام. [^1] We read similar expressions in other verses of the Quran regarding Yunus عليه السلام and Musa عليه السلام. When Yunus asked for Allah's forgiveness, he said: "سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ‌": "Glory be to You, I was indeed among those who wronged themselves." [^2] Regarding Prophet Musa عليه السلام, we read: "قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي فَاغْفِرْ لِي فَغَفَرَ لَهُ‌": "He said: My Lord, I have wronged myself, so forgive me, and He forgave him." [^3]

[^1]: For this reason, when "Tawbah" is attributed to the servant, it is made transitive with the word "ila" (to), and when attributed to Allah, it is made transitive with the word "ala" (upon). In the first case, it is said "Taba ilayh" and in the second case "Taba alayh" (Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr Razi and Tafsir al-Safi under the verse in question).

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses in question.

[^2]: Al-Anbiya, 87.

[^3]: Al-Qasas, 16.

In numerous narrations transmitted through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, we read:

That the intended meaning of the "words" was the teaching of the names of the best of Allah's creation, namely Muhammad, Ali, Fatimah, Hasan, and Husayn عليهم السلام. Adam sought forgiveness from Allah's threshold by interceding through these words, and Allah forgave him.

These three interpretations are in no way contradictory, as it is possible that all these words were taught to Adam so that, by attending to their reality and inner depth, a complete spiritual revolution would occur within him, and Allah would encompass him with His grace and guidance.

2- Why is the phrase "Get down" (Ihibitu) repeated?

In the verses under discussion and the verses preceding them, we read that both before and after the repentance, Adam and his wife Eve were addressed to descend to the Earth. There is discussion among commentators whether this repetition is for emphasis or refers to two different points. However, it appears that the second sentence points to the reality that Adam should not imagine that with the acceptance of his repentance, the descent to Earth was canceled; rather, he must still take this path! This is either because he was created for this purpose from the beginning, or because this descent was a consequential effect of his action, and such consequential effects are not altered by repentance.

3- Who is addressed in "Ihibitu"?

"Ihibitu" (Get down) is in the plural form, while Adam and Eve, who were the primary addressees of this speech, were only two people and should have been addressed in the dual form. However, because the descent of Adam and Eve to Earth resulted in their children and descendants also inhabiting the Earth, it was expressed in the plural form.

Ayah

يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتِىَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَأَوْفُوا۟ بِعَهْدِىٓ أُوفِ بِعَهْدِكُمْ وَإِيَّـٰىَ فَٱرْهَبُونِ (٤٠)
O Children of Israel, remember My blessing which I bestowed upon you, and fulfill My covenant that I may fulfill your covenant, and be in awe of Me [alone]. (40)

Commentary

Remembering Allah’s Bounties

We have heard the story of Adam’s vicegerency on earth and his honoring by the angels in the preceding verses, followed by his forgetting of the divine covenant, his departure from Paradise, and subsequently his repentance. From this narrative, a fundamental principle became clear: in this world, two opposing forces of Truth and Falsehood are constantly positioned against each other and engaged in struggle. Whoever follows Satan and chooses the path of Falsehood will ultimately end up distant from Paradise and felicity, entangled in suffering and pain, followed by regret.

Conversely, those who continue along the line of the Lord’s command, paying no heed to the whisperings of Satans and the followers of falsehood, shall live pure and free from pain and suffering.

Since the story of the salvation of the Children of Israel from the clutches of the Pharaohs and their vicegerency on earth—followed by their forgetting of the divine covenant and their entrapment in the clutches of suffering and misery—bears a great resemblance to the story of Adam, and is indeed considered a branch of that general principle, Allah expresses various episodes of the life of the Children of Israel and their destiny in the verse under discussion and dozens of verses following it. This is so that the educational lesson which began with the mention of Adam’s fate may be completed in these discussions.

Addressing the Children of Israel, He says: "O Children of Israel! Call to mind My favor which I bestowed upon you, and fulfill your covenant with Me, I will fulfill My covenant with you, and fear Me alone" (يَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتِيَ الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَأَوْفُوا بِعَهْدِي أُوفِ بِعَهْدِكُمْ وَإِيَّايَ فَارْهَبُونِ).

In reality, these three commands (remembering Allah’s great bounties, fulfilling the covenant of the Lord, and fearing disobedience to Him) constitute the foundation of all divine programs.

Remembering His bounties invites man toward His gnosis (Ma'rifah) and stirs the sense of gratitude within him. Then, paying attention to the fact that these bounties are not unconditional and that Allah has taken a covenant alongside them, directs man toward his duties and responsibilities. Subsequently, fearing no one and no position in the path of performing one's duty causes man to remove all obstacles from his path and fulfill his responsibilities and commitments. This is because one of the major obstacles in this path is groundless fears of this person or that person—especially in the case of the Children of Israel, who had lived under the domination of the Pharaohs for years, such that fear had become woven into the fabric of their being.

Key Points

1- The Jews in Medina

It is interesting to note that according to the explicit statements of some Quranic historians, Surah Al-Baqarah is the first Surah revealed in Medina, and a significant portion of it discusses the Jews. This is because the Jews were the most well-known population of the People of the Book there; before the appearance of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), they expected such an advent according to their own religious books and would give glad tidings of it to others. Economically, their situation was also very good, and overall, they held deep influence in Medina.

However, with the emergence of Islam—an Islam that blocked the paths to their illegitimate interests and prevented their deviations and self-will—they not only largely refused to accept the call of Islam but also rose against it both openly and secretly. This is the same struggle that still continues after fourteen centuries.

The above verse and the subsequent verses were revealed, placing them under the severest barrage of reproach. By accurately mentioning sensitive parts of their history, it shook them so profoundly that anyone with the slightest spirit of truth-seeking was awakened and came toward Islam. Furthermore, it was an instructive lesson for all Muslims.

In the coming verses, God willing, we shall read episodes such as their salvation from the clutches of Pharaoh, the parting of the sea and the drowning of the Pharaohs, Moses' rendezvous at Mount Tur, the calf-worship of the Children of Israel during Moses' absence, the command for the "bloody repentance," and the descent of Allah’s special bounties upon them, each of which contains one or more instructive lessons.

2- What were the twelve covenants of God with the Jews?

As inferred from the verses of the Quran, this covenant consisted of: worshipping the One God, kindness to parents, relatives, orphans, and the needy, good conduct with people, establishing prayer, paying zakat, avoiding harassment and injury, and refraining from bloodshed.

The evidence for this is verses 83 and 84 of this very Surah:

وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَاقَ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ لَا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ وَبِالْوَالِدَيْنِ إِحْسَانًا وَذِي الْقُرْبَىٰ وَالْيَتَامَىٰ وَالْمَسَاكِينِ وَقُولُوا لِلنَّاسِ حُسْنًا وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكَاةَ ... وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَاقَكُمْ لَا تَسْفِكُونَ دِمَاءَكُمْ وَلَا تُخْرِجُونَ أَنْفُسَكُمْ مِنْ دِيَارِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ تَشْهَدُونَ.

In fact, these two verses refer to ten different covenants that Allah had taken from the Jews. By adding verse 12 of Surah Al-Ma'idah (وَلَقَدْ أَخَذَ اللَّهُ مِيثَاقَ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ ... وَقَالَ اللَّهُ إِنِّي مَعَكُمْ لَئِنْ أَقَمْتُمُ الصَّلَاةَ وَآتَيْتُمُ الزَّكَاةَ وَآمَنْتُمْ بِرُسُلِي وَعَزَّرْتُمُوهُمْ ...), from which two other covenants regarding faith in the Messengers and supporting them are derived, it becomes clear that they had made numerous commitments in exchange for those great divine bounties. They were promised that if they remained faithful to these, they would be placed in gardens of Paradise beneath whose palaces and trees rivers flow (لَأُدْخِلَنَّكُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ). But unfortunately, they ultimately trampled upon all these covenants and continue their covenant-breaking to this day. Consequently, they became scattered and displaced, and as long as these breaches of covenant continue, this situation will also persist. If we see them making noise for a few days under the protection of others, it is by no means proof of their victory; we clearly see the day when the valiant children of Islam, far from racial or ethnic biases and solely under the shadow of the Quran, shall rise and put an end to these noises.

4- Allah also fulfills His covenant

Allah’s bounties are never unconditional; alongside every bounty, there lies a responsibility and a hidden condition.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that the meaning of "I will fulfill My covenant with you" (أُوفِ بِعَهْدِكُمْ) is: "I will fulfill My covenant and take you to Paradise." [1] If in part of this Hadith, faith in the Wilayah (guardianship) of Ali عليه السلام is mentioned as part of this covenant, it is not surprising. This is because one of the articles of the covenant of the Children of Israel was the acceptance of the mission of the divine Prophets and supporting them, and we know that accepting their successors is a continuation of that same issue of leadership and Wilayah, which must be realized in every age appropriate to that era.

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 72.

In the time of Moses عليه السلام, he held this position; in the era of the Prophet, it was the Prophet; and in subsequent times, it was Ali عليه السلام.

Furthermore, the phrase "fear Me alone" (إِيَّايَ فَارْهَبُونِ) is an emphasis on the fact that in the path of fulfilling divine covenants and obeying His command, one must not have fear or dread of anything or anyone. we derive this exclusivity from the word "Iyyaya" (Me alone) which precedes the phrase "farhabun" (fear).

5- Why are the Jews called the Children of Israel?

"Israel" is one of the names of Jacob (Ya'qub), the father of Joseph (Yusuf). Regarding the reason for naming Jacob with this name, non-Muslim historians have mentioned things mixed with superstitions.

As the "Bible Dictionary" writes: "Israel means one who prevailed over God!" It adds that "this word is the title of Jacob son of Isaac, which he was titled with during a wrestling match (struggle) with the angel of God!" The same author writes under the entry "Jacob": "He manifested his firmness, perseverance, and faith; at this time, God changed his name and called him 'Israel,' and promised that he would become the father of a multitude of tribes... and... finally died in extreme old age and was buried like one of the kings of the world! And the names Jacob and Israel are applied to all his people."

He also writes under the entry "Israel": "And this name has many applications, such that sometimes the intent is the progeny of Israel and the progeny of Jacob." [1] However, our scholars, such as the famous commentator Tabarsi in "Majma' al-Bayan," write the following on this matter:

[1]: Bible Dictionary, pp. 53 and 957.

"Israel is the same Jacob, son of Isaac, son of Abraham عليه السلام..."

He says, "Isr" means "servant" and "El" means "Allah," and this word collectively gives the meaning of "Abdullah" (Servant of Allah).

It is obvious that the story of Israel wrestling with the angel of God or with God Himself, which is found in the current distorted Torah, is a fabricated and childish tale that is entirely beneath the dignity of a Heavenly Book, and this itself is one of the evidences of the corruption (tahrif) of the current Torah.

Ayahs

وَءَامِنُوا۟ بِمَآ أَنزَلْتُ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَكُمْ وَلَا تَكُونُوٓا۟ أَوَّلَ كَافِرٍۭ بِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَشْتَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِى ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا وَإِيَّـٰىَ فَٱتَّقُونِ (٤١)
And believe in that which I have sent down confirming that which is with you, and do not be the first ones to deny it, and do not sell My signs for a paltry gain, and be wary of Me [alone]. (41)
وَلَا تَلْبِسُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ وَتَكْتُمُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٤٢)
Do not mix the truth with falsehood, nor conceal the truth while you know. (42)
وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱرْكَعُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلرَّٰكِعِينَ (٤٣)
And maintain the prayer, and give the zakat, and bow along with those who bow [in prayer]. (43)

Occasion of Revelation

Some great commentators have narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding the occasion of revelation for the first verses under discussion that: "Huyayy ibn Akhtab, Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf, and a group of other Jews used to have a (lavish) banquet organized for them every year by the Jewish community. They were not willing to let even this small benefit disappear due to the rise of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. For this reason (and other reasons), they distorted the verses of the Torah that concerned the descriptions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. This is the 'miserable price' (thaman qalil) and the small gain that the Quran refers to in this verse." [1]

Commentary

The Mercenary Nature of the Jews

Following the covenant that Allah had taken from the Jews—which included belief in Divine Prophets and obedience to His commands—the three verses under discussion point to nine sections of instructions given to the Jews.

First, He says: "Believe in what has been sent down to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, verses that are consistent with the descriptions found in your Torah" (وَ آمِنُوا بِما أَنْزَلْتُ مُصَدِّقاً لِما مَعَكُمْ).

The Quran is a "confirmer" (musaddiq) of the book that is with you. This means that the glad tidings given by the Torah and previous Prophets to their followers—that after us a Prophet with such attributes will appear and his Heavenly Book will have these characteristics—are now fulfilled. Now that you see the attributes of this Prophet and the characteristics of the Quran perfectly match the glad tidings in your books and are in complete harmony with them, why do you not believe in it?!

Then He says: "Do not be the first to disbelieve in this Heavenly Book and deny it" (وَ لا تَكُونُوا أَوَّلَ كافِرٍ بِهِ).

That is to say, if the Arab polytheists and idolaters disbelieve, it is not very surprising; what is surprising is your disbelief and denial, especially as the pioneers and first opponents. This is because you are more informed than them, you are People of the Book, and your Heavenly Books contain all these glad tidings regarding the appearance of such a Prophet. For this very reason, before his appearance, you were his first heralds. What happened that after his appearance, instead of being the first believers, you became the first disbelievers?

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed.

Indeed, many Jews are fundamentally a stubborn people, and were it not for this stubbornness, they should have believed much sooner than others.

In the third phrase, He says: "Do not sell My signs for a miserable price" and do not exchange them for an annual banquet (وَ لا تَشْتَرُوا بِآياتِي ثَمَناً قَلِيلًا).

Undoubtedly, the signs of Allah should not be exchanged for any price, whether small or great. However, this phrase actually points to the low-mindedness of this group of Jews who, for the sake of meager interests, consigned everything to oblivion. Those who until that day were heralds of the rise of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and his Heavenly Book, denied these glad tidings and distorted the verses of the Torah when they saw their interests in danger; for if the people became acquainted with the truth, the palace of their leadership would crumble.

Fundamentally, if the entire world were given to someone to deny one of the Divine signs, it would truly be a small and miserable price, because this life is ultimately perishable while the abode of the Hereafter is eternal and everlasting—let alone for a person to sacrifice these Divine signs for insignificant interests.

In the fourth instruction, He says: "Fear only Me" (وَ إِيَّايَ فَاتَّقُونِ). Do not fear that your sustenance will be cut off, and do not fear that a group of Jewish fanatics will rise against you leaders; fear only Me, meaning fear disobeying My command.

In the fifth instruction, He says: "Do not clothe the truth with falsehood" so that people fall into error (وَ لا تَلْبِسُوا الْحَقَّ بِالْباطِلِ). And in the sixth instruction, He forbids concealing the truth, saying: "Do not conceal the truth while you know and are aware" (وَ تَكْتُمُوا الْحَقَّ وَ أَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ).

Both concealing the truth is a crime and a sin, and mixing truth with falsehood; both are identical in terms of their result. Speak the truth even if it is to your detriment, and do not mix falsehood with it even if your fleeting interests are endangered.

Finally, He recounts the seventh, eighth, and ninth instructions in this manner: "Establish prayer, pay the zakat, and (do not forget collective worship) bow down with those who bow" (وَ أَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ آتُوا الزَّكاةَ وَ ارْكَعُوا مَعَ الرَّاكِعِينَ).

Although the last instruction refers to the issue of congregational prayer, out of all the actions of prayer, only "Ruku" (bowing) is mentioned, saying: "Bow with those who bow." This expression is perhaps because the prayer of the Jews lacked Ruku entirely; it is the prayer of Muslims in which Ruku is considered one of the main pillars.

Interestingly, He does not say "read the prayer," but says: أَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ (Establish the prayer). This means do not merely be prayer-performers yourselves, but act in such a way that the rite of prayer is established in human society, and people come toward it with love and passion.

Some commentators have said that the expression "aqimu" (establish) indicates that your prayer should not be merely recitations and litanies, but you should establish it perfectly, the most important pillar of which is the attention of the heart and the presence of the soul before God, and the impact of prayer on the human spirit and soul. [1] In reality, in these last three instructions, first the connection of the individual with the Creator (prayer) is stated, then the connection with the creation (zakat), and finally the collective connection of all people together in the way of Allah!

[1]: Al-Manar, Vol. 2, p. 293; and Raghib's Mufradat, under the root "q-w-m".

Key Points

Did the Quran Validate the Contents of the Torah and the Gospel?!

In numerous verses of the Holy Quran, this expression is seen: "The Quran confirms the contents of the previous books."
