Among them, we read in the verses under discussion: "مُصَدِّقاً لِما مَعَكُمْ" and in verses 89 and 101 of Surah Al-Baqarah we read: "مُصَدِّقٌ لِما مَعَهُمْ".

And in verse 48 of Al-Ma'idah, He says: وَ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ الْكِتابِ: "And We have sent down to you the Book in truth, confirming the Heavenly Books before it."

This very matter has caused a group of Jewish and Christian missionaries to take these verses as evidence for the non-distortion of the Torah and the Gospel, saying: The Torah and Gospel in the era of the Prophet of Islam certainly did not differ from today's Torah and Gospel, and if any distortion occurred in the Torah and Gospels, it must relate to before that time. Since the Quran confirmed the validity of the Torah and Gospel present in the era of the Prophet of Islam, Muslims must recognize these books as untouched Heavenly Books.

Answer

Various verses of the Quran testify that the signs of the Prophet of Islam and his religion existed in those very distorted books that were in the hands of the Jews and Christians at that time. It is certain that the meaning of the distortion of these Heavenly Books is not that all existing books are false and contrary to reality; rather, a portion of the real Torah and Gospel existed and exists within these very books, and the signs of the Prophet of Islam existed in these books or other religious books in the hands of the Jews and Christians (and even now there are glad tidings in them).

In this way, the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and his Heavenly Book practically confirmed all those signs because it corresponded with them.

Therefore, the meaning of the Quran's confirmation of the Torah and Gospel is that the attributes and characteristics of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Quran perfectly match the signs mentioned in the Torah and Gospel.

The use of the word "tasdiq" (confirmation) in the sense of "correspondence" (mutabaqat) is also observed in other verses of the Quran, including in verse 105 of Surah As-Saffat where He says to Ibrahim عليه السلام: قَدْ صَدَّقْتَ الرُّؤْيا (You have confirmed the vision), meaning your action corresponds to the dream you saw. And in verse 157 of Surah Al-A'raf, we read:

الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ الَّذِي يَجِدُونَهُ مَكْتُوباً عِنْدَهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَ الْإِنْجِيلِ... This meaning is explicitly stated, i.e., the attributes they see in him correspond to what they found in the Torah.

In any case, the above verses are nothing but a "practical confirmation" by the Quran and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the signs of his truthfulness that were in the past books, and they do not indicate a confirmation of all the contents of the Torah and Gospel. Furthermore, numerous verses of the Quran recount that they distorted the verses of the Torah and Gospel; this itself is a living witness to the above statement.

Another living witness:

"Fakhr al-Islam," the author of the book Anis al-A'lam, who was himself a renowned Christian priest, completed his studies under Christian priests, and attained a high rank in their view, describes the amazing story of his conversion to Islam in the introduction of this book as follows:

"...After much searching, extraordinary efforts, and traveling through cities, I reached the service of a high-ranking priest who was distinguished in asceticism and piety. The Catholic sect, including kings and others, referred their religious questions to him. I studied the various Christian denominations under him for a time. He had many students, but among them all, he had a special affection for me. The keys to the house... were all in my hands, except he kept the key to one of the small storerooms for himself...

Meanwhile, one day the said priest fell ill and told me: 'Tell the students I am not in a state to teach.' When I came to the students, I saw them engaged in a discussion. This discussion led to the meaning of the word 'Paraclete' (Fariqlita) in Syriac and 'Periclytos' in Greek...

Their dispute dragged on; everyone had an opinion...

Upon my return, the master asked: 'What did you discuss today?!' I related to him their disagreement over the word 'Paraclete'... He said: 'Which of the opinions did you choose?'

I said: 'I chose the preference of such-and-such commentator.'

The priest said: 'You have not fallen short, but the truth and reality are contrary to all these opinions, for the truth of this is known only by those firmly rooted in knowledge (al-rasikhuna fil-'ilm), and only a few of them are acquainted with that truth.' I insisted that he tell me its meaning. He wept bitterly and said: 'I will withhold nothing from you... there is a great effect in learning the meaning of this name, but as soon as it is spread, they will kill me and you! If you promise not to tell anyone, I will reveal this meaning...' I swore by all that is sacred not to reveal his name. Then he said: 'This name is one of the names of the Prophet of the Muslims and it means "Ahmad" and "Muhammad".'

After that, he gave me the key to that small room and said: 'Open such-and-such box and bring such-and-such books.' I brought the books to him. These two books were written on parchment in Greek and Syriac before the appearance of the Prophet of Islam.

In both books, they had translated the word 'Paraclete' to mean Ahmad and Muhammad. Then the master added: 'The Christian scholars before his appearance had no disagreement that "Paraclete" means "Ahmad and Muhammad," but after the appearance of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, for the sake of maintaining their leadership and material gain, they interpreted it differently and invented another meaning for it, and that meaning was certainly not intended by the author of the Gospel.'

I asked: 'What do you say regarding the religion of the Christians?' He said: 'It is abrogated with the coming of the religion of Islam.' He repeated this phrase three times. Then I said:

'In this time, what is the way of salvation and the straight path...?' He said: 'It is restricted to following Muhammad (ص).'

I said: 'Are his followers among the saved?' He said: 'Yes, by Allah (repeating it three times)...' Then the master wept and I also wept much. He said: 'If you want the Hereafter and salvation, you must certainly accept the true religion... and I will always pray for you, on the condition that on the Day of Resurrection you bear witness that I am a Muslim in my heart and among the followers of Hazrat Muhammad (ص)... there is no doubt that today on earth, the religion of Islam is the religion of God...'"!! [1]

As you observe, according to this document, the scholars of the People of the Book, after the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, interpreted and justified his name and signs in another way for the sake of their personal interests.

[1]: Adapted with slight abbreviation from "The Second Guidance," introduction to the book Anis al-A'lam.

Ayahs

۞ أَتَأْمُرُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ بِٱلْبِرِّ وَتَنسَوْنَ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ تَتْلُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٤٤)
Will you bid others to piety and forget yourselves, while you recite the Book? Do you not exercise your reason? (44)
وَٱسْتَعِينُوا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ وَٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ۚ وَإِنَّهَا لَكَبِيرَةٌ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلْخَـٰشِعِينَ (٤٥)
And take recourse in patience and prayer, and it is indeed hard except for the humble (45)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُم مُّلَـٰقُوا۟ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِ رَٰجِعُونَ (٤٦)
—those who are certain that they will encounter their Lord and that they will return to Him. (46)

Commentary

You Recommend to Others, But Why Not to Yourselves?!

Although the address in the above verses, like the verses before and after, is directed at the Children of Israel, its concept is certainly broad and includes others as well.

According to the famous commentator "Tabarsi" in Majma' al-Bayan, the Jewish scholars and sages before the mission of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله used to invite people to believe in him and gave glad tidings of his appearance, yet they themselves refrained from believing when he appeared.

The same great commentator also narrates that some Jewish scholars would advise their relatives who had embraced Islam to remain firm and steadfast in their faith, yet they themselves would not believe.

Therefore, the first verse under discussion rebukes them for this action, saying: "Do you enjoin right conduct on the people and forget your own selves?" (أَ تَأْمُرُونَ النَّاسَ بِالْبِرِّ وَ تَنْسَوْنَ أَنْفُسَكُمْ‌).

"While you read the Scripture? Do you not then understand?" (وَ أَنْتُمْ تَتْلُونَ الْكِتابَ أَ فَلا تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

Fundamentally, a basic program, especially for scholars, preachers, and callers to the path of Truth, is to propagate to the people through their actions more than their words, just as we read in the famous hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"Invite people [to goodness] by your actions, and do not be callers by your tongues." [^1]

The profound impact of "practical invitation" stems from the fact that whenever a listener knows the speaker speaks from the heart and has one hundred percent faith in his own words, he opens the ear of his soul to his speech. Speech that rises from the heart sits upon the heart and leaves an impression on the soul. The best sign of a speaker's faith in his words is that he acts upon them before others, just as Ali عليه السلام says:

"O people! By Allah, I do not urge you to any obedience except that I precede you in it, and I do not forbid you from any disobedience except that I desist from it before you." [^2]

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Among those who will face the severest punishment on the Day of Resurrection is one who describes justice but acts contrary to it." [^3]

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, under the root "Amal". [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 175. [^3]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 75.

The Jewish scholars feared that if they confessed to the messengership of the Prophet of Islam, the palace of their leadership would crumble and the Jewish commoners would no longer pay them heed. Therefore, they distorted the descriptions of the Prophet of Islam that appeared in the Torah.

In order for a human being to overcome the desires and whims of the heart and to expel the love of status and position from the head, the Quran says in the next verse: "Seek help through patience and prayer," and through steadfastness and self-control, triumph over internal passions (وَ اسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَ الصَّلاةِ).

Then it adds: "And indeed, it is difficult except for the humble" (وَ إِنَّها لَكَبِيرَةٌ إِلَّا عَلَى الْخاشِعِينَ‌).

In the final verse under discussion, it defines the humble as: "Those who are certain that they will meet their Lord and that they will return to Him" (الَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُلاقُوا رَبِّهِمْ وَ أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِ راجِعُونَ‌).

"Yazunnun" (يظنون) comes from the root "Zann" (ظن), which sometimes means "conjecture" and sometimes means "certainty" [^1]. Here, it certainly means faith and definitive certainty, because faith in the meeting with Allah and the return to Him enlivens the state of humility, God-fearingness, and a sense of responsibility in the human heart. This is one of the educational effects of faith in the Hereafter, which visualizes the scene of that Great Court before the human being everywhere and invites him to fulfill responsibilities, truth, and justice.

There is also the possibility that "Zann" here means "conjecture," and this is actually a type of hyperbole and emphasis: that if a person—hypothetically—does not have absolute faith in that Great Court and only suspects its existence, it should be enough to restrain him from any wrongdoing. In reality, it is a reproach to the Jewish scholars that if your faith even reached the level of suspicion and conjecture, you should still feel responsible and cease such distortions. [^1]

[^1]: Raghib in the book al-Mufradat says: "Zann" is a noun for a belief that arises from evidence and clues; this belief sometimes becomes strong and reaches the stage of certainty, and sometimes it is weak and does not exceed the limit of conjecture.

Key Points

1- What is "Meeting Allah" (Liqa' Allah)?

The expression "Meeting Allah" (Liqa' Allah) appears repeatedly in the Holy Quran, and all instances refer to being present at the scene of the Resurrection. Obviously, the meaning of "meeting" and encountering God is not a sensory meeting, like the meeting of human beings with one another, for Allah is neither a body nor does He have color or location to be seen with the physical eye. Rather, it either means witnessing the signs of His power on the scene of Resurrection and His rewards, punishments, blessings, and torments, as a group of commentators have stated.

Or it means a type of internal and spiritual witnessing, for a human being sometimes reaches a point where it is as if he observes God with the eye of the heart before him, such that no doubt or hesitation remains for him.

This state may occur for a group in this world as a result of purity, piety, worship, and self-purification, as we read in Nahj al-Balagha: One of the learned friends of Ali عليه السلام named "Dhi'lib al-Yamani" asked the Imam عليه السلام: "Have you seen your Lord?"

The Imam عليه السلام replied: "Would I worship that which I do not see?!" And when he asked for further explanation, the Imam added:

"The physical eyes do not perceive Him through sight, but the hearts perceive Him through the realities of faith." [^1]

However, this state of internal witnessing will occur for everyone in the Resurrection, because the signs of God's greatness and power are so manifest there that even the blind-hearted will gain definitive faith.

2- The Way to Victory Over Problems

For progress and victory over problems, two basic pillars are necessary: one is a powerful internal base and the other is a firm external support. In the above verses, these two basic pillars are referred to with the terms "patience" (sabr) and "prayer" (salat). Patience is that state of steadfastness, endurance, and standing firm on the front of difficulties, and prayer is a bond with God and a means of communication with this firm support.

Although the word "patience" has been interpreted as "fasting" in many narrations, it is certainly not limited to fasting; rather, fasting is mentioned as a prominent and clear example of it, because in the light of this great worship, a person gains a powerful will and a firm faith, and the sovereignty of his intellect over his passions becomes established.

Great commentators have narrated in the interpretation of this verse that whenever the Noble Messenger of Islam faced a problem that distressed him, he would seek help from prayer and fasting. [^2]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Saying 179. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

It is also narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "When you are faced with one of the sorrows of the world, perform ablution, go to the mosque, pray, and supplicate, for Allah has commanded: 'Seek help through patience and prayer.'" [^1] Turning toward prayer and intimate discourse with the Lord creates a new strength in man and empowers him to face difficulties.

In the book al-Kafi, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"Whenever a grave matter distressed Ali عليه السلام, he would stand for prayer and then recite this verse: 'Seek help through patience and prayer.'"

Yes, prayer connects man to an Infinite Power for whom all problems are easy and simple, and this very feeling causes man to be strong and composed in the face of events.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

Ayahs

يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتِىَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَأَنِّى فَضَّلْتُكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٧)
O Children of Israel, remember My blessing which I bestowed upon you, and that I gave you an advantage over all the nations. (47)
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ يَوْمًا لَّا تَجْزِى نَفْسٌ عَن نَّفْسٍ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يُقْبَلُ مِنْهَا شَفَـٰعَةٌ وَلَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْهَا عَدْلٌ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٤٨)
Beware of the day when no soul will compensate for another, neither any intercession shall be accepted from it, nor any ransom shall be received from it, nor will they be helped. (48)

Commentary

The Vain Imaginations of the Jews

In these verses, Allah once again addresses the Children of Israel, reminding them of His blessings and saying: "O Children of Israel! Remember My favor which I bestowed upon you" (يَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتِيَ الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ).

These blessings cover a vast spectrum, ranging from the blessing of guidance and faith to deliverance from the clutches of the Pharaohs and the regaining of greatness and independence.

Then, from among these blessings, He points to the blessing of excellence and superiority over the people of their time—which is a combination of various favors—and says: "And that I preferred you over the worlds" (وَأَنِّي فَضَّلْتُكُمْ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ).

Some might imagine that the phrase "فَضَّلْتُكُمْ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ" means He preferred them over all people of all eras. However, considering other verses of the Quran, it becomes clear that the intent is their superiority over the people of their own environment and age. This is because we read in the Quran: "كُنْتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ..." (You [Muslims] are the best nation produced for mankind...) (Surah Al-Imran, Verse 110).

Elsewhere regarding the Children of Israel, we read: "وَأَوْرَثْنَا الْقَوْمَ الَّذِينَ كَانُوا يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشَارِقَ الْأَرْضِ وَمَغَارِبَهَا" (And We caused the people who had been oppressed to inherit the eastern and western parts of the land) (Surah Al-A'raf, Verse 137). It is clear that the Children of Israel did not become heirs to the entire world at that time; thus, the meaning is that they became heirs to the east and west of their own region. Therefore, their excellence over "the worlds" is also a superiority relative to the people of that same environment.

In the next verse, the Quran strikes a line of cancellation through the vain imaginations of the Jews. They believed that because their ancestors and forefathers were Prophets of God, they would intercede for them. Or they imagined that one could provide a ransom or substitute for sins, just as they resorted to bribery in this world.

The Quran says: "And fear a Day when no soul shall suffice for another soul at all" (وَاتَّقُوا يَوْماً لَا تَجْزِي نَفْسٌ عَنْ نَفْسٍ شَيْئاً).

"Nor shall intercession (without the permission of the Lord) be accepted from it" (وَلَا يُقْبَلُ مِنْهَا شَفَاعَةٌ).

"Nor shall compensation or substitute be taken from it" (وَلَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْهَا عَدْلٌ).

"Nor shall they be helped" (وَلَا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ).

In short, the Ruler and Judge of that scene is One who accepts nothing but pure deeds, as we read in Verses 88 and 89 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara: يَوْمَ لَا يَنْفَعُ مَالٌ وَلَا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ (The Day when neither wealth nor children will avail, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart).

In reality, the above verse points out that in this world, it is customary to use various methods to save criminals from punishment: sometimes one person accepts the fine of another and pays it.

If this is not possible, he resorts to intercession and prompts individuals to intercede for him.

If this too fails, they try to free themselves by paying a ransom.

And if they have no access to this, they seek help from friends and allies to defend them so they do not fall into the clutches of punishment.

These are the various ways of escaping punishment in this world, but the Quran says:

The principles governing punishments in the Resurrection are entirely separate from these matters, and none of these things will be of use there. The only way to salvation is to take refuge in the shadow of faith and piety and to seek help from the grace of the Lord.

An analysis of the beliefs of idolaters or the deviant among the People of the Book shows that such superstitious thoughts were not uncommon among them. For instance, the author of Tafsir al-Manar mentions that in some regions of Egypt, some superstitious people would give cash to the one washing the deceased, calling it the "fee for transport to Paradise." [1] We also read regarding the Jews that they would offer sacrifices as atonement for their sins; if they could not afford a large sacrifice, they would sacrifice a pair of pigeons! [2] Regarding ancient peoples who likely lived in prehistoric times, we read:

[1] and [2]: Al-Manar, Vol. 1, p. 306.

...that they would bury the ornaments and weapons of the deceased with them so they could benefit from them in their future life! [1]

The Quran and the Issue of Intercession

Undoubtedly, Divine punishments, whether in this world or in the Resurrection, are not for the sake of revenge. Rather, they are all, in reality, a guarantee for the implementation of obedience to laws and, consequently, the progress and perfection of human beings. Therefore, anything that weakens this guarantee must be avoided so that audacity and boldness toward sin do not arise in people.

On the other hand, the path of return and reform must not be completely closed to sinners; rather, they must be given the possibility to reform themselves and return toward Allah, purity, and piety.

"Intercession" (Shafa'ah), in its correct sense, is for maintaining this very balance; it is a means for the return of sinners and the defiled. In its wrong and incorrect sense, it leads to encouragement and audacity toward sin.

Those who have not distinguished between the various aspects of intercession and its correct concepts have sometimes denied the issue of intercession altogether, equating it with the recommendations and nepotism found before oppressive kings and rulers! And sometimes, like the Wahhabis, they have used the above verse which says: "لَا يُقْبَلُ مِنْهَا شَفَاعَةٌ" (intercession shall not be accepted from it) in the Resurrection as a pretext—without paying attention to other verses—and have completely denied intercession.

In any case, the objections of the opponents of intercession can be summarized in several points:

  1. Belief in intercession weakens the spirit of effort and striving.

[1] Al-Mizan, Vol. 1, p. 156.

  1. Belief in intercession is a reflection of backward and feudal societies.

  2. Belief in intercession leads to encouragement of sin and the abandonment of responsibilities.

  3. Belief in intercession is a type of shirk (polytheism) and is contrary to the Quran!

  4. Belief in intercession implies a change in Allah's decrees and an alteration of His Will and Command!

However, as we shall explain, all these objections arise from confusing intercession in its Quranic concept with the deviant intercessions common among the masses.

Since this issue is of particular importance both in terms of affirmation and negation, we must discuss in detail the concept of intercession, the philosophy of intercession, intercession in the cosmic realm (takwin), intercession in the Quran and Hadith, and intercession and the issue of monotheism and polytheism here, so that any ambiguity regarding the above verse and other verses we will encounter in the future regarding intercession is removed.

1- The Real Concept of Intercession

The word "Shafa'ah" is derived from the root "shaf'" meaning "even" or "pairing a thing with its like." Its opposite is "watr" meaning single or alone. Subsequently, it was applied to the joining of a superior and stronger individual to help a weaker individual. This term is used in both common parlance and religious law with two different meanings:

A- Intercession in the common tongue: This refers to an intercessor using his position, personality, and influence to change the view of a powerful person regarding the punishment of his subordinates.

Sometimes this is done by using his influence or the fear others have of his influence.

At other times, it is by bringing up emotional issues and influencing the emotions of the other party.

And at other times, it is by changing the other's intellectual foundations regarding the crime of the offender and his desert of punishment, and the like...

In short, intercession according to this meaning creates no change in the psyche or thought of the criminal or the accused; all effects and changes pertain to the person to whom the intercession is made (pay close attention).

This type of intercession has absolutely no meaning in religious discussions, for Allah neither makes a mistake such that His opinion could be changed, nor does He have emotions in the human sense that could be stirred, nor does He take anyone's influence into account or feel fear, nor does His reward and punishment revolve around anything other than justice.

The other concept of intercession revolves around a transformation and change in the position of the "one being interceded for." That is, the person being interceded for provides the means to emerge from an undesirable state deserving of punishment and, through a connection with the intercessor, places himself in a desirable state worthy and deserving of forgiveness. As we shall see, faith in this type of intercession is, in fact, a high school of education and a means of reforming sinful and defiled individuals, and a source of awakening and awareness. Intercession in the logic of Islam is of this latter type.

We shall see that all objections, criticisms, and attacks are directed at the first interpretation of intercession, not the second concept, which is a logical, rational, and constructive meaning.

This was the summary interpretation of intercession in its two forms: "numbing" and "constructive."

2- Intercession in the Cosmic Realm (Takwin):

What we said regarding intercession in its correct and logical interpretation is also frequently seen in the world of creation and nature (in addition to the world of legislation). Stronger forces in this world join with weaker forces and propel them toward constructive goals. The sun shines and the rain falls, preparing the seeds in the heart of the earth to utilize their inner potentials and send out the first sprout of life, to split the husks of the seeds and emerge from the darkness of the soil toward the sky from which they received power.

These scenes are, in reality, a type of cosmic intercession in the resurrection of life. If we adopt a type of intercession in the "legislative" (tashri'i) scene by emulating this model, we have taken a straight path, the explanation of which we shall soon read.

3- Evidences for Intercession:

Now we turn to the primary and first-hand evidences regarding the issue of intercession:

In the Quran, the issue of "intercession" (by this very title) is discussed in about 30 instances (of course, other discussions and references to this issue without mentioning this title are also seen).

The verses in the Quran that discuss this issue are, in fact, divided into several categories:

Group One: Verses that negate intercession absolutely, such as: "Spend of that which We have provided for you before a Day comes when there will be no bargaining, nor friendship, nor intercession" (Al-Baqarah: 254) and like "وَلَا يُقْبَلُ مِنْهَا شَفَاعَةٌ" (Al-Baqarah: 48).

In these verses, the conceivable ways for the salvation of criminals other than faith and righteous deeds—whether through paying a material substitute, or a bond and history of friendship, or the issue of intercession—are negated.

Regarding some criminals, we read: "فَمَا تَنْفَعُهُمْ شَفَاعَةُ الشَّافِعِينَ" (So the intercession of intercessors will not benefit them) (Al-Muddaththir: 48).

Group Two: Verses that introduce the "Intercessor" as exclusively Allah, such as: "You have none besides Him as a protector or an intercessor" (As-Sajdah: 4) and "قُلْ لِلَّهِ الشَّفاعَةُ جَمِيعاً" (Say: To Allah belongs all intercession) (Az-Zumar: 44).

Group Three: Verses that condition intercession upon the permission and command of Allah, such as: "مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يَشْفَعُ عِنْدَهُ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ" (Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission?) (Al-Baqarah: 255) and "وَلَا تَنْفَعُ الشَّفَاعَةُ عِنْدَهُ إِلَّا لِمَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ" (Intercession will not benefit with Him except for him whom He permits) (Saba: 23).

Group Four: Verses that state conditions for the one being interceded for. Sometimes this condition is identified as the pleasure and approval of Allah, such as: "وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَى" (And they do not intercede except for him with whom He is pleased) (Al-Anbiya: 28).

According to this verse, the intercession of the intercessors exclusively includes those who have reached the station of "irtida"—that is, being accepted in the presence of Allah.

At other times, the condition is identified as having taken a covenant with Allah, such as: "لَا يَمْلِكُونَ الشَّفَاعَةَ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّخَذَ عِنْدَ الرَّحْمَنِ عَهْداً" (They will not possess intercession except him who has taken a covenant with the Most Merciful) (Maryam: 87). (The meaning of this covenant is faith in Allah and the Prophets of God).

And at times, the "eligibility to be interceded for" is stripped from some criminals, such as the denial of intercession for the oppressors in the following verse: "مَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ يُطَاعُ" (The oppressors will have no devoted friend nor any intercessor who is obeyed) (Ghafir: 18).

Thus, having a Divine covenant (faith), reaching the station of the Lord's pleasure, and abstaining from sins such as oppression and injustice are among the definitive conditions for intercession.

4- Various Conditions for Intercession

In summary, the verses on intercession clearly show that the issue of intercession, from the perspective of Islamic logic, is not an unconditional matter. Rather, it has constraints and conditions regarding the crime for which intercession is made on one hand, the person being interceded for on the second, and the intercessor on the third, which clarify the true face of intercession and its philosophy.

For example, sins such as oppression and injustice are considered entirely outside the circle of intercession, and the Quran says: the oppressors have no "obeyed intercessor"! If we interpret oppression in the broad sense of the word—as will appear in some hadiths later—intercession will be limited to criminals who are remorseful and repentant for their actions and are on the path of compensation and reform. In this case, intercession will be a support for repentance and remorse for sin (and the idea some have that with remorse and repentance there is no need for intercession is a mistake to which we will soon respond).

On the other hand, according to Verse 28 of Surah Al-Anbiya, only those who have reached the station of "irtida" are included in forgiveness through intercession, and according to Verse 87 of Maryam, they must possess a "Divine covenant."

These two titles, as understood from their linguistic meaning and from the narrations regarding the interpretation of these verses, mean faith in Allah, the Account, the Scale, reward and punishment, and the acknowledgment of good and evil deeds ("the goodness of good deeds and the evil of bad deeds") and testifying to the truth of all regulations revealed by Allah—a faith that reflects in one's thought and then in one's life. Its sign is that one removes oneself from the attribute of rebellious oppressors who recognize no sacred principle and compels oneself to reconsider one's programs.

In Verse 64 of Surah An-Nisa, regarding the forgiveness of sins under the shadow of intercession, we read: "وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ إِذْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ جَاءُوكَ فَاسْتَغْفَرُوا اللَّهَ وَاسْتَغْفَرَ لَهُمُ الرَّسُولُ لَوَجَدُوا اللَّهَ تَوَّاباً رَحِيماً" (And if, when they had wronged themselves, they had come to you and asked forgiveness of Allah and the Messenger had asked forgiveness for them, they would have found Allah Accepting of repentance and Merciful). In this verse, the repentance and seeking of forgiveness by the criminals is considered a prerequisite for the intercession of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family).

And in Verses 98 and 99 of Surah Yusuf: "قَالُوا يَا أَبَانَا اسْتَغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا إِنَّا كُنَّا خَاطِئِينَ قَالَ سَوْفَ أَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكُمْ رَبِّي إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ" (They said: O our father! Ask forgiveness for our sins for us; indeed, we were sinners. He said: I will soon ask my Lord for forgiveness for you; indeed, He is the Forgiving, the Merciful). Here too, the effects of remorse and regret for sin are clearly read in the request of Yusuf's brothers from their father.

Regarding the intercession of the angels (in Surah Ghafir, Verse 7), we read that their seeking of forgiveness and intercession is only for the people of faith and the followers of the Divine path and the followers of truth: "وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا رَبَّنَا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ رَحْمَةً وَعِلْماً فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تَابُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ وَقِهِمْ عَذَابَ الْجَحِيمِ" (And they ask forgiveness for those who have believed: Our Lord, You have encompassed all things in mercy and knowledge, so forgive those who have repented and followed Your way and protect them from the punishment of Hellfire).

Again, the question arises here: with the existence of repentance, following the Divine path, and stepping onto the path of truth, what need is there for intercession? We will answer this in the discussion on the reality of intercession.

Regarding the intercessors, the condition is also mentioned that they must be witnesses to the truth: "إِلَّا مَنْ شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ" (except him who bears witness to the truth) (Az-Zukhruf: 86). Thus, the one being interceded for must establish a type of connection and bond with the intercessor—a bond through attention to the truth and testifying to it in word and deed, which is itself another factor for construction and mobilizing forces in the path of truth.

5- Islamic Hadiths and Intercession

In Islamic narrations, we also see numerous expressions that complement the content of the above verses and are sometimes even more explicit. Among them:

1- In Tafsir al-Burhan, it is narrated from Imam al-Kazim (عليه السلام) from Ali (عليه السلام) that he said: I heard the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) say: "شَفَاعَتِي لِأَهْلِ الْكَبَائِرِ مِنْ أُمَّتِي..." "My intercession is for the perpetrators of major sins from my nation..." The narrator of the hadith, Ibn Abi Umayr, says: I asked Imam al-Kazim (عليه السلام): "How is intercession possible for the perpetrators of major sins while Allah says: 'وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَى'? It is certain that whoever commits major sins is not the object of Allah's pleasure and approval." The Imam (عليه السلام) replied: "Every person of faith who commits a sin will naturally feel remorse, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: 'Remorse for sin is repentance'... and whoever does not feel remorse is not a true believer, and there will be no intercession for him, and his action is 'oppression' (zulm), and Allah says: 'The oppressors have no friend nor intercessor.'" [1] The beginning of the hadith implies that intercession includes perpetrators of major sins. However, the end of the hadith clarifies that the primary condition for the acceptance of intercession is possessing a faith that brings the criminal to the stage of remorse, self-reform, and compensation, and saves him from oppression, rebellion, and law-breaking (pay close attention).

2- In the book al-Kafi, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in a circular letter he wrote to his companions: "مَنْ سَرَّهُ أَنْ يَنْفَعَهُ شَفَاعَةُ الشَّافِعِينَ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ فَلْيَطْلُبْ إِلَى اللَّهِ أَنْ يَرْضَى عَنْهُ" "Whoever is pleased that the intercession of the intercessors should benefit him with Allah, let him seek from Allah that He be pleased with him." The tone of this narration shows that it was issued to correct mistakes that had occurred regarding intercession for some of the Imam's companions specifically and a group of Muslims generally. It explicitly negates intercessions that encourage sin and says: "Whoever wishes to be included in intercession must seek to gain Allah's pleasure." [2]

[1] Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 3, p. 57. [2] Quoted from Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 3, p. 304 (old edition).

3- Again, in another meaningful hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: "إِذَا كَانَ يَوْمُ الْقِيَامَةِ بَعَثَ اللَّهُ الْعَالِمَ وَالْعَابِدَ، فَإِذَا وَقَفَا بَيْنَ يَدَيِ اللَّهِ عَزَّ وَجَلَّ قِيلَ لِلْعَابِدِ انْطَلِقْ إِلَى الْجَنَّةِ، وَقِيلَ لِلْعَالِمِ قِفْ تَشْفَعْ لِلنَّاسِ بِحُسْنِ تَأْدِيبِكَ لَهُمْ" "When the Day of Resurrection comes, Allah will raise the scholar ('alim) and the worshiper ('abid). When they stand before Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, it will be said to the worshiper: 'Proceed to Paradise,' and it will be said to the scholar: 'Wait, and intercede for the people because of your good discipline of them!'" [1] In this hadith, a connection is seen between the "discipline of the scholar" and his intercession for the people "who understood his school," which can cast light on many dark areas of this discussion. Furthermore, restricting intercession to the scholar and negating it for the worshiper is another sign that intercession in Islamic logic is not a contractual matter or nepotism, but rather an educational school and an embodiment of education in this world.

6- The Spiritual Effect of Intercession:

What we have brought regarding the narrations of intercession was a small portion of many, selected for specific points relevant to our discussion; otherwise, the narrations of intercession have reached the level of tawatur (successive transmission). Al-Nawawi al-Shafi'i [2] in his commentary on Sahih Muslim quotes from Qadi 'Iyad, the famous Sunni scholar, who says that intercession is mutawatir. [3]

[1] Al-Ikhtisas by al-Mufid, as quoted in Bihar, Vol. 3, p. 305. [2] His name is Yahya ibn Sharaf, one of the scholars of the 7th century AH, known as al-Nawawi because he was born in the town of Nawa near Damascus. [3] Bihar, Vol. 3, p. 307.

Even the followers of Ibn Taymiyyah (d. 728 AH) and Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab (d. 1206 AH), who have a particular strictness, prejudice, and stubbornness in such matters, have confessed to the tawatur of these narrations! In the book Fath al-Majid, authored by Sheikh Abd al-Rahman ibn Hasan—which is among the most famous books of the Wahhabis and is currently recognized as a textbook in many religious schools in the Hijaz—it is quoted from Ibn al-Qayyim: "The hadiths regarding the intercession of criminals from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) are mutawatir, and his companions and the People of the Sunnah generally have a consensus on this subject, and they consider its denier an innovator, criticize him, and deem him misguided." [1]

Now, before we discuss the social and psychological effects of intercession and examine the four objections in the light of the philosophy of intercession, let us look at its spiritual effects from the perspective of the logic of those who worship God and believe in intercession. This look will clarify the future discussion regarding the social and psychological reactions to this issue. [2]

Among the scholars of Islamic theology, there is a discussion regarding how intercession takes spiritual effect: a group known as the "Wa'idiyyah" (those who believe in the eternal punishment of perpetrators of major sins in Hell) believe that intercession has no effect in erasing the traces of sin; rather, its effect is only in the area of spiritual progress, perfection, and increasing reward and merit. On the other hand, the "Tafdiliyyah" (those who do not believe in the eternal punishment of the companions of major sins) believe that intercession is in the context of sinners, and its effect is the dropping of punishment and penalty.

[1] Fath al-Majid, p. 211. [2] Note that here we are discussing with the specific logic of the scholars of theology.

However, the famous researcher Khwaja Nasir al-Din al-Tusi, in the book Tajrid al-I'tiqadat, considers both to be correct and believes in both effects. Al-Allamah al-Hilli, in his commentary on his words in Kashf al-Murad, also did not deny this belief; rather, he brought evidences for it. We do not think, given what was previously said regarding the meaning of intercession from its linguistic root and its comparison with "cosmic intercession," that any doubt remains that the opinion of the researcher al-Tusi is closer to reality.

For on one hand, in the famous narration from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: "مَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنَ الْأَوَّلِينَ وَالْآخِرِينَ إِلَّا وَهُوَ مُحْتَاجٌ إِلَى شَفَاعَةِ مُحَمَّدٍ (ص) يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ" [1] (There is no one from the first or the last except that he is in need of the intercession of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) on the Day of Resurrection). Thus, even those who have repented of sin and whose crime has been forgiven are still in need of intercession, and this is only possible if the effect of intercession is two-sided and includes the elevation of status. And if we read in some narrations that the righteous do not need intercession, the intent is to negate that type of intercession which pertains to criminals and sinners.

On the other hand, we said: the reality of intercession is the joining of a stronger being to a weaker being to help it. This help may be to increase points of strength and may also be to prune points of weakness. Just as in cosmic intercession and beings that are on the path of perfection and growth, these two aspects are evident: sometimes lower beings need more powerful and superior factors to eliminate destructive factors (like the plant's need for sunlight to eliminate pests) and sometimes to add points of strength and progress (like the plant's need for sunlight to grow and develop). Similarly, a studious student needs a teacher both to correct his mistakes and to increase his various knowledge. Therefore, for various reasons, intercession has a dual effect and is not restricted to erasing the traces of crime and sin (pay close attention).

Given what has been said, it becomes clear why those who repent are also in need of intercession, even though repentance, according to definitive religious beliefs, alone leads to the forgiveness of sin. The reason for this is two things:

  1. Those who repent also need intercession for the increase of spiritual stations, growth, perfection, and elevation of status, even if their need regarding crime and sin has been met through "repentance."

  2. A great mistake that has occurred for many regarding the effect of repentance leads to such objections, which is the imagination that repentance, remorse, and regret for sin can return a person to the state before the sin. While we have said in its proper place that remorse for the past and determination regarding the future is only the first stage of repentance, and it is exactly like a medicine that cuts off a disease. It is obvious that with the cessation of fever and the elimination of the root of the disease, although the patient has recovered and become healthy, he has never returned to the state of a normal person; rather, he must spend a long time strengthening his physical constitution to reach the stage before the illness. In other words, repentance has stages, and remorse for sin and the determination to be pure in the future is only its first stage.

Its final stage is achieved when the repentant person returns in every respect to the spiritual state before the sin, and it is in this stage that the intercession of the intercessors and the bond and connection with them can be effective. A living witness for this statement is what we previously pointed to: in the verses related to seeking forgiveness, we see that in addition to the repentance of the criminal himself, the seeking of forgiveness by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is made a condition for the acceptance of repentance. This is also the case regarding the repentance of Yusuf's brothers and Ya'qub's seeking of forgiveness for them, and most clearly in the case of the angels seeking forgiveness for those who are righteous, good, and reformers, the verses of which were mentioned previously (pay close attention).

7- The Philosophy of Intercession

We have learned the "concept" of intercession and its "evidences"—which clarified its concept—and given that, understanding its "social and psychological" philosophies is not so difficult. Generally, attention to the principle of intercession can have several effects on believers:

"Combating the spirit of despair"—Those who commit heavy crimes suffer on one hand from the pangs of conscience and on the other from despair of forgiveness in the presence of Allah. Since they find the path of return closed to them, they are practically unwilling to make any reconsideration and, given the darkness of the future horizon in their view, they might resort to more rebellion and defiance, granting themselves a kind of freedom of action under the pretext that observing regulations is of no benefit to them—exactly like a patient who has despaired of recovery and has completely broken the barrier of abstinence because he considers it groundless and lacking effect. Sometimes the pangs of conscience resulting from such crimes lead to psychological disorders or stimulate a sense of revenge against the society that has so defiled him. Thus, the sinner turns into a dangerous element and a center of distress for society.

However, faith in intercession opens a window toward light for him, and the hope for forgiveness encourages him to control himself, reconsider, and even compensate for the past. The sense of revenge is not stimulated in him, and psychological peace allows him the possibility of turning into a healthy and righteous element. Therefore, if we say that attention to intercession in its correct sense is a constructive and deterrent factor that can make a righteous individual out of a criminal and sinner, we have not exaggerated. Thus, we observe that even for life prisoners, the window of intercession and pardon is left open in various laws of the world, lest despair and hopelessness turn them into a center of danger within the prisons themselves or cause them to suffer psychological disorders!

8- The "Constructive Conditions of Intercession"

Given that intercession in its correct sense has numerous constraints and conditions in several directions, those who believe in this principle are compelled to provide its conditions to be included in it. They must abstain from sins such as oppression, which reduces the hope of intercession to zero, and begin their program with a deep and comprehensive transformation in their own state. To reach the station of "irtida" and establish the "Divine covenant" (according to the interpretation mentioned), they must repent of sin or at least stand on the threshold of repentance. They must stop or at least reduce wrongdoing and the breaking of the barrier of Divine laws, and keep alive within themselves faith in Allah and the great court of Resurrection and respect His laws and regulations.

On the other hand, to establish a bond between "themselves" and the "intercessor," they must emulate his attributes and establish a kind of affinity—even if weak—between themselves and him. That is, just as in "cosmic intercession," readiness, affinity, and submission to the factors of perfection are conditions for the effect of cosmic causes, in the stage of "legislative intercession" as well, such readiness is necessary to reach the result (pay close attention). With this situation, no room for doubt remains that intercession in its correct sense will have an effective role in transforming the state of criminals and reforming their situation.

9- Analysis and Response to Objections:

As previously stated, there is a great distance between "intercession" in common parlance and "intercession" in Islamic logic. One is based on changing the mind of the "intercessor," and the other is based on various changes and transformations in the "one being interceded for." It is clear that intercession in the first sense attracts all the previous objections: it kills the spirit of effort and striving, and it encourages sin. It is a reflection of backward and feudal societies. And it involves a type of polytheism or deviation from monotheism. For if we believe that Allah's knowledge can be changed and an unknown regarding the "one being interceded for" can be clarified for Him, or that there exists a principle other than Him in the world through which Allah's anger can be extinguished or His love attracted, or if we believe that Allah might need the position of some of His servants and because of this need, He accepts their intercession regarding a criminal, or if we believe He might fear the influence of intermediaries and thus accept their intercession—all of these distance us from the principle of monotheism and the knowledge of God and hurl us into the valley of polytheism and idolatry. These are all the results of intercession in common parlance and its incorrect meaning.

However, intercession in its correct form, with the conditions, quality, characteristics, and features mentioned above, has none of these defects—rather, it is easy to see that it fosters the opposite positive points. This type of intercession does not encourage sin; rather, it is a means for abandoning sin. It does not call for lethargy and laziness; rather, by creating a spirit of hope, it mobilizes human forces to compensate for past errors. It has no connection with backward societies; rather, it is an effective educational means for reforming criminals, sinners, and transgressors. Not only is it not polytheism, but it is the essence of monotheism and attention to Allah and seeking help from His attributes and His permission and command. Again, to further clarify this discussion, we draw your attention to the following analysis:

10- Intercession and Monotheism:

Incorrect interpretations of the issue of intercession have provoked two groups, who are at two completely opposite poles, to oppose this subject. One group has a materialist way of thinking and imagines intercession to be a factor for numbing and extinguishing effort and striving, the response to which was given in detail. The other group consists of some religious extremists like the "Wahhabis" and their like-minded peers, who imagine belief in intercession to be a type of polytheism and deviation from the creed of monotheism. Although laying out their objections and responding to them would lengthen the discussion and take us out of the style of commentary, we deem it necessary for several reasons:

First, it is necessary to pay attention to the fact that the Wahhabis—who in the last two centuries, under the leadership of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, placed the land of Hijaz under the influence of their thoughts—in their sharp and acute beliefs, which are mostly in the area of monotheism, do not only oppose the Shi'a but are also severely opposed to the majority of Sunni Muslims. Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, who took his thoughts from Ibn Taymiyyah (Ahmad ibn Abd al-Halim al-Dimashqi, d. 728), who lived nearly four centuries before him, was in reality the executor of the thoughts and beliefs of Ibn Taymiyyah (the ideologue of Wahhabism).

Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, during the years 1160 to 1206 (the year of his death), with the cooperation of local rulers and by stirring the fires of harsh prejudices among the nomadic and Bedouin tribes of the Hijaz, was able—in the name of defending monotheism and fighting polytheism—to push back his opponents and gain control over the apparatus of government and political leadership, directly and indirectly. In this path, much blood of Muslims from the Hijaz and elsewhere was shed. The struggles of the followers of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab were not limited to the environment of the Hijaz. Rather, in the year 1216 (exactly ten years after the death of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab), his followers launched a surprise attack from the deserts of the Hijaz upon Karbala. Taking advantage of the city being quiet due to the holiday of Eid al-Ghadir and the travel of many residents of Karbala to Najaf for the Ghadir ceremonies, they breached the city walls and proceeded to destroy the shrine of Imam Husayn (عليه السلام) and other sacred Shi'a sites in Karbala. In the process, they took all the precious doors, paintings, exquisite gifts, and decorative items. About fifty people were killed near the sarcophagus, five hundred in the courtyard, and a large number in the city itself, with some estimating the number of slain at five thousand. In this incident, many houses were looted, and even the elderly, children, and women were not spared from this aggression.

In the year 1344, the jurists of Medina who had influence in the government apparatus issued a fatwa for the demolition of all the graves of the greats of Islam in the Hijaz. On the eighth of Shawwal, this decree was executed, and all the graves were destroyed one after another except for the grave of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), which was exempted out of fear of the general wrath of Muslims. On the whole, the followers of this school, like Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab himself, are harsh, inflexible, stubborn, superficial, and prejudiced individuals. More than relying on logic, they employ intensity of action and violence. Knowingly or unknowingly, they have summarized Islamic issues into fighting a few matters like the subject of intercession, visiting graves, and tawassul (recourse), and have practically kept the people away from the important social discussions of Islam, especially those related to social justice, the erasure of the traces of colonialism, and the logical struggle against the dominance of the spirit of materialism and atheistic schools. For this reason, in their intellectual environment, no mention of these issues is raised, and they live in a state of terrifying ignorance regarding the issues of the day.

In any case, regarding the issue of intercession, they say: No one has the right to seek intercession from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and say, for example, "O Muhammad, intercede for me with Allah," because Allah says: "وَأَنَّ الْمَسَاجِدَ لِلَّهِ فَلَا تَدْعُوا مَعَ اللَّهِ أَحَداً" (And that the mosques are for Allah, so do not invoke anyone along with Allah) (Surah Al-Jinn, Verse 18). In the treatise Kashf al-Shubuhat, written by Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, we read: "If someone says: 'We know that Allah granted the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) the station of intercession and by His permission and command he can intercede, so what prevents us from requesting from him what Allah has granted him?' In response, we say: It is true that Allah gave him the station of intercession, but despite this, He forbade us from seeking intercession from him! And He said: 'فَلَا تَدْعُوا مَعَ اللَّهِ أَحَداً'. Furthermore, the station of intercession is not exclusive to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله); the angels and the friends of Allah also have this station. Can we also request intercession from them? If someone says this, he has performed the worship and adoration of the righteous servants of Allah!" [1] Also, the aforementioned has a statement in the treatise Arba' Qawa'id, the summary of which is: Deliverance from polytheism is only possible by recognizing four rules: 1. The disbelievers whom the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) fought acknowledged that Allah is the Creator, Provider, and Disposer of the affairs of the universe, as the Quran says: "قُلْ مَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ... وَمَنْ يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ فَسَيَقُولُونَ اللَّهُ" (Say: Who provides for you from the heaven and the earth... and who disposes the affair? They will say: Allah) (Surah Yunus, Verse 31). Yet this acknowledgment and confession never placed them among the Muslims. 2. They would say: "Our attention to the idols and our worship of them is only for the sake of seeking proximity and intercession," "وَيَقُولُونَ هَؤُلَاءِ شُفَعَاؤُنَا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ" (And they say: These are our intercessors with Allah) (Yunus: 18). 3. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) condemned all those who worshiped other than Allah, whether they worshiped angels, prophets, and the righteous, or those who worshiped trees, stones, the sun, and the moon, and he made no distinction between them. 4. The polytheists of our time are worse in their path of polytheism than the polytheists of the time of Ignorance! For they would worship idols during times of ease, but in intensity and hardship, according to "فَإِذَا رَكِبُوا فِي الْفُلْكِ دَعَوُا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ" (And when they board a ship, they supplicate Allah, sincere to Him in religion) (Surah Al-Ankabut: 65), they would call only upon Allah. But the polytheists of our time resort to other than Allah in both ease and hardship!" [2]

It is strange that this group of Wahhabis, in attributing polytheism to other Muslims and those who do not harmonize with their beliefs—whether Sunni or Shi'a—are so bold...

[1] Kashf al-Shubuhat, as quoted in al-Brahin al-Jaliyyah, p. 17. [2] Treatise Arba' Qawa'id, pp. 24 to 27, as quoted in Kashf al-Irtiyab, p. 163.

...and audacious that they simply deem the blood and property of other Muslims permissible and lawful, and they permit their killing very easily, as they have shown this practically many times throughout their history. Sheikh Sulayman ibn Lahman says in the book al-Hadiyyah al-Saniyyah: "The Book and the Sunnah testify that whoever makes the angels and prophets or (for example) Ibn Abbas and Abu Talib and their likes intermediaries between himself and Allah to intercede for him in the presence of Allah—because they are close to Allah, just as intercession is made before kings—such people are disbelievers and polytheists! And their blood and property are permissible!! Even if they say 'I bear witness that there is no god but Allah and I bear witness that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah,' and even if they pray and fast." [1] The harshness, stubbornness, and obstinacy that drip from this speech are hidden from no one, as are the ignorance and lack of awareness regarding Islamic and Quranic issues.

Analysis regarding the logic of the Wahhabis on the issue of intercession

From what we have quoted from the words of the founder of this sect (Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab), it can be concluded that in attributing polytheism to the proponents of intercession, they actually rely heavily on two points: 1. Comparing the Muslims who support the intercession of prophets and the righteous with the polytheists of the time of Ignorance. 2. The explicit prohibition in the Quran against the worship and adoration of other than Allah and mentioning the names of others alongside the name of Allah, "فَلَا تَدْعُوا مَعَ اللَّهِ أَحَداً" (Surah Al-Jinn, Verse 18), and the idea that requesting intercession is a type of worship.

[1] al-Hadiyyah al-Saniyyah, p. 66, as quoted in al-Brahin al-Jaliyyah, p. 83.

Regarding the first part, it must be said that a great error has been committed in this comparison because:

Firstly, the Quran explicitly establishes the station of intercession (shafa'ah) for a group of the righteous, the pious, the prophets, and the angels, as previously discussed in earlier sections. However, it has made this contingent upon "Divine permission." It is highly illogical and absurd for us to say that Allah has granted someone such a station, yet He has forbidden us from requesting the exercise of this position, even when conditioned upon His permission.

Furthermore, the Quran explicitly mentions the recourse of Joseph’s brothers to their father, as well as the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to the Prophet, requesting him to seek forgiveness for them.

Is this not one of the clearest examples of requesting intercession? Requesting intercession from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) with the phrase "Intercede for us before Allah" is the same as what the brothers of Joseph said: "O our father! Ask forgiveness for our sins for us" (Surah Yusuf, Verse 97).

How can they label as polytheism (shirk) something that the Quran has explicitly permitted, and consider those who believe in it as polytheists whose blood and property are lawful to take?! If this act were polytheism, why did Jacob (عليه السلام) not forbid his sons from doing it?!

Secondly, there is no similarity whatsoever between "idolaters" and "monotheists who believe in intercession by the permission of Allah." This is because idolaters used to worship idols and consider them intercessors, whereas in the case of Muslims who believe in intercession, the issue of worshipping the intercessors is not raised at all. Rather, they only request intercession from them in the presence of Allah, and as we shall explain, requesting intercession has no connection to the issue of worship.

The idolaters were terrified of worshipping the One God and said: "Has he made the gods [all] into one God? Indeed, this is a curious thing" (Surah Sad, Verse 5).

The idolaters placed idols on the same level as Allah in terms of worship and said: "By Allah, we were indeed in manifest error when we equated you with the Lord of the worlds" (Surah Ash-Shu'ara, Verse 98).

As history clearly testifies, the idolaters believed that idols had an influence on their destiny and attributed an independent source of influence to them. In contrast, Muslims who believe in intercession consider all influence to originate from Allah and do not believe in the independent influence of any created being.

Comparing these two with each other is highly ignorant and far from logic.

As for the second point, we must see what "worship" (ibadah) is. If worship is interpreted as any form of humility and respect, it would mean that no one should show humility or respect to anyone else, and no one accepts such a conclusion.

Similarly, if it is interpreted as any kind of request or petition, it would mean that making a request to anyone is polytheism and idolatry; this too is contrary to the necessities of reason and religion.

"Worship" cannot be interpreted as one human following another either, because the logical following of a leader within social organizations and structures is part of the alphabet of human life, just as following the prophets and great leaders is considered an obligatory duty for every religious person.

Therefore, "worship" has a concept distinct from all of these; it is the ultimate degree of humility and submission performed as a sign of absolute dependence, attachment, and unconditional surrender of the "worshipper" (abid) before the "worshipped" (ma'bud).

This word, which shares a common root with the term abd (slave/servant), becomes clear when considering the concept of a servant: in reality, the worshipper demonstrates through his worship that he is in total submission before the Worshipped and considers his destiny to be in His hands. This is exactly what is understood from the word "worship" in both common usage and religious law.

Is there any trace of worship and adoration in this sense found in the request for intercession from the intercessors?

As for "calling upon other than Allah," which has been forbidden in numerous verses, there is no doubt that its meaning is not that, for example, calling someone by their name and saying "O Hasan" or "O Ahmad" is forbidden or polytheism.

There should also be no doubt that calling someone and requesting them to perform a task that is within their power and ability is neither a sin nor polytheism, because cooperation is one of the foundations of social life. All prophets and Imams acted in this manner (even the Wahhabis themselves do not consider this forbidden).

What might be subject to objection is what Ibn Taymiyyah himself addressed in the treatise Ziyarat al-Qubur:

"If a servant asks for a need from other than Allah that can only be fulfilled by Allah, he is a polytheist, like the worshippers of angels and idols of stone and wood, and those who took Christ and his mother as deities—such as saying to a living or dead creature: 'Forgive my sin,' or 'Make me victorious over my enemy,' or 'Heal my sickness!'...

But if it is among the matters that servants are also capable of performing, then there is no obstacle to requesting it from a human, though it has conditions. For a creature's request from another creature is sometimes permissible and sometimes forbidden... The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to Ibn Abbas: 'When you ask, ask of Allah, and when you seek help, seek help from Allah,' to the extent that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) advised a group of his companions never to ask anything of people. They went so far in acting upon this advice that if a whip fell from the hand of one of them (while mounted), he would not tell anyone: 'Give me this whip.' This is the 'disliked' (makruh) request. However, the permissible request is for a person to ask his brother in faith for a prayer (du'a)!" [1]

Therefore, we also say: if someone truly asks for the work of Allah from other than Allah and considers them independent in performing it, he is a polytheist. But if he asks them for intercession—which is their own function granted to them by Allah—it is not only not polytheism, but the very essence of faith and monotheism. The word "with" (ma'a) in the verse "So do not call upon anyone with Allah" is also evidence for this claim: that one must not consider anyone on the same level as Allah as an independent source of influence (reflect on this).

The purpose of insisting and emphasizing this discussion is that the distortion and corruption of the concept of intercession has not only provided an excuse for critics of the religion but has also caused incorrect interpretations and conclusions by some Islamic sects, becoming a factor for division and the scattering of ranks.

While a correct interpretation of intercession, in addition to fostering the moral growth and perfection of society and being a factor for the reform of corrupt individuals, will silence the tongues of ill-wishers and lead to unity of word within the Islamic community.

We hope that all Islamic scholars and thinkers, through a correct Quranic and logical analysis of this issue, will block the path of exploitation for the enemies and assist in the unification of their ranks.

[1]: Ziyarat al-Qubur, p. 152, as quoted in Kashf al-Irtiyab, p. 268.

Ayah

وَإِذْ نَجَّيْنَـٰكُم مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ يُذَبِّحُونَ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ وَيَسْتَحْيُونَ نِسَآءَكُمْ ۚ وَفِى ذَٰلِكُم بَلَآءٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ (٤٩)
[Remember] when We delivered you from Pharaoh’s clan who inflicted a terrible torment on you, and slaughtered your sons and spared your women, and in that there was a great test from your Lord. (49)

Commentary

The Blessing of Freedom

In this verse, the Quran refers to another of the great blessings bestowed upon the Children of Israel, which is the blessing of freedom from the clutches of oppressors—one of the greatest blessings of Allah. It reminds them: "And remember when We delivered you from the people of Pharaoh" (وَ إِذْ نَجَّيْناكُمْ مِنْ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ).

"Those who were constantly afflicting you with a horrible torment" (يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوءَ الْعَذابِ).

"Slaughtering your sons and letting your women live [for female slavery and service]" (يُذَبِّحُونَ أَبْناءَكُمْ وَ يَسْتَحْيُونَ نِساءَكُمْ).

"And in that was a great trial from your Lord" (وَ فِي ذلِكُمْ بَلاءٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ).

The Quran specifically uses the expression "yasumunakum" to visualize the torment of the Pharaohs against the Children of Israel ("yasumuna" is a present tense verb from the root "sawm," which originally means to pursue something; since the present tense usually implies continuity and persistence, we understand that the Children of Israel were under constant torture by the Pharaohs).

They saw with their own eyes their innocent sons being slaughtered, while on the other hand, their daughters were taken into slavery. Furthermore, they themselves were under constant torture, regarded as slaves, servants, and laborers for the Copts and Pharaoh’s entourage.

Importantly, the Quran considers this event a severe and great trial for the Children of Israel (one of the meanings of "bala" is trial), and indeed, enduring all these hardships was a difficult test.

There is also the possibility that "bala" here means punishment, because the Children of Israel previously possessed great power and blessings but were ungrateful, so Allah punished them.

A third possibility in the interpretation of this sentence has been mentioned by some commentators: that "bala" means blessing, meaning that deliverance from the clutches of the Pharaohs was a great blessing for you. [^1]

[^1]: "Bala" originally means wear and tear or exhaustion. Subsequently, testing is called "bala" because everything takes on a worn state due to repeated testing. Grief and sorrow are also called "bala" because they exhaust the human body and soul. Duties and responsibilities are also named "bala" because they weigh heavily upon the human body and soul. Since testing is sometimes through blessings and sometimes through calamities, this word has sometimes appeared with the meaning of blessing and sometimes with the meaning of calamity.

In any case, the day of the deliverance of the Children of Israel from the clutches of the Pharaohs was an important historical day upon which the Quran repeatedly emphasizes. [^1]

The Enslavement of Girls: Then and Now

The Quran calls the sparing of the daughters and the slaughtering of the sons of the Children of Israel a "torment," and considers freedom from this torture as His blessing.

It is as if He wishes to warn that human beings must strive to obtain and preserve their true freedom at any cost.

As Ali عليه السلام refers to this matter in his speech:

"Death is in your life while you are subjugated, and life is in your death while you are victorious." [^2] (To be alive and subjugated is death for you, and death in the way of attaining freedom is life for you).

However, today's world differs from the past in that during that time, Pharaoh, with his specific despotism, took the sons and men from his opposing population and left their daughters free. But in today's world, under other titles, the spirit of manhood is killed in individuals, and daughters fall into the captivity of the lusts of corrupted people.

But why did Pharaoh decide to kill the sons of the Children of Israel and keep their daughters alive? Some commentators believe the origin of this crime was a dream that Pharaoh had. You will read a more accurate answer to this under the commentary of verse 4 of Surah Al-Qasas, where you will learn that the reason for the massacre of the children of Israel was not solely that dream; rather, the Pharaohs' fear of the Children of Israel becoming powerful and taking over the government contributed to this matter.

[^1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 10, page 277.

[^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 51.

Ayah

وَإِذْ فَرَقْنَا بِكُمُ ٱلْبَحْرَ فَأَنجَيْنَـٰكُمْ وَأَغْرَقْنَآ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَأَنتُمْ تَنظُرُونَ (٥٠)
And when We parted the sea with you, and We delivered you and drowned Pharaoh’s clan as you looked on. (50)

Commentary

Salvation from the Clutches of the Pharaohs

Since the previous verse made a brief reference to the salvation of the Children of Israel from the clutches of the Pharaohs, the verse under discussion is, in fact, an explanation of the nature of this salvation, which itself is a sign of the great blessings of the Lord upon the Children of Israel.

It says: "And remember when We parted the sea for you" (وَ إِذْ فَرَقْنا بِكُمُ الْبَحْرَ).

"And We saved you and drowned the people of Pharaoh while you were looking on" (فَأَنْجَيْناكُمْ وَ أَغْرَقْنا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ أَنْتُمْ تَنْظُرُونَ‌).

The story of the drowning of the Pharaohs in the sea and the salvation of the Children of Israel from their clutches has appeared in several Surahs of the Quran, including Surah Al-A'raf verse 136, Al-Anfal verse 54, Al-Isra verse 103, Ash-Shu'ara verses 63 and 66, Az-Zukhruf verse 55, and Ad-Dukhan verse 17 onwards.

In these Surahs, almost all the details of this event are explained; however, in the verse under discussion, only a reference is made in terms of Allah's blessing and grace upon the Children of Israel, in order to encourage them to accept Islam, the new salvific religion [1].

[1]: For more details regarding this event, please refer to Volume 13 under verse 77 onwards of Surah Ta-Ha.

As you will read the detailed account of this event in the aforementioned Surahs, Moses عليه السلام, after extensive preaching and inviting Pharaoh and his people and presenting various miracles—which they refused to accept—was commanded to migrate from Egypt with the Children of Israel in the middle of the night. However, when they reached the edge of the sea (the great Nile River), they suddenly realized that Pharaoh and his army were pursuing them from behind. Anxiety and terror gripped the entire existence of the Children of Israel.

Before them was the sea, and behind them was the powerful army of Pharaoh, which they had no strength to resist. It was at this moment that Moses عليه السلام was commanded to strike the sea with his "staff." Multiple paths opened in the heart of the sea, and the multitude of the Children of Israel crossed the sea in safety. On the other side, the opposing army, which continued to pursue them, all reached the middle of the sea; the waters surged back together, and they all perished.

The dead bodies of Pharaoh's soldiers floated upon the waters, and the multitude of the Children of Israel saw with their own eyes what fate had befallen the enemy.

Both this state of anxiety and terror, and that salvation, are worthy of reflection, so that they may compare the two and give thanks to Allah.

The Quran intends to say to the Jews: Since I showed this much grace toward you and delivered you from that terror and anxiety, why do you show opposition to the Prophet of Islam (My messenger) and his commands?

Furthermore, this verse is a lesson for human beings that if they rely on Allah in life, trust in that inexhaustible Power, and do not cease any effort or struggle in the correct path, Allah will be their helper and supporter in the most difficult moments.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ وَٰعَدْنَا مُوسَىٰٓ أَرْبَعِينَ لَيْلَةً ثُمَّ ٱتَّخَذْتُمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ وَأَنتُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (٥١)
And when We made an appointment with Moses for forty nights, you took up the Calf [for worship] in his absence, and you were wrongdoers. (51)
ثُمَّ عَفَوْنَا عَنكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٥٢)
Then We excused you after that so that you might give thanks. (52)
وَإِذْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْفُرْقَانَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (٥٣)
And when We gave Moses the Book and the Criterion so that you might be guided. (53)
وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنَّكُمْ ظَلَمْتُمْ أَنفُسَكُم بِٱتِّخَاذِكُمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ فَتُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ بَارِئِكُمْ فَٱقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ عِندَ بَارِئِكُمْ فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٥٤)
And [recall] when Moses said to his people, ‘O my people! You have indeed wronged yourselves by taking up the Calf [for worship]. Now turn penitently to your Maker, and slay [the guilty among] your folks. That will be better for you with your Maker.’ Then He turned to you clemently. Indeed, He is the All-clement, the All-merciful. (54)

Commentary

The Greatest Deviation of the Children of Israel

In these four verses, the Quran refers to another part of the eventful history of the Children of Israel, reminding the Jews of shaking memories.

These verses speak of the greatest deviation of the Children of Israel throughout their history—the deviation from the principle of Monotheism (Tawhid) toward polytheism and calf-worship. It warns them that they once fell into such a fate due to the seduction of corruptors; therefore, they must now be vigilant, for the path of pure Monotheism (the path of Islam and the Quran) has been opened to them, and they must not abandon it.

First, It says: "And remember when We appointed for Moses forty nights" (وَ إِذْ واعَدْنا مُوسى‌ أَرْبَعِينَ لَيْلَةً).

When he departed from you, and his thirty-night appointment was extended to forty nights, "you took the calf [for worship] after him, while you were doing wrong to yourselves" (ثُمَّ اتَّخَذْتُمُ الْعِجْلَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ وَ أَنْتُمْ ظالِمُونَ‌).

The details of this event will appear extensively in Surah Al-A'raf from verse 142 onwards, and in Surah Ta-Ha from verse 86 onwards. The summary is as follows: After the Children of Israel were saved from the clutches of the Pharaohs and their drowning in the Nile, Moses was commissioned to go to Mount Tur for thirty nights to receive the Tablets of the Torah. However, to test the people, the period was extended by ten nights. Samiri, a deceitful man, took advantage of this opportunity. Using the gold and jewelry that the Children of Israel had kept from the Pharaohs, he fashioned a calf that produced a specific sound and invited the Children of Israel to worship it.

The vast majority of the Children of Israel joined him. Aaron عليه السلام, the deputy and brother of Moses عليه السلام, remained on the creed of Monotheism with a minority. However, no matter how hard they tried to prevent others from this great deviation, they did not succeed; in fact, they were close to killing Aaron himself.

Upon returning from Mount Tur, Moses was severely distressed by observing this scene and rebuked them harshly. They realized the ugliness of their deed and sought to repent. On behalf of Allah, Moses proposed an unprecedented form of repentance to them, the details of which will come in the following verses.

In the next verse, Allah says: "Then We forgave you after that, so that perhaps you would be grateful for Our blessings" (ثُمَّ عَفَوْنا عَنْكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِکَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ‌).

Continuing this discussion, He says: "And remember when We gave Moses the Book and the Criterion (to distinguish right from wrong), so that you might be guided" (وَ إِذْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ وَ الْفُرْقانَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ‌). "The Book" and "the Criterion" (al-Furqan) may both refer to the Torah. It is also possible that "the Book" refers to the Torah and "the Criterion" refers to the miracles Allah had placed at Moses' disposal (since Furqan originally means something that distinguishes truth from falsehood for a human being). Then, regarding the instruction for repenting from this sin, He says: "And remember when Moses said to his people: 'O my people! You have indeed wronged yourselves by taking the calf [for worship]'" (وَ إِذْ قالَ مُوسى‌ لِقَوْمِهِ يا قَوْمِ إِنَّكُمْ ظَلَمْتُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ بِاتِّخاذِكُمُ الْعِجْلَ‌).

"Now that it is so, repent and turn toward your Creator" (فَتُوبُوا إِلى‌ بارِئِكُمْ‌).
