"Bari" means Creator, and its root meaning is to separate one thing from another, because the Creator separates His creatures from their primary materials and also from one another. This is an indication that the command for this severe repentance is given by the very One who is your Creator.

"Your repentance must be such that you kill one another!" (فَاقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ‌).

"This is better for you in the sight of your Creator" (ذلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ عِنْدَ بارِئِكُمْ‌).

"And following this event, Allah accepted your repentance, for He is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful" (فَتابَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِنَّهُ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ‌).

A Great Sin and an Unprecedented Repentance

There is no doubt that worshipping Samiri's calf was no small matter. A nation that, after witnessing all those signs of God and the miracles of their great Prophet Moses عليه السلام, forgets everything and, with a short absence of their Prophet, completely tramples the fundamental principle of Monotheism and God's religion to become idolaters.

If this issue were not rooted out from their minds forever, a dangerous situation would arise. After every opportunity, especially after the death of Moses عليه السلام, all the signs of his mission might vanish, and the fate of his religion could be entirely jeopardized. Here, severity had to be exercised, and one should never be content with mere regret and the verbal recitation of a formula of repentance. Therefore, a severe command was issued by Allah, the like of which is not found in the entire history of the Prophets: while commanding repentance and a return to Monotheism, a decree for the collective execution of a large group of sinners by their own hands was issued.

This command had to be executed in a specific manner: they themselves had to take up swords and proceed to kill one another, where both being killed is a punishment and killing friends and acquaintances is an ordeal.

According to some narrations, Moses ordered that on a dark night, all those who had worshipped the calf should perform a ritual bath, don shrouds, stand in lines, and set the sword among one another!

It might be imagined: why should this repentance be carried out with such violence? Could Allah not have accepted their repentance without this bloodshed?

The answer to this question becomes clear from the above remarks. The issue of deviating from the principle of Monotheism and leaning toward idolatry was not a simple matter that could be easily overlooked—especially after witnessing all those clear miracles and great blessings of God.

In reality, all the principles of heavenly religions can be summarized in Monotheism and the worship of the One. The shaking of this principle is equivalent to the disappearance of all religious foundations. If the issue of calf-worship were treated lightly, it might have become a tradition for future generations, especially since the Children of Israel, as history testifies, were a stubborn and excuse-seeking people. Therefore, they had to be given such a lesson that its memory would remain throughout all centuries and ages, and no one would ever think of idolatry again. Perhaps the phrase "This is better for you in the sight of your Creator" (ذلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ عِنْدَ بارِئِكُمْ‌) refers to this very meaning.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قُلْتُمْ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّىٰ نَرَى ٱللَّهَ جَهْرَةً فَأَخَذَتْكُمُ ٱلصَّـٰعِقَةُ وَأَنتُمْ تَنظُرُونَ (٥٥)
And when you said, ‘O Moses, we will not believe you until we see Allah visibly.’ Thereupon a thunderbolt seized you as you looked on. (55)
ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَـٰكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَوْتِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٥٦)
Then We raised you up after your death so that you might give thanks. (56)

Commentary

A Strange Request!

These two verses recall another of Allah’s great blessings upon the Children of Israel, illustrating how they were a stubborn and excuse-seeking people, and how a severe Divine punishment overtook them, yet subsequently, Allah’s grace once again encompassed them.

The first verse says: "And remember when you said, 'O Moses, we will never believe you until we see Allah plainly with our own eyes'" (وَ إِذْ قُلْتُمْ يا مُوسى‌ لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى نَرَى اللَّهَ جَهْرَةً).

This request might have been due to their ignorance, as the understanding of ignorant individuals does not transcend their senses; they even wish to see God with their own eyes.

Or, it might have been due to stubbornness and excuse-seeking, which was one of the characteristics of this people.

In any case, they explicitly told Moses: "Until we see God clearly and with these very eyes, we will never believe!"

At this point, there was no choice but for them to witness one of Allah’s creations which they lacked the capacity to behold, so they might realize that the physical eye is too weak to see even many of Allah’s creatures, let alone the Pure Essence of the Lord. A thunderbolt descended and struck the mountain; the blinding flash, the terrifying sound, and the accompanying earthquake plunged them into such terror that they fell lifeless upon the ground.

As the Quran states following the aforementioned sentence: "Then the thunderbolt seized you while you were looking on" (فَأَخَذَتْكُمُ الصَّاعِقَةُ وَ أَنْتُمْ تَنْظُرُونَ‌).

Moses (عليه السلام) was deeply distressed by this event, for the death of seventy of the leaders of the Children of Israel in this incident would provide a significant pretext for the troublemakers among the Children of Israel to make his life dark and miserable. Therefore, he beseeched Allah for their return to life, and his request was granted, as the Quran says in the following verse: "Then We raised you up after your death so that perhaps you might give thanks for Allah’s blessing" (ثُمَّ بَعَثْناكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ مَوْتِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ‌).

What is mentioned briefly in these two verses is explained in more detail in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 155, and Surah An-Nisa, verse 153. [^1] In any case, this story demonstrates the immense difficulties the great Prophets of Allah faced in the path of inviting ignorant and stubborn people. Sometimes they would demand specific miracles, and sometimes they went further, requesting to see God with their physical eyes, categorically stating that until such a request was fulfilled, believing was impossible! And when they were met with a severe reaction from the Lord, a new problem would arise, such that if it were not for Allah’s grace, resisting these excuses would have been impossible.

Furthermore, this verse is among those that indicate the possibility of "Raj'at" (the Return) and returning to life in this world, for its occurrence in one instance is proof of its possibility in other instances.

Some Sunni commentators, because they were inclined not to accept "Raj'at and the return to life," have mentioned a justification for the above verse, saying the meaning is: "After the death of a group of you in the thunderbolt incident, Allah gave you many children and generations so that your lineage would not become extinct!" [^1] However, it is self-evident that this interpretation is completely contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse, as the phrasing of the sentence ثُمَّ بَعَثْناكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ مَوْتِكُمْ ("Then We raised you up after your death") is in no way compatible with this meaning. [^2]

[^1]: For further explanation regarding this extraordinary event and its instructive points, refer to Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 387 onwards.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 1, p. 322.

[^2]: Some commentators—as Alusi has narrated in Ruh al-Ma'ani—have taken "death" here to mean "unconsciousness," saying that the Children of Israel all became unconscious upon witnessing that great thunderbolt, and then regained consciousness by Allah’s command. Others have gone even further in their justifications, interpreting "death" as "ignorance" and "raising" (Ba'th) as "teaching"! However, careful reflection on the expressions of this verse and similar verses in Surah Al-A'raf clearly shows that such justifications are not befitting a truth-seeking commentator.

Ayah

وَظَلَّلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْغَمَامَ وَأَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَنَّ وَٱلسَّلْوَىٰ ۖ كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ ۖ وَمَا ظَلَمُونَا وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٥٧)
We shaded you with clouds, and sent down to you manna and quails [saying]: ‘Eat of the good things We have provided for you.’ And they did not wrong Us, but they used to wrong [only] themselves. (57)

Commentary

Various Blessings

As is understood from the verses of Surah Al-Ma'idah (20, 21, and 22), after the Children of Israel were saved from the clutches of the Pharaohs, Allah commanded them to move toward the Holy Land of Palestine and enter it. However, the Children of Israel did not submit to this command and said: "We will not enter this land until the oppressors (the Amalekites) leave it." They did not stop there, but said to Moses: "Go, you and your Lord, and fight; once you are victorious, we will enter!"

Moses was deeply distressed by these words and complained to the presence of Allah. Ultimately, it was decreed that the Children of Israel would remain wandering in the wilderness (the Sinai Desert) for forty years.

A group of them deeply repented for their actions and turned toward Allah. Once again, Allah included the Children of Israel in His blessings, a portion of which is mentioned in the verse under discussion:

"And We shaded you with clouds" (وَ ظَلَّلْنا عَلَيْكُمُ الْغَمامَ‌).

It is evident how much a traveler, walking in the desert under the heart of the sun from morning until sunset, enjoys a pleasant shade (like the shade of a cloud, which neither restricts the space around a person nor blocks the light or the breeze).

It is true that there is always a possibility of shading clouds existing in these deserts, but the verse clearly states that this matter was not ordinary for the Children of Israel; rather, by the grace of Allah, they frequently benefited from this great blessing.

On the other hand, the travelers of this dry and scorching desert, especially for a long period of forty years, required sufficient food supplies. Allah solved this problem for them as well, as He says in the continuation of the same verse: "And We sent down upon you Manna and Salwa (which were delicious and nourishing foods)" (وَ أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَنَّ وَ السَّلْوى‌).

"Eat of the good things We have provided for you (and do not disobey Allah's command, and be grateful for His blessing)" (كُلُوا مِنْ طَيِّباتِ ما رَزَقْناكُمْ‌).

But again, they did not approach with gratitude: "And they did not wrong Us, but they used to wrong themselves" (وَ ما ظَلَمُونا وَ لكِنْ كانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ‌).

Regarding the interpretation of "Manna and Salwa," we will discuss it in detail in the "Key Points."

Key Points

1- Life in an Atmosphere Free from Bondage

Regardless of how the clouds shaded this people during this period, and what Manna and Salwa were, it is necessary to note that a nation that for years served in weakness, humiliation, and abjectness as will-less slaves in the palaces of the Pharaohs or labored in their fields and gardens, cannot immediately free themselves from all past habits and temperaments to form an independent government based on Divine and revolutionary standards.

Inevitably, this people had to pass through a transitional period to eliminate past intellectual and moral residues and to gain readiness for an honorable future life, whether this period was forty years, or less, or more. If the Quran presents it as a punishment, it is a reformative and awakening punishment, for none of the Divine punishments are for the sake of revenge.

They had to remain for long years in that desert—which, due to their wandering, was named the "Tih" desert—away from any dominance of tyrants, so that a new generation with monotheistic and revolutionary characteristics could be nurtured and prepared to rule over the Holy Lands.

2- What are "Manna" and "Salwa"?

Commentators have said much regarding the interpretation of these two words, and we see no need to mention all of them. It is better to first address their literal meaning, then mention the interpretation that seems clearest and most consistent with the context of the verses:

"Manna" literally, according to some, refers to small droplets like dewdrops that settle on trees and have a sweet taste [^1], or in other words, a type of resin or sap

[^1]: Al-Mufradat by Raghib, under the root m-n-n.

from a tree with a sweet taste, and some have said its taste was sweet combined with sourness.

"Salwa" originally means peace and consolation, and some linguists and many commentators have considered it a type of "bird."

According to a narration from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله who said: "The truffle (kam'ah) is a type of Manna," it becomes clear that "Manna" included edible fungi that grew in that land.

Others have said that "Manna" refers to all those blessings that Allah "bestowed" (minnat) upon the Children of Israel, and "Salwa" refers to all the bounties that were a source of their peace and comfort.

In the Torah, we read that "Manna" was something like coriander seed that fell on the land at night, and the Children of Israel would gather and grind it to make bread, which tasted like oily bread.

Another possibility exists: that due to the beneficial rains that fell by the grace of Allah during the wandering of the Children of Israel in that desert, the trees of that environment produced a special resin and sap, and the Children of Israel utilized it.

Some others have also suggested that "Manna" was a type of natural honey that the Children of Israel reached in reservoirs during their movement in that desert, as on the edges of the Tih desert, there were mountains and rocky areas where many examples of natural honey could be seen.

This interpretation is supported by a commentary written on the Bible (Torah and Gospel), where we read: "The Holy Lands are famous for the abundance of various flowers and blossoms, and for this reason, swarms of bees always settle in the crevices of rocks,

branches of trees, and people's houses, such that even the poorest people can eat honey." [^1] Regarding "Salwa," although some commentators have taken it to mean "honey," almost all other commentators consider it a type of bird that came in abundance from the surroundings to that land, and the Children of Israel used their meat.

In a commentary written by some Christians on the Bible, we see support for this view, where it says: "Know that 'Salwa' (quail) moves in large numbers from Africa toward the north, such that in Capri Island, 16,000 of them were caught in one season... This bird comes by way of the Red Sea, crosses the Gulf of Aqaba and Suez, enters the Sinai Peninsula, and due to the great fatigue and hardship it has endured on the way, it is easily caught by hand; and when it flies, it is usually close to the ground... This part is discussed in the Book of Exodus and the Book of Numbers (of the Torah)." [^2] From this writing, it is also understood that "Salwa" refers to that specific fleshy bird that is similar in size to a pigeon, and this bird is well-known in that land.

Of course, the special grace of Allah toward the Children of Israel during their wandering in the Sinai desert caused this bird to be present in abundance throughout this period so they could utilize it; otherwise, it would normally be difficult for such a blessing to be granted to them.

[^1]: Bible Dictionary, page 612. [^2]: Bible Dictionary (Mr. Hawkes), page 483.

3- Why was the expression "Anzalna" (We sent down) used?

It should be noted that "Anzalna" does not always mean sending down from a physical height, as we read in Surah Az-Zumar, verse 6: "And He sent down (anzala) for you eight pairs of cattle."

It is clear that cattle did not descend from the sky; therefore, "Anzalna" in such cases either means "descent in status," meaning a blessing given from a higher authority to a lower one.

Or it is from the root "Inzal" meaning to host, for sometimes inzal and nuzul (rhyming with rusul) are used in the sense of providing hospitality, as we read in Surah Al-Waqi'ah, verse 93 regarding a group of the people of Hell: "Then a hospitality (nuzul) of scalding water!" And in Surah Al-Imran, verse 198 regarding the people of Paradise: "Abiding therein as a hospitality (nuzul) from Allah."

And since the Children of Israel were in fact the guests of Allah in that land, the expression of "sending down" Manna and Salwa was used for them.

The possibility also exists that "nuzul" here carries its well-known meaning, as these blessings, especially the birds (Salwa), came toward them from above.

4- What is Ghamam?

Some have considered "Ghamam" and "Sahab" to both mean cloud and do not distinguish between them, but some believe that "Ghamam" specifically refers to white-colored clouds, describing it as follows: "Ghamam is a cloud that is cooler

and thinner, while Sahab refers to another group of clouds that are the opposite. 'Ghamam' is originally from the root 'Gham' meaning to cover something, and the reason a cloud is called Ghamam is that it covers the face of the sky. If we call sorrow 'Gham,' it is also because it is as if it places the human heart under its cover." [^1] In any case, this expression might be because while the Children of Israel benefited from the shade of the clouds, sufficient light reached them due to their whiteness, and the sky was not dark and gloomy!

5- At the end of this discussion, it is necessary to mention that some commentators have interpreted "Manna" and "Salwa" in a sense other than what is well-known and famous. As we pointed out, they said "Manna" refers to the absolute kindness and unstinting blessing of Allah, and "Salwa" refers to the causes of peace and consolation of heart, both of which Allah granted to the Children of Israel after their rescue from the clutches of the Pharaohs. [^2] This interpretation, besides being almost contrary to the statements of all Islamic commentators and even the Biblical books, is not consistent with the text of the verse under discussion. For immediately after mentioning Manna and Salwa, the Quran says: "Eat of the good things We have provided for you," as this expression shows that Manna and Salwa were among foodstuffs. This expression appears not only in this verse but identically in verse 160 of Surah Al-A'raf.

[^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani under the discussed verses, and Al-Mufradat by Raghib, root gh-m-m. [^2]: "Partovi az Quran" (A Ray from the Quran), Vol. 1, page 165.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قُلْنَا ٱدْخُلُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةَ فَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمْ رَغَدًا وَٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْبَابَ سُجَّدًا وَقُولُوا۟ حِطَّةٌ نَّغْفِرْ لَكُمْ خَطَـٰيَـٰكُمْ ۚ وَسَنَزِيدُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٥٨)
And when We said, ‘Enter this town, and eat thereof freely whencesoever you wish, and enter while prostrating at the gate, and say, ‘‘Relieve [us of the burden of our sins],’’ so that We may forgive your iniquities and We will soon enhance the virtuous.’ (58)
فَبَدَّلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ قَوْلًا غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى قِيلَ لَهُمْ فَأَنزَلْنَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ رِجْزًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ (٥٩)
But the wrongdoers changed the saying with other than what they were told. So We sent down on those who were wrongdoers a plague from the sky because of the transgressions they used to commit. (59)

Commentary

The Intense Obstinacy of the Children of Israel

Here, we encounter another episode in the life of the Children of Israel related to their entry into the Holy Land.

The first verse says: And remember when We told them, "Enter this city (meaning the Holy Land)" (وَ إِذْ قُلْنَا ادْخُلُوا هذِهِ الْقَرْيَةَ).

Although the word "qaryah" in our everyday language means a village, in the Quran and Arabic linguistics, it refers to any place where people gather, whether they be large cities or villages. What is meant here is Jerusalem (Bayt al-Maqdis) and the lands of the Holy City.

Then it adds: "And eat of its bounties abundantly wherever you wish" (فَكُلُوا مِنْها حَيْثُ شِئْتُمْ رَغَداً).

"And enter the gate (of Jerusalem) with humility and prostration" (وَ ادْخُلُوا الْبابَ سُجَّداً).

And say: "O Allah, shed our sins from us" (وَ قُولُوا حِطَّةٌ).

"...so that We may forgive your errors, and We shall increase the reward of the doers of good" (نَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ خَطاياكُمْ وَ سَنَزِيدُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

It should be noted that "hittah" linguistically means "shedding" or "bringing down," and here it means: "O Allah, we ask of You the shedding of our sins."

Allah commanded them to utter this phrase from the depths of their hearts as a means of repentance for their sins, and He promised them that if they acted upon this command, He would overlook their errors. Perhaps it is for this reason that one of the gates of Jerusalem was named "Bab al-Hittah," as Abu Hayyan al-Andalusi states that the "gate" mentioned in the above verse refers to one of the gates of Jerusalem known as Bab al-Hittah. [^1] At the end, it adds: "For the pure and the doers of good, in addition to the forgiveness of sins, We shall grant an additional reward" (وَ سَنَزِيدُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

[^1]: As narrated in Tafsir al-Kashif under the same verse.

In any case, Allah commanded them to utter this phrase—which was a sign of repentance and a request for pardon—from the bottom of their hearts while showing humility before Him. He promised that if they followed this instruction, He would forgive their sins, and even grant an extra reward to the pure and righteous among them.

However, as we know and are aware of the obstinacy and stubbornness of the Children of Israel, a group of them even refused to say this phrase and instead uttered an inappropriate word in mockery. Therefore, the Quran says:

"But those who did wrong changed the word to something other than what they had been told" (فَبَدَّلَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا قَوْلًا غَيْرَ الَّذِي قِيلَ لَهُمْ).

"So We sent down upon those who did wrong a punishment from the sky because of their defiance and sin" (فَأَنْزَلْنا عَلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا رِجْزاً مِنَ السَّماءِ بِما كانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ).

The word "rijz," as Raghib states in Al-Mufradat, originally means agitation, deviation, and disorder. This expression is specifically used for a camel when it takes short, irregular steps due to weakness and exhaustion.

The great commentator Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan says: "Rijz" in the dialect of the people of Hijaz means "punishment." He narrates a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the plague, where he said:

“It is a 'rijz' (punishment) by which some of the nations before you were punished.” [^1]

From this, it becomes clear why in some narrations, "rijz" in the verse under discussion has been interpreted as a type of "plague" that rapidly spread among the Children of Israel and destroyed a group of them.

It might be asked how the plague can be something that "descends from the sky," but this expression may be because the agent of transmission for the plague microbes among the Children of Israel was contaminated dust that was spread among them by the wind at Allah's command.

Interestingly, one of the painful symptoms of the plague is that those afflicted suffer from agitation and disorder in speech and movement, which is perfectly consistent with the root meaning of the word "rijz."

It is also noteworthy that in the above verse, instead of saying "We sent down upon them," the Quran says: "فَأَنْزَلْنا عَلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا" (We sent down upon those who did wrong), to make it clear that this divine punishment and penalty only afflicted the "wrongdoers" of the Children of Israel, and the innocent were never burned along with the guilty.

Furthermore, at the end of the verse, it mentions the phrase "بِما كانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ" (because of their defiance) to further emphasize that their injustice and defiance were the cause of their punishment.

Given that the phrasing of the aforementioned sentence indicates that they persisted in these evil deeds and continued them, it becomes evident that when sin becomes concentrated in a society as a habit and a state of being, the likelihood of the descent of divine punishment at that time is very high.

[^1]: We have also discussed the meaning of rijz in Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 325.

Ayah

۞ وَإِذِ ٱسْتَسْقَىٰ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦ فَقُلْنَا ٱضْرِب بِّعَصَاكَ ٱلْحَجَرَ ۖ فَٱنفَجَرَتْ مِنْهُ ٱثْنَتَا عَشْرَةَ عَيْنًا ۖ قَدْ عَلِمَ كُلُّ أُنَاسٍ مَّشْرَبَهُمْ ۖ كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ مِن رِّزْقِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ (٦٠)
And when Moses prayed for water for his people, We said, ‘Strike the rock with your staff.’ Thereat twelve fountains gushed forth from it; every tribe came to know its drinking-place. ‘Eat and drink of Allah’s provision, and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption.’ (60)

Commentary

The Gushing of the Spring in the Desert

In this verse, Allah once again refers to another significant blessing He bestowed upon the Children of Israel, saying: "And remember when" Moses (in that dry and scorching desert where the Children of Israel were in severe distress for water) "prayed for water for his people" (وَ إِذِ اسْتَسْقى‌ مُوسى‌ لِقَوْمِهِ‌).

And Allah accepted this request, as the Quran states: "We said, 'Strike the rock with your staff'" (فَقُلْنَا اضْرِبْ بِعَصاكَ الْحَجَرَ).

"Suddenly, water began to gush forth from it, and twelve springs (exactly the number of the tribes of the Children of Israel) flowed from it with speed and intensity" (فَانْفَجَرَتْ مِنْهُ اثْنَتا عَشْرَةَ عَيْناً).

Each of these springs flowed toward a specific tribe, such that the clans and tribes of the Children of Israel "each knew their own drinking place" (قَدْ عَلِمَ كُلُّ أُناسٍ مَشْرَبَهُمْ‌).

Regarding what kind of rock this was, how Moses struck it with his staff, and how the flow of water was realized, much has been said. What the Quran says about this is no more than that Moses struck the rock with his staff, and twelve springs of water flowed from it.

Some commentators have said that this rock was a boulder in a mountainous area overlooking that desert, and the expression "unbajasat" (gushed forth) used in verse 160 of Surah Al-A'raf indicates that the water initially came out of that rock in a small amount and then increased to the point that each of the tribes of the Children of Israel and the animals with them were quenched. It is not surprising for such water to flow from a piece of rock in the mountains, but certainly, all of this was infused with a form of "miracle."

However, as for what a group has said—that this rock was a specific piece of stone that the Children of Israel carried with them, and whenever they needed water, they would place it on the ground and Moses would strike it with his staff and water would flow from it—there is no evidence for this in the verses of the Quran, although it has been mentioned in some narrations.

In the seventeenth chapter of the "Book of Exodus" in the Torah, we also read: "And the Lord said to Moses, 'Go out before the people, and take some of the elders of Israel with you, and take in your hand the staff with which you struck the river, and go. Behold, I will stand before you there on the rock at Horeb; and you shall strike the rock, and water will come out of it, that the people may drink.' And Moses did so in the sight of the elders of Israel." [1] In any case, Allah, on one hand, sent down Manna and Salwa upon them, and on the other hand, provided them with sufficient water, and said to them: "Eat and drink of the provision of Allah, and do not act wickedly on the earth, spreading corruption" (كُلُوا وَ اشْرَبُوا مِنْ رِزْقِ اللَّهِ وَ لا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ‌).

In truth, He reminds them that, at the very least as a sign of gratitude for these great blessings, they should set aside their stubbornness, obstinacy, and harassment of the prophets.

Key Points

1- The Difference Between "Ta'thaw" and "Mufsidin"

"La ta'thaw" is derived from the root "athiya" (rhyming with masya), meaning severe corruption. However, this word is mostly used for moral and spiritual corruption, while the root "ayth," which is similar in meaning, is mostly applied to physical or sensory corruption. Therefore, the phrase "la ta'thaw" conveys the same meaning as "mufsidin" (corruptors), but with greater emphasis and intensity.

It is also possible that the entire phrase refers to the fact that corruption begins from a small point and then expands and intensifies; this is exactly what is understood from the word "ta'thaw." In other words, "mufsidin" refers to the beginning of corruptive programs, and "ta'thaw" refers to their continuation and expansion.

[1]: Exodus, Chapter 17, verses 5 and 6.

2- Extraordinary Events in the Life of the Children of Israel

Some who are not familiar with the logic of miracles have considered the gushing of so much water and these springs from that rock to be far-fetched. However, such matters, which constitute a significant part of the miracles of the prophets—as we have stated in its proper place—are not impossible matters or exceptions to the law of causality. Rather, they are merely "extraordinary" (khariq al-'adah), meaning they are contrary to the cause and effect that we are accustomed to.

Obviously, changing the path of ordinary causes and effects is not difficult at all for Allah, who is the Creator of the heavens, the earth, and the entire universe. For if He had created these causes and effects differently from the first day and we had become accustomed to that, we would have considered the current situation extraordinary and impossible.

In short: the Creator of the universe and the system of cause and effect is the Ruler over it, not ruled by it. Even in our daily lives, exceptional cases in the existing system of cause and effect are not few. In any case, the issue of miracles, whether in the past or the present, does not create any intellectual or scientific problem. [1]

3- The Difference Between "Infajarat" and "Unbajasat"

In the verse under discussion, the expression "infajarat" is used regarding the gushing of water, while in verse 160 of Surah Al-A'raf, "unbajasat" is used instead. The former means a strong flow of water, and the latter means a light and gentle flow.

The second verse might refer to the initial stage of the flow of this water so as not to cause them fear, allowing the Children of Israel to properly harness and control it, while "infajarat" refers to its final stage, which is the intensity of the water's flow.

In Raghib's Mufradat, it is stated that "unbijas" is said when water comes out of a small opening, and "infijar" is said when it pours out from a wide place. This explanation is perfectly consistent with what we said previously.

[1]: For further explanation, refer to the book The Great Leaders (Rahbaran-e Bozorg).

Ayah

وَإِذْ قُلْتُمْ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ لَن نَّصْبِرَ عَلَىٰ طَعَامٍ وَٰحِدٍ فَٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُخْرِجْ لَنَا مِمَّا تُنۢبِتُ ٱلْأَرْضُ مِنۢ بَقْلِهَا وَقِثَّآئِهَا وَفُومِهَا وَعَدَسِهَا وَبَصَلِهَا ۖ قَالَ أَتَسْتَبْدِلُونَ ٱلَّذِى هُوَ أَدْنَىٰ بِٱلَّذِى هُوَ خَيْرٌ ۚ ٱهْبِطُوا۟ مِصْرًا فَإِنَّ لَكُم مَّا سَأَلْتُمْ ۗ وَضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلذِّلَّةُ وَٱلْمَسْكَنَةُ وَبَآءُو بِغَضَبٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوا۟ وَّكَانُوا۟ يَعْتَدُونَ (٦١)
And when you said, ‘O Moses, ‘We will not put up with one kind of food. So invoke your Lord for us, so that He may bring forth for us of that which the earth grows—its greens and cucumbers, its garlic, lentils, and onions.’ He said, ‘Do you seek to replace what is superior with that which is inferior? Go down to any town and you will indeed get what you ask for!’ So they were struck with abasement and poverty, and they earned Allah’s wrath. That, because they would deny the signs of Allah and kill the prophets unjustly. That, because they would disobey and commit transgressions. (61)

Commentary

The Craving for Diverse Foods

Following the description of the numerous blessings that Allah bestowed upon the Children of Israel, the verse under discussion reflects their ingratitude and thanklessness toward these great favors. It demonstrates how stubborn they were—perhaps to a degree unseen in all of history—that a people could receive such immense divine grace yet respond with such defiance and rebellion.

First, it says: "And remember when you said, 'O Moses, we can never endure one [kind of] food'" (Though Manna and Salwa are good and delicious, we desire a variety of foods) (وَ إِذْ قُلْتُمْ يا مُوسى‌ لَنْ نَصْبِرَ عَلى‌ طَعامٍ واحِدٍ).

"So supplicate to your Lord for us that He may bring forth for us of what the earth grows—of its herbs, its cucumbers, its garlic, its lentils, and its onions" (فَادْعُ لَنا رَبَّكَ يُخْرِجْ لَنا مِمَّا تُنْبِتُ الْأَرْضُ مِنْ بَقْلِها وَ قِثَّائِها وَ فُومِها وَ عَدَسِها وَ بَصَلِها).

But Moses said to them: "Would you exchange what is better for what is less?" (قالَ أَ تَسْتَبْدِلُونَ الَّذِي هُوَ أَدْنى‌ بِالَّذِي هُوَ خَيْرٌ).

"Now that this is the case, leave this wilderness and strive to enter a city, for indeed, you will find there what you have asked for" (اهْبِطُوا مِصْراً فَإِنَّ لَكُمْ ما سَأَلْتُمْ‌).

Then the Quran adds: "And humiliation and misery were stamped upon them" (وَ ضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الذِّلَّةُ وَ الْمَسْكَنَةُ).

"And they drew [upon themselves] wrath from Allah" (وَ باؤُ بِغَضَبٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ‌).

"That was because they used to disbelieve in the signs of Allah and kill the prophets unjustly" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كانُوا يَكْفُرُونَ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ وَ يقتُلُونَ النَّبِيِّينَ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِ‌).

And "that was because they disobeyed and used to transgress" (ذلِكَ بِما عَصَوْا وَ كانُوا يَعْتَدُونَ‌).

Key Points

1- Where is meant by "Misr" (Egypt/City) here?

Some commentators believe that "Misr" in this verse refers to the general concept of a "city." That is, you are currently in this wilderness in a program of self-building and trial; this is not a place for diverse foods. Go and enter the cities where all these things exist, but that program of self-purification is not there.

The reason for this view is that the Children of Israel neither requested to return to "Egypt" nor did they ever return there [1]. Others have chosen this same interpretation and added that the meaning is: your staying in the wilderness and consuming this non-diverse food is due to your weakness and lowliness; become strong, fight the enemies, and take the cities of Sham (Levant) and the Holy Land from them so that everything becomes available to you [2].

A third interpretation mentioned for this verse is that it refers to the country of Egypt. That is: if you are consuming non-diverse foods in this wilderness, in exchange, you have faith and are free and independent. If you do not want this, return and become slaves and captives of the Pharaohs or their like once again, so that you may benefit from the leftovers of their tables and their diverse foods. You are after your bellies and eating; you do not think at all that on that day you were slaves and captives, and today you are free and honorable. In reality, if you have a slight deprivation, this is the price of freedom you are paying [3]. However, the first interpretation seems the most appropriate of all.

[1]: Furthermore, the nunnation (tanwin) on "Misran" is evidence of it being an indefinite noun (a city), therefore it cannot refer to the well-known country of Egypt. [2]: Tafsir al-Manar, under the verse. [3]: Tafsir Fi Zilal.

2- Is the desire for variety not part of human nature?

Undoubtedly, variety is a necessity of life and part of human desires. It is completely natural for a person to grow tired of a monotonous diet after a while; this is not a wrong act. So why were the Children of Israel rebuked for requesting variety?

The answer to this question becomes clear by noting one point: in human life, there are truths that form the foundation of his existence, and they should not be sacrificed for eating, sleeping, and diverse pleasures.

Times arise when focusing on these matters distracts man from his primary goal—from faith, purity, piety, and liberty. It is here that he must turn his back on all of them.

The pursuit of variety is, in fact, a great trap set by the colonialists of yesterday and today. By utilizing it, they make free individuals so captive to types of food, clothing, vehicles, and housing that they completely forget their true selves and place the collar of servitude around their necks.

3- Were "Manna" and "Salwa" superior to all other foods?

Undoubtedly, the various plant-based foods that the Children of Israel requested from Moses are valuable foods. However, the issue is that one should not look at life from only one dimension. Is it worthy for a person to submit to captivity just to obtain various food substances?

Furthermore, assuming "Manna" was a type of mountain honey or a similar energizing sugary substance, it is one of the most useful and high-energy foods. The protein substances in fresh meat (like the "Salwa" bird) are superior in some respects to the proteins found in legumes, because the digestion and absorption of the former are very easy, while for the latter, the digestive system must engage in exhausting activity [1].

Additionally, "Fum," which is among the foods requested by the Children of Israel, has been interpreted by some as wheat and by others as garlic. Of course, each of these two substances has its own unique advantages, but some believe the meaning of wheat is more correct, as it is unlikely they would have requested a diet devoid of wheat [2].

4- Why was the stamp of humiliation placed upon the Children of Israel?

From the above verse, it is understood that they were afflicted with abasement and humiliation for two reasons:

One was for their disbelief and disobedience to Allah's commands, and their deviation from monotheism toward polytheism.

The other was that they killed men of truth and the messengers of Allah. This hard-heartedness, cruelty, and disregard for divine laws—indeed, for all human laws, which clearly continues among a group of Jews even today—became the source of that humiliation and misery [3]. Regarding the fate of the Jews and their painful life, we have discussed sufficiently under verse 112 of Surah Al-Imran (Volume 3, page 51).

[1]: The Quran Above the Centuries, page 112. [2]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, under the verse discussed. [3]: Even as I write these lines, the Islamic land of Lebanon is under the horrific and destructive strikes of this cruel people, and thousands of women, children, the elderly, the young, and even hospital patients have tasted the drink of martyrdom in a heart-wrenching manner, their mangled bodies scattered on the ground. Surely, they will pay the atonement for this hard-heartedness in the future.

Ayah

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ وَٱلصَّـٰبِـِٔينَ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٦٢)
Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Christians and the Sabaeans—those of them who have faith in Allah and the Last Day and act righteously—they shall have their reward from their Lord, and they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (62)

Commentary

The General Law of Salvation

Following the discussions related to the Children of Israel, the Quran here points to a general and universal principle, stating: what holds value is reality and truth, not pretension and outward appearance. In the presence of the Great Lord, only pure faith and righteous deeds are accepted. "Indeed, those who believed [in the Prophet of Islam], and those who were Jews, and the Christians, and the Sabians [followers of John, Noah, or Abraham]—whoever believed in Allah and the Last Day and performed righteous deeds—their reward is secure with their Lord" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا وَ النَّصارى‌ وَ الصَّابِئِينَ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ‌).

And consequently, "there shall be no fear upon them, nor shall they grieve" (وَ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ‌).

This verse appears with almost the same wording in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 69, and with more variation in Surah Al-Hajj, verse 17.

A study of the verses following this in Surah Al-Ma'idah shows that the Jews and Christians used to boast that their religion was better than others and considered Paradise to be exclusively theirs.

Perhaps such boasting also existed among a group of Muslims. The verse under discussion states:

Outward faith, especially without performing righteous deeds—whether from Muslims, Jews, Christians, or followers of other religions—is worthless. Only true and pure faith in Allah and the Great Court of Resurrection, accompanied by good works and righteous deeds, has value in the sight of Allah. Only this program brings about reward and attracts tranquility and security.

An Important Question

Some excuse-seekers have used the above verse as a pretext for incorrect ideas, such as "universal peace" (solh-e koll) and the notion that followers of every religion should simply act according to their own faith. They argue that based on this verse, it is not necessary for Jews, Christians, and followers of other religions to accept Islam; it is sufficient as long as they believe in God and the Hereafter and perform righteous deeds.

Answer

We know well that the verses of the Quran interpret one another. The Quran says in Surah Al-Imran, verse 85: "And whoever seeks a religion other than Islam, it will never be accepted from him" (وَ مَنْ يَبْتَغِ غَيْرَ الْإِسْلامِ دِيناً فَلَنْ يُقْبَلَ مِنْهُ‌).

Furthermore, the verses of the Quran are filled with invitations to the Jews, Christians, and followers of other religions toward this new faith. If the aforementioned interpretation were correct, it would be in explicit contradiction with a vast portion of the Quranic verses. Therefore, one must seek the true meaning of the verse.

Two interpretations seem the clearest and most appropriate here:

  1. If the Jews, Christians, and their like were to act upon the content of their own books, they would certainly believe in the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, because the glad tidings of his appearance, along with various descriptions and signs, are mentioned in these Heavenly Books, the details of which will come under the commentary of verse 146 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

For example, the Quran says in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 68: "Say, 'O People of the Scripture, you are standing on nothing until you uphold the Torah, the Gospel, and what has been revealed to you from your Lord'" (قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لَسْتُمْ عَلى‌ شَيْ‌ءٍ حَتَّى تُقِيمُوا التَّوْراةَ وَ الْإِنْجِيلَ وَ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ‌). (And among those things is to believe in the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله whose appearance was promised in your books).

  1. This verse addresses a question that was raised for many Muslims at the beginning of Islam. They were thinking: if the path of truth and salvation is only Islam, then what becomes of our ancestors and fathers? Will they be punished for not witnessing the time of the Prophet of Islam and not believing in him?

At this point, the above verse was revealed, declaring that anyone who, in their own era, believed in the true Prophet and the Heavenly Book of their time and performed righteous deeds is among the saved, and there is no cause for concern.

Therefore, the Jews who were believers and righteous in action before the appearance of Christ are among the saved, just as the Christian believers before the appearance of the Prophet of Islam.

This meaning is also derived from the "circumstances of revelation" (shan-e nuzul) mentioned for the above verse, which we will refer to later.

Key Points

1- The Interesting Story of Salman al-Farsi

It is not out of place to bring here the "circumstances of revelation" mentioned for the interpretation of the above verse, as narrated in the commentary Jami' al-Bayan (Tabari), Volume 1, to complete this explanation. In this commentary, we read:

"Salman" was from Jundishapur. He had a firm and unbreakable friendship with the son of the local governor. One day, they went to the wilderness to hunt. Suddenly, their eyes fell upon a monk who was busy reading a book. They asked him about the book. The monk replied: "It is a book revealed by Allah, in which He commands obedience to Him and forbids disobedience. In this book, adultery and taking people's property unjustly are forbidden; this is the 'Gospel' (Injil) revealed to Jesus Christ."

The monk's words affected their hearts, and after further investigation, they embraced his religion. He instructed them that the meat of sheep slaughtered by the people of that land was forbidden and they should not eat it.

Salman and the governor's son continued to learn religious matters from him daily. A festival day arrived, and the governor organized a banquet, inviting the nobles and elders of the city. He also asked his son to participate, but he refused.

They pressured him greatly, but the son declared that their food was forbidden to him. They asked, "Who gave you this instruction?" He introduced the monk.

The governor summoned the monk and told him: "Since execution is heavy for us and a very bad deed, we will not kill you, but leave our territory!" Salman and his friend met the monk at this time, and a meeting was scheduled at the "Monastery of Mosul." After the monk departed, Salman waited several days for his loyal friend to get ready, but his friend was still busy preparing for the journey. Finally, Salman could not wait any longer and set out alone.

In the Monastery of Mosul, Salman worshipped extensively. The aforementioned monk, who was the head of this monastery, cautioned him against excessive worship lest he become exhausted, but Salman asked: "Is the virtue of abundant worship greater or that of little worship?" He replied: "Of course, more worship has more reward."

After some time, the scholar of the monastery set out for Jerusalem and took Salman with him. There, he instructed Salman to attend the teaching sessions of the Christian scholars held in that mosque daily to acquire knowledge.

One day, he found Salman sad and asked the reason. Salman replied: "All the goodness was the lot of those who passed before, who were in the service of the Prophets of Allah."

The scholar of the monastery gave him the glad tidings that in these very days, a Prophet would appear among the Arab nation who is superior to all Prophets. The scholar added: "I have grown old and do not think I will witness him, but you are young; I hope you will witness him. But know this: this Prophet has signs, including a specific mark on his shoulder; he does not accept charity (sadaqah), but he accepts gifts (hadiyyah)."

On their return to Mosul, due to an unfortunate event, Salman lost the scholar of the monastery in the desert.

Two Arab men from the tribe of Bani Kalb arrived, took Salman captive, placed him on a camel, brought him to Medina, and sold him to a woman from the tribe of "Juhaynah"! Salman and another slave of that woman took turns grazing her flock daily. During this time, Salman collected some money and awaited the mission of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله.

One day, while he was busy grazing the flock, his companion arrived and said: "Do you know that today a person has entered Medina who thinks he is a Prophet and a messenger of Allah?!" Salman told his companion: "Stay here until I return." Salman entered the city, attended the Prophet's gathering, and circled around the Prophet of Islam, waiting for the Prophet's shirt to move aside so he could see the specific mark on his shoulder.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله noticed his desire, moved his garment aside, and Salman found the said mark—the first sign. Then he went to the market, bought a sheep and some bread, and brought it to the Prophet. The Prophet asked: "What is this?" Salman replied: "It is charity." The Prophet said: "I do not need it; give it to the poor Muslims to use."

Salman went to the market again, bought some meat and bread, and brought it to the Messenger of Allah. The Prophet asked: "What is this?" Salman replied: "It is a gift." The Prophet said: "Sit down." The Prophet and all those present ate from that gift. The matter became clear to Salman because he had found all three signs.

In the meantime, Salman spoke about his friends, his companion, and the monks of the Monastery of Mosul, describing their prayers, fasting, faith in the Prophet, and their anticipation of his mission.

Someone among those present said to Salman: "They are the people of Hell!" This statement was hard for Salman to bear, for he was certain that if they had witnessed the Prophet, they would have followed him.

It was here that the verse under discussion was revealed to the Prophet, declaring: those who had true faith in the religions of truth and did not witness the Prophet of Islam will have the reward of the believers.

2- Who are the Sabians?

The famous scholar "Raghib" writes in the book Al-Mufradat: They were a group of the followers of Noah عليه السلام, and mentioning this group alongside the believers, Jews, and Christians is evidence that they were a people adhering to one of the Heavenly religions and believed in Allah and the Resurrection.

The claim by some that they are polytheists and star-worshippers, and by others that they are Magians (Zoroastrians), is not correct, because verse 17 of Surah Al-Hajj mentions "polytheists" and "Magians" alongside "Sabians," saying: "Indeed, those who believed, and those who were Jews, and the Sabians, and the Christians, and the Magians, and those who associated others with Allah..." (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا وَ الصَّابِئِينَ وَ النَّصارى‌ وَ الْمَجُوسَ وَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا ...).

Therefore, the Sabians are certainly distinct from the polytheists and the Magians.

But who are they? There are various opinions among commentators and scholars of religions and sects. There is also a discussion regarding the root of this word (Sabi'in).

"Shahristani" writes in the book Al-Milal wa al-Nihal: "Sabi'ah" is derived from "Saba," because this group deviated from the path of truth and the religion of the Prophets; hence they are called "Sabi'ah."

In Al-Misbah al-Munir by Fayyumi, it is stated: "Saba" means one who has left a religion and embraced another.

In Dehkhoda Dictionary, after confirming that this word is Hebrew, it says: "Sabi'in" is the plural of "Sabi" and is derived from the Hebrew root (S-B-A) meaning to immerse in water (i.e., the Baptizers). When Arabized, the "A" was dropped, and "Mughtasilah" (the Washers), which has long been the name of the followers of this faith in Khuzestan, is a comprehensive and correct translation of the word "Sabi."

Contemporary and modern researchers also consider this word to be Hebrew.

The French Encyclopedia, Volume 4, page 22, considers this term Hebrew and defines it as immersing in water or baptism.

"Gesenius" of Germany says: Although this word is Hebrew, it is possible it is derived from a root meaning "star."

The author of Kashshaf Istilahat al-Funun says: "The Sabians are a sect who worship angels, read the Psalms (Zabur), and face the Qibla."

In the book Al-Tanbih wa al-Ishraf, as quoted in Amthal wa Hikam, page 1666, it is stated: "Before Zoroaster presented the Magian religion to 'Gushtasb' and he accepted it, the people of this land were on the religion of the 'Hunafa' (Haniifs), and they are the Sabians; that is the religion that 'Budhasb' brought during the time of 'Tahmuras'."

The reason for the differences and discussions regarding this group is that due to their small population, their insistence on keeping their faith secret, their prohibition of proselytizing and preaching, and their belief that their religion is exclusive rather than universal—believing their Prophet was sent only for their salvation—their status has become mysterious, and their population is heading toward extinction. This is due to those specific laws, elaborate washings, and long baptisms that must be performed in winter and summer. They consider marriage with those outside their faith forbidden and have strict orders regarding monasticism and avoiding social interaction with women as much as possible, and many of them change their religion due to extensive interaction with Muslims.

3- Beliefs of the Sabians

They believe that the first holy Heavenly books were revealed to Adam, then to Noah, then to Shem, then to "Ram," then to Abraham the Friend, then to Moses, and after him to John, son of Zechariah.

The holy books that are important to them include: 1- Ginza Rabba, which they also call Sidra or the Scrolls of Adam, which discusses the nature of creation and the origin of beings.

2- The book Draša d-Yahya or Sidra d-Yahya, which speaks about the life of Prophet John and his instructions and teachings. They believe this book was revealed and inspired to John by Gabriel.

3- The book Qolasta regarding marriage and wedding ceremonies, and they have many other books which are omitted for the sake of brevity.

As obtained from the above statements and the views of researchers regarding the nature of the followers of this faith, they are the followers of John, son of Zechariah. Currently, nearly five thousand followers of this faith live in Khuzestan (along the Karun River and in Ahvaz, Khorramshahr, Abadan, and Shadegan).

They have attributed their faith to Prophet John, son of Zechariah, whom the Christians call John the Baptist [^1]. However, the author of the book Bulugh al-Arab says: "The Sabians" are one of the great nations,

[^1]: For more information, refer to the book Ara' wa 'Aqa'id-e Bashari.

and the difference of opinion regarding them is proportional to the knowledge individuals have of their faith. As derived from verse 62 of Al-Baqarah, this group is divided into two categories: believers and disbelievers. These are the same people of Abraham the Friend, whom Abraham was commanded to invite. They lived in "Harran," the land of the Sabians, and were of two types: Hanif Sabians and Polytheist Sabians.

Their polytheists respected the stars, the sun, the moon, etc., while a group of them performed prayers and fasting, respected the Kaaba, and performed the Hajj. They considered carrion, blood, pork, and marriage with close relatives (incest) forbidden, just like Muslims. A number of followers of this religion were among the state dignitaries in Baghdad, including "Hilal ibn Muhsin" the Sabian.

They have established the foundation of their religion, in their own estimation, on the basis that one must take the good from each of the world's religions and avoid what is bad. They were called Sabians for this reason—that they turned away from being bound to all the instructions of a single religion...

Therefore, they agree with all religions from one perspective and disagree from another.

The Hanif Sabians aligned themselves with Islam, and their polytheists joined the idol-worshippers.

At the end of the discussion, he mentions again that this group was of two types: Polytheist Sabians and Hanif Sabians, and many debates and discussions took place between the two [^1]. From the sum of the above discussions, it appears that they were originally followers of one of the Divine Prophets, although there is disagreement in identifying the Prophet to whom they link themselves. It also became clear that they are a very small population that is in a state of extinction.

[^1]: Adapted from Bulugh al-Arab, Part 2, pages 228 and 222.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ وَرَفَعْنَا فَوْقَكُمُ ٱلطُّورَ خُذُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكُم بِقُوَّةٍ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ مَا فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (٦٣)
And when We took a pledge from you, and raised the Mount above you, [declaring], ‘Hold on with power to what We have given you and remember that which is in it so that you may be Godwary.’ (63)
ثُمَّ تَوَلَّيْتُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ فَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ لَكُنتُم مِّنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٦٤)
Again you turned away after that; and were it not for Allah’s grace on you and His mercy, you would have surely been among the losers. (64)

Commentary

Take the Signs of Allah with Might!

In these verses, reference is made to the taking of the covenant from the Children of Israel to act upon the contents of the Torah, and their subsequent violation of this covenant:

First, it says: "And remember when We took your covenant" (وَ إِذْ أَخَذْنا مِيثاقَكُمْ‌).

"And We raised the Mount (Tur) above you" (وَ رَفَعْنا فَوْقَكُمُ الطُّورَ).

"And We said: Hold firmly to what We have given you of the Divine signs with strength and might" (خُذُوا ما آتَيْناكُمْ بِقُوَّةٍ).

"And remember well what is in it (and act upon it) so that you may become God-fearing" (وَ اذْكُرُوا ما فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ‌).

"But you turned away after that," and you consigned your covenant to oblivion (ثُمَّ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ‌). "And had it not been for the grace of Allah upon you and His mercy, you would surely have been among the losers" (فَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ رَحْمَتُهُ لَكُنْتُمْ مِنَ الْخاسرِينَ‌).

Key Points

1- The Nature of the Covenant

The covenant referred to here is the same as mentioned in verse 40 of this Surah, and as will appear in verses 83 and 84. The articles of this covenant consisted of: the Oneness of the Lord, kindness to parents, relatives, orphans, and the needy, good speech, establishing prayer, paying the zakat, and refraining from bloodshed—all of which are also stated in the Torah.

From verse 12 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, it is also understood that Allah took a covenant from the Jews to believe in all the Divine Prophets and to support them, and to give charity and spend in the way of Allah. At the end of that same verse, He guarantees them that if they fulfill this covenant, they shall be among the people of Paradise.

2- How the Mountain was Placed Above the Children of Israel

The great commentator of Islam, the late Tabarsi, narrates from "Ibn Zayd" as follows:

When Musa عليه السلام returned from Mount Tur and brought the Torah with him, he announced to his people: "I have brought a Heavenly Book containing religious instructions, the lawful (halal), and the prohibited (haram); instructions that Allah has set as your program of action. Take it and act upon its rulings."

The Jews, under the pretext that the duties brought to them were difficult, began to disobey and rebel. Consequently, Allah commanded the angels to place a massive portion of Mount Tur above their heads.

At this moment, Musa عليه السلام announced: "If you pledge and act upon Allah's commands and repent from your rebellion and defiance, this punishment and penalty will be removed from you; otherwise, you will all perish."

They surrendered, accepted the Torah, and prostrated to Allah, while every moment they expected the mountain to fall upon their heads. However, by the blessing of repentance, this Divine punishment was eventually averted from them.

The same theme appears in verse 93 of Al-Baqarah, 154 of An-Nisa, and 171 of Al-A'raf with slight variations.

It is necessary to mention here that regarding how the mountain was positioned above the Children of Israel, a group of commentators believe that by Allah's command, Tur was uprooted and placed like a canopy over them [1]. Meanwhile, others say: a severe earthquake occurred in the mountain, and it shook so violently that those at the foot of the mountain saw the shadow of its upper parts over them and thought it might fall upon them at any moment. But by Divine grace, the earthquake subsided and the mountain settled in its place [2]. There is also the possibility that a massive piece of the mountain was uprooted by Allah's command due to a severe earthquake and lightning, and it passed over their heads such that for a few moments they saw it above them and imagined it would fall upon them.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan and several other commentaries (under verse 171 of Al-A'raf). [^2]: Al-Manar, under the verse being discussed.

3- What is the Benefit of a Forced Covenant?

In response to this question, some have said: The positioning of the mountain over them was for the purpose of "intimidation" and frightening, not compulsion; otherwise, a forced covenant has no value.

However, it is more accurate to say: There is no obstacle to making rebellious and defiant individuals submit to the truth by threatening them with punishment. This threat and pressure, which is temporary, breaks their pride and forces them into correct thought and reflection, and subsequently, they fulfill their duties with will and choice.

In any case, this covenant was mostly related to its practical aspects; otherwise, belief cannot be changed through coercion.

4- Mount Tur

Regarding whether "Tur" here is a generic noun meaning "mountain" in general or a specific mountain, there are two interpretations: Some have said Tur refers to the well-known mountain that was the site of revelation to Musa, while others have suggested that Tur here carries its literal meaning, the same thing referred to as "Jabal" (mountain) in verse 171 of Surah Al-A'raf (وَ إِذْ نَتَقْنَا الْجَبَلَ فَوْقَهُمْ‌) [1].

5- In the commentary of the phrase "Hold firmly to what We have given you with strength" (خُذُوا ما آتَيْناكُمْ بِقُوَّةٍ), it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he was asked: "Does it mean physical strength or the strength of the heart (spiritual strength)?" The Imam replied: "Both together" [1]. This is an instruction for all followers of Heavenly religions in every age and time: that to preserve these teachings and implement them, they must be equipped with both material forces and spiritual powers.

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi and Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr al-Razi under the verse being discussed. [^1]: [Translator's Note: This reference corresponds to the narration from Imam al-Sadiq mentioned in point 5].

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱعْتَدَوْا۟ مِنكُمْ فِى ٱلسَّبْتِ فَقُلْنَا لَهُمْ كُونُوا۟ قِرَدَةً خَـٰسِـِٔينَ (٦٥)
And certainly you know those of you who violated the Sabbath, whereupon We said to them, ‘Be you spurned apes.’ (65)
فَجَعَلْنَـٰهَا نَكَـٰلًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهَا وَمَا خَلْفَهَا وَمَوْعِظَةً لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (٦٦)
So We made it an exemplary punishment for the present and the succeeding [generations], and an advice to the Godwary. (66)

Commentary

The Rebels of the Sabbath!

These two verses, like the preceding ones, refer to the spirit of rebellion and disobedience prevalent among the Jews and their intense attachment to material matters:

[^1]: Mahasin al-Barqi (as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan under the verse in question).

First, it says: "And you certainly knew those among you who transgressed and sinned on the Sabbath" (وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ الَّذِينَ اعْتَدَوْا مِنْكُمْ فِي السَّبْتِ).

And you also knew that "We said to them: 'Become apes, despised,' and so they became" (فَقُلْنَا لَهُمْ كُونُوا قِرَدَةً خَاسِئِينَ) [^1].

"We made this a deterrent and a lesson for the people of that time and those who followed" (فَجَعَلْنَاهَا نَكَالًا لِمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهَا وَمَا خَلْفَهَا). "And also an admonition for the God-fearing" (وَمَوْعِظَةً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ).

The summary of the story was as follows: "Allah had commanded the Jews to observe the Sabbath (Saturday) as a day of rest. A group of them who lived by the sea were commanded, as a test, not to catch fish on that day. However, as it happened, on Saturdays, many fish would appear on the surface of the water. They resorted to trickery and, through a kind of legal loophole (religious pretext), they caught fish on the Sabbath. Allah punished them for this disobedience and transformed their countenances from human form into animals."

Did this maskh (metamorphosis) and transformation of appearance have a physical aspect or a psychological and moral one?

Furthermore, where did this people live? And what trickery did they resort to in order to catch the fish?

You will read the answers to all these questions and other related issues in Volume 6, under verses 163 to 166 of Surah Al-A'raf (Volume 6, pages 318 to 328).

The phrase "فَقُلْنَا لَهُمْ كُونُوا قِرَدَةً خَاسِئِينَ" (We said to them: 'Become apes, despised') is an allusion to the swiftness of the action, meaning that with a single

[^1]: "Khasi'" is derived from the root khasa'a (on the weight of nasl), meaning expulsion and humiliation. This root was originally used for driving away and pelting a dog with stones, and was later used in a broader sense for any expulsion accompanied by contempt.

divine indication and command, the faces of all those rebels were transformed.

Interestingly, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the meaning of this verse that they said: The meaning of "mā bayna yadayhā" (what was before it) is the generation of that time, and the meaning of "mā khalfahā" (what was behind it) is us Muslims. This means that this lesson was not exclusive to the Children of Israel, but encompasses all human beings [^1].

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse in question.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَن تَذْبَحُوا۟ بَقَرَةً ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَتَتَّخِذُنَا هُزُوًا ۖ قَالَ أَعُوذُ بِٱللَّهِ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (٦٧)
And when Moses said to his people, ‘Allah commands you to slaughter a cow,’ they said, ‘Are you mocking us?’ He said, ‘I seek Allah’s protection lest I should be one of the ignorant!’ (67)
قَالُوا۟ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّن لَّنَا مَا هِىَ ۚ قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ يَقُولُ إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ لَّا فَارِضٌ وَلَا بِكْرٌ عَوَانٌۢ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ فَٱفْعَلُوا۟ مَا تُؤْمَرُونَ (٦٨)
They said, ‘Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what she may be.’ He said, ‘He says, she is a cow, neither old nor young, of a middle age. Now do what you are commanded.’ (68)
قَالُوا۟ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّن لَّنَا مَا لَوْنُهَا ۚ قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ يَقُولُ إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ صَفْرَآءُ فَاقِعٌ لَّوْنُهَا تَسُرُّ ٱلنَّـٰظِرِينَ (٦٩)
They said, ‘Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what her colour may be.’ He said, ‘He says, she is a cow that is yellow, of a bright hue, pleasing to the onlookers.’ (69)
قَالُوا۟ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّن لَّنَا مَا هِىَ إِنَّ ٱلْبَقَرَ تَشَـٰبَهَ عَلَيْنَا وَإِنَّآ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَمُهْتَدُونَ (٧٠)
They said, ‘Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what she may be. Indeed all cows are much alike to us, and if Allah wishes we will surely be guided.’ (70)
قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ يَقُولُ إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ لَّا ذَلُولٌ تُثِيرُ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَلَا تَسْقِى ٱلْحَرْثَ مُسَلَّمَةٌ لَّا شِيَةَ فِيهَا ۚ قَالُوا۟ ٱلْـَٔـٰنَ جِئْتَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ فَذَبَحُوهَا وَمَا كَادُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ (٧١)
He said, ‘He says, She is a cow not broken to till the earth or water the tillage, sound and without blemish.’ They said, ‘Now have you come up with the truth!’ And they slaughtered it, though they were about not to do it. (71)
وَإِذْ قَتَلْتُمْ نَفْسًا فَٱدَّٰرَْٰٔتُمْ فِيهَا ۖ وَٱللَّهُ مُخْرِجٌ مَّا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ (٧٢)
And when you killed a soul, and accused one another about it—and Allah was to expose what you were concealing— (72)
فَقُلْنَا ٱضْرِبُوهُ بِبَعْضِهَا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُحْىِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَيُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٧٣)
We said, ‘Strike him with a piece of it:’ thus does Allah revive the dead, and He shows you His signs so that you may exercise your reason. (73)
ثُمَّ قَسَتْ قُلُوبُكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ فَهِىَ كَٱلْحِجَارَةِ أَوْ أَشَدُّ قَسْوَةً ۚ وَإِنَّ مِنَ ٱلْحِجَارَةِ لَمَا يَتَفَجَّرُ مِنْهُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ وَإِنَّ مِنْهَا لَمَا يَشَّقَّقُ فَيَخْرُجُ مِنْهُ ٱلْمَآءُ ۚ وَإِنَّ مِنْهَا لَمَا يَهْبِطُ مِنْ خَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٧٤)
Then your hearts hardened after that; so they are like stones, or even harder. For there are some stones from which streams gush forth, and there are some of them that split, and water issues from them, and there are some that fall for the fear of Allah. And Allah is not oblivious of what you do. (74)

Commentary

The Story of the Cow of the Children of Israel

In these verses, contrary to what we have read so far in Surah Al-Baqarah regarding the Children of Israel—which were all concise and summarized—a story is presented in detail. This is perhaps because this narrative is mentioned only once in the Quran; furthermore, it contains numerous instructive points that necessitate such an elaboration, including:

The intense excuse-making of the Children of Israel is evident throughout this story, and it also highlights the degree of their faith in the words of Musa (peace be upon him). Most importantly, it serves as a living witness to the possibility of the Resurrection.

The event (as derived from the Quran and commentaries) was that a person from the Children of Israel was mysteriously murdered, while the killer was completely unknown.

A dispute broke out among the tribes and clans of the Children of Israel; each attributed it to another tribe or individuals of another clan and exonerated themselves. They brought the judgment to Musa (peace be upon him) to settle the hostility and requested him to solve the problem. Since solving this case through ordinary means was impossible, and on the other hand, the continuation of this conflict could lead to a great fitna (strife) among the Children of Israel, Musa (peace be upon him), seeking help from the grace of the Lord, proceeded to solve this problem through a miraculous way, as you will read in the commentary of the verses. [^1]

[^1]: It should be noted that in Chapter 21 of the Book of Deuteronomy in the current Torah, a brief reference is made to this story. However, what is in the current Torah is actually in the form of a legal ruling, whereas what appears in the Quran is in the form of an event. In any case, in Chapter 21, verses 1 to 9, we read: "If one be found slain in the land which the Lord thy God giveth thee to possess it, lying in the field, and it be not known who hath slain him: Then thy elders and thy judges shall come forth, and they shall measure the distance unto the cities which are round about him that is slain: And it shall be, that the city which is next unto the slain man, even the elders of that city shall take an heifer, which hath not been wrought with, and which hath not drawn in the yoke; And the elders of that city shall bring down the heifer unto a rough valley, which is neither eared nor sown, and shall strike off the heifer's neck there in the valley: And the priests the sons of Levi shall come near; for them the Lord thy God hath chosen to minister unto Him, and to bless in the name of the Lord; and by their word shall every controversy and every stroke be tried: And all the elders of that city, that are next unto the slain man, shall wash their hands over the heifer that is beheaded in the valley: And they shall answer and say, Our hands have not shed this blood, neither have our eyes seen it. Be merciful, O Lord, unto Thy people Israel, whom Thou hast redeemed, and lay not innocent blood unto Thy people Israel's charge. And the blood shall be forgiven them. So shalt thou put away the guilt of innocent blood from among you, when thou shalt do that which is right in the sight of the Lord." (Old Testament, 1878 edition).

First, He says: "And remember when Musa said to his people: 'Allah commands you to slaughter a cow'" (وَ إِذْ قالَ مُوسى‌ لِقَوْمِهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَنْ تَذْبَحُوا بَقَرَةً).

Out of astonishment, "They said: 'Do you take us in mockery?'" (قالُوا أَ تَتَّخِذُنا هُزُواً).

"Musa said in response to them: 'I seek refuge in Allah from being among the ignorant'" (قالَ أَعُوذُ بِاللَّهِ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ الْجاهِلِينَ‌).

Meaning that mocking and ridiculing is the work of the unwise and ignorant, and a Prophet of God is never like that.

After they became certain that there was no mockery involved and the matter was serious, "They said: 'Now that it is so, call upon your Lord for us to make plain to us what [kind of cow] it should be!'" (قالُوا ادْعُ لَنا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّنْ لَنا ما هِيَ‌). The phrase "call upon your Lord," which was repeated several times in their requests, contains a kind of insolence or a veiled mockery. Did they consider the God of Musa (peace be upon him) to be separate from their own God?

In any case, Musa (peace be upon him) in response to them "said: 'He says it must be a cow neither old and incapacitated, nor a virgin and young, but of middle age between that'" (قالَ إِنَّهُ يَقُولُ إِنَّها بَقَرَةٌ لا فارِضٌ وَ لا بِكْرٌ عَوانٌ بَيْنَ ذلِكَ‌). [^1] And so that they would not prolong the matter further and delay God's command with excuses, he added at the end of his speech: "So do what you are commanded" (فَافْعَلُوا ما تُؤْمَرُونَ‌).

But again, they did not stop their talkativeness and stubbornness and "said: 'Call upon your Lord for us to make plain to us what its color should be!'" (قالُوا ادْعُ لَنا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّنْ لَنا ما لَوْنُها). Musa (peace be upon him) in response "said: 'He says: It should be a yellow cow, bright in its color, pleasing to the observers'" (قالَ إِنَّهُ يَقُولُ إِنَّها بَقَرَةٌ صَفْراءُ فاقِعٌ لَوْنُها تَسُرُّ النَّاظِرِينَ‌). [^2] In short, this cow must be perfectly well-colored and radiant, so beautiful that it strikes the observers with admiration.

And the wonder is that they still did not suffice with this amount, and each time with excuse-making, they made their task more difficult and narrowed the circle of the existence of such a cow.

Again "They said: 'Call upon your Lord for us to make plain to us what [kind of cow] it should be?'" (in terms of the type of work) (قالُوا ادْعُ لَنا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّنْ لَنا ما هِيَ‌). "For indeed, cows are all alike to us" (إِنَّ الْبَقَرَ تَشابَهَ عَلَيْنا).

[^1]: "Farid," as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means an old cow, but some commentators have said it is a cow that has specifically reached a stage of old age where it no longer reproduces; and "Awan" means middle-aged.

[^2]: "Faqi'" means pure and uniform yellow.

"And if Allah wills, we shall be guided!" (وَ إِنَّا إِنْ شاءَ اللَّهُ لَمُهْتَدُونَ‌).

Again "Musa said: 'He says: It is a cow neither trained to plow the earth nor to water the field'" (قالَ إِنَّهُ يَقُولُ إِنَّها بَقَرَةٌ لا ذَلُولٌ تُثِيرُ الْأَرْضَ وَ لا تَسْقِي الْحَرْثَ‌). "And free from any blemish" (مُسَلَّمَةٌ).

And "even having no other color in it" (لا شِيَةَ فِيها).

At this point, when they seemingly had no other question to raise, "They said: 'Now you have brought the truth!'" (قالُوا الْآنَ جِئْتَ بِالْحَقِ‌).

Then they obtained the cow with whatever difficulty it took "and they slaughtered it, though they were near not to do it!" (فَذَبَحُوها وَ ما كادُوا يَفْعَلُونَ‌).

After mentioning the details of this event, the Quran presents it again in a summarized and general way in the next two verses: "And remember when you killed a man, then you disputed concerning the killer, and Allah was to bring forth what you were hiding" (وَ إِذْ قَتَلْتُمْ نَفْساً فَادَّارَأْتُمْ فِيها وَ اللَّهُ مُخْرِجٌ ما كُنْتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ‌).

"Then We said: 'Strike him (the slain man) with a part of it (the cow)'" (so that he may come to life and identify his killer) (فَقُلْنا اضْرِبُوهُ بِبَعْضِها). "Thus Allah gives life to the dead" (كَذلِكَ يُحْيِ اللَّهُ الْمَوْتى‌).

"And He shows you His signs so that you may reason" (وَ يُرِيكُمْ آياتِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

In the last verse under discussion, addressing the issue of the callousness and hard-heartedness of the Children of Israel, it says: "After these events and seeing such signs and miracles and not submitting to them, your hearts became hardened like stones or even harder" (ثُمَّ قَسَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ فَهِيَ كَالْحِجارَةِ أَوْ أَشَدُّ قَسْوَةً). Because "indeed, there are some stones from which rivers gush forth" (وَ إِنَّ مِنَ الْحِجارَةِ لَما يَتَفَجَّرُ مِنْهُ الْأَنْهارُ).

Or at least "some of them split asunder and drops of water trickle from them" (وَ إِنَّ مِنْها لَما يَشَّقَّقُ فَيَخْرُجُ مِنْهُ الْماءُ).

And sometimes "some of them fall down (from the mountaintop) for fear of Allah" (وَ إِنَّ مِنْها لَما يَهْبِطُ مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ‌).

But your hearts are harder than these stones; neither a spring of emotions and knowledge gushes from them, nor do drops of affection trickle from them, nor do they ever throb with the fear of Allah.

And in the final sentence, He says: "And Allah is not unaware of what you do" (وَ مَا اللَّهُ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

And this is a veiled threat to this group of the Children of Israel and all those who continue their path.

Key Points

1- Numerous and Inappropriate Questions

Undoubtedly, "questioning" is the key to solving problems and removing ignorance, but like anything else, if it exceeds its limit and standard, or is done inappropriately, it is a sign of deviation and a cause of harm, as we observed its example in this story.

The Children of Israel were commanded to slaughter a cow; undoubtedly, if it had specific conditions, delaying the explanation from the time of need would not have been possible, and the All-Wise Allah would have stated it at the same moment He commanded them. Therefore, their duty in this regard had no conditions, and thus "cow" (baqarah) is mentioned here as an "indefinite" noun.

But they, indifferent to this established principle, began various questions, perhaps because they wanted the truth to be obscured and the killer not to be identified, so that this dispute would continue among the Children of Israel. The sentence "and they slaughtered it, though they were near not to do it" also points to this meaning; it says: they slaughtered the cow, but they did not want this to happen!

From the end of verse 72 of this same story, it is also inferred that at least a group of them knew the killer and were aware of the actual event, and perhaps this murder had taken place according to a prior conspiracy among them but they were concealing it, for at the end of this verse we read: "And Allah was to bring forth what you were hiding."

Furthermore, stubborn and self-centered individuals are often talkative and full of questions, and they make excuses for everything.

The evidence shows that fundamentally they had neither a complete recognition of Allah nor of the position of Musa (peace be upon him); hence, after all these questions, they said: "Now you have brought the truth!" as if everything before that had been falsehood!

In any case, the more they questioned, the more Allah made their obligation harder, because such individuals deserve such punishment. Therefore, we read in narrations that in every case where Allah has remained silent, do not ask questions, for there was a wisdom in it. Thus, in a narration from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (peace be upon him), it is stated that if they had chosen and slaughtered any cow at the very beginning, it would have sufficed, but "they were strict, so Allah was strict with them." [^1]

2- What was the Purpose of All These Attributes?
