As we said, the obligation of the Children of Israel was initially absolute and unconditional, but their strictness and defiance in performing the duty changed their ruling and made it harder. [^2] Nevertheless, the attributes and conditions mentioned later for this cow might point to a social reality in human life: the Quran seemingly wants to express this point—that the cow which is to play the role of a reviver must not be "dhalul," meaning unconditionally submissive, a beast of burden, or enslaved; likewise, different colors should not be seen in its body, but it must be of one pure color.

By greater reason, those who appear in the role of leadership and reviving society and want to revive dead hearts and thoughts must not become tamed by others; wealth, poverty, and the power of the forceful should not affect their goal. No one but Allah should have a place in their hearts; they should be submissive only to the Truth and committed to religion. No color should be found in their existence except the color of Allah, and it is these individuals...

[^1]: Tafsir al-Ayyashi as quoted from Tafsir al-Mizan under the verse discussed.

[^2]: This story shows that both the abrogation of a ruling before action is permissible based on certain interests, and that abrogation existed in the law of Musa (peace be upon him). It also shows that sometimes their obligation takes the form of a penalty and punishment; these are discussions addressed in their proper place.

...who can attend to people's affairs without anxiety or agitation, solve problems, and revive them.

But a heart that is inclined toward the world and tamed by the world, and this "color" has blemished it—such a person cannot revive dead hearts with this defect and deficiency they have within themselves and cannot play the role of a reviver.

3- What was the Motive for the Murder?

As derived from histories and commentaries, the motive for the murder in the event of the Children of Israel was considered to be wealth or the issue of marriage.

Some commentators believe that one of the wealthy men of the Children of Israel, who had vast wealth and no heir except his cousin, lived a long life. The cousin waited as much as he could for his old uncle to pass away so he could seize his property through inheritance, but it did not happen. Therefore, he decided to kill him.

Finally, he killed him secretly and threw his body in the middle of the road, then began wailing and crying and brought a complaint to Musa (peace be upon him) that "my uncle has been killed!" Other commentators have said that the motive for the murder was that the killer asked to marry his uncle's daughter but was rejected, and the daughter was married to a young man from the pure and righteous of the Children of Israel. The defeated cousin resorted to killing the girl's father, then complained to Musa (peace be upon him) that "my uncle is killed, find his killer!" In any case, this verse might also point to the fact that the source of corruption, murders, and crimes is often two subjects: "wealth" and "sexual promiscuity."

4- Instructive Points of This Story

This amazing story, besides being a proof of the infinite power of the Lord over all things, is also a proof for the issue of the Hereafter. Thus, in verse 73 we read: "Thus Allah gives life to the dead," which refers to the issue of the Resurrection, and "He shows you His signs," which refers to the power and greatness of the Lord.

Furthermore, it shows that if Allah becomes angry with a group, it is not without reason. The Children of Israel, in the expressions they used before Musa (peace be upon him) in this story, showed the utmost audacity toward him and even a lack of etiquette toward the Holy Presence of Allah.

At the beginning, they said: "Do you mock us?" and in this way, they accused the great Prophet of God of mockery.

In several instances, they say: "Call upon your Lord..." Did the God of Musa differ from their God? Even though Musa had explicitly said: "Allah commands you."

In one instance, they say: "If you answer this question, we shall be guided," which implies that your deficient explanation is the cause of [our] misguidance. And at the end of the matter, they say: "Now you have brought the truth!" All these expressions are evidence of their ignorance, self-centeredness, and stubbornness.

Moreover, this story teaches us not to be strict so that Allah does not become strict with us. Additionally, the choice of a cow for slaughtering was perhaps to purge the remnants of calf-worship and idolatry from their minds.

Kindness to the Father

Commentators mention here that this cow was unique in that environment and the Children of Israel bought it at a very exorbitant price.

They say the owner of this cow was a righteous man who showed great respect toward his father. One day, while his father was asleep, a very profitable trade opportunity arose for him, but because he did not want his father to be disturbed, he was not willing to wake him up to take the key to the chest from him; consequently, he passed up the trade.

And according to some commentators: "The seller was willing to sell that item for seventy thousand on the condition that he pay in cash, and paying cash depended on waking the father and taking the keys to the chests from him, but the young man was willing to buy it for eighty thousand but pay the money after the father woke up!" Finally, the trade did not take place.

To compensate for this sacrifice of the young man, Allah provided the aforementioned highly profitable trade for him.

Some commentators also say: After waking up, the father learned of the event and gifted the said cow to his son as a reward for this act, which eventually brought him that vast profit. [^1] The Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) says in this regard: "Look at righteousness, what it does for its people!" [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir Ibn Kathir, Vol. 1.

[^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 88.

Ayahs

۞ أَفَتَطْمَعُونَ أَن يُؤْمِنُوا۟ لَكُمْ وَقَدْ كَانَ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمْ يَسْمَعُونَ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُحَرِّفُونَهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا عَقَلُوهُ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (٧٥)
Are you then eager that they should believe you, though a part of them would hear the word of Allah and then they would distort it after they had understood it, and they knew [what they were doing]? (75)
وَإِذَا لَقُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَا بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ قَالُوٓا۟ أَتُحَدِّثُونَهُم بِمَا فَتَحَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لِيُحَآجُّوكُم بِهِۦ عِندَ رَبِّكُمْ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٧٦)
When they meet the faithful, they say, ‘We believe,’ and when they are alone with one another, they say, ‘Do you recount to them what Allah has revealed to you, so that they may argue with you therewith before your Lord? Do you not exercise your reason?’ (76)
أَوَلَا يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ (٧٧)
Do they not know that Allah knows whatever they hide and whatever they disclose? (77)

Occasion of Revelation

Some commentators have narrated the following from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding the occasion of revelation for the last two verses:

"A group of Jews, who held no enmity toward the truth, would inform the Muslims of what was mentioned in the Torah regarding the attributes of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله whenever they met them. The leaders of the Jews became aware of this and forbade them from doing so, saying: 'Do not recount the attributes of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله mentioned in the Torah to them, lest they use it as an argument against you before God.' Consequently, the above verses were revealed as a response to them." [1]

Commentary

Unjustified Expectation!

In these verses, as you observe, the Quran leaves the narrative of the Children of Israel and addresses the Muslims, drawing an instructive conclusion. It says: "How do you expect this people to believe in the commands of your religion, while a group of them used to hear the words of Allah and then, after understanding it, would distort it while they knew exactly what they were doing?" (أَ فَتَطْمَعُونَ أَنْ يُؤْمِنُوا لَكُمْ وَ قَدْ كانَ فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ يَسْمَعُونَ كَلامَ اللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُحَرِّفُونَهُ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما عَقَلُوهُ وَ هُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ).

Therefore, if you see that they do not submit to the vivid explanations of the Quran and the miracles of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, do not be concerned. These are the descendants of the same people who went to Mount Sinai as the chosen ones of the nation along with Moses, heard the words of Allah, and understood His commands, yet upon their return, they distorted them.

From the phrase "وَ قَدْ كانَ فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ..." (while a group of them...), it is understood that not all of them were distorters; rather, this was the act of a specific group that perhaps constituted the majority.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses being discussed.

In "Asbab al-Nuzul," it is mentioned that a group of Jews, upon returning from Mount Sinai, told the people: "We heard Allah command Moses: 'Perform My commands where you are able, and where you are unable, leave them!'" And this was the first distortion.

In any case, at the beginning of the Prophet of Islam's mission, it was expected that the Jewish people would be the first to respond to the call of Islam, as they were the People of the Book (unlike the polytheists). Furthermore, they had read the attributes of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله in their own books. However, the Quran says: Given their bad track record, your expectation is misplaced, for sometimes the deviant traits and spirit of a group cause them to remain far from the truth despite being so close to it.

The next verse pulls back the curtain on another bitter reality regarding this deceitful and hypocritical group, saying: "The pure-hearted among them, when they meet the believers, declare their faith" (and inform them of the Prophet's attributes mentioned in their books) (وَ إِذا لَقُوا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا قالُوا آمَنَّا). "But in private and seclusion, some of them say to others: 'Why do you tell the Muslims what Allah has revealed to you in the Torah?'" (وَ إِذا خَلا بَعْضُهُمْ إِلى‌ بَعْضٍ قالُوا أَ تُحَدِّثُونَهُمْ بِما فَتَحَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ).

"So that they may use it to argue against you before your Lord on the Day of Resurrection? Do you not understand?" (لِيُحَاجُّوكُمْ بِهِ عِنْدَ رَبِّكُمْ أَ فَلا تَعْقِلُونَ).

There is also this possibility in the interpretation of the verse: that the beginning of the verse speaks of the Jewish hypocrites who claimed faith in the presence of Muslims but denied it in their absence, and even rebuked the pure-hearted Jews for why they had placed the secrets of the Holy Books at the disposal of the Muslims.

In any case, this is a confirmation of what was in the previous verse: that you should not expect much faith from a population dominated by such a spirit.

The phrase "فَتَحَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ" (what Allah has opened/revealed to you) may mean the divine decree and command that was at the disposal of the Children of Israel, or it may refer to the opening of the doors of divine secrets and future news regarding the new Sharia to them.

It is noteworthy that it is clearly understood from this verse that the faith of this hypocritical group regarding Allah was so weak that they imagined Him to be like ordinary humans, thinking that if they concealed a truth from the Muslims, it would remain concealed from Allah as well!

Therefore, the next verse explicitly states: "Do they not know that Allah knows what they keep secret and what they make known?" (أَ وَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ ما يُسِرُّونَ وَ ما يُعْلِنُونَ).

Ayahs

وَمِنْهُمْ أُمِّيُّونَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ إِلَّآ أَمَانِىَّ وَإِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ (٧٨)
And among them are the illiterate who know nothing of the Book except hearsay, and they only make conjectures. (78)
فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يَكْتُبُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ ثُمَّ يَقُولُونَ هَـٰذَا مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ لِيَشْتَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۖ فَوَيْلٌ لَّهُم مِّمَّا كَتَبَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَوَيْلٌ لَّهُم مِّمَّا يَكْسِبُونَ (٧٩)
So woe to them who write the Book with their hands and then say, ‘This is from Allah,’ that they may sell it for a paltry gain. So woe to them for what their hands have written, and woe to them for what they earn! (79)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of Jewish scholars altered the descriptions of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that were mentioned in the Torah. This alteration was done to preserve their own status and the benefits they received annually from the common people.

When the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was commissioned and they saw his attributes matching what was in the Torah, they feared that if this reality became clear, their interests would be endangered. Therefore, instead of the actual attributes mentioned in the Torah, they wrote qualities contrary to them.

The common Jewish people, who had more or less heard his true attributes until then, would ask their scholars: "Is this not the same promised Prophet whose appearance you used to give glad tidings of?"

The scholars would read the distorted verses of the Torah to them to convince them otherwise. [^1]

Commentary

The Jewish Plots to Exploit the Masses!

Following the previous verses regarding the wrongdoings of the Jews, the verses under discussion divide their population into two distinct groups: the "commoners" and the "deceitful scholars" (of course, there was a minority of their scholars who believed, accepted the truth, and joined the ranks of the Muslims).

It says: "And among them are unlettered ones who do not know the Scripture except [as] wishful thinking, and they are only assuming" (وَ مِنْهُمْ أُمِّيُّونَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ الْكِتابَ إِلَّا أَمانِيَّ وَ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ‌).

"Ummiyyun" is the plural of "Ummi," which here means "uneducated"—one who remains in the state they were born from their mother, having seen no school or teacher. Alternatively, it refers to those whose mothers, out of ignorant affection, would not separate their children from themselves and did not allow them to go to school. [^2] "Amani" is the plural of "umniyyah," meaning a wish or desire. Here, it may refer to the delusions and imaginary privileges that the Jews claimed for themselves, such as:

[^1]: This occasion of revelation is mentioned briefly in Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed, and in detail under other relevant verses.

[^2]: The meaning of "Ummi" is discussed more extensively in Volume 6, page 396 (under verse 157 of Al-A'raf).

They would say: "We are the children of Allah and His beloved ones" (نَحْنُ أَبْناءُ اللَّهِ وَ أَحِبَّاؤُهُ‌ - Al-Ma'idah: 18), or they would say: "The Fire shall never touch us except for a few days," which we will encounter in the subsequent verses regarding the speech of the Jews.

There is also the possibility that "amani" refers to the distorted verses that the Jewish scholars provided to the commoners, and the phrase "they do not know the Scripture except [as] wishful thinking" is more consistent with this meaning.

In any case, the end of this verse (إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ‌) is evidence that following conjecture and guesswork in the foundations and principles of religion and the recognition of the school of revelation is incorrect and blameworthy; everyone must proceed in this area based on sufficient investigation.

The other group was their scholars who distorted the truths for their own benefit, as the Quran says in the next verse: "So woe to those who write the 'scripture' with their own hands, then say, 'This is from Allah'" (فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ يَكْتُبُونَ الْكِتابَ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ ثُمَّ يَقُولُونَ هذا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ‌). "And their goal is to get for it a small price" (لِيَشْتَرُوا بِهِ ثَمَناً قَلِيلًا).

"So woe to them for what their hands have written" (فَوَيْلٌ لَهُمْ مِمَّا كَتَبَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ‌).

"And woe to them for what they earn [through these betrayals]" (وَ وَيْلٌ لَهُمْ مِمَّا يَكْسِبُونَ‌).

From the final phrases of this verse, it is clearly understood that they employed both unholy means and obtained improper results.

Some commentators have narrated a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام under the verse discussed, which contains noteworthy points. The hadith is as follows:

"A man said to Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: 'Since the common Jews had no knowledge of their Heavenly Book except through their scholars, how does Allah blame them for imitating their scholars and accepting from them?! (referring to the verses under discussion). Are the common Jews different from our common people who follow their own scholars?'...

The Imam replied: 'Between our commoners and the Jewish commoners, there is a difference in one respect and equality in another. In the respect where they are equal, Allah has blamed our commoners just as He rebuked the Jewish commoners.

But as for how they differ: the Jewish commoners were aware of the condition of their scholars; they knew that they lied explicitly, consumed the forbidden and bribes, and changed the rulings of Allah. They realized through their innate nature (fitrah) that such people are transgressors (fasiq) and it is not permissible to accept their words regarding Allah and His rulings, nor is it proper to accept their testimony regarding the Prophets. For this reason, Allah rebuked them (whereas our commoners do not follow such scholars).

And if our commoners see manifest transgression, intense prejudice, and greed for the world and forbidden wealth in their scholars, whoever follows them is like the Jews whom Allah rebuked for following transgressing scholars.

فاما من كان من الفقهاء صائنا لنفسه، حافظا لدينه، مخالفا على هواه، مطيعا لامر مولاه فللعوام ان يقلدوه:

"But as for the scholars who protect their souls, guard their religion, oppose their whims, and are obedient to the command of their Master, then the commoners may follow them...'" [^1] It is clear that this hadith does not refer to devotional imitation (taqlid) in practical rulings, but rather refers to following the guidance of scholars to obtain knowledge and certainty in the principles of religion (usul al-din), because the hadith speaks about the recognition of the Prophet, which is certainly among the principles of religion where devotional imitation is not permissible.

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 18, p. 94 (Book of Judgment, Chapter 10; Tafsir al-Safi under the verses discussed).

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ لَن تَمَسَّنَا ٱلنَّارُ إِلَّآ أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَةً ۚ قُلْ أَتَّخَذْتُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ عَهْدًا فَلَن يُخْلِفَ ٱللَّهُ عَهْدَهُۥٓ ۖ أَمْ تَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٨٠)
And they say, ‘The Fire shall not touch us except for a number of days.’ Say, ‘Have you taken a promise from Allah? If so, Allah will never break His promise. Do you ascribe to Allah what you do not know?’ (80)
بَلَىٰ مَن كَسَبَ سَيِّئَةً وَأَحَـٰطَتْ بِهِۦ خَطِيٓـَٔتُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٨١)
Certainly whoever commits misdeeds and is besieged by his iniquity—such shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they will remain in it [forever]. (81)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٨٢)
And those who have faith and do righteous deeds—they shall be the inhabitants of paradise; they will remain in it [forever]. (82)

Commentary

Ambition and Empty Claims!

The Quran here refers to one of the baseless claims of the Jews which had made them arrogant and became the source of a portion of their deviations, and responds to it:

First, It says: "And they say, 'The Fire shall never touch us except for a few numbered days'" (وَ قالُوا لَنْ تَمَسَّنَا النَّارُ إِلَّا أَيَّاماً مَعْدُودَةً).

"Say, 'Have you taken a covenant with Allah, for Allah will never break His covenant, or do you attribute to Allah that which you do not know?'" (قُلْ أَتَّخَذْتُمْ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ عَهْداً فَلَنْ يُخْلِفَ اللَّهُ عَهْدَهُ أَمْ تَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌).

The belief in the racial superiority of the Jewish nation, and that they are a distinct entity, and that their sinners will only face punishment and retribution for a few days, after which God's Paradise will be at their disposal forever, is one of the clear proofs of the selfishness and self-worship of this group.

This seeking of privilege is not compatible with any logic, because there is no difference among human beings regarding the punishment and reward of deeds before Allah.

What had the Jews done that a proviso should be added to the general law of punishment in their favor?

In any case, the above verse refutes this false notion with a logical statement and says that your claim is one of two cases: either you must have taken a specific covenant from Allah in this regard—which you have not—or you are attributing lies and slanders to Allah!

The next verse expresses a general and universal law that is logical from every perspective; It says: "Yes, whoever earns evil and his sin encompasses him—those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally" (بَلى‌ مَنْ كَسَبَ سَيِّئَةً وَ أَحاطَتْ بِهِ خَطِيئَتُهُ فَأُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ‌).

This is a general law regarding sinners from every nation, race, group, and community.

And as for the pious believers, there is also a general and universal law which the next verse expresses: "And those who believe and do righteous deeds—those are the companions of Paradise; they will abide therein eternally" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ‌).

Key Points

1- Earning "Evil" (Sayyi'ah)

"Kasb" and "Iktisab" mean to acquire something out of will and choice. Therefore, the phrase "Yes, whoever earns evil" (بَلى‌ مَنْ كَسَبَ سَيِّئَةً) refers to those who commit sins with knowledge and volition. The expression "earning" (kasb) is perhaps from the perspective that the sinner, in a short-sighted calculation, imagines committing the sin to be to his benefit and abandoning it to be to his detriment. These are the same people referred to a few verses later, saying: they have purchased the life of this world at the price of the Hereafter, so there is no reduction in their punishment.

2- What is the Encompassing of Sin?

"Khati'ah" (sin/error) in many cases means sins that were not committed intentionally, but in the verse under discussion, it means a major sin [^1] or the effects of sin [^2] that settle upon the heart and soul of man.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr Razi, under the verse. [^2]: Tafsir al-Mizan, under the verse.

In any case, the concept of the "encompassing of sin" is that a person sinks so deep into sins that he builds a prison for himself—a prison whose vents are closed.

To explain: small and large sins are initially an "action," then they turn into a "state," and with continuation and persistence, they take the form of a "habit" (malakah). When it reaches its peak intensity, it colors the entire existence of the person with the color of sin and becomes the very essence of the person's being.

At this stage, no advice, sermon, or guidance from guides will affect him, and in reality, he has transformed his own nature by his own hand.

From one perspective, such people are like silkworms that weave a cocoon around themselves—a cocoon that imprisons them and eventually suffocates them. It is clear that there will be no fate for them except eternal residence in the Fire.

And considering the verses that say Allah only does not forgive the polytheists (mushrikin) but anything other than polytheism is forgivable (Indeed, Allah does not forgive that partners be associated with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills—An-Nisa, 48), and considering that the verses under discussion speak of eternity (khulud) in the Fire, it can be concluded that such sinners eventually lose the jewel of faith and depart from this world as polytheists and faithless!

3- Jewish Racism

From the verses under discussion, it is understood that the spirit of Jewish racial discrimination, which has become the source of many miseries in the world today, existed among the Jews since that time. They believed in imaginary privileges for the race of the Children of Israel, and unfortunately, after thousands of years, that spirit still dominates them. In fact, the origin of the emergence of the usurping state of Israel is this very spirit of racism.

They not only claim superiority for themselves in this world but also believe that this racial privilege will rush to their aid in the Hereafter, and their sinners, unlike other individuals, will only see a short-term and light punishment. These very false notions have contaminated them with all kinds of crimes, miseries, and misfortunes! [^1]

[^1]: Under verse 122 of Surah An-Nisa, we have also provided a discussion titled "False Privileges" in this regard (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 4, page 140 onwards).

Ayahs

وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ لَا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَانًا وَذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَقُولُوا۟ لِلنَّاسِ حُسْنًا وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ ثُمَّ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنكُمْ وَأَنتُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٨٣)
When We took a pledge from the Children of Israel, [saying]: ‘Worship no one but Allah, do good to your parents, relatives, orphans, and the needy, speak kindly to people, maintain the prayer, and give the zakat,’ you turned away, except a few of you, and you were disregardful. (83)
وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ لَا تَسْفِكُونَ دِمَآءَكُمْ وَلَا تُخْرِجُونَ أَنفُسَكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَنتُمْ تَشْهَدُونَ (٨٤)
And when We took a pledge from you, [saying]: ‘You shall not shed your [own people’s] blood, and you shall not expel your folks from your homes,’ you pledged, and you testify [to this pledge of your ancestors]. (84)
ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ تَقْتُلُونَ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَتُخْرِجُونَ فَرِيقًا مِّنكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ تَظَـٰهَرُونَ عَلَيْهِم بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ وَإِن يَأْتُوكُمْ أُسَـٰرَىٰ تُفَـٰدُوهُمْ وَهُوَ مُحَرَّمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ إِخْرَاجُهُمْ ۚ أَفَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِبَعْضِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَتَكْفُرُونَ بِبَعْضٍ ۚ فَمَا جَزَآءُ مَن يَفْعَلُ ذَٰلِكَ مِنكُمْ إِلَّا خِزْىٌ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰٓ أَشَدِّ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٨٥)
Then there you were, killing your folks and expelling a part of your folks from their homes, backing one another against them in sin and aggression! And if they came to you as captives, you would ransom them, though their expulsion itself was forbidden you. What! Do you believe in part of the Book and deny another part? So what is the requital of those of you who do that except disgrace in the life of this world? And on the Day of Resurrection, they shall be consigned to the severest punishment. And Allah is not oblivious of what you do. (85)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ فَلَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٨٦)
They are the ones who bought the life of this world for the Hereafter; so their punishment shall not be lightened, nor will they be helped. (86)

Commentary

The Covenant-Breakers!

In the preceding verses, mention was made of the covenant of the Children of Israel, but no details were provided. In the verses under discussion, Allah recounts the articles of this covenant. Most of these articles—if not all—are matters that must be considered part of the fundamental principles and constant laws of divine religions, for these covenants and commands exist in some form in all heavenly religions.

In these verses, the Holy Quran severely rebukes the Jews: why did they break these covenants? It threatens them with disgrace in this world and severe punishment in the hereafter for this breach of contract.

In this covenant, which the Children of Israel themselves witnessed and confessed to, the following points are mentioned:

  1. Monotheism and the worship of the One God, as the first verse states: "And remember when We took a covenant from the Children of Israel, [saying], 'Do not worship except Allah (the One God), (and do not prostrate in submission before any idol)'" (وَ إِذْ أَخَذْنا مِيثاقَ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ لا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ‌).

  2. "And to parents do good" (وَ بِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً).

  3. "And to relatives, orphans, and the needy [behave with goodness]" (وَ ذِي الْقُرْبى‌ وَ الْيَتامى‌ وَ الْمَساكِينِ‌).

  4. "And speak to people good words" (وَ قُولُوا لِلنَّاسِ حُسْناً).

  5. "Establish prayer" (and be mindful of God at all times) (وَ أَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ).

  6. "And do not fall short in giving zakat and the rights of the deprived" (وَ آتُوا الزَّكاةَ).

"But you turned away, except for a few of you, and you were averse [to fulfilling your covenant]" (ثُمَّ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِنْكُمْ وَ أَنْتُمْ مُعْرِضُونَ‌).

  1. And remember "when We took your covenant, [saying], 'Do not shed each other's blood'" (وَ إِذْ أَخَذْنا مِيثاقَكُمْ لا تَسْفِكُونَ دِماءَكُمْ‌).

  2. "And do not evict one another from your homes and dwellings" (وَ لا تُخْرِجُونَ أَنْفُسَكُمْ مِنْ ديارِكُمْ‌).

  3. If someone among you is taken captive during war, all should help for his freedom, pay the ransom, and set him free (this article of the covenant is inferred from the phrase "Do you believe in part of the Scripture and disbelieve in part?" which will follow).

"Then you acknowledged [this] while you were witnessing" (ثُمَّ أَقْرَرْتُمْ وَ أَنْتُمْ تَشْهَدُونَ‌).

But you trampled upon many articles of this divine covenant: "Then, you are those [same ones who are] killing one another and evicting a party of your people from their homes" (ثُمَّ أَنْتُمْ هؤُلاءِ تَقْتُلُونَ أَنْفُسَكُمْ وَ تُخْرِجُونَ فَرِيقاً مِنْكُمْ مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ‌). "And you support one another against them in sin and aggression" (تَظاهَرُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِالْإِثْمِ وَ الْعُدْوانِ‌).

All of this was contrary to the covenant you had made with God.

"But in the midst of this, when some of them come to you as captives, you ransom them [and set them free]" (وَ إِنْ يَأْتُوكُمْ أُسارى‌ تُفادُوهُمْ‌).

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 1, p. 330

"While their eviction [from their homes] was forbidden to you from the beginning" (وَ هُوَ مُحَرَّمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ إِخْراجُهُمْ‌).

And how strange it is that in paying the ransom and freeing the captives, you cite the ruling of the Torah and the divine covenant: "So do you believe in part of the Scripture and disbelieve in part?" (أَ فَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِبَعْضِ الْكِتابِ وَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِبَعْضٍ‌).

"What is the recompense of those who do that among you except disgrace in worldly life" (فَما جَزاءُ مَنْ يَفْعَلُ ذلِكَ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا خِزْيٌ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا) [^1] "and on the Day of Resurrection they will be sent back to the severest of punishment" (وَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يُرَدُّونَ إِلى‌ أَشَدِّ الْعَذابِ‌).

"And Allah is not unaware of what you do" (وَ مَا اللَّهُ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

He has precisely enumerated all of it and will judge you according to it in His court of justice.

The last verse under discussion actually explains the primary motive for these contradictory actions, saying: "Those are the ones who have bought the life of this world [at the price of] the Hereafter" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ اشْتَرَوُا الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا بِالْآخِرَةِ). For this reason, "their punishment will not be lightened, nor will they be aided" (فَلا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ الْعَذابُ وَ لا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ‌).

Key Points

1- Historical Context of the Verses

[^1]: The word "Ma" in the phrase "Ma Jaza'..." may be "negative" or it may be an "interrogative of denial," and the result is the same.

As many commentators have narrated, the tribes of "Bani Qurayza" and "Bani Nadir"—both of whom were Jewish tribes [^1] and closely related—opposed each other for the sake of worldly interests. The Bani Nadir allied with the "Khazraj" tribe (who were polytheists of Medina), and the Bani Qurayza allied with the "Aws" tribe. In the wars that occurred between those two tribes, each of these Jewish groups would help their allied tribe and kill members of the other Jewish tribe. However, when the flames of war subsided, all the Jews would gather and join hands to free their captives by paying ransom, citing the ruling and law of the Torah (whereas, firstly, both Aws and Khazraj were polytheists and helping them was not permissible, and secondly, the same law that commanded the ransom had also commanded refraining from killing) [^2]. The Jews—like other stubborn and ignorant peoples—had many such contradictory actions.

2- Discrimination in God's Rulings and its Motive

We mentioned that the Holy Quran severely rebukes the Jews for their contradictory actions and their discrimination among God's rulings, threatening them with the severest punishment. This is especially because they would act upon the minor rulings but would oppose the more important ones, namely the law prohibiting the shedding of each other's blood and evicting their co-religionists from their homes and lands.

[^1]: "Qurayza" and "Nadir," like "Aws" and "Khazraj," were two brothers from whom each tribe originated.

[^2]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir al-Manar, and Tafsir Fi Zilal under the verses discussed.

In reality, they only valued and respected the commands that were to their benefit regarding worldly life. Where their interests required it, they would shed each other's blood, but since the possibility of captivity existed for everyone, they did not hesitate to pay ransom to free captives in order to ensure their own potential future rescue from captivity.

Fundamentally, acting upon commands that are to one's own benefit is not considered a sign of obedience to God's command, because the motive was not God's command but rather the preservation of personal interests. The obedient is distinguished from the rebellious and sinful when acting upon a command that goes against personal interests. Those who follow such a law are the true believers, and those who discriminate are the real rebels. Therefore, discrimination in the implementation of laws is a sign of a spirit of rebellion and, at times, a lack of faith.

In other words, the effect of faith and submission appears where a law is against a person's personal interests yet they respect it; otherwise, acting upon divine commands where they protect one's interests is neither a credit nor a sign of faith. Thus, believers can always be distinguished from hypocrites in this way: believers are submissive to all divine laws, while hypocrites favor discrimination.

As the Quran says, the result of this action is disgrace, humiliation, and misery. A nation that thinks of nothing but the material aspects of religion, and even then only through the lens of personal and private interests, will sooner or later be captured in the claws of a powerful nation, fall from the height of honor, and be disgraced in human societies.

This is regarding this world; as for the hereafter, as the Quran says, the severest punishment awaits such discriminators.

Of course, this law was not exclusive to the Children of Israel; it is constant for everyone and for us Muslims today. And how many are the discriminators, and how miserable and disgraced is this group?!

3- A Program for the Survival of Nations

Although these verses were revealed concerning the Children of Israel, they contain a series of general laws for all nations of the world, reminding them of the factors for the survival, endurance, and pride of nations, as well as the secret of their defeat.

From the Quranic perspective, the establishment and honor of nations occur when they rely on the greatest of forces and powers and seek help from Him at all times—relying on a power that is not subject to annihilation or decline, and bowing in submission only before Him. If they do so, they will have no fear or terror of anyone. It is clear that such a source can be none other than the Great Creator. Yes, this support is Allah (لا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ‌).

On the other hand, for the survival and immortality of nations, a specific solidarity among their individuals is necessary. This is possible only if everyone shows goodness toward their parents—whose circle of connection is closest to them—and in the next stage toward relatives, and after them toward all members of society, so that all may be wings for one another. Especially, they should take the weak under their protection so they do not fall into the enemy's trap (وَ بِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً وَ ذِي الْقُرْبى‌ ...وَ قُولُوا لِلنَّاسِ حُسْناً).

Strengthening the financial foundation of every nation and eliminating class disparity also plays a major role in keeping the spirit of that nation alive, one pillar of which is the payment of zakat (وَ آتُوا الزَّكاةَ).

These are on one hand; on the other hand, it considers the secret of the defeat and annihilation of nations to be the disruption of this solidarity and the emergence of internal conflicts and wars. A nation that stands in ranks against one another, where the stone of division is thrown among them, and instead of helping each other they fall upon one another's lives, seeking to seize each other's property and lands, rolling up their sleeves to shed each other's blood, and each group rising to evict and seize the property of another—such a nation will sooner or later be destroyed, their country will be ruined, and they themselves will become wretched and miserable (لا تَسْفِكُونَ دِماءَكُمْ وَ لا تُخْرِجُونَ أَنْفُسَكُمْ مِنْ دِيارِكُمْ ...).

And finally, another factor in the fall of nations is that very discrimination in the implementation of laws: that they implement whatever law protects their personal interests and forget what is for the benefit of society (أَ فَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِبَعْضِ الْكِتابِ وَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِبَعْضٍ‌).

And these are the causes of the growth and defeat of nations from the perspective of the Quran.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَقَفَّيْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ بِٱلرُّسُلِ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَا عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَأَيَّدْنَـٰهُ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ ۗ أَفَكُلَّمَا جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ أَنفُسُكُمُ ٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ فَفَرِيقًا كَذَّبْتُمْ وَفَرِيقًا تَقْتُلُونَ (٨٧)
Certainly, We gave Moses the Book and followed him with the apostles, and We gave Jesus, the son of Mary, manifest proofs and confirmed him with the Holy Spirit. Is it not that whenever an apostle brought you that which was not to your liking, you would act arrogantly; so you would impugn a group [of them], and slay a[nother] group? (87)
وَقَالُوا۟ قُلُوبُنَا غُلْفٌۢ ۚ بَل لَّعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَقَلِيلًا مَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٨٨)
And they say, ‘Our hearts are uncircumcised.’ Rather, Allah has cursed them for their unfaith, so few of them have faith. (88)

Commentary

Hearts That Are Veiled

Once again, the discourse in these verses is addressed to the Children of Israel, although its concepts and criteria are universal and encompass everyone:

First, It says: "We gave Moses the Book (the Torah)" (وَ لَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ).

"And after him, We sent messengers in succession" (وَ قَفَّيْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ بِالرُّسُلِ).

Messengers such as David, Solomon, Joshua, Zechariah, and John (Yahya)...

"And We gave Jesus, son of Mary, clear proofs, and supported him with the Holy Spirit" (وَ آتَيْنا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ الْبَيِّناتِ وَ أَيَّدْناهُ بِرُوحِ الْقُدُسِ).

"But is it not that whenever a messenger came to you with what your souls did not desire, you acted arrogantly and refused to submit to his command?!" (أَ فَكُلَّما جاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ بِما لا تَهْوى‌ أَنْفُسُكُمُ اسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ).

This dominance of whims and desires over you was so intense that "a group [of them] you denied, and another group you kill" (فَفَرِيقاً كَذَّبْتُمْ وَ فَرِيقاً تَقْتُلُونَ). If your denial was effective and your goal was achieved, perhaps you would have sufficed with that; otherwise, you would stain your hands with the blood of God's prophets!

In the commentary of the preceding verses under the title "Discrimination in Divine Laws," we explained the reality that the criterion for faith and submission to the Truth is found in instances that go against one's personal desires and whims; otherwise, every faithless ego-worshipper is compliant and submissive toward laws that align with his desires and interests.

Furthermore, it is clearly understood from this verse that Divine leaders, in the path of conveying Their mission, paid no heed to the opposition of ego-worshippers. Indeed, it must be so, for correct leadership is nothing other than this. If the prophets were to adapt themselves to the unconditional whims and desires of the people, their work would be following the misguided rather than leading those who seek the path of Truth.

The next verse says: "In response to the call of the prophets—or your call—they said mockingly: 'Our hearts are encased (in covers),'" and we do not understand any of these words (وَ قالُوا قُلُوبُنا غُلْفٌ) 1. Yes, it is so: "Allah has cursed them for their disbelief and distanced them from His mercy (for this reason they understand nothing), and little is it that they believe" (بَلْ لَعَنَهُمُ اللَّهُ بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَقَلِيلًا ما يُؤْمِنُونَ).

It is possible that the above sentence refers to the Jews who denied or killed the prophets, and it is also possible it refers to the Jews contemporary with the Prophet who adopted a stubborn, obstinate, and inflexible approach toward his words. In any case, it expresses the reality that as a result of following rebellious desires, man is so driven away from God's presence and veils fall upon his heart that the Truth rarely finds a way into it.

Key Points

1- The Mission of Prophets Throughout Different Ages

As we mentioned, faithless ego-worshippers resisted the call of the prophets because they did not see it as being in harmony with their base interests. Especially with the passage of time, they would consign Their teachings to oblivion. For this reason, it was necessary for God's messengers to be sent one after another to remind humanity anew, so that Their school of thought would not be forgotten.

In Surah Al-Mu'minun, verse 44, we read: ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنا رُسُلَنا تَتْرا كُلَّ ما جاءَ أُمَّةً رَسُولُها كَذَّبُوهُ فَأَتْبَعْنا بَعْضَهُمْ بَعْضاً: "Then We sent Our messengers in succession. Every time there came to a nation its messenger, they denied him, so We made them follow one another [to destruction]."

In Nahj al-Balagha, where the purpose of the prophets' mission is explained, this same reality is recounted:

"So He sent among them His messengers, and dispatched His prophets to them in succession, to ask them to fulfill the covenant of their nature (fitrah), to remind them of His forgotten bounties, to protest to them through communication, and to uncover for them the buried treasures of intellects." 1

Therefore, the coming of Divine prophets in different ages and centuries was to remind people of His blessings, to request the fulfillment of the primordial covenant, and to renew the calls and preachings of previous prophets, so that their efforts and reformative programs would not be abandoned or forgotten.

As for how the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله is the Seal of the Prophets and why there is no need for a prophet after him, this will be discussed, God willing, under verse 40 of Surah Al-Ahzab.

2- What is the Holy Spirit (Ruh al-Qudus)?

Great commentators have various interpretations regarding the Holy Spirit:

1- Some have said it refers to Gabriel. Thus, the meaning of the verse under discussion would be that Allah helped and supported Jesus by means of Gabriel.

The evidence for this is verse 102 of Surah An-Nahl: قُلْ نَزَّلَهُ رُوحُ الْقُدُسِ مِنْ رَبِّكَ بِالْحَقِ: "Say, the Holy Spirit has brought it down from your Lord in truth."

But why is Gabriel called the Holy Spirit? It is because, on one hand, the aspect of spirituality in angels is a clear matter, and applying the word "Spirit" (Ruh) to them is entirely correct. Adding it to "Al-Qudus" (The Holy) refers to the extraordinary purity and sanctity of this angel.

2- Others believe that the "Holy Spirit" is that same unseen power that supported Jesus عليه السلام, and with that same mysterious Divine power, he would bring the dead to life by God's command.

Of course, this unseen power exists in a weaker form in all believers, varying with the degrees of faith; it is those same Divine aids that assist man in performing acts of obedience and difficult tasks and restrain him from sins. Hence, in some hadiths regarding certain poets of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, we read that after one recited his poems for the Imam, the Imam said to him:

"The Holy Spirit has only breathed upon your tongue" (meaning what you said was with his help). 1

3- Some commentators have also interpreted the Holy Spirit to mean the "Gospel" (Injil) [2], but the first two interpretations seem closer to the truth.

1: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 1. 1: Regarding the famous poet contemporary with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Hassan ibn Thabit, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said about him: "The Holy Spirit will not cease to be with you as long as you defend us [with your poetry]" (Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 495). [2]: Tafsir al-Manar, under the verse being discussed.

3- The Christian Belief Regarding the Holy Spirit

In the "Dictionary of the Holy Bible," we read: "The Holy Spirit is called the third person of the Divine Trinity, and is called 'Spirit' because He is the originator and inventor of life, and is called 'Holy' because one of His specific works is to sanctify the hearts of believers. Due to His connection to God and Christ, He is also called the 'Spirit of God' and the 'Spirit of Christ'."

Another interpretation found in this book is: "But the Holy Spirit, who is our Comforter, is the one who constantly encourages us to accept and understand truth, faith, and obedience. It is He who brings to life those who have died in sin and error, making them pure and worthy of praising the Necessary Being (God)."

As you observe, these passages from the Bible Dictionary point to two meanings: one is that the Holy Spirit is one of the three gods, which accords with the doctrine of the Trinity—that polytheistic belief which we reject from every perspective—and the other is similar to the second interpretation we mentioned above.

4- Unaware and Veiled Hearts!

The Jews in Medina showed resistance against the preaching of the Noble Messenger صلى الله عليه وآله and refused to accept his call. Every time, they would fabricate an excuse to shirk the Prophet's invitation, one of which is mentioned in this verse.

They would say: "Our hearts are in veils and casings, and we do not understand what you recite to us!"

Certainly, they said this out of mockery and derision, but the Quran says: The matter is exactly as they say, for due to disbelief and hypocrisy, their hearts have been placed in veils of darkness, sin, and infidelity, and Allah has distanced them from His mercy. For this reason, very few of them believe.

In Surah An-Nisa, verse 155, this same point is reminded: وَ قَوْلِهِمْ قُلُوبُنا غُلْفٌ بَلْ طَبَعَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْها بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا: "And their saying, 'Our hearts are wrapped'; rather, Allah has set a seal upon them because of their disbelief, so they believe not, except for a few."

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ كِتَـٰبٌ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ وَكَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم مَّا عَرَفُوا۟ كَفَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ فَلَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٨٩)
And when there came to them a Book from Allah, confirming that which is with them—and earlier they would pray for victory over the pagans—so when there came to them what they recognized, they denied it. So may the curse of Allah be on the faithless! (89)
بِئْسَمَا ٱشْتَرَوْا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَنفُسَهُمْ أَن يَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بَغْيًا أَن يُنَزِّلَ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ فَبَآءُو بِغَضَبٍ عَلَىٰ غَضَبٍ ۚ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (٩٠)
Evil is that for which they have sold their souls, by defying what Allah has sent down, out of envy that Allah should bestow His grace on any of His servants that He wishes. Thus, they earned wrath upon wrath, and there is a humiliating punishment for the faithless. (90)

Circumstances of Revelation

It has been narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding these verses: "The Jews had seen in their books that the place of migration for the Prophet of Islam would be between Mount 'Ayr' and Mount 'Uhud' (two mountains on two sides of Medina). The Jews left their lands and went in search of the land of the migration of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله. During this journey, they reached a mountain named 'Haddad.' They said, 'Haddad is the same as Uhud.' They dispersed there, and each group settled in a place; some in the land of 'Tayma,' some in 'Fadak,' and others in 'Khaybar.'

Those who were in 'Tayma' desired to visit their brothers. In the meantime, an Arab was passing by, and they hired a mount from him. He said, 'I will take you through the path between Mount Ayr and Uhud.' They told him, 'When you reach the area between these two mountains, inform us.'

When the Arab man reached the land of Medina, he announced, 'This is the land situated between the two mountains of Ayr and Uhud.' Then he pointed and said, 'This is Ayr and that is Uhud.' The Jews dismounted and said, 'We have reached our destination; we no longer need your mount, go wherever you wish.'

They wrote a letter to their brothers saying, 'We have found that land; you too migrate to us.' In response, they wrote, 'We have settled here and acquired houses and property, and we are not far from that land. When the Promised Prophet migrates there, we will come to you swiftly!' They remained in the land of Medina and acquired much wealth. News of this reached a sovereign named 'Tubba’.' He fought them, and the Jews took refuge in their fortresses. He besieged them and then granted them amnesty. They came to the sovereign, and Tubba’ said, 'I have taken a liking to this land and shall remain here.' In response, they said, 'This shall not be, for this land is the place of migration for a Prophet, and no one besides him can remain in this land as a leader.'

Tubba’ said, 'Therefore, I shall place some of my kinsmen here so that when the Promised Prophet comes, they may assist him.' Thus, he settled the two famous tribes of Aws and Khazraj in that location.

When these two tribes grew in population, they encroached upon the property of the Jews. The Jews would say to them, 'When Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله is commissioned, he will expel you from our land!' When Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله was commissioned, the Aws and Khazraj—who became known as the Ansar—believed in him, while the Jews denied him. This is the meaning of the verse: 'And before that they used to pray for victory over those who disbelieved' (وَكانُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا)."

Indeed, the very people who had come with special love and devotion to believe in the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, and whose pride against the Aws and Khazraj was that a Prophet would be commissioned and they would be his special companions, ended up in the ranks of his enemies due to prejudice, stubbornness, and worldliness, while those from afar gathered around him and joined his companions.

Commentary

They were the preachers, yet they became the disbelievers!

Once again, these verses speak of the Jews and the events of their lives. As mentioned in the circumstances of revelation, they had settled in the land of Medina with a special love and desire to believe in the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله. They used to read the signs of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in their heavenly book, the Torah, and were impatiently awaiting his appearance. "But when there came to them a Book (the Quran) from Allah, confirming what was with them—although before this they used to foretell the appearance of this Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and hoped for victory over their enemies through him—yes, when there came to them that which they [already] recognized, they disbelieved in it" (وَ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ كِتابٌ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِما مَعَهُمْ وَ كانُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ ما عَرَفُوا كَفَرُوا بِهِ).

"So let the curse of Allah be upon the disbelievers" (فَلَعْنَةُ اللَّهِ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ).

Indeed, sometimes a person passionately pursues a truth, but when they reach it and find it contrary to their personal interests, they turn their back on it and bid it farewell due to following their whims; rather, they sometimes even rise in opposition to it.

In reality, the Jews made a losing bargain. Those who had migrated from their lands to follow the Promised Prophet and settled in the land of Medina with great difficulty to reach their goal, ultimately ended up in the ranks of the deniers and disbelievers. Therefore, the Quran says: "Evil is that for which they have sold themselves" (بِئْسَمَا اشْتَرَوْا بِهِ أَنْفُسَهُمْ).

"They disbelieved in what Allah has revealed, out of envy, objecting that Allah should send down His grace upon whomsoever He wills of His servants" (أَنْ يَكْفُرُوا بِما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ بَغْياً أَنْ يُنَزِّلَ اللَّهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ عَلى‌ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ).

It seems they expected the Promised Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to be from the Children of Israel and from among themselves, and they were upset by the revelation of the Quran to someone else! At the end of the verse, it says: "Thus they drew upon themselves wrath upon wrath, and for the disbelievers is a humiliating punishment" (فَباؤُ بِغَضَبٍ عَلى‌ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ).

Key Points

1- A Losing Bargain

Indeed, the Jews made a losing bargain, for in the beginning, they were among the heralds of Islam and even chose to live in Medina with all its hardships to achieve this goal. However, after the appearance of the Prophet of Islam, simply because he was not from the Children of Israel or because he endangered their personal interests, they disbelieved in him. What bargain could be more losing than for a person not only to fail in reaching their goal but, after spending all their energy, to stand in opposition to it and bring upon themselves the wrath and anger of God?

In a saying from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read: "Indeed, there is no price for your souls except Paradise, so do not sell them for anything else." [^1] It is noteworthy that here, he mentions the capital of the bargain as the very essence of their existence, because with disbelief, the value of their being completely collapses; it is as if they lose their own identity. In other words, they become like slaves who have sold their existence and entered the captivity of another. Yes, they are captives of whim and servants of Satan.

The word "Ishtaraw" (اشتروا), although usually meaning "to purchase," sometimes—as specified in linguistics—also means "to sell," and the above verse is of this type.

2- Commentary on "Wrath upon Wrath" (فَباؤُ بِغَضَبٍ عَلى‌ غَضَبٍ)

In the story of the Children of Israel when they were wandering in the wilderness (Sinai), the Holy Quran says: "And they returned with wrath from Allah" (وَ باؤُ بِغَضَبٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ). Then it adds that this anger of God toward them was because of their killing the Prophets and disbelieving in the signs of Allah.

In Surah Al-Imran, verse 112, the same meaning is seen: that the Jews were subjected to God's wrath for disbelieving in the signs of Allah and killing the Prophets. This is the first wrath that overtook them.

Their descendants, after the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, continued the same path as their ancestors regarding this Prophet. That is, they not only disbelieved in his religion but also rose to fight against him. This caused a new wrath and anger to encompass them, and this is the second wrath. Therefore, it says: "So they drew upon themselves wrath upon wrath" (فباءوابِغَضَبٍ عَلى‌ غَضَبٍ).

"Ba'u" (بائوا) originally means "they returned and took up residence," and here it is a metaphor for "deserving" or "incurring." That is, they chose the wrath of the Lord as their residence and place.

This rebellious group, both before the uprising of Moses and before the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, were staunch supporters of such an uprising. However, after the appearance of both, they turned back from their belief and incurred the wrath and anger of God one after another.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, Saying 456.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ قَالُوا۟ نُؤْمِنُ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا وَيَكْفُرُونَ بِمَا وَرَآءَهُۥ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ ۗ قُلْ فَلِمَ تَقْتُلُونَ أَنۢبِيَآءَ ٱللَّهِ مِن قَبْلُ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٩١)
And when they are told, ‘Believe in what Allah has sent down,’ they say, ‘We believe in what was sent down to us,’ and they disbelieve what is besides it, though it is the truth confirming what is with them. Say, ‘Then why would you kill the prophets of Allah formerly, should you be faithful?’ (91)
۞ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَكُم مُّوسَىٰ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱتَّخَذْتُمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ وَأَنتُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (٩٢)
Certainly Moses brought you manifest proofs, but then you took up the Calf in his absence and you were wrongdoers. (92)
وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ وَرَفَعْنَا فَوْقَكُمُ ٱلطُّورَ خُذُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكُم بِقُوَّةٍ وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ ۖ قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَعَصَيْنَا وَأُشْرِبُوا۟ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ بِكُفْرِهِمْ ۚ قُلْ بِئْسَمَا يَأْمُرُكُم بِهِۦٓ إِيمَـٰنُكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٩٣)
And when We took covenant with you and raised the Mount above you, [declaring], ‘Hold on with power to what We have given you, and do listen!’ They said, ‘We hear, and disobey,’ and their hearts had been imbued with [the love of] the Calf, due to their unfaith. Say, ‘Evil is that to which your faith prompts you, should you be faithful!’ (93)

Commentary

The Racial Prejudices of the Jews

In the commentary of the previous verses, we read that despite all the hardships and difficulties the Jews endured to reach the "Promised Prophet of the Torah," they refused to obey and believe in him due to envy, or because this Prophet was not from the Children of Israel, and because he endangered their personal interests.

Following that, the verses under discussion point to the aspect of racial prejudice for which the Jews are world-renowned, stating: "And when it is said to them, 'Believe in what Allah has sent down,' they say, 'We believe in what was sent down to us' (and not to other nations), and they disbelieve in what is beyond it" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ آمِنُوا بِما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ قالُوا نُؤْمِنُ بِما أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْنا وَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِما وَراءَهُ‌).

They believed neither in the Gospel nor in the Quran; rather, they only considered racial aspects and their own interests, "while this Quran is the truth, confirming that which is with them" (وَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ مُصَدِّقاً لِما مَعَهُمْ‌), and it aligns with the signs and marks they had read in their own book.

Thereafter, the Quran pulls back the curtain on their lies and says: If the excuse for your lack of faith is that Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) is not from among you, "then why did you kill the prophets of Allah before, if you are [indeed] believers?" (قُلْ فَلِمَ تَقْتُلُونَ أَنْبِياءَ اللَّهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

If they truly believed in the Torah—a Torah that considers the taking of a life a great sin—they should not have murdered the great Prophets of God.

Furthermore, the claim that "we only believe in instructions revealed to us" is a clear deviation from the principles of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against polytheism. This is a form of egoism and self-worship, whether it manifests in an individual or racial form.

Monotheism came to uproot such ugly traits from human existence, so that humans accept God's commands solely because they are from God.

In other words, if the acceptance of Divine commands is conditional upon them being revealed to ourselves, this is in reality polytheism, not faith; it is disbelief, not Islam. Accepting such commands would never be evidence of faith.

It is interesting that the above verse says: "When it is said to them, 'Believe in what Allah has sent down'..." In this phrasing, neither Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) is mentioned, nor Moses or Jesus (عليه السلام); rather, it is simply "what Allah has sent down" (ما انزل اللَّه).

To further clarify their falsehood and lies, the Quran, in the next verse, exposes another piece of evidence against them, saying: "And Moses certainly came to you with clear proofs, then you took the calf [for worship] after him, and you were wrongdoers!" (وَ لَقَدْ جاءَكُمْ مُوسى‌ بِالْبَيِّناتِ ثُمَّ اتَّخَذْتُمُ الْعِجْلَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ وَ أَنْتُمْ ظالِمُونَ‌).

If you are truthful and believe in your own Prophet, then what was this calf-worship after all those clear monotheistic proofs? What kind of faith is this that flies out of your hearts as soon as Moses is absent and goes to Mount Tur, only for disbelief to take its place and the calf to replace Monotheism?

Indeed, by this act, you wronged both yourselves and your society and future generations.

In the third verse under discussion, another piece of evidence for the invalidity of their claim is mentioned, bringing up the issue of the covenant at Mount Tur: "And [recall] when We took your covenant and raised over you the mount, [saying], 'Take what We have given you with firmness and listen.' They said, 'We heard and disobeyed.'" (وَ إِذْ أَخَذْنا مِيثاقَكُمْ وَ رَفَعْنا فَوْقَكُمُ الطُّورَ خُذُوا ما آتَيْناكُمْ بِقُوَّةٍ وَ اسْمَعُوا قالُوا سَمِعْنا وَ عَصَيْنا).

"And their hearts were made to drink [the love of] the calf because of their disbelief!" (وَ أُشْرِبُوا فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْعِجْلَ بِكُفْرِهِمْ‌).

Indeed, polytheism and world-worship—exemplified by the love for Samiri's golden calf—penetrated the fabric of their hearts and took root throughout their entire being; for this reason, they forgot Allah.

How strange! What kind of faith is this that is compatible with killing the Prophets of God, permits calf-worship, and consigns firm Divine covenants to oblivion?! Indeed, "Say, 'Wretched is that which your faith enjoins upon you, if you should be believers.'" (قُلْ بِئْسَما يَأْمُرُكُمْ بِهِ إِيمانُكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌) [1]

[1]: Regarding the covenant of the Children of Israel and its details and characteristics, it was discussed under verses 51 and 63 of this same Surah.

Key Points

1- Commentary on the phrase: "قالُوا سَمِعْنا وَ عَصَيْنا"

The phrase "قالُوا سَمِعْنا وَ عَصَيْنا" (They said, "We heard and we disobeyed") does not mean they necessarily uttered these words with their tongues; rather, it apparently means they demonstrated this reality through their actions. This is a beautiful form of metaphor (kinayah) that is also seen in everyday speech.

2- Commentary on the phrase: "وَ أُشْرِبُوا فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْعِجْلَ"

The phrase "وَ أُشْرِبُوا فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْعِجْلَ" (And they were made to drink the calf into their hearts) also reflects a striking metaphor describing the state of the Jewish people. To explain: the word "ishrab," as derived from Mufradat al-Raghib, has two meanings:

If it is from the form "ashrabtu al-ba'ir," it means "I tied the rope around the camel's neck." According to this meaning, the sentence would mean that a firm rope of affection and love had connected their hearts to the calf.

And if it is from the root "ishrab" meaning "to water" or "to give someone water to drink," then in this case, the word "love" (hubb) is implied. The overall meaning of the sentence would be: The Children of Israel watered their hearts with the love of Samiri's calf.

It is among the customs of the Arabs that whenever they wish to convey an intense affection or a great grudge toward something, they use an expression similar to the one above.

Furthermore, another point can be derived from the above expression: one should not be surprised by these improper actions of the Children of Israel, for these deeds are the product of the soil of their hearts which has been watered with the water of polytheism. A land watered with such water will yield no harvest other than betrayal, the killing of Prophets, sin, and oppression.

The importance of this subject becomes clearer when one notes the gravity of homicide in the Jewish faith, which speaks of this matter with particular emphasis. The Jewish religion considered this crime so heinous that, according to the Bible Dictionary (page 687): "Premeditated murder and its vileness were of such importance to the Israelites that over time, taking sanctuary in cities of refuge or seeking asylum in holy places would not result in the release or exoneration of the killer; rather, in any case, he would be subjected to retribution (qisas)."

This is the meaning of killing human beings according to the Torah, let alone killing the Prophets of God.

Ayahs

قُلْ إِن كَانَتْ لَكُمُ ٱلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَالِصَةً مِّن دُونِ ٱلنَّاسِ فَتَمَنَّوُا۟ ٱلْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٩٤)
Say, ‘If the abode of the Hereafter were exclusively for you with Allah, and not for other people, then long for death, should you be truthful.’ (94)
وَلَن يَتَمَنَّوْهُ أَبَدًۢا بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٩٥)
But they will not long for it ever because of what their hands have sent ahead, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers. (95)
وَلَتَجِدَنَّهُمْ أَحْرَصَ ٱلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ حَيَوٰةٍ وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ ۚ يَوَدُّ أَحَدُهُمْ لَوْ يُعَمَّرُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ وَمَا هُوَ بِمُزَحْزِحِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ أَن يُعَمَّرَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ (٩٦)
Surely, you will find them the greediest for life, of all people even the idolaters. Each of them is eager to live a thousand years, though it would not deliver him from the punishment, were he to live [that long]. And Allah watches what they do. (96)

Commentary

The Self-Satisfied Group

From the history of the lives of the Jews—in addition to various verses of the Holy Quran—it appears that they considered themselves a superior race. They believed they were the "flower of the flock" of human society, that Paradise was created solely for them, and that the fire of Hell would have little to do with them! They claimed to be the children of Allah and His special friends; in short, they believed that whatever virtues others might possess, they alone held them all. This foolish egoism is reflected in various verses of the Quran that speak of the Jews.

In Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 18, we read: نَحْنُ أَبْناءُ اللَّهِ‌ وَ أَحِبَّاؤُهُ: "We are the children of Allah and His beloved ones."

In Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 111, we read: وَ قالُوا لَنْ يَدْخُلَ الْجَنَّةَ إِلَّا مَنْ كانَ هُوداً أَوْ نَصارى‌: "And they say, 'None shall enter Paradise unless he be a Jew or a Christian.'"

And in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 80, we read: وَ قالُوا لَنْ تَمَسَّنَا النَّارُ إِلَّا أَيَّاماً مَعْدُودَةً: "And they say, 'The Fire shall not touch us except for a few numbered days.'"

These delusional imaginings, on one hand, invited them toward oppression, crime, sin, and rebellion, and on the other hand, toward arrogance, self-conceit, and a sense of superiority.

The Holy Quran, in the aforementioned verses, provides a crushing response, saying: "If the home of the Hereafter with Allah is purely for you, to the exclusion of other people (as you claim), then long for death, if you are truthful" (قُلْ إِنْ كانَتْ لَكُمُ الدَّارُ الْآخِرَةُ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ خالِصَةً مِنْ دُونِ النَّاسِ فَتَمَنَّوُا الْمَوْتَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ‌).

Do you not wish to seek refuge in the proximity of Allah's mercy and have the endless blessings of Paradise at your disposal? Does a friend not long to see his beloved?! By saying that Paradise is exclusive to them or that they will only burn in the Fire for a few days, the Jews wanted to discourage the Muslims regarding their faith.

However, the Quran pulls back the curtain on their lies and deception, for they are in no way ready to leave the life of this world, and this itself is a strong proof of their falsehood. Truly, if a person has such faith in the abode of the Hereafter, why would he be so attached to the life of this world? And why would he commit a thousand types of crimes to attain it?

In the next verse, the Quran adds: "But they will never long for it, because of what their hands have sent before them" (وَ لَنْ يَتَمَنَّوْهُ أَبَداً بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ‌). "And Allah is well-aware of the wrongdoers" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِالظَّالِمِينَ‌).

Yes, they knew what black and dark spots existed in their record of deeds; they were aware of their ugly and shameful actions. Allah is also aware of the deeds of these oppressors; therefore, the abode of the Hereafter is for them a home of punishment, torture, and disgrace, and for this reason, they do not desire it.

The last verse under discussion speaks of their intense greed for material things: "And you will surely find them the greediest of mankind for life" (وَ لَتَجِدَنَّهُمْ أَحْرَصَ النَّاسِ عَلى‌ حَياةٍ). "Even [greedier] than those who associate partners with Allah" (وَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا).

Greedy in amassing wealth and riches, greedy in monopolizing the world, greedy in exclusivism. They are even greedier than the polytheists, who naturally should be the most covetous in gathering property and have no qualms about obtaining it by any means.

"They have such an attachment to the world that" each one of them wishes he could be granted a life of a thousand years (يَوَدُّ أَحَدُهُمْ لَوْ يُعَمَّرُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ).

Whether to gather more wealth or out of fear of punishment! Yes, each one longs for a thousand-year life, "but his being granted a long life will not remove him from the punishment" (وَ ما هُوَ بِمُزَحْزِحِهِ مِنَ الْعَذابِ أَنْ يُعَمَّرُ).

And if they imagine that Allah is unaware of their deeds, they are mistaken: "And Allah is Seer of what they do" (وَ اللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

Key Points

1- The intent of "a thousand years" is not the literal number one thousand

It must be noted that the intent of "a thousand years" is not the specific number one thousand, but rather an allusion to a very long life. In other words, it is a number of "multiplicity" (takthir) rather than "enumeration" (ta'dad).

Some commentators say: The number one thousand was the largest number known to the Arabs at that time, and numbers larger than it did not have a specific name; therefore, it was considered the most eloquent expression for abundance. [1]

2- Commentary on "عَلى‌ حَياةٍ" (for life)

The expression "عَلى‌ حَياةٍ" (in the indefinite form), according to a group of commentators, is for belittlement (tahqir). That is, they are so attached to the life of this world that they prefer even the lowest life of this world—one in the depths of misery—over the abode of the Hereafter. [2]

3- The Crimes of the Racists

Undoubtedly, the source of many wars and bloodshed throughout human history has been racial superiority. Especially in the First and Second World Wars, which brought the highest figures of casualties and destruction in history, the factor of German racism (or the Nazi party) was an undeniable element.

[1]: Al-Manar, Vol. 1, p. 331. [2]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 1, p. 230; Al-Manar, Vol. 1, p. 390.

If one were to rank the racists of the world, the Jews would undoubtedly be placed in the top ranks. Even now, the state they have formed under the name of "Israel" is established based on this issue of race. What horrific crimes they committed to form it, and what terrifying crimes they commit to maintain it!

They have even confined the religion of Moses (peace be upon him) to their own race, and if someone from outside the Jewish race wishes to accept this religion, it is not of interest to them. For this reason, they do not propagate or invite others to their religion among other nations.

This specific situation of theirs has caused them to be detested in the eyes of the world, for the people of the world never love those who claim a privilege for themselves over others.

Fundamentally, racism is a branch of polytheism (shirk), and for this reason, Islam has fought against it intensely and considers all humans to be from one father and mother, whose only distinction is through piety and God-wariness.

4- The Roots of the Fear of Death

Most people fear death and are terrified of it. Upon analysis and investigation, we see that the root of this fear consists of one of two things:

1- Many people interpret death as meaning annihilation, non-existence, and destruction. It is natural that a human fears non-existence. If a person interprets death in this way, he will certainly flee from it. Thus, even in the best states of life and at the peak of success, the thought that one day this life will end turns the nectar of life into poison in their mouths, and they are always anxious from this perspective.

2- Individuals who do not consider death the end of life, but see it as a prelude to life in a wider and more exalted realm, fear death because of their own actions and wrongdoings. This is because they see death as the beginning of reaching the grim consequences of their deeds. Therefore, to escape Divine reckoning and the punishment for their actions, they wish to postpone death as much as possible. The verse above refers to this second group.

However, the great Prophets of Allah, on one hand, revived faith in eternal life after death in the hearts and transformed the terrifying outward appearance of death in people's eyes, showing them its true face as a gateway to a higher life.

On the other hand, they called for "purity of action" so that the fear of death due to the punishment for deeds would also be removed. Therefore, people of faith have no fear whatsoever of the end of life and death.

Ayahs

قُلْ مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُۥ نَزَّلَهُۥ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَهُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٩٧)
Say, ‘Whoever is an enemy of Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down on your heart with the will of Allah, confirming what has been [revealed] before it and as a guidance and good news for the faithful.’ (97)
مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّلَّهِ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتِهِۦ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَجِبْرِيلَ وَمِيكَىٰلَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَدُوٌّ لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٩٨)
[Say,] ‘Whoever is an enemy of Allah, His angels and His apostles and Gabriel and Michael, [let him know that] Allah is the enemy of the faithless.’ (98)

Occasion of Revelation

When the Prophet ص came to Medina, one day Ibn Suriya (one of the Jewish scholars) along with a group of Jews from Fadak came to the Prophet of Islam ص. They asked him various questions and searched for the signs that bore witness to his prophethood and mission. Among other things, they asked: "O Muhammad, how is your sleep? For we have been given information regarding the sleep of the promised Prophet." He replied: "My eyes sleep, but my heart is awake!" (تنام عيناى و قلبى يقظان). They said: "You have spoken the truth, O Muhammad!" After several other questions, Ibn Suriya said: "One question remains; if you answer it correctly, we will believe in you and follow you. What is the name of the angel who descends upon you?" He replied: "It is Gabriel (Jibril)."

"Ibn Suriya" said: "He is our enemy; he brings difficult commands regarding Jihad and war, whereas Michael (Mikail) always brought simple and easy commands. If your angel of revelation were Michael, we would have believed in you!" [1]

Commentary

A Pretext-Seeking Nation!

Examining the occasion of revelation for the above verse reminds one once again of the pretext-seeking nature of the Jewish people, who have followed this pattern from the time of the great Prophet Moses عليه السلام until now. To shirk the burden of truth, they seek a new excuse at every turn.

Here, as we observe, their only excuse is: "Because Gabriel is your angel of revelation and he conveys the heavy obligations of God, we will not believe; we are his enemies. If the angel of revelation were Michael, it would be very well, and we would believe."

One must ask them: Do the divine angels differ from one another in terms of performing their duties? Fundamentally, do they act according to their own desires or say anything of their own accord? They are exactly as the Quran describes: "They do not disobey Allah in what He commands them" (لا يَعْصُونَ اللَّهَ ما أَمَرَهُمْ) (At-Tahrim: 6).

In any case, the Quran responds to these pretexts by saying: "Tell them:

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan narrated this Hadith from Ibn Abbas, and it has also appeared (with variations) in other books such as Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Al-Mizan, Al-Manar, and others.

Whoever is an enemy to Gabriel (is in reality an enemy to Allah), for he has brought the Quran down upon your heart by Allah's command" (قُلْ مَنْ كانَ عَدُوًّا لِجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلى‌ قَلْبِكَ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ‌).

"A Quran that confirms the previous heavenly books" (and is consistent with their signs) (مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ‌).

"A Quran that is a source of guidance and glad tidings for the believers" (وَ هُدىً وَ بُشْرى‌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

In fact, three responses are given to this group in this verse:

First, Gabriel brings nothing from himself; everything is "by Allah's permission" (بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ).

Second, the sign of truthfulness from previous books exists within it, as it matches their signs.

Third, its content itself is proof of its authenticity and truthfulness. [1]

The next verse expresses the same subject with greater emphasis accompanied by a threat, saying: "Whoever is an enemy to Allah, His angels, His messengers, Gabriel, and Michael, then surely Allah is an enemy to the disbelievers" (مَنْ كانَ عَدُوًّا لِلَّهِ وَ مَلائِكَتِهِ وَ رُسُلِهِ وَ جِبْرِيلَ وَ مِيكالَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ عَدُوٌّ لِلْكافِرِينَ‌).

This indicates that these are inseparable: Allah, His angels, His messengers, Gabriel, Michael, and every other angel; in reality, enmity toward one is enmity toward the rest.

In other words, divine commands that bring perfection to humanity are sent down from Allah

[1]: Al-Mizan, under the verses being discussed.

by the angels to the Prophets. If there is a difference between their missions, it is of the nature of a division of responsibility, not a contradiction in mission. They are all aligned toward a single goal; therefore, enmity toward one of them is enmity toward Allah.

"Gabriel" and "Michael"

The name "Gabriel" (Jibril) appears three times, and the name "Michael" (Mikal) appears once in the Holy Quran, in this very context. [1] From these verses, it is understood that both are among the great and archangels of God (in the common pronunciation of Muslims, both Jibril and Mikail are pronounced with a Hamza and Ya, but in the text of the Quran, they appear only as Jibril and Mikal).
