Some believe that "Jibril" is a Hebrew word, originally "Jibra-el," meaning "Man of God" or "Power of God" ("Jibr" meaning "power or man" and "El" meaning "God").

According to the verses under discussion, Gabriel was the messenger of Allah's revelation to the Prophet and the one who descended the Quran upon his pure heart, while in Surah An-Nahl, verse 102, the intermediary of revelation is introduced as the "Holy Spirit" (Ruh al-Qudus).

And in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verse 191, we read that the "Trustworthy Spirit" (Ruh al-Amin) brought the Quran for him. However, as commentators have clarified, the "Holy Spirit" and the "Trustworthy Spirit" refer to Gabriel himself.

Furthermore, we have traditions stating that Gabriel would descend upon the Prophet in various forms, and in Medina, Gabriel often appeared to him in the form of Dihyah al-Kalbi, who was a very handsome man.

From Surah An-Najm, it is understood that the Prophet ص saw Gabriel twice (in his

[1]: The name "Jibril" is mentioned twice in the verses under discussion and once in Surah At-Tahrim, verse 4.

original form). [1] In Islamic books, the four archangels of God are usually listed as Gabriel, Michael, Israfil, and Azrael, among whom Gabriel is the most superior.

In Jewish sources, Gabriel and Michael are also mentioned. For instance, in the Book of Daniel, Gabriel is introduced as the defeater of the prince of demons, and Michael as the protector of the people of Israel. Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۖ وَمَا يَكْفُرُ بِهَآ إِلَّا ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٩٩)
We have certainly sent down manifest signs to you, and no one denies them except transgressors. (99)
أَوَكُلَّمَا عَـٰهَدُوا۟ عَهْدًا نَّبَذَهُۥ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٠٠)
Is it not that whenever they made a covenant, a part of them would cast it away? Rather, the majority of them do not have faith. (100)
وَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ نَبَذَ فَرِيقٌ مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ كِتَـٰبَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَآءَ ظُهُورِهِمْ كَأَنَّهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٠١)
And when there came to them an apostle from Allah, confirming that which is with them, a part of those who were given the Book cast the Book of Allah behind their back, as if they did not know [that it is Allah’s Book]. (101)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the first verse, an occasion of revelation has been narrated from Ibn Abbas: "Ibn Suriya," a Jewish scholar, out of obstinacy and enmity, said to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: "You have not brought anything that is intelligible to us! And Allah has not sent down any clear sign upon you so that we might follow you." The above verse was revealed and gave him an explicit response. [^1]

Commentary

The Covenant-Breakers of the Jews

In the first verse under discussion, the Quran points to the fact that sufficient proofs, clear signs, and manifest verses are at the disposal of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. Those who deny them have, in reality, realized the truthfulness of His invitation but have risen in opposition due to specific ulterior motives. It says: "We have indeed sent down to you manifest signs, and none disbelieve in them except the rebellious (fasiqun)" (وَ لَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ وَ ما يَكْفُرُ بِها إِلَّا الْفاسِقُونَ‌).

Contemplating the verses of the Quran illuminates the paths for every pure-hearted and truth-seeking human being. By studying these verses, one can realize the truthfulness of the Prophet of Islam's صلى الله عليه وآله mission and the greatness of the Quran. However, only those whose hearts have not been darkened by sin perceive this reality. Therefore, it is no surprise that the rebellious, those polluted by sin, and those who have turned away from obeying Allah's command never believe in it.

Then, it points to one of the very vile characteristics of a group of Jews—namely, covenant-breaking, which seems to accompany their history—and says: "Is it not that whenever they made a covenant [with Allah and the Prophet], a party of them cast it aside [and opposed it]?" (أَ وَ كُلَّما عاهَدُوا عَهْداً نَبَذَهُ فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ‌). Indeed, "most of them do not believe" (بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

Allah took a covenant from them at Mount Tur to act upon the commands of the Torah,

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan and Tafsir al-Qurtubi, under the verse discussed.

but ultimately they broke this covenant and trampled His command underfoot.

Furthermore, a covenant had been taken from them to believe in the promised Prophet (the Prophet of Islam whose arrival was foretold in the Torah); they did not fulfill this covenant either.

The Jews of "Bani Nadir" and "Bani Qurayza" also made a covenant with the Prophet of Islam upon his arrival in Medina that they would at least not assist his enemies, but they eventually broke this covenant as well and cooperated with the polytheists of Mecca against Islam during the Battle of Ahzab.

Fundamentally, this is the long-standing method of the majority of the Jews—that they are not bound by their word. Even now, we clearly see that whenever the interests of the Zionists and the usurping Israel are endangered, they trample upon all private and international treaties and consign them all to oblivion with hollow excuses.

The last verse under discussion places a more explicit and eloquent emphasis on this same subject, saying: "And when there came to them a messenger from Allah confirming what was with them, a party of those who were given the Scripture threw the Book of Allah behind their backs as if they did not know [it]" (وَ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِما مَعَهُمْ نَبَذَ فَرِيقٌ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ كِتابَ اللَّهِ وَراءَ ظُهُورِهِمْ كَأَنَّهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌). Until the time the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was appointed, the Jewish scholars used to give the people glad tidings of his coming and would enumerate his signs and characteristics. However, when he was appointed to the messengership, they turned away from the contents of the Torah in such a way as if they had never seen or read it.

Yes, this is the result of selfishness and worldliness, which turns a person who was initially among the staunch propagators of the truth into one of its irreconcilable enemies upon its arrival.

Key Points

1- The Expression "Nuzul" or "Inzal" (Descent)

It is clear that the expression "Nuzul" (descending) or "Inzal" (sending down) regarding the Holy Quran does not mean, for example, that Allah has a physical location in the heavens and sent the Quran down from that high place. Rather, this expression refers to the exalted spiritual and majestic status of the Lord.

2- The Concept of the Word "Fasiq"

The word "Fasiq" is derived from the root "fisq," which originally means the kernel of a fresh date (rutab) emerging from its skin. This occurs when a fresh date falls from the palm branch and the pit pops out of it; the Arabs describe this as "fasaqat al-nawah." Subsequently, all those who have cast off the garment of obedience to the Lord and have exited the path and custom of servitude are called "Fasiq."

In reality, just as the date pit abandons that sweet, useful, and nutritious layer upon emerging, they too lose all their value and character through their actions.

3- The Quran Never Condemns an Entire Nation Because of the Majority

In the above discussions, as in other discussions, the Quran never blames an entire people because of the sins of the majority. Rather, by using the term "fariq" (a party) and also "akthar" (most), it preserves the share of the pious and believing minority. This is the way of truth-seeking and justice.

Ayahs

وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ مَا تَتْلُوا۟ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ عَلَىٰ مُلْكِ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ ۖ وَمَا كَفَرَ سُلَيْمَـٰنُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُعَلِّمُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ ٱلسِّحْرَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَى ٱلْمَلَكَيْنِ بِبَابِلَ هَـٰرُوتَ وَمَـٰرُوتَ ۚ وَمَا يُعَلِّمَانِ مِنْ أَحَدٍ حَتَّىٰ يَقُولَآ إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ فِتْنَةٌ فَلَا تَكْفُرْ ۖ فَيَتَعَلَّمُونَ مِنْهُمَا مَا يُفَرِّقُونَ بِهِۦ بَيْنَ ٱلْمَرْءِ وَزَوْجِهِۦ ۚ وَمَا هُم بِضَآرِّينَ بِهِۦ مِنْ أَحَدٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَيَتَعَلَّمُونَ مَا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَلَا يَنفَعُهُمْ ۚ وَلَقَدْ عَلِمُوا۟ لَمَنِ ٱشْتَرَىٰهُ مَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنْ خَلَـٰقٍ ۚ وَلَبِئْسَ مَا شَرَوْا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (١٠٢)
And they followed what the devils pursued during Solomon’s reign —and Solomon was not faithless but it was the devils who were faithless—teaching the people magic and what was sent down to the two angels at Babylon, Harut and Marut, who would not teach anyone without telling [him], ‘We are only a test, so do not be faithless.’ But they would learn from those two that with which they would cause a split between man and his wife—though they could not harm anyone with it except with Allah’s leave. They would learn that which would harm them and bring them no benefit; though they certainly knew that anyone who buys it has no share in the Hereafter. Surely, evil is that for which they sold their souls, had they known! (102)
وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ لَمَثُوبَةٌ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ ۖ لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (١٠٣)
Had they been faithful and Godwary, the reward from Allah would have been better, had they known! (103)

Commentary

Solomon and the Sorcerers of Babylon

It appears from the hadiths that during the time of Prophet Solomon (peace be upon him), a group in his country engaged in the practice of magic and sorcery. Solomon ordered all their writings and papers to be collected and kept in a special place (this preservation might have been because they contained useful information for neutralizing the magic of sorcerers).

After the passing of Solomon, a group brought them out and began spreading and teaching magic. Some took advantage of this situation and claimed: "Solomon was not a prophet at all, but rather he dominated his country and performed extraordinary feats with the help of this very magic and sorcery!" A group of the Children of Israel followed them and became deeply attached to sorcery, to the point that they even abandoned the Torah.

When the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) appeared and announced through the verses of the Quran that Solomon was among the prophets of God, some of the Jewish rabbis and scholars said: "Are you not surprised by Muhammad, who says Solomon was a prophet, while he was a sorcerer?"

This statement by the Jews, besides being a great slander and fabrication against this divine prophet, implied the takfir (charging with disbelief) of Solomon (peace be upon him). According to their claim, Solomon was a sorcerer who falsely called himself a prophet, and such an act constitutes disbelief (kufr).

The above verses respond to them. [^1]

In any case, the first verse under discussion introduces another chapter of the Jews' misdeeds: their accusation of magic and sorcery against the great Prophet of God, Solomon. It says: "And they followed what the devils recited during the reign of Solomon" (وَ اتَّبَعُوا ما تَتْلُوا الشَّياطِينُ عَلى‌ مُلْكِ سُلَيْمانَ‌). The pronoun in the phrase "and they followed" may refer to the Jews contemporary with the Prophet, or those contemporary with Solomon, or all of them.

The term "devils" (shayaṭīn) may refer to rebellious humans, or jinn, or both.

Then, following this statement, the Quran adds: "Solomon did not disbelieve" (وَ ما كَفَرَ سُلَيْمانُ‌).

He never resorted to magic, nor did he use sorcery to advance his goals, "but the devils disbelieved, teaching people magic" (وَ لكِنَّ الشَّياطِينَ كَفَرُوا يُعَلِّمُونَ النَّاسَ السِّحْرَ).

[^1]: Sirah Ibn Hisham, Vol. 2, p. 192, and Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed (with slight variations).

"And [they followed] what was revealed to the two angels in Babylon, Harut and Marut" (وَ ما أُنْزِلَ عَلَى الْمَلَكَيْنِ بِبابِلَ هارُوتَ وَ مارُوتَ‌). [^1] Yes, they reached out for magic from two sources: one from the teachings of the devils during Solomon's era, and the other from the teachings given to the people by Harut and Marut—two angels of God—regarding the nullification of magic.

"While the two [angels] (their only goal was to acquaint people with the way to nullify the magic of sorcerers) and thus" "they did not teach anyone anything until they had said, 'We are only a trial, so do not disbelieve!'" (and do not misuse these teachings) (وَ ما يُعَلِّمانِ مِنْ أَحَدٍ حَتَّى يَقُولا إِنَّما نَحْنُ فِتْنَةٌ فَلا تَكْفُرْ).

In short, these two angels came among the people at a time when the market for magic was thriving and people were caught in the clutches of sorcerers. They taught people how to nullify the magic of sorcerers. However, since neutralizing a subject (like defusing a bomb) is contingent upon first knowing the subject itself and then learning how to neutralize it, they were forced to first explain the tricks and techniques of magic.

But the Jewish opportunists used this as a means to spread magic even further, going so far as to accuse the great divine prophet, Solomon, claiming that if natural elements were under his command or if jinn and men obeyed him, it was all the result of magic. Indeed, this is the way of the wicked: they always accuse great figures of following their own path to justify their school of thought.

[^1]: Some commentators consider the phrase "what was revealed..." to be linked to "what they recited" (and the commentary mentioned above is based on this). Others consider it linked to "magic."

In any case, they did not emerge victorious from this divine trial: "And they learned from those two that by which they might cause separation between a man and his wife" (فَيَتَعَلَّمُونَ مِنْهُما ما يُفَرِّقُونَ بِهِ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَ زَوْجِهِ‌).

But the power of Allah is above all these powers: "But they could not harm anyone with it except by Allah's permission" (وَ ما هُمْ بِضارِّينَ بِهِ مِنْ أَحَدٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ‌).

"And they learned what harmed them and did not benefit them" (وَ يَتَعَلَّمُونَ ما يَضُرُّهُمْ وَ لا يَنْفَعُهُمْ‌).

Yes, they distorted this constructive divine program; instead of using it as a means of reform and combating magic, they made it a means of corruption, "even though they knew that whoever purchased such a commodity would have no share in the Hereafter" (وَ لَقَدْ عَلِمُوا لَمَنِ اشْتَراهُ ما لَهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنْ خَلاقٍ‌). [^1] "And wretched is that for which they sold themselves, if they only knew" (وَ لَبِئْسَ ما شَرَوْا بِهِ أَنْفُسَهُمْ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

They knowingly turned their backs on their own prosperity and that of the society they belonged to, and plunged into the whirlpool of disbelief and sin, "whereas if they had believed and feared Allah, the reward from Allah would have been better for them, if they only realized" (وَ لَوْ أَنَّهُمْ آمَنُوا وَ اتَّقَوْا لَمَثُوبَةٌ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

[^1]: "Khalaq" originally means good character that a person acquires, and sometimes it means a portion or share; in the above verse, it carries this latter meaning.

Key Points

1- The Story of Harut and Marut

Regarding these two angels who came to the land of Babylon, strange legends and myths have been fabricated by storytellers and attributed to these two great divine angels, to the point of giving them a superstitious image. This has even made the task of research and study regarding this historical event difficult for scholars. What seems most correct among all these, and is consistent with rational and historical standards and hadith sources, is what you read below:

"In the land of Babylon, magic and sorcery reached their peak and caused distress and harm to the people. Allah commissioned two angels in human form to teach people the elements of magic and the way to nullify it, so they could protect themselves from the evil of sorcerers.

However, these teachings were ultimately susceptible to misuse, because the angels had to explain the methods of magic in order to nullify the sorcerers' magic, so that people could take preventive measures. This led a group, after learning the methods of magic, to join the ranks of sorcerers themselves, causing new harassment for the people.

Even though the two angels warned the people that 'this is a type of divine trial for you' and even said, 'misusing these teachings is a form of disbelief,' they engaged in acts that resulted in harm and loss to the people." [^1] What we have presented above is what is derived from many hadiths and Islamic sources, and its harmony with reason and logic is evident. Among these is a hadith narrated from Uyun Akhbar al-Rida (peace be upon him) (which in one chain is from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (peace be upon him) himself and in another chain from Imam Hasan al-Askari (peace be upon him)), which clearly confirms this meaning. [^1]

Unfortunately, however, some historians, encyclopedia writers, and even some commentators have been influenced by fabricated legends in this regard and have mentioned the story famous among some common folk about these two infallible divine angels: "They were two angels whom Allah sent to earth to see if they, too, would be immune to sin if they were in the place of humans. After descending to earth, they committed several major sins, followed by a legend fabricated about the planet Venus." All of this is baseless and part of superstitions, and the Quran is free from such matters. If we only reflect on the text of the above verses, we will see that the Quran's statement has no connection to these issues.

2- The Terms Harut and Marut

The names "Harut" and "Marut," according to some writers, are of Iranian origin. One writer says that in Armenian books, he encountered the name "Harrot" meaning fertility and "Marrot" meaning immortality. He believes that Harut and Marut are derived from these two terms. [^2] However, this inference lacks clear evidence.

In the "Avesta," the terms "Haurvatat," which is "Khordad," and also "Ameretat," meaning immortality, which is "Mordad," are seen. [^1] Dehkhoda, in his dictionary, has also cited material in this regard that is not unlike the latter meaning.

Strangely, some have considered Harut and Marut to be two men from the inhabitants of Babylon, and some have even introduced them as devils, while the above verse clearly rejects these notions.

3- How Can an Angel Become a Teacher to Humans?

A question remains here: according to the apparent meaning of the above verses and numerous narrations—as we said—Harut and Marut were two divine angels who taught people to combat the harassment of sorcerers. Can an angel truly be a teacher to a human?

The answer to this question is mentioned in the same hadiths: Allah transformed them into the form of humans so they could fulfill their mission. This reality can also be understood from verse 9 of Surah Al-An'am, where it says: "And if We had made him an angel, We would have made him [appear as] a man" (وَ لَوْ جَعَلْناهُ مَلَكاً لَجَعَلْناهُ رَجُلًا).

4- No One is Capable of Anything Without Allah's Permission

In the above verses, we read that sorcerers could not harm anyone without the permission of the Lord. This does not mean there is compulsion or fatalism involved; rather, it refers to one of the fundamental principles of monotheism (Tawhid): that all powers in this world originate from the power of the Lord. Even the burning of fire and the cutting of a sword are not without His permission and command. It is not the case that a sorcerer can interfere in the world of creation against Allah's will, nor can he limit Allah within His realm of sovereignty. Rather, these are properties and effects He has placed in various beings; some make good use of them, and some misuse them. This freedom and choice that Allah has given to humans is also a means for testing and perfecting them.

5- What is Magic and When Did It Originate?

There is much discussion about what "magic" (sihr) is and from what date it originated. It can be said that magic has been prevalent among people since very ancient times, but no precise history is available for it, nor can it be said who first created sorcery.

However, regarding the meaning and reality of magic, it can be said: magic is a type of extraordinary act that leaves effects on human beings; sometimes it is a type of sleight of hand and trickery, and sometimes it has only a psychological and imaginary aspect.

Linguistically, magic has two meanings: 1. Meaning deception, trickery, illusion, and sleight of hand; as the Qamus al-Lughah defines it, magic means to deceive. 2. "Everything whose cause is subtle and minute": "That whose factors are invisible and mysterious."

In Raghib's Mufradat, which is specific to Quranic terms, three meanings are mentioned: 1. Deception and imaginations without reality or truth, like sleight of hand and trickery. 2. Attracting devils through specific ways and seeking help from them. 3. Another meaning that some have imagined: that it is possible to change the essence and form of persons and beings using certain means—for example, turning a human into an animal through it. However, this is nothing more than an illusion and fancy and has no reality. [^1]

From examining about 51 instances of the word "magic" and its derivatives in Quranic surahs such as Taha, Ash-Shu'ara, Yunus, Al-A'raf, etc., regarding the stories of the prophets of God—Moses, Jesus, and the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family)—we conclude that magic, from the Quran's perspective, is divided into two parts:

  1. Where the intent is deception, trickery, illusion, and sleight of hand, having no reality. As we read: "Then suddenly their ropes and staffs seemed to him by their magic to be moving" (Surah Taha, verse 66). And in another verse: "When they threw, they bewitched the eyes of the people and struck terror into them" (Al-A'raf, verse 116). From these verses, it becomes clear that magic does not possess a reality that can manipulate objects and leave an effect; rather, it is the trickery and sleight of hand of the sorcerers that makes it appear so.

  2. It is understood from some Quranic verses that some types of magic truly leave an effect, such as the above verse which says they learned magic that caused separation between a man and his wife (فَيَتَعَلَّمُونَ مِنْهُما ما يُفَرِّقُونَ بِهِ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَ زَوْجِهِ‌), or the other expression in the above verses that they learned things that were harmful to them and not beneficial (وَ يَتَعَلَّمُونَ ما يَضُرُّهُمْ وَ لا يَنْفَعُهُمْ‌).

But does the effect of magic only have a psychological aspect, or can it also have a physical and external effect? This is not explicitly mentioned in the above verses, and therefore some believe the effect of magic is only in psychological aspects.

Another point necessary to mention here is that it seems a significant portion of magic was performed by using chemical and physical properties to deceive simple-minded people. For example, in the history of the sorcerers of Moses' time (peace be upon him), we read that they had placed certain chemical substances (possibly mercury and the like) inside their ropes and staffs, which moved after the sun shone on them or due to heating devices they had installed beneath them, and the spectators imagined they had come to life. Such types of magic are not rare even in our time.

Magic from the Perspective of Islam

In this regard, Islamic jurists all say that learning and performing acts of magic and sorcery is forbidden (haram). In this section, hadiths from the great leaders of Islam have reached us and are narrated in our reliable books, including:

Ali (peace be upon him) says: "Whoever learns anything of magic, little or much, has disbelieved, and his last covenant with his Lord is severed..." [^1]

However, as we said, if learning it is for the purpose of nullifying the magic of sorcerers, there is no problem. In fact, sometimes it becomes a collective obligation (wajib kifa'i) for a group to learn magic so that if a false claimant wants to deceive or mislead people through this path, they can nullify his magic and sorcery and expose the claimant's lie.

The evidence for this—that if magic is for nullifying magic and solving or undoing it, it is permissible—is a hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him). In this hadith, we read:

"One of the sorcerers and magicians who used to receive payment for performing magic came to Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) and said: 'My profession was magic and I received payment for it; my living expenses were provided through this path, and with that income, I performed the Hajj to the House of Allah. But now I have abandoned it and repented; is there a way of salvation for me?' Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) replied: 'Untie the knot of magic, but do not tie the knot of sorcery.'" [^1] From this hadith, it is understood that for undoing the knot of magic, learning and practicing it is without objection.

Sorcery from the Perspective of the Torah

Magic and sorcery are also improper and very detestable from the perspective of the books of the Old Testament (the Torah and its associated books). In the Torah, we read: "Do not turn to mediums or seek out spiritists, for you will be defiled by them. I am the Lord your God." [^2] And in another place in the Torah: "I will set my face against anyone who turns to mediums and spiritists to prostitute themselves by following them, and I will cut them off from their people." [^1] The Dictionary of the Holy Bible writes in this regard: "And it is very clear that magic had no place in the law of Moses; rather, the law prohibited persons who sought counsel from magic with the severest punishments."

But what is interesting here is that the author of the Dictionary of the Holy Bible admits that despite this, the Jews learned magic and sorcery and believed in it contrary to the Torah. Following the previous point, he says: "But despite these, this corrupt matter entered among the Jewish people; the people believed in it and took refuge in it in times of need." [^2] For this reason, the Quran has severely rebuked them and considers them losers who sold themselves for the worst price.

Magic in Our Era

Today, there are a series of sciences that sorcerers used in the past to carry out their programs:

  1. Using unknown physical and chemical properties of objects, as we previously mentioned. Just as in the story of the sorcerers of Moses' time (peace be upon him), they were able to make things in the shape of snakes and move them using physical and chemical properties like mercury and its compounds. Of course, using the physical and chemical properties of objects is never forbidden; rather, one should become as aware of them as possible and use them in life. However, if their mysterious properties are used to deceive and mislead unaware people, it will be considered one of the instances of magic (take note).

  2. Using magnetic sleep, hypnotism, mesmerism, and telepathy (transferring thoughts from a distance). Of course, these sciences are also among the positive sciences that can be utilized correctly in many aspects of life, but sorcerers misused them and employed them to deceive and trick people. If today someone uses them in such a way before uninformed people, it will be considered magic.

In short, magic has a broad meaning that encompasses all that we have said here and previously mentioned. It has also been proven that the power of human will has great strength, and when it becomes stronger in the light of spiritual disciplines, it reaches a point where it affects the beings in its environment, just as ascetics perform extraordinary acts through discipline.

It is also noteworthy that disciplines are sometimes legitimate and sometimes illegitimate. Legitimate disciplines create a constructive power in pure souls, while illegitimate disciplines create a satanic power, and both can be the source of extraordinary feats—the first being positive and constructive, and the second destructive.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَقُولُوا۟ رَٰعِنَا وَقُولُوا۟ ٱنظُرْنَا وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ ۗ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٠٤)
O you who have faith! Do not say Ra‘ina, but say Unzurna, and listen! And there is a painful punishment for the faithless. (104)
مَّا يَوَدُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَلَا ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ أَن يُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَخْتَصُّ بِرَحْمَتِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (١٠٥)
Neither the faithless from among the People of the Book nor the idolaters like that any good be showered on you from your Lord; but Allah singles out for His mercy whomever He wishes, and Allah is dispenser of a mighty grace. (105)

Occasion of Revelation

The famous commentator Ibn Abbas narrates: In the early days of Islam, when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was speaking and explaining Divine verses and rulings, the Muslims would sometimes ask him to speak with a bit more deliberation so they could properly comprehend the material and also raise their questions and requests. To make this request, they used the phrase:

"Ra'ina" (راعنا), which is derived from the root "al-ra'y" (الرعى), meaning "to give us time" or "grant us a respite."

However, the Jews used this same word, "Ra'ina," deriving it from the root "al-ru'unah" (الرعونه), which means "stupidity" or "folly." (In the first case, the meaning is "give us time," but in the second case, it means "treat us as fools"!).

Here, a pretext was found for the Jews to ridicule the Prophet or the Muslims by using the very same phrase the Muslims were saying.

The first verse above was revealed, and to prevent this exploitation, it commanded the believers to use the phrase "Unzurna" (انظرنا) instead of "Ra'ina," which conveys the same meaning but provides no pretext for the stubborn enemy.

Some other commentators have said that the phrase "Ra'ina" was a type of insult in the language of the Jews, meaning "listen, may you never hear." They would repeat this phrase and laugh!

Some commentators have also narrated that the Jews would say "Ra'ina" (our shepherd) instead of "Ra'ina," addressing the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and ridiculing him in this way. [^1] These occasions of revelation are not in conflict with one another, and it is possible that all of them are correct.

Commentary

Do Not Give a Pretext to the Enemy

Considering what was mentioned in the occasion of revelation, the first verse under discussion says:

O you who have believed! (When you request time from the Prophet to comprehend the verses of the Quran) do not say "Ra'ina," but say "Unzurna" (because it carries the same meaning and is not a pretext for the enemy) (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَقُولُوا رَاعِنَا وَقُولُوا انْظُرْنَا).

"And listen to what you are commanded, and for the disbelievers and mockers is a painful punishment" (وَاسْمَعُوا وَلِلْكَافِرِينَ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

From this verse, it is clearly understood that Muslims must be careful in their programs never to give an excuse to the enemy. They should avoid even a short phrase that might become a subject for exploitation by enemies. The Quran explicitly advises the believers, in order to prevent exploitation by opponents, to refrain from saying even a homonymous word that the enemy might intend with another meaning to weaken the morale of the believers. The scope of speech and expression is vast; what necessity is there for a person to use a sentence that is susceptible to distortion and mockery by the enemy?

When Islam does not permit giving a pretext to enemies to this extent, the duty of Muslims in larger and more significant matters is clear. Even now, sometimes actions are performed by us that lead to misinterpretations and exploitation by the propaganda loudspeakers of internal enemies or international circles. Our duty is to strictly avoid such actions and not needlessly give a pretext to these internal and external mischief-makers.

It is also noteworthy that the phrase "Ra'ina"—in addition to what has been said—is not devoid of a type of impolite expression, because "Ra'ina" is from the root of "mura'at" (the mufa'alah form), and its concept is: "You observe us, so that we also observe you." Since this expression (in addition to the exploitations the Jews made of it) was far from etiquette, the Quran forbade the Muslims from it. [^1]

The next verse pulls back the curtain on the grudge and enmity of the group of polytheists and the People of the Book toward the believers, saying: "Neither those who disbelieve from the People of the Book nor the polytheists wish that any good or blessing should be sent down to you from your Lord" (مَا يَوَدُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ وَلَا الْمُشْرِكِينَ أَنْ يُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ).

But this is nothing more than a mere wish, for "Allah chooses for His mercy whom He wills" (وَاللَّهُ يَخْتَصُّ بِرَحْمَتِهِ مَنْ يَشَاءُ).

"And Allah is the Possessor of Great Bounty" (وَاللَّهُ ذُو الْفَضْلِ الْعَظِيمِ).

Yes, the enemies, out of the intensity of their grudge and envy, were not willing to see this honor and blessing upon the Muslims—that a great Prophet, possessing a magnificent Heavenly Book, should be sent to them from Allah. But can one block the path of Allah's grace and mercy?!

Key Points The Precise Concept of "يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا"

This honorable and soul-nurturing address is seen in more than 80 instances in the Holy Quran. The verse above is the first verse we encounter containing this address.

Interestingly, this expression is exclusively found in verses revealed in Medina, and there is no trace of it in Meccan verses. Perhaps this is because, with the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله migration to Medina, the situation of the Muslims became stabilized; they became a settled and influential community and were saved from fragmentation. Therefore, Allah addressed them with the phrase "يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا" (O you who have believed).

This expression also contains another point: now that you have believed, submitted to the Truth, and made a covenant of obedience with your God, you must act according to the requirements of this covenant and the commands that follow this phrase. In other words, your faith necessitates that you implement these instructions.

It is noteworthy that in many Islamic books, including Sunni sources, it is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

"Allah has not revealed any verse containing 'يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا' except that Ali عليه السلام is its head and its leader." [^1]

[^1]: This hadith is narrated in Al-Durr al-Manthur from Abu Nu'aym in Hilyat al-Awliya from Ibn Abbas.

Ayahs

۞ مَا نَنسَخْ مِنْ ءَايَةٍ أَوْ نُنسِهَا نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِّنْهَآ أَوْ مِثْلِهَآ ۗ أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٠٦)
For any verse that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring another which is better than it, or similar to it. Do you not know that Allah has power over all things? (106)
أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (١٠٧)
Do you not know that to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth? And besides Allah, you do not have any guardian or helper. (107)

Commentary

The Purpose of Abrogation (Naskh)

Once again, these verses address the malicious propaganda of the Jews against the Muslims.

At times, they would tell the Muslims: "The [true] religion is the religion of the Jews, and the [true] Qibla is the Qibla of the Jews; hence, your Prophet prays toward our Qibla (Bayt al-Maqdis)." However, when the ruling regarding the Qibla changed and, according to verse 144 of this same Surah, Muslims were commanded to pray toward the Kaaba, this pretext was taken away from the Jews. They then struck a new chord, saying: "If the first Qibla was correct, then what is this second command? And if the second command is correct, then your past deeds are void!" In these verses, the Quran responds to their objections and illuminates the hearts of the believers [^1], stating: "We do not abrogate any verse, nor do We delay its abrogation, except that We bring [one] better than it or similar to it" (مَا نَنْسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنْسِهَا نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِنْهَا أَوْ مِثْلِهَا).

And this is easy for Allah: "Do you not know that Allah is over all things powerful?" (أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

"Do you not know that the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth belongs to Allah?" (أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ). He has the right to make any change or substitution in His rulings according to the [divine] wisdom and interests (masalih), and He is the most knowledgeable and discerning regarding the interests of His servants.

"And do you not know that besides Allah, there is for you no protector or helper?" (وَمَا لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ).

In fact, the first part of this verse refers to Allah's sovereignty over rulings and His ability to discern all the interests of His servants; therefore, believers should not listen to the misplaced words of biased individuals who cast doubt on the issue of the abrogation of rulings.

The second part is a warning to those who choose a support other than Allah, for in the world, there is no real support except Him.

[^1]: There is also the possibility that the above verses refer to the change and abrogation of some other Islamic rulings, as Fakhr al-Razi mentioned in his Tafsir and Sayyid Qutb in Fi Zilal al-Quran.

Key Points

1- Is Abrogation in Rulings Permissible?

"Naskh" (abrogation) literally means to eliminate or nullify. In the logic of the Sharia, it means changing a ruling and substituting another ruling in its place. For example:

1- After the migration to Medina, Muslims prayed toward Bayt al-Maqdis for sixteen months. After that, the command to change the Qibla was issued, and they were obligated to face the Kaaba during prayer.

2- In Surah An-Nisa, verse 15, regarding the punishment for adulterous women, it was commanded that if four witnesses testify, they should be confined to their homes until death overtakes them or Allah ordains another way for them.

This verse was abrogated by verse 2 of Surah An-Nur, and in that verse, their punishment was changed to one hundred lashes.

Here, a famous objection is raised: If the first ruling contained a benefit, why was it abrogated? And if it did not, why was it legislated from the beginning? In other words: Why was the ruling not legislated from the start in such a way that it required no abrogation or change?

Islamic scholars have provided the answer to this question since ancient times in their books, and the summary of it, with our explanation, is as follows:

We know that human needs sometimes change with the passage of time and environmental conditions, and sometimes they remain constant. One day, a program guarantees his happiness, but another day, due to changing conditions, that same program might be an obstacle in his path.

One day, a medicine is extremely beneficial for a patient and the doctor prescribes it, but another day, due to the patient's relative recovery, this medicine might even be harmful; therefore, he orders its cessation and substitutes another medicine.

A lesson might be constructive for a student this year, but the same lesson might be useless for next year. A wise teacher must organize the curriculum such that the lessons needed by the students are taught year by year.

This issue becomes even clearer when considering the law of human and societal evolution; in the evolutionary process of humans, a program is sometimes beneficial and constructive, and sometimes harmful and in need of change. Especially at the beginning of social and ideological revolutions, the necessity of changing programs at different intervals of time appears more evident.

Of course, it must not be forgotten that the principles of divine rulings, which form the fundamental pillars, are the same everywhere. The principle of Monotheism (Tawhid) or social justice and hundreds of rulings like them never change; the change is in smaller, secondary matters.

This point should also not be forgotten: it is possible for the evolution of religions to reach a point where the final religion is revealed as the Seal of Religions, such that no change in its rulings occurs thereafter (we will provide a full explanation of this topic under verse 40 of Surah Al-Ahzab in the discussion on Finality, God willing).

Although it is well known that the Jews completely deny abrogation—and for this reason, they criticized the Muslims for the change of Qibla—according to their own religious sources, they are forced to accept abrogation.

For according to the Torah, when Noah (عليه السلام) disembarked from the ark, Allah made all animals lawful for him, but this ruling was abrogated in the law of Moses (عليه السلام) and a portion of the animals were prohibited.

In the Torah, Genesis, Chapter 9, Verse 3, we read: "Every moving thing that liveth shall be meat for you; even as the green herb have I given you all things." But the generality of this ruling was later abrogated.

2- What is Meant by "Ayah"?

"Ayah" literally means a sign or mark, and in the Quran, it is used in various meanings, including:

1- The segments of the Quran that are separated from each other by specific marks and are known as verses (ayahs), as we read in the Quran itself: "These are the verses of Allah which We recite to you in truth" (Al-Baqarah: 252).

2- Miracles are also introduced as ayahs, as we read regarding the famous miracle of Moses (عليه السلام), the White Hand: "And draw your hand to your side; it will come out white without disease, as another sign (ayah)" (Taha: 22).

3- It also appears in the sense of a proof or sign of the knowledge of God or the Resurrection, as we read: "And We made the night and the day two signs (ayatan)" (Surah Al-Isra: 12). And regarding the argument for the Resurrection, He says: "And of His signs (ayahs) is that you see the earth barren, but when We send down upon it water, it quivers and grows. Indeed, He who gives it life is the Giver of Life to the dead. Indeed, He is over all things powerful" (Fussilat: 39).

4- It also appears in the sense of eye-catching things like high and lofty buildings, as we read: "Do you build on every high place a sign (ayah), amusing yourselves?" (Ash-Shu'ara: 128).

However, it is clear that these various meanings actually all return to a common denominator, which is the concept of a "sign."

But in the verses under discussion, where the Quran says: "If We abrogate a verse, We will bring its like or better than it," it refers to rulings; if one is abrogated, a better one is revealed, or if the miracle of one of the prophets is abrogated, a more eloquent miracle is given to the next prophet [^1]. It is noteworthy that in some narrations regarding the interpretation of the above verse, we see that "abrogating a verse" is interpreted as the death of an Imam and the succession of another Imam, which is, of course, stated as an example of an instance, not to limit the broad concept of the verse.

3- Commentary on the Phrase "Nunsiha"

The phrase "nunsiha," which is conjoined to the phrase "nansakh" in the verses under discussion, originally comes from the root "insa'," meaning to delay, or to remove and cause to be forgotten from minds [^2]. Now the question arises: what is the meaning of this phrase considering the phrase "nansakh"?

In response, we say: the meaning here is that if We abrogate a verse or delay its abrogation according to certain interests, in any case, We bring [one] better than it or

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 116.

[^2]: In the first case, it is from the root "nasa'" (to delay), and in the second case, it is from the root "nasiya" (to forget).

similar to it. Therefore, the phrase "nansakh" refers to abrogation in the short term, and the phrase "nunsiha" refers to abrogation in the long term (take note).

Here, some other possibilities have been suggested, which are not very significant compared to the interpretation we have mentioned.

4- Commentary on "Or Its Like" (Aw Mithliha)

Another question arises: what is meant by "or its like"? If a ruling is the same as the previous ruling, then changing the first one seems pointless. What necessity is there to abrogate something and substitute its like in its place? The abrogator (nasikh) must be better than the abrogated (mansukh) for the abrogation to be acceptable.

In response to this question, it must be said: the meaning of "like" is that We bring a ruling and law that, in the subsequent time, has an effect similar to the effect the previous law had in the past time.

To explain: a ruling might have effects and benefits today, but tomorrow it might lose these effects. In this case, this ruling must be abrogated and a new ruling must take its place which, if not better than it, at least has the effects in this time that the previous ruling had in the past time. In this way, no objection remains.

Ayah

أَمْ تُرِيدُونَ أَن تَسْـَٔلُوا۟ رَسُولَكُمْ كَمَا سُئِلَ مُوسَىٰ مِن قَبْلُ ۗ وَمَن يَتَبَدَّلِ ٱلْكُفْرَ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (١٠٨)
Would you question your Apostle as Moses was questioned formerly? Whoever changes faith for unfaith certainly strays from the right way. (108)

Occasion of Revelation

In the books of revelation occasions, several accounts have been mentioned for this verse, all of which are nearly identical in their conclusion:

First, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that "Wahb bin Zayd" and "Rafi' bin Harmalah" came to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "Bring us a letter from Allah addressed to us so that we may read it and then believe! Or cause rivers to flow for us so that we may follow you!"

Others have said that a group of Arabs made the same demand of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that the Jews had made of Moses (peace be upon him); they said: "Show Allah to us openly so that we may see Him with our own eyes and believe!" Others have written that they requested the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to designate an idol for them from a specific tree called "Dhat Anwat" so they could worship it, just as the ignorant among the Children of Israel said to Moses: اجْعَلْ لَنا إِلهاً كَما لَهُمْ‌ آلهة ("Make for us a god as they have gods")!

The above verse was revealed and provided them with an answer.

Commentary

Groundless Excuses

Although this verse is not addressed to the Jews, but rather to a group of weak-faith Muslims or polytheists, it is, as we shall see, not unrelated to the history of the Jews.

Perhaps it was after the incident of the change of the Qiblah that a group of Muslims and polytheists, influenced by the insinuations of the Jews, made inappropriate and baseless demands of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, examples of which were mentioned above. Allah the Almighty forbade them from such questioning and says:

"Do you wish to ask your Messenger the same unreasonable demands that were asked of Moses before?" (And through such excuse-seeking, evade the responsibility of faith) (أَمْ تُرِيدُونَ أَنْ تَسْئَلُوا رَسُولَكُمْ كَما سُئِلَ مُوسى‌ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌).

And since this act is a form of exchanging "faith" for "disbelief," He adds at the end of the verse: "And whoever exchanges faith for disbelief has certainly strayed from the right way" (وَ مَنْ يَتَبَدَّلِ الْكُفْرَ بِالْإِيمانِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَواءَ السَّبِيلِ‌).

Let there be no misunderstanding: Islam never prevents scientific inquiries, logical questions, or the request for miracles to ascertain the truthfulness of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call, for these are the paths to understanding, comprehension, and faith. However, there was a group that used baseless questions and arbitrary requests for miracles as an excuse to avoid submitting to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله invitation. Even though the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had provided sufficient evidence and miracles, every newcomer would propose a new supernatural feat.

This is while we know that miracles and supernatural acts are not playthings for this person or 그 person; they are necessary only to the extent that they create confidence in the truth of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله words. Otherwise, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is not a performer of wonders who sits in a corner for anyone to come and propose a miracle according to their own whim and taste.

Furthermore, they sometimes made unreasonable demands such as seeing Allah with their eyes or the fashioning of an idol.

In reality, the Quran wants to warn the people that if they pursue such unreasonable demands, what befell the people of Moses will befall them as well.

Ayahs

وَدَّ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَوْ يَرُدُّونَكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ كُفَّارًا حَسَدًا مِّنْ عِندِ أَنفُسِهِم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۖ فَٱعْفُوا۟ وَٱصْفَحُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِىَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٠٩)
Many of the People of the Book are eager to turn you into unbelievers after your faith, out of their inner envy, [and] after the truth had become manifest to them. Yet excuse [them] and forbear until Allah issues His edict. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (109)
وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ ۚ وَمَا تُقَدِّمُوا۟ لِأَنفُسِكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ تَجِدُوهُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (١١٠)
And maintain the prayer and give the zakat. Any good that you send ahead for your own souls, you shall find it with Allah. Indeed Allah watches what you do. (110)

Commentary

The Stubborn Envious Ones

There were many among the People of the Book, especially the Jews, who were not content with merely refusing to accept the religion of Islam themselves; rather, they insisted that the believers should also turn back from their faith, and their motive in this matter was nothing but envy.

The Quran refers to this matter in the above verses, stating: "Many of the People of the Book wish they could turn you back into disbelievers after you have believed, out of envy from their own selves, even after the truth has become manifest to them" (وَدَّ كَثِيرٌ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ لَوْ يَرُدُّونَكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِيمانِكُمْ كُفَّاراً حَسَداً مِنْ عِنْدِ أَنْفُسِهِمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْحَقُّ).

Here, the Quran commands the Muslims regarding these deviant and destructive efforts: "But pardon and overlook until Allah brings His command; indeed, Allah is over all things competent" (فَاعْفُوا وَ اصْفَحُوا حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ اللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

This is, in fact, a tactical instruction given to the Muslims to use the weapon of pardon and forgiveness in the face of intense enemy pressure under those specific conditions, and to focus on building themselves and the Islamic society while awaiting Allah's command.

According to many commentators, the "command of Allah" here refers to the "command of Jihad," which had not yet been revealed at that time—perhaps because they did not yet have comprehensive readiness for this command. Therefore, many believe that this verse was abrogated by the verses of Jihad that will be mentioned later.

However, the term "abrogation" (naskh) might not be accurate here, because abrogation occurs when a ruling is legislated in a seemingly unlimited manner but is internally temporary. Yet, the ruling of pardon and forgiveness in the verse under discussion is expressed in a limited form—limited to the time before the Divine command regarding Jihad had arrived.

The next verse gives two important constructive instructions to the believers: one regarding prayer (Salat), which establishes a firm connection between man and Allah, and the other regarding the poor-rate (Zakat), which is the secret to social solidarity. Both of these are necessary for victory over the enemy. It says:

"Establish prayer and give Zakat," and strengthen your soul and body through these two means (وَ أَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ آتُوا الزَّكاةَ).

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 1; p. 400

Then it adds: Do not imagine that the good deeds you perform and the wealth you spend in the way of Allah will perish; no, "whatever good you send forth for yourselves, you will find it with Allah (in the other abode)" (وَ ما تُقَدِّمُوا لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ تَجِدُوهُ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ‌).

"Indeed, Allah is Seeing of what you do" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ).

He knows precisely which action you performed for His sake and which one for other than Him.

Key Points

1- The word "Isfahu" (اصفحوا) is derived from the root "Safh" (صفح), which originally means the side of a mountain, the flat side of a sword, or the surface of the face. This phrase is usually used to mean turning away and disregarding. With the context of the phrase "Fa'fu" (فَاعْفُوا - pardon), it becomes clear that this turning away is not out of anger or indifference, but rather out of magnanimous forgiveness.

Furthermore, these two expressions show that even at that time, the Muslims possessed enough strength and power that they could have chosen not to pardon and instead confront the enemies. However, so that the enemy might be reformed if they are reformable, He first commands pardon and forgiveness. In other words, in dealing with the enemy, violence should never be the first

plan; rather, Islamic ethics require that the first plan be pardon and forgiveness. If that proves ineffective, then one may resort to sternness.

2- The phrase "إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ" (Indeed, Allah is over all things competent) may be an indication that Allah is able to grant you victory over them even now through extraordinary means, but the nature of human life and the world of creation is that every task should be accomplished gradually and through the preparation of necessary preliminaries.

3- The phrase "حَسَداً مِنْ عِنْدِ أَنْفُسِهِمْ‌" (out of envy from their own selves) may indicate that envy sometimes reflects itself in the form of a goal and is given a religious coloring, but the envy they showed in this regard did not even have this coloring; rather, it was purely personal in nature. [1] There is also the possibility that it refers to envy that has taken deep root in their souls.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Manar, under the verse being discussed.

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ لَن يَدْخُلَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ إِلَّا مَن كَانَ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَـٰرَىٰ ۗ تِلْكَ أَمَانِيُّهُمْ ۗ قُلْ هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١١١)
And they say, ‘No one will enter paradise except one who is a Jew or Christian.’ Those are their [false] hopes! Say, ‘Produce your evidence, should you be truthful.’ (111)
بَلَىٰ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُۥ لِلَّهِ وَهُوَ مُحْسِنٌ فَلَهُۥٓ أَجْرُهُۥ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (١١٢)
Certainly, whoever submits his will to Allah and is virtuous, he shall have his reward from his Lord, and they will have no fear, nor shall they grieve. (112)

Commentary

The Monopolizers of Paradise!

In the aforementioned verses, the Quran refers to another of the vain and baseless claims made by a group of Jews and Christians, and subsequently provides them with a crushing response: "And they said: None shall enter Paradise except he who is a Jew or a Christian" (وَ قالُوا لَنْ يَدْخُلَ الْجَنَّةَ إِلَّا مَنْ كانَ هُوداً أَوْ نَصارى‌) [1]

[1]: Although the phrase "they said" (قالوا) is mentioned as a single unit, it is evident that it describes the state of two groups, each making a separate claim; the Jews claimed Paradise is exclusive to us, and the Christians likewise said Paradise belongs to us.

In response, He first states: "These are only their desires" (and they shall never reach this wish) (تِلْكَ أَمانِيُّهُمْ‌).

Then, addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He says: "Tell them: every claim requires proof; bring your proof if you are truthful in this claim" (قُلْ هاتُوا بُرْهانَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ‌).

After establishing the fact that they have no evidence for this claim and that the assertion of Paradise being exclusive is merely a dream and fantasy they nurture in their heads, He states the primary and fundamental criterion for entering Paradise as a general law, saying: "Yes, whoever submits his face to Allah and is a doer of good, his reward is certain with his Lord" (بَلى‌ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُ لِلَّهِ وَ هُوَ مُحْسِنٌ فَلَهُ أَجْرُهُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِ‌).

And consequently, "such people shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve" (وَ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ‌).

In short, Paradise, Allah's reward, and the attainment of eternal salvation are not the monopoly of any particular tribe; rather, they belong to those who possess two conditions:

In the first stage, absolute submission to the command of Truth and the abandonment of discrimination regarding Divine laws; it should not be that they accept whatever command aligns with their interests and cast behind their backs whatever opposes them. They are completely submitted to the Truth.

In the next stage, the effects of this faith are reflected in their actions in the form of performing good deeds; they are doers of good (beneficent), toward everyone and in all programs.

In reality, with this statement, the Quran completely rejects the issue of racism and misplaced prejudices, and removes salvation and happiness from the monopoly of a specific tribe.

Furthermore, it specifies the criterion for salvation, which is faith and righteous action.

Key Points

1- The Meaning of "Amāniyyuhum"

"Amāniyy" (امانی) is the plural of "Umniyyah" (امنیة), meaning a desire or wish that a person does not reach.

Of course, here these claimants among the People of the Book had no more than one wish, which was the exclusivity of Paradise for themselves; however, since this wish is itself the source of other wishes and has, so to speak, other branches and leaves, it is mentioned in the plural form (Amāniyy).

2- The Meaning of "Wajh" (Face)

It is noteworthy that in the above verse, submission (Islam) is attributed to the "face" (they submit their faces to Allah). This is because the clearest evidence of submission to something is for a person to turn toward it with their entire face.

There is also the possibility that "face" (Wajh) means "essence" (Dhat), meaning they are submitted to the command of the Lord with their entire being.

3- The above verses also teach all Muslims this point: never to yield to baseless words in any matter, and to demand proof from anyone who makes a claim. In this way, the barrier of blind imitation is broken, and logical thinking prevails over their society.

4- The Meaning of the Phrase "وَ هُوَ مُحْسِنٌ" (And he is a doer of good)

Mentioning the phrase "and he is a doer of good" after stating the issue of submission is an indication that as long as deep-rooted faith does not exist, "doing good" in the broad sense of the word will not be achieved.

Furthermore, this phrase shows that doing good for these people of faith is not a fleeting act, but has become their attribute and has penetrated the depths of their souls.

The reason for the negation of fear and grief from the followers of the path of Monotheism (Tawhid) is clear, for they fear only Allah and are terrified of nothing else. However, superstitious polytheists fear everything: the words of this person or that, bad omens, superstitious traditions, and many other things.

Ayah

وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ لَيْسَتِ ٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ وَقَالَتِ ٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ لَيْسَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ وَهُمْ يَتْلُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ مِثْلَ قَوْلِهِمْ ۚ فَٱللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (١١٣)
The Jews say, ‘The Christians stand on nothing,’ and the Christians say, ‘The Jews stand on nothing,’ though they follow the [same] Book. So said those who had no knowledge, [words] similar to what they say. Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ. (113)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators narrated from Ibn Abbas as follows: When a group of Christians from "Najran" came to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, a number of Jewish scholars were also present there. A dispute and argument broke out between them and the Christians in the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

"Rafi' bin Harmalah," one of the Jews, turned to the Christian group and said: "Your religion has no foundation," and he denied the prophethood of Jesus عليه السلام and his book, the Gospel. A man from the Christians of Najran repeated this exact sentence in response to that Jew, saying that the Jewish religion has no foundation. At this moment, the above verse was revealed and blamed both groups for their incorrect speech. [^1]

Commentary

Contradictions Arising from Exclusivism

In the previous verses, we saw a glimpse of the baseless claims made by a group of Jews and Christians. The verse under discussion shows that when baseless claims are involved, the result is exclusivism and subsequently, contradiction.

It says: "The Jews said: The Christians have no standing with Allah, and the Christians said: The Jews have no standing and are upon falsehood" (وَ قالَتِ الْيَهُودُ لَيْسَتِ النَّصارى‌ عَلى‌ شَيْ‌ءٍ وَ قالَتِ النَّصارى‌ لَيْسَتِ الْيَهُودُ عَلى‌ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

The phrase "is not... upon anything" (ليست ... على شى‌ء) refers to the idea that they have no status in the presence of Allah, or that their religion and creed are of no significance.

Then it adds: "They say these words while they recite the [Heavenly] Book!" (وَ هُمْ يَتْلُونَ الْكِتابَ‌).

This means that while possessing Divine Books that could guide them in these matters, such words—which have no source other than prejudice, enmity, and stubbornness—are very strange.

[^1]: Tafsir "Majma' al-Bayan," Tafsir "al-Qurtubi," and Tafsir "al-Manar," under the aforementioned verses.

Then the Quran adds: "The ignorant polytheists also said the same thing as they say" (even though these are People of the Book and those are idol-worshippers) (كَذلِكَ قالَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ مِثْلَ قَوْلِهِمْ‌).

This verse identifies the primary source of prejudice as ignorance and lack of knowledge, for ignorant individuals are always confined within their own environment and do not accept anything else. They become deeply attached to the creed they have been familiar with since childhood, however superstitious and baseless it may be, and they deny everything else.

At the end of the verse, it states: "Allah will judge their dispute on the Day of Resurrection" (فَاللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ‌).

It is there that truths become clearer and the evidence and documents for everything are manifest; no one can deny the truth, and in this way, disputes will be settled. Indeed, one of the characteristics of the Day of Resurrection is the ending of disputes.

Furthermore, the above verse gives encouragement to Muslims that if the followers of these religions have risen to oppose them and negate their faith, they should never be worried. They do not even accept one another; each strikes the other with the rod of negation. Fundamentally, ignorance is the source of prejudice, and prejudice is the source of exclusivism.

Ayah

وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن مَّنَعَ مَسَـٰجِدَ ٱللَّهِ أَن يُذْكَرَ فِيهَا ٱسْمُهُۥ وَسَعَىٰ فِى خَرَابِهَآ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَا كَانَ لَهُمْ أَن يَدْخُلُوهَآ إِلَّا خَآئِفِينَ ۚ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا خِزْىٌ وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١١٤)
Who is a greater wrongdoer than those who deny access to the mosques of Allah lest His Name be celebrated therein, and try to ruin them? Such ones may not enter them, except in fear. There is disgrace for them in this world and a great punishment in the Hereafter. (114)

Occasion of Revelation

In the book "Asbab al-Nuzul," it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that this verse was revealed regarding "Phultos" the Roman and his Christian companions; they fought against the Children of Israel, burned the Torah, took their children into captivity, destroyed the Holy House (Bayt al-Maqdis), and cast carcasses into it.

The late Tabarsi in "Majma' al-Bayan" quotes from Ibn Abbas that this effort to destroy and annihilate Bayt al-Maqdis continued until the time it was conquered by the Muslims.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we also read that this verse was revealed regarding the Quraysh, at the time when they prevented the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله from entering the city of Mecca and Masjid al-Haram.

Some have suggested a third occasion of revelation for the verse, which is that it refers to the places Muslims had for prayer in Mecca, which the polytheists destroyed after the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله migration [1]. There is no obstacle to the revelation of the verse being observant of all these events; therefore, each of the above occasions of revelation reflects one dimension of the issue.

Commentary

The Most Unjust of People

An analysis of the above occasions of revelation shows that the discourse in the verse is addressed to all three groups: the Jews, the Christians, and the polytheists, even though the discussions in the preceding verses referred more to the Jews and occasionally to the Christians.

In any case, the "Jews," by creating doubts regarding the issue of the change of Qiblah, sought to have the Muslims pray toward Bayt al-Maqdis so they could maintain a sense of superiority over them and cause Masjid al-Haram and the Kaaba to lose their prominence [2]. The "polytheists of Mecca" also took steps toward the destruction of this divine edifice by preventing the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims from visiting the House of Allah.

The "Christians" also strove for its destruction by seizing Bayt al-Maqdis and creating the deplorable situation mentioned above from Ibn Abbas.

The Quran, in the face of these three groups and all those who walk a similar path, says: "And who is more unjust than he who prevents the name of Allah from being mentioned in the mosques of Allah and strives for their destruction?"

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan and Al-Mizan, under the verse being discussed. [2]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, under the verse.

(وَ مَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنْ مَنَعَ مَساجِدَ اللَّهِ أَنْ يُذْكَرَ فِيهَا اسْمُهُ وَ سَعى‌ فِي خَرابِها).

In this way, the Quran introduces this prevention as a great injustice and its perpetrators as the most unjust of people. Indeed, what injustice is greater than striving to destroy the strongholds of monotheism, hindering people from the remembrance of the Truth, and spreading polytheism and corruption in society?

Then, in the continuation of this verse, It says: "It is not fitting for them to enter these places except in fear and dread" (أُولئِكَ ما كانَ لَهُمْ أَنْ يَدْخُلُوها إِلَّا خائِفِينَ‌).

This means that the Muslims and monotheists of the world must stand so firm that the hands of these oppressors are cut off from these holy places, and none of them can enter these sacred sites openly and without fear and dread.

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the above sentence that such unjust individuals will never succeed in keeping these centers of worship under their control. Rather, the end will be such that they cannot set foot in them except with terror—exactly the fate that the polytheists of Mecca faced regarding Masjid al-Haram.

At the end of the verse, It describes the punishment of this world and the hereafter for these oppressors with a shaking expression, saying: "For them in this world is disgrace, and for them in the hereafter is a great punishment" (لَهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيا خِزْيٌ وَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ‌).

And this is the fate of those who wish to create a separation between the servants and their God.

Key Points

1- Methods of Destroying Mosques

Undoubtedly, the concept of the above verse is broad and extensive and is not limited to a specific time or place, just like other verses that were revealed under specific circumstances but whose ruling remains constant throughout all centuries and ages. Therefore, anyone or any group that strives in any way to destroy the mosques of Allah or prevents the name of Allah and His worship from being performed there is subject to the same disgrace and the same great punishment mentioned in the verse.

It is also necessary to note that preventing entry into the mosque, the mention of the Lord's name, and striving for its destruction is not only by, for example, destroying its structure with shovels and pickaxes; rather, any action that results in the destruction of mosques or their loss of prominence is also subject to this ruling.

For in the commentary of the verse: "Only he shall maintain the mosques of Allah..." (Surah At-Tawbah, Verse 18), as will be discussed, according to the explicit text of some narrations, the meaning of "maintaining" (imran) a mosque is not just its physical building, but presence in them and attention to the religious gatherings and circles formed within them which lead to the remembrance of Allah is also a type of maintenance—in fact, it is considered the most important form of maintenance.

Therefore, on the opposite side, whatever causes people to become heedless of the remembrance of Allah and stay away from mosques is a very great injustice!

It is strange that in our age and time, a group of ignorant, rigid, and illogical fanatics among the Wahhabis, under the pretext of reviving monotheism, strive to destroy some mosques and buildings erected over the graves of the greats of Islam and the righteous, which have always been centers for the remembrance of Allah. Even stranger is that these illogical oppressors carry out their actions under the title of fighting polytheism and commit various sins and major transgressions in this path.

While if, hypothetically, a wrong act is performed in one of these holy centers, that act should be prevented, not that these houses of monotheism should be brought to destruction—for this work is identical to the work of the polytheists of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah).

2- The Greatest Injustice

Another point that must be noted in this verse is that Allah has counted such persons as the most unjust of individuals, and in reality, it is so. For the closure and destruction of mosques and the prevention of centers of monotheism will have no result other than driving people toward irreligiousness, and we know that the harm of this work is greater than any other action and its grim consequences are more painful.

Of course, in other instances in the Quran, the word "Azlam" (the most unjust of people) has been used regarding some other sins, all of which in reality return to the issue of "polytheism" and the denial of monotheism.

You will read a further explanation of this discourse in Volume 5, page 183 (under Verse 21 of Surah Al-An'am).

Ayah

وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْمَشْرِقُ وَٱلْمَغْرِبُ ۚ فَأَيْنَمَا تُوَلُّوا۟ فَثَمَّ وَجْهُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (١١٥)
To Allah belong the east and the west: so whichever way you turn, there is the face of Allah! Allah is indeed all-bounteous, all-knowing. (115)

Occasion of Revelation

Various narrations have been cited regarding the occasion of the revelation of this verse:

Ibn Abbas states: This verse pertains to the change of the Qiblah. When the Qiblah of the Muslims was changed from Jerusalem to the Kaaba, the Jews rose in denial and criticized the Muslims, asking how it is possible to change the Qiblah. The verse was revealed and answered them that the East and the West of the world belong to Allah.

In another narration, we read: This verse was revealed regarding recommended (Mustahabb) prayers; whenever a person is riding a mount, they may perform recommended prayers in whichever direction they are traveling (even if their back is toward the Qiblah).

Others have narrated from Jabir that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent a group of Muslims to a battlefield. At night, when darkness enveloped everywhere, they were unable to identify the Qiblah, and each group prayed in a different direction. At sunrise, they realized they had all prayed toward a direction other than the Qiblah. They questioned the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the above verse was revealed, announcing to them that their prayers in such a state were valid (of course, this ruling has specific conditions mentioned in books of jurisprudence).

There is no obstacle to all the above occasions of revelation being applicable to the verse. The verse can simultaneously oversee the issue of the change of Qiblah, the performance of supererogatory (Nafilah) prayers on a mount, and obligatory prayers when the Qiblah is unknown. Furthermore, fundamentally, no verse is restricted to its specific occasion of revelation; its concept must be considered as a general rule, and many different rulings may be derived from it.

Commentary

Whichever Way You Turn, Allah is There!

In the previous verse, the discussion was about the oppressors who prevented people from the mosques of Allah and strove to destroy them. The verse under discussion follows that same theme, stating:

"To Allah belong the East and the West; so whichever way you turn, there is the Face of Allah" (وَ لِلَّهِ الْمَشْرِقُ وَ الْمَغْرِبُ فَأَيْنَما تُوَلُّوا فَثَمَّ وَجْهُ اللَّهِ‌).

It is not the case that if they prevent you from going to the mosques and the bases of monotheism, the path of worshipping Allah will be closed. No, the East and the West of this world belong to His Pure Essence, and whichever way you turn, He is there. Likewise, the change of Qiblah, which took place due to specific circumstances, has the slightest effect on this matter; is there any place that is void of Allah? Fundamentally, Allah has no physical location.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, He says: "Indeed, Allah is All-Encompassing, All-Knowing" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ واسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ‌).

It is necessary to note that the meaning of "East and West" in the above verse does not refer to two specific directions; rather, this expression is an allusion to all directions. Just as we might say, for example: "The enemies of Ali concealed his virtues out of enmity, and his friends did so out of fear, yet his virtues filled the East and the West of the world" (meaning the entire world). Perhaps the emphasis on the East and the West specifically is because the first directions a human recognizes are these two, and other directions are identified by means of the East and the West.

In the Holy Quran, we also read: وَ أَوْرَثْنَا الْقَوْمَ الَّذِينَ كانُوا يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشارِقَ الْأَرْضِ وَ مَغارِبَهَا: "And We made the people who were deemed weak to inherit the eastern and western parts of the land" (Al-A'raf, 137).

Key Points

1- The Philosophy of the Qiblah

The first question that arises here is: if Allah is present whichever way we turn, then what is the necessity of facing the Qiblah?

However, as we will point out later, facing the Qiblah never means limiting the Pure Essence of Allah to a specific direction. Rather, since man is a material being and must ultimately face some direction while praying, it has been commanded that everyone face one direction so that unity and harmony appear in the ranks of the Muslims, and chaos and fragmentation are prevented. Consider how unsightly and discordant it would be if everyone prayed in a different direction and formed scattered ranks.

Furthermore, the direction designated as the Qiblah (the direction of the Kaaba) is a sacred point that is among the oldest bases of monotheism, and turning toward it awakens monotheistic memories.

2- The Expression "وَجْهُ اللَّهِ‌" (The Face of Allah)

The expression "وَجْهُ اللَّهِ‌" does not mean the physical face of Allah; rather, "Wajh" (Face) here means "Essence" (Dhat).

3- In numerous narrations, we read that this verse has been cited to validate the prayers of those who prayed toward a direction other than the Kaaba by mistake or due to an inability to investigate. Similarly, this verse has been used to argue for the validity of praying while on a mount (for further explanation, refer to the book Wasa'il al-Shia, "The Book of Prayer," "Chapters of the Qiblah").

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَدًا ۗ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۖ بَل لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ كُلٌّ لَّهُۥ قَـٰنِتُونَ (١١٦)
And they say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He! Rather, to Him belongs whatever there is in the heavens and the earth. All are obedient to Him, (116)
بَدِيعُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (١١٧)
the Originator of the heavens and the earth. When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is. (117)

Commentary

The Superstitions of the Jews, Christians, and Polytheists

This superstitious belief that Allah has a child is accepted by the Christians, a group of the Jews, and the polytheists alike; all three groups believed that Allah has chosen a child for Himself.

In Surah At-Tawbah, verse 30, we read: وَ قالَتِ الْيَهُودُ عُزَيْرٌ ابْنُ اللَّهِ وَ قالَتِ النَّصارى‌ الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ اللَّهِ ذلِكَ قَوْلُهُمْ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ يُضاهِؤُنَ قَوْلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ قاتَلَهُمُ اللَّهُ أَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ‌: "The Jews said, 'Ezra is the son of Allah,' and the Christians said, 'The Messiah is the son of Allah.' That is their statement from their mouths; they imitate the saying of those who disbelieved before [them]. May Allah destroy them; how are they deluded?" In verse 68 of Surah Yunus, we also read regarding the polytheists: قالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَداً سُبْحانَهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُ‌: "They have said, 'Allah has taken a son.' Exalted is He; He is the Free of need." In many other verses of the Quran, this unjust attribution has been narrated from them.

The first verse under discussion, in order to crush this superstition, states: "They say, 'Allah has taken a son.' Exalted is He!" (وَ قالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَداً سُبْحانَهُ‌) from these unjust attributions.

What need does Allah have to choose a child for Himself? Is He in need? Is He limited? Does He require assistance? Does He need the survival of His lineage? "Rather, to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth" (بَلْ لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌). "And all are devoutly obedient to Him" (كُلٌّ لَهُ قانِتُونَ‌).

He is not only the Owner of all beings in the universe, but "He is the Originator of the heavens and the earth" (بَدِيعُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌). He initiated them all even without a prior blueprint and without the need for the existence of matter.

What need does He have for a child when "whenever He decrees a matter, He only says to it, 'Be,' and it is" (وَ إِذا قَضى‌ أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ‌).

Key Points

1- Arguments for the Negation of a Child

The claim that Allah has a child is undoubtedly the product of the weak thoughts of humans who compared God in every aspect to their own limited existence.

A human needs a child for various reasons: On one hand, his lifespan is limited, and the birth of a child is necessary for the continuation of the lineage. On the other hand, his power is limited, and especially in old age and weakness, he needs an assistant to help him in his affairs.
