Thirdly, emotional aspects and the spirit of seeking companionship necessitate that a human has a confidant in his living environment, which is also provided through children.

It is obvious that none of these matters make sense regarding Allah, who is the Creator of the universe, Powerful over all things, and Eternal and Everlasting. Furthermore, having a child necessitates being a physical body, from which Allah is also sanctified [1].

2- Commentary on the Phrase "كُنْ فَيَكُونُ‌" (Be, and it is)

This expression appears in several verses of the Quran, including Surah Al-Imran, verses 47 and 59; Surah Al-An'am, verse 73; Surah An-Nahl, verse 40; Surah Maryam, verse 35; Surah Ya-Sin, verse 82, and others.

This sentence speaks of the creative will (iradah takwini) of Allah and His sovereignty in the matter of creation.

To explain: the meaning of the phrase "كُنْ فَيَكُونُ‌" (Be, and it immediately becomes) is not that Allah issues a verbal command with the meaning "Be." Rather, it means that when His will attaches to the existence of something—whether it be large or small, complex or simple, the size of an atom or the size of the entirety of the heavens and the earth—it is realized without the need for any other cause. There is not even a moment's gap between this will and the emergence of that being.

Fundamentally, time cannot exist in between, and for this reason, the letter "fa" (in the word fayakun), which usually denotes a chronological delay coupled with connection, here only signifies a delay in rank (as proven in philosophy that the effect is posterior to its cause, not in a chronological sense, but in a rank-based sense—pay close attention).

Let there be no misunderstanding: the meaning is not that everything Allah wills becomes existent at that very moment; rather, the meaning is that in whatever way He wills, it becomes existent in that exact manner.

For example, if He wills that the heavens and the earth come into existence in six periods, they will certainly come into existence in that duration without any deficiency. And if He wills them to exist in a single moment, they will all exist in a single moment. This depends on how He wills and what He deems beneficial.

Or, for instance, when Allah wills that a fetus in the mother's womb undergoes its developmental period for exactly nine months and nine days, it is completed without a moment's increase or decrease. And if He wills this developmental period to take place in less than a thousandth of a second, it will certainly happen that way, because His will is the "Complete Cause" (al-illah al-tammah) for creation, and there can be no gap between the Complete Cause and the existence of the effect.

3- How does something come into existence from non-existence?

The word "Badi'" is from the root "bad'" meaning to bring something into existence without precedent. This indicates that Allah brought the heavens and the earth into existence without any prior matter or model.

Now this question arises: how can something come into existence from non-existence? Since non-existence is the contradiction of existence, how can it be the cause and origin of existence? And can it truly be believed that "nothingness" becomes the source of "being"? This is the same objection raised by materialists regarding the issue of "origination" (ibda'), and from it, they conclude that the primary matter of the world is eternal and everlasting, and it absolutely neither comes into being nor ceases to exist.

Answer

In the first stage, this very objection applies to the materialists themselves. To explain: they believe that the matter of this world is ancient and eternal and that nothing has been reduced from it so far. They claim that the changes we see in the world are only in its "form," which is constantly changing, not the original matter. We ask them: the current form of matter, which certainly did not exist before, how did it come into existence? Did it come from non-existence? If so, then how can "non-existence" be the origin of the "existence" of a form? (Pay close attention).

For example: a painter draws a beautiful landscape with a pen and color on paper. The materialists say: the colored matter existed, but this landscape and this "form" which did not exist before, how did it come into being? Whatever answer they give for the emergence of "form" from "non-existence," we will give the same answer regarding "matter."

In the second stage, it must be noted that the mistake arises from the word "from." They imagine that when we say "the world came into being from non-existence," it is like saying "the table is made from wood," where in making the table, the wood must exist beforehand for the table to be made. However, the meaning of the sentence "the world came into being from non-existence" is not this; rather, it means "the world did not exist before, then it became existent." Do you see any contradiction or paradox in this statement?

In philosophical terms: every contingent being (that which does not have existence from its own essence) is composed of two aspects: "essence" (mahiyah) and "existence" (wujud). "Essence" is a "conceptual meaning" whose relationship to existence and non-existence is equal. In other words, the common denominator obtained from considering the existence and non-existence of something is called "essence." For example, this tree did not exist previously and now it does; so-and-so did not exist previously and now has come into existence. That which we subjected to the two states of existence and non-existence is the "essence."

Therefore, the meaning of the statement "Allah brought the world into existence from non-existence" is that Allah brought the essence from the state of non-existence to the state of existence, or in other words, He clothed the "essence" in the garment of "existence" [1].

[1]: For further explanation, refer to the book "Afaridegar-e Jahan" (The Creator of the World). [2]: Regarding this topic, we have also discussed under verse 26 of Surah Al-Anbiya (Volume 13 of Tafsir Nemouneh).

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ لَوْلَا يُكَلِّمُنَا ٱللَّهُ أَوْ تَأْتِينَآ ءَايَةٌ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّثْلَ قَوْلِهِمْ ۘ تَشَـٰبَهَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ قَدْ بَيَّنَّا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ (١١٨)
Those who have no knowledge say, ‘Why does not Allah speak to us, or come to us a sign?’ So said those who were before them, [words] similar to what they say. Alike are their hearts. We have certainly made the signs clear for a people who have certainty. (118)
إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا ۖ وَلَا تُسْـَٔلُ عَنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١١٩)
Indeed We have sent you with the truth, as a bearer of good news and as a warner, and you will not be questioned concerning the inmates of hell. (119)

Commentary

Another Excuse: Why Does Allah Not Speak to Us?!

In connection with the excuses of the Jews mentioned in the preceding verses, the text now speaks of another group of excuse-seekers who were apparently the Arab polytheists. It says:

"And those who do not know said: 'Why does Allah not speak to us? Or why does a sign (miracle) not come to us?'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ لَوْ لا يُكَلِّمُنَا اللَّهُ أَوْ تَأْتِينا آيَةٌ).

In reality, this group, whom the Quran refers to as "those who do not know" (الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ), had two illogical demands:

  1. Why does Allah not speak to us directly?
  2. Why is a sign (miracle) not revealed specifically to us?

In response to these stubborn and egoistic claims, the Quran says: "Likewise, those before them spoke similar words; their hearts and thoughts are alike. But We have made the signs clear (in sufficient measure) for those who seek the truth and possess certainty" (كَذلِكَ قالَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِثْلَ قَوْلِهِمْ تَشابَهَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ قَدْ بَيَّنَّا الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ).

If their intention were truly to perceive the truth and reality, these very verses that We have revealed to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله are a clear sign of the truthfulness of his words. What necessity is there for signs to be revealed directly and independently to every single individual? And what sense does it make for me to insist that God must speak directly to me?! We read a similar sentiment in Surah Al-Muddaththir, verse 52: "Nay, every one of them desires that he should be given pages spread out!" (بَلْ يُرِيدُ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُؤْتى‌ صُحُفاً مُنَشَّرَةً). What an out-of-place expectation!

Fundamentally, this matter—besides being entirely unnecessary—is contrary to the Wisdom of the Lord. Firstly, proving the truthfulness of the Prophets to all people is perfectly possible through the signs revealed to the Prophets themselves.

Secondly, the descent of signs and miracles upon everyone is not possible; it requires a certain type of merit, readiness, and purity of soul. This is exactly like all the wires in a city's vast electrical grid (including both high-voltage and very thin wires) expecting that the same extraordinarily powerful electricity transmitted to the primary heavy cables should also be transmitted to them.

Certainly, this expectation is wrong and misplaced. The engineer who arranged those wires to perform various functions has considered the share of each; some receive power directly from the generator, and some receive it indirectly at various voltages.

The next verse addresses the Prophet and defines his duty in the face of requested miracles and other excuses, saying: "Verily, We have sent you with the truth as a bearer of glad tidings and a warner (to the people of the world)" (إِنَّا أَرْسَلْناكَ بِالْحَقِّ بَشِيراً وَ نَذِيراً).

You have the duty to convey Our commands to all people, show them the miracles, and explain the truths with logic. This invitation must be accompanied by the encouragement of the righteous and the warning of the wicked; this is your duty.

"But if a group among them does not believe after this mission is fulfilled, you are not responsible for the misguidance of the dwellers of the Blazing Fire" (وَ لا تُسْئَلُ عَنْ أَصْحابِ الْجَحِيمِ).

Key Points

1- Their Hearts Are Alike

In the verses above, we read that the Quran says: these excuses are not new; the deviant nations of the past also said the same things, as if their hearts were fashioned exactly alike. This expression also points to the fact that the passage of time and the teachings of the Prophets should have had the effect of making future generations possess a greater share of awareness and set aside baseless words that are signs of ultimate ignorance. However, unfortunately, this group has gained no share from this evolutionary program.

They continue to stagnate, as if they belong to thousands of years ago, and the passage of time has not given the slightest jolt to their thinking.

2- Two Important Educational Principles

"Glad tidings" (Basharat) and "Warning" (Inzar), or "Encouragement" and "Threat," constitute a major part of educational motivations and social movements. A human being must be "encouraged" for performing a good deed and "punished" for an evil deed, so that they may find greater readiness to traverse the first path and refrain from stepping onto the second.

"Encouragement" alone is not sufficient for the perfection of an individual or society, because in that case, the person feels certain that committing a sin poses no danger to them.

For example, we see that the current followers of Christ عليه السلام believe in "Atonement" (Fida) and hold that Prophet Jesus عليه السلام was sacrificed for their sins. Even their leaders sometimes sell them certificates for Paradise and sometimes forgive their sins on behalf of God! Certainly, such a population will commit sins easily.

In the "Bible Dictionary," we read: "...Atonement also refers to the ransom of the precious blood of Christ, upon whom the sins of all of us were placed, and he bore our sins in his body on the cross!" Certainly, this incorrect logic makes individuals bold in committing sins.

In short, those who imagine that encouragement alone is sufficient for human education (whether for children or adults) and that punishment, threats, and penalties should be completely washed away and set aside are gravely mistaken. Similarly, those who base education solely on fear and threats and are negligent of the encouraging aspects are also misguided and unaware.

Both of these groups are mistaken in their understanding of the human being, for they do not realize that a human is a composite of fear and hope, of self-love and a desire for life, and a hatred for annihilation and non-existence. He is a combination of seeking benefit and repelling harm. Is it possible for a human being, whose spiritual dimensions are formed by these two, to have his education based on only one part?

Specifically, a balance between these two is necessary; if encouragement and hope exceed the limit, they lead to audacity and negligence, and if fear and warning are excessive, the result is despair, hopelessness, and the extinguishing of the flames of love and movement.

For this very reason, in the verses of the Quran, "Warner" (Nadhir) and "Giver of Glad Tidings" (Bashir), or "Warning" and "Glad Tidings," are placed side by side. Sometimes "Glad Tidings" precedes "Warning," as in the verse under discussion (بَشِيراً وَ نَذِيراً), and sometimes conversely, "Warning" precedes "Glad Tidings," as in verse 188 of Surah Al-A'raf: "I am but a warner and a bearer of glad tidings to a people who believe" (إِنْ أَنَا إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ وَ بَشِيرٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ).

Although in most Quranic verses, glad tidings are mentioned first, this may also be because, overall, Allah's mercy precedes His punishment and wrath (O He whose mercy precedes His wrath - يا من سبقت رحمته غضبه).

Ayahs

وَلَن تَرْضَىٰ عَنكَ ٱلْيَهُودُ وَلَا ٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ حَتَّىٰ تَتَّبِعَ مِلَّتَهُمْ ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ هُوَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ ۗ وَلَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَآءَهُم بَعْدَ ٱلَّذِى جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ ۙ مَا لَكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (١٢٠)
Never will the Jews be pleased with you, nor the Christians, unless you followed their creed. Say, ‘Indeed it is the guidance of Allah which is [true] guidance.’ And should you follow their desires after the knowledge that has come to you, you will not have against Allah any guardian or helper. (120)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَتْلُونَهُۥ حَقَّ تِلَاوَتِهِۦٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۗ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (١٢١)
Those to whom We have given the Book follow it as it ought to be followed: they have faith in it. As for those who deny it—it is they who are the losers. (121)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation for the first verse, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that the Jews of Medina and the Christians of Najran expected the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله to always agree with them regarding the Qiblah. When Allah turned the Qiblah of the Muslims from Jerusalem toward the Kaaba, they became despondent toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (and perhaps among them, some groups of Muslims objected that one should not act in a way that causes offense to the Jews and Christians) [^1]. The above verse was revealed, announcing to the Prophet that these groups of Jews and Christians will not be satisfied with you through coordination in the Qiblah or anything else, unless you accept their creed entirely.

Others have narrated that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was very insistent on satisfying these two groups, hoping they might accept Islam. The above verse was revealed and informed the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to put this thought out of his mind, for they will not be satisfied at any price except by following their creed [^2]. Regarding the occasion of revelation for the second verse, there are also various narrations: some commentators believe this verse was revealed concerning the individuals who came from Abyssinia with Ja'far ibn Abi Talib and were among those who joined him there. They were forty people: thirty-two from Abyssinia and eight monks from Syria, among whom was the famous monk "Bahira."

Others believe the verse was revealed concerning individuals from the Jews, such as "Abdullah ibn Salam," "Sa'id ibn Amr," "Tammam ibn Yahuda," and their like, who accepted Islam and became true believers [^3].

Commentary

Gaining the Satisfaction of This Group is Impossible

Since the previous verse absolved the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of responsibility regarding the stubborn misguided ones, the above verses continue this discussion by telling the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi and Tafsir Fakhr Razi (with slight differences). [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed. [^3]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh and Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

Do not insist on gaining the satisfaction of the Jews and Christians, for "Never will the Jews nor the Christians be satisfied with you until you follow their creed" (وَلَنْ تَرْضَىٰ عَنْكَ الْيَهُودُ وَلَا النَّصَارَىٰ حَتَّىٰ تَتَّبِعَ مِلَّتَهُمْ).

You have the duty "to tell them that guidance is only the guidance of Allah" (قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى اللَّهِ هُوَ الْهُدَىٰ).

A guidance that is not mixed with superstitions and the debased thoughts of ignorant individuals; yes, such pure guidance must be followed.

Then He adds: "If you were to follow their desires after the knowledge that has come to you through Divine revelation, you would have no protector or helper against Allah" (وَلَئِنِ اتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَاءَهُمْ بَعْدَ الَّذِي جَاءَكَ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ مَا لَكَ مِنَ اللَّهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ).

However, since a group of truth-seekers among the Jews and Christians responded to the call of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and accepted this religion, the Quran, after censuring the previous group, mentions these people favorably and says: "Those to whom We have given the Book and who recite it as it ought to be recited (which involves reflection, contemplation, and then action) believe in the Prophet of Islam عليه السلام" (الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ يَتْلُونَهُ حَقَّ تِلَاوَتِهِ أُولَٰئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ). "And those who disbelieve in it have wronged themselves; they are the losers" (وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِهِ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْخَاسِرُونَ).

These were the ones who truly fulfilled the right of reciting their own Heavenly Book, and that became the cause of their guidance, because they found the glad tidings of the appearance of the Promised Prophet—which they had read in those books—to be consistent with the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and they submitted, and Allah has appreciated them.

Key Points

[Commentary on the phrase "And if you follow their desires"]

1- Question: The phrase "And if you follow their desires" (وَلَئِنِ اتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَاءَهُمْ) might raise a question for some: is it possible for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, with his station of infallibility (Ismah), to follow the desires of the deviant Jews?

Answer: We say: such expressions, which are frequently seen in the verses of the Quran, are in no way incompatible with the station of the infallibility of the Prophets. Firstly, it is a conditional sentence, and a conditional sentence does not imply the occurrence of the condition.

Secondly, being infallible does not make sin impossible for the Prophets; rather, the Prophet and the Imam, while having the power to sin and not having their free will stripped from them, never stain themselves with sin. In other words, they have the power to sin, but their faith, knowledge, and piety are at such a level that they never go toward sin. Therefore, warnings such as the one above are entirely appropriate regarding them. Thirdly, although this address is directed at the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the intended audience may be all people.

2- Gaining the Enemy's Satisfaction Has a Limit

It is true that one should invite enemies toward the truth using the attractive force of ethics, but this applies to flexible individuals. However, there are those who will never submit to the word of truth; one should not be concerned with gaining their satisfaction. It is here that if they do not believe, one must say: "To hell with them!" and one should not waste time on them in vain.

3- Guidance is Only Divine Guidance

From the above verses, this reality is also inferred: the only law that can be the source of salvation for humanity is the law and guidance of Allah. For no matter how much human knowledge evolves, it remains mixed with ignorance, doubt, and inadequacy in various aspects. Guidance found in the light of such imperfect knowledge will not be absolute guidance. Only He can lead a program of "absolute guidance" who possesses "absolute knowledge" and is free from ignorance and inadequacy, and He is only Allah.

4- What is the Right of Recitation?

This expression is a very meaningful one and defines a clear path for us regarding the Holy Quran and Heavenly Books, for people are divided into several groups regarding these Divine verses:

One group's entire insistence is on the pronunciation of words and letters from their correct points of articulation. They are constantly thinking about pauses, connections, the rules of Yarmalun, Shaddah, and Madd, and they give the least importance to the content and meaning, let alone acting upon it. According to the Quran, these are like an animal carrying books (كَمَثَلِ الْحِمَارِ يَحْمِلُ أَسْفَارًا - Surah Al-Jumu'ah, Verse 5).

Another group goes beyond the words and scrutinizes the meanings, reflecting on the subtleties and points of the Quran and being aware of its sciences, but there is no sign of action!

But there is a third group who are the true believers. They have accepted the Quran as a book of action and a complete program for life. They consider reading the words, reflecting on the meanings, and understanding the concepts of this great Book as a prelude to action. Therefore, whenever they read the Quran, a new spirit is born within them—new determination, new will, new readiness, and new deeds—and this is the "right of recitation."

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the commentary of this verse, we read that he said:

"It means they recite its verses clearly, understand it deeply, act upon its rulings, hope for its promise, fear its threat, take lessons from its stories, obey its commands, and accept its prohibitions. By Allah, it does not mean memorizing its verses, studying its letters, reciting its Surahs, and studying its A'shar and Akhmas [1]—they memorized its letters but neglected its boundaries. It only means to contemplate the verses of the Quran and act upon its rulings, as Allah says: 'This is a blessed Book which We have revealed to you so that they may contemplate its verses.'" [2]

[^1]: "A'shar" and "Akhmas" refer to divisions made in the Quran, such as the division into thirty parts (Juz) or each part into four parties (Hizb) and the like. [^2]: Quoted from Irshad al-Daylami as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan, under the verses being discussed.

Ayahs

يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتِىَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَأَنِّى فَضَّلْتُكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٢٢)
O Children of Israel, remember My blessing which I bestowed upon you, and that I gave you an advantage over all the nations. (122)
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ يَوْمًا لَّا تَجْزِى نَفْسٌ عَن نَّفْسٍ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يُقْبَلُ مِنْهَا عَدْلٌ وَلَا تَنفَعُهَا شَفَـٰعَةٌ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (١٢٣)
Beware of the Day when no soul will compensate for another, neither will any ransom be accepted from it, nor will any intercession benefit it, nor will they be helped. (123)

Commentary

Once again, Allah addresses the Children of Israel and enumerates His blessings upon them, particularly reminding them of the superiority and excellence He granted them over the people of their time.

He says: "O Children of Israel! Remember My favor which I bestowed upon you, and that I preferred you over the worlds (over all the people who lived during that time)" (يا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتِيَ الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ أَنِّي فَضَّلْتُكُمْ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ‌).

However, since no blessing comes without responsibility—rather, Allah places a duty and commitment upon man's shoulders in exchange for every gift He bestows—He warns them in the next verse, saying: "And fear a Day when no soul shall suffice for another soul at all" (وَ اتَّقُوا يَوْماً لا تَجْزِي نَفْسٌ عَنْ نَفْسٍ شَيْئاً).

"And nothing in the form of compensation or ransom, to act as a shield for them, shall be accepted" (وَ لا يُقْبَلُ مِنْها عَدْلٌ‌).

"And no intercession (except by the permission of the Lord) shall benefit it" (وَ لا تَنْفَعُها شَفاعَةٌ).

And if you think that anyone there—other than Allah—can help a person, it is a mistake, because "they shall not be helped" (وَ لا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ‌).

Therefore, all the paths of salvation that you resort to in this world are closed; only one single path remains open, and that is the path of faith and righteous deeds, and repenting for sins and reforming oneself.

Since these exact issues were discussed in verses 47 and 48 of this same Surah (with a slight change in expression) and we have discussed them there in detail, we shall suffice with what has been mentioned above.

Ayah

۞ وَإِذِ ٱبْتَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ رَبُّهُۥ بِكَلِمَـٰتٍ فَأَتَمَّهُنَّ ۖ قَالَ إِنِّى جَاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِمَامًا ۖ قَالَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِى ۖ قَالَ لَا يَنَالُ عَهْدِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٢٤)
When his Lord tested Abraham with certain words and he fulfilled them, He said, ‘I am making you the Imam of mankind.’ Said he, ‘And from among my descendants?’ He said, ‘My pledge does not extend to the unjust.’ (124)

Commentary

"Imamate," the Pinnacle of Ibrahim’s (عليه السلام) Honor

From these verses onward, the discourse turns to Ibrahim, the great Prophet of Allah and the hero of monotheism, the construction of the Kaaba, and the significance of this great center of Tawhid and worship; these matters are enumerated across eighteen verses.

The objective of these verses is effectively threefold:

First, to serve as an introduction to the issue of the change of the Qiblah (direction of prayer) which will be discussed later, so that Muslims may know that this Kaaba is a legacy of Ibrahim, the idol-breaking Prophet. If today the polytheists and idolaters have turned it into a house of idols, this is a superficial contamination and does not diminish the value or status of the Kaaba.

Second, the Jews and Christians claimed to be the heirs of Ibrahim and his religion. These verses (in connection with the numerous verses previously discussed regarding the Jews) clarify to what extent they are estranged from the religion of Ibrahim.

Third, the Arab polytheists also claimed an unbreakable bond between themselves and Ibrahim; they too must be made to understand that their practices have no connection to the program of this great idol-breaking Prophet.

In the verse under discussion, it first says: "And remember when Ibrahim was tested by his Lord with various means, and he fulfilled them [successfully]" (وَ إِذِ ابْتَلى‌ إِبْراهِيمَ رَبُّهُ بِكَلِماتٍ فَأَتَمَّهُنَ‌).

Indeed, this verse speaks of one of the most important chapters of Ibrahim’s (عليه السلام) life—namely, his great trials and his victory in the arena of testing; trials that fully defined the greatness of Ibrahim’s status and personality and revealed the value of his existence.

When he successfully emerged from these trials, Allah was to grant him a reward: "He said: 'Indeed, I will make you an Imam (leader) for mankind'" (قالَ إِنِّي جاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِماماً).

"Ibrahim requested: 'And from my offspring [as well]?'" so that this chain of Prophethood and Imamate would not be severed and would not be limited to his person alone (قالَ وَ مِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِي‌).

However, Allah replied to him: "My covenant—meaning the status of Imamate—shall not reach the wrongdoers" (قالَ لا يَنالُ عَهْدِي الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

I accepted your request, but only those among your offspring who are pure and infallible (Ma'sum) are worthy of this station!

Key Points

There are several important subjects here that must be examined precisely:

1- What is meant by "Words" (Kalimat)?

From an examination of the Quranic verses and the significant, remarkable deeds performed by Ibrahim which earned Allah's praise, it is inferred that the "Words" (the commands by which Allah tested Ibrahim) were a series of heavy and difficult responsibilities that God placed upon Ibrahim’s shoulders. This sincere Prophet performed all of them in the most excellent manner. These commands included:

Taking his son to the place of sacrifice and being seriously prepared to sacrifice him by Allah's command! Taking his wife and child and leaving them in the dry, waterless, and barren land of Mecca where not a single person resided! Rising against the idolaters of Babylon, breaking the idols, defending [the faith] most courageously in that historic trial, being cast into the heart of the fire, and maintaining complete composure and faith throughout all these stages! Emigrating from the land of the idolaters, turning his back on his life, and entering distant lands to fulfill his mission, and the like. [^1] Truly, each of these was a very heavy and difficult test, but with the power and strength of faith, he overcame them all and proved his worthiness for the station of "Imamate."

2- Who is an Imam?

From the verse under discussion, it is generally inferred that the station of Imamate granted to Ibrahim after his victory in all these trials was superior to the station of Prophethood (Nubuwwah) and Messengership (Risalah).

To explain: Imamate has various meanings:

1- "Imamate" in the sense of leadership and headship in the worldly affairs of the people (as the Sunnis say).

[^1]: In the Al-Manar commentary, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that he extracted these trials of Ibrahim from various verses in four chapters of the Quran, totaling thirty subjects (Al-Manar, under the verses discussed). However, the essence is what was mentioned above.

2- "Imamate" in the sense of leadership in both religious and worldly affairs (as some others among them have interpreted).

3- Imamate consists of the realization of religious programs, including governance in the broad sense of the word, the implementation of Allah's limits and laws, the execution of social justice, as well as the cultivation and refinement of souls both "outwardly" and "inwardly." This station is higher than the station of Messengership and Prophethood, because "Prophethood" and "Messengership" are merely receiving news from Allah, conveying His command, and giving glad tidings and warnings. However, in the case of "Imamate," all of these exist plus the "implementation of laws" and the "cultivation of souls outwardly and inwardly" (it is clear, of course, that many Prophets also held the station of Imamate).

In reality, the station of Imamate is the station of realizing the goals of religion and guidance in the sense of "conveying to the objective" (Isal ila al-Matlub), not just "showing the way" (Ira'at al-Tariq).

Furthermore, it includes "ontological guidance" (Hidayat-e Takwini), meaning the inward influence and spiritual authority of the Imam and the radiance of his existence into the hearts of prepared individuals and their spiritual guidance (reflect carefully).

In this regard, the Imam is exactly like the sun, which nourishes plants with its life-giving rays and bestows soul and life upon living beings. The role of the Imam in spiritual life is this very role.

In the Holy Quran, we read: "It is He who confers blessing upon you, and His angels [ask Him to do so] that He may bring you out from darknesses into the light. And He is ever, to the believers, Merciful" (Al-Ahzab: 43).

From this verse, it is well understood that Allah's special mercies and the unseen assistance of the angels can lead believers from darkness into light.

This subject is also true regarding the Imam; the inward power of the Imam and the great Prophets who also held the station of Imamate, and their successors, has a profound effect on training capable and ready individuals and bringing them out from the darkness of ignorance and misguidance toward the light of guidance.

There is no doubt that the meaning of Imamate in the verse under discussion is the third meaning, because it is inferred from several Quranic verses that the concept of "guidance" is embedded in the concept of Imamate, as we read in Surah As-Sajdah, verse 24: "And We made from among them Imams guiding by Our command when they were patient and when they were certain of Our signs."

This guidance is not in the sense of "showing the way," because Ibrahim already possessed the station of Prophethood, Messengership, and guidance in the sense of showing the way.

In conclusion, clear evidence testifies that the station of Imamate, which was granted to Ibrahim after difficult examinations and traversing the stages of certainty, courage, and steadfastness, was something other than the station of guidance in the sense of glad tidings, conveyance, and warning.

Thus, the guidance embedded in the concept of Imamate is nothing but "conveying to the objective," "realizing the spirit of religion," and implementing educational programs within prepared souls.

This reality is summarized in a meaningful and interesting Hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام):

"Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted, took Ibrahim as a servant before He took him as a Prophet. And Allah took him as a Prophet before He took him as a Messenger. And Allah took him as a Messenger before He took him as a Friend (Khalil). And Allah took him as a Friend before He made him an Imam. When He had gathered all these things for him, He said: 'Indeed, I will make you an Imam for mankind.' Because of its greatness in Ibrahim's eyes, he said: 'And from my offspring?' Allah said: 'My covenant does not reach the wrongdoers.' He [the Imam] said: A foolish person cannot be an Imam for the God-fearing." [^1]

3- The Difference Between Prophethood, Imamate, and Messengership

As appears from the indications in the verses and the various expressions found in the Hadiths, those commissioned by Allah held different stations:

1- The station of Prophethood (Nubuwwah)—meaning receiving revelation from Allah. Therefore, a "Prophet" (Nabi) is one upon whom revelation descends, and he places what he receives through revelation at the disposal of the people if they ask him.

2- The station of Messengership (Risalah)—meaning the station of conveying the revelation, preaching and spreading Allah's laws, and training souls through teaching and raising awareness. Therefore, a Messenger (Rasul) is one who is duty-bound to strive within his mission's domain and use every means to invite people toward Allah and convey His command, striving for a cultural, intellectual, and ideological revolution.

3- The station of Imamate—meaning the leadership and headship of the creation. In reality, an Imam is one who,

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Volume 1, Chapter on the Classes of Prophets, Messengers, and Imams, page 133.

by forming a divine government and acquiring the necessary powers, attempts to practically implement and execute Allah's laws. Even if he cannot officially form a government, he strives to implement the laws to the extent of his ability.

In other words, the duty of the Imam is the execution of divine commands, while the duty of the Messenger is the conveyance of these commands. To put it another way, the Messenger "shows the way," but the Imam "conveys to the objective" (in addition to the other heavy duties previously mentioned).

It goes without saying that many Prophets, such as the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), held all three stations: they received revelation, they preached divine commands, they strove to form a government and implement laws, and they engaged in the spiritual training of souls through inward means.

In short: Imamate is the station of comprehensive leadership—material and spiritual, physical and soulful, outward and inward. The Imam is the head of the government, the leader of society, the religious head, the moral educator, and the inward spiritual guide.

On one hand, the Imam leads worthy individuals along the path of inward perfection through his mysterious spiritual power.

Through his scientific power, he teaches the ignorant.

And through the power of his government or other executive powers, he implements the principles of justice.

4- Imamate: Ibrahim’s Final Evolutionary Stage

From what we have said regarding the reality of Imamate, it is clearly understood that a person may hold the station of Prophethood, preaching, and Messengership, yet the station of Imamate may not be within him. This station requires immense worthiness in all aspects and is the very station Ibrahim attained after all those trials and demonstrations of merit; it was the final link in Ibrahim’s evolutionary journey.

Those who imagine that Imamate only means "being a worthy and exemplary individual" seemingly do not realize that such a quality existed in Ibrahim from the beginning of his Prophethood.

And those who imagine that Imamate meant being a "role model and pattern" for the people must be told that this attribute exists for Ibrahim and all Prophets and Messengers from the start of their prophetic call; for this reason, a Prophet must be infallible (Ma'sum) because his actions are a pattern.

Therefore, the station of Imamate is a station higher than these, and even superior to Prophethood and Messengership. This is the very station Ibrahim received from Allah after proving his worthiness.

5- Who is a Wrongdoer (Zalim)?

The meaning of "wrongdoing" (Zulm) in the phrase "My covenant shall not reach the wrongdoers" is not limited to oppressing others. Rather, Zulm (as the opposite of Adl or justice) is used here in its broad sense, as the opposite of justice, which means placing everything in its proper position.

Therefore, Zulm is to place a person, an action, or a thing in a position for which it is not worthy.

Since the station of Imamate and the outward and inward leadership of creation is an exceptionally responsible and magnificent station, a single moment of sin, disobedience, or a bad record causes the loss of eligibility for this station.

Thus, we read in the Hadiths that the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (علیهم السلام) argued using this very verse to prove that the immediate succession to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was restricted to Ali (عليه السلام), pointing out that others were idolaters during the era of Jahiliyyah (Ignorance). The only person who did not prostrate before an idol for even a single moment was Ali (عليه السلام). What wrongdoing is greater than a human practicing idolatry? Did Luqman not say to his son: "O my son, do not associate [anything] with Allah. Indeed, association [with Him] is a great injustice (Zulm)" (Luqman: 13).

As an example, Hisham ibn Salim narrates from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) that he said:

"Ibrahim was a Prophet before he was an Imam, until Allah said: 'Indeed, I will make you an Imam for mankind.' He said: 'And from my offspring?' Allah said: 'My covenant does not reach the wrongdoers.' Whoever has worshipped an idol or a statue shall not be an Imam." [^1]

In another Hadith, Abdullah ibn Mas'ud narrates from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that Allah said to Ibrahim:

"I do not give you a covenant for the wrongdoer among your offspring. Ibrahim said: 'O Lord, and who is the wrongdoer among my children who shall not reach Your covenant?' He said: 'Whoever prostrates to an idol instead of Me, I shall never make him an Imam, and it is not fitting for him to be an Imam!'" [^2]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Volume 1, Chapter on "The Classes of Prophets and Messengers," Hadith 1. [^2]: Amali of Sheikh Mufid and Manaqib of Ibn al-Maghazili, as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan under the verse discussed.

6- The Imam is Appointed by Allah

From the verse under discussion, it is also inferred that the Imam (the comprehensive infallible leader of the people) must be appointed by Allah. This is because:

First, Imamate is a type of divine covenant and pledge, and it is obvious that such a person must be appointed by Allah, who is the party to the covenant.

Second, individuals who have been tainted by wrongdoing and whose lives contain a dark spot of injustice—whether injustice to themselves or injustice to others—and even a single moment of idolatry, do not possess the capacity for Imamate. In other words, the Imam must be infallible (Ma'sum) throughout his entire life.

Can anyone other than Allah be aware of the existence of this quality?

And if we wish to appoint the successor of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) by this criterion, it can be no one other than the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام).

Interestingly, the author of Al-Manar narrates from Abu Hanifa that he believed the Caliphate was exclusively the right of the Alawids, and for this reason, he considered rebellion against the government of the time (Mansur the Abbasid) to be permissible. Consequently, he refused to accept the position of judge in the government of the Abbasid caliphs.

The author of Al-Manar then adds that the "Four Imams of the Sunnis" were all opposed to the governments of their time and did not consider them worthy of leading the Muslims, as they were wrongdoers and oppressors. [^1] Yet it is strange that in our era, many Sunni scholars support and strengthen oppressive, tyrannical, and autocratic governments whose ties to the enemies of Islam are certain and proven, and whose oppression and corruption are hidden from no one—nay, they even consider them

[^1]: Al-Manar, Volume 1, pages 457-458.

"Those in Authority" (Ulul-Amr) and "obligatory to obey."

7- Answer to Two Questions

1- From what we have said in interpreting the meaning of Imamate, a question may arise: if the Imam's work is "conveying to the objective" and "implementing divine programs," this meaning has not been realized on a general scale for many Prophets, even the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself and the Pure Imams; rather, there were always very contaminated and misguided individuals opposing them.

In response, we say: the meaning of this statement is not that the Imam forcibly brings people to the truth, but rather, while maintaining the principle of free will and having readiness and worthiness, they can be guided through the Imam's outward and inward influence.

Just as we say the sun was created to nourish living beings, or the Quran says the work of rain is to revive dead lands; certainly, this influence is general, but it occurs in beings that are ready to accept these effects and prepared for growth.

2- Another question is that the above interpretation implies that every Imam must first be a Prophet and a Messenger and then reach the station of Imamate, whereas the infallible successors of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) were not so.

In response, we say: it is not necessary for the person of the Imam to have previously reached the station of Prophethood and Messengership. Rather, if there was someone before him in whom the stations of Prophethood, Messengership, and Imamate were combined (like the Prophet of Islam), his successor can continue his program of Imamate. This is the case when there is no need for a new Messengership, such as the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) who is the Seal of the Prophets.

In other words, if the stage of receiving divine revelation and conveying all laws has been completed, and only the stage of implementation remains, the Prophet's successor can continue the Prophet's executive line, and there is no need for him to be a Prophet or a Messenger himself.

8- The Distinguished Personality of Ibrahim

The name Ibrahim is mentioned in 69 instances in the Holy Quran, and he is spoken of in 25 chapters. In the verses of the Quran, this great Prophet is highly praised and lauded, and his valuable attributes are recalled.

He was a role model and an example in every respect, a specimen of a perfect human being.

His level of gnosis regarding Allah, his eloquent logic against the idolaters, his relentless and tireless struggles against tyrants, his sacrifice and selflessness before the command of the Lord, his unparalleled steadfastness in the face of the storm of events and his severe trials—each of these is a detailed story and each is a lesson for Muslims and travelers on the path of "Allah."

According to the Quran, he was among the good, [^1] the righteous, [^2] the devoutly obedient, [^3] the truthful, [^4] the forbearing, [^5] and those who fulfill their covenant. He possessed unparalleled courage and extraordinary generosity.

By Allah's will, in the commentary of Surah Ibrahim (especially the final section of the chapter), you will read a detailed discussion on this subject (refer to Volume 10 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 397 onwards).

[^1]: Surah Sad, verse 47. [^2]: Surah An-Nahl, verse 122. [^3]: Surah An-Nahl, verse 120. [^4]: Surah Maryam, verse 41. [^5]: Surah At-Tawbah, verse 114.

Ayah

وَإِذْ جَعَلْنَا ٱلْبَيْتَ مَثَابَةً لِّلنَّاسِ وَأَمْنًا وَٱتَّخِذُوا۟ مِن مَّقَامِ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ مُصَلًّى ۖ وَعَهِدْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ أَن طَهِّرَا بَيْتِىَ لِلطَّآئِفِينَ وَٱلْعَـٰكِفِينَ وَٱلرُّكَّعِ ٱلسُّجُودِ (١٢٥)
And [remember] when We made the House a place of reward for mankind and a sanctuary, [declaring], ‘Take the venue of prayer from Abraham’s Station.’ We charged Abraham and Ishmael [with its upkeep, saying], ‘Purify My House for those who go around it, [for] those who make it a retreat and [for] those who bow and prostrate.’ (125)

Commentary

The Grandeur of the House of Allah

Following the reference to the exalted station of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) in the previous verse, the text proceeds to describe the grandeur of the House of the Kaaba, which was built and prepared by the hands of Ibrahim (peace be upon him), stating:

"And remember when We made the House (the Kaaba) a mathaba (a place of return and resort) for mankind and a sanctuary (of peace and security)" (وَ إِذْ جَعَلْنَا الْبَيْتَ مَثابَةً لِلنَّاسِ وَ أَمْناً).

The word mathaba is originally derived from the root thawb, meaning the return of something to its original state. Since the House of the Kaaba has been a center for monotheists who turn toward it every year—not only physically but also spiritually, returning to monotheism and their primordial nature (fitra)—it is introduced as a mathaba. Furthermore, since a person's home, which is their permanent place of return, is a site of tranquility and comfort, the word mathaba also carries a sense of peace and mental reassurance.

This meaning is emphasized by the word amnan (security/peace) mentioned immediately after it. Specifically, the phrase lin-nas (for the people) indicates that this center of peace and security is a universal refuge for all the worlds, for all human beings, and for the masses of people.

This is, in fact, the fulfillment of one of the requests that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) made to Allah, which will be mentioned later.

Then it adds: "And take the Station of Ibrahim as a place of prayer" (وَ اتَّخِذُوا مِنْ مَقامِ إِبْراهِيمَ مُصَلًّى‌).

There is discussion among commentators regarding what is meant by the "Station of Ibrahim" (Maqam Ibrahim). Some have said the entire Hajj is the Station of Ibrahim; some have interpreted it as Arafah, Mash'ar al-Haram, and the three Jamarat; and some have said the entire Sanctuary (Haram) of Mecca is considered the Station.

However, the apparent meaning of the verse—as stated in Islamic narrations and by many commentators—refers to the well-known Station of Ibrahim, which is a place near the House of the Kaaba. After performing the circumambulation (Tawaf), pilgrims go near it and perform the Tawaf prayer. Therefore, the meaning of musalla is also a place of prayer.

Then, it refers to the covenant taken from Ibrahim (peace be upon him) and his son Ismail (peace be upon him) regarding the purification of the House of the Kaaba, saying: "And We commanded Ibrahim and Ismail, 'Purify My House for those who perform Tawaf, those who stay there for worship (I'tikaf), and those who bow and prostrate (the worshippers)'" (وَ عَهِدْنا إِلى‌ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ إِسْماعِيلَ أَنْ طَهِّرا بَيْتِيَ لِلطَّائِفِينَ وَ الْعاكِفِينَ وَ الرُّكَّعِ السُّجُودِ).

What is meant by purity and purification here? Some have said: purification from the filth of the existence of idols.

Some have said: from physical impurities, especially from the blood and contents of the stomachs of animals that were sacrificed, as some ignorant individuals would perform such acts.

And some have said purification here means sincerity of intention during the construction of this House of Monotheism.

However, there is no reason for us to limit the concept of purification here; rather, it means the physical and spiritual cleansing of this House of Monotheism from every kind of pollution.

Hence, we read in some narrations that this verse is interpreted as cleansing it from polytheists, and in others, as washing the body and cleanliness from impurities.

Key Points

1- The Social and Educational Effects of This Secure Refuge

According to the above verse, the House of Allah (the Kaaba) has been declared by the Lord as a refuge and a center of peace and security. We know that in Islam, strict regulations have been established to avoid any kind of conflict, dispute, war, and bloodshed in this holy land. This is to the extent that not only must human beings of every class and group and in all conditions be in security there, but even animals and birds live in peace and security there, and no one has the right to disturb them.

In a world where conflict and dispute always exist, the presence of such a center can demonstrate a specific profound effect on solving people's problems. This is because the security of this region allows people, despite all their differences, to sit together in its vicinity and engage in dialogue. In this way, one of the most important problems—which usually exists in opening the door for negotiations to resolve hostilities and disputes—is solved.

This is because it often happens that the parties to a conflict, or the hostile governments of the world, wish to resolve hostilities and sit down for negotiations for this purpose, but they cannot find a place that is sacred and respected by both sides and recognized as a center of peace and security. However, in Islam, this provision has been made, and Mecca has been declared as such a center.

Even now, all the Muslims of the world, who unfortunately are entangled in deadly conflicts and disputes, can use the sanctity and security of this land to open the door for negotiations and use the spirituality of this holy place—which creates a special light and spirituality in the hearts—to resolve their differences. [1]

2- Why "The House of Allah"?

In the above verse, the House of the Kaaba is referred to as Bayti (My House), while it is clear that Allah is neither a physical body nor in need of a house. The purpose of this possessive construction is "honorific attribution" (idafah tashrifiyyah), meaning that to express the honor and grandeur of something, it is attributed to Allah. Thus, the month of Ramadan is called Shahr Allah (the Month of Allah) and the House of the Kaaba is called Bayt Allah (the House of Allah).

[1]: We have also discussed the security of the land of Mecca in Volume 10 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 366 (under the commentary of Surah Ibrahim, verse 35).

Ayah

وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ رَبِّ ٱجْعَلْ هَـٰذَا بَلَدًا ءَامِنًا وَٱرْزُقْ أَهْلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ مِنْهُم بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۖ قَالَ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَأُمَتِّعُهُۥ قَلِيلًا ثُمَّ أَضْطَرُّهُۥٓ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٢٦)
And when Abraham said, ‘My Lord, make this a secure town, and provide its people with fruits—such of them as have faith in Allah and the Last Day,’ He said, ‘As for him who is faithless, I will provide for him [too] for a short time, then I will shove him toward the punishment of the Fire and it is an evil destination.’ (126)

Commentary

Ibrahim’s Requests from the Presence of the Lord

In this verse, Ibrahim makes two important requests from the Lord for the inhabitants of this holy land, one of which was also mentioned in the previous verse.

The Quran says: "And remember when Ibrahim said, 'My Lord, make this a city of security'" (وَ إِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هذا بَلَداً آمِناً).

And as we read in the previous verse, this prayer of Ibrahim was answered; Allah made this holy land a center of peace and safety, granting it security both outwardly and inwardly.

His second request is: "And provide its people with fruits—such of them as believe in Allah and the Last Day" (وَ ارْزُقْ أَهْلَهُ مِنَ الثَّمَراتِ مَنْ آمَنَ مِنْهُمْ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ).

It is interesting that Ibrahim first requests "security" and then asks for "economic blessings," and this is itself an indication of the reality that as long as security does not prevail in a city or country, establishing a healthy economy is not possible!

Regarding what is meant by "fruits" (ثمرات), commentators have various discussions, but it appears that "fruits" has a broad meaning that includes every kind of material blessing, whether fruits and other food items, or spiritual blessings. In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "هي ثمرات القلوب" (It refers to the fruits of the hearts)! This is an allusion to the fact that Allah attracts the love and affection of people toward the inhabitants of this land.

It is also noteworthy that Ibrahim makes this request only for those who believe in Monotheism and the Day of Judgment. This is perhaps because, from the phrase "My covenant does not include the wrongdoers" in the preceding verses, he realized that a group of his future descendants would follow the path of polytheism and oppression; thus, he observed proper etiquette here and excluded them from his prayer.

In any case, Allah replied to this request of Ibrahim by saying: "As for him who disbelieves, I shall grant him enjoyment for a short while" (and I will not deprive them entirely!) (قالَ وَ مَنْ كَفَرَ فَأُمَتِّعُهُ قَلِيلًا).

But in the Hereafter, "I shall compel him to the punishment of the Fire, and what an evil destination it is!" (ثُمَّ أَضْطَرُّهُ إِلى‌ عَذابِ النَّارِ وَ بِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ).

This is, in fact, the attribute of "Rahmaniyyah" (General Mercy)—the same universal mercy of the Lord from whose unsparing table of blessings everyone partakes, and from whose Unseen treasury both the good and the bad receive their sustenance. However, the Hereafter is the abode of His "Rahimiyyah" (Special Mercy), and there is no mercy or salvation for them there.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ ٱلْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ ٱلْبَيْتِ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلُ رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ مِنَّآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (١٢٧)
As Abraham raised the foundations of the House with Ishmael, [they prayed]: ‘Our Lord, accept it from us! Indeed You are the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (127)
رَبَّنَا وَٱجْعَلْنَا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَآ أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ وَأَرِنَا مَنَاسِكَنَا وَتُبْ عَلَيْنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٢٨)
‘Our Lord, make us submissive to You, and [raise] from our progeny a nation submissive to You, and show us our rites [of worship], and turn to us clemently. Indeed You are the All-clement, the All-merciful.’ (128)
رَبَّنَا وَٱبْعَثْ فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِكَ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١٢٩)
‘Our Lord, raise amongst them an apostle from among them, who will recite to them Your signs and teach them the Book and wisdom and purify them. Indeed You are the All-mighty, the All-wise.’ (129)

Commentary

Abraham Builds the Kaaba

From various Quranic verses, Hadiths, and Islamic histories, it is clearly understood that the House of the Kaaba was established before Abraham (peace be upon him), even dating back to the time of Adam (peace be upon him). In Surah Ibrahim, verse 37, we read the words of this great Prophet: رَبَّنا إِنِّي أَسْكَنْتُ مِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِي بِوادٍ غَيْرِ ذِي زَرْعٍ عِنْدَ بَيْتِكَ الْمُحَرَّمِ‌: "O our Lord! I have made some of my offspring to dwell in an uncultivable valley by Your Sacred House."

This verse testifies that when Abraham arrived in the land of Mecca with his infant son Ishmael and his wife, some trace of the Kaaba already existed.

Furthermore, in Surah Al-Imran, verse 96, we read: إِنَّ أَوَّلَ بَيْتٍ وُضِعَ لِلنَّاسِ لَلَّذِي بِبَكَّةَ مُبارَكاً: "Indeed, the first House appointed for mankind was that at Bakka (Mecca), full of blessing." It is certain that the worship of Allah and the construction of a center for worship did not begin with Abraham; rather, it existed previously, even from the time of Adam عليه السلام.

Coincidentally, the expression found in the first verse under discussion also conveys this same meaning where it says: "And remember when Abraham and Ishmael were raising the foundations of the House (the Kaaba), saying: 'Our Lord! Accept (this service) from us. Verily! You are the All-Hearer, the All-Knower'" (وَ إِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْراهِيمُ الْقَواعِدَ مِنَ الْبَيْتِ وَ إِسْماعِيلُ رَبَّنا تَقَبَّلْ مِنَّا إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ‌).

This expression indicates that the foundations of the Kaaba already existed, and Abraham and Ishmael were raising those foundations.

In the famous sermon of al-Qasi'ah from Nahj al-Balagha, we also read:

ا لا ترون ان اللَّه سبحانه اختبر الاولين من لدن آدم صلوات اللَّه عليه الى الاخرين من هذا العالم باحجار ...، فجعلها بيته الحرام ...، ثم امر آدم و ولده يثنوا اعطافهم نحوه ...

"Do you not see that Allah, the Glorified, has tried the people of the world from the time of Adam (peace be upon him) until the last ones of this world with stones... and made them His Sacred House... then He commanded Adam and his children to turn their attention toward it (circumambulate it)."

In short: the Quranic verses and narrations confirm this well-known history—that the Kaaba was first built by Adam, then it collapsed during the Flood of Noah, and was later rebuilt by Abraham and his son Ishmael. [^1]

In the last two verses under discussion, Abraham and his son Ishmael make five important requests to the Lord of the worlds. These requests, made while they were occupied with rebuilding the Kaaba, are so calculated and comprehensive of all material and spiritual life needs that they fully acquaint one with the greatness of the souls of these two great Prophets of God:

First, they submit: "Our Lord! Make us submissive to Your command" (رَبَّنا وَ اجْعَلْنا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ‌).

Then they request: "And from our offspring a nation submissive to Your command" (وَ مِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِنا أُمَّةً مُسْلِمَةً لَكَ‌).

Next, they ask: "And show us our ways of worship (and ceremonies), and inform us of them" (وَ أَرِنا مَناسِكَنا), so that we may worship You in a manner worthy of Your status.

After that, they seek repentance from God, saying: "And accept our repentance and turn Your mercy toward us, for You are the Oft-Returning, the Most Merciful" (وَ تُبْ عَلَيْنا إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ‌).

[^1]: The author of Tafsir al-Manar has completely denied this matter and considers Abraham and Ishmael to be the first founders of the Kaaba, whereas this statement is not only inconsistent with narrations and history but also with the Quranic verses themselves.

Their fifth request is: "Our Lord! Send among them a Messenger from among themselves" (رَبَّنا وَ ابْعَثْ فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِنْهُمْ‌).

"Who shall recite unto them Your verses and instruct them in the Book and Wisdom, and purify them" (يَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِكَ وَ يُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَ الْحِكْمَةَ وَ يُزَكِّيهِمْ‌).

"For You are the All-Mighty, the All-Wise" (إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ‌).

Key Points

1- The Goal of the Prophets' Mission

In the above verses, after Abraham and Ishmael request the appearance of the Prophet of Islam, they state three goals for his mission:

First, the recitation of Allah's verses to the people. This phrase refers to awakening thoughts in the light of the captivating, attractive, and striking verses that descend upon the Prophet's ص قلب through the channel of revelation, by which he awakens sleeping souls.

"Yatlu" (يتلوا) comes from the root "Tilawah," which linguistically means to bring things one after another. When phrases are read consecutively and according to a correct system, the Arabs refer to it as Tilawah. Therefore, organized and continuous recitation is a prelude to awakening and creating readiness for education and purification.

Then, it mentions the teaching of the "Book" and "Wisdom" as the second goal, because until awareness is achieved, purification—which is the third stage—cannot take place.

The difference between "Book" and "Wisdom" may be that "Book" refers to the Heavenly Scriptures, while "Wisdom" refers to the sciences, knowledge, secrets, causes, and results of the rulings taught by the Prophet.

Finally, it states the last goal, which is the issue of "Purification" (Tazkiyah).

"Tazkiyah" (تزكيه) in linguistics means both to cause growth and to cleanse.

In this way, the perfection of human existence in both "scientific" and "practical" aspects is introduced as the ultimate goal of the Prophet's mission.

It is particularly noteworthy that human knowledge is limited and mixed with thousands of points of ambiguity and numerous errors; furthermore, regarding what man does know, he sometimes cannot be precisely hopeful because he has seen his own errors and those of others.

It is here that the Prophets must come among the people with true knowledge, free from any error, received from the source of revelation, to remove their errors, teach them what they do not know, and give them peace of mind regarding what they do know.

Another point to remember in this regard is that half of our personality is formed by "intellect and reason" and the other half by "instincts and desires." For this reason, just as we need "instruction" (education), we also need "purification" (upbringing). Both our intellect must reach perfection and our inner instincts must be guided toward a correct goal.

Therefore, Prophets are both teachers and mentors; they are both instructors and cultivators.

2- Does "Instruction" Precede "Purification"?

It is interesting that in the four instances in the Holy Quran where the issue of instruction and purification are mentioned together as the goal of the Prophets, in three instances "Purification" (Tazkiyah) is mentioned before "Instruction" (Ta'lim) (Surah Al-Baqarah 151, Al-Imran 164, Al-Jumu'ah 2), and only in one instance is "Instruction" mentioned before "Purification" (the verse under discussion). This is despite the fact that we know, usually, no purification takes place until there is instruction.

Therefore, where instruction precedes purification, it refers to its natural sequence, and in the majority of cases where purification is mentioned first, it likely refers to its status as the ultimate goal; for the primary goal is purification, and everything else is a prelude to it.

3- A Prophet from Among Themselves

The expression used with the word "minhum" (from among them) in the above verses indicates that the leaders and mentors of humanity must be of their own kind, possessing the same human attributes and instincts, so they can serve as worthy role models in practical aspects. Obviously, if they were of a species other than human, they could neither understand the pains, needs, problems, and various entanglements of humans, nor could humans take them as role models.

Ayahs

وَمَن يَرْغَبُ عَن مِّلَّةِ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ إِلَّا مَن سَفِهَ نَفْسَهُۥ ۚ وَلَقَدِ ٱصْطَفَيْنَـٰهُ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٣٠)
And who will [ever] forsake Abraham’s creed except one who debases himself? We certainly chose him in the [present] world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous. (130)
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُۥ رَبُّهُۥٓ أَسْلِمْ ۖ قَالَ أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٣١)
When his Lord said to him, ‘Submit,’ he said, ‘I submit to the Lord of all the worlds.’ (131)
وَوَصَّىٰ بِهَآ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ بَنِيهِ وَيَعْقُوبُ يَـٰبَنِىَّ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰ لَكُمُ ٱلدِّينَ فَلَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (١٣٢)
Abraham enjoined this [creed] upon his children, and [so did] Jacob, [saying], ‘My children! Allah has indeed chosen this religion for you; so do not die except as those who have surrendered themselves [to Allah]. (132)

Commentary

Abraham, the Exemplary Human

In the preceding verses, the personality of Abraham was illustrated to some extent; some of Abraham's services and a significant portion of his requests and supplications—which encompassed both material and spiritual dimensions—were examined.

From the sum of these discussions, it is clearly understood that this great Prophet can serve as a model and an exemplar for all truth-seekers of the world, and his school of thought can be utilized by everyone as a system for human development.

Based on this very point, the first verse under discussion states: "And who—except for those who have fooled themselves into folly—would turn away from the pure religion of Abraham?" (وَ مَنْ يَرْغَبُ عَنْ مِلَّةِ إِبْراهِيمَ إِلَّا مَنْ سَفِهَ نَفْسَهُ).

Is it not folly for a person to abandon a religion of such purity and brilliance and step into the stray paths of polytheism, disbelief, and corruption? To leave a religion that is familiar and compatible with the human soul and nature (fitrah), and harmonious with intellect and reason—a religion that contains both the Hereafter and this world—and to pursue programs that are enemies of reason, contrary to nature, and destroyers of both faith and worldly life.

Then it adds: "We chose Abraham (because of these great merits of his) in this world, and in the world to come he is among the righteous" (وَ لَقَدِ اصْطَفَيْناهُ فِي الدُّنْيا وَ إِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ).

Yes, Abraham is the chosen of Allah and the head of the lineage of the righteous, and for this reason, he must be an "exemplar" (uswah) and a "role model" (qudwah).

The next verse, as an emphasis, points to another of the characteristics of Abraham's chosen qualities, which is in fact the root of the rest of his attributes, saying: "Remember when his Lord said to him, 'Submit to My command,' he said, 'I have submitted to the Lord of the worlds'" (إِذْ قالَ لَهُ رَبُّهُ أَسْلِمْ قالَ أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

Yes, Abraham, that self-sacrificing and selfless man, when he hears the call of nature from within himself that the Lord commands him to "Submit," he becomes completely submissive to Allah. Abraham, through his thought and perception, realizes and sees that the stars, the moon, and the sun all set and vanish and are subject to the laws of creation; therefore, he says: These are not my gods. إِنِّي وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِيَ لِلَّذِي فَطَرَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ حَنِيفاً وَ ما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ: [^1] "I have turned my face toward Him who created the heavens and the earth, being upright, and I am not of the polytheists" (Surah Al-An'am, Verse 79).

And in the previous verses, we saw that after building the Kaaba, the first request Abraham and Ishmael made to Allah—after the request for the acceptance of their deeds—was that they truly be submissive to His command and that a Muslim nation arise from their progeny.

In truth, the first step toward creating value in a human being is the issue of sincerity and purity. It is for this reason—that Abraham submitted himself exclusively to the command of the Truth—that he became beloved by Allah, and Allah chose him and introduced him and his school of thought under this title.

All of Abraham's actions from the beginning of his life to the end were peerless: his persistent struggle against idolaters and star-worshippers, and his placement in the heart of the fire, which affected even his fierce enemy Nimrod, who involuntarily said: Whoever takes a god, let him take a god like the God of Abraham. [^2] Likewise, bringing his wife and nursing infant to that dry and scorching desert, to that holy land, and the building of the Kaaba and taking his youth to the place of sacrifice—each was an example of his way of action.

The will and counsel he gave to his children in the final days of his life were also exemplary, as we read in the last verse under discussion: "Abraham and Jacob enjoined their children to this pure monotheistic religion in the final moments of their lives" (وَ وَصَّى بِها إِبْراهِيمُ بَنِيهِ وَ يَعْقُوبُ). Each said to their children: "O my children! Allah has chosen this monotheistic religion for you" (يا بَنِيَّ إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفى‌ لَكُمُ الدِّينَ).

"Therefore, do not travel except upon this path, and do not bid farewell to the world except with a heart overflowing with faith and submission" (فَلا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ).

By quoting Abraham's will, the Quran seemingly wants to express this reality: that you humans are not only responsible for your children's today, but you are also responsible for their future. Do not only worry about the material life of your children after your death when closing your eyes to the world; think of their spiritual life as well.

Not only did Abraham make such a will, but his grandson Jacob also adopted this same method from his forefather Abraham, and in his final breath of life, he reminded his children that the secret of victory, success, and happiness is summarized in one short phrase: "Submission to the Truth."

The mention of "Jacob" among all the prophets here is perhaps for the purpose of making the Jews and Christians—each of whom linked themselves in some way to Jacob—understand that this polytheistic religion you hold, and this lack of pure submission to the Truth which constitutes your program, is not compatible with the way of the one to whom you link yourselves.

[^1]: Surah Al-An'am, Verse 79. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 439.

Ayahs

أَمْ كُنتُمْ شُهَدَآءَ إِذْ حَضَرَ يَعْقُوبَ ٱلْمَوْتُ إِذْ قَالَ لِبَنِيهِ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنۢ بَعْدِى قَالُوا۟ نَعْبُدُ إِلَـٰهَكَ وَإِلَـٰهَ ءَابَآئِكَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ إِلَـٰهًا وَٰحِدًا وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ (١٣٣)
Were you witnesses when death approached Jacob, when he said to his children, ‘What will you worship after me?’ They said, ‘We will worship your God and the God of your fathers, Abraham, Ishmael, and Isaac, the One God, and to Him do we submit.’ (133)
تِلْكَ أُمَّةٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ ۖ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَلَكُم مَّا كَسَبْتُمْ ۖ وَلَا تُسْـَٔلُونَ عَمَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٣٤)
That was a nation that has passed: for it there will be what it has earned, and for you there will be what you have earned, and you will not be questioned about what they used to do. (134)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of Jews believed that at the time of his death, Jacob (Ya'qub) exhorted his children to follow the religion that the Jews currently believe in (with all its distortions). Allah revealed this verse to refute their belief (Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, under the verse in question).

Commentary

Everyone is Responsible for Their Own Deeds

As we read in the occasion of revelation, it also appears from the literal wording of the verse that a discussion had taken place, and a group of deniers of Islam were attributing an incorrect matter to Jacob, the Prophet of God (this matter was apparently the same one mentioned in the occasion of revelation).

To refute this baseless claim, the Quran says: "Were you present when death approached Jacob" that he made such a recommendation to his children? (أَمْ كُنْتُمْ شُهَداءَ إِذْ حَضَرَ يَعْقُوبَ الْمَوْتُ).

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 1; P. 464

The reality was not what you attribute to him; what happened was that "at that time, he asked his children: 'What will you worship after me?'" (إِذْ قالَ لِبَنِيهِ ما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ بَعْدِي).

They replied: "We will worship your God and the God of your fathers, Abraham, Ishmael, and Isaac—the One and Only God" (قالُوا نَعْبُدُ إِلهَكَ وَ إِلهَ آبائِكَ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ إِسْماعِيلَ وَ إِسْحاقَ إِلهاً واحِداً) "and to Him we are submissive" (وَ نَحْنُ لَهُ مُسْلِمُونَ).

Indeed, he gave no recommendation except for monotheism (Tawhid) and submission to the command of Truth, which is the root of accepting all Divine programs.

It appears from the verse under discussion that at the time of death, signs of a kind of distress and concern regarding the future state of his children were felt within Jacob. Finally, he voiced this concern and asked: "My children, what will you worship after me?"

He specifically said "what thing" (ما) and did not say "whom" (من)? This is because in his living environment, there was a group of idolaters who prostrated before objects. Jacob wanted to know if there was any inclination toward this creed in the depths of their souls. However, after the children's response, he regained his peace of mind.

It is also noteworthy that Prophet Ishmael (Isma'il) was not the father or grandfather of Jacob, but rather his uncle. Yet, in the verse under discussion, the word "آباء" (fathers), the plural of "اب" (father), is used. From this, it becomes clear that in the Arabic language, the word "اب" is sometimes applied to an uncle. For this reason, we say that if this word is used regarding "Azar" in the Quran, it does not prevent Azar from being Abraham's uncle rather than his father (reflect on this).

The final verse under discussion is seemingly a response to one of the misconceptions of the Jews, as they relied heavily on the issue of their ancestors, their honors, and their greatness before God. Their assumption was that even if they themselves were polluted with sin, they would be among the saved in the light of such ancestors.

The Quran says: "That was a nation that has passed away; for them is what they earned, and for you is what you earn" (تِلْكَ أُمَّةٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ لَها ما كَسَبَتْ وَ لَكُمْ ما كَسَبْتُمْ).

"And you will never be questioned about what they used to do" (just as they are not responsible for your deeds) (وَ لا تُسْئَلُونَ عَمَّا كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

Therefore, instead of devoting all your efforts to researching, boasting, and taking pride in your ancestors, strive to reform your own beliefs and actions.

Although the addressees in this verse are apparently the Jews and the People of the Book, it is clear that this rule is not exclusive to them. We Muslims are also subject to this same fundamental principle: "What benefit do you gain from the virtue of your father?!" [if you lack it yourself].

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ كُونُوا۟ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَـٰرَىٰ تَهْتَدُوا۟ ۗ قُلْ بَلْ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ حَنِيفًا ۖ وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٣٥)
They say, ‘Be either Jews or Christians, that you may be [rightly] guided.’ Say, ‘No, rather, [we will follow] the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, and he was not one of the polytheists.’ (135)
قُولُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْنَا وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطِ وَمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَمَآ أُوتِىَ ٱلنَّبِيُّونَ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ (١٣٦)
Say, ‘We have faith in Allah and what has been sent down to us, and what was sent down to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob and the Tribes, and that which Moses and Jesus were given, and that which the prophets were given from their Lord; we make no distinction between any of them and to Him do we submit.’ (136)
فَإِنْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِمِثْلِ مَآ ءَامَنتُم بِهِۦ فَقَدِ ٱهْتَدَوا۟ ۖ وَّإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّمَا هُمْ فِى شِقَاقٍ ۖ فَسَيَكْفِيكَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (١٣٧)
So if they believe in the like of what you believe in, then they are surely guided; but if they turn away, then [know that] they are only [steeped] in defiance. Allah will suffice you against them, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (137)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation for these verses, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that several Jewish scholars and Christians of Najran were engaged in a debate with the Muslims. Each of these two groups considered themselves the most entitled and worthy of the true religion and rejected the other. The Jews would say: "Moses, our prophet, is superior to all prophets, and our book, the Torah, is the best of books." The Christians made the exact same claim, asserting that Christ is the best guide and the Gospel is the superior heavenly book. Each of the followers of these two religions invited the Muslims to their own faith. The above verses were revealed and provided them with an answer.

Commentary

Only We Are on the Right Path!

Self-conceit and self-centeredness usually cause a person to restrict the truth to themselves, consider everyone else to be on a path of falsehood, and strive to make others conform to their own likeness. As the Quran states in the first verse under discussion: "The People of the Book said: 'Become Jews or Christians so that you may be guided'!" (وَ قالُوا كُونُوا هُوداً أَوْ نَصارى‌ تَهْتَدُوا).

Say: Distorted religions can never bring about the guidance of humanity; "Rather, follow the pure religion of Abraham so that you may be guided, and he was never of the polytheists" (قُلْ بَلْ مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ حَنِيفاً وَ ما كانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ‌).

The truly religious are those who follow the pure monotheistic faith—a monotheism that is not mixed with any form of polytheism (shirk). The most important fundamental principle for distinguishing a pure religion from deviant ones is this complete observance of the principle of monotheism.

Islam teaches us not to create division between the prophets of Allah and to respect the religion of all of them, because the principles of the true religion are the same everywhere. Moses and Jesus were also followers of the monotheistic and pure faith of Abraham, even though their religions were distorted by ignorant followers and became mixed with polytheism. (Of course, this statement does not contradict the fact that today, in performing our duties, we must follow the latest heavenly religion, namely Islam, which has been revealed by Allah for this time).

The next verse commands the Muslims to say to their opponents: "Say: We believe in Allah, and in what has been revealed to us, and what was revealed to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the prophets of the tribes of the Children of Israel, and also in what was given to Moses and Jesus and the other prophets from their Lord" (قُولُوا آمَنَّا بِاللَّهِ وَ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْنا وَ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلى‌ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ إِسْماعِيلَ وَ إِسْحاقَ وَ يَعْقُوبَ وَ الْأَسْباطِ وَ ما أُوتِيَ مُوسى‌ وَ عِيسى‌ وَ ما أُوتِيَ النَّبِيُّونَ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ‌). "We make no distinction between any of them, and we are submissive to the command of Truth" (لا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ وَ نَحْنُ لَهُ مُسْلِمُونَ‌).
