Self-centeredness and racial prejudices should never cause us to accept some and reject others. They were all divine teachers who engaged in guiding humanity during different educational periods. They all had but one goal: the guidance of humanity in the light of pure monotheism, truth, and justice, even though each of them had specific duties and characteristics in their own particular time periods.

Then it adds: "If they believe in the same things that you have believed in, then they are guided" (فَإِنْ آمَنُوا بِمِثْلِ ما آمَنْتُمْ بِهِ فَقَدِ اهْتَدَوْا). "And if they turn away, they have separated themselves from the truth" (وَ إِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَإِنَّما هُمْ فِي شِقاقٍ‌).

If they do not involve racial and tribal issues in religion, and recognize all divine prophets without exception, they too are guided. Otherwise, they have abandoned the truth and pursued falsehood.

The word "Shiqaq" (شقاق) originally means a split, dispute, and war. Here, some have interpreted it as disbelief (kufr), some as misguidance, and sometimes as separation from the truth and turning toward falsehood; all of these actually return to a single reality.

Some commentators have narrated that after the revelation of the previous verse and the mention of Jesus alongside other prophets, a group of Christians said: "We do not accept this statement; Jesus was not like other prophets, he was the son of God!" The last verse under discussion was revealed, warning them that they are in misguidance, disbelief, and "Shiqaq."

In any case, at the end of the verse, it gives heart to the Muslims not to fear the conspiracies of the enemies, saying: "Allah will suffice you against them, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing." He hears their words and is aware of their conspiracies (فَسَيَكْفِيكَهُمُ اللَّهُ وَ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ‌).

Key Points

1- The Unity of the Prophets' Call

The Quran repeatedly mentions in various verses that all prophets of Allah pursued a single goal and there is no separation between them, because they all received from one source of revelation and inspiration. Therefore, it advises Muslims to respect all divine prophets equally. However, as we have said, this subject does not prevent every new religion revealed by Allah from abrogating previous religions, and for the religion of Islam to be the final religion.

This is because the prophets of Allah were like teachers, each of whom educated human society in a particular class. Naturally, when the period of instruction for one was completed, they were handed over to another teacher in a higher class. Based on this, human society is obligated to implement the programs of the final Prophet, which is the final stage of the evolution of religion for this age, and this will never negate the truthfulness of the call of other prophets.

2- Who Were the "Asbat"?

"Sabt" (سَبْط) and "Sibt" (سِبْط) and "Inbisat" (انْبِساط) originally mean the expansion and development of something with ease and comfort. Sometimes a tree is called "Sabt" because its branches spread out easily. The children and branches of a family are called "Sibt" and "Asbat" because of the expansion that occurs in the lineage.

What is meant by "Asbat" are the branches and tribes of the Children of Israel or the descendants who came from the twelve children of Jacob. Since there were prophets among them, the above verse counts them among those to whom the signs of Allah were revealed.

Therefore, the meaning is the tribes of the Children of Israel or the tribes of the children of Jacob who had prophets, not the children of Jacob themselves—lest it be said that they were not all qualified for prophethood because they committed a sin regarding their brother.

3- What is the Meaning of "Hanif"?

"Hanif" (حنيف) is from the root "Hanaf" (حَنَف), meaning to incline from misguidance toward correctness and truth, whereas "Janaf" (جَنَف) is its opposite, meaning to incline from truth toward crookedness. Since the followers of pure monotheism turn away from polytheism and incline toward this fundamental principle, they are called "Hanif." For this same reason, one of the meanings of "Hanif" is straight and upright.

From this, it becomes clear that the interpretations commentators have given for the word "Hanif"—such as the pilgrimage to the House of Allah, following the truth, following Abraham, or sincerity of action—all return to that same general and comprehensive meaning, and each of these is an instance of it.

Ayahs

صِبْغَةَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ صِبْغَةً ۖ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ عَـٰبِدُونَ (١٣٨)
‘The baptism of Allah, and who baptizes better than Allah? And Him do we worship.’ (138)
قُلْ أَتُحَآجُّونَنَا فِى ٱللَّهِ وَهُوَ رَبُّنَا وَرَبُّكُمْ وَلَنَآ أَعْمَـٰلُنَا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُخْلِصُونَ (١٣٩)
Say, ‘Will you argue with us concerning Allah, while He is our Lord and your Lord, and to us belong our deeds, and to you belong your deeds, and we worship Him dedicatedly?’ (139)
أَمْ تَقُولُونَ إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطَ كَانُوا۟ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَـٰرَىٰ ۗ قُلْ ءَأَنتُمْ أَعْلَمُ أَمِ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن كَتَمَ شَهَـٰدَةً عِندَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٤٠)
[Ask them,] ‘Do you say that Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the Tribes were Jews or Christians?’ Say, ‘Is it you who know better, or Allah?’ And who is a greater wrongdoer than someone who conceals a testimony that is with him from Allah? And Allah is not oblivious of what you do. (140)
تِلْكَ أُمَّةٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ ۖ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَلَكُم مَّا كَسَبْتُمْ ۖ وَلَا تُسْـَٔلُونَ عَمَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٤١)
That was a nation that has passed: for it there will be what it has earned, and for you there will be what you have earned, and you will not be questioned about what they used to do. (141)

Commentary

Wash Away Non-Divine Colors!

Following the invitation extended in the previous verses to all followers of religions to follow the programs of all the Prophets, the first verse under discussion commands them all: "Accept only the coloring of Allah" (which is the color of faith and pure monotheism) (صِبْغَةَ اللَّهِ) [^1]. It then adds: "And who is better than Allah in coloring? And Him alone we worship" (وَ مَنْ أَحْسَنُ مِنَ اللَّهِ صِبْغَةً وَ نَحْنُ لَهُ عابِدُونَ).

In this way, the Quran commands that all racial, tribal, and other divisive colors be cast aside, and that everyone should take on the Divine color.

Commentators have written that it was customary among Christians to "baptize" their children. Sometimes they would add a specific yellow dye to the water and say: "This baptism, especially with this specific color, results in the purification of the newborn from the original sin inherited from Adam."

The Quran draws a line of cancellation through this baseless logic and says: It is better that instead of outward colors and superstitious, divisive colors, you accept the color of truth and Divinity so that your soul and spirit may be purified from every pollution.

Truly, what a beautiful and subtle expression! If people accept the Divine color—meaning the color of unity, greatness, purity, and piety; the color of "colorlessness," justice, equality, brotherhood, and parity; and the color of monotheism and sincerity—they can, under its light, eliminate all the disputes and conflicts that arise "whenever colorlessness becomes a captive of color," and uproot the seeds of polytheism, hypocrisy, and division.

In reality, this is that very "colorlessness" and the removal of all [other] colors.

In several traditions from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse, it is narrated that: "The meaning of 'صِبْغَةَ اللَّهِ' is the pure religion of Islam." [^1] This tradition also points to exactly what was mentioned above.

Since the Jews and others would sometimes engage in arguments and debates with the Muslims, saying: "All the Prophets arose from among our people, and our religion is the oldest of religions, and our book is the most ancient of Heavenly Books; if Muhammad were also a Prophet, he should have been appointed from among us!" Sometimes they said: "Our race is more prepared than the Arab race to accept faith and revelation, for they were idol-worshippers and we were not." And sometimes they called themselves the children of God and considered Paradise their exclusive domain!

[^1]: Regarding the grammatical position (I'rab) of "صِبْغَةَ اللَّهِ", commentators have offered several possibilities, the clearest of which are the following three: First, that it is a "cognate accusative" (Maf'ul Mutlaq) for an omitted verb (Istabighu Sibghat Allah). Second, that it is an "appositive" (Badal) for "مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ" mentioned in the preceding verses. Third, that it is a "direct object" (Maf'ul Bihi) for an omitted verb (Ittabi'u Sibghat Allah).

The Quran draws a line of cancellation across all these imaginings in the above verses. First, it tells the Prophet: "Say to them: Do you argue with us concerning Allah? While He is our Lord and your Lord" (قُلْ أَ تُحَاجُّونَنا فِي اللَّهِ وَ هُوَ رَبُّنا وَ رَبُّكُمْ).

This Lord is not the exclusive property of any race or tribe; He is the Lord of all the worlds and the entire existence.

Know also that "we are pledged to our own deeds and you are pledged to your own deeds," and there is no distinction for anyone except in the light of their actions (وَ لَنَا أَعْمَالُنَا وَ لَكُمْ أَعْمَالُكُمْ).

With the difference that "we worship Him with sincerity and are pure monotheists" (whereas many of you have tainted monotheism with polytheism) (وَ نَحْنُ لَهُ مُخْلِصُونَ).

The next verse responds to another part of these baseless claims, saying: "Or do you say that Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the Tribes were Jews or Christians?" (أَمْ تَقُولُونَ إِنَّ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ إِسْماعِيلَ وَ إِسْحاقَ وَ يَعْقُوبَ وَ الْأَسْباطَ كانُوا هُوداً أَوْ نَصارى).

"Say: Do you know better or does Allah?" (قُلْ أَ أَنْتُمْ أَعْلَمُ أَمِ اللَّهُ).

Allah knows better than anyone that they were neither Jews nor Christians.

You also know, more or less, that many of these Prophets walked the earth before Moses and Jesus. Even if you did not know, making such an attribution to them without knowledge is a slander, a sin, and a concealment of the truth: "And who is more unjust than he who conceals a testimony he has from Allah?" (وَ مَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنْ كَتَمَ شَهادَةً عِنْدَهُ مِنَ اللَّهِ).

"But know that Allah is not heedless of what you do" (وَ مَا اللَّهُ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ).

It is strange; when a person falls into the gear of stubbornness and prejudice, they even deny historical certainties. For example, they count Prophets such as Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob—who were born and passed away before Moses and Jesus—as followers of Moses or Christ, and they conceal a reality so clear. A reality that concerns the destiny of the people and their religion and faith; for this reason, the Quran has introduced them as the most unjust of individuals, for no injustice is greater than for people to knowingly conceal truths and leave people wandering in the wilderness.

In the final verse under discussion, it responds to them in another way: It says, assuming all these claims were true, "They were a community that has passed away; their record of deeds is closed, their era has ended, and their deeds belong to themselves" (تِلْكَ أُمَّةٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ لَها ما كَسَبَتْ).

"And you are responsible for your own deeds, and you have no responsibility for their deeds" (وَ لَكُمْ ما كَسَبْتُمْ وَ لا تُسْئَلُونَ عَمَّا كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

In short, a living nation must rely on its own actions, not on its past history. A human being must rely on his own virtue, for no gain comes to you from the virtue of your father, even if your father was virtuous!

End of the first Juz' of the Holy Quran Holy Mashhad, 16 Muharram al-Haram, 1403

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 132.

Ayah

۞ سَيَقُولُ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَا وَلَّىٰهُمْ عَن قِبْلَتِهِمُ ٱلَّتِى كَانُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا ۚ قُل لِّلَّهِ ٱلْمَشْرِقُ وَٱلْمَغْرِبُ ۚ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (١٤٢)
The foolish among the people will say, ‘What has turned them away from the qiblah they were following?’ Say, ‘To Allah belong the east and the west. He guides whomever He wishes to a straight path.’ (142)

Commentary

The Event of the Change of Qiblah

This verse and the several verses following it refer to one of the most significant developments in the history of Islam, which created a massive wave among the people. To explain: by the command of God, the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله prayed toward "Bayt al-Maqdis" (Jerusalem) for thirteen years after his appointment (Ba'thah) in Mecca, and for several months after the migration (Hijrah) in Medina. However, after that, the Qiblah was changed, and the Muslims were commanded to pray toward the "Kaaba."

Commentators differ regarding how many months the Muslims worshipped toward Bayt al-Maqdis while in Medina; they have mentioned periods ranging from seven to seventeen months. Regardless of the duration, during this time, they were subject to the reproach of the Jews, because Bayt al-Maqdis was originally the Qiblah of the Jews. They would say to the Muslims: "These people have no independence of their own and pray toward our Qiblah, and this is proof that we are on the right path."

These conversations were distressing for the Prophet of Islam and the Muslims. On one hand, they were obedient to Allah's command, and on the other hand, the taunts of the Jews did not cease. For this reason, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would look toward the sky at night, as if he were waiting for Divine revelation.

Some time passed in this anticipation until the command to change the Qiblah was issued. While the Prophet had already prayed two units (rak'ahs) of the Dhuhr prayer in the "Bani Salim" Mosque toward Bayt al-Maqdis, Gabriel was commissioned to take the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله arm and turn him toward the Kaaba [1]. The Jews were deeply upset by this event and, according to their long-standing habit, began making excuses and raising objections. Previously, they had said: "We are better than the Muslims because they lack independence regarding the Qiblah and follow us." But as soon as the command to change the Qiblah was issued by Allah, they began to protest, as the Quran states in the verse under discussion:

"The foolish among the people will soon say, 'What has turned them (the Muslims) away from the Qiblah they used to face?'" (سَيَقُولُ السُّفَهاءُ مِنَ النَّاسِ ما وَلَّاهُمْ عَنْ قِبْلَتِهِمُ الَّتِي كانُوا عَلَيْها).

Why did they turn away from the Qiblah of the previous prophets today? If the first Qiblah was correct, what is the meaning of this change? And if the second one is correct, why did you pray toward Bayt al-Maqdis for thirteen years and several months?! Allah commands His Prophet: "Say to them: To Allah belong both the East and the West; He guides whom He wills to a straight path" (قُلْ لِلَّهِ الْمَشْرِقُ وَ الْمَغْرِبُ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ‌).

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Volume 1, Page 223.

This was a decisive and clear argument against the excuse-seekers: that Bayt al-Maqdis, the Kaaba, and everywhere else are the property of Allah. In essence, Allah has no house or physical location; what matters is submission to His command. Wherever Allah commands to pray toward, that direction is sacred and respected, and no place possesses inherent nobility without His grace.

The change of Qiblah was, in reality, different stages of testing and perfection, and each is an instance of Divine guidance. It is He who leads humanity to the straight path.

Key Points

1- The Meaning of "Sufaha"

"Sufaha" (سفهاء) is the plural of "Safih" (سفيه), which originally refers to someone whose body is light and moves easily. The Arabs call the lightweight reins of animals that move in every direction "Safih." However, this word gradually came to be used to mean "light-headed" or "foolish" and became its primary meaning, whether this foolishness is in religious or worldly matters.

2- The Issue of "Abrogation" (Naskh)

As we have previously mentioned, the issue of the "abrogation" of laws and the changing of educational programs at different intervals of time is not a new or strange matter to be objected to. However, the Jewish excuse-seekers considered this a good pretext to divert minds from Islam and campaigned heavily on it. But as we shall see in the following verses, the Quran has given them logical and crushing responses.

3- Commentary on the Phrase "يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ"

The phrase "يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ" (He guides whom He wills), as we have also stated before, does not mean that Allah guides someone without reason or account. Rather, this Will stems from Allah's "Wisdom" and the consideration of benefits and harms.

Ayah

وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ أُمَّةً وَسَطًا لِّتَكُونُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَيَكُونَ ٱلرَّسُولُ عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيدًا ۗ وَمَا جَعَلْنَا ٱلْقِبْلَةَ ٱلَّتِى كُنتَ عَلَيْهَآ إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَن يَتَّبِعُ ٱلرَّسُولَ مِمَّن يَنقَلِبُ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ ۚ وَإِن كَانَتْ لَكَبِيرَةً إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ هَدَى ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُضِيعَ إِيمَـٰنَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِٱلنَّاسِ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٤٣)
Thus We have made you a middle nation that you may be witnesses to the people, and that the Apostle may be a witness to you. We did not appoint the qiblah you were following, but that We may ascertain those who follow the Apostle from those who turn back on their heels. It was indeed a hard thing except for those whom Allah has guided. And Allah would not let your prayers go to waste. Indeed Allah is most kind and merciful to mankind. (143)

Commentary

The Middle Nation

In the verse under discussion, reference is made to part of the philosophy and secrets behind the change of the Qiblah.

First, It says: Just as (Your Qiblah is a middle Qiblah), We have made you a middle nation (وَ كَذلِكَ جَعَلْناكُمْ أُمَّةً وَسَطاً).

A nation that is moderate in every aspect—neither lagging behind nor rushing ahead, neither in excess nor in deficiency—a model and an example.

But why is the Qiblah of the Muslims a middle Qiblah? Because the Christians stood approximately toward the East, as most Christian nations lived in Arab lands and, to face the birthplace of Jesus in Jerusalem, they had to face East; thus, the East was considered their Qiblah. On the other hand, the Jews, who mostly resided in the Levant (Sham), Babylon, and similar regions, faced Jerusalem, which was approximately to their West; thus, the West was their Qiblah.

However, the "Kaaba," which for the Muslims of that time (the Muslims of Medina) was located to the South—between the East and the West—was considered a middle line.

In fact, all these points can be inferred from the phrase "وَ كَذلِكَ" (And thus), although commentators have offered other possibilities for this phrase that are subject to debate and critique.

In any case, it seems the Quran intends to mention a connection between all Islamic programs: that not only is the Muslims' Qiblah a middle one, but all their programs possess this characteristic.

Then It adds: "The purpose was that you might be witnesses over the people, and the Prophet be a witness over you" (لِتَكُونُوا شُهَداءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَ يَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيداً).

The expression of the Islamic nation being "witnesses" over the people of the world, and likewise the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله being a "witness" over the Muslims, may refer to being a role model and an exemplar, as witnesses are always chosen from among exemplary individuals.

This means that by possessing these beliefs and teachings, you are a model nation, just as the Prophet is a model individual among you.

Through your actions and programs, you testify that a human being can be both a person of religion and a person of the world; while being social, one can fully preserve their spiritual and internal aspects. With these beliefs and programs, you testify that religion and science, this world and the hereafter, are not only not in conflict but rather one serves the other.

Then, referring to another secret of the change of Qiblah, It says: "And We did not appoint the Qiblah which you used to face (Jerusalem) except that We might know those who follow the Messenger from those who turn back on their heels" (وَ ما جَعَلْنَا الْقِبْلَةَ الَّتِي كُنْتَ عَلَيْها إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَنْ يَتَّبِعُ الرَّسُولُ مِمَّنْ يَنْقَلِبُ عَلى‌ عَقِبَيْهِ‌).

It is noteworthy that He does not say: "so those who follow you," but rather says: "those who follow the Messenger of Allah," indicating that you are the leader and the envoy of God, and for this reason, they must submit to your command in everything. The Qiblah is a simple matter; even if he were to command something beyond that, making excuses would be evidence of retaining the temperament of the era of polytheism and idolatry.

The phrase "مِمَّنْ يَنْقَلِبُ عَلى‌ عَقِبَيْهِ‌," which literally means turning back on one's heels, refers to regression and going backward.

Then It adds: "And indeed, it was difficult except for those whom Allah has guided" (وَ إِنْ كانَتْ لَكَبِيرَةً إِلَّا عَلَى الَّذِينَ هَدَى اللَّهُ‌).

Indeed, without divine guidance, that spirit of absolute submission to His command is not attained. The important thing is for a person to be so submissive that they do not feel heaviness or difficulty even in executing such commands; rather, because it is from Him, it should be sweeter than honey to their palate.

And since whispering enemies or ignorant friends thought that with the change of Qiblah, our previous deeds and worship might be void and our reward lost, at the end of the verse It adds: "Allah would never let your faith (prayers) go to waste, for Allah is Kind and Merciful to all people" (وَ ما كانَ اللَّهُ لِيُضِيعَ إِيمانَكُمْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِالنَّاسِ لَرَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

His commands are like a physician's prescriptions; one day this prescription is life-saving, and another day another prescription. Each is good in its place and a guarantor of happiness and perfection. Therefore, the change of Qiblah should not cause you any concern regarding past or future prayers and worship, as all of them were and are correct.

Key Points

1- The Secrets of the Change of Qiblah

The change of the Qiblah from Jerusalem to the Kaaba raised questions for everyone. Those who thought every decree must be permanent said: "If we were meant to pray toward the Kaaba, why wasn't it said from the first day? And if Jerusalem was superior, being the Qiblah of previous prophets, why was it changed?"

Enemies also found a wide field for spreading poison. They might have said: "At first, he turned toward the Qiblah of past prophets, but after his victories, racism overcame him and he changed it to the Qiblah of his own people!" Or they said: "To attract the Jews and Christians, he first accepted Jerusalem; when it didn't work, he changed it to the Kaaba."

It is obvious what anxiety and distress these whispers would cause, especially in a society where the light of knowledge and faith had not yet illuminated its corners, and the residues of the era of polytheism and idolatry still existed.

Therefore, the Quran explicitly states in the above verse: This was a great test to distinguish the positions of the believers and the polytheists.

It is not unlikely that one of the important reasons for the change of Qiblah was the following:

Since the Kaaba was at that time the center of the polytheists' idols, Muslims were commanded to temporarily pray toward Jerusalem to separate their ranks from the polytheists.

However, when they migrated to Medina and formed a government and a nation, and their ranks became completely distinct from others, the continuation of this situation was no longer necessary. At this point, they returned to the Kaaba—the oldest center of monotheism and the most long-standing hub of the prophets.

Clearly, both praying toward Jerusalem was difficult for those who considered the Kaaba the spiritual capital of their race, and returning to the Kaaba after Jerusalem was difficult after becoming accustomed to the first Qiblah.

In this way, the Muslims were placed in the crucible of testing, so that whatever traces of polytheism remained in their being would burn away in this hot furnace, and they would sever their ties with their polytheistic past, and the spirit of absolute submission to the command of Truth would emerge within them.

Fundamentally, as we have said, Allah has no place or location; the Qiblah is a symbol for the unity of ranks and the revival of monotheistic memories, and changing it changes nothing [of His essence]. The important thing is submission to His command and breaking the idols of prejudice, stubbornness, and selfishness.

2- The Islamic Nation: A Middle Nation

The word "وسط" (middle/center) in the language means both the midpoint between two things and something attractive, beautiful, excellent, and noble. Both of these apparently return to one reality, because usually nobility and beauty lie in something being far from excess and deficiency and being in a state of moderation.

What a beautiful expression the Quran uses here regarding the Islamic nation, calling them a middle and moderate nation.

Moderate in "terms of belief," neither following the path of "exaggeration" (ghuluww) nor the path of "shortcoming and polytheism"; neither supporters of "compulsion" (jabr) nor "absolute delegation" (tafwid); neither believing in "anthropomorphism" (tashbih) regarding God's attributes nor "divesting" (ta'til) them.

Moderate in terms of spiritual and material values: neither completely submerged in the material world such that spirituality is forgotten, nor so absorbed in the spiritual realm that they are completely unaware of the material world; neither like a large group of Jews who recognize nothing but material inclination, nor like Christian monks who completely renounce the world.

Moderate in terms of science and knowledge: neither so stagnant in their own knowledge that they do not accept the sciences of others, nor so lost that they rise at every sound.

Moderate in terms of social relations: neither building a wall around themselves to be completely separated from the world, nor losing their identity and independence to be dissolved into this or that nation like those infatuated with the West or the East!

Moderate in terms of moral methods, in terms of worship, in terms of thinking—in short, moderate in all aspects of life and existence.

A true Muslim can never be a one-dimensional human being; rather, he is a human being with various dimensions: thoughtful, faithful, just, a striver (mujahid), a fighter, brave, kind, active, aware, and forgiving.

The expression "middle limit" is one that, on one hand, defines the issue of the Islamic nation being a witness, because those who stand on the middle line can see all the deviant lines on the left and right! On the other hand, it is an expression that contains the reason for the claim, saying: If you are witnesses over the creation of the world, it is because of this moderation and your being a middle nation. [^1]

3- A Nation That Can Be a Model in Every Aspect

If what we said above regarding the meaning of the "middle nation" is gathered in a people, they are undoubtedly the pioneers of Truth and the witnesses of reality, because their programs are the scale and criterion for distinguishing truth from falsehood.

Interestingly, in several narrations transmitted to us from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, we read:

"We are the middle nation, and we are the witnesses of Allah over His creation and His proofs on His earth... We are the witnesses over the people... To us the exaggerator (ghali) must return, and with us the one who falls short (muqassir) must catch up." [^2]

Undoubtedly, these narrations—as we have said many times—do not limit the broad concept of the verse but rather state the most perfect instances of this model nation and describe the exemplars who stand in the front rank.

[^1]: Al-Manar, under the verses discussed. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 134.

4- Commentary on the Phrase "Lina'lama" (That We Might Know)

The phrase "لِنَعْلَمَ" (that We might know) and similar expressions used repeatedly in the Quran regarding Allah do not mean that Allah did not know something and then became aware of it. Rather, what is meant is the actualization and external manifestation of these realities.

To explain: Allah has been aware of all events and beings from eternity, even though they come into existence gradually. Therefore, the occurrence of events and beings adds nothing to His knowledge; rather, what He knew previously is implemented in this way. It is like an architect who designs a building plan and is aware of all its details before its existence, and then gradually implements that plan. When the architect decides to implement a part of the building plan, he says, "I am doing this so that I may see what I had in mind," meaning "I want to clothe my intellectual plan in the garment of 'action'." (Undoubtedly, God's knowledge differs greatly from human knowledge—as we have discussed in the section on God's attributes—the purpose is to provide an example to clarify the discussion).

The phrase "وَ إِنْ كانَتْ لَكَبِيرَةً إِلَّا عَلَى الَّذِينَ هَدَى اللَّهُ‌" reflects the reality that stepping against habit and not being influenced by misplaced emotions is a very difficult task, except for those who truly believe in Allah and are submissive to His command.

Ayah

قَدْ نَرَىٰ تَقَلُّبَ وَجْهِكَ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۖ فَلَنُوَلِّيَنَّكَ قِبْلَةً تَرْضَىٰهَا ۚ فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۚ وَحَيْثُ مَا كُنتُمْ فَوَلُّوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ شَطْرَهُۥ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ لَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ (١٤٤)
We certainly see you turning your face about in the sky. We will surely turn you to a qiblah of your liking: so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque, and wherever you may be, turn your faces towards it! Indeed those who were given the Book surely know that it is the truth from their Lord. And Allah is not oblivious of what they do. (144)

Commentary

Turn Towards the Kaaba Everywhere

As previously mentioned, Jerusalem (Bayt al-Maqdis) was the first and temporary Qibla of the Muslims. Therefore, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was waiting for the command to change the Qibla to be issued, especially since the Jews, after the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) arrival in Medina, had used this issue as a pretext to constantly reproach the Muslims, saying: "These people have no independence of their own; before we taught them, they did not understand the matter of the Qibla at all! Their acceptance of our Qibla is proof of their acceptance of our religion!" and such claims...

In the verse under discussion, this matter is addressed and the command to change the Qibla is issued. It says: "We certainly see the turning of your face toward the heaven (the center of the descent of revelation)" (قَدْ نَرى‌ تَقَلُّبَ وَجْهِكَ فِي السَّماءِ).

"Now We will surely turn you to a Qibla with which you will be pleased" (فَلَنُوَلِّيَنَّكَ قِبْلَةً تَرْضاها).

"So turn your face toward the Sacred Mosque (Masjid al-Haram) and the House of the Kaaba" (فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ‌).

And not only in Medina, "And wherever you [believers] are, turn your faces toward it" (وَ حَيْثُ ما كُنْتُمْ فَوَلُّوا وُجُوهَكُمْ شَطْرَهُ‌).

We know that this change of Qibla—according to narrations—took place during the Dhuhr (noon) prayer, occurring at a sensitive and remarkable moment. The messenger of Allah's revelation took the arms of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and turned him from Jerusalem toward the Kaaba, and the Muslims immediately changed their rows. In one narration, we even read that the women moved to the places of the men and the men to the places of the women (it should be noted that Jerusalem was approximately to the north, while the Kaaba was located exactly to the south).

Interestingly, the change of Qibla was mentioned in previous scriptures as one of the signs of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), as they had read that he would pray toward two Qiblas: "He prays toward the two Qiblas" (يصلى الى القبلتين).

Therefore, in the above verse, after this command, it adds: "And indeed, those who have been given the Scripture well know that it is the truth from their Lord" (وَ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ لَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ‌).

Furthermore, the very fact that the Prophet of Islam was not influenced by the customs of his environment and initially set aside the Kaaba—which was the center of idols and beloved by all Arabs—and recognized the Qibla of a limited minority, was itself proof of the truthfulness of his call and the divine nature of his programs.

And at the end, it adds: "And Allah is not unaware of what they do" (وَ مَا اللَّهُ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

This means that instead of introducing this change of Qibla as a sign of his truthfulness mentioned in previous books, they concealed it and instead created an uproar over it. Allah is aware of both their actions and their intentions.

Key Points

1- The Order of the Verses

The content of the verse under discussion clearly shows that it was revealed before the preceding verse. If we see it placed after it in the Quran, it is because the verses of the Quran were not collected according to the chronological order of revelation; rather, sometimes certain contexts required that a verse revealed later be mentioned earlier by the order of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), which originated from Allah's command (among these contexts is observing the priority and importance of the subjects).

2- A Painful Anticipation!

From the above verse, it is understood that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) had a special affection for the Kaaba and was waiting for the command for it to become the Qibla. The reason for this should be sought in the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) deep affection for Ibrahim (عليه السلام) and his legacy. Beyond this, the "Kaaba" was the oldest house of Monotheism. He knew that "Jerusalem" was the temporary Qibla of the Muslims and wished for the original and final Qibla to be determined sooner.

However, since the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was submissive to Allah's command, he did not even utter this request; only his expectant gazes toward the sky showed that he held a deep longing and passion in his heart.

Additionally, the expression "heaven" (sky) might be because the "Angel of Revelation" descended upon him from above; otherwise, we know that Allah has no physical place, nor does His revelation have a specific location.

3- The Meaning of "Shatr"

Another point worthy of attention here is that in the above verse, instead of the word "Kaaba," the phrase "Shatr al-Masjid al-Haram" (the direction of the Sacred Mosque) is mentioned.

This expression may be because for those praying in places far from Mecca, aligning perfectly with the House of the Kaaba is very difficult or impossible. Therefore, instead of the "Kaaba," the "Sacred Mosque," which is a larger area, is mentioned. Specifically, the word "Shatr," which means "direction or side," was chosen so that fulfilling this Islamic command would be possible for everyone wherever they may be. Furthermore, it clarifies the status of long rows in congregational prayers, which are often longer than the width of the Kaaba itself.

It is obvious that precise alignment with the House of the Kaaba, or even the Sacred Mosque, is a very difficult task for those praying from afar, but standing toward its direction is easy for everyone. [^1]

[^1]: Some commentators have said that one of the meanings of "Shatr" is "half," and therefore the concept of "Shatr al-Masjid al-Haram" would be the same as "the middle of the Sacred Mosque," and we know that the House of the Kaaba is located in the middle of the Sacred Mosque (Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr al-Razi, under the verse in question).

4- A Universal Address

Undoubtedly, the addresses of the Quran, even if outwardly directed at the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), have a general meaning that includes all Muslims (except for a few cases where we have evidence of it being exclusive to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)). Given this, the question arises: why does the above verse command the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) once to pray toward the Sacred Mosque, and then address all believers once more?

This repetition might be because the issue of the change of Qibla was a controversial and noisy matter. It was possible that due to the uproars and propaganda, the minds of new Muslims might become confused, and they might shirk the duty of praying toward the Kaaba under the pretext that the command "turn your face" was exclusive to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). Therefore, after this specific address, Allah directed a general address to all Muslims to emphasize that this change of Qibla is by no means a private matter but is for all Muslims.

5- Was the Change of Qibla for the Prophet's Satisfaction?

The phrase "a Qibla with which you will be pleased" (قبلة ترضاها) might create the misconception that this change took place solely for the Prophet's satisfaction. However, this misconception is dispelled by noting that Jerusalem was a temporary Qibla, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was waiting for the announcement of the final Qibla. With its issuance, on one hand, the Jews' tongue of reproach against the Muslims would be cut off, and on the other hand, it would provide more grounds for the people of Hijaz—who had a special devotion to the Kaaba—to accept the religion of Islam. Additionally, the announcement of the first Qibla also had the effect of separating Islam from being a racial religion, and it struck a blow against the idols of the pagans that were placed in the Kaaba.

6- The Kaaba: Center of a Great Circle

If someone were to look from outside the Earth at the rows of Muslim worshippers praying toward the Kaaba, they would see numerous circles, one inside the other, reaching the main focus which is the point of the Kaaba, highlighting its unity and centrality.

Undoubtedly, this Qibla is more inspiring than the practice of, for example, the Christians, who all pray toward the East regardless of where they are.

Interestingly, the specific attention of Muslims to determining the direction of the Kaaba caused the sciences of astronomy and geography to grow rapidly among Muslims in the early days of Islam, as calculating the direction of the Qibla in different points on Earth was impossible without familiarity with these sciences.

Ayah

وَلَئِنْ أَتَيْتَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِكُلِّ ءَايَةٍ مَّا تَبِعُوا۟ قِبْلَتَكَ ۚ وَمَآ أَنتَ بِتَابِعٍ قِبْلَتَهُمْ ۚ وَمَا بَعْضُهُم بِتَابِعٍ قِبْلَةَ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَلَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَآءَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ ۙ إِنَّكَ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٤٥)
Even if you bring those who were given the Book every [kind of] sign, they will not follow your qiblah. Nor shall you follow their qiblah, nor will any of them follow the qiblah of the other. And if you follow their desires, after the knowledge that has come to you, you will be one of the wrongdoers. (145)

Commentary

They Will Not Be Satisfied at Any Price

In the commentary of the previous verse, we read that the People of the Book knew that the change of the Qiblah from Jerusalem toward the Kaaba was not only not a flaw against the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, but was among the signs of his truthfulness; for they had read in their own books that the promised Prophet would pray toward two Qiblahs. However, prejudices prevented them from accepting the truth.

In principle, as long as a person has not made a prior decision regarding issues, they are open to understanding, and one can change their opinion or prove a truth to them through logic, reasoning, or the presentation of miracles.

However, when they have already firmly established their position—especially in the case of prejudiced and ignorant individuals—such people cannot be changed at any price.

Therefore, the Quran states with decisiveness in the verse under discussion: "And even if you were to bring every sign and proof to those who were given the Scripture, they would not follow your Qiblah" (وَ لَئِنْ أَتَيْتَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ بِكُلِّ آيَةٍ ما تَبِعُوا قِبْلَتَكَ‌).

Thus, do not exhaust yourself, for they will never submit to the truth at any price, because the spirit of seeking the truth has died within them.

Unfortunately, all Divine Prophets were faced with such individuals, who were either influential wealthy elites, deviant and world-worshipping scholars, or the ignorant and prejudiced masses!

Afterward, It adds: "Nor will you ever be a follower of their Qiblah" (وَ ما أَنْتَ بِتابِعٍ قِبْلَتَهُمْ‌).

This means that if they imagine that with these outcries and commotions the Qiblah of the Muslims will change once again, they are sorely mistaken; this is the permanent and final Qiblah of the Muslims.

This expression is, in fact, one of the ways to end the controversies of the opponents: that a person stands with decisiveness and shows that they will not create any change in their path due to these noises.

Then It adds: and they too are so prejudiced in their beliefs that "none of them is a follower of the Qiblah of the other" (وَ ما بَعْضُهُمْ بِتابِعٍ قِبْلَةَ بَعْضٍ‌).

Neither do the Jews follow the Qiblah of the Christians, nor do the Christians follow the Qiblah of the Jews.

And again, for further emphasis and decisiveness, It warns the Prophet: "And if you were to follow their desires after the knowledge that has come to you, then you would surely be among the wrongdoers" (وَ لَئِنِ اتَّبَعْتَ أَهْواءَهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَكَ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِنَّكَ إِذاً لَمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

In the Quran, similar addresses to the Prophet in the form of conditional statements are frequently seen, and their objective is actually three things:

First, so that everyone knows that in Divine laws there is no discrimination or difference among people, and even the Prophets are subject to these laws. Therefore, if—hypothetically and impossibly—the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله were to deviate from the truth, he would be subject to the punishment of the Lord, even though such a premise is impossible regarding the Prophets given their overflowing faith, knowledge, and station of piety and God-wariness (and as it is said, a conditional statement does not imply the occurrence of the condition).

Second, so that other people take full account of themselves and realize that when the Prophet is addressed as such, how they must be mindful of their own responsibilities; they must never surrender to the deviant desires of the enemies and their hue and cry.

Third, to make it clear that the Prophet himself has no authority to make any change or alteration in the commands of Allah, and so to speak, it is not such that they can come to terms or compromise with him; rather, he too is a servant submissive to the command of the Lord.

Ayahs

ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَعْرِفُونَهُۥ كَمَا يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ ۖ وَإِنَّ فَرِيقًا مِّنْهُمْ لَيَكْتُمُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (١٤٦)
Those whom We have given the Book recognize him just as they recognize their sons, but a part of them indeed conceal the truth while they know. (146)
ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۖ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ (١٤٧)
This is the truth from your Lord; so do not be among the skeptics. (147)

Commentary

They Know Him Well

Following the previous discussions regarding the stubbornness and prejudice of a group of the People of the Book, the first verse under discussion states: "The scholars of the People of the Book recognize the Prophet of Islam as well as they recognize their own children" (الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ يَعْرِفُونَهُ كَما يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْناءَهُمْ‌).

They have read his name, signs, and characteristics in their own religious books.

"But a group of them deliberately attempt to conceal the truth" (وَ إِنَّ فَرِيقاً مِنْهُمْ لَيَكْتُمُونَ الْحَقَّ وَ هُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

Although a group of them accepted Islam upon witnessing these clear signs—as it is narrated from Abdullah ibn Salam, who was among the Jewish scholars and later embraced Islam, that he used to say: "I know the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله better than I know my own son!" (انا اعلم به منى يا بنى) [1]

[1]: Al-Manar, Volume 2, and Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr Razi, under the verse being discussed.

This verse unveils a fascinating reality: that the description of the physical and spiritual attributes and characteristics of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله in previous scriptures was so vivid and clear that it formed a complete portrait of him in the minds of those who were familiar with those books.

Can anyone possibly imagine that there was no mention of the Prophet of Islam or any sign of him in those books, yet the Prophet of Islam would say to them with such explicitness: "All my attributes are present in your books"? If that were the case, would not all the scholars of the People of the Book have risen in fierce and explicit opposition against him? Would they not have said to him, "Here are we and here are our books; where in them are your name and attributes recorded?" And would it have been possible for even a single one of their scholars to submit to the Prophet of Islam?! Therefore, such verses are themselves evidence of their own truthfulness and authenticity.

Then, as an emphasis on the previous discussions regarding the change of the Qiblah, or Islamic rulings in general, He says: "This is the truth from your Lord, so never be among the doubters" (الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ فَلا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُمْتَرِينَ‌).

With this sentence, He consoles the Prophet and emphasizes that he should not allow the slightest doubt to enter his mind—whether regarding the issue of the change of Qiblah or otherwise—in the face of the enemies' propaganda, even if the enemies mobilize all their forces in this path.

Although the addressee in this statement is the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, as we mentioned in previous verses, the actual target is all people. Otherwise, it is certain that a Prophet who deals with revelation through direct witnessing never allows doubt or hesitation to enter his heart, for revelation to him has the quality of sensory perception and the "Certainty of Sight" (Ayn al-Yaqin).

Ayah

وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيهَا ۖ فَٱسْتَبِقُوا۟ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ ۚ أَيْنَ مَا تَكُونُوا۟ يَأْتِ بِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٤٨)
Everyone has a cynosure to which he turns; so take the lead in all good works. Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you all together. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (148)

Commentary

Every Nation Has a Direction

This verse is, in fact, a response to the Jewish people, who, as we have seen, had raised a great deal of commotion regarding the issue of the change of the Qiblah. It states: "For every nation there is a direction to which they turn (which Allah has appointed for them)" (وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيهَا).

Throughout the history of the Prophets, there have been various directions for prayer (Qiblahs), and changing it is not a strange occurrence. The Qiblah is not like the fundamental principles of religion (Usul al-Din) which are immutable, nor is it like the laws of the physical universe (Takwini) where deviation is impossible. Therefore, do not engage in excessive debate regarding the Qiblah; instead, "race with one another towards good deeds" (فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْرَاتِ).

Instead of spending all your time discussing this secondary issue, pursue goodness and purity, which offer a vast and expansive field; strive to surpass one another in it, for the criterion of your existential value is your pure and righteous actions.

This theme is exactly like what appears in verse 177 of this same Surah:

لَيْسَ الْبِرَّ أَنْ تُوَلُّوا وُجُوهَكُمْ قِبَلَ الْمَشْرِقِ وَ الْمَغْرِبِ وَ لكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَ الْمَلائِكَةِ وَ الْكِتابِ وَ النَّبِيِّينَ‌: "Righteousness is not that you turn your faces toward the east or the west, but [true] righteousness is [in] one who believes in Allah, the Last Day, the angels, the Book, and the prophets..." (and performs righteous deeds inspired by firm faith).

If you wish to test "Islam" or "the Muslims," test them by these programs, not by the issue of the change of the Qiblah.

Then, as a warning to the critics and an encouragement to the doers of good, He says:

"Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you all together" (أَيْنَ مَا تَكُونُوا يَأْتِ بِكُمُ اللَّهُ جَمِيعاً).

In that great court of the Resurrection, which is the final scene of reward and punishment.

It is not the case that some are busy performing the best of deeds while another group does nothing but spread poison, destroy, and disrupt, and yet they remain equal without any accountability or recompense. Rest assured that such a day is ahead, and all accounts will be settled.

And since this sentence might seem extraordinary to some—how Allah will gather the scattered particles of human dust wherever they may be and clothe them in the garment of a new life—He immediately adds: "Indeed, Allah is powerful over all things" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

In fact, this phrase at the end of the verse serves as the evidence for the preceding statement: "Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you all together."

Key Points

1. The Day When the Companions of al-Mahdi (عليه السلام) Gather

In several narrations transmitted to us from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, the phrase "Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you all together" has been interpreted as referring to the companions of al-Mahdi عليه السلام.

Among these, we read in the book Rawdah al-Kafi from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that after mentioning this phrase, he said:

"It refers to the companions of the Qa'im, the three hundred and thirteen men; by Allah, they are the 'numbered nation' (al-ummah al-ma'dudah). He said: By Allah, they will gather in a single hour, like autumn clouds gathering and accumulating driven by a swift wind!" [1]

It is also narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام:

"By Allah, when our Qa'im rises, Allah will gather all our followers from all cities toward him." [2]

Undoubtedly, this interpretation is among the "inner meanings" (butun) of the verse. For we know that according to narrations, the verses of the Quran sometimes have multiple meanings: one is the apparent and general meaning, and the other is the internal meaning—the so-called batn of the verse—which none know except the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the Imams عليه السلام, and those whom Allah wills.

In other words: In reality, these narrations point to the fact that the God who has the power to gather the scattered particles of human dust on the Day of Resurrection from different parts of the world can easily gather the companions of al-Mahdi in a single day and a single hour to ignite the first sparks of the revolution for the purpose of establishing the government of Divine Justice and ending oppression and tyranny.

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 139. [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

2. Commentary on the Phrase: "For every nation there is a direction to which they turn"

We mentioned that the apparent meaning of the verse refers to the various Qiblahs that Allah has established for different nations. However, some have suggested the possibility that it refers to a broader meaning encompassing the methods and programs of the creative order (Takwini), and in fact, the meaning of creative destiny (Qada and Qadar) was also intended by it [1] (reflect on this).

Of course, if it were not for the context before and after this verse, such an interpretation would be possible. However, given these contextual clues, the apparent meaning is the first one mentioned, and the phrase "He is the one who turns toward it" (هُوَ مُوَلِّيهَا) is similar to "We shall surely turn you to a Qiblah that shall please you" (فَلَنُوَلِّيَنَّكَ قِبْلَةً تَرْضَاهَا). Even assuming the verse points to that [broader] meaning, it never implies a concept of compulsory destiny; rather, it is that destiny which is compatible with the principle of free will and choice [2].

[1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 1, p. 331. [2]: For further explanation, refer to the book The Motive for the Emergence of Religion, the chapter on Qada and Qadar.

Ayahs

وَمِنْ حَيْثُ خَرَجْتَ فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٤٩)
Whencesoever you may go out, turn your face towards the Holy Mosque. Indeed it is the truth from your Lord, and Allah is not oblivious of what you do. (149)
وَمِنْ حَيْثُ خَرَجْتَ فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۚ وَحَيْثُ مَا كُنتُمْ فَوَلُّوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ شَطْرَهُۥ لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَيْكُمْ حُجَّةٌ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ فَلَا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَٱخْشَوْنِى وَلِأُتِمَّ نِعْمَتِى عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (١٥٠)
And whencesoever you may go out, turn your face towards the Holy Mosque, and wherever you may be, turn your faces towards it, so that the people may have no allegation against you, neither those of them who are wrongdoers. So do not fear them, but fear Me, that I may complete My blessing on you and so that you may be guided. (150)

Commentary

Fear Only Allah

These verses continue to follow the issue of the change of the Qiblah and its consequences.

In the first verse, addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) as an emphatic command, it says: "And from wherever you go out (from any city or land), turn your face (during prayer) toward al-Masjid al-Haram" (وَ مِنْ حَيْثُ خَرَجْتَ فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ‌).

And again, as a further emphasis, it adds: "And indeed, it is the truth from your Lord" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَلْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ‌).

At the end of this verse, as a threat to the conspirators and a warning to the believers, it says: "And Allah is not unaware of what you do" (وَ مَا اللَّهُ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

These successive emphases, which will also be followed in the next verse, all indicate the reality that the issue of the change of the Qiblah and the abrogation of the previous ruling was difficult and heavy for a group of new Muslims, just as it was a pretext for the stubborn enemies to spread their poison.

Here—and generally in all evolutionary transformations and revolutions—the thing that can end the discussions and dispel doubts and uncertainties is decisiveness, clarity, and continuous, crushing emphases. Whenever the leader of a community, at these sensitive moments, defines his position with a decisive, sharp, and unchangeable tone, he will make the friends more determined and the enemies forever despondent. This point is frequently and clearly seen in the Quran.

Furthermore, these repetitions and emphases are not, in reality, mere repetition; rather, they carry new instructions. Among them is that in the previous verses, the duty of the Muslims regarding the Qiblah was specified in relation to the city in which they lived. However, in this verse and the next, it clarifies the ruling for those praying while traveling in any location or land.

In the next verse, it repeats the general ruling of facing toward al-Masjid al-Haram in every place and every point, saying: "And from wherever you go out and to whatever point you turn, turn your face toward al-Masjid al-Haram during prayer" (وَ مِنْ حَيْثُ خَرَجْتَ فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ‌). It is true that the address in this sentence is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), but it certainly intends the generality of the worshipers. However, in the next sentence, for emphasis and explicit clarification, it adds: "And wherever you may be, turn your faces toward it" (وَ حَيْثُ ما كُنْتُمْ فَوَلُّوا وُجُوهَكُمْ شَطْرَهُ‌).

Then, at the conclusion of this same verse, it points to three important matters:

1- Silencing the opponents—It says: "This change of Qiblah took place so that the people may have no argument against you" (لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَيْكُمْ حُجَّةٌ).

Had this change of Qiblah not occurred, on one hand, the tongues of the Jews would have been opened against the Muslims, saying: "We have read in our books that the sign of the promised Prophet is that he prays toward two Qiblahs, and this sign is not in Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله)." On the other hand, the polytheists would have objected that he claims to have come to revive the religion of Abraham, so why has he forgotten the House of the Kaaba, which was founded by Abraham? However, the ruling to change from the temporary Qiblah to the permanent Qiblah silenced both groups.

But since there are always excuse-seekers and wrongdoers who do not submit to any logic, He makes an exception for this group, saying: "Except those of them who do wrong" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْهُمْ‌).

These people are not steadfast on any path. If you pray toward Jerusalem, they say, "This is the Qiblah of the Jews and you are followers of others." And if you return toward the Kaaba, they say, "You have no stability or permanence; the rest of your religion will soon be subject to change!" These excuse-seekers truly deserve the name "wrongdoers" and "oppressors," for they wrong both themselves and the people by blocking the path of their guidance.

2- Since mentioning this stubborn group by the name "wrongdoers" might create fear in some, He says: "So fear them not, but fear Me" (فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَ اخْشَوْنِي‌).

This is one of the general and fundamental principles of Islamic monotheistic (Tawhidi) education: that one should fear nothing and no one except Allah. Only the fear of Allah (or more correctly, the fear of disobeying Him) is the motto of every faithful Muslim. If this principle shines upon their soul and spirit, they will never be defeated.

However, those who only appear to be Muslims and, contrary to the above command, sometimes fear the "East" and sometimes the power of the "West," sometimes "internal hypocrites" and sometimes "external enemies"—in short, they fear everything and everyone except Allah—they are always abject, insignificant, and defeated.

3- The completion of Allah's favor is mentioned as the final goal for the change of Qiblah, as He says: "This was so that I may perfect you and free you from the bonds of prejudice, 'and that I may complete My favor upon you and that you may be guided'" (وَ لِأُتِمَّ نِعْمَتِي عَلَيْكُمْ وَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ‌).

In reality, the change of Qiblah was a type of education, perfection, and favor for the Muslims, so they might become acquainted with Islamic discipline and be freed from imitation and prejudice. For as we have said, in the beginning, to separate the ranks of the Muslims from the idolaters—who prostrated before the Kaaba, which was a great house of idols at that time—Allah commanded them to pray toward Jerusalem so that their position against the polytheists would be clear. However, after the migration of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and concurrent with the formation of the Islamic government, the command to face the Kaaba was issued, and the Muslims turned toward the oldest house of Monotheism. In this way, a stage of the evolution of the Islamic society was realized.

Ayahs

كَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا فِيكُمْ رَسُولًا مِّنكُمْ يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا وَيُزَكِّيكُمْ وَيُعَلِّمُكُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَيُعَلِّمُكُم مَّا لَمْ تَكُونُوا۟ تَعْلَمُونَ (١٥١)
Even as We sent to you an apostle from among yourselves, who recites to you Our signs and purifies you, and teaches you the Book and wisdom, and teaches you what you did not know. (151)
فَٱذْكُرُونِىٓ أَذْكُرْكُمْ وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لِى وَلَا تَكْفُرُونِ (١٥٢)
Remember Me and I will remember you, and thank Me, and do not be ungrateful to Me. (152)

Commentary

The Programs of the Messenger of Allah

In the final phrase of the previous verse, Allah stated that one of the reasons for the change of the Qiblah was to complete His favor upon the people and to guide them. In the verse under discussion, by using the word "كما" (just as), He points to the fact that the change of the Qiblah was not God's only favor upon you, but rather He has bestowed many other blessings upon you: "Just as We have sent among you a Messenger from among yourselves" (كَما أَرْسَلْنا فِيكُمْ رَسُولًا مِنْكُمْ‌).

The word "منكم" (from among you) may refer to the fact that he is of the human species, and only a human can be a teacher, leader, and role model for humans, being aware of their pains, needs, and issues; this in itself is a great blessing.

Alternatively, it may mean that he is of your race and your compatriot, for the pre-Islamic Arabs, due to intense racial prejudice, would likely not have submitted to a prophet from a race other than their own. As we read in verses 198 and 199 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara: وَ لَوْ نَزَّلْناهُ عَلى‌ بَعْضِ الْأَعْجَمِينَ فَقَرَأَهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌: "And even if We had revealed it to one of the non-Arabs, and he had recited it to them, they would not have believed in it." Thus, it was considered a significant blessing for them that the Prophet was from among themselves.

Of course, this was for the beginning of the mission; eventually, the issue of race and (geographical) homeland was removed from the programs, and the primary and eternal decree of Islam—which introduces the "world" as the homeland and "humanity" as the race—was announced.

After mentioning this blessing, He points to four other blessings that reached the Muslims through the grace of this Prophet:

1- "He recites Our verses to you" (يَتْلُوا عَلَيْكُمْ آياتِنا).

"يتلوا" is derived from the root "تلاوت" (tilawah), meaning to bring things forth sequentially. Therefore, when phrases are read consecutively (and according to a correct system), it is referred to as tilawah. This means the Prophet recites the words of God to you in a correct and appropriate sequence to prepare your hearts to accept their meanings. Thus, the organized and calculated recitation by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is for creating readiness for the education and purification mentioned in the subsequent phrases.

2- "He purifies you" (وَ يُزَكِّيكُمْ‌).

"تزكيه" (Tazkiyah) originally (as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat) means to increase and cause to grow. That is, by seeking help from the verses of God, he increases your spiritual and material perfections and causes your soul to grow; he makes the flowers of virtue bloom upon the branches of your existence and wipes away the various ugly traits that had engulfed your society during the Age of Ignorance.

3- "And he teaches you the Book and wisdom" (وَ يُعَلِّمُكُمُ الْكِتابَ وَ الْحِكْمَةَ).

Although "teaching" (ta'lim) naturally precedes "purification" (tarbiyah), as we have previously noted, the Holy Quran often places purification before teaching to establish the fact that the ultimate goal is "purification."

The difference between "the Book" and "wisdom" (hikmah) might be that "the Book" refers to the verses of the Quran and divine revelation revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as a miracle, while "wisdom" refers to the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his teachings, which are called the Sunnah.

It is also possible that "the Book" refers to the fundamental decrees, while "wisdom" refers to their "secrets, philosophies, causes, and results."

Some commentators have also suggested that "wisdom" refers to a state and faculty resulting from the teachings of the Book, through which a person can perform every action in its proper place. [^1] The author of Tafsir al-Manar, after mentioning the first interpretation (that wisdom means the Sunnah), considers it incorrect and argues using verse 39 of Surah Al-Isra: ذلِكَ مِمَّا أَوْحى‌ إِلَيْكَ رَبُّكَ مِنَ الْحِكْمَةِ (That is from what your Lord has revealed to you of wisdom).

However, in our view, the answer to this objection is clear, because "wisdom" has a broad meaning and can be applied to the verses of the Quran and the secrets revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله through it. But where "wisdom" is placed in contrast to "the Book" (the Quran)—as in the verse under discussion and similar verses—it certainly refers to something other than the Book, and that would be nothing other than the Sunnah.

[^1]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 1, p. 1.

4- "And he teaches you that which you did not know" (وَ يُعَلِّمُكُمْ ما لَمْ تَكُونُوا تَعْلَمُونَ‌).

Although this subject was present in the previous phrase regarding the teaching of the Book and wisdom, the Quran specifically separates it to make them understand that if it were not for the prophets, many sciences would have remained hidden from you forever. They were not only moral and social leaders but also intellectual leaders, without whose guidance human sciences would not have flourished.

In view of the various divine blessings described in this verse, the next verse announces to the people that it is fitting for them to give thanks for these great blessings and, by utilizing these favors correctly, fulfill the right of gratitude to Him. He says: "Remember Me, and I will remember you; and be grateful to Me, and do not be ungrateful" (فَاذْكُرُونِي أَذْكُرْكُمْ وَ اشْكُرُوا لِي وَ لا تَكْفُرُونِ‌).

It is obvious that the phrase "Remember Me, and I will remember you" does not refer to an emotional sense between God and His servants in the way it exists among humans, where they say to each other: "Remember us so that we remember you."

Rather, it refers to a principle of education and creation; that is: "Be mindful of Me, be mindful of the Pure Essence who is the source of all goodness and beauty, and by this means, purify and enlighten your soul and spirit, making them ready to receive the Lord's mercy." Your attention to this Pure Essence makes you more sincere, determined, powerful, and united in your activities.

Similarly, the purpose of "gratitude and the absence of ingratitude" is not merely a formal matter or something said with the tongue; rather, it means that you should use every blessing correctly in its proper place and employ it toward the very goal for which it was created, so that it may become a cause for the increase of God's mercy and favor.

Key Points

1- The Views of Commentators on the Interpretation of the Phrase "فَاذْكُرُونِي أَذْكُرْكُمْ‌"

Commentators have mentioned various interpretations in explaining this phrase and what is meant by the servants' remembrance and how Allah's remembrance is. Fakhr al-Razi, in his Tafsir al-Kabir, summarized them into ten themes:

1- Remember Me through "obedience," so I may remember you with My "mercy" (the evidence for this is verse 132 of Surah Al-Imran: أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَ الرَّسُولَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ‌).

2- Remember Me through "supplication," so I may remember you with "acceptance" (the evidence for this is verse 60 of Surah Ghafir: ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ‌).

3- Remember Me with "praise and obedience," so I may remember you with "praise and favor."

4- Remember Me in "this world," so I may remember you in "the next world."

5- Remember Me in "seclusion," so I may remember you in "gathering."

6- Remember Me "during times of abundance," so I may remember you in "hardships."

7- Remember Me through "worship," so I may remember you with "help" (the evidence for this is the phrase إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وَ إِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ‌).

8- Remember Me through "striving" (jihad), so I may remember you with "guidance" (the evidence for this is verse 69 of Surah Al-Ankabut: وَ الَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا).

9- Remember Me with "truthfulness and sincerity," and I will remember you with "deliverance and increased distinction."

10- Remember Me through "Lordship" (Rububiyyah), and I will remember you with "mercy" (the entirety of Surah Al-Fatihah can be a witness to this meaning). [^1]

Of course, each of these matters is a manifestation of the broad meaning of this verse, and the verse encompasses all these meanings and even others: "Remember Me with 'gratitude' so I may remember you with 'increase of blessings'," as we read in verse 7 of Surah Ibrahim: لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ‌.

Undoubtedly, every form of attention to God—as we have said—has an educational effect that shines within the human being in the light of God's remembrance, and his soul and spirit, through this attention, gain the readiness for the descent of new blessings proportionate to the manner of his remembrance.

2- What is the Remembrance of God?

It is certain that the remembrance of God does not mean only verbal remembrance, for the tongue is the translator of the heart. The goal is to pay attention to the Pure Essence of God with all one's heart and soul—the kind of attention that restrains a person from sin and invites him to obey His command.

For this reason, it has been narrated in numerous hadiths from the leaders of Islam that the remembrance of God means practical remembrance. In a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he gave a testament to Ali عليه السلام, and among his instructions was:

"There are three things that this Ummah does not have the strength to perform (completely): equality and sharing with a religious brother in one's wealth, giving people their rights by judging fairly regarding oneself and others, and remembering Allah in every state. It is not [just] saying 'Subhan Allah,' 'Alhamdulillah,' 'La ilaha illa Allah,' and 'Allahu Akbar,' but rather it is that when a forbidden act comes before him, he fears Allah the Almighty regarding it and abandons it." [^1]

In any case, how beautiful it is that Allah, with all His greatness, places the remembrance of His servants alongside His own remembrance—the servants in their limited and small world, and the Great God in that unlimited and infinite world with all those mercies and blessings!

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 4, p. 144 (with minor changes and some additions).

[^1]: Kitab al-Khisal, as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 140.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱسْتَعِينُوا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ وَٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٥٣)
O you who have faith! Take recourse in patience and prayer; indeed Allah is with the patient. (153)
وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ لِمَن يُقْتَلُ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَمْوَٰتٌۢ ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَآءٌ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تَشْعُرُونَ (١٥٤)
Do not call those who were slain in Allah’s way ‘dead.’ No, they are living, but you are not aware. (154)

Circumstances of Revelation

Some commentators have narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding the circumstances of the revelation of the second verse under discussion that it was revealed concerning those slain in the Battle of Badr. They were fourteen individuals: six from the Muhajirun (Emigrants) and eight from the Ansar (Helpers). After the war ended, some people would use expressions such as "so-and-so has died." The above verse was revealed and explicitly forbade them from applying the word "dead" (mayyit) to the martyrs.

Commentary

Martyrs are Alive...

Since the preceding verses spoke of education, purification, remembrance (dhikr), and gratitude (shukr)—and considering the very broad meaning these concepts hold, encompassing most religious instructions—the first verse under discussion brings up the subject of patience and perseverance, without which the aforementioned concepts would never be realized.

First, It says: "O you who have believed, seek help through patience and prayer" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلَاةِ).

With these two forces (steadfastness and turning towards God), go to war against difficulties and harsh events, for victory belongs to you, "Indeed, Allah is with the patient" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ).

Contrary to what some imagine, "patience" (sabr) never means enduring misfortunes, submitting to humiliation, or surrendering to the factors of defeat; rather, patience and forbearance mean steadfastness and resistance against every problem and every incident.

Therefore, many scholars of ethics have mentioned three branches for "patience":

  1. Patience in obedience (resistance against the difficulties that exist in the path of obeying God).
  2. Patience against disobedience (standing firm against the motives of sin and rebellious, overflowing desires).
  3. Patience in affliction (steadfastness against unpleasant events, not losing one's self-control or spirit, and refraining from lamentation and restlessness).

One can find few subjects in the Quran that have been repeated and emphasized as much as "patience." In the Holy Quran, "patience" is mentioned in about seventy instances, more than ten of which specifically pertain to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

The history of great men testifies that one of the important factors—or rather the most important factor—of their victory was steadfastness and forbearance. Individuals who lack this quality succumb very quickly in the face of hardships. It can be said that the role this factor plays in the progress of individuals and societies is matched neither by the availability of resources nor by talent, intelligence, or the like.

For this reason, the Holy Quran emphasizes this subject with the most emphatic expression, where It says: إِنَّمَا يُوَفَّى الصَّابِرُونَ أَجْرَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ: "Indeed, the patient will be given their reward without account" (Az-Zumar, 10).

In another instance, after mentioning "patience against events," we read: إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ: "Indeed, that is of the matters [requiring] determination" (Luqman, 17).

Fundamentally, the characteristic of steadfastness and fortitude is such that other virtues would have no value or worth without it, as patience is the backbone of them all. Thus, we read in Nahj al-Balagha among the "Short Sayings":

"And you must practice patience, for patience in relation to faith is like the head to the body; there is no good in a body that has no head, and there is no good in faith that has no patience." [1] In Islamic narrations, the highest form of patience is considered to be when a person resists and foregoes the pleasure of sin when the means for disobedience and sin are provided.

In the verse under discussion, the first revolutionary Muslims—who were surrounded from all sides by powerful, bloodthirsty, and ruthless enemies—were specifically commanded to seek help from the power of patience and fortitude in the face of various events. The result of this is independence of character, self-reliance, and self-help under the protection of faith in Allah. The history of Islam well demonstrates this reality: that this very fundamental principle was the main foundation of all victories.

Another subject introduced in the above verse as an important pillar alongside patience is "Salah" (prayer). Thus, we read in Islamic hadiths:

"Whenever Ali عليه السلام was distressed by a matter, he would rise for prayer and then recite this verse: اسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلَاةِ..." [1] One should never be surprised by this, for when a human being faces harsh events and exhausting problems and finds his own strength insufficient to confront them, he needs a support that is unlimited and infinite in every respect. Prayer connects him to such a Source, and by relying on Him, he can break the fierce waves of problems with a reassured and calm spirit.

Therefore, the above verse actually recommends two principles: one is reliance on Allah, of which prayer is the manifestation, and the other is the issue of self-help and self-reliance, which is referred to as patience.

Following the issue of patience and steadfastness, the next verse speaks of the eternal life of the martyrs, which has a close connection to their steadfastness and patience. First, It says: "And do not say of those who are killed in the way of Allah that they are dead" (وَلَا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ يُقْتَلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْوَاتٌ).

Then, for further emphasis, It adds: "Rather, they are alive, but you perceive [it] not" (بَلْ أَحْيَاءٌ وَلَٰكِنْ لَا تَشْعُرُونَ).

Fundamentally, in every movement, a group of comfort-seekers and cowards pull themselves aside; not only do they do nothing themselves, but they also try to discourage others.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 82. [^1]: Kitab al-Kafi, as quoted in Al-Mizan, Vol. 1, p. 154.

As soon as an unpleasant incident occurs, they express regret and consider it a reason for the fruitlessness of that uprising, oblivious to the fact that no sacred and precious goal has ever been achieved without offering a sacrifice or sacrifices, and this is one of the laws of this world.

The Holy Quran repeatedly mentions this group and severely rebukes them. There was a group of such people at the dawn of Islam who, whenever one of the Muslims attained the honor of martyrdom on the field of Jihad, would say, "So-and-so died!" and by expressing regret over his death, they would make others anxious.

In response to these poisonous statements, Allah unveils a great reality and explicitly says: You have no right to call those who give their lives in the way of Allah "dead." They are alive—eternally alive—and they benefit from spiritual provisions in the presence of Allah. They speak with one another and are completely satisfied with their fruitful destiny. However, you, who are imprisoned and confined within the four walls of the material world, cannot perceive these realities.

Key Points

1. The Nature of the Eternal Life of Martyrs

There is discussion among commentators regarding how the life of martyrs is. Undoubtedly, the apparent meaning of the verse is that they possess a type of purgatorial (barzakhi) and spiritual life, because their physical bodies have disintegrated. According to Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, that life is with an "imaginal body" (badan-e mithali)—a body free from ordinary matter yet similar to this body—the explanation of which will come under Surah Al-Mu'minun, verse 100: وَمِنْ وَرَائِهِمْ بَرْزَخٌ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ. [1]

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 559, under verse 100 of Surah Al-Mu'minun.

Some commentators have considered this life to be a "hidden life" (hayat-e ghaybi) exclusive to martyrs, stating that we do not have further explanation regarding the nature of this life and how it is utilized.

Others have interpreted "life" here as "guidance" and "death" as "misguidance," saying the meaning of the verse is: do not say whoever is killed is misguided; rather, he is guided. Others consider the eternal life of martyrs to be the survival of their name and their school of thought.

However, considering what we said in the first interpretation, it becomes clear that none of these possibilities are acceptable. There is no necessity to interpret the verse with metaphorical meanings, nor to consider the issue of purgatorial life as exclusive to martyrs.
