Rather, martyrs possess a spiritual and purgatorial life with the distinction that they are in the proximity of the Lord's mercy and are blessed with various types of His bounties.

2. A School of Thought that Prides Itself on Martyrdom

With the depiction of the issue of martyrdom in the above verse and other verses of the Quran, Islam has introduced a very important new factor into the battlefield of truth against falsehood—a factor whose application is greater than any weapon and whose impact is superior to all. It is a factor that can break the most dangerous and terrifying weapons of our age, just as we clearly saw this reality with our own eyes in the history of the Islamic Revolution of our country, Iran, where the love for martyrdom, despite all apparent shortages, became the factor for the victory of the soldiers of Islam against great powers.

If we examine the history of Islam, the eternal epics of Islamic Jihads, and the accounts of the self-sacrifice of those who gave their lives with their whole being for the advancement of this pure religion, we see that one important reason for all those victories was that Islam, in its teachings, had taught them this great lesson: that martyrdom in the way of Allah and in the path of truth and justice does not mean annihilation, destruction, or death; rather, it is happiness, eternal life, and everlasting honor.

Soldiers who have learned such a lesson in this great school of thought are never comparable to ordinary soldiers. An ordinary soldier thinks of preserving his own life, but they fight to preserve the school of thought; like moths, they burn and are sacrificed, and they take pride in it.

3. Purgatorial Life and the Survival of the Soul

Incidentally, from this verse, the subject of the survival of the soul and the purgatorial life of human beings (life after death and before the Resurrection) is clearly proven. It is an explicit answer to those who say the Quran has not alluded to the issue of the survival of the soul and purgatorial life.

For a more detailed explanation regarding this subject, as well as the issue of the eternal life of martyrs and the significant reward and high station of those slain in the way of Allah, please study Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 168 (under verse 169 of Surah Al-Imran).

Ayahs

وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُم بِشَىْءٍ مِّنَ ٱلْخَوْفِ وَٱلْجُوعِ وَنَقْصٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَنفُسِ وَٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٥٥)
We will surely test you with a measure of fear and hunger and a loss of wealth, lives, and fruits; and give good news to the patient (155)
ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَآ أَصَـٰبَتْهُم مُّصِيبَةٌ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّآ إِلَيْهِ رَٰجِعُونَ (١٥٦)
—those who, when an affliction visits them, say, ‘Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him do we indeed return.’ (156)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ صَلَوَٰتٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَرَحْمَةٌ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُهْتَدُونَ (١٥٧)
It is they who receive the blessings of their Lord and [His] mercy, and it is they who are the [rightly] guided. (157)

Commentary

The World is a Scene of Divine Trial

Following the mention of martyrdom in the way of Allah and the eternal life of the martyrs, as well as the subjects of patience and gratitude—each being a facet of Divine trials—this verse addresses the issue of "trial in general" and its various forms.

As a certain and inevitable matter, He states: "And We will surely test you with something of fear and hunger and a loss of wealth and lives and fruits" (وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُمْ بِشَيْءٍ مِنَ الْخَوْفِ وَالْجُوعِ وَنَقْصٍ مِنَ الْأَمْوالِ وَالْأَنْفُسِ وَالثَّمَراتِ).

Since victory in these examinations is impossible except under the shadow of resistance and steadfastness, He says at the end of the verse: "and give good tidings to the patient" (وَبَشِّرِ الصَّابِرِينَ).

It is they who successfully endure these difficult trials, and the tidings of victory belong to them; however, those of weak resolve and lack of endurance emerge from the crucible of these tests blackened with failure.

The next verse introduces the patient, saying: "Who, when disaster strikes them, say, 'Indeed we belong to Allah, and indeed to Him we will return'" (الَّذِينَ إِذا أَصابَتْهُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ قالُوا إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّا إِلَيْهِ راجِعُونَ). Attending to the reality that we all belong to Him teaches us never to be distressed by the loss of blessings, for all these bounties—and indeed we ourselves—belong to Him. One day He grants, and another day He sees fit to take back from us, and both are for our own good.

Furthermore, attending to the reality that we all return to Him announces to us that this is not an eternal abode. The fading of blessings, the lack of bounties, or their abundance are all fleeting. All of these are means for traversing the stages of perfection. Mindfulness of these two fundamental principles has a profound effect on creating a spirit of endurance and patience.

It is obvious that the purpose of reciting the phrase "إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّا إِلَيْهِ راجِعُونَ" is not merely its verbal

utterance, but rather attending to its truth and spirit, within the depths of which lies a world of Monotheism (Tawhid) and faith.

In the final verse under discussion, He recounts the great Divine favors for the patient and the hardworking who have successfully passed these great examinations, saying: "Those are the ones upon whom are blessings from their Lord and mercy" (أُولئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ صَلَواتٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ وَرَحْمَةٌ) [1]. These favors and mercies empower them so that they do not fall into error or deviation on this path full of fear and danger; thus, at the end of the verse, He says: "And it is those who are the [rightly] guided" (وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُهْتَدُونَ).

In this way, He clarifies a summary of the great issue of trial, its ultimate goal, its various forms, the factors of victory, and its results in these few short sentences.

Key Points

1- Why Does Allah Test People?

There is much discussion regarding the issue of Divine trial. The first question that comes to mind is: Is not testing for the purpose of identifying ambiguous or unknown persons or things and reducing one's ignorance? If so,

[1]: The author of Al-Manar says: The meaning of "Salawat" (blessings) here refers to types of honoring, victories, and elevation of status before Allah and His servants, and he narrates from Ibn Abbas that it means the forgiveness of sins (Al-Manar, Vol. 2, p. 40). However, it is clear that "Salawat" has a broad concept that includes both these matters and other Divine mercies and blessings.

why does Allah—whose knowledge encompasses everything and who is aware of the inner and outer secrets of everyone and everything, knowing the unseen of the heavens and the earth with His infinite knowledge—conduct tests? Is something hidden from Him that would be revealed through a test?! The answer to this important question must be sought in the fact that the concept of trial and examination regarding Allah is very different from our own tests.

Our tests are as mentioned above—for greater recognition and the removal of ambiguity and ignorance. However, Divine trial is, in reality, "nurturing and cultivation."

To explain: in the Quran, trial is attributed to Allah in more than twenty instances. This is a general law and a permanent Sunnah (tradition) of the Lord to blossom latent talents (and bring them from potentiality to actuality) and consequently to nurture His servants by testing them. That is, just as steel is forged in a furnace to become stronger—or so-called "tempered"—man is also nurtured in the furnace of difficult events to become resilient.

In reality, Allah's test is similar to the work of an experienced gardener who sows capable seeds in prepared lands. These seeds begin to grow and develop using natural gifts; gradually they struggle with difficulties and battle against events, showing resistance against harsh storms, killing cold, and scorching heat until a beautiful flower branch or a sturdy, fruitful tree emerges that can continue its life and existence against harsh events.

Soldiers are taken to maneuvers and mock battles to become militarily powerful and strong, and they are placed before various difficulties of thirst, hunger, heat, cold, difficult events, and hard obstacles so they become seasoned and tempered.

And this is the secret of Divine trials.

The Holy Quran explicitly states this truth elsewhere, saying: "And that Allah might test what is in your breasts and purify what is in your hearts. And Allah is Knowing of that within the breasts" (وَلِيَبْتَلِيَ اللَّهُ ما فِي صُدُورِكُمْ وَلِيَمُحِّصَ ما فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ) (Al-Imran: 154).

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, has a very meaningful definition regarding the philosophy of Divine examinations, saying:

"Even though He, Glory be to Him, knows them better than they know themselves, He tests them so that the actions by which they deserve reward or punishment may manifest" [1]. That is, man's inner qualities alone cannot be a criterion for reward and punishment unless they manifest within the folds of human actions. Allah tests His servants so that what they have within may be revealed in action, bringing talents from potentiality to actuality, making them deserving of His reward or punishment.

If it were not for Divine trial, these talents would not blossom, and the fruits of deeds would not appear on the branches of the tree of human existence. This is the philosophy of Divine trial in Islamic logic.

2- Allah's Trial is Universal

Since the system of life in the universe is a system of evolution and nurturing, and all living beings traverse the path of perfection—even trees manifest their latent talents through fruit—all people, from the Prophets to others, according to this general law,

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 93.

must be tested and have their talents blossomed.

Although Divine examinations vary—some are difficult, some easy—and naturally their results differ, trial exists for everyone nonetheless. The Holy Quran refers to the universal examination of humans, saying: "Do the people think that they will be left to say, 'We believe' and they will not be tried?" (أَحَسِبَ النَّاسُ أَنْ يُتْرَكُوا أَنْ يَقُولُوا آمَنَّا وَهُمْ لا يُفْتَنُونَ) (Al-Ankabut: 1).

The Quran also recounts examples of the examinations of the Prophets, where it says: "And when his Lord tried Ibrahim" (وَإِذِ ابْتَلى‌ إِبْراهِيمَ رَبُّهُ) (Al-Baqarah: 124).

Elsewhere it is stated: "And when he saw it placed before him, he said, 'This is from the favor of my Lord to test me whether I will be grateful or ungrateful'" (فَلَمَّا رَآهُ مُسْتَقِرًّا عِنْدَهُ قالَ هذا مِنْ فَضْلِ رَبِّي لِيَبْلُوَنِي أَأَشْكُرُ أَمْ أَكْفُرُ). When one of Solomon's followers brought the throne of Bilqis to him from a great distance in less than the blink of an eye, Solomon said this is Allah's grace to test me whether I give thanks or show ingratitude (Al-Naml: 40).

3- Methods of Trial

In the above verse, examples of matters by which man is tested are mentioned, such as: fear, hunger, financial losses, and death... but Allah's means of testing are not limited to these. Rather, other matters are also mentioned in the Quran as means of trial, such as children, Prophets, and Allah's commands; even some dreams may be means of trial. "Evils and goods are also considered Divine trials" (وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ) (Al-Anbiya: 35).

Therefore, the cases enumerated in the verse are not exhaustive, even though they are

vivid and clear examples of Divine trials.

We know that people are divided into two groups regarding Allah's trials: One group passes the examinations, and another group fails.

For example, when the issue of "fear" arises, one group loses heart and, to avoid the slightest harm, they shirk their responsibilities, flee in wars, or adopt a path of compromise, evading Divine duty with excuses such as: "We fear that a misfortune may strike us" (نَخْشى‌ أَنْ تُصِيبَنا دائِرَةٌ) (Surah Al-Ma'idah: 5). But another group, in the face of factors of fear, prepares themselves for any sacrifice with greater faith and trust, as stated in the Quran: "Those to whom the people said, 'Indeed, the people have gathered against you, so fear them.' But it [only] increased them in faith, and they said, 'Sufficient for us is Allah, and [He is] the best Disposer of affairs'" (الَّذِينَ قالَ لَهُمُ النَّاسُ إِنَّ النَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا لَكُمْ فَاخْشَوْهُمْ فَزادَهُمْ إِيماناً وَقالُوا حَسْبُنَا اللَّهُ وَنِعْمَ الْوَكِيلُ) (Al-Imran: 173).

Regarding other difficulties and factors of trial mentioned in the verse, such as hunger and financial or physical losses, not all people are the same. Examples of these trials are mentioned in the text of the Quran, and we will refer to them in appropriate verses.

4- The Secret of Victory in the Trial

Here another question arises: Now that all humans participate in a vast Divine examination, what is the way to succeed in these trials?

The answer to this question is provided by the last part of the verse under discussion and other verses of the Quran:

1- The first and most important step for victory is what is mentioned in the short

and meaningful phrase "and give good tidings to the patient" in the above verse. This phrase explicitly states: The secret of victory on this path is patience and endurance, and for this reason, it gives the tidings of victory only to the patient and steadfast individuals.

2- Attending to the fleeting nature of the events of this world and its hardships and difficulties, and that this world is nothing more than a passage, is considered another factor for victory, which is mentioned in the phrase "Indeed we belong to Allah, and indeed to Him we will return" (إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّا إِلَيْهِ راجِعُونَ). Fundamentally, this phrase—referred to as the "Word of Istirja"—is an essence of the highest lessons of Monotheism, detachment unto Allah, and reliance on His Pure Essence in everything and at all times. If we see the greats of Islam repeating this phrase upon the occurrence of severe calamities, drawing inspiration from the Holy Quran, it was so that the intensity of the calamity would not shake them, and that they might absorb these events within themselves in the light of faith in Allah's ownership and the return of all beings to Him.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says in the interpretation of this phrase: "Our saying 'Indeed we belong to Allah' is an admission of His ownership over us, and our saying 'and indeed to Him we will return' is an admission that we shall perish [from this world]" and our place is elsewhere [1].

3- Seeking help from the power of faith and Divine favors is another important factor. There are those who, whenever they are subject to events, lose their balance and fall into anxiety. But the friends of Allah, because they have a clear program and goal, continue their path confidently and calmly without confusion or wandering. Allah also

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 99.

gives them greater insight so they do not make mistakes in choosing the correct path, as the Quran says: "And those who strive for Us - We will surely guide them to Our ways" (وَالَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا) (Al-Ankabut: 69).

4- Attending to the history of predecessors and examining their stance toward Divine trials is very effective in preparing the human soul for the Lord's examinations. Fundamentally, if a person feels alone in the issues that arise for him, his power of resistance will decrease. However, attending to the reality that these grueling difficulties and harsh Divine trials have existed for all nations and peoples throughout history causes an increase in man's power of endurance. For this reason, the Holy Quran repeatedly refers to the history of those past and the painful events of their lives to comfort the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and strengthen his morale and that of the believers. For example, it says: "And already were messengers ridiculed before you" (وَلَقَدِ اسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ). If they ridicule you, do not worry; the previous Prophets were also subject to the ridicule of the ignorant, but they triumphed over them with the power of endurance (Al-An'am: 10).

Elsewhere it says: "And certainly were messengers denied before you, but they were patient over [the effects of] denial and were harmed until Our victory came to them" (وَلَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ فَصَبَرُوا عَلى‌ ما كُذِّبُوا وَأُوذُوا حَتَّى أَتاهُمْ نَصْرُنا). If they deny you, it is no surprise; the previous Prophets were also denied, and they showed endurance and patience against the denial of opponents and were harmed until finally Our victory and help came to them (Al-An'am: 34).

5- Attending to the reality that all these events occur in the presence of Allah and that He is aware of everything is another factor for endurance. Those who participate in a difficult and grueling competition, as soon as they feel that a group of their friends around the field of competition sees them,

bearing difficulties becomes easy for them, and they rise to battle events with more passion and love. Where the presence of a few spectators has such an effect on the human soul, attending to the reality that Allah sees our struggles in the scenes of trial—what love and passion for continuing this Jihad will it create in us!

The Quran says: When Noah عليه السلام was commanded to build the Ark under the most intense pressure from his people, Allah ordered him: "And construct the Ark before Our eyes" (وَاصْنَعِ الْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا) (Hud: 37). The phrase "before Our eyes" (before the eyes of Our knowledge) gave such heart-strength to Noah عليه السلام that the pressure and ridicule of the enemies did not create the slightest flaw in his powerful will.

The same meaning is narrated from the Master of Martyrs and Mujahideen in the way of Allah, Imam Hussain عليه السلام, who said on the scene of Karbala when some of his loved ones drank the cup of martyrdom in the most tragic manner: "What has befallen me is easy for me because it is before the eye of Allah" [1].

5- Trial Through Blessing and Affliction

Divine examinations are not always through harsh and unpleasant events; rather, sometimes Allah tests His servants with an abundance of blessings and successes, as the Quran says: "And We test you with evil and with good as a trial" (وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً) (Al-Anbiya: 35).

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 45, p. 46.

And elsewhere we read from the words of Solomon عليه السلام: "This is from the favor of my Lord to test me whether I will be grateful or ungrateful" (هذا مِنْ فَضْلِ رَبِّي لِيَبْلُوَنِي أَأَشْكُرُ أَمْ أَكْفُرُ) (Surah Al-Naml: 40). Attending to a few other points here is also necessary:

One is that it is not necessary for all people to be tested with all means; rather, it is possible that the trial of each group is with something specific, for compatibility with the temperaments and individual and social status of people is a condition here.

Another is that it is possible for a person to pass some examinations well while failing others miserably.

Also, it is possible that the trial of one individual is a means of trial for another. For example, Allah tests someone with the calamity of his beloved child; this trial also brings others into the field of examination—whether they come forward in sympathy and strive to alleviate the pain of the bereaved person or not.

The final point is: as we indicated, Divine examinations have a general and universal aspect, and even the Prophets are not exempt from them; rather, their trial, given the weight of their responsibility, is degrees harder than the trials of others.

The verses of various Surahs of the Quran indicate this reality—that each of the Prophets, in his own turn, was placed in the vortex of intense trials. Some of them even had a long period of various trials before reaching the station of Messengership to become fully seasoned and find complete readiness for leading and guiding the creation.

Among the followers of the school of the Prophets, brilliant examples of patience and endurance in the scene of trial are seen, each of which can be a model and an exemplar.

There was a nomadic Muslim woman named "Umm Aqil" who had

two guests arrive. Her son was in the desert with the camels. At that moment, she was informed that a raging camel had thrown her son into a well and he had passed away. The woman of faith said to the one who brought the news of her son's death: "Dismount from your mount and help in entertaining the guests." She had a sheep ready, gave it to him to slaughter, and finally, the food was prepared and placed before the guests. They ate and were amazed at the patience and endurance of this woman. One of those present says: When we finished eating, the woman of faith came to us and said: "Is there anyone among you who is well-acquainted with the Quran?" One of those present said: "Yes, I am." She said: "Recite verses from the Quran so that they may be a source of consolation for me in the face of my son's death." He says:

I recited these verses for her: "And give good tidings to the patient, who, when disaster strikes them, say, 'Indeed we belong to Allah, and indeed to Him we will return.' Those are the ones upon whom are blessings from their Lord and mercy. And it is those who are the [rightly] guided."

She bid farewell, then stood facing the Qibla and performed a few units of prayer, then said: "O Allah! I have done what You commanded me, so fulfill for me what You promised me" (اللهم انى فعلت ما امرتنى، فانجز لى ما وعدتنى). "O Allah! I did what You ordered and did not let go of the thread of patience; You also bestow upon me what You promised of mercy and blessings."

Then she added: "If anyone were to remain for anyone in this world..." One of those present says: I thought she wanted to say my son would remain for me, but I saw her continue: "the Prophet of Islam, Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, would have remained for his Ummah!" [1].

[1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 7 (under the root Sabr).

Ayah

۞ إِنَّ ٱلصَّفَا وَٱلْمَرْوَةَ مِن شَعَآئِرِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَمَنْ حَجَّ ٱلْبَيْتَ أَوِ ٱعْتَمَرَ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِ أَن يَطَّوَّفَ بِهِمَا ۚ وَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَاكِرٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٥٨)
Indeed Safa and Marwah are among Allah’s sacraments. So whoever makes hajj to the House, or performs the ‘umrah, there is no sin upon him to circuit between them. Should anyone do good of his own accord, then Allah is indeed appreciative, all-knowing. (158)

Occasion of Revelation

Before the advent of Islam, as well as concurrent with it, the polytheists and idolaters would come to Mecca to perform the Hajj rituals. They performed the Hajj ceremonies—the origin of which belonged to Ibrahim عليه السلام but which they had mixed with a great deal of superstition and polytheism—including the standing at Arafat, sacrifice, circumambulation (Tawaf), and the Sa'y between Safa and Marwah. Of course, these acts were carried out in a specific manner.

Through the reform and purification it applied to this program, Islam endorsed the essence of this great worship and its correct rituals, purified of polytheism, while drawing a line of cancellation through the superstitions.

Among the acts and rituals performed was the "Sa'y," meaning the movement between the two famous hills of Safa and Marwah.

In many narrations transmitted through both Shi'a and Sunni chains, we read that during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), the polytheists had installed an idol atop Mount Safa named "Isaf" and another idol on Mount Marwah named "Na'ilah." During the Sa'y, they would ascend these two hills and stroke those two idols with their hands for blessings. Because of this, the Muslims felt a sense of aversion toward performing the Sa'y between Safa and Marwah, thinking that under these conditions, the Sa'y of Safa and Marwah was not a proper act.

The above verse was revealed and announced to them that Safa and Marwah are among the symbols (Sha'a'ir) of Allah; if ignorant people have defiled them, it is no reason for Muslims to abandon the obligatory duty of Sa'y.

Furthermore, there is discussion regarding when this noble verse was revealed. According to some narrations, it was during the Umrat al-Qada (in the seventh year of the Hijra). One of the conditions the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله made with the polytheists during this journey was that they remove those two idols from Safa and Marwah. They complied with this condition, but later returned them to their places! This very act caused some Muslims to refrain from the Sa'y of Safa and Marwah, which the noble verse then prohibited them from doing.

Some have suggested the possibility that the verse was revealed during the Hujjat al-Wada' (the Prophet's final Hajj in the tenth year of the Hijra). If we accept this possibility, certainly at that time, not only were there no idols on Safa and Marwah, but there was no trace of idols in the entire environment of Mecca. Therefore, we must accept that the Muslims' discomfort with the Sa'y between Safa and Marwah was due to that historical precedent and their past state when the idols "Isaf and Na'ilah" were placed upon them.

Commentary

The Actions of the Ignorant Should Not Prevent Positive Work

In view of the specific psychological conditions mentioned in the occasion of revelation, this verse first informs the Muslims that: "Indeed, Safa and Marwah are among the symbols of Allah" (إِنَّ الصَّفا وَ الْمَرْوَةَ مِنْ شَعائِرِ اللَّهِ).

From this premise, It draws the following conclusion: "So whoever performs Hajj to the House or performs Umrah, there is no sin upon him to walk between them" (فَمَنْ حَجَّ الْبَيْتَ أَوِ اعْتَمَرَ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِ أَنْ يَطَّوَّفَ بِهِما).

The reckless actions of the polytheists, who had defiled these Divine symbols with idols, must never diminish the importance of these two holy places.

At the end of the verse, He says: "And whoever volunteers good as an act of obedience to Allah, then indeed Allah is Appreciative and All-Knowing" (وَ مَنْ تَطَوَّعَ خَيْراً فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ شاكِرٌ عَلِيمٌ).

In return for obedience and the performance of good deeds, He thanks His servants' actions by providing a good reward, and He is well aware of their intentions; He knows who is fond of idols and who loathes them.

Key Points

1- Safa and Marwah

"Safa" and "Marwah" are the names of two small hills in Mecca which today, due to the expansion of Masjid al-Haram, are located on the eastern side of the mosque, in the direction where the Black Stone (Hajar al-Aswad) and the Station of Ibrahim (Maqam Ibrahim) are situated.

These two small hills are located approximately 420 meters apart, facing each other. Today, this distance has taken the form of a massive roofed hall where pilgrims perform the Sa'y under its ceiling. The height of Mount Safa is fifteen meters and Marwah is eight meters.

Although the two words Safa and Marwah are currently the names of these two hills (and are proper nouns), linguistically, Safa means a hard, smooth stone that is not mixed with soil or sand, and Marwah means a hard, rough stone.

"Sha'a'ir" is the plural of "Sha'irah," meaning a sign or symbol. "Sha'a'ir Allah" are the signs that remind a person of Allah and renew the memory of a sacred event in one's mind.

"I'tamara" is derived from the root "Umrah," which originally means the additional parts added to a building that lead to its completion. However, in Sharia terminology, it refers to specific acts that are added to the Hajj rituals (and are sometimes performed separately under the name "Umrah Mufradah"). Of course, Umrah resembles Hajj in many aspects, though the differences are also significant.

2- Some Secrets of the Sa'y of Safa and Marwah

It is true that reading and hearing the life history of great men draws a person toward their path, but there is a more correct and profound way: observing the scenes where the men of God rose to struggle and seeing the centers where the original events took place.

This is, in fact, considered living and vibrant history, unlike history books which are silent and lifeless. In such centers, by removing the temporal distances and by being present in the original location, a person feels himself in the heart of the event, as if seeing everything with his own eyes.

The educational effect of this can never be compared to the educational effects of a lecture or studying books and the like. Here, it is a matter of feeling, not just perception; of affirmation, not just conception; and of objectivity, not just subjectivity.

On the other hand, we know that among the great prophets, few have faced various scenes of struggle and difficult trials like Ibrahim عليه السلام, to the extent that the Quran says regarding him: "Indeed, this was the manifest trial" (As-Saffat: 106) (إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ الْبَلاءُ الْمُبِينُ).

It was these very strivings, struggles, and heavy, difficult trials that nurtured Ibrahim such that the crown of honor of "Imamate" was placed upon his head.

The Hajj rituals, in reality, embody a complete cycle of the scenes of Ibrahim's struggles and the stations of monotheism, servitude, sacrifice, and sincerity in one's memory.

If Muslims, while performing these rituals, are aware of their spirit and secrets and reflect on their various "symbolic" aspects, it is a great educational class and a complete course in theology, prophetology, and anthropology.

With this introduction, we return to the story of Ibrahim and the historical aspect of Safa and Marwah: Although Ibrahim عليه السلام had reached old age, he had no children. He asked Allah for offspring. In that same old age, a son was granted to him from his handmaid, Hagar (Hajar), whom he named "Ishmael" (Isma'il).

His first wife, Sarah, could not bear for Ibrahim to have a child from someone other than her. Allah commanded Ibrahim to take the mother and child to Mecca—which at that time was a waterless and barren desert—and settle them there.

Ibrahim obeyed Allah's command and took them to the land of Mecca, which in those days was a dry, waterless, and barren land where not even a bird would fly. As he prepared to return alone, his wife began to cry: what should a woman and a nursing infant do in this desert without water or vegetation?

Her burning tears, mixed with the tears of the nursing child, shook Ibrahim's heart. He raised his hands in prayer and said: "O Allah! I am leaving my wife and child alone in this scorching desert without water or vegetation for the sake of Your command, so that Your name may be exalted and Your House inhabited." He said this and bid them farewell amidst deep sorrow and love.

It did not take long before the mother's store of food and water was exhausted, and the milk in her breast dried up. The restlessness of the nursing child and his pleading looks made the mother so anxious that she forgot her own thirst and rose to strive and search for water. First, she came to the side of Mount "Safa," but saw no sign of water there. The shimmer of a mirage from the direction of Mount "Marwah" caught her eye, and thinking it was water, she hurried toward it, but there was no sign of water there either. From there, she saw the same shimmer on Mount "Safa" and returned toward it. This effort and struggle to sustain life and fight against death was repeated seven times. In the final moments, when the nursing infant was perhaps passing the last minutes of his life, from near his feet—to her utter amazement—the spring of Zamzam began to gush! The mother and child drank from it and were saved from certain death.

Since water is the symbol of life, birds came toward the spring from every direction, and caravans, observing the flight of the birds, changed their route toward that point. Eventually, from the blessing of the sacrifice of a seemingly small family, a great and magnificent center was established.

Today, next to the House of Allah, a sanctuary has been opened for Hagar and her son Ishmael (named Hijr Isma'il), where every year hundreds of thousands of people from around the world come and are obliged, during the circumambulation of the House of Allah, to include that sanctuary—which is the burial place of that woman and child—as part of the Kaaba.

The hills of Safa and Marwah teach us a lesson: that for the revival of the name of Truth and to attain the greatness of His religion, everyone, even a nursing infant, must stand firm until the end of their life.

The Sa'y of Safa and Marwah teaches us that in despairs, there are many hopes; Hagar, the mother of Ishmael, strived in a place where no water was visible, and Allah quenched her thirst from a way she did not imagine.

The Sa'y of Safa and Marwah tells us: there was a time when idols were installed atop them, but today, as a result of the persistent activities of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, the cry of "There is no god but Allah" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ) echoes on its slopes day and night.

Mount Safa has the right to pride itself and say: I was the first base for the propagation of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله; when the city of Mecca was drowned in the darkness of polytheism, the sun of guidance rose from me. You who perform the Sa'y of Safa and Marwah today, remember that if today thousands of people have answered the Prophet's call beside this mountain, there was a time when the Prophet invited people to Allah atop this mountain and no one answered him. You too, take a step in the way of Truth, and if you find no response from those you hope will welcome it, do not despair and continue your work just the same.

The Sa'y of Safa and Marwah tells us: appreciate this religion and center of monotheism; individuals brought themselves to the brink of death to preserve this center of monotheism for you today.

For this reason, Allah has made it obligatory for every pilgrim to His House, in a specific dress and state, devoid of any privilege or distinction, to traverse between these two hills seven times to renew those memories.

Those who, due to pride and arrogance, were not willing to even walk in public passages and for whom it was impossible to walk quickly in the streets, must there—in obedience to Allah's command—sometimes walk slowly and at other times "Harwalah" (jog) with speed. According to numerous narrations, this is a place whose instructions are meant to awaken the arrogant!

In any case, after stating that Safa and Marwah are two great signs and centers of servitude for the people and are among the Divine symbols, He adds that whoever performs Hajj to the House or performs Umrah, there is no blame upon him to circumambulate (perform Tawaf) between these two hills. The meaning of the word Tawaf here is the Sa'y, and this does not contradict the linguistic meaning of Tawaf, because any movement where a person returns to the starting point at the end is called Tawaf, whether the movement is circular or not.

3- Answer to a Question

A question arises here: from the perspective of Islamic jurisprudence, the Sa'y between the two hills of Safa and Marwah is obligatory, whether in the acts of Hajj or Umrah. However, the apparent meaning of the phrase "there is no sin" (فَلا جُناحَ) is that the Sa'y between Safa and Marwah is permissible, but it does not indicate obligation.

We find the answer to this question clearly from the narrations regarding the occasion of revelation, as the Muslims thought that given the history of these two hills—having once been the site of the idols "Isaf and Na'ilah" which the disbelievers would stroke during their Sa'y—it was no longer appropriate for Muslims to perform Sa'y between them.

This verse tells them there is no harm in you performing Sa'y because these two hills are among the symbols of Allah. To put it more clearly, the expression "no sin" [^1] is used to remove that aversion.

Furthermore, in the Holy Quran, other obligatory commands are expressed with this phrase and its like. For example, regarding the prayer of a traveler, we read: "And when you travel throughout the land, there is no sin upon you if you shorten the prayer" (An-Nisa: 101) (وَ إِذا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ أَنْ تَقْصُرُوا مِنَ الصَّلاةِ), even though we know that shortening the prayer (Qasr) is obligatory for the traveler, not merely permissible. Generally, the phrase "no sin" is used in cases where the listener's mental background regarding that thing is mixed with a sense of concern or negativity.

Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام also points to this same method in a hadith narrated from him in the book Man La Yahduruhu al-Faqih.

4- What is "Tatawwu'"?

"Tatawwu'" linguistically means accepting obedience and following a command, and in the convention of jurists, it usually refers to recommended (Mustahabb) acts. For this reason, most commentators have considered this sentence to refer to performing a recommended Hajj or Umrah, or a recommended Tawaf, or any type of recommended good deed. That is, whoever performs a good deed and obeys Allah's command regarding it, Allah is aware of his work and will provide the necessary reward for it.

However, it is possible that this sentence is a completion and emphasis of the preceding sentences, and the meaning of "Tatawwu'" is accepting obedience in that which is difficult for a person.

Therefore, the meaning of the sentence becomes: those who perform the Sa'y of Safa and Marwah with all the hardship it entails, and contrary to the inner inclination stemming from the practices of the pre-Islamic Arabs, complete their Hajj with it, Allah will give them the necessary reward.

5- Allah's Appreciation

Additionally, it should be noted that the expression "Appreciative" (Shakir) in relation to the Lord is a beautiful expression that reflects Allah's ultimate respect for the good deeds of humans. When He is appreciative of the deeds of His servants, their duty toward one another and toward Allah is clear.

[^1]: "Junah" (sin/blame) originally means leaning to one side, and since sin deviates and leans a person away from the truth, it is called Junah.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْتُمُونَ مَآ أَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَٱلْهُدَىٰ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا بَيَّنَّـٰهُ لِلنَّاسِ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَلْعَنُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَيَلْعَنُهُمُ ٱللَّـٰعِنُونَ (١٥٩)
Indeed those who conceal what We have sent down of manifest proofs and guidance, after We have clarified it in the Book for mankind—they shall be cursed by Allah and cursed by the cursers, (159)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ وَبَيَّنُوا۟ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَأَنَا ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٦٠)
except such as repent, make amends, and clarify—those I shall pardon, and I am the All-clement, the All-merciful. (160)

Occasion of Revelation

Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti narrates from Ibn Abbas in Asbab al-Nuzul: A group of Muslims, including Mu'adh ibn Jabal, Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, and Kharijah ibn Zayd, asked Jewish scholars regarding certain matters in the Torah (related to the appearance of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله). However, they concealed the truth and refused to provide an explanation. Consequently, the above verse was revealed concerning them (warning them of the responsibility of concealing the truth). [1]

Commentary

Concealing the Truth is Forbidden!

Although the address in this verse, according to the occasion of revelation, is directed at the Jewish scholars, this meaning by no means limits the scope of the verse, which expresses a general and universal ruling regarding those who conceal the truth.

The noble verse rebukes these individuals in the severest tone, stating: "Indeed, those who conceal what We have sent down of clear proofs and guidance, after We made it clear for the people in the Scripture—those are cursed by Allah and cursed by [all] cursers" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْتُمُونَ ما أَنْزَلْنا مِنَ الْبَيِّناتِ وَ الْهُدى‌ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما بَيَّنَّاهُ لِلنَّاسِ فِي الْكِتابِ أُولئِكَ يَلْعَنُهُمُ اللَّهُ وَ يَلْعَنُهُمُ اللَّاعِنُونَ‌).

From this verse, it is clearly understood that Allah, all His servants, and His angels detest this act. In other words, "concealing the truth" is an act that provokes the wrath of all supporters of the truth. What treachery could be greater than scholars concealing Allah's verses—which are His trusts—for the sake of their personal interests, thereby leading people into misguidance?

The phrase "after We made it clear for the people in the Scripture" indicates that such individuals, through this act, essentially squander the efforts of the prophets and the sacrifices of men of God in spreading the signs of the Lord. This is a grave and inexcusable sin.

[1]: Lubab al-Nuqul fi Asbab al-Nuzul, page 22.

Additionally, it should be noted that the word "yal'an" (curses), which is mentioned twice in the verse for emphasis, is a "present/future tense verb" (fi'l mudari'). As we know, the present tense verb denotes continuity. Therefore, the meaning of the verse is: the curse and condemnation of Allah and all cursers are directed forever and continuously toward those who conceal the truths. This is the severest punishment that can be designated for a human being.

"Clear proofs" (bayyinat) and "guidance" (huda) have a broad meaning that encompasses all means of guidance and clear evidences that serve as a source of awareness, awakening, and salvation for the people.

Since the Quran, as a book of guidance, never closes the window of hope or the path of return to the people and does not make them despair of Allah's mercy—no matter how polluted with sin they may be—the next verse explains the way of salvation and compensation for this great sin as follows:

"Except for those who repent and return to Allah and set out to compensate and reform their deeds, and manifest the truths they had hidden to the people; those I will forgive, and I will renew My mercy which I had cut off from them, for I am the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا وَ أَصْلَحُوا وَ بَيَّنُوا فَأُولئِكَ أَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ أَنَا التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ‌).

The phrase "And I am the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful," especially considering it follows the phrase "those I will forgive (accept their repentance)," indicates the ultimate love and perfect kindness of the Lord toward those who repent. It says: if they return, I too shall return; if they return to obedience and servitude and disclose the truth, I too shall return to mercy and grant them again the blessings I had withheld.

Interestingly, He does not say "you repent so that I may accept your repentance," but rather says: "you return, and I too shall return." The difference between these two expressions is clear.

Furthermore, each word in the phrase "And I am the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful" radiates such grace that it is beyond measure.

To explain: the expression "Ana" (I), which is the first-person singular pronoun, is used in cases where the speaker is expressing His direct relationship with the listener. Especially if a great person says, "I myself will do this for you," it is very different from saying, "We shall do so." The grace and love hidden in the first expression are hidden from no one.

The word "Tawwab" is also an intensive form (sighah mubalaghah) and means one who returns [to mercy] very much. This expression breathes such a spirit of hope into man that it completely clears the veils of despair and hopelessness from the sky of his soul, particularly as it is accompanied by the word "Rahim," which refers to the special mercy of the Lord.

Key Points

1- The Evils of Concealing the Truth

A subject that has long caused widespread violation of rights in human societies, and whose deadly effects continue to this day, is the issue of concealing the truth. Although the above verses were revealed regarding a specific event, as we have said, their attacks are undoubtedly directed at all who have a share in this act.

The threat and condemnation mentioned in the verse under discussion regarding those who conceal the truth is unique in the Quran. Why should it not be so? Is it not true that this ugly act can keep nations and generations in misguidance? Just as manifesting the truth can be the source of salvation for nations.

Man is naturally a seeker of truth, and those who conceal the truth are, in reality, hindering human society from its natural evolutionary path.

If, at the time of the emergence of Islam and thereafter, the scholars of the Jews and Christians had fully disclosed the glad tidings of the Two Testaments and placed what they knew in this regard at the disposal of other people, it would have been possible for all three nations to gather under one banner in a short time and enjoy the blessings of this unity.

Concealing the truth is certainly not limited to concealing the verses of Allah and the signs of prophethood; rather, the hiding of anything that can lead people to a reality is included in the broad concept of this word.

Even silence in a place where one must speak and disclose can be an instance of concealing the truth. This occurs when people have a dire need to understand a reality, and knowledgeable scholars can fulfill this urgent need by stating the truth.

In other words, disclosing truths in matters that people are afflicted with is not conditional upon being asked. What the author of Tafsir al-Manar quoted from some under the verse—that concealment is specific to where a question is asked—does not seem correct.

Especially since the Quran does not only speak of the issue of concealment but also considers the explanation and clarification of truths to be necessary. This mistake is perhaps what caused a group of scholars to remain silent and refrain from recounting truths under the pretext that no one asked them, while the Holy Quran says: "And [remember] when Allah took a covenant from those who were given the Scripture, [saying], 'You must make it clear to the people and not conceal it'" (Al-Imran: 187).

It is also noteworthy that sometimes occupying Allah's creation with minor and secondary issues that cause them to forget the main and vital issues is also a type of concealing the truth. Even if the term "concealing the truth" does not technically include it, the criteria and philosophy of the prohibition of concealing the truth certainly exist within it.

2- Concealing the Truth in Islamic Hadiths

In Islamic hadiths as well, the severest attacks are directed at scholars who conceal truths. Among them, the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله says: "Whoever is asked about knowledge that he knows and conceals it, he will be bridled on the Day of Resurrection with a bridle of fire!" [1] We repeat again that sometimes the very state of need and the people's affliction with an issue takes the place of their question, and disclosure becomes obligatory.

In another hadith, we read that Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام was asked: "Who is the worst of Allah's creation after Iblis and Pharaoh...?" The Imam replied: "They are the corrupt scholars who manifest falsehoods and conceal truths, and it is about them that Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, said: 'Those are cursed by Allah and cursed by [all] cursers'!" [2]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed. [2]: Ihtijaj al-Tabarsi (as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 3, p. 139).

3- What is a Curse (La'n)?

"La'n" (curse) originally means expulsion and distancing that is mixed with anger and wrath. Therefore, a curse from Allah's side means distancing someone from His mercy and from all the gifts and blessings that reach the servants from His side. The fact that some prominent linguists have said that a curse in the Hereafter means punishment and retribution, and in this world means the deprivation of success (tawfiq), is actually an explanation of instances (masadiq), not that the concept of a curse is limited to these two subjects.

The term "al-La'inun" (the cursers) has a broad meaning that includes not only angels and believers but also every being that speaks through its state (lisan al-hal) or words (lisan al-qal) is included in this broad concept. Especially since we read in some narrations that (for example) the good prayers and seeking of forgiveness from all beings in the earth and heaven, even the fish in the sea, include those who seek knowledge (و انه يستغفر لطالب العلم من فى السماء و من فى الارض حتى الحوت فى البحر) [1]. Where they seek forgiveness for the seekers of knowledge, they will curse those who conceal knowledge!

4- The word "Tawwab," which is an intensive form, also points to the reality that if satanic whispers deceive a person and he breaks his repentance, the doors of repentance will still not be closed to him. He must repent again, return to Allah, and disclose the truth, for Allah is the Oft-Returning, and one must never despair of His pardon and mercy.

[1]: Usul al-Kafi, vol. 1, Chapter on the Reward of the Scholar and the Student, Hadith 1.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ (١٦١)
Indeed those who turn faithless and die while they are faithless—it is they on whom shall be the curse of Allah, the angels and all mankind. (161)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ لَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ (١٦٢)
They will remain in it [forever] and their punishment shall not be lightened, nor will they be granted any respite. (162)
وَإِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٦٣)
Your god is the One God; there is no god except Him, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. (163)

Commentary

Those Who Die as Disbelievers

In the previous verses, we saw the consequences of concealing the truth. The verses under discussion, in completing that topic, refer to the disbelievers who persist in their stubbornness, concealment, disbelief, and denial of the truth until the moment of death. First, it says:

"Indeed, those who disbelieve and die while they are disbelievers, upon them is the curse of Allah and the angels and all of mankind" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ ماتُوا وَ هُمْ كُفَّارٌ أُولئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةُ اللَّهِ وَ الْمَلائِكَةِ وَ النَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ‌).

This group, like those who conceal the truth, is subject to the curse of Allah, the angels, and the people, with the difference that since they persisted in disbelief until the end of their lives, naturally no path of return remains for them.

Then it adds: "They will abide eternally in this [curse of Allah and the curse of the angels and mankind], without the punishment of Allah being lightened for them, nor will they be given respite or delay" (خالِدِينَ فِيها لا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ الْعَذابُ وَ لا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ‌).

And since the principle of Monotheism (Tawhid) puts an end to all these miseries, in the final verse under discussion, it says: "And your God is one God" (وَ إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ). Again, for further emphasis, it adds: "There is no deity except Him," and no one other than Him is worthy of worship (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ).

In the final phrase, as an explanation, proof, and cause, He says: "He is the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful" (الرَّحْمنُ الرَّحِيمُ‌).

Indeed, the One whose general mercy has encompassed everyone on one hand, and who has established a special mercy for the believers on the other—yes, He is worthy of servitude, not those who are entirely in need and destitute.

Key Points

1- Numerous verses of the Quran clarify this point: that whoever departs from this world in a state of disbelief and enmity toward the truth, there is no way of salvation for them. And it must be so, because considering that the happiness and misery of the next world are the direct result of the provisions we take with us from this world, this reality becomes clear.

For one who has burnt their wings and feathers with the fire of disbelief and enmity toward the truth will certainly have no power to fly in that world, and their fall into the "depths of Hell" is certain. And since the other world is not a place for acquiring means, they will remain in such a state forever.

This matter is exactly like a person who, due to lust and whims, loses their eyes knowingly and intentionally and is forced to remain blind until the end of their life.

It is obvious that this fate is specific to those disbelievers who tread the path of disbelief and enmity toward the truth knowingly and intentionally (for more explanation regarding the issue of eternity [Khulud], please study Volume 9, page 239, under verses 107 and 108 of Surah Hud).

2- This verse expresses the Oneness of Allah in a way that negates every kind of deviation and polytheism (Shirk). From time to time, we encounter beings that possess unique attributes and are, so to speak, "one of a kind." However, it goes without saying that all of them may be unique and one of a kind in one or several specific attributes, but Allah is One in His Essence, One in His Attributes, and One in His Actions. The Oneness of God is rationally incapable of plurality; He is a Oneness that is Pre-eternal and Everlasting—a Oneness that events do not affect. His Oneness is both in the mind and outside the mind; in short, He is One even in His Oneness!

3- Is the curse of Allah not sufficient?

  • In the above verse, in addition to the curse of Allah, the curse of all cursers is also directed at those who conceal the truth. Here, the question arises: Is the curse of Allah not sufficient on its own?

The answer to this question is clear, for this is actually a type of emphasis and an expression of hatred and disgust by all the inhabitants of the worlds toward those who commit such a great sin.

And if it is asked why "mankind" (an-Nas) is mentioned generally, while at least those who are partners in this crime do not curse these criminals?

We say: even they are disgusted by the nature of this act itself; for this reason, if someone were to conceal the truth regarding themselves, they would certainly become upset and would send curses and imprecations upon them! However, where their own interests are involved, they exceptionally turn a blind eye.

Ayah

إِنَّ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلَّتِى تَجْرِى فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِمَا يَنفَعُ ٱلنَّاسَ وَمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مِن مَّآءٍ فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا وَبَثَّ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ دَآبَّةٍ وَتَصْرِيفِ ٱلرِّيَـٰحِ وَٱلسَّحَابِ ٱلْمُسَخَّرِ بَيْنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (١٦٤)
Indeed in the creation of the heavens and the earth, and the alternation of night and day, and the ships that sail at sea with profit to men, and the water that Allah sends down from the sky—with which He revives the earth after its death, and scatters therein every kind of animal—and the changing of the winds, and the clouds disposed between the sky and the earth, there are surely signs for a people who exercise their reason. (164)

Commentary

Manifestations of His Pure Essence Across the Realm of Existence

Since the final verse of the previous discussion spoke of the Oneness of the Lord, the verse under discussion serves as a proof for that very issue—the proof of God's existence, His Tawhid (Monotheism), and the uniqueness of His Pure Essence.

As a preliminary point, it must be noted that everywhere, "order and cohesion" are evidence of the existence of knowledge and wisdom, and everywhere, "harmony" is evidence of unity and oneness.

Based on this principle, which we have explained in books of theology, when we encounter manifestations of order in the universe on one hand, and the harmony and unified action of these organized systems on the other, we become aware of a Source of unique Knowledge and Power from whom all these echoes originate.

For instance, when we examine each of the seven layers of the eye with its specific and delicate structure, we know it is impossible for mindless, deaf, and blind nature to be the source of such an exquisite work. Then, when we consider the cooperation and harmony of these seven layers with one another, the harmony of the entire eye with the whole human body, the harmony of one human with other humans, and the harmony of the entire human society with the collective system of existence, we realize that all of these spring from a single source and are all effects of the power of one Pure Essence.

Does not a beautiful, subtle, and profound poem guide us toward the overflowing taste and talent of the poet?

And is not the perfect harmony between the various stanzas of poetry in a Diwan (collection) proof that they all emanated from the talent of a single capable poet?! With this brief and concise introduction in mind, we return to the commentary of the verse:

In this verse, six sections of the manifestations of order in the universe are mentioned, each of which is a sign and an indication of that Great Source:

1- "In the creation of the heavens and the earth..." (إِنَّ فِي خَلْقِ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ ...).

Indeed, the creation of these magnificent heavens and all these celestial spheres—meaning millions upon millions of brilliant suns and thousands upon thousands of fixed and planetary stars that speak to us with their meaningful twinkling on a dark, starry night, or reveal themselves to us behind massive telescopes—with that precise and wondrous system that links them all together like the rings of a chain; and likewise, the creation of the earth with various manifestations of life appearing in highly diverse forms and in the garb of hundreds of thousands of species of plants and animals—all are signs of His Pure Essence and brilliant mirrors of His power, knowledge, and oneness.

The wonder is that the further human science and knowledge progress, the greater the majesty and vastness of this universe appear in man's eyes, and it is unknown how long this scientific expansion will continue!

Today, scientists tell us: there are thousands upon thousands of galaxies in the upper world, of which our solar system is but a part of one. In our galaxy alone, there are hundreds of millions of suns and brilliant stars, and according to scientific calculations, among them are millions of inhabited planets with billions of living beings! Oh, what majesty! What power!

2- "And [in] the alternation of night and day..." (وَ اخْتِلافِ اللَّيْلِ وَ النَّهارِ ...).

Indeed, this transformation of night and day, and this coming and going of light and darkness with that specific and gradual order—where one is constantly diminished and added to the other, and through which the four seasons are created, and trees, plants, and living beings traverse their evolutionary stages step by step in the light of these gradual changes—these are another sign of His Essence and His exalted attributes.

If this gradual change did not exist, or if these changes occurred with chaos, or if it were always day or always night, life would be completely wiped from the face of the earth; and if it did exist by some hypothesis, it would be in constant turmoil and disorder. [^1]

[^1]: "Ikhtilaf" (alternation) may mean "coming and going," as it is derived from the root "khalf" and "khilaf" meaning to succeed one another. It may also mean the difference and the increase or decrease in the duration of night and day, or both. In any case, this specific order, which has highly calculated effects, cannot be accidental and without the intervention of an All-Knowing, All-Powerful Essence; hence, in numerous verses of the Quran, it is counted among the proofs of the Pure Essence of Allah.

3- "And the ships that sail through the sea for the benefit of mankind..." (وَ الْفُلْكِ الَّتِي تَجْرِي فِي الْبَحْرِ بِما يَنْفَعُ النَّاسَ ...).

Indeed, man traverses the stage of the oceans by means of large and small ships, and thereby travels to various points of the earth to fulfill his purposes. This movement, especially with sailing ships, is the result of several systems:

First, the regular winds that blow across the surface of the oceans (whether the global winds that move continuously from the North and South Poles toward the Equator and from the Equator toward the North and South Poles, known as "Alizé" [Trade Winds] and "Contre-Alizé," or regional winds that move according to specific schedules and allow ships to utilize this abundant and free natural force to move toward their destination). Also, the natural property of wood or the specific pressure exerted by water on objects that makes them float on the surface, as well as the immutable property of the earth's two magnetic poles that aligns compass needles, or the system of the stars in the sky that shows man the way to his destination. Indeed, until all these systems join hands, the use of ships with their immense benefits is not possible [^1], and consequently, these too are a sign of His Pure Essence.

The wonder is that today, not only has the emergence of motorized ships not diminished the greatness of this concept, but it has increased it manifold; for the most important means of human transport are still giant ships that are sometimes as large as a city, containing squares, recreation centers, playgrounds, and even markets, or having a massive airfield on their deck for the takeoff and landing of many aircraft.

[^1]: The word "Fulk" means ship, and its singular and plural forms are the same.

4- "And the water which Allah sends down from the sky, giving life thereby to the earth after its death and spreading every [kind of] moving creature therein..." (وَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ مِنَ السَّماءِ مِنْ ماءٍ فَأَحْيا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها وَ بَثَّ فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ دَابَّةٍ ...).

Indeed, the life-giving seeds of rain and the fresh, blessed drops of this naturally purified water spread life wherever they fall, bringing movement, blessing, prosperity, and bounty with them. This water, which falls according to a specific system, and all those creatures and moving beings that receive life from this inanimate liquid, are all messengers of His power and majesty.

5- "And the directing and regular blowing of the winds..." (وَ تَصْرِيفِ الرِّياحِ ...).

Which do not only blow upon the seas to move ships, but have made the surface of the lands, mountains, valleys, and plains their playground. Sometimes they scatter male pollen onto the female parts of plants, aiding in their pollination and fertilization, gifting us fruits and spreading various seeds.

At times, by moving the waves of the oceans, they constantly mix them so that a suitable environment is provided for the life of marine creatures.

And sometimes, by transferring heat from tropical regions to cold regions, and transferring cold from frigid regions to warm regions, they help moderate the temperature of the earth.

And at times, by displacing the toxic and oxygen-depleted air of cities to deserts and forests, they provide the means of purification and ventilation for mankind.

Indeed, the blowing of the winds with all these benefits and blessings is another sign of His infinite wisdom and grace.

6- "And the clouds controlled between the heaven and the earth..." (وَ السَّحابِ الْمُسَخَّرِ بَيْنَ السَّماءِ وَ الْأَرْضِ ...).

These dense clouds that circulate above our heads, holding billions of tons of water suspended between the earth and the sky contrary to the law of gravity, and carrying them from one point to another without causing the slightest danger, are themselves a sign of His majesty.

Furthermore, if it were not for their irrigation and "water-giving office," there would be neither a drop of water to drink nor a spring or stream for plants to grow throughout the dry lands of the earth. Everywhere would be a ruin and a desert, and dead dust would be scattered everywhere. This too is another manifestation of His knowledge and power.

Indeed, all of these "are signs and marks of His Pure Essence, but for a people who have intellect and intelligence and who reflect" (لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ).

Not for the heedless simpletons, those with eyes but no insight, and those with ears who are deaf!

Ayahs

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَتَّخِذُ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَندَادًا يُحِبُّونَهُمْ كَحُبِّ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَشَدُّ حُبًّا لِّلَّهِ ۗ وَلَوْ يَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ إِذْ يَرَوْنَ ٱلْعَذَابَ أَنَّ ٱلْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعَذَابِ (١٦٥)
Among the people are those who set up compeers besides Allah, loving them as if loving Allah—but the faithful have a more ardent love for Allah—though the wrongdoers will see, when they sight the punishment, that power, altogether, belongs to Allah, and that Allah is severe in punishment. (165)
إِذْ تَبَرَّأَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتُّبِعُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ وَرَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ وَتَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَسْبَابُ (١٦٦)
When those who were followed will disown the followers, and they will sight the punishment while all their means of recourse will be cut off, (166)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّ لَنَا كَرَّةً فَنَتَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُمْ كَمَا تَبَرَّءُوا۟ مِنَّا ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُرِيهِمُ ٱللَّهُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ حَسَرَٰتٍ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ وَمَا هُم بِخَـٰرِجِينَ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ (١٦٧)
and when the followers will say, ‘Had there been another turn for us, we would disown them as they disown us [now]!’ Thus shall Allah show them their deeds as regrets for themselves, and they shall not leave the Fire. (167)

Commentary

The Disavowal of the Leaders of Disbelief from Their Followers!

In the preceding verses, the discussion focused on the proofs of God's existence and the affirmation of His Oneness through the system of creation. In the verses under discussion, the address is directed toward those who have turned a blind eye to these clear and decisive proofs and have stepped onto the path of polytheism, idol-worship, and the multiplicity of gods. It speaks of those who bow down before these hollow deities and cherish them with a love that is only worthy of Allah, who is the source of all perfections and the Bestower of all blessings.

First, it says: "And of mankind are some who take for themselves objects of worship other than Allah" (وَ مِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَتَّخِذُ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ أَنْداداً) [^1]. Not only have they chosen idols as their deities, but "they love them as they should love Allah" (يُحِبُّونَهُمْ كَحُبِّ اللَّهِ‌).

"But those who believe are stronger in their love for Allah" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَشَدُّ حُبًّا لِلَّهِ‌).

This is because they are a thoughtful and wise people, and they never abandon His Pure Essence, which is the source of all perfections. Every desire and affection, when compared to the love of Allah, is worthless and insignificant in their eyes. In fact, they do not see anyone other than Him as worthy of love and affection; they do not act except for His sake and in His way. They are so immersed in the boundless ocean of Allah's love that, like Ali عليه السلام, they say:

فهبنى صبرت على عذابك فكيف اصبر على فراقك! "Suppose I could endure Your punishment, but how could I endure being separated from You?" [^2]

Fundamentally, true love is always directed toward some form of perfection. Man never falls in love with non-existence or deficiencies; rather, he always seeks existence and perfection. For this reason, He whose existence and perfection are superior to all is the most deserving of being loved by everyone.

In short, as the above verse states: the love and affection of the believers toward Allah is more deep-rooted, profound, and intense than the love of the idolaters for their imaginary deities.

How could it be otherwise? Can one who has realized a reality and loves it be equal to one who is trapped in superstition and imagination? The love of the believers stems from intellect, knowledge, and gnosis, while the love of the disbelievers stems from ignorance, superstition, and fantasy!

And for this same reason, the former love is never shaken, whereas the love of the polytheists has no stability or permanence.

Therefore, the verse continues: "If only those who are unjust could see, when they behold the punishment, that all power belongs to Allah and that Allah is severe in punishment," at that moment they will realize the ugliness of their deeds and the evil of their end, and they will confess that they were deviant human beings.

[^1]: "Andad" is the plural of "Nidd," meaning "like" or "equal." However, according to a group of linguists, it refers to a likeness that has an essential (substantial) similarity to something else, whereas "Mithl" has a more general meaning. Thus, the meaning of the verse is that the polytheists believed [their idols] were similar to Allah in the essence of His Being—or in other words, out of ignorance, they attributed Divine attributes to them. [^2]: From Dua Kumeil.

(وَ لَوْ يَرَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا إِذْ يَرَوْنَ الْعَذابَ أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ شَدِيدُ الْعَذابِ‌) [^1]

At this moment, the veils of ignorance, pride, and negligence are removed from before their eyes, and they realize their mistake. But since they have no support or refuge, out of extreme desperation, they involuntarily reach out to their deities and leaders. However, "at that time, their misguided leaders will reject them and disown their followers" (إِذْ تَبَرَّأَ الَّذِينَ اتُّبِعُوا مِنَ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوا).

"And at that same moment, they see the divine punishment with their own eyes, and all their ties are severed" (وَ رَأَوُا الْعَذابَ وَ تَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ الْأَسْبابُ‌).

It is obvious that the "deities" referred to here are not stone and wooden idols, but rather the tyrannical and autocratic humans and devils to whom these polytheists completely surrendered themselves and to whom they gave unconditional submission.

But these misguided followers, seeing the disloyalty of their deities so clearly, say to console their own hearts: "If only we could return [to the world] once more, so that we might disown them as they have disowned us today!" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوا لَوْ أَنَّ لَنا كَرَّةً فَنَتَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُمْ كَما تَبَرَّؤُا مِنَّا).

But what is the use? The matter is settled, and there is no return to the world. A similar statement is found in Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 38: (حَتَّى إِذا جاءَنا قالَ يا لَيْتَ بَيْنِي وَ بَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ فَبِئْسَ الْقَرِينُ‌).

"When he comes before Us [on the Day of Resurrection], he says to his misguiding leader: 'I wish there were between me and you the distance between the East and the West!'"

And at the end of the verse, it says: Yes, "Thus Allah will show them their deeds as a source of regret for them" (كَذلِكَ يُرِيهِمُ اللَّهُ أَعْمالَهُمْ حَسَراتٍ عَلَيْهِمْ‌).

And they will never emerge from the Fire of Hell (وَ ما هُمْ بِخارِجِينَ مِنَ النَّارِ).

Indeed, what can they do except feel regret? Regret over the wealth they accumulated, the benefit of which others enjoyed; regret over the extraordinary opportunities they had for salvation and deliverance but lost; regret over the worship of incompetent and worthless deities instead of worshipping Allah, the Almighty, the Most High.

But it is a futile regret, for it is neither the time for action nor the place for compensation; rather, it is only the time for punishment and seeing the result of one's deeds!

[^1]: Some commentators consider the word "Law" (if) here to express a wish (tamanni), but a larger number consider it "conditional" (shartiyyah). In this case, its result (jaza) is omitted, and it would be a phrase such as "they would surely see the evil of their actions and the evil of their end."

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ كُلُوا۟ مِمَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ حَلَـٰلًا طَيِّبًا وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (١٦٨)
O mankind! Eat of what is lawful and pure in the earth, and do not follow in Satan’s steps. Indeed, he is your manifest enemy. (168)
إِنَّمَا يَأْمُرُكُم بِٱلسُّوٓءِ وَٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَأَن تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (١٦٩)
He only prompts you to [commit] evil and indecent acts, and that you attribute to Allah what you do not know. (169)

Commentary

The Footsteps of Satan!

In the preceding verses, there was a severe rebuke of polytheism and idol worship. One form of polytheism is for a human to consider someone other than Allah as a legislator, placing the system of legislation and the determination of the lawful (halal) and the prohibited (haram) in their hands. The verses under discussion introduce this act as a satanic deed, stating: "O mankind, eat from whatever is on earth [that is] lawful and good" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ كُلُوا مِمَّا فِي الْأَرْضِ حَلَالًا طَيِّبًا).

"And do not follow the footsteps of Satan. Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy" (وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا خُطُواتِ الشَّيْطانِ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ).

It is noteworthy that the addresses in the Quran regarding the use of food are numerous, and they are usually accompanied by two conditions: "lawful" (halal) and "good" (tayyib).

"Halal" refers to something that has no legal prohibition, and "tayyib" refers to pure things that are compatible with healthy human nature—the opposite of "khabith" (vile), which human nature finds repulsive.

"Khutuwat" is the plural of "khutwah," meaning a step or pace. The "footsteps of Satan" are the steps that Satan takes to achieve his goal and to seduce people.

The phrase "do not follow the footsteps of Satan" appears in five places in the Holy Quran, two of which concern the use of food and divine sustenance. In reality, it warns humans not to consume these lawful blessings in improper ways and to use these divine favors as a means for obedience and servitude, rather than for rebellion and corruption on earth.

Following these footsteps of Satan is, in truth, the same thing mentioned in other verses of the Quran following the instruction to use lawful foods, such as: "Eat and drink from the provision of Allah, and do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption" (Surah Al-Baqarah, 60).

And similarly: "Eat from the good things with which We have provided you and do not transgress therein" (Surah Ta-Ha, 81).

In short, these blessings and resources should be a strength for obedience, not a means for sin.

The phrase "Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy," which appears more than ten times in the Holy Quran following the name of Satan, is intended to mobilize all human forces to fight against this great and manifest enemy.

The next verse provides a clear reason for the persistent enmity of Satan, who has no goal other than the misery and wretchedness of man, saying: "He only commands you to evil and immorality" (إِنَّمَا يَأْمُرُكُمْ بِالسُّوءِ وَالْفَحْشَاءِ). "And that you say about Allah what you do not know" (وَأَنْ تَقُولُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ).

Therefore, satanic programs are summarized in these three matters: "evils," "immoralities," and "saying improper and undocumented words against the Pure Essence of the Lord."

"Fahsha" is derived from the root "fuhsh," meaning any action that exceeds the bounds of moderation and takes on a "gross" or "obscene" form. Thus, it includes all clear and manifest vices and abominations. However, the fact that we see this word used today specifically for acts contrary to chastity or for sins that carry a legal punishment (hadd) is actually an instance of using a "general" term for some of its specific applications.

The phrase "say about Allah what you do not know" may refer to the prohibition of certain lawful foods that the Arabs in the Age of Ignorance attributed to Allah. According to some great commentators, the remnants of this way of thinking even persisted among a group of new Muslims [1], or it may have a broader meaning that includes attributing partners or likenesses to Allah.

In any case, this phrase indicates that such actions are, at the very least, speaking without knowledge, and that too against the Great Lord; this is an act that is incompatible with any logic, reason, or intellect.

Fundamentally, if people were committed to ensuring that every word they spoke was backed by definitive evidence, many of the disorders and miseries of human society would be eliminated.

In fact, all superstitions in divine religions and creeds have infiltrated through this very channel by illogical individuals, and a significant portion of ideological and practical deviations is due to the failure to observe this fundamental principle. Therefore, this act—alongside evils and immoralities—occupies an independent title among the satanic footsteps in the above verse.

Key Points

1. The Principle of Permissibility (Asl al-Hilliyyah)

This verse is evidence that the primary principle regarding all foods existing on earth is permissibility (halal), and prohibited foods are exceptional. Therefore, the prohibition of something requires proof, not its permissibility.

Since legislative laws must be in harmony with the laws of creation, the nature of creation also necessitates this. To put it more clearly, whatever Allah has created certainly has a benefit and was intended for the use of His servants; thus, it makes no sense for the primary principle to be prohibition. The result is that any food whose prohibition is not proven by valid evidence is lawful according to the above noble verse, as long as it is not a source of corruption, harm, or injury to the individual or society.

2. Gradual Deviations

The phrase "footsteps of Satan" (khutuwat al-Shaytan) seemingly points to a subtle educational point: that deviations and crimes usually infiltrate a person gradually, not all at once or instantly. For example, for a young person to become addicted to drugs, gambling, or wine, there are usually stages: first, they attend one of these sessions as a spectator and consider the act to be simple.

The second step is recreational participation in gambling (without winning or losing) or using drugs to relieve fatigue or treat an illness and the like.

The third step is using these substances in small amounts with the intention of quitting in a short time.

Finally, the steps are taken one after another, and the person becomes a dangerous professional gambler or a severely afflicted and wretched addict.

The whispers of Satan are usually in this form; he leads man step by step and gradually behind him toward the precipice. This subject is not limited to the original Satan; all satanic and corrupt systems use this "step-by-step" (khutuwat) method to implement their sinister plans. Therefore, the Quran says: one must be alert from the very first step and not accompany Satan.

It is also noteworthy that in Islamic hadiths, superstitious and illogical acts are introduced as "footsteps of Satan."

For example, we read in a hadith that a man had vowed to slaughter his son (for Allah). Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said: "That is from the footsteps of Satan" [1]. In another hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "Every oath by other than Allah is from the footsteps of Satan" [2]. Again, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever takes an oath to abandon something whose performance is better, should not pay attention to this oath and should perform that good deed; there is no expiation (kaffarah) for it, and this is from the footsteps of Satan" [3].

3. Satan: An Ancient Enemy
