The fact that at the end of the above verse Satan is introduced as a "clear enemy" is either because of his enmity from the first day with "Adam," whereby he lost everything due to his disobedience in refusing to prostrate to Adam, or because his commands—such as murder, crime, and wickedness—are obvious to everyone. Everyone knows that such programs do not come from a friend, but are invitations from a dangerous enemy who wants nothing but human misery.

Or it is because Satan explicitly announced his enmity toward man, girded himself for hostility, and declared: "I will surely mislead them all" (لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ)!

4. The Nature of Satan's Whispering

A question arises here: the verse says, "Satan commands you to go toward evil and immorality." Certainly, "command" here refers to the whisperings of Satan, yet when we perform evil deeds, we do not feel any external command or stimulation, and Satan's effort to mislead us is never tangible.

The answer is: as is also understood from the word "waswasah" (whispering), Satan's influence on the human being is a kind of hidden and unconscious influence, which in some verses is referred to as "iwha" (revelation/suggestion). In Surah Al-An'am, verse 121, we read: "And indeed, the devils do inspire (layuhuna) their allies..." (وَإِنَّ الشَّيَاطِينَ لَيُوحُونَ إِلَى أَوْلِيَائِهِمْ). As we know, "wahy" (revelation) is originally a hidden and mysterious voice and sometimes unconscious influences.

However, a human can well distinguish "Divine inspirations" from "Satanic whisperings" because there is a clear sign for distinguishing them:

Divine inspirations, because they are familiar with the pure nature (fitrah) of man and the structure of his body and soul, produce a state of expansion and joy when they occur in the heart.

In contrast, Satanic whisperings, because they are not harmonious with his nature and structure, cause a feeling of darkness, discomfort, and heaviness when they are created in his heart. And if his desires are stimulated such that he does not have this feeling during the commission of the sin, it will occur to him after the act is done. This is the difference between Satanic inspirations and Divine inspirations.

[1] Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 1, p. 425. [1], [2], [3] Al-Mizan, Vol. 1, p. 428.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ قَالُوا۟ بَلْ نَتَّبِعُ مَآ أَلْفَيْنَا عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَنَآ ۗ أَوَلَوْ كَانَ ءَابَآؤُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ (١٧٠)
When they are told, ‘Follow what Allah has sent down,’ they say, ‘No, We will follow what we have found our fathers following.’ What, even if their fathers neither exercised their reason nor were guided?! (170)
وَمَثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ كَمَثَلِ ٱلَّذِى يَنْعِقُ بِمَا لَا يَسْمَعُ إِلَّا دُعَآءً وَنِدَآءً ۚ صُمٌّۢ بُكْمٌ عُمْىٌ فَهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (١٧١)
The parable of the faithless is that of someone who shouts after that which does not hear [anything] except a call and cry: deaf, dumb, and blind, they do not exercise their reason. (171)

Commentary

Blind Imitation of Ancestors

Here, the Quran refers to the weak logic of the polytheists regarding the issue of groundlessly prohibiting lawful foods, or idol worship, stating: "When it is said to them, 'Follow what Allah has revealed,' they say, 'Rather, we will follow that which we found our fathers doing'" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اتَّبِعُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ قالُوا بَلْ نَتَّبِعُ ما أَلْفَيْنا عَلَيْهِ آباءَنا) ^1. The Quran immediately condemns this superstitious logic and blind imitation of ancestors with this short and eloquent phrase: "Even though their fathers understood nothing, nor were they guided?" (أَ وَ لَوْ كانَ آباؤُهُمْ لا يَعْقِلُونَ شَيْئاً وَ لا يَهْتَدُونَ).

That is to say, if their ancestors had been insightful scholars and guided individuals, there might have been grounds to follow them. However, while they know that their ancestors were ignorant, illiterate, and superstitious people, what is the meaning of following them? Is this not an instance of the ignorant imitating the ignorant? The issue of ethnicity and tribal prejudices, especially where it concerns ancestors, has existed among polytheists in general since the earliest days, and frequently among others, and it continues to this day.

However, a believer in God rejects this logic, and the Holy Quran has severely censured blind imitation and prejudice toward ancestors in many instances, considering the logic of following one's fathers with closed eyes and ears to be completely rejected.

Fundamentally, following predecessors in such a way that a person surrenders their intellect and thought entirely to them will result in nothing but regression and reactionism, because usually, subsequent generations are more experienced and aware than previous ones.

But unfortunately, this ignorant way of thinking still governs many individuals and nations who worship their ancestors like "idols." They accept a handful of superstitious customs and traditions without question, simply because they are "relics of the predecessors,"

and they wrap them in deceptive coverings such as "preserving national identity" and "historical documents of a nation."

This way of thinking is one of the most effective factors in transferring superstitions from one generation to another.

Of course, there is no obstacle to future generations analyzing and examining the customs and traditions of those who came before. They should preserve with the utmost respect whatever is consistent with reason and logic, and discard whatever is superstitious, illusory, and baseless—what could be better than this? This kind of critique of past customs and traditions is worthy of being called "preserving national and historical authenticity." However, total and blind submission to them is nothing but superstition, reactionism, and folly.

It is noteworthy that regarding their ancestors, we read in the above verse: they neither understood anything, nor were they guided. This means one can follow two types of people: someone who possesses knowledge, intellect, and wisdom themselves, or someone who, if not a scholar themselves, has accepted the guidance of a scholar.

But their predecessors were neither aware individuals themselves, nor did they have an aware leader or guide. We know that the imitation which ruins people is precisely this imitation of the ignorant by the ignorant, for "A hundred curses be upon such imitation!"

In the next verse, it proceeds to explain why this group shows no flexibility in the face of these clear proofs and continues to persist in misguidance and disbelief. It says: "The example of you calling this faithless folk toward faith and breaking the barrier of blind imitations is like one who calls out to sheep and animals (to save them from danger), but they perceive nothing except a shout and a cry" (وَ مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي يَنْعِقُ بِما لا يَسْمَعُ إِلَّا دُعاءً وَ نِداءً).

Yes, they are like sheep and animals that understand nothing from the cries of a benevolent and compassionate shepherd except a noise that only produces a momentary stimulation in them.

At the end of the verse, for further emphasis and clarification, it adds: "They are deaf, dumb, and blind, so they do not understand!" (صُمٌّ بُكْمٌ عُمْيٌ فَهُمْ لا يَعْقِلُونَ).

For this reason, they have clung only to the wrong and superstitious traditions of their fathers and turn away from every constructive call. ^1 Some commentators have mentioned another interpretation for the verse, which is: the example of those who call upon idols and fabricated gods is like one who calls upon senseless animals; neither do those animals understand anything of his words, nor do these fabricated deities understand the words of their worshippers, for these idols are deaf, blind, and dumb (observe closely).

However, most commentators have chosen the first interpretation, and it has been alluded to in Islamic narrations as well, and we have also chosen it.

^1: Of course, according to this interpretation, the verse requires an implied word (taqdir), and originally it is: "The example of the caller of those who disbelieve toward faith..." meaning the example of the one who invites the disbelievers to faith is like the compassionate shepherd...

Therefore, the phrase "deaf, dumb, and blind, so they do not understand" is a description for such individuals who have practically lost all tools of perception—not that they lack eyes, ears, and tongues; they have them, but since they do not use them, it is as if they do not have them!

Key Points

1- Tools of Cognition

Undoubtedly, for a human to connect with the outside world, tools are necessary which are called "tools of cognition." Most important of all are the eyes and ears for seeing and hearing, and the tongue for questioning. Therefore, in the above verse, after comparing these individuals to the deaf, dumb, and blind because of their failure to use the tools of cognition, it immediately says, using the "fa" of consequence (fa al-ta'riq) which is for drawing a conclusion:

"Therefore, they do not understand."

In this way, the Quran testifies that the most fundamental basis of sciences and knowledge are the eyes, ears, and tongue: eyes and ears for direct perception, and the tongue for establishing a relationship with others and acquiring their knowledge.

In philosophy, this truth is also proven: that even non-sensory sciences initially stem from sensory sciences. This is an extensive discussion that is not the place for explanation here (for more explanation regarding the blessing of the tools of cognition, refer to Volume 11, page 335 onwards, under verse 78 of Surah An-Nahl).

2- "Yan'iqu" (ينعق) "Yan'iqu" is from the root "na'q" (نعق), which originally means the sound of a crow when it does not scream. However, "naghq" (نغق) (with the letter "ghayn") means the sound of a crow in the form of a scream accompanied by stretching its neck. ^1 But later, this meaning was expanded and applied to the sounds made toward animals. It is obvious that they do not become aware of the meaning of words and sentences, and if they sometimes show reactions, it is mostly because of the tone of voice and the manner of pronouncing the words.

^1: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses being discussed.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لِلَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ (١٧٢)
O you who have faith! Eat of the good things We have provided you, and thank Allah, if it is Him that you worship. (172)
إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَيْتَةَ وَٱلدَّمَ وَلَحْمَ ٱلْخِنزِيرِ وَمَآ أُهِلَّ بِهِۦ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ بَاغٍ وَلَا عَادٍ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٧٣)
He has forbidden you only carrion, blood, the flesh of the swine, and that which has been offered to other than Allah. But should someone be compelled, without being rebellious or transgressive, there shall be no sin upon him. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (173)

Commentary

Pure and Impure Things

Since the Quran employs the method of emphasis and repetition in various forms regarding deep-rooted deviations, these verses once again return to the issue of the groundless prohibition of certain lawful and healthy foods during the Age of Ignorance by the polytheists. However, here the address is directed toward the believers, whereas in the preceding verses, the address was to all of mankind.

It says: "O you who have faith! Eat of the good things which We have provided for you" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُلُوا مِنْ طَيِّباتِ مَا رَزَقْنَاكُمْ).

"And give thanks to Allah if it is Him you worship" (وَ اشْكُرُوا لِلَّهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ).

These pure and lawful blessings, which carry no prohibition and are consistent with healthy human nature and disposition, have been created for you; why then should you not utilize them?!

These provide you with strength so that you may fulfill your duties; furthermore, they remind you to thank the Lord and worship Him.

Comparing this verse with the verse: "O mankind! Eat of what is in the earth..." (Verse 168 of this same Surah) and observing the differences between the two reveals two subtle points:

Here it says: "of the good things which We have provided for you" (مِنْ طَيِّباتِ مَا رَزَقْنَاكُمْ), whereas there it said: "of what is in the earth" (مِمَّا فِي الْأَرْضِ). This difference seemingly points to the fact that pure blessings are originally created for the people of faith, and those without faith receive their sustenance by virtue of them—much like the water a gardener directs through channels for the flowers, yet the thorns and weeds also benefit from it! Secondly, it tells ordinary people to eat and not follow the footsteps of Satan, but to the believers in the verse under discussion, it says: "Eat and give thanks to Allah," meaning it does not suffice with merely not misusing these blessings, but rather considers proper use (gratitude) as a condition.

In truth, from ordinary people, it is only expected that they do not commit sin, but from the people of faith, it is expected that they consume these blessings in the best possible way.

Additionally, the repeated recommendation regarding the use of pure foods mentioned in multiple verses might raise a question for some. However, if we pay a little attention to the history of the Age of Ignorance and their superstitious customs—knowing how they would prohibit lawful blessings upon themselves without reason, a habit so ingrained that it was treated like divine revelation and sometimes explicitly attributed to Allah—the reason for this emphasis becomes clear. Through this, the Quran intends to completely purge these superstitious thoughts from their minds.

Furthermore, the emphasis on the term "Tayyib" (pure/good) draws everyone's attention to this Islamic instruction: to avoid impure foods, meats such as carrion, predators, and insects, as well as intoxicants which were heavily prevalent among the people of that time.

In Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 149 onwards, we have a detailed discussion regarding the use of pure sustenance and reasonable adornments by believers (under Verse 32 of Al-A'raf).

The next verse, to clarify forbidden and prohibited foods and to cut off any excuse, states: "He has only forbidden you carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, and that which has been sacrificed to other than Allah" (إِنَّما حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةَ وَ الدَّمَ وَ لَحْمَ الْخِنْزِيرِ وَ ما أُهِلَّ بِهِ لِغَيْرِ اللَّهِ).

In this way, it mentions three types of forbidden meats plus blood, which were the most common issues for the people of that environment. Some of these have an apparent filthiness that is hidden from no one, such as carrion, blood, and pork; others have a spiritual filthiness, such as sacrifices made for idols.

The exclusivity derived from the word "Innama" (only) in the verse is what is technically called "relative restriction" (hasr idafi). That is, the intent is not to list all prohibitions, but rather the goal is to negate the innovations they held regarding certain lawful meats. In other words, they would prohibit some pure and lawful meats upon themselves based on superstitions and delusions, yet in times of food shortage, they would consume contaminated carrion, pork, or blood! The Quran announces to them that these are forbidden for you, not those (and this is the meaning of "relative restriction").

And since necessities sometimes arise where a person is forced to use some forbidden foods to save their life, the Quran makes an exception at the end of the verse, saying: "But whoever is compelled (to save their life from death) to eat of them, there is no sin upon him, provided he is not a rebel or a transgressor!" (فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ باغٍ وَ لا عادٍ فَلا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ).

Thus, to prevent "necessity" from becoming a pretext or excuse for overindulgence in forbidden foods, it cautions with the two phrases "not a rebel" (ghayra baghin) and "nor a transgressor" (la 'adin) that this permission is only for those who do not seek pleasure in eating these prohibitions and do not exceed the necessary amount required to escape death. (Baghin and 'adin were originally baghi and 'adi; baghi is from the root baghy meaning "to seek," and here it means seeking pleasure, and 'adi means "transgressor," i.e., one who exceeds the limit of necessity).

Another interpretation for the phrase "not a rebel nor a transgressor" has been mentioned, which does not contradict the first meaning, and both may be combined in the verse's concept: given that one of the meanings of baghy is oppression and injustice, the intent is that the permission to eat forbidden meats is specific to those whose journey is not a journey of oppression or sin. (It should be noted that necessity and compulsion usually occurred in journeys like those of that time, where caravans were stranded in the desert or lost their way).

Therefore, if the journey is a journey of sin, although they must eat forbidden food to save their lives by the dictate of reason, the sin will still be recorded in their book of deeds. In other words, it is obligatory for these oppressors to eat these meats to save their lives, but this obligation does not diminish their responsibility, because they encountered such compulsion while on a wrong path.

Narrations stating that this verse refers to those who do not take steps toward rebelling against the Imam of the Muslims also point to this same reality. Just as it is established in the rulings of a traveler's prayer that the ruling for shortened prayer is only for travelers whose journey is not a sinful one; hence, in narrations, the phrase "not a rebel nor a transgressor" has been used as evidence for both rulings (the ruling for a traveler's prayer and the ruling for the necessity of eating forbidden meats). [1] At the end of the verse, it says: "Indeed, Allah is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

The same Allah who prohibited these meats has, by His special mercy, permitted their use in cases of extreme necessity.

Key Points

1- The Philosophy Behind the Prohibition of Forbidden Meats

Undoubtedly, the foods prohibited in the above verse, like other divine prohibitions, have a specific philosophy and were legislated with full consideration of the state of the human body and soul with all their characteristics. In Islamic narrations, the harms of each have been detailed, and human scientific progress has pulled back the curtain on them.

For example, in the book Al-Kafi, regarding carrion, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"As for carrion, no one partakes of it except that his body becomes weak, his strength departs, and his progeny is cut off; and the eater of carrion dies only suddenly."

The Imam, after an introduction stating that all these rulings are for the benefit of mankind, says: "As for carrion, no one eats of it except that his body becomes weak and ailing, it diminishes his strength, and cuts off the lineage, and he who continues this act will pass away from a stroke or sudden death!" [1] These harms may be because the digestive system cannot produce healthy, living blood from carrion; furthermore, carrion is a hub for various microbes. Islam has not only forbidden eating carrion but has also declared it najis (ritually impure) so that Muslims avoid it completely.

The second thing prohibited in the verse is "blood" (وَ الدَّمَ). Consuming blood has both physical harm and an adverse moral effect, because blood, on one hand, is a substance perfectly ready for the cultivation of various microbes.

All microbes that enter the human body attack the blood and make it the center of their activity. For this reason, white blood cells, which are the guards and soldiers of the human body's kingdom, always stand guard in the blood region so that microbes do not find a way into this sensitive fortress that is closely connected to all regions of the body.

Especially when blood stops flowing and, so to speak, "dies," the white blood cells perish. For this reason, microbes, seeing the field empty of rivals, rapidly reproduce and spread. Therefore, if it is said that blood, when it stops flowing, is the most contaminated part of the human or animal body, it is no exaggeration.

On the other hand, it has been proven in modern nutritional science that foods affect human temperaments and morals through their influence on glands and the creation of hormones. Since ancient times, the effect of blood consumption on cruelty and hard-heartedness has been experienced and has even become a proverb. Thus, we read in a narration: "Those who consume blood become so hard-hearted that they might even kill their own parents or children!" [1]

The third thing prohibited in the verse is eating "swine" (pork) (وَ لَحْمَ الْخِنْزِيرِ).

The pig, even among Europeans who mostly consume its meat, is a symbol of a lack of honor (bi-ghayrati) and is a filthy animal. The pig is exceptionally indifferent and reckless in sexual matters, and besides the scientifically proven effect of food on temperament, the effect of this food specifically on recklessness in sexual matters is observable.

In the law of Prophet Moses عليه السلام, the prohibition of pork was also announced, and in the Gospels, sinners are likened to pigs, and within stories, the manifestation of Satan is introduced as a pig.

It is surprising that some see with their own eyes that, on one hand, the pig's diet typically consists of filth and sometimes its own waste, and on the other hand, it has become clear to everyone that the meat of this foul animal contains two types of dangerous parasites called "Trichina" worms and a type of "tapeworm," yet they still insist on using its meat.

[1]: In a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq (ع), we read that in interpreting the above verse he said: "The 'rebel' (baghi) is one who goes out for hunting (for recreation, not out of need) and the 'transgressor' ('adi) is a thief; they have no right to eat carrion and it is forbidden for them, and they cannot pray the shortened prayer" (Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 5, p. 509). [1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 16, p. 310. [1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 16, p. 310.

The "Trichina" worm alone is enough to lay 15,000 eggs in one month and cause various diseases in humans such as anemia, dizziness, specific fevers, diarrhea, rheumatic pains, nerve tension, internal itching, accumulation of fat, bruising and fatigue, difficulty in chewing, swallowing food, breathing, and so on.

In one kilogram of pork, there may be 400 million larvae of the "Trichina" worm, and perhaps these factors led to the prohibition of pork consumption in parts of Russia a few years ago.

Yes, a religion whose instructions gain fresh brilliance over time is the religion of God, the religion of Islam.

Some say that with modern means, all these parasites can be killed and pork can be purified of them. But even assuming that through hygienic means and cooking pork at high temperatures the aforementioned parasites are completely destroyed, the harm of pork is still undeniable. For according to the established principle mentioned, the meat of every animal contains the traits of that animal and affects the morals of those who feed on it through glands and their secretions (hormones). In this way, eating pork can transfer the trait of sexual promiscuity and indifference to matters of honor—which are prominent characteristics of the male of this animal—to the consumer.

And perhaps one of the causes of the severe sexual promiscuity prevailing in Western countries is the consumption of the meat of this contaminated animal.

The fourth thing prohibited is meat over which a name other than Allah's was invoked at the time of slaughter (وَ ما أُهِلَّ بِهِ لِغَيْرِ اللَّهِ).

Among the meats forbidden in this verse is the meat of animals slaughtered, as in the Age of Ignorance, in the name of something other than Allah (idols).

Does mentioning the name of Allah or someone else during slaughter affect the meat of the animal from a hygienic perspective?! In response, it must be said that one should not forget that it is not necessary for the name of Allah or someone else to affect the physical essence of the meat hygienically. Prohibitions in Islam are based on various aspects: sometimes the prohibition of something is for hygiene and physical preservation, sometimes for the purification of the soul, and sometimes for the preservation of the social order. The prohibition of meats slaughtered in the name of idols actually has a spiritual, moral, and educational aspect. They distance man from God and have an undesirable psychological and educational effect, as they are among the traditions of polytheism and idolatry and serve to renew their memory.

2- Repetition and Emphasis

The prohibition of the four subjects mentioned above is cited in four surahs of the Quran, two of which were revealed in Mecca (Al-An'am 145 and Al-Nahl 115) and two in Medina (Al-Baqarah 173, which is the verse under discussion, and Al-Ma'idah 3).

It appears that the first time was at the beginning of the Mission when the prohibition of these meats was announced; the second time was toward the end of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله stay in Mecca; the third time was in the early Hijra in Medina; and finally, the fourth time was toward the end of the Prophet's life in Surah Al-Ma'idah, which is among the last surahs of the Quran.

This manner of revelation, which is unprecedented or rare, is due to the importance of this subject and its numerous physical and spiritual dangers, and also because of the heavy involvement of the people of that time in those practices.

3- Using Blood for Transfusion

It perhaps needs no explanation that the intent of prohibiting blood in the above verse is the prohibition of consuming it. Therefore, other reasonable uses, such as blood transfusions to save the lives of the injured and the sick and the like, pose no problem. There is not even evidence for the prohibition of buying and selling blood in these cases, as it is a rational, legitimate use required for public need.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْتُمُونَ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَيَشْتَرُونَ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَا يَأْكُلُونَ فِى بُطُونِهِمْ إِلَّا ٱلنَّارَ وَلَا يُكَلِّمُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَلَا يُزَكِّيهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٧٤)
Indeed those who conceal what Allah has sent down of the Book and sell it for a paltry gain—they do not take in, into their bellies, [anything] except fire, and Allah shall not speak to them on the Day of Resurrection, nor shall He purify them, and there is a painful punishment for them. (174)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةَ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَٱلْعَذَابَ بِٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ ۚ فَمَآ أَصْبَرَهُمْ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ (١٧٥)
They are the ones who bought error for guidance, and punishment for pardon: how patient of them to face the Fire! (175)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ نَزَّلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَفِى شِقَاقٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (١٧٦)
That is so because Allah has sent down the Book with the truth, and those who differ about the Book are surely in extreme defiance. (176)

Occasion of Revelation

All commentators unanimously agree that the above verses were revealed concerning the People of the Book. According to many, they specifically refer to the Jewish scholars who, before the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, used to describe his attributes and signs to the people as they had found them in their own books. However, after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله appeared and they witnessed the people's inclination toward him, they feared that if they continued their previous method, their interests would be jeopardized and the gifts and banquets arranged for them would be lost! Consequently, they concealed the descriptions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that were revealed in the Torah. The above verses were revealed and severely rebuked them.

Commentary

Further Rebuke for Concealing the Truth

These verses are an emphasis on what was mentioned in verse 159 regarding the concealment of the truth. Although the address is directed at the Jewish scholars, as we have repeatedly reminded, the meaning of the verses is in no case restricted to the occasion of revelation. In reality, the occasions of revelation are a means to explain general and universal rulings, of which they themselves are considered one instance.

Therefore, all those who conceal God's rulings and the truths needed by the people, and commit this great betrayal for the sake of gaining status or acquiring wealth, must know that they have sold a precious reality for a paltry price. For even if the concealment of truth were exchanged for the entire world, the perpetrator would still have suffered a loss! In the first verse, He says: "Indeed, those who conceal what Allah has sent down of the Book and exchange it for a small price—those consume into their bellies nothing but fire!" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْتُمُونَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَ يَشْتَرُونَ بِهِ ثَمَناً قَلِيلًا أُولئِكَ ما يَأْكُلُونَ فِي بُطُونِهِمْ إِلَّا النَّارَ).

Yes, the gifts and wealth they acquire through this path are scorching fires that enter their very being.

This expression, meanwhile, once again clarifies the issue of the "embodiment of deeds" in the Hereafter and shows that the forbidden wealth obtained through this means is, in reality, a fire that enters their hearts, which will manifest in its true form on the Day of Resurrection.

Then, He addresses an important spiritual punishment that is far more painful than the material punishment, saying: "Allah will not speak to them on the Day of Resurrection, nor will He purify them, and for them is a painful punishment!" (وَ لا يُكَلِّمُهُمُ اللَّهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَ لا يُزَكِّيهِمْ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

In verse 77 of Surah Al-Imran, a similar painful punishment is mentioned for those who trample upon the covenant of Allah and their oaths for the sake of meager benefits: "Indeed, those who exchange the covenant of Allah and their [own] oaths for a small price will have no share in the Hereafter, and Allah will not speak to them or look at them on the Day of Resurrection, nor will He purify them; and they will have a painful punishment." According to this verse, Allah will not speak to such covenant-breakers on the Day of Resurrection, will not look upon them with grace, will not purify them, and for them is a painful punishment.

From this verse and the verse under discussion, it is understood that one of the greatest Divine blessings in the other world is that Allah speaks to the people of faith through grace. That is, the same station that the Prophets of God held in this world—benefiting from the pleasure of conversing with the Lord—the believers will also be honored with in that world. What pleasure could be greater than this?

Furthermore, Allah looks upon them with grace and washes them with the water of His forgiveness and mercy, making them pure and clean. What blessing could be higher than this?

It is obvious that Allah speaking with His servants does not mean that Allah has a tongue or is a physical body; rather, with His infinite power, He creates sound waves in space in a way that can be perceived and heard (just as He spoke to Moses in the valley of Tuwa), or He speaks to His special servants through inspiration and the language of the heart.

In any case, this great grace of the Lord and this unique spiritual pleasure are for the pure-hearted servants who have a truth-telling tongue and acquaint people with the realities, who stand by their covenant and never sacrifice the seeking of truth for material interests.

Here a question arises: it is understood from some verses of the Quran that Allah also speaks to some criminals and disbelievers on the Day of Judgment, such as: "He will say, 'Remain despised therein and do not speak to Me'" (Surah Al-Mu'minun, verse 108).

Allah says this to the sinners who request to be released from the fire of Hell, saying: "Our Lord, take us out of it, and if we return [to evil], then we are indeed wrongdoers."

In Surah Al-Jathiyah, verses 30 and 31, a similar dialogue from Allah with the criminals is also observed.

The answer is that the "speaking" intended in the verses under discussion is speaking out of grace, respect, and special love, not a speech intended as unkindness, humiliation, rejection, and punishment—which itself is one of the most painful penalties.

This point also requires little explanation: the meaning of the phrase "do not sell it for a small price" is not that concealing the truth in exchange for a huge price is permissible. Rather, the point is that any material price taken in exchange for concealing the truth is meager and worthless, even if it were the entire world!

The next verse defines the situation of this group more clearly and recounts the result of their work in this loss-making transaction: "Those are the ones who have exchanged guidance for error and punishment for forgiveness" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ اشْتَرَوُا الضَّلالَةَ بِالْهُدى‌ وَ الْعَذابَ بِالْمَغْفِرَةِ).

In this way, they have been afflicted with loss from two sides: on one hand, abandoning guidance and choosing misguidance in its place, and on the other hand, losing the mercy and forgiveness of Allah and substituting it with painful Divine punishment. This is a transaction that no rational person would pursue.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, it adds: truly it is strange, "How patient they are [in pursuit of] the Fire!" (فَما أَصْبَرَهُمْ عَلَى النَّارِ). This means how bold and indifferent they are in the face of the fire of Allah's wrath and anger.

The last verse under discussion says: These threats and promises of punishment described for those who conceal the truth are because Allah has sent down the Heavenly Book, the Quran, with the truth and accompanied by clear proofs, so that no room for doubt or ambiguity remains for anyone (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ نَزَّلَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِ‌).

Despite this, a group, for the sake of preserving their filthy interests, resorts to justification and distortion, creating divisions and disagreements regarding the Heavenly Book, so to speak, to "muddy the waters to catch fish."

"And indeed, those who differ over the Book are in extreme dissension" (وَ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ اخْتَلَفُوا فِي الْكِتابِ لَفِي شِقاقٍ بَعِيدٍ).

The word "Shiqaq" (شقاق) originally means a crack or separation. This expression perhaps points to the fact that faith, piety, and disclosing the truth are the secrets of unity and harmony in human society, whereas betrayal and concealing truths cause dispersion, separation, and a rift—not a superficial rift that can be ignored, but a deep separation and rift!

Ayah

۞ لَّيْسَ ٱلْبِرَّ أَن تُوَلُّوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ قِبَلَ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ وَٱلْمَغْرِبِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْبِرَّ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ وَءَاتَى ٱلْمَالَ عَلَىٰ حُبِّهِۦ ذَوِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينَ وَٱبْنَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَٱلسَّآئِلِينَ وَفِى ٱلرِّقَابِ وَأَقَامَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَى ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱلْمُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِهِمْ إِذَا عَـٰهَدُوا۟ ۖ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ فِى ٱلْبَأْسَآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ وَحِينَ ٱلْبَأْسِ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا۟ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ (١٧٧)
Piety is not to turn your faces to the east or the west; rather, piety is [personified by] those who have faith in Allah and the Last Day, the angels, the Book, and the prophets, and who give their wealth, for the love of Him, to relatives, orphans, the needy, the traveller and the beggar, and for [the freeing of] the slaves, and maintain the prayer and give the zakat, and those who fulfill their covenants, when they pledge themselves, and those who are patient in stress and distress, and in the heat of battle. They are the ones who are true [to their covenant], and it is they who are the Godwary. (177)

Occasion of Revelation

Since the change of the Qiblah caused a great deal of commotion among the people, especially the Jews and Christians, the Jews—who had lost their greatest point of pride (the Muslims following their Qiblah)—began to voice their protests. The Quran refers to this in verse 142 with the phrase (سَيَقُولُ السُّفَهاءُ). The above verse was revealed to confirm that all this talk regarding the issue of the Qiblah is not correct; rather, there are other matters more important than the Qiblah which serve as the criteria for human value and must be attended to. These matters are explained in this verse.

Commentary

The Root and Foundation of All Goodness

As mentioned in the commentary on the verses regarding the change of the Qiblah, the Christians turned toward the East during worship and the Jews toward the West to perform their devotions. However, Allah established the Kaaba as the Qiblah for the Muslims, which was toward the South and was considered a middle point between the two.

We also saw what a commotion the opponents of Islam on one hand, and the new Muslims on the other, raised regarding the change of the Qiblah.

The above verse addresses these groups, saying: "Righteousness is not merely turning your faces toward the East and the West at the time of prayer and spending all your time on this issue" (لَيْسَ الْبِرَّ أَنْ تُوَلُّوا وُجُوهَكُمْ قِبَلَ الْمَشْرِقِ وَ الْمَغْرِبِ‌).

"Birr" (with a short 'i' sound) originally means expansion; subsequently, it has been used to mean goodness, virtues, and benevolence, because these acts are not limited within a person's being but expand and reach others, allowing them to benefit as well.

And "Barr" (with a short 'a' sound) has a descriptive aspect and means a righteous person; originally, it means a wilderness or a vast place, and since righteous people possess a vast and expansive soul, this term is applied to them.

The Quran then proceeds to explain the most important principles of goodness in the realms of faith, ethics, and action through six headings, stating: "But righteousness (the righteous) are those who believe in Allah, the Last Day, the angels, the Heavenly Books, and the Prophets" (وَ لكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَ الْمَلائِكَةِ وَ الْكِتابِ وَ النَّبِيِّينَ‌).

In truth, this is the first foundation of all goodness and virtues: faith in the Origin (Mabda') and the Return (Ma'ad), the Divine programs, the Prophets who were commissioned to convey and implement these programs, and the angels who were considered the intermediaries for conveying this call. This is faith in principles that illuminate the entire being of a human and serve as a powerful motive for moving toward constructive programs and righteous deeds.

It is interesting that it does not say "the righteous" are those who... but rather says "righteousness" are those who...

This is because in Arabic literature, as well as in some other languages, when they want to express the highest degree of emphasis on something, they bring it in the form of an infinitive (noun) rather than an adjective. For example, it is said: "Ali عليه السلام is the justice of the human world," meaning he is so practiced in justice that it is as if his entire being has dissolved into it and he is immersed in justice from head to toe, such that whenever you look at him, you see nothing but justice! Similarly, on the opposite side, we say: "The Umayyads were the humiliation of Islam," as if their entire existence had turned into ignominy.

Therefore, from this expression, a firm and powerful faith at a high level is inferred.

After faith, it refers to the issue of spending (Infaq), sacrifice, and financial giving, saying:

"And give their wealth, despite their love for it, to kinsfolk, orphans, the needy, the wayfarer, those who ask, and for the freeing of slaves" (وَ آتَى الْمالَ عَلى‌ حُبِّهِ ذَوِي الْقُرْبى‌ وَ الْيَتامى‌ وَ الْمَساكِينَ وَ ابْنَ السَّبِيلِ وَ السَّائِلِينَ وَ فِي الرِّقابِ‌).

Undoubtedly, parting with wealth and property is not an easy task for everyone, especially when it reaches the stage of sacrifice (Ithar), because the love for it exists in almost all hearts. The expression "despite their love for it" (عَلى‌ حُبِّهِ) also refers to this reality—that they resist this desire of the heart for the sake of Allah's pleasure.

It is noteworthy that six groups of the needy are mentioned here: first, honorable relatives and kinsfolk; second, orphans and the poor; then those whose need is entirely temporary, such as wayfarers; then those who ask, indicating that not all needy people are those who ask—sometimes they are so self-restrained that they appear wealthy while being deeply in need internally, as the Quran says elsewhere: "The ignorant person thinks them wealthy because of their self-restraint" (Surah Al-Baqarah, 273); and finally, it mentions slaves who need freedom and independence, even if their material needs are ostensibly provided for by their owners.

The third principle among the principles of goodness is established as performing the prayer, saying: "And they establish prayer" (وَ أَقامَ الصَّلاةَ).

A prayer which, if performed with its conditions and limits, and with sincerity and humility, restrains a person from every sin and encourages them toward every good and felicity.

The fourth program mentioned is the payment of Zakat and obligatory financial dues, saying:

"And they pay the Zakat" (وَ آتَى الزَّكاةَ).

There are many individuals who are willing to help the poor in certain instances but are negligent in paying obligatory dues. Conversely, there is a group that will not submit to any help other than paying the obligatory dues; they are not even willing to give a single dinar to the most desperate individuals. The above verse, by mentioning "recommended spending and sacrifice" on one hand, and "payment of obligatory dues" on the other, excludes both these groups from the ranks of the truly righteous and considers the righteous person to be one who fulfills their duty in both fields.

It is interesting that regarding recommended spending, the phrase "despite their love for it" (عَلى‌ حُبِّهِ) is mentioned (since wealth is beloved to them), but regarding the obligatory Zakat it is not. This is because paying obligatory financial dues is a Divine and social duty, and fundamentally—according to Islamic logic—the needy are partners in the wealth of the rich to a certain proportion; paying the partner's share does not require such an expression.

The fifth characteristic is fulfilling covenants, saying: "And those who fulfill their covenant when they make a covenant" (وَ الْمُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِهِمْ إِذا عاهَدُوا).

This is because the capital of social life is the mutual trust of the members of society, and among the sins that sever the cord of confidence and trust and weaken the foundation of social relations is the failure to fulfill a covenant. For this reason, we read in Islamic narrations that Muslims are duty-bound to carry out three programs regarding everyone, whether the other party is a Muslim or a disbeliever, righteous or wicked:

Fulfilling the covenant, returning trusts, and respecting parents. [1]

[1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Kindness to Parents, p. 129, Hadith 15.

And finally, the sixth and last program of this righteous group is described as follows:

"And those who are patient and steadfast in hardship and poverty, and in sickness and pain, and at the time of war against the enemy, and do not kneel before these events" (وَ الصَّابِرِينَ فِي الْبَأْساءِ وَ الضَّرَّاءِ وَ حِينَ الْبَأْسِ‌). [1] At the end of the verse, as a summary and emphasis on the six previous sublime qualities, it says: "These are the ones who are truthful, and these are the God-fearing" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُتَّقُونَ‌).

Their truthfulness is evident from the fact that their actions and behavior are consistent in every respect with their belief and faith. Their piety (Taqwa) is known from the fact that they fulfill their duty toward "Allah," toward the needy and deprived, toward the entire human society, and toward their own selves.

It is noteworthy that the six prominent qualities above encompass both principles of belief and ethics as well as practical programs.

In the field of belief, all the main foundations are mentioned. Among the practical programs, spending, prayer, and Zakat—which are symbols of the relationship between the creation and the Creator, and between creation and creation—are pointed out. Among the ethical programs, emphasis is placed on fulfilling covenants and steadfastness and endurance, which form the root of all sublime ethical qualities.

[1]: "Ba'sa" is from the root "Bu's" meaning poverty; "Darra" means pain and sickness; and "Hina al-Ba's" means the time of war (Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed).

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْقِصَاصُ فِى ٱلْقَتْلَى ۖ ٱلْحُرُّ بِٱلْحُرِّ وَٱلْعَبْدُ بِٱلْعَبْدِ وَٱلْأُنثَىٰ بِٱلْأُنثَىٰ ۚ فَمَنْ عُفِىَ لَهُۥ مِنْ أَخِيهِ شَىْءٌ فَٱتِّبَاعٌۢ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَأَدَآءٌ إِلَيْهِ بِإِحْسَـٰنٍ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ تَخْفِيفٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَرَحْمَةٌ ۗ فَمَنِ ٱعْتَدَىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٧٨)
O you who have faith! Retribution is prescribed for you regarding the slain: freeman for freeman, slave for slave, and female for female. But if one is granted any extenuation by his brother, let the follow up [for the blood-money] be honourable, and let the payment to him be with kindness. That is a remission from your Lord and a mercy; and should anyone transgress after that, there shall be a painful punishment for him. (178)
وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْقِصَاصِ حَيَوٰةٌ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (١٧٩)
There is life for you in retribution, O you who possess intellects! Maybe you will be Godwary! (179)

Circumstances of Revelation

It was the custom of the Arabs in the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) that if someone from their tribe was killed, they would resolve to kill as many as they could from the killer's tribe. This mindset had progressed to the point where they were willing to destroy an entire clan for the killing of a single individual. The above verse was revealed to establish the just law of Retaliation (Qisas).

This Islamic ruling was, in fact, a middle ground between two different laws existing at that time: some considered Qisas mandatory and permitted nothing else, while others considered only blood money (Diyah) mandatory. Islam established Qisas in the event that the guardians of the deceased do not grant pardon, and Diyah when there is mutual consent between both parties.

Commentary

Retaliation is the Source of Life for You!

From these verses onward, a series of Islamic rulings are presented, completing the previous verses which were under the heading of "Righteousness and Virtue" and explained a significant portion of Islam's programs.

First, it begins with the issue of preserving the sanctity of blood, which is an exceptionally important matter in social relations, and strikes a line of cancellation through the customs and traditions of the Jahiliyyah. Addressing the believers, It says:

"O you who have believed, prescribed for you is legal retribution for those murdered" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْقِصَاصُ فِي الْقَتْلَى).

The Quran sometimes expresses mandatory instructions with the phrase Kutiba Alaykum ("It is written/prescribed for you"). This expression is seen in the above verse as well as in future verses discussing wills and fasting. In any case, this phrasing clarifies the importance and emphasis of the matter, as things are always "written" when they have become finalized and serious from every perspective.

Qisas is derived from the root Qass (rhyming with Sadd), meaning to search for or track the traces of something. Any matter that follows another is called a Qissah (story/event) by the Arabs. Since Qisas is a killing that follows another killing, this word has been applied to it.

As mentioned in the circumstances of revelation, these verses aim to moderate the excesses practiced during the Jahiliyyah regarding homicide. By choosing the word Qisas, it shows that the guardians of the murdered person have the right to perform against the killer exactly what he committed.

However, It does not stop there; in the continuation of the verse, It raises the issue of equality with greater clarity, saying: "the free for the free, the slave for the slave, and the female for the female" (الْحُرُّ بِالْحُرِّ وَالْعَبْدُ بِالْعَبْدِ وَالْأُنْثَى بِالْأُنْثَى).

God willing, we will explain that this issue is not evidence of the superiority of a man's blood over a woman's, and a male killer can indeed be retaliated against for a female victim (under certain conditions).

Then, to clarify that the matter of Qisas is a right for the guardians of the deceased and is by no means an obligatory decree—and that if they wish, they may forgive the killer and accept blood money, or even forgo blood money entirely—It adds: "But whoever overlooks from his brother anything (and the ruling of Qisas is converted to blood money by mutual consent), then there should be a suitable follow-up (and he should not pressure the party for payment) and payment to him with good conduct" (فَمَنْ عُفِيَ لَهُ مِنْ أَخِيهِ شَيْءٌ فَاتِّبَاعٌ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَأَدَاءٌ إِلَيْهِ بِإِحْسَانٍ).

In this way, on one hand, It advises the guardians of the deceased that if they have waived Qisas, they should not be excessive in demanding blood money, but should collect it in a proper manner, considering the fair amount established by Islam and in installments that the other party has the power to pay.

On the other hand, with the phrase "and payment to him with good conduct," It advises the killer to adopt a correct method in paying the blood money and to fulfill his debt completely and on time without negligence. Thus, the duty and program for each of the two parties are specified.

At the end of the verse, to emphasize and draw attention to the fact that transgression of the limit by anyone will result in severe punishment, It says: "This is an alleviation from your Lord and a mercy. But whoever transgresses after that will have a painful punishment" (ذَلِكَ تَخْفِيفٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَرَحْمَةٌ فَمَنِ اعْتَدَى بَعْدَ ذَلِكَ فَلَهُ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

This just instruction of Qisas and "Pardon," which forms a completely humane and logical set, on one hand, condemns the corrupt method of the Age of Ignorance—where they recognized no equality in retaliation and, like the executioners of the space age, would sometimes slaughter hundreds for one person.

On the other hand, it does not close the door of pardon to the people, while simultaneously not diminishing the respect for blood nor making killers audacious. Thirdly, it states that after the program of pardon and accepting blood money, neither party has the right to transgress—unlike the Jahiliyyah tribes where the guardians of the deceased would sometimes kill the murderer even after pardoning him and receiving blood money!

The next verse, with a short and very profound phrase, answers many questions regarding the issue of Qisas, saying: "And there is for you in legal retribution [saving of] life, O you of understanding, that you may become righteous" (وَلَكُمْ فِي الْقِصَاصِ حَيَاةٌ يَا أُولِي الْأَلْبَابِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ).

This verse, composed of ten words and possessing the ultimate eloquence and rhetoric, is so striking that it has been etched into everyone's minds as an Islamic slogan. It clearly shows that Islamic Qisas is by no means an act of revenge; rather, it is a window toward the life and survival of human beings.

On one hand, it guarantees the life of society; for if the law of Qisas did not exist at all and cold-hearted individuals felt secure, the lives of innocent people would be endangered—just as in countries where the law of Qisas has been completely abolished, the statistics of murder and crime have rapidly increased.

On the other hand, it is a source of life for the killer, as it largely deters and controls him from the thought of homicide.

Thirdly, due to the requirement of equality and parity, it prevents successive killings and puts an end to Jahiliyyah traditions where one murder sometimes led to several more, which in turn led to even more killings. In this way, too, it is a source of life for society.

Furthermore, considering that the ruling of Qisas is conditional upon the absence of pardon, another window to life is opened.

The phrase "that you may become righteous" (la'allakum tattaqun), which is a warning to avoid any form of transgression or violation, completes this wise Islamic decree.

Key Points

1. Retaliation and Pardon: A Just Synthesis

Islam, which pursues matters in every case with realism and comprehensive analysis, has expressed the truth regarding the blood of the innocent without any extremism or negligence. It does not rely solely on Qisas like the distorted Jewish law, nor does it only recommend the path of pardon or blood money to its followers like modern Christianity; for the latter encourages audacity, and the former is a factor for violence and revenge.

Suppose the killer and the killed were brothers or had a history of friendship and social ties; in this case, forcing Qisas would place a fresh wound upon the guardians of the deceased. Especially for individuals full of human emotions, being forced to retaliate would be considered another form of torture for them. Meanwhile, limiting the law only to pardon and blood money would make criminal individuals more daring.

Therefore, It established Qisas as the primary ruling and mentioned the ruling of pardon alongside it to moderate it.

To put it more clearly, the guardians of the deceased have the right to implement one of three rulings against the killer:

  1. Executing Qisas.
  2. Pardoning without taking blood money.
  3. Pardoning while taking blood money (of course, in this case, the killer's consent is also a condition).

2. Is Retaliation Contrary to Reason and Human Emotions?

A group that has criticized some of Islam's penal issues without reflection has raised an outcry particularly regarding the issue of Qisas, saying:

  1. The crime the killer committed is no more than destroying a human being, yet you repeat the same act during Qisas!
  2. Qisas is nothing but revenge and cruelty; this unpleasant trait should be removed from people through proper education, whereas supporters of Qisas breathe new life into this unpleasant trait of revenge every day!
  3. Homicide is not a sin committed by ordinary or healthy persons; the killer must certainly be suffering from a psychological illness and must be treated, and Qisas cannot be the medicine for such patients.
  4. Issues related to the social system must grow side-by-side with society; therefore, a law implemented fourteen hundred years ago should not be practiced in today's society!
  5. Is it not better to imprison killers instead of Qisas and use their existence for the benefit of society through forced labor? By this act, society remains safe from their evil, and their existence is utilized as much as possible.

These are the summaries of the objections raised around the issue of Qisas.

Answer

Reflection on the verses of Qisas in the Holy Quran clarifies the answer to these objections: "And there is for you in legal retribution [saving of] life, O you of understanding."

For eliminating disruptive and dangerous individuals is sometimes the best means for the growth and perfection of society. Since in such cases the issue of Qisas guarantees life and continued survival, perhaps for this reason, Qisas has been placed as an instinct within the human nature.

The systems of medicine, agriculture, and animal husbandry are all built upon this rational principle (the removal of a dangerous and disruptive entity). We see that to preserve the body, a decayed limb is amputated, or for the growth of a plant, harmful and disruptive branches are pruned. Those who view the killing of a murderer as merely the loss of another individual have only an individualistic view. If they considered the welfare of society and knew what role the execution of Qisas plays in the protection and education of other individuals, they would reconsider their words. Eliminating these bloodthirsty individuals in society is like cutting off and destroying a disruptive and harmful limb or branch, which by the decree of reason must be severed. It goes without saying that so far, no one has objected to the pruning of decayed and harmful branches or limbs. This addresses the first objection.

Regarding the second objection, it must be noted that fundamentally, the legislation of Qisas has no connection to the issue of revenge. Revenge means quenching the fire of anger for a personal matter, whereas Qisas is for the purpose of preventing the repetition of oppression and tyranny against society, and its goal is the pursuit of justice and the protection of other innocent individuals.

Regarding the third objection—that the killer must be suffering from a mental illness and such a crime cannot be committed by ordinary persons—it must be said: in some cases, this statement is correct, and in such instances, Islam has not prescribed Qisas for an insane killer or the like. However, one cannot present the killer's "illness" as a general law and an excuse, for the corruption this proposal would bring and the audacity it would give to the criminals of society is beyond doubt for anyone. If this reasoning were correct for a murderer, it should also be correct for all transgressors and those who violate the rights of others, for a person possessing complete sanity never transgresses against others. Thus, all penal laws would have to be abolished, and all violators and transgressors would be sent to psychiatric hospitals instead of prisons and punishment.

As for the claim that the growth of society does not accept the law of Qisas, and that Qisas only played a role in ancient societies but is now considered a ruling contrary to conscience that must be removed, the answer is a single sentence:

The aforementioned claim is a worthless assertion in the face of the horrific expansion of crimes in today's world and the statistics of killings on battlefields and elsewhere; it is more akin to a fantasy. Even assuming such a world comes into existence, Islam has placed the law of pardon alongside Qisas and has never introduced Qisas as the exclusive path. Certainly, in such an environment, the people themselves would prefer to pardon the killer. But in the current world, where crimes under various guises are certainly more frequent and brutal than in the past, removing this law would have no effect other than expanding the scope of crimes.

Regarding the fifth objection, it must be noted that the goal of Qisas, as the Quran explicitly states, is the preservation of the general life of society and the prevention of the repetition of murder and crimes. Certainly, prison cannot have a significant effect (especially modern prisons, whose conditions are better than many of the criminals' homes). For this reason, in countries where the death penalty has been abolished, the statistics of murder and crime have increased in a short period—especially if the prison sentence is, as usual, subject to pardon, in which case criminals commit crimes with a more tranquil mind and a more relaxed imagination.

3. Is a Man's Blood More "Colorful"?

Some might object that the verses of Qisas instruct that a "man" should not be retaliated against for the murder of a "woman." Is a man's blood more "colorful" than a woman's? Why should a criminal man not be retaliated against for killing a woman and shedding the innocent blood of humans who constitute more than half the earth's population?! In response, it must be said: the meaning of the verse is not that a man should not be retaliated against for a woman; rather, as explained in detail in Islamic jurisprudence, the guardians of a murdered woman can bring the criminal man to Qisas on the condition that they pay half the amount of the blood money (Diyah).

In other words: the meaning of not retaliating against a man for the murder of a woman refers to unconditional Qisas; however, with the payment of half the Diyah, his execution is permissible.

It is unnecessary to explain that the payment of the aforementioned amount for the execution of Qisas is not because a woman is further from the rank of humanity or that her blood is "paler" than a man's blood. This is a completely misplaced and illogical delusion, for which the term "blood money" (Khun-baha) might be the root. The payment of half the Diyah is solely to compensate for the loss that the family of the man suffers from his Qisas (reflect carefully).

To explain: men are often the effective economic members of the family, bearing the family's expenses and keeping the wheels of the family's life turning through their economic activities. Therefore, the difference between the loss of a "man" and a "woman" from an economic and financial perspective is hidden from no one. If this difference is not observed, an unjustified loss is inflicted upon the survivors of the executed man and his innocent children. Therefore, Islam, with the law of paying half the amount in the case of a man's Qisas, has respected the rights of all individuals and prevented this economic vacuum and unforgivable blow that hits a family. Islam never allows the rights of other individuals, such as the children of the person being retaliated against, to be trampled under the pretext of the word "equality."

Of course, there may be women who are greater breadwinners for their families than men, but we know that rulings and laws do not revolve around individuals; rather, men as a whole must be weighed against women as a whole (reflect carefully).

  1. Another point that draws attention in the verse and is derived from the phrase min akhihi ("from his brother") is that the Quran considers the bond of brotherhood among Muslims to be so firm that it remains even after the shedding of innocent blood. Therefore, to stir the emotions of the guardians of the deceased, It introduces them as the brothers of the killer and encourages them toward pardon and leniency with this expression; this is both remarkable and beautiful. Of course, this applies to those who have committed such a great sin due to an outburst of emotions, anger, or the like, and are regretful of their action. However, criminals who take pride in their crime and have no remorse or regret are neither worthy of the name "brother" nor deserving of pardon and forgiveness!

Ayahs

كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ إِن تَرَكَ خَيْرًا ٱلْوَصِيَّةُ لِلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۖ حَقًّا عَلَى ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٨٠)
Prescribed for you, when death approaches any of you and he leaves behind any property, is that he make a bequest for his parents and relatives, in an honourable manner—an obligation on the Godwary. (180)
فَمَنۢ بَدَّلَهُۥ بَعْدَ مَا سَمِعَهُۥ فَإِنَّمَآ إِثْمُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُبَدِّلُونَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٨١)
And should anyone alter it after hearing it, its sin shall indeed lie on those who alter it. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (181)
فَمَنْ خَافَ مِن مُّوصٍ جَنَفًا أَوْ إِثْمًا فَأَصْلَحَ بَيْنَهُمْ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٨٢)
But should someone, fearing deviance or sin on the testator’s behalf, set things right between them, there is no sin upon him. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (182)

Commentary

Worthy Wills

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to matters of life and retaliation (Qisas). In these verses, the Quran addresses a portion of the rulings regarding wills (Wasiyyah) related to financial matters, stating as an obligatory decree:

"It is prescribed for you, when death approaches one of you, if he leaves behind good (wealth), to make a will in a proper manner for parents and near relatives" (كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ إِنْ تَرَكَ خَيْراً الْوَصِيَّةُ لِلْوالِدَيْنِ وَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ).

And at the end of the verse, It adds: "This is a duty upon the righteous" (حَقًّا عَلَى الْمُتَّقِينَ).

As we previously noted, the phrase "It is prescribed for you" (كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمْ) literally implies obligation. For this reason, this expression regarding the will has been the subject of various interpretations:

  1. It is sometimes said that although making a will is a recommended (Mustahabb) act in Islamic law, because it is a highly emphasized recommendation, it is expressed with the phrase "It is prescribed for you." The end of the verse interprets this, as it says: "a duty upon the righteous" (حَقًّا عَلَى الْمُتَّقِينَ). If it were a strictly obligatory ruling, it should have said "a duty upon the believers" (حقا على المؤمنين).

  2. Others believe that this verse was revealed before the laws of inheritance. At that time, making a will regarding property was obligatory so that heirs would not fall into dispute and conflict. However, after the revelation of the inheritance verses, this obligation was abrogated and became a recommended ruling. A Hadith mentioned in Tafsir al-Ayyashi under this verse also supports this meaning.

  3. There is also the possibility that the verse refers to cases of necessity and need—meaning where a person is in debt or has a duty upon them, in which case making a will is necessary. (However, among these interpretations, the first seems closer to the truth).

It is interesting that here, instead of the word "wealth" (Mal), the word "good" (Khayr) is used; It says if he leaves behind "something good," he should make a will.

This expression shows that Islam considers wealth and capital obtained through legitimate means and utilized for the benefit of society as "good" and a blessing. It strikes a line through the incorrect thoughts of those who consider the essence of wealth to be evil. It expresses disdain for those deviant pseudo-ascetics who have not understood the spirit of Islam and equate asceticism (Zuhd) with poverty—thoughts that cause the stagnation of Islamic society and the advancement of exploiters.

Furthermore, this expression is a subtle allusion to the legitimacy of wealth, for illegitimate property left behind by a person is not "good" but rather "evil" and a calamity.

It is also understood from some narrations that the term "good" implies a significant amount of property; otherwise, minor assets do not require a will. It is better for the heirs to divide it among themselves according to the law of inheritance. In other words, a small amount of wealth is not something for which a person should set aside a third as a will [1]. Additionally, the phrase "when death approaches one of you" (إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ) is to state the final opportunity for a will, which if delayed, will be lost. Otherwise, there is no obstacle to a person planning ahead and preparing their will; rather, it is understood from narrations that this is a very

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 159.

worthy act.

It is also the height of narrow-mindedness for a person to imagine that by making a will, they are inviting bad luck or hastening their own death. Rather, a will is a type of undeniable foresight and realism, and if it does not cause a long life, it will certainly never cause a short one.

Qualifying the will in the above verse with the condition "in a proper manner" (بِالْمَعْرُوفِ) indicates that the will must be reasonable from every aspect, as "Ma'ruf" means that which is recognized by intellect and reason.

Both in terms of the amount and quantity, and in terms of the person in whose name the will is made, and in other aspects, it must be such that the common sense of the wise considers it a worthy act, not a type of undue discrimination or a cause for dispute, quarrel, and deviation from the principles of truth and justice.

When a will encompasses all the above characteristics, it is respected and sacred from every perspective, and any kind of change or alteration to it is forbidden and Haraam. Therefore, the next verse says: "Whoever changes it after hearing it, the sin is only upon those who change it" (فَمَنْ بَدَّلَهُ بَعْدَ ما سَمِعَهُ فَإِنَّما إِثْمُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يُبَدِّلُونَهُ). And if they imagine that Allah is unaware of their conspiracies, they are gravely mistaken: "Indeed, Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ).

The above verse may also point to the fact that the wrongdoings of the "executor" (Wasi—the one responsible for carrying out the will) never diminish the reward of the testator (the one who made the will). He has reached his reward, and the sin lies only on the neck of the executor who changed the quantity, quality, or the essence of the will.

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that it means if, due to the wrongdoing of the executor, the deceased's property is given to individuals who are not entitled (and they are unaware of this), there is no sin upon them; the sin is only directed at the executor who knowingly committed such a wrongful act.

It should be noted that these two interpretations are not in conflict, and both may be included in the meaning of the verse.

Up to this point, this Islamic ruling has become completely clear: that any kind of change or alteration in wills, in any form and to any extent, is a sin. However, since every law has an exception, the final verse under discussion says: "But if the executor fears deviation from the testator—whether this deviation is unintentional or intentional and conscious—and settles the matter between them, there is no sin upon him (and he is not subject to the law of altering the will). Indeed, Allah is Forgiving, Merciful" (فَمَنْ خافَ مِنْ مُوصٍ جَنَفاً أَوْ إِثْماً فَأَصْلَحَ بَيْنَهُمْ فَلا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Therefore, the exception only relates to cases where the will was not made in a proper manner; it is only here that the executor has the right to change it. Of course, if the testator is alive, he points out the issues to him so he may change it, and if he has passed away, he personally takes action to change it. In Islamic jurisprudence, this is limited to the following cases:

  1. Whenever the will is for an amount exceeding one-third of the total wealth, because it has been narrated in numerous traditions from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام that a will is permissible up to one-third of the wealth, and anything beyond that is forbidden [1]. Therefore, what is common among uninformed individuals—distributing all their property through a will—is by no means correct according to Islamic laws,

[1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 13, p. 361 (Kitab Ahkam al-Wasaya, Bab 10).

and it is necessary for the executor to correct it and reduce it to the limit of one-third.

  1. Where the will is for injustice, sin, or a wrongful act, such as bequeathing a portion of his wealth to be spent on developing centers of corruption, or if the will causes the abandonment of an obligatory duty.

  2. That which causes dispute, corruption, and bloodshed; here, it must be corrected under the supervision of the religious judge (Hakim al-Shar').

Additionally, the expression "Janaf" (جنف), which means deviation from the truth and one-sided inclination, refers to deviations that affect the testator unintentionally, while the expression "Ithm" (إثم) refers to intentional deviations.

The phrase "Indeed, Allah is Forgiving, Merciful" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ) at the end of the verse may point to the fact that whenever the executor, through effective action, corrects a wrongful act committed by the testator and returns him to the path of truth, Allah will also overlook his error.

Key Points

1. The Philosophy of the Will

From the law of inheritance, only a certain group of relatives benefit according to a specific calculation, while perhaps other relatives, and occasionally some close friends and acquaintances, have an urgent need for financial assistance.

Furthermore, in the case of some heirs, sometimes the inheritance amount does not meet their needs. The comprehensiveness of Islamic laws does not allow these gaps to remain unfilled; therefore, alongside the law of inheritance, It placed the law of the will and allows Muslims to decide regarding one-third of their property (for after their death).

Beyond this, sometimes a person wishes to perform charitable acts, but during his

lifetime, he is unable to do so due to his own financial needs. The logic of reason dictates that he should not be deprived of using the wealth he worked hard to acquire for these charitable deeds, at least after his death.

The sum of these matters led to the legislation of the law of the will in Islam, and It emphasized it with the phrase "a duty upon the righteous" (حَقًّا عَلَى الْمُتَّقِينَ).

Of course, the will is not limited to the above cases; rather, a person must clarify the status of his debts, the trusts (Amanat) entrusted to him, and the like in the will, such that no ambiguous matter remains regarding the rights of people or the rights of Allah that are upon him.

In Islamic narrations, there are numerous emphases regarding the will. Among them, we read in a Hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

"It is not proper for a Muslim to spend a night except that his will is under his head" [1]. Of course, the phrase "under his head" is for emphasis, meaning the will should be ready.

In another narration, we read:

"Whoever dies without a will, dies a death of ignorance (Jahiliyyah)" 2.

2. Justice in the Will:

In Islamic narrations, considering the discussion in the above verses regarding not transgressing in the will, numerous emphases are seen on "non-injustice" (Adam al-Jawr) and "non-harm" (Adam al-Dirar) in the will. From the sum of these, it is understood that just as much as a will is a worthy and good act, transgression in it is reprehensible and among the major sins.

[1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 13, p. 352.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read:

"Whoever is just in his will is like one who gave that wealth in charity during his lifetime; and whoever is unjust in his will shall meet Allah, the Almighty, on the Day of Resurrection while He is turned away from him!" [1].

Transgression, injustice, and harm in a will occur when a person bequeaths more than a third, depriving the heirs of their legitimate right, or when he practices undue discrimination due to baseless love or hatred. Even in some cases where the heirs are in dire need, it is instructed not to bequeath even a third, but to reduce it to a fourth or a fifth 2. The subject of justice in the will has been emphasized in the words of the leaders of Islam to such an extent that we read in a Hadith:

"One of the men from the Ansar passed away, leaving behind small children. At the threshold of death, he spent his wealth in the way of Allah such that no other property remained from him. When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله became aware of this incident, he asked: 'What did you do with that man?' They said: 'We buried him.' He said: 'Had I known earlier, I would not have allowed you to bury him in the cemetery of the Muslims, for he left his small children to beg!'" [3].

3. Obligatory and Recommended Wills

Although a will is inherently among the highly emphasized recommended acts, sometimes—as we

[1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 13, p. 359. 2: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 13, p. 360. [3]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 659, entry "Wasiyyah".

noted—it takes an obligatory form. For example, if a person has been negligent in paying obligatory divine rights (Huquq Allah), or if there are trusts from people with him which he fears might be lost if he does not make a will. More importantly, sometimes a person's position in society is such that if he does not make a will, a severe blow and irreparable damage may be dealt to the healthy social or religious order. In all these cases, making a will becomes obligatory.

4. A Will is Changeable During One's Lifetime

Islamic laws do not limit the testator to what he previously bequeathed; rather, they allow him, as long as he is alive, to revise the amount, the manner of the will, and the executor, because with the passage of time, his interests and views in this regard may change.

  1. It also seems necessary to mention that a person should use his will as a means to compensate for and repair past shortcomings. Even if some relatives were unkind to him, he should show them kindness through the will. We read in narrations that the leaders of Islam would specifically make wills for relatives who treated them with unkindness and would set aside an amount for them to re-establish the broken bond of affection. They would also manumit their slaves or bequeath their freedom.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلصِّيَامُ كَمَا كُتِبَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (١٨٣)
O you who have faith! Prescribed for you is fasting as it was prescribed for those who were before you, so that you may be Godwary. (183)
أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ ۚ فَمَن كَانَ مِنكُم مَّرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِّنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُطِيقُونَهُۥ فِدْيَةٌ طَعَامُ مِسْكِينٍ ۖ فَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُۥ ۚ وَأَن تَصُومُوا۟ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (١٨٤)
That for known days. But should any of you be sick or on a journey, let it be a [similar] number of other days. Those who find it straining shall be liable to atonement by feeding a needy person. Should anyone do good of his own accord, that is better for him, and to fast is better for you, should you know. (184)
شَهْرُ رَمَضَانَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ فِيهِ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ هُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ وَبَيِّنَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَٱلْفُرْقَانِ ۚ فَمَن شَهِدَ مِنكُمُ ٱلشَّهْرَ فَلْيَصُمْهُ ۖ وَمَن كَانَ مَرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِّنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ ۗ يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ بِكُمُ ٱلْيُسْرَ وَلَا يُرِيدُ بِكُمُ ٱلْعُسْرَ وَلِتُكْمِلُوا۟ ٱلْعِدَّةَ وَلِتُكَبِّرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا هَدَىٰكُمْ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (١٨٥)
The month of Ramadan is one in which the Quran was sent down as guidance to mankind, with manifest proofs of guidance and the Criterion. So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it, and as for someone who is sick or on a journey, let it be a [similar] number of other days. Allah desires ease for you, and He does not desire hardship for you, and so that you may complete the number and magnify Allah for guiding you, and that you may give thanks. (185)

Commentary

Fasting, the Fountainhead of Piety

Following several important Islamic rulings mentioned in the previous verses, the verses under discussion address another of these rulings, which is considered one of the most important acts of worship: fasting. Using the same emphatic tone as before, it states: "O you who have believed, fasting has been prescribed for you as it was prescribed for those before you" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الصِّيامُ كَما كُتِبَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ).
