Immediately, it explains the philosophy of this human-building and character-forming worship in a short yet profoundly meaningful sentence: "that you may become righteous" (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ).

Indeed, fasting—as will be explained—is an effective factor in nurturing the spirit of piety and God-wariness in all fields and dimensions.

Since performing this act of worship involves deprivation of material pleasures and certain difficulties—especially during the summer season—various expressions are used in the above verse to prepare the human soul to accept this ruling.

First, it begins with the address: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (O believers!). Then, it states the fact that fasting is not exclusive to you, but existed in previous nations as well.

Finally, by stating its philosophy and the fact that the fruitful effects of this divine obligation return one hundred percent to yourselves, it makes it a beloved and pleasant subject.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is narrated that he said: "The sweetness of the address 'O you who have believed' is such that it has removed the hardship and toil of this worship!" [^1]

In the next verse, to further reduce the perceived weight of fasting, He mentions several other instructions in this regard. First, He says: "Fasting is for a fixed number of days" (أَيَّاماً مَعْدُوداتٍ).

It is not as if you are forced to fast the entire year or a major part of it; rather, fasting occupies only a small portion of it.

Furthermore: "Whoever among you is ill or on a journey [and finds fasting difficult] is exempt from this ruling and must fast an equal number of other days instead" (فَمَنْ كانَ مِنْكُمْ مَرِيضاً أَوْ عَلى‌ سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ).

Third: "Those who can only fast with extreme difficulty (such as the elderly

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

and the chronically ill for whom there is no hope of recovery) are not required to fast at all; instead, they must pay a ransom (fidyah) by feeding a needy person" (وَعَلَى الَّذِينَ يُطِيقُونَهُ فِدْيَةٌ طَعامُ مِسْكِينٍ) [^1]. "And whoever volunteers more good [in feeding the poor] it is better for him" (فَمَنْ تَطَوَّعَ خَيْراً فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَهُ) [^2]. Finally, at the end of the verse, He reveals this reality: "And to fast is better for you, if only you knew" (وَأَنْ تَصُومُوا خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ).

Although some have tried to take this sentence as evidence that fasting was an optional obligation at the beginning of its legislation—meaning Muslims could choose to fast or pay a "fidyah" to gradually get used to fasting before it became an absolute obligation—

[^1]: "Yutiqunahu" is derived from the root "tawq," which originally means a ring placed around the neck or one that exists naturally (like the colored ring on the neck of some birds). Later, it came to mean the utmost limit of one's ability. The phrase "yutiqunahu" here, considering the pronoun at the end refers to fasting, means they must exert their absolute maximum effort to fast, meaning it causes them extreme hardship; this applies to the elderly and the incurably ill. Therefore, they are exempt from the ruling of fasting and only pay a fidyah (however, those ill who recover are obliged to make up the fast). Some have also said the meaning of "yutiqunahu" is that they were able in the past (kanu yutiqunahu) but are no longer able (this meaning also appears in some narrations). In any case, the above ruling has not been abrogated and remains in force today. The claim by some that fasting was initially optional is not consistent with the context of the verse, and we have no evidence for it.

[^2]: Some consider the phrase "tatawwa'a khayran" to refer to recommended fasts, while others say the meaning is that, given the importance and philosophy of fasting, one should perform it out of desire and inclination, not out of reluctance or compulsion.

this ruling was not abrogated. Rather, it appears that this sentence is another emphasis on the philosophy of fasting, and that this worship—like all other acts of worship—adds nothing to the glory and majesty of Allah; rather, all its benefits and advantages return to the worshippers themselves.

Evidence for this is found in similar expressions in other verses of the Quran, such as: "That is better for you, if only you knew," which is mentioned after the ruling on the obligation of Friday prayer (Surah Al-Jumu'ah, Verse 9).

And in Surah Al-Ankabut we read: "And [We sent] Abraham, when he said to his people, 'Worship Allah and fear Him. That is better for you, if only you knew.'"

Thus, it becomes clear that the phrase "And to fast is better for you" is addressed to all fasters, not a specific group among them.

The last verse under discussion explains the timing of fasting and some of its rulings and philosophies. First, it says: "The few numbered days in which you must fast is the month of Ramadan" (شَهْرُ رَمَضانَ). "The month in which the Quran was revealed" (الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ فِيهِ الْقُرْآنُ).

The same Quran that is "a guidance for the people and clear proofs of guidance and the criterion [between right and wrong]" (هُدىً لِلنَّاسِ وَ بَيِّناتٍ مِنَ الْهُدى‌ وَ الْفُرْقانِ).

Then, once again, it reiterates the ruling for travelers and the sick as an emphasis: "Whoever among you is present [at home] during the month shall fast it; but whoever is ill or on a journey, then an equal number of other days" (فَمَنْ شَهِدَ مِنْكُمُ الشَّهْرَ فَلْيَصُمْهُ وَ مَنْ كانَ مَرِيضاً أَوْ عَلى‌ سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ) [^1]. The repetition of the ruling for the traveler and the sick in this verse and the previous one may be because some, thinking that breaking the fast is never a good thing, might insist on fasting while ill or traveling. Therefore, by repeating this ruling, the Quran wants to make Muslims understand that just as fasting is a divine obligation for healthy individuals, breaking the fast is also a divine command for the sick and travelers, and opposing it is a sin.

In the final part of the verse, it returns to the philosophy of the legislation of fasting, saying:

"Allah intends for you ease and does not intend for you hardship" (يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ بِكُمُ الْيُسْرَ وَ لا يُرِيدُ بِكُمُ الْعُسْرَ).

This points to the fact that although fasting appears to be a type of strictness and limitation, its ultimate result is ease and comfort for man, both spiritually and materially (as will be discussed in the philosophy of fasting).

This sentence may also point to the fact that Divine commands are not like the commands of tyrannical rulers; rather, in cases where performing them causes severe hardship, He provides an easier duty. Thus, He has removed the ruling of fasting, despite all its importance, from the sick, travelers, and the frail.

Then He adds: "The goal is that you complete the number [of days]" (وَ لِتُكْمِلُوا الْعِدَّةَ).

[^1]: Some commentators have interpreted the phrase "man shahida minkumu al-shahr" as referring to the sighting of the crescent moon, saying that whoever sees the moon, fasting becomes obligatory for them. However, this interpretation seems very unlikely; the correct view is what was stated above, which is consistent with the preceding and following sentences of the verse and is also mentioned in the narrations of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (ع).

This means it is necessary for every healthy person to fast one month in a year, as it is necessary for the cultivation of their body and soul. For this reason, if you were ill or traveling during the month of Ramadan, you must make up the fast for that number of days so that the said number is completed. Even menstruating women, who are exempt from making up prayers, are not exempt from making up the fasts.

And in the final sentence, He says: "And that you may magnify Allah for having guided you, and perhaps you will be grateful" (وَ لِتُكَبِّرُوا اللَّهَ عَلى‌ ما هَداكُمْ وَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ).

Yes, you must rise to the station of magnifying the Lord for all that guidance, and be grateful for all the blessings He has bestowed upon you.

It is noteworthy that the issue of gratitude is mentioned with the word "la'alla" (perhaps), but the issue of magnifying the Lord is mentioned decisively. This difference in expression may be because performing this worship (fasting) is, in any case, a magnification of the Lord's station. However, gratitude—which is the utilization of blessings in their proper place and benefiting from the practical effects and philosophies of fasting—has conditions that are not realized until those conditions are met. The most important of those conditions are complete sincerity, understanding the reality of fasting, and awareness of its philosophies.

Key Points

1- The Educational, Social, and Health Effects of Fasting

Fasting has various dimensions and leaves numerous effects on the human being, both materially and spiritually. The most important of all is its "ethical dimension" and educational philosophy.

One of the important benefits of fasting is that it "refines" the human soul, "strengthens" the human will, and "moderates" his instincts.

While fasting, despite hunger and thirst, the faster must abstain from food and water as well as sexual pleasure, and practically prove that he is not like an animal confined to a stable and fodder. He can take the reins of the rebellious self and master his whims and desires.

In reality, the greatest philosophy of fasting is this spiritual and psychological effect. A person who has various foods and drinks at his disposal and goes for them whenever he is thirsty or hungry is like trees that grow behind garden walls on the banks of rivers. These pampered trees have very little resistance and durability; if water is cut off from them for a few days, they wither and dry up.

But trees that grow between rocks in the heart of mountains and deserts, whose branches have been caressed since infancy by harsh storms, scorching sun, and winter cold, and who struggle with all kinds of deprivations, are strong, durable, resilient, hardworking, and tough!

Fasting does the same to the human soul and spirit. Through temporary limitations, it grants him resilience, willpower, and the ability to combat difficult events. And because it controls rebellious instincts, it sprinkles light and purity upon the human heart.

In short, fasting elevates man from the animal realm and ascends him to the world of angels. The phrase "that you may become righteous" (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ) refers to all these realities.

Also, the famous Hadith: "Fasting is a shield against the Fire" refers to this very subject. [^1] In another Hadith from Ali عليه السلام, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was asked:

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 96, p. 256.

"What should we do so that Satan stays away from us?" He replied: "Fasting blackens his face, and charity in the way of Allah breaks his back, and loving for the sake of Allah and persistence in righteous deeds cuts off his tail, and seeking forgiveness cuts his heart's vein!" [^1] In Nahj al-Balagha, when Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام explains the philosophy of acts of worship, he says regarding fasting: "And [Allah prescribed] fasting as a trial for the sincerity of the creation." [^2] And in another Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Indeed, Paradise has a gate called 'al-Rayyan' (the well-watered) through which none but those who fast shall enter." The late Saduq writes in "Ma'ani al-Akhbar" in the explanation of this Hadith that the choice of this name for this gate of Paradise is because the greatest hardship for the faster comes from thirst; when the fasters enter through this gate, they will be so quenched that they will never be thirsty again. [^3]

The Social Effect of Fasting

It is hidden from no one. Fasting is a lesson in equality and parity among the members of society. By performing this religious instruction, well-to-do individuals tangibly realize the condition of the hungry and the deprived of society, and

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 96, p. 255. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 252. [^3]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 96, p. 252.

by saving on their daily food, they can hasten to their aid.

Of course, it is possible to make the satiated aware of the state of the hungry by describing their condition, but if this issue takes on a sensory and objective aspect, it has a different effect. Fasting gives a sensory color to this important social issue. Therefore, in a famous Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is narrated that Hisham ibn al-Hakam asked about the reason for the legislation of fasting. The Imam عليه السلام replied: "Allah made fasting obligatory so that the rich and the poor would be made equal. This is because the rich would not otherwise feel the touch of hunger to have mercy on the poor, for the rich can obtain whatever they want. So Allah the Exalted desired to make His creation equal, and to make the rich taste the touch of hunger and pain, so that they might feel pity for the weak and have mercy on the hungry." [^1] Truly, if the wealthy countries of the world were to fast for a few days a year and taste the flavor of hunger, would there still be so many hungry people in the world?!

The Health and Therapeutic Effect of Fasting

In modern medicine as well as ancient medicine, the miraculous effect of "abstinence" in treating various diseases has been proven and is undeniable. There is hardly a physician who has not mentioned this reality in his writings, for we know that the cause of many diseases

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 7, Chapter 1 of the Book of Fasting, p. 3.

is overindulgence in eating various foods. Because excess materials, unabsorbed, remain as troublesome fats in various parts of the body, or as excess fat and sugar in the blood, these extra materials within the muscles are actually foul-smelling swamps for breeding various infectious disease microbes. In this state, the best way to combat these diseases is to destroy these swamps through abstinence and fasting!

Fasting burns the waste and excess unabsorbed materials of the body and, in fact, "house-cleans" the body.

Furthermore, it is a significant type of rest for the digestive system and an effective factor for servicing it. Considering that this system is one of the most sensitive systems of the body and is constantly working throughout the year, this rest is of the utmost necessity for it.

It is obvious that a fasting person, according to Islamic instructions, should not overindulge in food at the time of "Iftar" (breaking the fast) and "Suhoor" (pre-dawn meal), so as to gain the full benefit of this health effect; otherwise, the result may be the opposite.

"Alexey Suvorin," a Russian scientist, writes in his book: "Treatment through fasting has special benefits for treating anemia, intestinal weakness, simple and chronic inflammation, external and internal boils, tuberculosis, sclerosis, rheumatism, gout, dropsy, neurasthenia, sciatica, skin peeling, eye diseases, diabetes, skin diseases, kidney and liver diseases, and other illnesses. Treatment through abstinence is not limited to the above diseases; rather, it also heals diseases related to the fundamental principles of the human body that are mixed with the body's cells, such as: cancer, syphilis, tuberculosis, and the plague!" [^1]

[^1]: "Fasting: A Modern Method for Treating Diseases," p. 65, 1st Edition.

In a famous Hadith, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of Islam says: "Fast, so that you may become healthy." [^1] And in another famous Hadith also from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "The stomach is the house of every pain, and abstinence is the head of every remedy!" (Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 14, old edition).

2- Fasting in Previous Nations

It also appears from the current Torah and Gospel that fasting existed among the Jews and Christians, and other tribes and nations would fast when faced with grief and sorrow. As stated in the "Dictionary of the Holy Bible": "General fasting at all times, among every tribe, nation, and religion, was customary at the onset of unexpected sorrow and hardship." [^2] It also appears from the Torah that Moses عليه السلام fasted for forty days, as we read: "When I went up the mountain to receive the stone tablets, the tablets of the covenant that the Lord made with you, I stayed on the mountain forty days and forty nights; I ate no bread and drank no water." [^3] Likewise, at the time of repentance and seeking the pleasure of God, the Jews would fast: "The Jewish people often fasted when they found the opportunity and wanted to show humility and modesty in the presence of God, so that they might confess their sins and, through fasting and repentance, obtain the pleasure of the Holy Presence of God." [^4]

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 96, p. 255. [^2]: Dictionary of the Holy Bible, p. 427. [^3]: Torah, Deuteronomy, Chapter 9, No. 9. [^4]: Dictionary of the Holy Bible, p. 428.

"The Great Fast with atonement was likely the only specific annual one-day fast that was customary among the Jewish tribe. Of course, they also had other temporary fasts in memory of the destruction of Jerusalem and so on." [^1] Jesus عليه السلام also, as understood from the "Gospel," fasted for forty days: "Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil... After fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry." [^2] It also appears from the Gospel of "Luke" that the disciples of Jesus also fasted. [^3] Again, in the Dictionary of the Holy Bible, it says: "Therefore, the life of the disciples and believers of past days was a life full of denial of pleasures, countless hardships, and fasting." [^4] In this way, when the Quran says "as it was prescribed for those before you" (كَما كُتِبَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ), it has many historical evidences that are seen in the sources of other religions—even after their distortion.

3- The Distinction of the Holy Month of Ramadan

The reason the month of Ramadan was chosen for fasting is that this month has superiority over other months. In the verse under discussion, the point of its superiority is stated as being that the Quran—the book of guidance and the guide for humanity, which is the "Furqan" (Criterion), meaning that through its instructions and laws, it separates correct methods from incorrect ones and guarantees the happiness of humans—was revealed in this month. In Islamic narrations, it is also stated that all the great

[^1]: Dictionary of the Holy Bible, p. 428. [^2]: Gospel of Matthew, Chapter 4, No. 1 and 2. [^3]: Gospel of Luke, Chapter 5, No. 33-35. [^4]: Dictionary of the Holy Bible, p. 428.

divine books—the "Torah," the "Gospel," the "Psalms," the "Scrolls," and the "Quran"—were all revealed in this month.

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: "The Torah was revealed on the sixth of the holy month of Ramadan, the Gospel on the twelfth, the Psalms on the eighteenth, and the Holy Quran on the Night of Power (Laylat al-Qadr)." [^1] Thus, the month of Ramadan has always been the month of the revelation of great divine books and the month of education and training, for training and cultivation are not possible without correct teaching and instruction. The educational program of fasting must also be coordinated with the greatest and deepest awareness of divine teachings, so that it may wash the body and soul of man from the pollution of sin.

On the last Friday of the month of Sha'ban, the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله delivered a sermon to prepare his companions to welcome the holy month of Ramadan, highlighting the importance of this month as follows: "O people! The month of Allah has approached you with blessing, forgiveness, and mercy. This month is the best of months. Its days are better than other days, its nights are the best of nights, and its moments and hours are the best of hours. It is a month in which you have been invited to the banquet of Allah and have been placed among those honored by Allah. Your breaths in it are glorification (tasbih), your sleep in it is worship, your deeds in it are accepted, and your prayers in it are answered. Therefore, ask Allah your Lord with sincere intentions and pure hearts to grant you success in

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 7, Chapters on the Rulings of the Month of Ramadan, Chapter 18, Hadith 16.

fasting and reciting the Quran during this month, for the wretched one is he who is deprived of divine forgiveness in this great month. With your hunger and thirst in it, remember the hunger and thirst of the Resurrection. Give charity to your poor and needy, honor your elders, have mercy on your young ones, and strengthen your ties of kinship. Restrain your tongues from sin, close your eyes to what is not permissible to look at, and withhold your ears from what is forbidden to hear. Show compassion and love to the orphans of the people, so that your orphans may be treated likewise..." [^1]

4- The Rule of "No Hardship" (La Haraj)

In the above verses, there was an indication that Allah intends ease for you and does not want you to fall into hardship. Certainly, this indication here refers to the issue of fasting, its benefits, and the ruling for the traveler and the sick. However, given its general nature, a universal rule regarding all Islamic rulings is derived from it, and it is one of the documents for the famous rule

[^1]: He said: "O people! Indeed, the month of Allah has approached you with blessing, mercy, and forgiveness; a month which is, in the sight of Allah, the best of months, its days are the best of days, its nights are the best of nights, and its hours are the best of hours. It is a month in which you have been invited to the banquet of Allah and have been made of the people of Allah's honor. Your breaths in it are tasbih, your sleep in it is worship, your deeds in it are accepted, and your prayers in it are answered. So ask Allah your Lord with sincere intentions and pure hearts to grant you success in its fasting and the recitation of His Book, for the wretched one is he who is deprived of Allah's forgiveness in this great month. And remember by your hunger and thirst in it the hunger of the Day of Resurrection and its thirst. Give charity to your poor and needy, respect your elders, have mercy on your young ones, maintain your ties of kinship, guard your tongues, and lower your gazes from what is not permissible to look at, and your ears from what is not permissible to listen to, and be kind to the orphans of the people so that your orphans may be treated with kindness..." (Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 7, Hadith 20, Chapter 18 of the Chapters on the Rulings of the Month of Ramadan).

of "La Haraj" (No Hardship).

This rule states: The foundation of Islamic laws is not based on strictness, and if a ruling produces severe hardship in a certain instance, it is temporarily lifted. As the jurists have stated: "Whenever performing ablution (wudu) or standing during prayer and the like causes a person extreme difficulty, it changes to dry ablution (tayammum) and sitting prayer."

In Verse 78 of Surah Al-Hajj, we also read: "He has chosen you and has not placed upon you in the religion any hardship" (هُوَ اجْتَباكُمْ وَ ما جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ).

The famous Hadith of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "I was sent with a lenient and easy law" also refers to this same point.

Ayah

وَإِذَا سَأَلَكَ عِبَادِى عَنِّى فَإِنِّى قَرِيبٌ ۖ أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ ٱلدَّاعِ إِذَا دَعَانِ ۖ فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لِى وَلْيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِى لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْشُدُونَ (١٨٦)
When My servants ask you about Me, [tell them that] I am indeed nearmost. I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me. So let them respond to Me, and let them have faith in Me, so that they may fare rightly. (186)

Circumstances of Revelation

Someone asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Is our Lord near, so that we may confide in Him in a whisper? Or is He far, so that we must call upon Him with a loud voice?" The above verse was revealed and (answered them that God is near to His servants). [^1]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse in question.

Commentary

A Weapon Named Supplication and Prayer

Since one of the means of communication between servants and Allah is the matter of supplication (Du'a) and prayer, following the mention of a significant portion of Islamic rulings in the preceding verses, the verse under discussion speaks of it. Although it contains a general program for all those who wish to commune with Allah, its placement among the verses related to fasting gives it a fresh meaning, for the spirit of every worship is proximity to Allah and secret conversation with Him.

This verse addresses the Prophet, saying: "And when My servants ask you concerning Me, then surely I am near" (وَ إِذا سَأَلَكَ عِبادِي عَنِّي فَإِنِّي قَرِيبٌ).

Nearer than you can imagine, nearer to you than yourselves, and nearer than your jugular veins, as we read elsewhere: "And We are nearer to him than his jugular vein" (Surah Qaf, Verse 16).

Then He adds: "I answer the prayer of the suppliant when he calls on Me" (أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ).

"Therefore, My servants should accept My invitation" (فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا لِي).

"And believe in Me" (وَ لْيُؤْمِنُوا بِي).

"So that they may find their way and reach their destination" (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْشُدُونَ).

It is interesting that in this short verse, Allah has referred to His Pure Essence seven times and to His servants seven times! In this way, He has visualized the ultimate closeness, proximity, connection, and His love toward them.

"Abdullah ibn Sinan" says: I heard Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) say:

"Supplicate much, for supplication is the key to Allah's forgiveness and the means to attaining every need. There are blessings and mercies with the Lord that cannot be reached except through supplication! And know that whichever door you knock upon, it will eventually be opened!" [^1] Yes, He is near to us; how could He be far from us when His place is between us and our hearts! "And know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart" (Surah al-Anfal, Verse 24).

Key Points

1- The Philosophy of Supplication and Prayer

Those who have not recognized the reality and spirit of supplication and its educational and psychological effects raise various objections to the matter of Du'a:

Sometimes they say: Supplication is a narcotic factor, because instead of activity, effort, and utilizing the means of progress and victory, it sends people toward prayer and teaches them to pray instead of all those efforts! And sometimes they say: Fundamentally, is supplicating not an interference in Allah's work? Allah does whatever He deems beneficial; He loves us and knows our interests better than we do, so why should we ask Him for something every hour according to our own desires? And at times they say: Beyond all this, does supplication not contradict the station of contentment (Rida) and submission to the will of Allah?

Those who raise such objections are heedless of the psychological, social, educational, and spiritual effects of supplication and prayer. For man needs a pillar to strengthen his will and eliminate distress; supplication lights the lamp of hope within man.

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 468.

A people who forget supplication and prayer will face undesirable psychological and social reactions.

In the words of a famous psychologist:

"The lack of prayer among a nation is equal to the fall of that nation! A society that has killed the need for prayer within itself will usually not be immune to corruption and decline. Of course, one must not forget that praying only in the morning and spending the rest of the day like a savage is futile; prayer must be performed continuously and with mindfulness at all times so that it does not lose its deep effect on man." [^1]

Those who attribute a narcotic effect to supplication have not understood the meaning of Du'a. For the meaning of supplication is not to abandon natural means and causes and reach out in prayer instead; rather, the intent is that after we have exerted our utmost effort in using all available means, where our hands fall short and we reach a dead end, we turn to supplication. By turning to and relying on Allah, we revive the spirit of hope and movement within ourselves and seek help from the boundless assistance of that Great Origin.

Therefore, supplication is specific to inadequacies and dead ends, not a factor to replace natural causes.

"While prayer produces tranquility, it also stimulates a kind of inner blossoming and expansion in man's mental activities, and sometimes a spirit of heroism and bravery. Prayer manifests its qualities with very distinct and unique signs: clarity of gaze, serenity of behavior, inner expansion and joy, a face full of certainty, the capacity for guidance, and also welcoming events—these are what tell of a hidden treasure in the depths of our soul. Under this power, even backward and less talented people can better utilize their intellectual and moral strength and benefit more from it. But unfortunately, in our world, those who know prayer in its true face are very few." [^1]

From what we have said, the answer to the objection that supplication is contrary to "contentment and submission" also becomes clear. For supplication, as explained above, is a type of acquiring the capacity to receive a larger share of the Lord's infinite grace.

In other words, through supplication, man finds greater focus and merit to perceive Allah's grace. It is obvious that striving for perfection and acquiring greater merit is the very essence of submission to the laws of creation, not something contrary to it.

Above all, "supplication" is a form of worship, humility, and servitude, and through it, man finds a fresh focus on the Essence of Allah. Just as all acts of worship have an educational effect, supplication will also have such an effect.

And as for those who say: "Supplication is interference in Allah's work! And Allah does whatever is beneficial," they do not realize that Divine boons are distributed according to capacities and merits. The greater the capacity and merit, the larger the share of those boons man receives.

Hence, we see Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) saying:

"Indeed, with Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, there are ranks that cannot be reached except through asking (supplication)!" [^2]

A scholar says: "When we pray, we connect and link ourselves to the inexhaustible power that binds the entire universe together." [^3]

[^1]: "Prayer" by the famous physician and psychologist Alexis Carrel. [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 338, Chapter on the Virtue of Supplication and Encouragement toward it, Hadith 3. [^3]: How to Stop Worrying and Start Living (Persian translation: "A'in-e Zendegi"), p. 156.

He also says: "Today, the newest science, namely psychiatry, teaches the same things that the prophets taught. Why? Because mental health physicians have discovered that prayer, salat, and having a firm faith in religion eliminate anxiety, distress, excitement, and fear, which cause more than half of our troubles." [^1]

2- The Real Concept of Supplication

After we have understood that supplication is for instances where our power is inadequate, not for instances of ability and strength—in other words, the answered prayer is the one described by the verse: "Or, Who listens to the (soul) distressed when it calls on Him, and Who relieves its suffering" (Surah al-Naml, Verse 62), performed at the time of desperation and when all efforts and attempts have proven futile—it becomes clear that the concept of supplication is a request for the provision of causes and factors that are outside the circle of human power, from One whose power is infinite and for whom every matter is easy.

However, this request should not merely be issued from the human tongue; rather, it should arise from his entire being, and the tongue in this part should be the representative and translator of all the particles of man's existence and his limbs.

The heart and soul should find a close bond with Allah through supplication, and like a drop that joins the boundless ocean, find a spiritual connection with that Great Origin of power. We will soon examine the effects of this spiritual connection and bond.

Of course, it must be noted that we have another type of supplication that is performed even in cases of ability and power. That is the supplication which demonstrates the lack of independence of our powers against the power of the Lord. In other words, its concept is attention to the reality that natural causes and factors, whatever they have, they have from Him and are under His command. If we seek medicine and look for healing from it, it is because He has granted that effect to the medicine (this is another type of supplication mentioned in Islamic hadiths).

In short, supplication is a type of self-awareness, an awakening of the heart and thought, and an inner bond with the Origin of all goodness and virtues. Hence, we read in the words of Imam Ali (عليه السلام):

"Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, does not accept the supplication of a distracted heart." [^1] And in another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) to the same effect: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, does not answer a supplication from the back of a heedless heart." [^2]

3- Conditions for the Fulfillment of Supplication

Attention to the nature of these conditions also clarifies new realities regarding the seemingly complex issue of supplication and reveals its constructive effect. In Islamic narrations, we read conditions for the fulfillment of supplication, including:

1- For the fulfillment of supplication, one must first and foremost strive for the purity of heart and soul, repent from sin, engage in self-rectification, and take inspiration from the lives of Divine leaders.

It is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) that he said: "Beware that none of you asks his Lord for anything of the needs of this world or the hereafter until he begins by praising Allah, extolling Him, sending blessings upon the Prophet and his family, then confessing his sin (and repenting), then asking." [^3]

[^1]: How to Stop Worrying and Start Living, p. 152. [^1] and [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 342, Chapter on Attentiveness in Supplication, Hadith 1. [^3]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, pp. 448-449.

2- One should strive to keep his life pure from usurped property, injustice, and oppression, and his nourishment should not be from the forbidden (Haram). It is narrated from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Whoever loves for his supplication to be answered, let him purify his food and his earning." [^1]

3- One should not refrain from fighting against corruption and inviting toward the Truth, for those who abandon "Enjoining the Good and Forbidding the Evil" do not have their prayers answered. As narrated from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله): "You must enjoin the good and forbid the evil, or else Allah will set the wicked among you over the virtuous among you, and the virtuous will supplicate but they will not be answered!" [^2]

In reality, abandoning this great duty of "national oversight" creates disorders in society, the result of which is leaving the social arena open for evildoers, and supplication to remove its consequences is ineffective because this situation is the definite result of man's own actions.

4- Fulfilling Divine Covenants—Faith, righteous deeds, trustworthiness, and integrity are other conditions for the fulfillment of supplication. For he who does not fulfill his covenant toward his Lord should not expect to be included in the promise of answered supplication from the Lord.

Someone complained to the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), about the lack of fulfillment of his prayer, saying: "Even though Allah has said 'Call upon Me, I will answer you,' why do we supplicate and it is not answered?" He replied:

"Indeed, your hearts have betrayed in eight traits:

[^1] and [^2]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, pp. 448-449.

First: You have known Allah but you have not fulfilled His right as He made obligatory upon you; thus, your knowledge did not benefit you at all.

Second: You believed in His Messenger then you opposed his Sunnah and killed his Sharia; so where is the fruit of your faith?

Third: You read His Book revealed to you but you did not act upon it; you said 'We heard and we obeyed' then you opposed!

Fourth: You said you fear the Fire, yet at every moment you advance toward it by your sins; so where is your fear?

Fifth: You said you desire Paradise, yet at every moment you do that which distances you from it; so where is your desire for it?

Sixth: You ate the blessing of the Master but did not offer thanks for it!

Seventh: Allah commanded you to be enemies of Satan and said: 'Surely Satan is an enemy to you, so take him as an enemy,' yet you befriended him in word and allied with him without opposition.

Eighth: You placed the faults of people before your eyes and your own faults behind your backs, blaming those whom you are more deserving of blame than. So what prayer will be answered for you with this, when you have blocked its doors and paths? So fear Allah, rectify your deeds, purify your inner secrets, and enjoin the good and forbid the evil, then He will answer your supplication." [^1]

This meaningful hadith explicitly states: Allah's promise to answer supplication is a conditional promise, not an absolute one—conditioned upon you fulfilling your promises and covenants, whereas you have broken the covenant in eight ways. If you end this breach of covenant, your prayer will be answered.

Acting upon the eight instructions above, which are in fact the conditions for the fulfillment of supplication, is sufficient for the education of man and the utilization of his powers in a constructive and fruitful path.

5- Another condition for the fulfillment of supplication is its accompaniment by action, effort, and striving. In the short sayings of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), we read: "The suppliant without action is like an archer without a bowstring!" (Nahj al-Balagha, Wisdom 337).

Considering that the "bowstring" (Watar) is the factor of movement and the means of propelling the arrow toward the target, the role of "action" in the effectiveness of "supplication" becomes clear.

The sum of the five conditions above clarifies the reality that supplication should not only not replace natural causes and ordinary means for reaching a goal, but for its fulfillment, a total transformation must occur in the life programs of the suppliant, his psyche must be rebuilt, and previous actions must be reconsidered. Is labeling supplication as a "narcotic" under such conditions not a sign of ignorance or the exercise of bias?

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, pp. 448-449.

Ayah

أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ لَيْلَةَ ٱلصِّيَامِ ٱلرَّفَثُ إِلَىٰ نِسَآئِكُمْ ۚ هُنَّ لِبَاسٌ لَّكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لِبَاسٌ لَّهُنَّ ۗ عَلِمَ ٱللَّهُ أَنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَخْتَانُونَ أَنفُسَكُمْ فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَعَفَا عَنكُمْ ۖ فَٱلْـَٔـٰنَ بَـٰشِرُوهُنَّ وَٱبْتَغُوا۟ مَا كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۚ وَكُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكُمُ ٱلْخَيْطُ ٱلْأَبْيَضُ مِنَ ٱلْخَيْطِ ٱلْأَسْوَدِ مِنَ ٱلْفَجْرِ ۖ ثُمَّ أَتِمُّوا۟ ٱلصِّيَامَ إِلَى ٱلَّيْلِ ۚ وَلَا تُبَـٰشِرُوهُنَّ وَأَنتُمْ عَـٰكِفُونَ فِى ٱلْمَسَـٰجِدِ ۗ تِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا تَقْرَبُوهَا ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (١٨٧)
You are permitted on the night of the fast to go into your wives: they are a garment for you, and you are a garment for them. Allah knew that you would betray yourselves, so He pardoned you and excused you. So now consort with them and seek what Allah has ordained for you, and eat and drink until the white streak becomes manifest to you from the dark streak at the crack of dawn. Then complete the fast until nightfall, and do not consort with them while you dwell in confinement in the mosques. These are Allah’s bounds, so do not approach them. Thus does Allah clarify His signs for mankind so that they may be Godwary. (187)

Occasion of Revelation

It is understood from Islamic narrations that at the beginning of the revelation of the ruling on fasting, Muslims were only permitted to eat before their nightly sleep; if someone fell asleep at night and then woke up, eating and drinking became forbidden for them.

Furthermore, at that time, sexual intercourse with spouses was absolutely prohibited during both the day and night of the month of Ramadan.

One of the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) named "Mut'im ibn Jubayr," who was a physically weak man yet still observed the fast, entered his home at the time of Iftar. While his wife went to prepare food for his breaking of the fast, exhaustion overcame him and he fell asleep. When he woke up, he said, "I no longer have the right to break my fast." In that state, he slept through the night and in the morning, while fasting, he went to the outskirts of Medina to dig the trench (on the eve of the Battle of Ahzab). During the labor and effort, he fainted due to extreme weakness and hunger. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) came to his side and was deeply moved by observing his condition.

Additionally, a group of young Muslims who lacked the power of self-control had engaged in intercourse with their wives during the nights of Ramadan.

At this time, the verse was revealed, granting Muslims permission to eat throughout the entire night and to have sexual relations with their spouses.

Commentary

An Expansion in the Ruling of Fasting

As we read in the occasion of revelation, at the dawn of Islam, intercourse with spouses was absolutely forbidden during the nights and days of Ramadan, as was eating and drinking after sleeping.

This was perhaps a trial for the Muslims and also to prepare them for the acceptance of the rulings of fasting.

The verse under discussion, which includes four Islamic rulings regarding fasting and I'tikaf (spiritual retreat), first states in its opening part: "It has been made lawful for you to have sexual relations with your wives on the night of the fasts" (أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ لَيْلَةَ الصِّيامِ الرَّفَثُ إِلى‌ نِسائِكُمْ‌) [1]. Then, addressing the philosophy of this matter, it says: "They are a garment for you and you are a garment for them" (هُنَّ لِباسٌ لَكُمْ وَ أَنْتُمْ لِباسٌ لَهُنَ‌).

A garment, on one hand, protects a person from heat, cold, and the danger of objects hitting the body; on the other hand, it covers one's flaws; and thirdly, it is an adornment for the human body. This metaphor used in the above verse points to all these nuances.

Two spouses protect each other from deviations, cover each other's flaws, are a means of comfort and tranquility for one another, and each is considered an adornment for the other.

This expression perfectly clarifies the ultimate spiritual connection between man and woman and their closeness to each other, as well as their equality in this regard, because the same expression used for men is also used for women without any change.

Then the Quran explains the reason for this change in the Divine law, saying: "Allah knew that you used to deceive yourselves (and occasionally performed this act which was forbidden), so He turned to you in mercy and forgave you" (عَلِمَ اللَّهُ أَنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ تَخْتانُونَ أَنْفُسَكُمْ فَتابَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ عَفا عَنْكُمْ‌).

Indeed, so that you would not become further stained by sin, Allah, through His grace and mercy, made this program easy for you and reduced the duration of its restriction.

"Now, therefore, associate with them and seek what Allah has ordained for you" (فَالْآنَ بَاشِرُوهُنَّ وَ ابْتَغُوا ما كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ‌).

Certainly, this command does not imply obligation (wujub), but rather it is a permission granted after a prohibition, which in the terminology of the scholars of Usul is called "a command following a prohibition" (amr 'aqib al-hazr) and serves as evidence for permissibility (jawaz).

The phrase "and seek what Allah has ordained for you" (وابْتَغُوا ما كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ‌) indicates that there is no obstacle to utilizing this expansion and concession, which is in line with the laws of creation, the preservation of order, and the survival of the lineage.

Then He proceeds to state the second ruling, saying: "And eat and drink until the white thread of dawn becomes distinct to you from the black thread of night" (وَ كُلُوا وَ اشْرَبُوا حَتَّى يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكُمُ الْخَيْطُ الْأَبْيَضُ مِنَ الْخَيْطِ الْأَسْوَدِ مِنَ الْفَجْرِ).

In this way, Muslims had the right to utilize food and drink throughout the entire night, but must abstain at the rising of the dawn.

Next, He states the third ruling, saying: "Then complete the fast until the night" (ثُمَّ أَتِمُّوا الصِّيامَ إِلَى اللَّيْلِ‌).

This sentence is an emphasis on the prohibition of eating, drinking, and sexual intercourse during the days for those fasting, and it also indicates the beginning and end of the fast, which starts from the break of dawn and ends at night.

Finally, He addresses the fourth and last ruling, saying: "And do not associate with them while you are in retreat (I'tikaf) in the mosques" (وَ لا تُبَاشِرُوهُنَّ وَ أَنْتُمْ عاكِفُونَ فِي الْمَساجِدِ).

The statement of this ruling is like an exception to the previous ruling, because during I'tikaf—the minimum duration of which is three days—people fast, but during this period they have no right to sexual intercourse with women, neither during the day nor at night.

At the end of the verse, referring to all the previous rulings, He says: "These are the limits of Allah, so do not approach them" (تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ فَلا تَقْرَبُوها).

For approaching the boundary is tempting, and sometimes causes a person to cross the limit and fall into sin.

"Thus does Allah make clear His signs to the people that they may perhaps practice piety" (كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ آياتِهِ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ‌).

[^1]: Rafath (on the weight of Tabas) means speaking about sexual matters, but for this reason, it is also sometimes used for sexual intercourse itself, and in the verse under discussion, it is of this type.

Key Points

1- The Divine Limits

As we read in the above verses after the mention of parts of the rulings on fasting and I'tikaf, these rulings are described as "the limits of Allah." The boundary between the lawful (halal) and the forbidden (haram), the boundary between the prohibited and the permitted. Interestingly, He does not say "do not cross the limits," He says:

"Do not approach them!" Because approaching the boundary is tempting and sometimes causes one to cross it due to the rebellion of desires or falling into error.

For this reason, in some Islamic laws, stepping into areas that cause a person to slip into sin is forbidden, such as participating in a gathering of sin, even if one is not personally stained by that sin, or being in seclusion with a ajnabiyyah (being with a non-mahram woman in a secluded and completely private place where others have no access).

This same meaning is expressed in other Hadiths under the title of "protecting the sanctuary" (keeping the perimeter of the forbidden zone):

The Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) says:

"Indeed, the sanctuary of Allah is His prohibitions; whoever grazes his sheep near the sanctuary is on the verge of falling into it." [1] Again, for this reason, individuals who are committed to the principles of piety and God-wariness not only refrain from committing prohibitions but also strive not to even step near the forbidden.

2- I'tikaf

"I'tikaf" originally means to remain confined and to stay beside something for a long time, and in Sharia terminology, it is staying in mosques for worship, the minimum of which is three days, and its condition is fasting and abandoning certain other pleasures.

This worship has a profound effect on purifying the soul and giving special attention to the Lord, and its etiquettes and conditions are mentioned in books of jurisprudence. Of course, this worship is inherently recommended (mustahabb), but in some exceptional cases, it takes the form of an obligation (wujub). In any case, the verse under discussion only mentions one of its conditions, which is the absence of intercourse with women (whether at night or during the day), specifically because of its connection and link to the issue of fasting.

3- The Rising of Dawn

"Fajr" originally means "to split," and the reason the rising of the morning is referred to as fajr is that the black curtain of night is split asunder by the appearance of the first light of morning.

In the verses under discussion, in addition to this term, the expression "until the white thread becomes distinct to you from the black thread" is used. Interestingly, we read in a Hadith that "Adiy ibn Hatim" said to the Prophet: "I had placed a black and a white string and was looking at them to distinguish the two from each other to identify the start of the fasting time!" The Prophet laughed at this statement so much that his blessed teeth became visible, and he said: "O son of Hatim! The intention is the white streak of morning from the black streak of night on the horizon, which is the beginning of the obligation of fasting." [1] Additionally, it should be noted that this expression clarifies another point, which is distinguishing the "True Dawn" (al-fajr al-sadiq) from the "False Dawn" (al-fajr al-kadhib), because:

At the end of the night, first, a very faint whiteness appears vertically in the sky, which has been likened to a fox's tail; this is the False Dawn. But shortly after that, a transparent whiteness appears horizontally along the horizon, which is like a white thread drawn alongside the black thread of night; this is the True Dawn, which is the beginning of the fast and the start of the time for the Morning Prayer, and it bears no resemblance to the False Dawn.

4- The Beginning and End is Piety

It is interesting that in the first verse related to the rulings of fasting, we read that the ultimate goal of it is piety (taqwa); this same expression appears identically at the end of the last verse as well (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ‌). This shows that all these programs are a means for cultivating the spirit of piety, self-control, the habit of avoiding sin, and a sense of responsibility toward human duties.

O Lord! We rub our foreheads in prostration at Your holy threshold for giving us this success to revise the first volume of this Tafsir and to rectify its deficiencies to the best of our ability, so that we may perhaps introduce Your great Heavenly Book, the Holy Quran, more than ever before to our Muslim brothers and sisters. O Allah! We thank You for including us in this grace to step on the path of interpreting Your great and precious words.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verse being discussed. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses being discussed.

O God! Do not deprive us of this great honor until the end of the work, so that we may be able to organize and publish the remainder of this Tafsir in the best possible manner.

O Allah! We are grateful and thankful that You turned the hearts of Your special servants toward this book, and they welcomed it so much, and perhaps they offer a good prayer for us in the heart of the nights or during the days. [1]
