Ayah

وَلَا تَأْكُلُوٓا۟ أَمْوَٰلَكُم بَيْنَكُم بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ وَتُدْلُوا۟ بِهَآ إِلَى ٱلْحُكَّامِ لِتَأْكُلُوا۟ فَرِيقًا مِّنْ أَمْوَٰلِ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (١٨٨)
Do not eat up your wealth among yourselves wrongfully, nor proffer it to the judges in order to eat up a part of the people’s wealth sinfully, while you know [that it is immoral to do so]. (188)

Commentary

The Main Outlines of Islamic Economics

This verse refers to a general and important Islamic principle that governs all economic matters; in a sense, all chapters of Islamic jurisprudence (Fiqh) regarding economics can be placed under its umbrella. For this reason, our great jurists rely on this verse in many sections of Islamic Fiqh. It states: "And do not consume one another's wealth among yourselves unjustly" (وَ لا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ بَيْنَكُمْ بِالْباطِلِ).

Regarding what is meant by "falsehood" (باطل) here, various interpretations have been mentioned. Some have considered it to mean wealth acquired through usurpation and oppression.

Others suggest it refers to wealth obtained through gambling and the like.

And some consider it a reference to wealth acquired through false oaths (and various types of fraudulent legal cases).

However, it appears that the concept of the verse is general and includes all these matters and more. Since "falsehood" (باطل)—which means that which is null, void, and perishing—encompasses them all. If in some narrations from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام it is interpreted as "false oaths," and in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام as "gambling," these are actually instances of explaining the most prominent examples.

Therefore, any form of appropriation of others' property through improper and unjust means is included in this divine prohibition.

All transactions that do not pursue a sound objective and lack a rational (intellectual) basis are covered by this verse.

This same meaning is mentioned with more detail in Surah An-Nisa, verse 29, addressing the believers: "O you who have believed, do not consume one another's wealth among yourselves unjustly but only [in lawful] business by mutual consent" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ بَيْنَكُمْ بِالْباطِلِ إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونَ تِجارَةً عَنْ تَراضٍ مِنْكُمْ).

The exception of trade accompanied by mutual consent is, in fact, the mention of a clear example of legitimate and permissible methods; it does not negate gifts, inheritance, presents, wills, and the like, as these are also legitimate rational methods.

Interestingly, some commentators have noted that the placement of the verse under discussion after the verses of fasting (verses 182-187) indicates a type of correlation between the two. There, He prohibits eating and drinking for the sake of performing a divine act of worship, and here, He prohibits the unjust consumption of people's wealth, which is another form of fasting and spiritual discipline. In reality, both are considered branches of Taqwa (piety)—the very piety introduced as the ultimate goal of fasting. [^1] It is also necessary to mention that the expression "consumption" (اكل) has a broad meaning that includes any form of appropriation; in fact, this expression is a metaphor for all types of usage, and "eating" is simply its most obvious manifestation.

Then, at the end of the verse, it points to a prominent example of "consuming wealth unjustly" (اكل مال به باطل) which some people consider their right, imagining that because they obtained it through a judge's ruling, it is theirs. It says: "And do not offer it [as bribery] to the judges so that you may consume a portion of the wealth of the people in sin while you know [it is unlawful]" (وَ تُدْلُوا بِها إِلَى الْحُكَّامِ لِتَأْكُلُوا فَرِيقاً مِنْ أَمْوالِ النَّاسِ بِالْإِثْمِ وَ أَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ). [^1] The word "tudlu" (تُدْلُوا) comes from the root "idla’," which originally means lowering a bucket into a well to draw water. This is a beautiful metaphor used when a person employs means and schemes to reach a specific goal.

There are two possibilities in the interpretation of this sentence:

First: The meaning is that they give a portion of the wealth as a gift or a bribe (both are the same here) to the judges so that they may take possession of the rest. The Quran says: Even if you have apparently seized the wealth by the judge's decree, this is still consuming wealth unjustly and is a sin.

Second: The meaning is that you should not take financial matters to the rulers for the purpose of exploitation—for instance, when a trust or the property of an orphan is with a person without witnesses, and when the party demands it, he takes them to the judge. Since the other party has no evidence or witness, he takes possession of their property by the judge's ruling. This act is also a sin and the unjust consumption of wealth.

There is no obstacle to the verse having a broad meaning that encompasses both interpretations in the phrase "la tudlu," even though each commentator has accepted one possibility here.

Interestingly, we read in a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "I am only a human being, and litigants come to me; perhaps some of you are more eloquent in their argument than others, so I judge in his favor. But for whomever I judge in favor of regarding the right of a Muslim [which is not truly his], it is but a piece of fire; so let him carry it or leave it." [^1]

Key Points Bribery, the Great Plague of Societies!

One of the great plagues that has afflicted humanity since ancient times and continues with greater intensity today is the plague of bribery. It has been and remains one of the greatest obstacles to the implementation of social justice, causing laws—which should normally protect the interests of the weak—to be used in favor of the oppressions of the powerful classes whom the law should restrict.

This is because the powerful are always able to defend their interests through their own strength; it is the weak whose interests and rights must be preserved under the protection of the law. Obviously, if the door to bribery is opened, sound laws will yield the opposite result, because it is the powerful who have the ability to pay bribes. Consequently, laws will become a new plaything in their hands to continue oppression, tyranny, and the violation of the rights of the weak.

For this reason, in any society where bribery penetrates, the fabric of their life unravels, and oppression, corruption, injustice, and discrimination permeate all their organizations, leaving nothing of the law of justice but a name. Therefore, in Islam, the issue of bribery is condemned and denounced with the utmost severity and is considered one of the major sins.

However, it is noteworthy that the ugliness of bribery causes this sinister goal to be carried out behind the veil of other deceptive phrases and titles. The bribe-taker and the bribe-giver use names such as "gift," "courtesy," "commission," "service fee," and "tip." But it is clear that these changes in name in no way change its essence; in any case, the money taken through this means is haram and illegitimate.

In Nahj al-Balagha, in the story of Ash'ath ibn Qays bringing a gift, we read that he resorted to bribery to prevail over his opponent in the court of justice of Ali عليه السلام. He brought a vessel full of delicious sweets to the door of Ali's عليه السلام house at night and called it a gift. Ali عليه السلام became enraged and said:

"May the mourning women weep for you! Have you come to deceive me through the religion of Allah? ... By Allah, if I were given the seven climes with all that is under their heavens to disobey Allah by stealing the husk of a grain of barley from the mouth of an ant, I would not do it. Your world is more insignificant to me than a chewed leaf in the mouth of a locust. What has Ali to do with perishing bounties and fleeting pleasures? ..." [^1]

Islam has condemned bribery in every shape and form. In the history of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life, we read that he was informed that one of his governors had accepted a bribe in the form of a gift. The Prophet became angry and said to him:

"Why do you take what is not rightfully yours?!"

He replied apologetically: "It was a gift, O Messenger of Allah."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "If you were to sit in your house and were not a local governor on my behalf, would people give you any gift?" Then he ordered the gift to be taken and placed in the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) and dismissed him from his post. [^1]

Islam, even to prevent a judge from falling into hidden and unseen bribes, commands that a judge should not go to the market personally, lest price discounts unconsciously affect the judge and cause him to favor the one who gave the discount during judgment. How good it would be if Muslims took inspiration from their Heavenly Book and did not sacrifice everything they have at the altar of the idol of bribery.

The issue of bribery is so important in Islam that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says regarding it: "As for bribery in judgment, it is disbelief (kufr) in Allah the Almighty." [^2] And in a famous hadith narrated from the Messenger of Allah, we read: "May Allah curse the bribe-giver, the bribe-taker, and the mediator between them." [^3]

[^1]: Adapted from Tafsir Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 1, p. 252. [^1]: The word "tudlu" is conjoined to "ta'kulu," thus its meaning becomes "la tudlu" (do not offer). [^1]: Tafsir Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 1, p. 252. [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 224. [^1]: Al-Imam Ali عليه السلام, Vol. 1, pp. 155-156. [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 12, Hadith 2, Chapter 5 of the Chapters on Forbidden Gains. [^3]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 101, p. 274, Hadith 11 (Chapter on Bribery in Judgment).

Ayah

۞ يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْأَهِلَّةِ ۖ قُلْ هِىَ مَوَٰقِيتُ لِلنَّاسِ وَٱلْحَجِّ ۗ وَلَيْسَ ٱلْبِرُّ بِأَن تَأْتُوا۟ ٱلْبُيُوتَ مِن ظُهُورِهَا وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْبِرَّ مَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ ۗ وَأْتُوا۟ ٱلْبُيُوتَ مِنْ أَبْوَٰبِهَا ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (١٨٩)
They question you concerning the new moons. Say, ‘They are timekeeping signs for the people and [for the sake of] hajj.’ It is not piety that you enter houses from their rear; rather, piety is [personified by] one who is Godwary, and enter houses from their doors and be wary of Allah, so that you may be felicitous. (189)

Occasion of Revelation

In a Hadith, we read that Mu'adh ibn Jabal came to the Holy Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and said: "People frequently ask us, what is this new moon (crescent)? And what is its benefit? (Why does the moon gradually become a full moon and then return to its original state?)" Thereupon, Allah revealed the above verse and provided them with the answer.

In another narration, it is stated that a group of Jews asked the Messenger of Allah صلي الله عليه وآله: "What is the purpose of the new moon? And what is its benefit?" The above verse was revealed and explained its material and spiritual benefits in the system of human life.

Commentary

The Natural Calendar

As mentioned in the occasion of revelation, a group had questions for the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله regarding the new moons, seeking to know the cause and the results this phenomenon entails. The Quran reflects their question in this manner: "They ask you about the new moons" (يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَهِلَّةِ).

"Ahillah" (أَهِلَّة) is the plural of "Hilal" (هلال), referring to the moon on the first and second nights. Some have said it is called "Hilal" during the first three nights, after which it is called "Qamar" (moon), while others have extended the name Hilal beyond that.

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan and some other great commentators believe that this word is originally derived from Istihlal al-Sabi, meaning the cry of an infant at the moment of birth. Subsequently, it was used for the beginning of the month. It is also used when pilgrims raise their voices for the Talbiyah (Ahalla al-qawmu bi-l-hajj).

However, from the words of Raghib in Al-Mufradat, the opposite is inferred; he considers the root of the word to be the new moon itself, from which Istihlal al-Sabi (the infant's cry) is derived.

In any case, from the phrase "Yas'alunaka" (they ask you), which is used in the present continuous tense, it is evident that this question was repeatedly put to the Messenger of Allah صلي الله عليه وآله.

Then He says: "Say: They are [means of] measuring time (natural) for the people and for the Hajj" (قُلْ هِيَ مَواقِيتُ لِلنَّاسِ وَ الْحَجِ‌).

They use it in their daily lives as well as in acts of worship that have a specific time during the year. In reality, the moon is considered a natural calendar for human beings, which people—whether literate or illiterate and in any part of the world—can utilize. Not only can the beginning, middle, and end of the month be recognized by it, but with precision, the specific nights of the month can also be identified. It is obvious that the system of human social life is impossible without a calendar—that is, an accurate and universal means for determining dates. For this reason, the Great Allah has placed this global calendar at the disposal of everyone for the organization of life.

Fundamentally, one of the distinctions of Islamic laws is that its instructions are based on natural scales, because natural scales are tools available to everyone and are not affected by the passage of time. Conversely, non-natural scales are not available to all; even in our era, not all people have been able to utilize global [standardized] scales.

Thus, we see that Islam sometimes sets the scale as a span (of the hand), sometimes a pace, sometimes a finger-joint, and sometimes the height of a person. Regarding the determination of time, it has set the sunset, the dawn, the sun passing the meridian, and the sighting of the moon as the scales.

From here, the advantage of lunar months over solar months becomes clear. Although both are derived from the movements of celestial bodies, the lunar months are observable by everyone, whereas solar months can only be determined by astronomers with their instruments—identifying, for instance, which constellation or zodiac sign the sun is positioned against in a given month.

A question arises here: Did those questioning the new moon intend to ask about the benefit of these changes, or about the mechanism of the moon's appearance and its transformations from crescent to full moon? Some commentators have accepted the first possibility and some the second, adding that since asking about the causes of its formation held no benefit for them and perhaps the answer would have been difficult for many to grasp, the Quran proceeded to explain its results to teach people to always seek the practical outcomes.

Then, in the latter part of the verse, in connection with the mention of Hajj and the determination of its season by the new moon at the beginning of the verse, it refers to one of the superstitious customs of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) regarding Hajj and forbids people from it. It says: "Righteousness is not that you enter houses from their backs, but righteousness is [the quality of] one who fears Allah. Enter houses through their doors and fear Allah so that you may be successful" (وَ لَيْسَ الْبِرُّ بِأَنْ تَأْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ ظُهُورِها وَ لكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنِ اتَّقى‌ وَ أْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ أَبْوابِها وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ‌).

Many commentators have stated that during the Jahiliyyah, when they would put on the Ihram garments, they would not enter their homes through the usual door, believing this was forbidden for a person in Ihram. Instead, they would dig a hole in the back of the house and enter only through it. They believed this act was a "good deed" because it was a departure from habit, and since Ihram is a collection of abandoning habits, it should be completed with this particular abandonment [1]. Others have said this was because they did not want to go under a roof while in Ihram, and passing through a hole in the wall was deemed better for this purpose than passing through a door. However, the Quran explicitly states that righteousness lies in Taqwa (piety/God-consciousness), not in superstitious customs, and immediately commands that you must enter houses through their normal routes.

This verse also has a broader and more general meaning: that to undertake any task, whether religious or non-religious, one must enter through the correct path, not through deviant or inverted ways. Jabir narrated this same meaning from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام [2]. From this, another link can be found between the beginning and the end of the verse: that every task must be done through its proper channel, and an act of worship like Hajj must also be performed at the prescribed time determined by the new moon.

A third interpretation has been mentioned for the verse: that to find goodness, one must seek out those qualified for it (Ahl) and not seek it from the unqualified. However, this interpretation can be included within the second one. In the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام:

"The Family of Muhammad are the doors of Allah and His paths, the callers to Paradise and the leaders toward it, and the guides to it until the Day of Resurrection." [1] This Hadith can be an allusion to one of the instances of the general concept of the verse, as it says: in all your religious affairs, enter through the original path—namely the Ahl al-Bayt of the Prophet, who according to the Hadith al-Thaqalayn are the companions of the Quran—and take your programs from them, for Divine Revelation descended in their house and they are the ones nurtured in the school of the Quran.

The phrase "Righteousness is not..." might also point to another subtle point: that your questioning about the new moons instead of asking about religious knowledge is like the act of someone who leaves the main entrance of a house and enters through a hole made in the back wall—what an ungraceful act.

Additionally, it is necessary to note that He says: "But righteousness is [the quality of] one who fears Allah" (لكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنِ اتَّقى‌), because the existence of the pious is a gushing spring of goodness, such that it is as if they themselves are the essence of righteousness [2].

[1] Tafsir al-Baydawi, under the verse in question. [2] Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse in question (Vol. 1, p. 284). [1] Ibid. [2] Some have said there is an omitted word here, and the estimation is: "But righteousness is the righteousness of one who fears Allah."

Key Points

1- Various Questions Put to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله

In fifteen instances in the Quranic verses, the phrase "Yas'alunaka" (they ask you) appears, which shows that people frequently asked the Holy Prophet various questions on diverse issues. Interestingly, the Prophet was not only unbothered by these questions but welcomed them with open arms and answered them through the Quranic verses.

Fundamentally, asking questions is one of the rights of the people over their leaders. Even enemies should be given this right to pose their questions reasonably. Questioning is the key to solving problems; it is the window to the sciences and the means of transferring knowledge.

In principle, the posing of various questions in any society is a sign of the vitality of thoughts and the awakening of minds. The existence of so many questions during the era of the Prophet is a sign of the stirring of people's thoughts in that environment under the light of the Quran and Islam.

From this, it becomes clear that those who oppose the posing of logical questions in society are acting inconsistently with the spirit of Islamic teachings.

2- The Calendar and the System of Life

Neither individual nor social life can be organized without proper order. Order in planning and order in management and execution—a single look at the world of creation, from the galaxies of the high heavens to the structure of the human body and its various organs, is sufficient to realize this general principle that governs all of creation.

On this basis, Allah provided the means for this order to humanity. He made the regular movements of the Earth around itself and around the sun, as well as the regular orbit of the moon, a means for a time-keeping system, so that the programs of life—both material and spiritual—could come under a system.

Consider what chaos would emerge throughout our lives if the specific order of day and night, the sun, and the moon did not exist and we had no scale for measuring time. Therefore, Allah mentions this as one of His important blessings in Surah Yunus, verse 5, saying: "It is He who made the sun a shining light and the moon a derived light and determined for it phases—that you may know the number of years and the account [of time]. Allah has not created this except in truth. He details the signs for a people who know."

A similar meaning appears in Surah Al-Isra, verse 12, regarding the system governing night and day [1].

[1] We have also discussed this in Volume 12 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 12 of Surah Al-Isra, and in Volume 8 under verse 5 of Surah Yunus.

Ayahs

وَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِينَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ وَلَا تَعْتَدُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ (١٩٠)
Fight in the way of Allah those who fight you, but do not transgress. Indeed Allah does not like transgressors. (190)
وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ ثَقِفْتُمُوهُمْ وَأَخْرِجُوهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ أَخْرَجُوكُمْ ۚ وَٱلْفِتْنَةُ أَشَدُّ مِنَ ٱلْقَتْلِ ۚ وَلَا تُقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ عِندَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ حَتَّىٰ يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ فِيهِ ۖ فَإِن قَـٰتَلُوكُمْ فَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٩١)
And kill them wherever you confront them, and expel them from where they expelled you, for persecution is graver than killing. But do not fight them near the Holy Mosque unless they fight you therein; but if they fight you, kill them; such is the requital of the faithless. (191)
فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٩٢)
But if they desist, then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (192)
وَقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّىٰ لَا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وَيَكُونَ ٱلدِّينُ لِلَّهِ ۖ فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟ فَلَا عُدْوَٰنَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٩٣)
Fight them until persecution is no more, and religion becomes [exclusively] for Allah. Then if they desist, there shall be no reprisal except against the wrongdoers. (193)

Circumstances of Revelation

Some commentators have narrated two circumstances of revelation for the first of these verses.

First, that this was the first verse revealed regarding fighting the enemies of Islam. After its revelation, the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله fought those who initiated combat against them and refrained from those who did not seek war. This continued until the command: فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ (kill the polytheists) was revealed, which granted permission to fight all polytheists. [^1]

The second circumstance of revelation is narrated from Ibn Abbas, stating that this verse was revealed regarding the Peace of Hudaybiyyah. The event was such that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, along with 1,400 of his companions, prepared for the Umrah pilgrimage. When they reached the land of Hudaybiyyah (a place near Mecca), the polytheists prevented them from entering Mecca and performing the Umrah rites. After much negotiation with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they reached a settlement that they would come to Mecca for Umrah the following year, and the polytheists would vacate Mecca for three days for him and the Muslims to circumambulate the House of Allah.

The following year, when they prepared to go to Mecca, they feared that the polytheists might not fulfill their promise and might obstruct them, leading to a battle. In the event of an incident or war, the Prophet was uneasy about fighting during the sacred month. Thus, the above verse was revealed, commanding that if the enemy begins the battle, you too must rise to fight against them. [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Vol. 5, p. 127. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1, p. 284 (under the discussed verse); references to these circumstances of revelation are also seen in other commentaries.

It appears that the first circumstance of revelation is suitable for the first verse, and the second is suitable for the subsequent verses. However, in any case, the context of the verses indicates that they were all revealed together or within a short interval.

Commentary

The Command to Fight the Oppressors

In the first verse, the Quran issues the command to fight and struggle against those who draw swords against Muslims, granting them permission to take up arms to silence the enemies. In other words, the period of patience and forbearance for the Muslims had ended, and they had gained sufficient strength and power to defend themselves and their rights with courage and clarity. It says: "Fight in the way of Allah those who fight you" (وَ قاتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ الَّذِينَ يُقاتِلُونَكُمْ).

The expression "in the way of Allah" (فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ) clarifies the primary objective of Islamic wars: that war in the logic of Islam is never for the sake of revenge, ambition, expansionism, obtaining spoils, or occupying the lands of others. Islam condemns all of these and states: taking up arms and engaging in Jihad must only be in the way of Allah, for the propagation of Divine laws, the expansion of Monotheism and justice, the defense of truth, and the uprooting of oppression, corruption, and depravity.

It is this point that distinguishes Islamic wars from all other wars occurring in the world. Furthermore, this objective influences all dimensions of war, giving the quantity and quality of combat, the type of weaponry, and the manner of treating prisoners the "color" of being for the sake of Allah.

According to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, "Sabil" originally means a path that is easy to traverse. Some have interpreted it exclusively as the path of truth. However, considering that this word is applied in the Holy Quran to both paths of truth and falsehood, perhaps their intention is that its truthfulness is understood from contextual clues.

There is no doubt that traversing the path of Allah (Sabil Allah)—or in other words, the path of His religion and creed—despite all its difficulties, is an easy and pleasant task because it accords with the soul and spirit of the believers. For this reason, believers welcome it voluntarily and openly, even if its end is martyrdom.

The phrase "those who fight you" (الَّذِينَ يُقاتِلُونَكُمْ) is also explicit that this command is specifically for confronting those who take up arms. Until the enemy rises to fight and struggle, Muslims must not attack. As we shall see, this principle (except in some exceptional cases to be mentioned) has been respected everywhere.

A group of commentators has limited the scope of "those who fight you" to a specific circle, whereas the verse has a broad meaning and includes all those who rise to battle in any manner.

Additionally, it is inferred from this verse that non-combatants (especially women and children) must never be attacked, as they have not risen to fight; therefore, they possess immunity.

Then, it recommends the observance of justice, even on the battlefield and against enemies, saying: "and do not transgress" (وَ لا تَعْتَدُوا).

For indeed, Allah does not love the transgressors (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُعْتَدِينَ).

Indeed, when war is for Allah and in the way of Allah, there must be no transgression or violation. It is precisely for this reason that in Islamic wars—unlike the wars of our era—the observance of numerous ethical principles is recommended. For example, individuals who lay down their weapons, those who have lost the ability to fight, or those who fundamentally lack the power to fight—such as the wounded, the elderly, women, and children—must not be transgressed against. Orchards, plants, and crops must not be destroyed, and toxic substances must not be used to poison the enemy's drinking water (chemical and biological warfare).

Ali عليه السلام—according to what is recorded in Nahj al-Balagha—gave a comprehensive instruction to his troops before the start of the Battle of Siffin, which serves as a vivid witness to our discussion:

"Do not fight them until they begin [the fight] with you, for by the grace of Allah, you possess the proof [of being on the right], and leaving them until they begin the battle is another proof for you against them. Then, when you defeat them by Allah's permission, do not kill one who flees, do not strike the helpless, do not finish off the wounded, and do not provoke women with harm, even if they insult your honor and revile your leaders." [^1]

It is also necessary to mention that some commentators, based on certain narrations, have considered this verse as an abrogation of the verse that prohibits fighting, such as: "Restrain your hands [from fighting]" (كُفُّوا أَيْدِيَكُمْ) [^2]. Others have considered it abrogated by the verse: "And fight the polytheists all together" (وَ قاتِلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ كَافَّةً) [^3]. However, the truth is that this verse is neither an abrogator nor abrogated. This is because the prohibition of war with the aggressive disbelievers was at a time when Muslims did not possess sufficient strength; with the change of conditions, they became duty-bound to defend themselves. Likewise, the issue of fighting the polytheists is actually considered an exception to the verse. Therefore, a change in ruling due to a change in conditions is neither abrogation nor an exception. However, evidence shows that in narrations and the words of the predecessors, "abrogation" (naskh) had a meaning different from today's concept, such that its broad meaning included these types of matters as well.

In the next verse, which completes the instruction of the previous verse, it speaks with greater clarity, saying: "And kill them (the same polytheists who did not refrain from any blow against the Muslims) wherever you find them, and drive them out from where they drove you out (Mecca)," because this is a just defense and a logical retaliation (وَ اقْتُلُوهُمْ حيثُ ثَقِفْتُمُوهُمْ وَ أَخْرِجُوهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَخْرَجُوكُمْ).

Then it adds: "and Fitnah (persecution/sedition) is worse than killing" (وَ الْفِتْنَةُ أَشَدُّ مِنَ الْقَتْلِ).

Regarding what "Fitnah" is, commentators and linguists have discussed it. This word is originally derived from "fatn," which according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat means placing gold in fire to reveal its quality, and according to some, placing gold in fire to purify it from impurities. [^2] The word Fitnah and its derivatives are mentioned dozens of times in the Holy Quran and are used in various meanings.

Sometimes it means trial and examination, such as: "Do people think they will be left alone because they say: 'We believe,' and they will not be tested (yuf-tanun)?" [^1]. Sometimes it means deceiving, as He says: "O children of Adam, let not Satan deceive (yaftinannakum) you" [^2]. Sometimes it appears in the sense of affliction and punishment, such as: "The Day they will be punished (yuftanun) over the Fire. Taste your punishment!" [^3]. Sometimes it means misguidance, such as: "And he for whom Allah intends misguidance (fitnatahu), you will never possess for him anything against Allah" [^4]. And sometimes it means polytheism and idolatry or obstructing the path of the believers, as in the verse under discussion and some subsequent verses which say: "And fight them until there is no more Fitnah and the religion is for Allah" (وَ قاتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّى لا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وَ يَكُونَ الدِّينُ لِلَّهِ).

However, it appears that all these meanings return to the same original root mentioned for the meaning of Fitnah (as is the case with most homonymous words). Since the original meaning is placing gold under the pressure of fire for purification or separating the genuine from the counterfeit, this word is used in every case where a type of pressure and intensity exists. Examples include an examination, which is usually accompanied by pressure and difficulties; punishment, which is another type of intensity and pressure; and deception and trickery, which are carried out under pressures; as well as polytheism or creating obstacles in the path of guiding people, each of which involves a type of pressure and intensity.

In short: the creed of idolatry and the various individual and social corruptions resulting from it had become prevalent in the land of Mecca, polluting the sacred sanctuary of Allah, and its corruption was greater than killing and slaughter. The verse under discussion says: "Do not abandon the struggle against idolatry for fear of bloodshed, for idolatry is worse than killing."

The possibility has also been raised by some commentators that "Fitnah" here refers to social corruption, including the exile of believers from their beloved homeland, as these matters are sometimes more painful than killing or cause killings and slaughter in society. In Surah Al-Anfal, verse 73, we read: "Unless you do this (severing ties with the disbelievers), there will be Fitnah on earth and great corruption" (in this verse, Fitnah and corruption are placed side by side).

Then, it points to another matter in this regard, saying: Muslims must maintain the sanctity of Masjid al-Haram, and this sacred sanctuary of Allah must always be respected. Therefore, "do not fight them near Masjid al-Haram unless they fight you there" (وَ لا تُقاتِلُوهُمْ عِنْدَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ حَتَّى يُقاتِلُوكُمْ فِيهِ).

"But if they fight you [there], then kill them; such is the recompense of the disbelievers" (فَإِنْ قاتَلُوكُمْ فَاقْتُلُوهُمْ كَذلِكَ جَزاءُ الْكافِرِينَ).

For when they break the sanctity of this secure sanctuary, silence toward them is no longer permissible, and a firm response must be given so that they never exploit the sanctity and respect of Allah's secure sanctuary.

However, since Islam always blends the "sting and the honey," warning and glad tidings, to provide a healthy educational remedy for sinners, in the next verse it opens the way of return for them and says: "But if they cease, then indeed Allah is Forgiving and Merciful" (فَإِنِ انْتَهَوْا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Indeed, if they abandon polytheism and extinguish the fire of Fitnah and corruption, they will be your Muslim brothers. Even as stated in other instructions, the punishments and indemnities intended for criminals will be waived in their case.

Some have interpreted the phrase "if they cease" (فَإِنِ انْتَهَوْا) to mean ceasing from polytheism and disbelief (as we said above). Others have taken it to mean ceasing from fighting in Masjid al-Haram or its surroundings. Combining both meanings is also possible.

In the next verse, it points to the objective of Jihad in Islam, saying: "And fight them until there is no more Fitnah" (وَ قاتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّى لا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ). "And the religion is for Allah" (وَ يَكُونَ الدِّينُ لِلَّهِ).

Then it adds: "But if they cease (from their belief in wrong actions), then there is to be no aggression except against the oppressors" (فَإِنِ انْتَهَوْا فَلا عُدْوانَ إِلَّا عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ).

Apparently, three objectives for Jihad are mentioned in this verse: eliminating Fitnah, erasing polytheism and idolatry, and preventing oppression and tyranny.

It is possible that Fitnah here means polytheism; thus, the first and second objectives would be merged. It is also possible that "oppression" (zulm) here also refers to polytheism, as we read in Surah Luqman, verse 13: "Indeed, polytheism is a great oppression" (إِنَّ الشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ).

In this way, all three objectives return to a single goal: the struggle against polytheism and idolatry, which is the source of all types of Fitnah, injustices, and oppressions.

Some have also interpreted "oppression" in this verse as being the initiator of war, or being the initiator of war in the sacred sanctuary of Allah.

However, the first possibility—that the verse pursues three separate objectives for war—seems stronger. It is true that polytheism is one of the instances of Fitnah, but "Fitnah" has a broader concept than polytheism. And it is true that polytheism is one of the instances of oppression, but "oppression" has a wider meaning, and if it is sometimes interpreted as polytheism, it is the statement of one instance.

Accordingly, Islamic Jihad is not for the sake of ruling the earth or expansionism, nor for the purpose of seizing spoils, nor for securing sales markets or possessing the vital resources of other countries, nor for granting racial superiority of one race over another.

The objective is one of three things: extinguishing the fire of Fitnah and turmoil that strips people of freedom and security; erasing the traces of polytheism and idolatry; and confronting aggressors and oppressors and defending against them.

Key Points

1- The Issue of Jihad in Islam

In many distorted religions, Jihad does not exist at all, and everything revolves around recommendations, advice, and exhortations. Some are even submerged in surprise when they hear that armed Jihad is one of the pillars of Islamic programs, wondering how a religion can be coupled with war.

However, considering that powerful, autocratic individuals—the Pharaohs, Nimrods, and Qaruns—who saw the objectives of the Prophets as an obstacle to their interests, always stood against them and were satisfied with nothing less than the erasure of Allah's religion and creed, it becomes clear that true religious people, while relying on reason, logic, and ethics, must stand against these tyrannical and oppressive rebels and open their way forward by struggling and crushing them. Fundamentally, Jihad is one of the signs of a living being and a general law in the world of life. All living beings, including humans, animals, and plants, are in a state of struggle against the factors of their destruction for their own survival. You will read a more detailed explanation of this in Surah An-Nisa under verses 95 and 96.

In any case, it is among our honors as Muslims to blend religion with the issue of governance and to have the command of Jihad in religious programs. However, one must see what objectives Islamic Jihad pursues; it is this that distinguishes us from others.

2- The Objectives of Jihad in Islam

Some Westernized individuals insist on limiting Islamic Jihad exclusively to defensive Jihad, and with whatever effort possible, they try to justify all the battles (ghazawat) of the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or the wars after him in this light. However, we have no evidence for this, nor are all the battles of the Messenger of Allah summarized in this meaning. It is better, instead of these incorrect inferences, to return to the Quran and take the objectives of Jihad from the Quran—objectives that are all logical and presentable to both friend and foe.

As we read in the above verses, Jihad in Islam is permitted for several objectives:

A- Jihad to Extinguish Fitnah

In other words, "liberating initial Jihad." We know that Allah has designed instructions and programs for the prosperity, freedom, evolution, happiness, and comfort of humans, and has duty-bound His Prophets to convey these instructions to the people. Now, if an individual or a group sees the delivery of these commands as an obstacle to their lowly interests and creates barriers in the path of the Prophets' invitation, the Prophets have the right to remove these obstacles from the path of their invitation—first through peaceful means, and if not possible, by resorting to force—to gain the freedom of propagation for themselves.

In other words: people in all societies have the right to hear the call of the callers to the path of truth and be free in accepting their invitation. Now, if some wish to deprive them of their legitimate right and not allow the voice of the callers to the path of God to reach their souls, or prevent them from being freed from the bonds of intellectual and social slavery, the supporters of this program have the right to use any means to provide this freedom. From here, the necessity of "initial Jihad" in Islam and other heavenly religions becomes clear.

Likewise, if some put believers under pressure to return to their former creed, any means can be used to remove this pressure.

B- Defensive Jihad

Is it correct for someone to attack a person and for that person not to defend himself? Or for an aggressive and hegemonic nation to launch an assault on another nation while they sit idly by and watch the destruction of their country and people?

Here, all heavenly and human laws give the person or group that has been attacked the right to rise for self-defense and use whatever is in their power, not neglecting any logical action to preserve their existence.

This type of Jihad is called "Defensive Jihad." Wars such as the Battle of the Trench (Ahzab), Uhud, Mu'tah, Tabuk, Hunayn, and some other Islamic battles were part of this section of Jihad and had a defensive aspect.

Even now, many enemies of Islam have imposed war on Muslims, occupied Islamic lands, and taken their resources under their control. How could Islam allow silence in the face of them?

C- Jihad to Support the Oppressed

Another branch of Jihad mentioned in other verses of the Quran is Jihad to support the oppressed. In Surah An-Nisa, verse 75, we read: "And what is [the matter] with you that you fight not in the cause of Allah and [for] the oppressed among men, women, and children who say, 'Our Lord, take us out of this city whose people are oppressors and appoint for us from Yourself a protector and appoint for us from Yourself a helper'?"

In this way, the Quran asks Muslims to perform Jihad both in the way of Allah and for the oppressed, and fundamentally these two are not separate. Considering that there is no restriction or condition in the above verse, these oppressed and weakened people, wherever they may be in the world, must be defended; near or far, inside or outside the country makes no difference.

In other words, supporting the oppressed against the oppressors is a principle in Islam that must be observed, even if it leads to Jihad. Islam does not allow Muslims to be indifferent to the pressures exerted on the oppressed of the world, and this instruction is one of the most valuable Islamic commands that testifies to the truthfulness of this creed.

D- Jihad to Erase Polytheism and Idolatry

While Islam respects freedom of belief and does not invite anyone to this creed by compulsion—for this reason, it gives sufficient opportunity to peoples who possess a Heavenly Book to accept the creed of Islam through study and reflection, and if they do not accept, it treats them as a "covenanted minority" (Ahl al-Dhimmah) and establishes peaceful coexistence with them under specific conditions that are neither complex nor difficult—it is nevertheless strict toward polytheism and idolatry. This is because polytheism and idolatry are neither a religion nor a creed, nor are they respected; rather, they are a type of superstition, deviation, and stupidity, and in fact, a type of intellectual and moral disease that must be uprooted at any cost possible.

The word "freedom" and "respect for the thoughts of others" are used in cases where the thought or belief has at least a correct root. However, deviation, superstition, misguidance, and disease are not things to be respected. For this reason, Islam commands that idolatry be uprooted from human society at any cost, even at the cost of war. Idol temples and the ominous traces of idolatry, if not possible through peaceful means, should be demolished and destroyed by force.

Yes, Islam says the face of the earth must be cleansed from the pollution of polytheism and idolatry, and it gives glad tidings to all Muslims that eventually Monotheism and the worship of the One will govern the entire world, and polytheism and idolatry will be uprooted.

From what has been mentioned above regarding the objectives of Jihad, it becomes clear that Islam has harmonized Jihad with correct principles and the logic of reason, and has never made it a means for hegemony, expansionism, usurping the rights of others, imposing belief, colonialism, or exploitation.

However, we know that the enemies of Islam—especially the church authorities and biased Orientalists—by distorting the truths, have delivered many speeches against the issue of Islamic Jihad and have accused Islam of violence and resorting to force and the sword to impose belief. They have severely attacked this Islamic law.

It seems that their terror of the progress of Islam in the world, due to its strong teachings and calculated programs, has caused them to create a false, terrifying image of Islam to prevent its progress in the world.

3- Why was the command of Jihad revealed in Medina?

We know that Jihad became obligatory for Muslims in the second year of the Hijrah and was not obligatory before that.

The reason for this position is clear: on one hand, the number of Muslims in Mecca was so small that an armed uprising practically meant nothing but suicide. On the other hand, the enemy in Mecca was extremely powerful and was actually considered the main center of anti-Islamic powers; fighting them inside Mecca was not possible.

However, when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله came to Medina, a large number of people believed in him, and he expanded his invitation openly inside and outside Medina. He was able to establish a righteous government and prepare the necessary means for struggle and battle with the enemy. Since Medina was a great distance from Mecca, this work was carried out with peace of mind, and the revolutionary and liberating forces became ready for struggle and defense against the enemy.

Ayah

ٱلشَّهْرُ ٱلْحَرَامُ بِٱلشَّهْرِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَٱلْحُرُمَـٰتُ قِصَاصٌ ۚ فَمَنِ ٱعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَٱعْتَدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا ٱعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٩٤)
A sacred month for a sacred month, and all sanctities require retribution. So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you, and be wary of Allah, and know that Allah is with the Godwary. (194)

Commentary

Respecting the Sacred Months and Reciprocal Retaliation

This verse completes the discussion regarding Jihad in general, which appeared in the preceding verses. In reality, it is a response to the objections of uninformed individuals who asked: "How can one fight during a sacred month, given that Islam has issued such a command?"

To explain: The polytheists of Mecca knew that fighting during the sacred months (Dhu al-Qi'dah, Dhu al-Hijjah, Muharram, and Rajab) is not permissible according to Islam. In other words, Islam had endorsed this pre-existing tradition. This act is especially more prohibited within the Masjid al-Haram and Mecca, and the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله respected both rulings.

For this reason, they intended to catch the Muslims off guard and attack them during the sacred months. Perhaps their assumption was that if they ignored the sanctity of the sacred months, the Muslims would not rise to retaliate, and if this happened, they would achieve their objective.

The verse under discussion put an end to these notions and thwarted their potential plans. It commanded that if they took up arms during the sacred months, the Muslims must stand against them. It says: "The sacred month is for the sacred month" (الشَّهْرُ الْحَرامُ بِالشَّهْرِ الْحَرامِ‌).

This means that if the enemies broke its sanctity and fought you therein, you also have the right to retaliate in kind.

"For [violation of] sacred things, there is legal retribution" (وَ الْحُرُماتُ قِصاصٌ‌).

"Hurumat" (حرمات) is the plural of "Hurmah" (حرمه), meaning something that must be protected and whose respect must be maintained. The "Haram" (Sanctuary) is called "Haram" because it is a respected place and its desecration is not permissible. Unlawful and vile acts are called "Haram" (forbidden) because they are prohibited, just as certain actions are prohibited regarding the Sanctuary or the sacred month.

This sentence is, in fact, a crushing response to those who criticized the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله for permitting fighting during the sacred months. That is, the sanctity of the sacred month, or the land of Mecca—the secure Sanctuary of Allah—is for those who respect it. However, for those who trample its sanctity, its observance is not required, and Muslims have the right to engage in battle with them. This is actually a form of legal retribution (Qisas), so that the polytheists would no longer think of exploiting the sanctity of the sacred month or the respected land of Mecca.

Then, it points to a general and universal command that also includes the subject under discussion, saying: "Whoever transgresses against you, then transgress against him in like manner as he has transgressed against you, but fear Allah (and do not exceed the limits), and know that Allah is with the righteous" (فَمَنِ اعْتَدى‌ عَلَيْكُمْ فَاعْتَدُوا عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا اعْتَدى‌ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ مَعَ الْمُتَّقِينَ‌).

Islam, unlike contemporary Christianity which says: "Whosoever shall smite thee on thy right cheek, turn to him the other also," [1] does not give such a command. This is because such a deviant instruction emboldens the oppressor and the aggressor. Even the Christians of the world today never act upon such a command; they respond to the slightest aggression with a much harsher reaction, which is also contrary to the command of Islam.

Islam says: One must stand against the aggressor, and it gives everyone the right that if they are wronged, they may retaliate to the same extent. Submission to the aggressor is equal to death, and resistance is equal to life; this is the logic of Islam.

Interestingly, the verse has a broad meaning and is not limited to the issue of retribution for murder or other crimes; rather, it includes financial matters and other rights as well.

Of course, this subject does not conflict in any way with the issue of forgiveness and pardon, which takes place regarding friends, or individuals who are defeated, or those who are remorseful and repentant.

Sometimes, some among the common folk imagine that the meaning of the verse is that if someone kills another person's child, reciprocal retaliation allows the father of the victim to kill the child of the murderer; or if someone strikes another's brother, he may strike the brother of the criminal. But this is a great mistake, for the Quran says: the aggressor and transgressor himself must be punished (punished to the same extent), not another innocent individual. Furthermore, the meaning of this statement is not that if someone burns down a person's house, they should burn down his house; rather, its meaning is that they should take the equivalent value of the house from him.

[1]: Gospel of Matthew, Chapter 5, Verse 39.

Additionally, the emphasis on Taqwa (piety/God-wariness) mentioned at the end of the verse is an emphasis on not exceeding the limit. In other words, reciprocal retaliation must not take the form of seeking vengeance—the kind of vengeance that knows no boundaries or limits.

And His saying: "Allah is with the righteous," is an indication that He does not leave them alone in difficulties and grants them aid; for whoever is with another helps him in hardships and protects him against the enemy.

Ayah

وَأَنفِقُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تُلْقُوا۟ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ إِلَى ٱلتَّهْلُكَةِ ۛ وَأَحْسِنُوٓا۟ ۛ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٩٥)
Spend in the way of Allah, and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction; and be virtuous. Indeed Allah loves the virtuous. (195)

Commentary

Spending and Deliverance from Hardships

This verse serves as a completion to the verses on Jihad whose commentary has previously passed. This is because Jihad requires wealth and resources just as much as it requires sincere and experienced men. Jihad necessitates individuals who are prepared both spiritually and physically, as well as various types of weapons and military equipment. It is true that the determining factor in the fate of a war is primarily the soldiers; however, a soldier without sufficient means and equipment (including weapons, ammunition, means of transport, food supplies, and medical equipment) can achieve nothing.

Therefore, in Islam, providing the means for Jihad against enemies is considered an obligation. Specifically, in the verse under discussion, it explicitly commands and says: "And spend in the way of Allah and do not throw [yourselves] with your [own] hands into destruction" (وَ أَنْفِقُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَ لا تُلْقُوا بِأَيْدِيكُمْ إِلَى التَّهْلُكَةِ).

Particularly during the era of the revelation of these verses, many Muslims held the passion and fervor for Jihad in their hearts, but since everyone had to personally provide their own war equipment, some were poor and needy. As described in verse 92 of Surah At-Tawbah, they would weep and shed tears because they lacked the means to participate in the war: "they turned back while their eyes overflowed with tears out of grief that they could not find something to spend." Yes, in such circumstances, the role of spending (Infaq) was extremely vital and decisive.

The phrase "وَ لا تُلْقُوا بِأَيْدِيكُمْ إِلَى التَّهْلُكَةِ" (and do not throw yourselves with your own hands into destruction), although revealed regarding the abandonment of spending for Islamic Jihad, carries a broad and extensive meaning that includes many other instances. For example, a person has no right to travel through dangerous roads (whether due to insecurity, atmospheric factors, or otherwise) without necessary precautions, or to consume food that is highly likely to be contaminated with poison, or even to act in the field of Jihad without a plan or program. In all these cases, the person has endangered their life pointlessly and is held responsible.

However, the notion held by some uninformed individuals—that any form of initiatory Jihad is "throwing oneself into destruction"—and who sometimes go so far as to consider the uprising of the Master of Martyrs, Imam Hussain عليه السلام, in Karbala as an instance of this, stems from extreme ignorance and a lack of understanding of the verse. Throwing oneself into destruction applies to situations where no goal higher than life itself is at risk; otherwise, one must sacrifice their life to preserve that sacred goal, just as Imam Hussain عليه السلام and all the martyrs in the way of Allah did.

If someone sees that the life of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is in danger and makes himself a shield to protect him (the very act Ali عليه السلام performed in the Battle of Uhud, or on the Night of Mabit when he slept in the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله bed), has such a person thrown himself into destruction or committed a wrong act? Should he sit by until the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is killed and say that throwing oneself into destruction is not permissible?

The truth is that the concept of the verse is clear, and invoking it in such cases is a form of folly.

Yes, if the goal is not important enough to be worth losing one's life for, or if it is important but has better and more suitable solutions, then one should not endanger their life (cases of permissible Taqiyyah are of this nature).

At the end of the verse, it commands toward doing good, saying: "And do good; indeed, Allah loves the doers of good" (وَ أَحْسِنُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

Regarding what is meant by "Ihsan" (doing good) here, several possibilities are seen in the words of the commentators: First, that you should have a good opinion of Allah (and not think that your spending will cause disruption to your livelihood); second, that it refers to economy and moderation in the matter of spending; and third, that it refers to blending spending with good conduct toward the needy, such that it is accompanied by cheerfulness and kindness, and is free from any kind of reminding of favors or anything that causes offense or distress to the recipient.

There is no obstacle to all three of these meanings being combined within the concept and content of the verse.

Key Points

1- Spending as a Means of Preventing the Destruction of Societies

Regarding whether there is a connection between the two phrases "وَ أَنْفِقُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ" (And spend in the way of Allah) and "وَ لا تُلْقُوا بِأَيْدِيكُمْ إِلَى التَّهْلُكَةِ" (and do not throw yourselves with your own hands into destruction), considering that all expressions in the Quran are calculated, there is certainly a relationship between the two. It seems the relationship is this: if you do not spend in the path of Jihad and for the sake of Allah, you have thrown yourselves into destruction with your own hands. In fact, one can take the matter further and say:

Although this verse appears following the verses of Jihad, it expresses a general and social truth: that spending, in general, is a cause for saving societies from lethal vices. This is because when the issue of spending is forgotten, and wealth is concentrated in the hands of a limited group while facing them is a deprived and destitute majority, it will not be long before a massive explosion occurs in society. In its fire, the lives and properties of the wealthy will also burn. From here, the relationship between the issue of spending and the prevention of destruction becomes clear.

Therefore, spending, before being beneficial to the deprived, is to the advantage of the wealthy, because the moderation of wealth preserves wealth.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, alluded to this truth in one of his short sayings: "Protect your wealth by [paying] Zakat." [^1] And as some commentators have expressed, refraining from spending in the way of Allah will cause both the death of the human spirit due to stinginess and the death of society due to weakness and inability, especially in a system like the Islamic system which is built upon doing good. [^2]

2- Misuse of the Verse's Content

As we mentioned, some comfort-seekers, in order to flee from Jihad in the way of Allah, have clung to the phrase "وَ لا تُلْقُوا بِأَيْدِيكُمْ إِلَى التَّهْلُكَةِ" (and do not throw yourselves with your own hands into destruction). They have reached such a level of disgrace as to count the uprising of Imam Hussain عليه السلام on Ashura—which was the cause of the salvation and survival of Islam against enemies like the Umayyads—as an instance of this verse. They are oblivious to the fact that if this door were opened, Jihad would have to be completely abandoned. Fundamentally, "destruction" (tahlukah) and "martyrdom" are two distinct concepts. Destruction means a pointless death, whereas martyrdom is being sacrificed in the way of a goal and attaining eternal life.

One must pay attention to the truth that a person's life is not the most valuable asset of their existence. We have truths more valuable than life: faith in Allah, the religion of Islam, the preservation of the Quran and its sacred goals, and indeed, the preservation of the dignity and honor of the Islamic society. These are goals higher than human life, and being sacrificed in their path is not destruction, and it has never been forbidden.

In a Hadith, we read that a group of Muslims had gone to Constantinople for Jihad. One of the brave men attacked the Roman army and entered their ranks. One of those present said: "He has thrown himself into destruction with his own hands." At this point, Abu Ayyub al-Ansari stood up and shouted: "O people! Why do you interpret and explain this verse wrongly? This verse was revealed concerning us, the tribe of the Ansar. When Allah granted honor to His religion and His helpers increased, some of us said to others secretly, so that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would not hear: 'Our wealth has been lost, and Allah has honored Islam and increased its helpers; if we stay in Medina to attend to our lost wealth, it would be better.' The above verse was revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said to us: 'Spend in the way of Allah and do not throw yourselves into destruction,' and the meaning of destruction was staying in Medina to fix our wealth and abandoning Jihad." [^1]

3- What is Meant by "Ihsan"?

Ihsan is usually interpreted as "doing good," but sometimes a broader meaning is mentioned for it, which is any righteous deed, or rather, the motivations for righteous deeds. As we read in a Hadith from the Great Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, who said in the interpretation of Ihsan: "It is to worship Allah as if you see Him, for if you do not see Him, He surely sees you." [^1] It is obvious that when a person has such faith in Allah that it is as if he sees Him, and considers Him present and watching at all times, he will pursue righteous deeds and refrain from any kind of sin and disobedience.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Wisdom 146. [^2]: Tafsir Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 1, p. 276. [^1]: Ad-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 1, p. 207—a similar meaning appears in Ruh al-Ma'ani, Tafsir al-Kabir, and Al-Mizan under the verse in question. (It is also mentioned in the Maqtal of Ibn Tawus). [^1]: Tafsir Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 1, p. 276.

Ayah

وَأَتِمُّوا۟ ٱلْحَجَّ وَٱلْعُمْرَةَ لِلَّهِ ۚ فَإِنْ أُحْصِرْتُمْ فَمَا ٱسْتَيْسَرَ مِنَ ٱلْهَدْىِ ۖ وَلَا تَحْلِقُوا۟ رُءُوسَكُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ ٱلْهَدْىُ مَحِلَّهُۥ ۚ فَمَن كَانَ مِنكُم مَّرِيضًا أَوْ بِهِۦٓ أَذًى مِّن رَّأْسِهِۦ فَفِدْيَةٌ مِّن صِيَامٍ أَوْ صَدَقَةٍ أَوْ نُسُكٍ ۚ فَإِذَآ أَمِنتُمْ فَمَن تَمَتَّعَ بِٱلْعُمْرَةِ إِلَى ٱلْحَجِّ فَمَا ٱسْتَيْسَرَ مِنَ ٱلْهَدْىِ ۚ فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ ثَلَـٰثَةِ أَيَّامٍ فِى ٱلْحَجِّ وَسَبْعَةٍ إِذَا رَجَعْتُمْ ۗ تِلْكَ عَشَرَةٌ كَامِلَةٌ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن لَّمْ يَكُنْ أَهْلُهُۥ حَاضِرِى ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (١٩٦)
Complete the hajj and the ‘umrah for Allah’s sake, and if you are prevented, then [make] such [sacrificial] offering as is feasible. And do not shave your heads until the offering reaches its [assigned] place. But should any of you be sick, or have a hurt in his head, let the atonement be by fasting, or charity, or sacrifice. And when you have security—for those who enjoy [release from the restrictions] by virtue of their ‘umrah until the hajj—let the offering be such as is feasible. As for someone who cannot afford [the offering], let him fast three days during the hajj and seven when you return; that is [a period of] ten complete [days]. That is for someone whose family does not dwell by the Holy Mosque. And be wary of Allah, and know that Allah is severe in retribution. (196)

Commentary

A Portion of the Important Rulings of Hajj

It is not precisely clear when the verses related to Hajj in the Holy Quran were revealed. However, some great commentators believe they were revealed during the Farewell Pilgrimage (Hajjat al-Wada'). [^1] Meanwhile, others have stated that the phrase "But if you are prevented, then [offer] what can be obtained with ease of sacrificial animals" refers to the event of Hudaybiyyah, which occurred in the sixth year of the Hijra when Muslims were barred from visiting the House of Allah. [^2] In this verse, many rulings are explained:

  1. At the beginning, a general command is given to perform the obligation of Hajj and Umrah completely and in obedience to Allah's command, stating: "And complete the Hajj and 'Umrah for Allah" (وَ أَتِمُّوا الْحَجَّ وَ الْعُمْرَةَ لِلَّهِ).

In fact, before anything else, it addresses the motives for these two acts of worship, advising that nothing should be involved except a divine motive and the intention of seeking proximity to His Pure Essence. Furthermore, the action must be complete and comprehensive in every respect, as necessitated by the command "And complete".

  1. It then addresses those who, after donning the Ihram, are unable to complete the rites of Hajj or Umrah due to an obstacle, such as severe illness or fear of an enemy. It says: "But if you are prevented (and obstacles do not allow you to enter Mecca after donning the Ihram), then [offer] what can be obtained with ease of sacrificial animals" (فَإِنْ أُحْصِرْتُمْ فَمَا اسْتَيْسَرَ مِنَ الْهَدْيِ). [^3] In any case, individuals who are trapped and do not find the ability to perform the ceremonies of Hajj and Umrah can use this provision to exit the Ihram and return to their normal state.

We know that the sacrifice (Hady) can be a camel, a cow, or a sheep, the easiest of which is a sheep; therefore, the phrase "what can be obtained with ease of sacrificial animals" has mostly been interpreted as referring to a sheep.

  1. Then, it points to another instruction, saying: "And do not shave your heads until the sacrificial animal has reached its place of slaughter" (وَ لا تَحْلِقُوا رُؤُسَكُمْ حَتَّى يَبْلُغَ الْهَدْيُ مَحِلَّهُ).

Does this instruction apply to those who are prevented and barred from performing the Hajj rites—essentially being a completion of the previous instruction—or does it apply to all pilgrims? Some commentators have chosen the first meaning, stating that the "place of the Hady" (the place of sacrifice) is the Haram (the sacred precinct). Others have said it refers to the very place where the obstacle or hindrance occurs, citing the action of the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the story of Hudaybiyyah, which is a location outside the Haram of Mecca. After the polytheists prevented him, the Prophet slaughtered his sacrifice right there and commanded his companions to do the same.

The distinguished commentator, the late Tabarsi, says: "According to our scholars, if the prevention is due to illness, the sacrifice must be slaughtered in the Haram, but if it is due to being blocked by an enemy, it is slaughtered at the very place where they were blocked."

However, other commentators consider this sentence to apply to all pilgrims, stating that no one has the right to perform Taqsir (shaving the head or cutting hair to exit Ihram) unless they slaughter the sacrifice at its designated place (the Hajj sacrifice in Mina and the Umrah sacrifice in Mecca).

In any case, the meaning of the sacrifice "reaching its place" is that it arrives at that location and is slaughtered, and this expression is a metaphor for the act of slaughtering.

Given the generality of the expression used in the verse, the second interpretation seems more appropriate, as it includes both those who are prevented and those who are not.

  1. It then states: "And whoever among you is ill or has an ailment of the head (and is thus forced to shave his head before that time), then a ransom [is required] of fasting or charity or sacrifice" (فَمَنْ كانَ مِنْكُمْ مَرِيضاً أَوْ بِهِ أَذىً مِنْ رَأْسِهِ فَفِدْيَةٌ مِنْ صِيامٍ أَوْ صَدَقَةٍ أَوْ نُسُكٍ).

"Nusuk" was originally the plural of "Nasikah", meaning a slaughtered animal. This word has also been used to mean worship. [^1] Therefore, Raghib in Al-Mufradat, after defining Nusuk as worship, says: "This word is used regarding the rites of Hajj, and Nasikah means a sacrifice (Dhabihah)."

Some commentators also believe its root meaning refers to silver ingots, and worship is called Nusuk because it makes a person pure and refined. [^2] In any case, the literal meaning of the verse is that such a person has a choice among these three (fasting, charity, or slaughtering a sheep).

In the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, it is stated that the fasting in this case must be for three days, the charity is to six needy people (and in one narration, ten), and the Nusuk is one sheep. [^3]

  1. It then adds: "And when you are secure (from illness and the enemy), then whoever performs 'Umrah [during the Hajj months] followed by Hajj [offers] what can be obtained with ease of sacrificial animals" (فَإِذا أَمِنْتُمْ فَمَنْ تَمَتَّعَ بِالْعُمْرَةِ إِلَى الْحَجِّ فَمَا اسْتَيْسَرَ مِنَ الْهَدْيِ).

This indicates that in Hajj al-Tamattu', where the Umrah is performed first and then the Hajj is carried out, a sacrifice is mandatory. It makes no difference whether this sacrifice is a camel, a cow, or a sheep; without it, one does not exit the Ihram.

Regarding the origin of the word "Hady", according to the late Tabarsi, there are two views: first, that it is derived from "Hadiyyah" (gift), and since the sacrifice is effectively a gift toward the House of Allah, this term is applied to it. Second, that it is derived from the root of "guidance" (Hidayah), because the sacrificial animal was brought along and guided toward the House of Allah and the place of slaughter.

However, the apparent words of Raghib in Al-Mufradat are that it is derived only from "Hadiyyah", stating that "Hady" is a plural and its singular is "Hadiyyah".

In Mu'jam Maqayis al-Lughah, two roots are mentioned for this word: guidance and gift, but it is not unlikely that both return to the concept of guidance. (Reflect on this).

  1. It then proceeds to state the ruling for those who, while performing Hajj al-Tamattu', are unable to provide a sacrifice: "And whoever cannot find [or afford such an animal] - then a fast of three days during Hajj and of seven when you have returned [home]. Those are ten [days] complete" (فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ ثَلاثَةِ أَيَّامٍ فِي الْحَجِّ وَ سَبْعَةٍ إِذا رَجَعْتُمْ تِلْكَ عَشَرَةٌ كامِلَةٌ).

Therefore, if a sacrifice is not found, or if a person's financial situation does not allow it, the compensation is ten days of fasting, three of which (the 7th, 8th, and 9th of Dhu al-Hijjah) occur during the Hajj days—this is one of the fasts that is permissible to perform while traveling—and the other seven days are performed after returning home.

Even though it is obvious that three days plus seven days equals ten, the Quran nevertheless says: "Those are ten [days] complete."

Some commentators, in explaining this phrase, have said this is because although the "and" (waw) is usually for conjunction and not for choice, it is sometimes used in the sense of choice; here, to remove any ambiguity, Allah stated the phrase "Those are ten [days] complete."

It is also possible that the expression "complete" (kamilah) indicates that the ten days of fasting can fully substitute for the sacrifice, and the visitors of the House of Allah should not worry about this, for all the rewards given by Allah to those who offer a sacrifice will be given to them as well.

Some have also said: This expression points to a subtle point regarding the number ten, for from one perspective, ten is the most complete of numbers. When we start counting from one, the ascending sequence completes itself at ten, and the numbers after that are repetitions and combinations of ten and other numbers, like eleven and twelve. The Arabs call twenty "'ishrin" (meaning two tens) and thirty "thalathin" (three tens), and so on. [^1]

  1. Then it proceeds to state another ruling, saying: "This is for those whose family is not present [i.e., resident] near al-Masjid al-Haram" (ذلِكَ لِمَنْ لَمْ يَكُنْ أَهْلُهُ حاضِرِي الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ).

Therefore, those who are residents of Mecca or its surroundings do not perform Hajj al-Tamattu'; rather, Hajj al-Tamattu' is specific to individuals far from this region. What is well-known among the jurists is that anyone who is further than 48 miles from Mecca has the duty of Hajj al-Tamattu', while those at a shorter distance have the duty of Hajj al-Qiran or Hajj al-Ifrad, the Umrah of which is performed after the Hajj ceremonies (the details of this subject and its evidences are found in books of jurisprudence). After stating these seven rulings, the verse concludes with a command to piety: "And fear Allah and know that Allah is severe in penalty" (وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ).

This emphasis is perhaps because Muslims should not fall short in any of the details of this important Islamic worship, for negligence in it can sometimes lead to the invalidation of the Hajj and the loss of its significant blessings.

Key Points

1. The Importance of Hajj Among Islamic Duties!

Hajj is one of the most important acts of worship legislated in Islam and possesses numerous and countless effects and blessings. Hajj is a source of greatness for Islam, strength for the religion, and unity for Muslims. Hajj is a ceremony that makes the enemies tremble and injects fresh blood into the veins of Muslims every year.

Hajj is that very worship which the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام called the "flag" and the important motto of Islam, and in his will during the final hours of his life, he said:

"Fear Allah, fear Allah regarding the House of your Lord! Do not leave it empty as long as you live, for if it is left empty, you will not be given respite." [^1] (And divine calamity will encompass you).

This sentence is also famous from the enemies of Islam who say: "As long as Hajj remains flourishing, we will not prevail over them." [^2] Another scholar says: "Woe to the Muslims if they do not understand the meaning of Hajj, and woe to others if they do."

In a famous hadith where Ali عليه السلام—as narrated in Nahj al-Balagha, Wisdom 252—explains the philosophy of the rulings, there is a meaningful reference to the importance of Hajj. He says:

"Allah ordained faith as a purification from polytheism... and Hajj as a strengthening for the religion." [^3]

We conclude this discussion with a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام (leaving further explanation to Volume 14 of Tafsir Nemouneh, under verses 26 to 28 of Surah Al-Hajj, where the importance, philosophy, and secrets of Hajj are discussed extensively), where he said:

"The religion will continue to stand as long as the Kaaba stands." [^4]

2. Types of Hajj and a Summary of the Rites of Hajj al-Tamattu'

Our great jurists, inspired by the verses of the Quran and the Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, have divided Hajj into three types: Hajj al-Tamattu', Hajj al-Qiran, and Hajj al-Ifrad. Hajj al-Tamattu' is specific to those whose distance from Mecca is 48 miles or more (16 farsakhs, approximately 96 kilometers), while Hajj al-Qiran and Hajj al-Ifrad pertain to those who live within a shorter distance.

In Hajj al-Tamattu', they first perform the Umrah, then exit the Ihram, and later perform the Hajj ceremonies during its specific days. However, in Hajj al-Qiran and Hajj al-Ifrad, the Hajj ceremonies are performed first, followed by the Umrah ceremonies after their completion—with the difference that in Hajj al-Qiran, a sacrifice is brought along, whereas in Hajj al-Ifrad, there is no such sacrifice (though according to the Sunnis, Hajj al-Qiran is when one intends Hajj and Umrah together in a single Ihram).

The rites of Hajj al-Tamattu' are as follows

First, they don the Ihram from specific points called Miqat: meaning they commit to abstaining from a series of actions forbidden to one in Ihram, and they wear the Ihram garment, which consists of two pieces of unstitched cloth. Reciting the Talbiyah, they come toward the House of Allah. First, they circumambulate the House of Allah seven times (Tawaf), then they perform two units of prayer at the place known as Maqam Ibrahim. Afterward, they walk back and forth seven times between the two hills of Safa and Marwah (Sa'y). Then, by cutting a bit of their hair or nails (Taqsir), they exit the Ihram. For the Hajj ceremonies, they don the Ihram from Mecca and on the 9th day of Dhu al-Hijjah, they go to Arafat, a plain four farsakhs from Mecca, staying there from noon until sunset, occupied with supplicating to the Lord. Then, after sunset, they depart for "Mash'ar al-Haram," located about two and a half farsakhs from Mecca, staying in that sacred valley until morning. At sunrise, they move from that land to Mina, which is nearby. On that same day, which is the day of Eid al-Adha, they throw seven pebbles at a specific pillar called "Jamrat al-Aqaba," then they offer a sacrifice and exit the Ihram by shaving their heads.

On that same day or afterward, they return to Mecca and perform the Tawaf of the House of Allah, the Tawaf prayer, the Sa'y of Safa and Marwah, the Tawaf al-Nisa', and the Tawaf al-Nisa' prayer. On the 11th and 12th days, they throw seven pebbles at each of the three specific pillars in Mina called "Jamarat," one after the other, and they stay in the land of Mina on the nights of the 11th and 12th. In this way, they perform the Hajj ceremonies, each of which revives a historical memory and contains metaphors and allusions to matters related to the purification of the soul and social philosophies, the philosophy of each of which will be explained in the appropriate verses. [^5]

3. Why have some consigned Hajj al-Tamattu' to oblivion?

The apparent meaning of the above verse is that the duty of those not residing in Mecca is Hajj al-Tamattu' (the Hajj that begins with Umrah, after which they exit Ihram, then re-enter Ihram for Hajj and perform the Hajj rites). There is no evidence for the abrogation of this verse, and many narrations have been cited in both Shia and Sunni books in this regard. Famous Sunni traditionists such as al-Nasa'i in his Sunan, Ahmad in his Musnad, Ibn Majah in his Sunan, al-Bayhaqi in al-Sunan al-Kubra, al-Tirmidhi in his Sahih, and Muslim in his famous book, have narrated numerous traditions regarding Hajj al-Tamattu', stating that this ruling has not been abrogated and remains until the Day of Resurrection.

Many Sunni jurists also consider it the most virtuous type of Hajj, even though they permit Hajj al-Qiran and Hajj al-Ifrad alongside it (of course, Qiran in the sense mentioned above from their jurists).

However, in a famous tradition narrated from Umar, we read that he said: "Two Mut'ahs were present during the time of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and I forbid them and will punish for them: the Mut'ah of women and the Mut'ah of Hajj." Fakhr al-Razi, under the verse being discussed, after quoting this tradition from Umar, says:

"The meaning of the Mut'ah of Hajj is to combine both Ihrams (the Ihram of Hajj and the Ihram of Umrah) together, then cancel the intention for Hajj and perform Umrah, and after that perform Hajj." [^1]

It is obvious that no one except the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has the right to declare the abrogation of a ruling. Fundamentally, the expression "the Prophet said such and I say such" is an expression that is intolerable for anyone. Can one set aside the command of the Prophet and follow the words of others? This is unacceptable.

In any case, today many Sunni scholars have abandoned the aforementioned tradition and accepted Hajj al-Tamattu' as the most virtuous type of Hajj and act according to it!

Ayahs

ٱلْحَجُّ أَشْهُرٌ مَّعْلُومَـٰتٌ ۚ فَمَن فَرَضَ فِيهِنَّ ٱلْحَجَّ فَلَا رَفَثَ وَلَا فُسُوقَ وَلَا جِدَالَ فِى ٱلْحَجِّ ۗ وَمَا تَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ يَعْلَمْهُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَتَزَوَّدُوا۟ فَإِنَّ خَيْرَ ٱلزَّادِ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ۚ وَٱتَّقُونِ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (١٩٧)
The hajj [season] is in months well-known; so whoever decides on hajj [pilgrimage] therein, [should know that] there is to be no sexual contact, vicious talk, or disputing during the hajj.
