And whatever good you do, Allah knows it. And take provision, for indeed the best provision is Godwariness. So be wary of Me, O you who possess intellects! (197)
لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَبْتَغُوا۟ فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۚ فَإِذَآ أَفَضْتُم مِّنْ عَرَفَـٰتٍ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عِندَ ٱلْمَشْعَرِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۖ وَٱذْكُرُوهُ كَمَا هَدَىٰكُمْ وَإِن كُنتُم مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمِنَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (١٩٨)
There is no sin upon you in seeking your Lord’s bounty [during the hajj season]. Then when you stream out of ‘Arafat remember Allah at the Holy Mash‘ar, and remember Him as He has guided you, and earlier you were indeed among the astray. (198)
ثُمَّ أَفِيضُوا۟ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَفَاضَ ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٩٩)
Then stream out from where the people stream out, and plead to Allah for forgiveness; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (199)

Commentary

The Best Provision

These verses continue to follow the rulings of Hajj and the pilgrimage to the House of Allah, introducing new instructions:

1- First, He says: "Hajj is in the well-known months" (الْحَجُّ أَشْهُرٌ مَعْلُوماتٌ). ^1 These months refer to Shawwal, Dhu al-Qi'dah, and Dhu al-Hijjah (either the entire month of Dhu al-Hijjah or its first ten days). These are called the "Months of Hajj" because certain acts of Hajj (the Umrah rituals) cannot be performed outside these months, and specific rites must be performed exclusively from the ninth to the twelfth of Dhu al-Hijjah. The reason the Quran does not explicitly name these months is that they were well-known to everyone, and the Quran uses this phrasing to emphasize them.

Furthermore, this sentence negates a superstitious custom of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), where they would sometimes change or swap the months of Hajj, moving them forward or backward due to conflicts, wars, or other reasons. The Quran states: "These months are fixed and determined, and no advancement or postponement is permissible." [^2]

2- Then, it points to another instruction regarding those who begin the Hajj rituals by donning the Ihram, stating: "Whoever undertakes the Hajj therein (and has entered the state of Ihram must know) there is to be no sexual relations, no sin, and no quarreling during Hajj" (فَمَنْ فَرَضَ فِيهِنَّ الْحَجَّ فَلا رَفَثَ وَ لا فُسُوقَ وَ لا جِدالَ فِي الْحَجِ).

"Rafath" (رَفَثَ) originally refers to speech containing matters that are indecent to mention, whether regarding sexual intercourse or its preliminaries; subsequently, it became a metaphor for intercourse itself. However, some have specified that the term Rafath only applies to such talk when in the presence of women; if it occurs in their absence, it is not called Rafath. ^1 Others consider its root to mean a practical inclination toward women, starting from joking and touching and ending in sexual intercourse. [^2] "Fusuq" (فُسُوقَ) means sin and departing from obedience to Allah. "Jidal" (جِدالَ) means speech accompanied by conflict; its root meaning is to twist a rope tightly, and since two parties in a contentious argument "twist" around each other, each trying to impose their word, this term is used.

In any case, according to this instruction, pilgrims in the state of Ihram have no right to intimacy with their spouses, nor to use false words or insults (though this is forbidden even outside of Ihram, it is one of the twenty-five things a Muhrim must abstain from). Also among the forbidden acts is quarreling and swearing oaths, whether true or false, such as saying "No, by Allah" or "Yes, by Allah."

Thus, the environment of Hajj must be purified from sexual gratifications, as well as from committing sins, useless talk, vain arguments, and disputes. This is because it is an environment of worship, sincerity, and the abandonment of material pleasures—an environment where the human soul must draw strength, detach itself entirely from the material world, and find a path to the metaphysical realm. At the same time, the bonds of affection, unity, agreement, and brotherhood among Muslims must be strengthened, and any action that contradicts these matters is prohibited.

Of course, each of these rulings has detailed explanations and conditions found in the books of Hajj rituals (Manasik).

3- In the next stage, it points to the spiritual aspects of Hajj and matters related to sincerity, saying: "Whatever good you do, Allah knows it" (وَ ما تَفْعَلُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ يَعْلَمْهُ اللَّهُ).

What reward for the faithful doers of good could be higher than knowing that every good deed they perform is known to Allah, and that their Master and Deity is Present and Watching? It is deeply delightful that good deeds are performed in His presence; this is a reward preceding the other spiritual and material rewards that the All-Knowing Allah bestows.

Continuing this theme, He says: "And take provisions, but indeed, the best provision is Taqwa (piety/God-consciousness). And fear Me, O people of understanding" (وَ تَزَوَّدُوا فَإِنَّ خَيْرَ الزَّادِ التَّقْوى‌ وَ اتَّقُونِ يا أُولِي الْأَلْبابِ).

Many commentators have stated that this verse refers to a group (some say a group from Yemen) who, when they set out for the pilgrimage to the House of Allah, would take no provisions with them. Even if they had provisions, they would throw them away upon entering Ihram, saying, "We are going to visit the House of Allah; how could He not feed us?" (Consequently, they would sometimes fall into hardship or find it necessary to beg from others). The Quran negates this false thinking and says to prepare provisions for yourselves, but simultaneously guides them to a more important spiritual matter: that beyond these physical provisions, there is another provision that must be prepared for the journey of the Hereafter, and that is Taqwa.

This sentence may be a subtle allusion to the fact that during the Hajj journey, there are numerous opportunities to gather spiritual provisions that one must not neglect. There, the history of Islam is personified; one sees the living scenes of the sacrifice of Ibrahim عليه السلام, the hero of monotheism, and unique manifestations of proximity to the Lord that are found nowhere else in the world. Those with an awakened soul and living thought can gather spiritual provisions for a lifetime from this unique spiritual journey.

It is noteworthy that following this, He again commands Taqwa and addresses the "Uli al-Albab" ^1—that is, the people of intellect and thought. Yes, it is they who comprehend the spirit of Hajj and derive the maximum benefit from this sublime educational program, while others only have a share of its outer shell and do not grasp its essence. Indeed, it is the people of intellect and thinkers who understand the effects of Taqwa and piety on the individual and society.

In the next verse, the Quran proceeds to correct certain misconceptions regarding Hajj, stating: "There is no sin upon you for seeking bounty from your Lord (and economic benefits during the days of Hajj)" (لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا فَضْلًا مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ).

During the Age of Ignorance, people considered any form of trade, commerce, transport, or passenger service during the Hajj rituals to be a sin, and they deemed the Hajj of those who did so to be void.

The verse under discussion declared this pagan ruling worthless and void, stating there is no obstacle to benefiting from lawful trade and commerce—which is part of Allah's bounty upon His servants—during the Hajj season, or to working and utilizing the fruits of one's labor.

Fundamentally, the mindset that existed in the Age of Ignorance sometimes appears in our time as well—the idea that this great worship, Hajj, must be pure from any material taint. However, since this issue would cause hardship for many workers and those who provide logistics and services—including drivers, attendants, doctors, guides, and other servants—and since the people of that land can resolve many economic problems during Hajj, this thinking has been rejected. Such individuals can perform the Hajj rituals alongside their services and not be placed in a state of constriction.

In fact, beyond this, it is clearly understood from Islamic sources that Hajj, in addition to its important moral philosophy and the purification of souls, also possesses political, cultural, and economic philosophies.

To explain: The travel of Muslims from different parts of the world toward the House of Allah and the formation of that great Islamic congress can serve as a foundation for a general economic leap in Islamic societies. This can happen if the economic thinkers of the Muslims sit together after or before the Hajj rituals and, through consultation and cooperation, lay a firm foundation for the economy of Islamic societies, creating such a powerful economy through proper commercial exchanges that they become independent of enemies and strangers.

Therefore, these commercial transactions and exchanges are themselves a means of strengthening the Islamic society against the enemies of Islam, for we know that no nation will have complete independence without a powerful economy. However, it is obvious that commercial activities must be overshadowed by the devotional and moral aspects of Hajj, not dominate or take precedence over them. Fortunately, Muslims have sufficient time before or after the Hajj rites for this purpose.

Hisham ibn al-Hakam says: I asked Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: ... "I said to him: What is the reason for which Allah obligated the servants with Hajj and circumambulation of the House? He said: ... He placed therein the gathering from the East and the West so they may know one another, and so that every people may bring trade from one land to another, and so that the transporters and camel-drivers may benefit... and if every people were to speak only about their own lands and what is in them, they would perish, the lands would be ruined, and imports and profits would fall."

"...He [Allah] created humans... and commanded them to perform Hajj, which encompasses the obedience of their religion and the interests of their worldly life. During the Hajj season, Muslims from the East and the West gather to become acquainted with one another, and so that every nation may benefit from the trade and economic products of other nations, and so that travelers and transporters may profit during this journey by renting out their means of transport. (And so that they may become acquainted with the legacy and reports of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and so these legacies remain alive and are not consigned to oblivion). If every nation were to speak only about its own environment, they would perish, cities would be ruined, and commercial benefits and profits would vanish." ^1

Then, in the continuation of the same verse, it turns attention back to the Hajj rituals, saying:

"When you depart from Arafat, remember Allah at al-Mash'ar al-Haram" (فَإِذا أَفَضْتُمْ مِنْ عَرَفاتٍ فَاذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ عِنْدَ الْمَشْعَرِ الْحَرامِ).

"And remember Him as He guided you, though before that you were among those astray" (وَ اذْكُرُوهُ كَما هَداكُمْ وَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ لَمِنَ الضَّالِّينَ).

Again, continuing this theme, it says: "Then depart from where the people depart (from al-Mash'ar al-Haram toward the land of Mina)" (ثُمَّ أَفِيضُوا مِنْ حَيْثُ أَفاضَ النَّاسُ).

And at the end, it commands seeking forgiveness and repentance, saying: "And ask forgiveness of Allah. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful" (وَ اسْتَغْفِرُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

In this section of the verses, three stations of the Hajj stations are mentioned: "Arafat," which is a place about 20 kilometers from Makkah where pilgrims stay from the noon of the ninth of Dhu al-Hijjah until sunset, engaging in the worship of the Lord; then the stay at "al-Mash'ar al-Haram" or "Muzdalifah," where they stay for part of the night of Eid al-Adha and the morning before sunrise, engaging in intimate prayer with the Lord; and third, the land of "Mina," which is the place for sacrifice, the stoning of the pillars (Ramy al-Jamarat), ending the Ihram, and performing the Eid ceremonies.

Key Points

1- The First Station of Hajj

The pilgrims of the House of Allah, after performing the Umrah rituals, head toward the Hajj rituals, and the first stage is the stay in Arafat, where they stop from midday on the ninth of Dhu al-Hijjah until sunset. Then they move from there toward al-Mash'ar al-Haram (both parts are mentioned in the above verses).

Various reasons have been mentioned for naming the land "Arafat." Sometimes it is said that when the messenger of Allah's revelation, Jibril, showed the Hajj rituals to Ibrahim there, Ibrahim said: "Araftu, Araftu" (I have known, I have known); thus it was called Arafat.

Sometimes it is said this story occurred for Adam, and sometimes it is said that Adam and Hawwa (Eve) found and recognized each other in that land. It is also said that the visitors of the House of Allah see and recognize one another there, among other interpretations. ^1 [^2]

However, it is not unlikely that this naming refers to another reality: that this land is an environment highly prepared for the Ma'rifah (knowledge/recognition) of the Lord and the recognition of His Pure Essence.

Truly, that spiritual and soulful attraction that a person finds upon entering Arafat cannot be described by any words—one must simply go and see. Everyone is in the same form, everyone uniform, everyone living in the desert, everyone having fled the clamor of the city and the noise of the material world and its glitter. Under that blue sky, in that open air pure from the pollution of sin, where the angel of revelation beats his wings, where from within its breezes the sound of Jibril's whisper, the masculine melody of Ibrahim al-Khalil, and the life-giving resonance of the voice of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the warriors of early Islam can be heard.

In this memory-filled land, where it seems a window to the metaphysical world has been opened, a person is not only intoxicated by the state of knowing the Lord and for a moment becomes synchronized with the general hymn of praise of creation, but also finds within their own being themselves—whom they have lost for a lifetime and have been searching for. They become a "knower" (Arif) of their own state and realize they are not that person who, day and night, greedily traverses mountains and deserts in search of a livelihood, whose thirst is never quenched by whatever they find. They realize another jewel is hidden within their soul, which is their true self. Yes, they call this land "Arafat"—truly, what an interesting and appropriate name!

2- Al-Mash'ar al-Haram: The Second Station of Hajj

Regarding the naming of "al-Mash'ar al-Haram," it is also said that it is a center for the "Sha'a'ir (symbols) of Hajj" and a sign of these great, magnificent, and heavenly rituals.

However, one must not forget that "Mash'ar" comes from the root "Shu'ur" (consciousness/awareness). On that historic and exciting night (the night of the tenth of Dhu al-Hijjah), when the pilgrims of the House of Allah, after completing their period of cultivation in Arafat, migrate there and spend a night until morning on the soft sands under the star-filled sky—in a land that is a small sample of the Great Gathering (Mahshar) and a curtain from the great Resurrection—where the crowd, like the roaring waves of the sea during a storm, fills everywhere and the voices of the people in motion do not fall silent until morning.

Yes, in such an unadorned, pure, and soul-shaking environment, within the innocent garments of Ihram and upon those soft sands, a person feels the bubbling of new springs of thought, reflection, and consciousness (Shu'ur) within themselves and clearly hears the sound of its flow in the depths of their heart. That is why they call it "Mash'ar"!

3- A Lesson in Oneness and Unity

It is mentioned in narrations that the tribe of Quraysh in the Age of Ignorance claimed unjust privileges for themselves and did not grant any other Arab the status they believed they held. They called themselves "Hums" (meaning those firm and steadfast in their religion) and, as the children of Ibrahim and the guardians of the Kaaba, considered themselves superior to everyone.

Among these claims was that they said: "We must not go to Arafat during the Hajj rituals because Arafat is outside the Sanctuary (Haram) of Makkah; if we do so, the Arabs will not value us. The Sanctuary of Makkah is not equal to the areas outside the Sanctuary." They said this while knowing that the stay in Arafat was part of the Abrahamic Hajj rituals. ^1 The Quran, in the above verses, drew a line of cancellation through these delusions and commanded the Muslims to all stay together in Arafat, and from there all migrate toward al-Mash'ar al-Haram, and from there toward Mina. The phrase "Then depart from where the people depart" (ثُمَّ أَفِيضُوا مِنْ حَيْثُ أَفاضَ النَّاسُ) is a subtle allusion to this meaning.

In fact, with this statement, the Quran commands that all people without exception first go to Arafat, and from there to al-Mash'ar al-Haram, and then migrate to the land of Mina. ^1 The command for seeking forgiveness at the end of the last verse above points to the fact that they should ask Allah for the forgiveness of all sins and move away from those pagan thoughts and imaginations that oppose the spirit of equality and parity in Hajj, reminding them that if they desist, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.

It should be noted that "Ifadhah" (افاضه) from the root "Faydh" (فیض) originally means the flow and pouring of water, and since when people move collectively from one place to another with speed, it is not unlike a flowing river, this expression is used.

4- The Connection Between the Verses

Regarding the relationship between "seeking the bounty of Allah" (commercial activities) and the issue of staying in "Arafat" and moving from there toward "al-Mash'ar al-Haram" and then "Mina"—which are placed together in the above verses—it may be an allusion to the point that economic effort, if it is for Allah and for an honorable life, is also a type of worship, just like the Hajj rituals. Or, it may be that the transport and movement of pilgrims from Makkah to Arafat and from there to other stations require costs and services, and if every kind of work, service, and receiving of wages were forbidden and haram during these days, the pilgrims of the House of Allah would surely fall into great hardship.

Therefore, they are placed together in this way, or perhaps to warn: "Lest engaging in economic activities distances you from the remembrance of Allah and from paying attention to the greatness of these stations."

^1: Since Hajj itself is not these specific months, commentators have said the verse has an implied word, such as: "The months of Hajj are well-known months." Some have also suggested the verse has no implied word and is a metaphor for the intensity of the connection between Hajj and these specific months, as if Hajj itself is these months. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1, p. 293; Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 5, p. 160. ^1: Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 5, p. 164. [^2]: Al-Tahqiq fi Kalimat al-Quran al-Karim.

^1: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 8, p. 8, Book of Hajj, Chapters on the Obligation of Hajj, Chapter 1, Hadith 18. ^1: Fakhr al-Razi mentions eight opinions here regarding the meaning of "Arafat" (Vol. 5, pp. 173-174). [^2]: There is much discussion among commentators whether "Arafat" is singular or plural; some consider it the plural of "Arafah," and some say Arafah is the name of the time of those acts (9th of Dhu al-Hijjah) and Arafat is the name of that place (Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 2, p. 87). ^1: Sirat Ibn Hisham, Vol. 1, pp. 211-212. ^1: The use of "Thumma" (Then) indicates that after the Ifadhah and migrating from Arafat to al-Mash'ar al-Haram, they should migrate from there (to the land of Mina).

Ayahs

فَإِذَا قَضَيْتُم مَّنَـٰسِكَكُمْ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَذِكْرِكُمْ ءَابَآءَكُمْ أَوْ أَشَدَّ ذِكْرًا ۗ فَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِنَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَمَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنْ خَلَـٰقٍ (٢٠٠)
And when you finish your rites, then remember Allah as you would remember your fathers, or with a more ardent remembrance. Among the people there are those who say, ‘Our Lord, give us in this world,’ but for such there is no share in the Hereafter. (200)
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَقُولُ رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِنَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ حَسَنَةً وَقِنَا عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ (٢٠١)
And among them there are those who say, ‘Our Lord, give us good in this world and good in the Hereafter, and save us from the punishment of the Fire.’ (201)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٢٠٢)
Such shall partake of what they have earned, and Allah is swift at reckoning. (202)

Occasion of Revelation

In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read that during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), when people finished the Hajj rituals, they would gather and recount the boastful achievements of their ancestors, mentioning their past days and their abundant generosity (recounting imaginary honors for themselves). The above verses were revealed and commanded them to remember Allah instead of this (incorrect) practice, and to recall the unceasing blessings of Allah and His bounties. They were told to remember the Divine blessings with the same special passion and fervor they had when boasting about their forefathers in the Age of Ignorance, or even more and greater [^1]. This same meaning or something similar has been narrated by other commentators from Ibn Abbas and others, stating that the people of the Age of Ignorance would hold gatherings after Hajj to boast about their fathers and describe their distinctions, or they would gather in markets such as the markets of 'Ukaz, Dhu al-Majaz, and Majannah—which were not only places for trade but centers for mentioning the (imaginary) honors of ancestors—and engage in this practice [^2].

Commentary

Hajj is the Symbol of Unity for the World's Muslims

These verses continue the discussions related to Hajj. Although the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance had received the Hajj rituals through various intermediaries from Ibrahim the Friend (Khalil), it had become so mixed with superstitions that this great and soul-building worship—which is a turning point in the lives of the pilgrims of the House of Allah and the cause of their spiritual rebirth—had been distorted and stripped of its original form and educational philosophies, turning into a means for division and hypocrisy.

In the first verse, the Quran says: "And when you have performed your (Hajj) rituals, remember Allah as you remember your fathers or with [even] greater remembrance" (فَإِذا قَضَيْتُمْ مَناسِكَكُمْ فَاذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ كَذِكْرِكُمْ آباءَكُمْ أَوْ أَشَدَّ ذِكْراً).

Greatness and nobility lie in the light of connection with Allah, not in boasting about imaginary connections to ancestors. The purpose of this expression is not that you should mention both your ancestors and Allah; rather, it is an allusion to the reality that if they are worthy of being remembered for some minor gifts, then why do you not turn to Allah, from Whom comes the entire universe and all the blessings of the world? He is the source of all perfections, the Possessor of the attributes of Majesty and Beauty, and the Benefactor of everyone.

Regarding what is meant by "remembrance of Allah" (dhikr) here, many opinions are found among the commentators, but it appears that it includes all Divine remembrances after the rituals. In fact, one should thank Allah for all His blessings, especially the blessing of faith and guidance to this great ritual, and perfect the educational effects of Hajj through the remembrance of God.

Here, the Quran divides people into two groups, saying: "And among the people is he who says, 'Our Lord, give us in this world,' and he will have in the Hereafter no share" (فَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَقُولُ رَبَّنا آتِنا فِي الدُّنْيا وَ ما لَهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنْ خَلاقٍ) [^1].

"And among them is he who says, 'Our Lord, give us in this world [that which is] good and in the Hereafter [that which is] good and protect us from the punishment of the Fire'" (وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَقُولُ رَبَّنا آتِنا فِي الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةً وَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ حَسَنَةً وَ قِنا عَذابَ النَّارِ).

In truth, this part of the verses refers to the desires of the people and their goals in this great worship. Some look only toward the material bounties of the world and ask nothing else from Allah; obviously, they will have no share in everything in the Hereafter.

However, another group seeks both the material bounties of the world and the spiritual bounties; rather, they seek the life of this world as a preliminary step toward spiritual perfection. This is the logic of Islam, which looks at both the body and matter as well as the soul and meaning, considering the former as the groundwork for the latter. It is never compatible with one-dimensional humans—those who are immersed in materialism and grant it primacy, or those who are completely estranged from the life of this world.

Regarding what is meant by "good" (hasanah) in this verse, various interpretations have been mentioned. In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is interpreted as "abundance in sustenance and good character in this world, and the pleasure of Allah and Paradise in the Hereafter" (انها السعة فى الرزق و المعاش و حسن الخلق فى الدنيا و رضوان اللَّه و الجنة فى الاخرة) [^1]. Some commentators have considered it to mean knowledge and worship in this world and Paradise in the Hereafter, or wealth in this world and Paradise in the Hereafter, or a good and righteous spouse in this world and Paradise in the Hereafter. In a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: "Whoever is given a grateful heart, a tongue engaged in the remembrance of the Truth, and a believing spouse who assists him in the affairs of his world and his hereafter, has indeed been given the good of this world and the good of the Hereafter and has been protected from the punishment of the Fire" [^2].

It is evident that "hasanah" means every kind of goodness and benefit, and it has a broad and expansive concept that includes all material and spiritual bounties. Therefore, what has appeared in the above narrations or the words of the commentators are expressions of its clear examples and do not limit the concept of the verse. The notion held by some commentators that because "hasanah" is a singular indefinite noun in the verse, it does not include every kind of goodness—leading to debates among them in determining its specific instance [^3]—is nothing but an error. This is because sometimes a singular indefinite noun can also convey a generic meaning, and the case in this verse is apparently of this type. As some commentators have said, the believers ask for the essence of "good" from Allah without selecting a specific type, leaving it all to the Divine Will, Intention, and Favor [^4].

In the final verse, referring to the second group (the same group that seeks the good of this world and the Hereafter from Allah), He says: "Those will have a share of what they have earned, and Allah is swift in account" (أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا وَ اللَّهُ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ).

In reality, this verse is the opposite of the sentence that appeared in the previous verses regarding the first group, where He says: "and he will have in the Hereafter no share" (وَ ما لَهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنْ خَلاقٍ).

Some have also suggested that it might refer back to both groups: the first group takes a share of the worldly goods, and the second group is granted the good of this world and the Hereafter. This is similar to what appears in verses 18 to 20 of Surah Al-Isra, which says: "Whoever should desire the fleeting life—We hasten for him therein what We will to whom We intend. Then We have made for him Hell... And whoever desires the Hereafter and exerts for it the effort due to it while he is a believer—it is those whose effort is ever appreciated. To each [category] We extend—to these and to those—from the gift of your Lord."

However, the first interpretation is more harmonious with the verses under discussion.

The expression "share" (nasib), although it appears in the indefinite form, the context testifies that the indefinite form here is to express greatness. The expression "of what they have earned" (mimma kasabu) is not an indication of the smallness of this share, as the "min" here might be causative (ibtidaiyyah) rather than partitive (tab'idiyyah).

The use of the word "earned" (kasab) in the phrase "of what they have earned," according to many commentators, refers to this very supplication they make for the good of this world and the Hereafter. The choice of this expression may point to a subtle point: that supplicating is itself one of the best acts of worship and deeds.

From an examination of dozens of verses in the Holy Quran where the root "kasab" and its derivatives are used, it is clearly understood that this word is also used for non-physical actions—that is, spiritual and heart-centered deeds. As we read in verse 225 of Surah Al-Baqarah: "but He will call you to account for what your hearts have earned" (وَ لكِن يُؤاخِذُكُم بِما كَسَبَت قُلوبُكُم).

Therefore, it is not surprising that supplication is a type of earning and acquisition, especially since true supplication is not only with the tongue but with the heart and the entire being of a person.

The phrase "and Allah is swift in account" (وَ اللَّهُ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ) which appears at the end of the above verses, indicates that Allah both settles the accounts of His servants with speed and quickly grants the rewards and punishments He has promised. All of this is immediate and swift, not on credit or accompanied by delay.

In a Hadith, we read: "Indeed, Allah the Almighty settles the accounts of all creatures in the duration of a blink of an eye" (ان اللَّه تعالى يحاسب الخلائق كلهم فى مقدار لمح البصر) [^1]. This is because the knowledge of Allah is not like that of created beings, where its limitation would cause one matter to distract Him from another.

Furthermore, the reckoning of the Lord does not require time, because our actions leave an effect on our bodies and souls, and even on the surrounding existence—the earth and airwaves—and other objects. From this perspective, the existence of a human can be likened to types of automobiles that, by having a speedometer/odometer, always clearly and specifically show the amount of their work at every moment, and there is no longer a need for calculating and recording the distances the car has traveled throughout its life.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1, p. 297. [^2]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 2, p. 89; Qurtubi, Vol. 2, p. 803; Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 5, p. 183; Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 1, p. 289; Al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 203. [^1]: "Khalaq," according to Raghib, means the moral virtues that a person acquires, and here, according to the late Tabarsi, it means "share" and "portion" (which is the result of those moral virtues). [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1, p. 297. [^2]: Ibid, p. 298. [^3]: Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 5, p. 189. [^4]: Tafsir Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 1, p. 290. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1, p. 298.

Ayah

۞ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ ۚ فَمَن تَعَجَّلَ فِى يَوْمَيْنِ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ وَمَن تَأَخَّرَ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ لِمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُمْ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٢٠٣)
Remember Allah in the appointed days. Then whoever hastens off in a couple of days, there is no sin upon him, and whoever delays, there is no sin upon him—that for one who has been Godwary—and be wary of Allah and know that toward Him you will be mustered. (203)

Commentary

The Final Word Regarding the Hajj

This verse is, in fact, the final verse here that speaks regarding the Hajj rituals. It shatters the pagan traditions related to vain boasting about ancestors and predecessors, and advises them to remember Allah (after the festivities of the Eid). It says: "And remember Allah during the appointed days" (وَ اذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ فِي أَيَّامٍ مَعْدُوداتٍ‌).

Considering that this command, by the context of the preceding verses, pertains to the conclusion of the Hajj rituals, it refers to the eleventh, twelfth, and thirteenth days of the month, which are named in the language of the narrations as the "Days of Tashriq." As the name suggests, these are illuminating days that brighten the human soul and body in the light of these rituals.

In verse 28 of Surah Al-Hajj, the command to mention the name of Allah during "appointed days" (أَيَّامٍ مَعْلُوماتٍ‌) was given, while here it mentions "numbered days" (أَيَّامٍ مَعْدُوداتٍ‌). It is well-known that "appointed days" refers to the first ten days of Dhu al-Hijjah, and "numbered days" refers to the same Days of Tashriq mentioned above. However, some commentators have offered other possibilities, the details of which will be discussed under verse 28 of Surah Al-Hajj [1]. Regarding what is meant by these "remembrances" (adhkar), it has been specified in Islamic hadiths that after fifteen prayers—beginning with the Dhuhr prayer on the day of Eid al-Adha and ending with the prayer on the thirteenth day—the following inspiring phrases should be repeated:

"Allah is the Greatest, Allah is the Greatest; there is no god but Allah, and Allah is the Greatest; and to Allah belongs all praise. Allah is the Greatest for that which He has guided us; Allah is the Greatest for that which He has provided us of the beasts of cattle."

Then, following this command, it adds: "Then whoever hastens (to leave) in two days, there is no sin upon him; and whoever delays (and stays for three days), there is no sin upon him—for those who fear Allah" (فَمَنْ تَعَجَّلَ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ فَلا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ وَ مَنْ تَأَخَّرَ فَلا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ لِمَنِ اتَّقى‌).

This expression, in reality, points to a kind of choice in performing the remembrance of Allah between two days and three days.

The phrase "for those who fear Allah" (لِمَنِ اتَّقى‌) is apparently a condition for the matter of hastening in two days; meaning, being content with two days is specifically for such individuals.

In the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام, it is stated that "piety" (taqwa) here refers to abstaining from hunting. That is, those who, during their Ihram, abstained from hunting or from all the prohibitions of Ihram, may stay in "Mina" for two days after Eid al-Adha to perform the rituals and remember Allah. However, those who did not abstain must stay for three days to perform those rituals and remember Allah.

Some consider the phrase "there is no sin upon him" (فَلا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ‌) to be an indication of the negation of all sin from the pilgrims of the House of Allah. This means that after performing the Hajj rituals, the end of which are these remembrances, in the case of complete faith and sincerity, all traces of previous sins and the residues of transgressions are washed away from their hearts and souls, and they return from these rituals with a spirit pure from any pollution.

Although this statement is inherently correct, the verse is apparently more consistent with the first meaning.

At the end of the verse, it gives a general command regarding piety, saying: "And fear Allah, and know that unto Him you shall be gathered" (وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُمْ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ‌).

According to one of the two interpretations mentioned above, this sentence could be an indication that the spiritual rituals of Hajj have cleansed your past sins, and you return from these rituals pure like a newborn child; however, be careful not to pollute yourselves afterward.

[^1]: Although "Ayyam" (days) is the plural of "Yawm" (masculine), its adjectives "Ma'lumat" or "Ma'dudat" are brought in the feminine form (broken plurals are usually described with feminine adjectives, but typically singular feminine, not the plural with 'alif' and 'ta'). Some have said this is because days are composed of hours (Sa'at), which carries the 'ta' of femininity. Perhaps it is an allusion to the point that you should be in remembrance of Allah during all the hours of these days (Refer to Imla' ma manna bihi al-Rahman, Vol. 1, p. 88).

Ayahs

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُعْجِبُكَ قَوْلُهُۥ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَيُشْهِدُ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا فِى قَلْبِهِۦ وَهُوَ أَلَدُّ ٱلْخِصَامِ (٢٠٤)
Among the people is he whose talk about worldly life impresses you, and he holds Allah witness to what is in his heart, though he is the staunchest of enemies. (204)
وَإِذَا تَوَلَّىٰ سَعَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيُفْسِدَ فِيهَا وَيُهْلِكَ ٱلْحَرْثَ وَٱلنَّسْلَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْفَسَادَ (٢٠٥)
If he were to wield authority, he would try to cause corruption in the land and to ruin the crop and the stock, and Allah does not like corruption. (205)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُ ٱتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ أَخَذَتْهُ ٱلْعِزَّةُ بِٱلْإِثْمِ ۚ فَحَسْبُهُۥ جَهَنَّمُ ۚ وَلَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ (٢٠٦)
And when he is told, ‘Be wary of Allah,’ conceit seizes him sinfully; so let hell suffice him, and it is surely an evil resting place! (206)

Occasion of Revelation

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 2; p. 72

Regarding these verses, two occasions of revelation have been mentioned: 1- These verses were revealed concerning "Akhnas ibn Shariq," who was a handsome and eloquent man. He would feign friendship with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and present himself as a Muslim, swearing that he loved the Prophet and believed in Allah. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, being commanded to judge by outward appearances, treated him warmly and showed him affection. However, in reality, he was a hypocrite. In one incident, he set fire to the crops of some Muslims and killed their livestock (thereby lifting the veil from his true nature). At this point, the above verses were revealed. [^1] 2- Others have narrated from Ibn Abbas that the aforementioned verses were revealed during one of the Islamic battles (the Expedition of Raji'), during which a group of Islamic missionaries, sent by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to preach to the tribes around Medina, were martyred in a treacherous conspiracy. [^2] However, the first occasion of revelation is more consistent with the content of the verses. In any case, the lesson learned from these verses is general, universal, and for all times.

Commentary

The Fate of the Corruptors on Earth

In the first verse, making a brief reference to some of the hypocrites, He says:

"And of the people is he whose speech pleases you in the worldly life (but inwardly it is not so), and Allah is witness to what is in his heart, yet he is the most quarrelsome of opponents" (وَ مِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يُعْجِبُكَ قَوْلُهُ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ يُشْهِدُ اللَّهَ عَلى‌ ما فِي قَلْبِهِ وَ هُوَ أَلَدُّ الْخِصامِ‌).

"Aladd" (أَلَدّ) refers to someone who possesses intense enmity; its root is derived from "ladid," which refers to the two sides of the neck, and it is a metaphor for someone who, from whichever side he approaches, prevails in enmity. "Khisam" (خِصام) is a verbal noun meaning dispute and enmity.

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, and many other commentaries under the verse in question. [^2]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Vol. 2, p. 140 (this occasion of revelation is less frequently narrated).

Then He adds: "The sign of his internal enmity is that when he turns away and leaves your presence, he strives to spread corruption in the land and destroy crops and livestock (even though he knows) Allah does not love corruption" (وَ إِذا تَوَلَّى سَعى‌ فِي الْأَرْضِ لِيُفْسِدَ فِيها وَ يُهْلِكَ الْحَرْثَ وَ النَّسْلَ وَ اللَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْفَسادَ). Yes, in this way, Allah removes the veil from their actions and reveals the contents of their hearts to His Prophet. For if they were sincere in their profession of friendship and love for the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and his followers, they would never have resorted to corruption and destruction, nor would they have ruthlessly attacked crops and livestock. Their outward appearance is one of sincere friendship, but inwardly, they are the most merciless and stubborn of enemies.

Many commentators have suggested that the phrase "when he turns away" (إِذا تَوَلَّى) refers to the issue of assuming authority and governance. That is, when hypocrites reach a stage of governance and power, they resort to corruption and destruction, initiating oppression and tyranny among the servants of Allah. Due to their oppression, settlements fall into ruin, livestock perish, and the lives and property of the people are lost. [^1] "Harth" (حَرْث) means crops/tillage, and "Nasl" (نَسْل) means offspring, and it applies to the offspring of both humans and non-humans. Therefore, destroying "Harth and Nasl" means the annihilation of every kind of living being, whether plant, animal, or human.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 2, p. 96—this meaning is also mentioned in Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Abu al-Futuh Razi, and Tafsir al-Kabir under the verse in question, though this view is less compatible with the occasion of revelation, even if the concept of the verse is broad and extensive.

Regarding the meaning of "Harth and Nasl," other interpretations have also been mentioned, including: that "Harth" refers to women, based on the evidence of the noble verse (نِساؤُكُمْ حَرْثٌ لَكُمْ‌) [^1] and "Nasl" refers to children. Or that "Harth" here refers to religion and faith, and "Nasl" refers to the people (this interpretation is according to a Hadith from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام narrated in Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan). [^2] In any case, the expression "destroys crops and offspring" (يُهْلِكَ الْحَرْثَ وَ النَّسْلَ) is a very concise and comprehensive statement that encompasses the production of corruption at the societal level regarding property and human beings.

In the next verse, He adds: When he is forbidden from this ugly act "and it is said to him, 'Fear Allah,' (the fire of stubbornness flares up within him) and pride and prejudice lead him to sin" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ أَخَذَتْهُ الْعِزَّةُ بِالْإِثْمِ‌). [^3] He neither listens to the advice of counselors nor to Divine warnings; rather, with his characteristic arrogance and vanity, he continuously increases his wrongdoings. Such a person is tamed by nothing but the fire of Hell, and thus at the end of the verse, He says: "Hell is sufficient for them, and what an evil resting place!" (فَحَسْبُهُ جَهَنَّمُ وَ لَبِئْسَ الْمِهادُ). In truth, this is one of the ugly and reprehensible traits of hypocrites: that due to rigid and violent prejudice and stubbornness, they do not submit to any truth, and this very prejudice and arrogance leads them to the worst of sins. Obviously, these "crooked pieces of wood" cannot be straightened except by the fire of Hell!

[^1]: Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 223. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1, p. 300. [^3]: "Izzah" (عزت) is originally the opposite of "Dhillah" (humiliation), but here it means pride and "vanity" (Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 2, p. 96). However, Raghib in "Al-Mufradat" has interpreted this word as invincible power and says: sometimes it is used metaphorically to mean blameworthy pride and vanity, as in the verse in question.

According to some commentators, Allah has described such individuals with five characteristics in the above verses: First, they have deceptive speech; second, the inside of their hearts is polluted and dark; third, they are the most stubborn of enemies; fourth, when the opportunity arises, they show mercy neither to humans nor to animals or crops; fifth, due to pride and vanity, they do not accept the advice of any counselor.

Ayah

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَشْرِى نَفْسَهُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ رَءُوفٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ (٢٠٧)
And among the people is he who sells his soul seeking the pleasure of Allah, and Allah is most kind to [His] servants. (207)

Circumstances of Revelation

The famous Sunni commentator, "Tha'labi," states: When the Prophet of Islam decided to migrate, he appointed Ali عليه السلام in his place to settle his debts and return the trusts that were with him. At night, when he intended to head toward the Cave of "Thawr" while the polytheists had surrounded his house to attack him, he commanded Ali عليه السلام to sleep in his bed and cover himself with a green cloth (a Hadrami cloak) that belonged specifically to the Prophet himself. At this moment, Allah sent a revelation to "Gabriel" and "Michael," saying: "I have established brotherhood between you two and made the lifespan of one of you longer than the other; which of you is willing to sacrifice his life and prefer the life of the other over his own?" Neither of them was willing. It was then revealed to them: "Now Ali عليه السلام is sleeping in the Prophet's bed and has prepared himself to sacrifice his life for him. Go down to earth and be his protectors and guardians."

While Gabriel sat at the head and Michael at the feet of Ali عليه السلام, Gabriel said: "Bravo! Congratulations to you, O Ali! Allah boasts to the angels because of you."

At this moment, the above verse was revealed, and for this reason, that historic night has been named "Laylat al-Mabit."

"Ibn Abbas" says this verse was revealed concerning Ali عليه السلام when he slept in the Prophet's bed, while the Prophet had withdrawn from the polytheists and was going toward the cave with Abu Bakr.

"Abu Ja'far al-Iskafi" says: (As "Ibn Abi al-Hadid" mentioned in the Commentary on Nahj al-Balagha, Vol. 3, p. 270) the incident of Ali عليه السلام sleeping in the Prophet's bed is established through tawatur (successive transmission), and no one denies it except those who are not Muslims or are light-headed individuals. In the second volume of Al-Ghadir, under the discussion of this verse (p. 48 onwards), it is noted that: "Ghazali" in the book Ihya' al-Ulum Vol. 3, p. 238, "Ganji" in the book Kifayat al-Talib p. 114, "Safuri" in Nuzhat al-Majalis Vol. 2, p. 209, "Ibn Sabbagh al-Maliki" in the book Al-Fusul al-Muhimmah p. 33, "Sibt ibn al-Jawzi al-Hanafi" in Tadhkirat al-Khawass p. 21, "Shablanji" in Nur al-Absar p. 86, Ahmad in Musnad Vol. 1, p. 348, Tarikh al-Tabari Vol. 2, pp. 99-101, "Ibn Hisham" in Sirah Vol. 2, p. 291, "Halabi" in his Sirah, and Tarikh al-Ya'qubi Vol. 2, p. 29, have all narrated the incident of "Laylat al-Mabit."

Commentary

Great Sacrifice on the Historic Night of Migration

Although the above verse, as mentioned in its circumstances of revelation, was revealed regarding the event of the Prophet's migration and the sacrifice of Ali عليه السلام and his sleeping in the Prophet's bed, like other verses of the Quran, it carries a general and universal concept and content. In fact, it is the exact opposite of what was mentioned in the previous verses regarding the hypocrites.

It says: "And among the people is he who sells his soul seeking the pleasure of Allah, and Allah is Kind to [His] servants" (وَ مِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَشْرِي نَفْسَهُ ابْتِغاءَ مَرْضاتِ اللَّهِ وَ اللَّهُ رَؤُفٌ بِالْعِبادِ).

That group [the hypocrites] were selfish, conceited, stubborn, and antagonistic people who gained a reputation among the people through hypocrisy; outwardly they showed themselves as believers and well-wishers, but their actions pulled the curtain back from their words, for they did nothing but cause corruption on earth and destroy crops and progeny.

But this group [the true believers] deal only with Allah and sell everything they have, even their own lives, to Him, and they seek nothing but His pleasure and satisfaction. It is through their sacrifice and selflessness that the affairs of religion and the world are rectified, truth becomes living and enduring, human life becomes pleasant, and the tree of Islam becomes fruitful.

The phrase "وَ اللَّهُ رَؤُفٌ بِالْعِبادِ" (And Allah is Kind to [His] servants), which is indeed the opposite of what came in the previous verses about the hypocrites who spread corruption on earth—"fahasbuhu jahannam wa labi'sa al-mihad" (Hell is enough for him, and an evil resting place it is)—might be an indication that while Allah is the One who granted life to man, He is the same One who purchases it and pays the highest price, which is His own pleasure, to man.

It is noteworthy that the seller is "man," the buyer is Allah, the commodity is "life," and the price of the transaction is the pleasure of His Pure Essence. This is while in other instances, the price of such transactions is mentioned as eternal Paradise and salvation from Hell; for example, He says: "Indeed, Allah has purchased from the believers their lives and their properties [in exchange] for that they will have Paradise. They fight in the cause of Allah, so they kill and are killed..." [1]. Perhaps for this reason, the verse under discussion begins with the partitive "man" (وَ مِنَ النَّاسِ—And among the people), meaning only some people are capable of performing this extraordinary act and seeking only Allah's pleasure as the price for sacrificing their lives. However, in verse 111 of Surah At-Tawbah cited above, all believers are invited to this transaction with Allah in exchange for eternal Paradise.

There is also another possibility in the interpretation of the phrase "وَ اللَّهُ رَؤُفٌ بِالْعِبادِ" and its relevance to the beginning of the verse: it intends to clarify the reality that the existence of such loyal and selfless individuals among the people stems from Allah's kindness and mercy toward His servants. For if such self-sacrificing humans did not exist in human societies, the pillars of religion and society would collapse; but the Merciful Allah, through these selfless friends of His, prevents the destruction caused by enemies.

In any case, this verse, considering the circumstances of revelation explained in detail, is one of the greatest virtues of Ali عليه السلام, which has appeared in most Islamic sources. It is so striking that Mu'awiyah, due to his specific enmity toward Ali عليه السلام, according to a narration, was so troubled by this virtue that he bribed "Samurah ibn Jundab" with four hundred thousand dirhams to say that this verse was revealed (according to a fabricated hadith) regarding Abd al-Rahman ibn Muljam, the killer of Ali عليه السلام. That criminal hypocrite did so, but as expected, not a single person accepted this fabricated hadith [2].

[^1]: Surah At-Tawbah, Verse 111. [^2]: Ibn Abi al-Hadid narrated the story of this transaction in the Commentary on Nahj al-Balagha, Vol. 4, p. 73.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ فِى ٱلسِّلْمِ كَآفَّةً وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (٢٠٨)
O you who have faith! Enter into submission, all together, and do not follow in Satan’s steps; he is indeed your manifest enemy. (208)
فَإِن زَلَلْتُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْكُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٢٠٩)
And should you stumble after the manifest proofs that have come to you, know that Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (209)

Commentary

Islam is the Religion of Peace and Harmony

After referring to two groups (the group of very sincere believers and the corrupt hypocrites) in the preceding verses, the first verse under discussion addresses all believers, stating: "O you who have believed, enter into peace and harmony, all of you" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ادْخُلُوا فِي السِّلْمِ كَافَّةً).

The words "Silm" and "Salam" linguistically mean peace and tranquility, and some have interpreted them to mean obedience. This verse invites all individuals with faith to peace, harmony, and submission to the command of Allah.

From the concept of this verse, it can be inferred that peace and tranquility are only possible in the light of faith. Relying solely on material laws will never eradicate war, insecurity, and anxiety from the world, because the material world and attachment to it are always the source of conflicts and clashes. If the spiritual power of faith does not control man, peace is impossible.

Rather, it can be said from the universal invitation of this verse—which calls all believers without exception, regardless of language, race, geographical region, or social class, to peace and sincerity—that in the light of faith in Allah, the formation of a single global government where peace manifests everywhere under its shadow is possible.

Fundamentally, in the face of factors of division (language, race, etc.), a strong bond of connection among human hearts is necessary, and this bond is only faith in Allah, which transcends these differences.

Faith in Allah and submission to His command is the point of unity for human society and the secret to the connection between tribes and nations. A striking example of this can be observed in the Hajj pilgrimage: how people of different colors, from diverse races, possessing discordant languages, ethnicities, and geographical regions, all stand beside one another like brothers and participate in that great spiritual ceremony, looking upon each other with ultimate peace and sincerity. If we compare this with the system governing countries lacking faith in Allah—where insecurity regarding life, property, honor, and dignity prevails—the difference between faithful and faithless societies in terms of "Silm," peace, harmony, and tranquility becomes clear.

Another possibility has also been suggested in the interpretation of the verse: that some of the People of the Book (Jews and Christians), when they entered Islam, remained loyal to some of their previous beliefs or programs. Therefore, they were commanded to enter Islam with their whole being and be submissive to all its instructions [1]. Then it adds: "and do not follow the footsteps of Satan; indeed, he is to you a clear enemy" (وَ لَا تَتَّبِعُوا خُطُواتِ الشَّيْطانِ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ).

As mentioned in the commentary of verse 168 of this same Surah, many deviations and satanic whisperings occur gradually, and each stage is, in fact, one of the "footsteps of Satan."

"Khutuwat" is the plural of "Khutwah" (on the pattern of "Sufrah"), meaning a step or pace. Here too, this reality is repeated: that deviation from peace and justice, and surrendering to motives of enmity, hostility, war, and bloodshed, begins from simple and small stages and leads to acute and dangerous stages. According to the famous Arabic proverb, "The beginning of a fight is a slap," sometimes a small movement born of enmity ignites the fire of a devastating war. Therefore, the faithful individuals addressed in this verse must be vigilant from the very beginning and extinguish the small sparks of enmity and hostility.

It is noteworthy that this expression appears five times in the Holy Quran and has been emphasized in various contexts.

Some commentators have narrated that "Abdullah bin Salam" and his companions, who were Jews and had accepted Islam, asked the Prophet of Islam for permission to recite the Torah in prayer and to act upon some of its instructions. The above verse was revealed and forbade them from following the footsteps of Satan [1]. This occasion of revelation also shows that Satan influences man step by step, and one must stand against him at the very first steps so as not to reach dangerous stages.

The phrase "إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ" (Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy) contains a vivid and clear argument, saying:

Satan's enmity toward you is not something hidden or concealed; from the beginning of Adam's creation, he girded himself for enmity against him and swore that if he could, he would mislead everyone except the sincere ones who are beyond his reach. In such a state, how can you surrender to his whisperings?

In the next verse, He warns all believers: "But if you slip after (all these) clear signs and programs have come to you, and you surrender to the whisperings of Satan and take a step contrary to peace and harmony, then know (that you cannot escape the grip of Allah's justice), for Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise" (فَإِنْ زَلَلْتُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْكُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ).

The program is clear, the path is clear, and the destination is also known; despite this, there is no room for slipping or accepting satanic whisperings! If you deviate, you are certainly the one at fault, and know that the All-Powerful and Wise Allah will punish you justly.

"Bayyinat" means clear proofs and has a broad meaning that includes both intellectual arguments and what has been clarified for the people through revelation or miracles.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 5, p. 207; and Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 2, p. 97. However, considering that "kaffatan" (all of you) refers to the generality of the believers and not the generality of Islamic instructions (in fact, it is a "hal" [circumstantial adverb] for "those who believe," not for "peace"), the first interpretation seems more correct.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 2, p. 832.

Ayah

هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن يَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى ظُلَلٍ مِّنَ ٱلْغَمَامِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (٢١٠)
Do they await anything but that Allah [’s command] should come to them in the shades of the clouds, with the angels, and the matter be decided [once for all]? To Allah all matters are returned. (210)

Commentary

Unreasonable and Improper Expectations

Although this verse may appear to be among the complex verses of the Quran, careful attention to its expressions removes the ambiguity.

Here, the address is directed to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). In fact, following the discussion in the previous verse—which asked whether all these clear signs and reasons are not sufficient to prevent man's lapse and to rescue him from the clutches of the "manifest enemy," namely Satan—Allah says in this verse: "Do they wait for anything but that Allah and the angels should come to them in the shadows of clouds" and provide them with other proofs, even though such a thing is impossible? And even if it were not impossible, what necessity would there be for it? (هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ اللَّهُ فِي ظُلَلٍ مِنَ الْغَمامِ وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ). [^1] "While the matter has already been decided" (وَ قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ).

Regarding what is meant by the "matter being decided":

[^1]: "Zulal" (ظُلَل) is the plural of "Zullah" (ظُلَّة), referring to anything that casts a shadow. "Ghamam" (غَمام) means cloud and here refers to the genus.

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan considers it to mean the conclusion of the accounting of humanity on the Day of Resurrection, with the people of Paradise entering Paradise and the people of Hell entering Hell; thus, it would refer to the Hereafter. However, the apparent context of the verse relates to this world. Therefore, it is not unlikely that it refers to the descent of divine punishment upon the obstinate disbelievers, which has also been mentioned as a possibility in the words of Tabarsi and others.

It is also possible that it refers to the completion of the task of propagation and the explanation of all truths, which were referred to as "clear proofs" (Bayyinat) in the previous verse. Given this, their expectation is meaningless. Even if it were possible for Allah and the angels to appear before them, no need for it would be felt, because everything necessary for their guidance has been provided to them sufficiently. According to this interpretation, there is no omitted word (taqdir) in the verse, and the very words of the verse are expressive; thus, the interrogation in the verse is a rhetorical denial.

However, a group of commentators do not consider the interrogation to be a denial but rather a type of threat for the sinners and those who have followed Satanic programs—whether this threat is for the punishment of the Hereafter or this world. Therefore, they assume the word "Command" (Amr) is omitted before the word "Allah," so the meaning of the verse becomes: "Do these people, with these actions of theirs, want the Command of Allah and His angels to arrive for their punishment and torment, so they may be afflicted with the punishment of this world or the Hereafter, and their matter be concluded?"

But the interpretation mentioned first seems more appropriate and does not require assuming omitted words.

In short, there are three interpretations regarding the verse:

  1. The meaning is that Allah has sufficiently completed the proof (itmam al-hujjah), and obstinate individuals should not wait for Allah and the angels to come to them and recount the truths, as this is impossible, and even if it were possible, it would not be necessary.

  2. The meaning is: Do they, with this obstinacy in disbelief, wait for the command of divine punishment and the angels of torment to arrive and uproot them?

  3. The meaning is: Do they, by this conduct of theirs, wait for the Resurrection to be established and the command of punishment along with the divine angels to arrive, for everyone's account to be settled, and for them to meet their retribution? [^1]

The expression "in the shadows of clouds" (ظُلَلٍ مِنَ الْغَمامِ)—according to the second and third interpretations—is, as many commentators state, an indication that divine punishment overshadows them suddenly like clouds. Especially since man expects the falling of the rain of mercy upon seeing a cloud, it is more painful when a punishment in the form of a lightning bolt or the like descends from it (note that the punishment of some previous nations was also in the form of lightning bolts that descended from clouds). [^2] However, according to the first interpretation, it might refer to the superstitious belief of the disbelievers who imagined that God sometimes descends from the sky while clouds cast shadows over Him. [^3]

At the end of the verse, He says: "And to Allah all matters are returned" (وَ إِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ).

Matters related to the sending of prophets, the revelation of heavenly books, and the clarification of truths return to Him, just as the matter of accounting, punishment, retribution, and reward returns to Him.

[^1]: In the first interpretation, there is no omitted word, while in the second and third interpretations, the word "Command" (Amr) must be assumed before "Allah." [^2] and [^3]: Refer to verse 189 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara and its commentary in Volume 15 of Tafsir Nemouneh.

Key Point: The Vision of Allah is Impossible!

Undoubtedly, sensory observation only occurs in the case of bodies that possess color, place, and location. Therefore, it is meaningless regarding the Essence of Allah, who is beyond time and space.

His Pure Essence is seen neither in this world with this eye nor in the Hereafter. The rational proofs for this issue are so clear that they render us independent of detailed elaboration.

Yet, unfortunately, a group of Sunni scholars, relying on some weak hadiths and certain metaphorical (mutashabih) verses, insist that Allah will be seen in the Resurrection with these very eyes, and that He takes the form of a body and possesses color and place. Some have considered the verse under discussion as referring to this meaning, perhaps not realizing what corruptions and problems arise from this path.

Of course, observing Allah with the "eye of the heart" is possible both in this world and in the next, and certainly in the Resurrection, where His Pure Essence has a stronger manifestation and appearance, this observation will be more powerful.

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), we read that in response to the question "Will Allah be seen on the Day of Resurrection?" he said: "Exalted is Allah from such a thing, and greatly Exalted." Then he added:

"Indeed, the eyes do not perceive except that which has color and quality, while Allah the Almighty is the Creator of colors and quality." [^1]

Regarding the impossibility of seeing Allah in this world and the Hereafter, a sufficient explanation has been given under various verses of Tafsir Nemouneh, including under verse 103 of Surah Al-An'am: "Vision perceives Him not, but He perceives [all] vision." It has been discussed even more extensively in the fourth volume of Payam-e-Quran from pages 221 to 250.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 753.

Ayah

سَلْ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ كَمْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍۭ بَيِّنَةٍ ۗ وَمَن يُبَدِّلْ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٢١١)
Ask the Children of Israel how many a manifest sign We had given them. Whoever changes Allah’s blessing after it has come to him, indeed Allah is severe in retribution. (211)

Commentary

The Transformation of Blessing into Punishment

This verse is, in fact, one of the manifestations of the previous verses; for the preceding verses spoke of the believers, the disbelievers, and the hypocrites—disbelievers who, out of stubbornness, ignored clear signs and evidences and resorted to making excuses. The Children of Israel are one of the clearest examples of this meaning.

It says: "Ask the Children of Israel how many clear signs We gave them?" But they ignored these clear signs and spent Divine blessings in the wrong way (سَلْ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ كَمْ آتَيْناهُمْ مِنْ آيَةٍ بَيِّنَةٍ).

Then it adds: "And whoever changes the blessing of Allah after it has come to him (and misuses it, shall be seized by Allah’s severe punishment), for Allah is severe in retribution" (وَ مَنْ يُبَدِّلْ نِعْمَةَ اللَّهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ).

The meaning of "changing the blessing" is that a human being employs the material and spiritual resources and capabilities at his disposal in deviant paths and sin. We know that Allah bestowed all kinds of blessings upon the Children of Israel: great Prophets, powerful rulers, and abundant material resources. However, they neither benefited from those Divine educators nor made correct use of the material favors. In this way, they committed the "transformation of blessing," and for this very reason, they became wanderers in this world, and a painful punishment awaits them in the Hereafter.

The fact that He says: "سَلْ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ" (Ask the Children of Israel) is so that a confession may be extracted from them, and also to serve as a lesson for the Muslims and a warning to those who do not make the necessary use of Divine blessings and favors.

The issue of changing the blessing and the painful fate resulting from it is not exclusive to the Children of Israel; any people or nation that falls into it will be afflicted with Allah’s severe punishment in this world and the next.

Even now, the industrial world is afflicted with this great misery. Although Allah has placed favors, blessings, and possibilities at the disposal of modern man that are unprecedented in any period of history, due to distance from the Divine teachings of the Prophets, man has fallen into the "transformation of blessing." He has employed them in a terrifying manner toward his own destruction and annihilation, creating the most destructive weapons for the ruin of the world, or utilizing his material power for the expansion of oppression, colonialism, and exploitation, turning the world into an insecure place in every respect.

"The blessing of Allah" in this verse could refer to the Divine verses, and changing them would mean their distortion (tahrif); or it could have a broad meaning that includes all Divine resources and favors, and the second meaning is preferred.

Ayah

زُيِّنَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَيَسْخَرُونَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۘ وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (٢١٢)
Worldly life has been glamorized for the faithless, and they ridicule the faithful. But those who are Godwary shall be above them on the Day of Resurrection, and Allah provides for whomever He wishes without any reckoning. (212)

Occasion of Revelation

The famous commentator, Ibn Abbas, states: This verse was revealed concerning the aristocratic minority and the leaders of the Quraysh who enjoyed a very luxurious life. They used to mock and ridicule a group of steadfast believers from the early days of Islam, such as Ammar, Bilal, and others, who were poor and destitute in terms of material life. They would say: "If the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله possessed any status and was truly from God, the nobles and elites would have followed him." The above verse was revealed and responded to their baseless claims.

Commentary

The World-Worshipping Disbelievers

The aforementioned occasion of revelation, which states the verse refers to the self-centered nobles of the Quraysh, does not prevent us from deriving a general and universal rule from it, or considering it a complement to the previous verse regarding the Jews. The verse says: "Beautified for those who disbelieve is the life of this world" (زُيِّنَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا).

Consequently, they have become intoxicated with the wine of pride "and they ridicule those who believe" (وَيَسْخَرُونَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا), who may occasionally be empty-handed.

This is while "those who fear Allah are above them on the Day of Resurrection" (they are in the highest heights of Paradise, while these are in the lowest depths of Hell) (وَالَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ).

This is because, in that world, spiritual statuses take on an objective form, and the believers will be placed in high degrees; it is as if they are soaring above the heavens while these [disbelievers] are descending into the depths of the earth.

And this is not a matter of surprise, "for Allah provides for whom He wills without account" (وَاللَّهُ يَرْزُقُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ).

These words are, in fact, glad tidings and comfort for the poor believers, and a warning and threat to the arrogant and faithless wealthy.

There is also the possibility that the final sentence refers to the fact that Allah will grant the believers limitless sustenance in the future, just as this reality was realized with the advancement of Islam.

The "accountless" nature of Allah's provision to the people of faith indicates that Divine rewards and blessings are never merely in proportion to our deeds; rather, they are according to His Generosity and Grace, and we know that His Grace and Generosity have no bounds or limits.

Key Points

Faithless and world-worshipping individuals, who are severely enchanted by the glitz and glamour of the world and whose horizon of vision does not extend beyond the four walls of matter, always consider material possibilities as the scale for evaluating all values. For this reason, in their short-sighted and infirm minds, those whose hands are empty of wealth lack personality, and thus they subject them to ridicule. Meanwhile, the insightful believers, who do not look upon this handful of dust [the world] with such regard, see material values as colorless in the face of spiritual values and look upon them like children's toys. And if they seek the material blessings of the world, it is because they search for the Hereafter within them.

One question remains here: what is the meaning of the passive verb "Zuyyina" (has been beautified) and who is its subject? Who is it that has beautified the life of the world in the eyes of the disbelievers? You will read the answer to this question under the commentary of verse 14 of Surah Al-Imran.

Ayah

كَانَ ٱلنَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ وَأَنزَلَ مَعَهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ فِيمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ وَمَا ٱخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ فَهَدَى ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لِمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٢١٣)
Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed, and none differed in it except those who had been given it, after the manifest proofs had come to them, out of envy among themselves. Then Allah guided those who had faith to the truth of what they differed in, by His will, and Allah guides whomever He wishes to a straight path. (213)

Commentary

The Path to Attaining Unity

After describing the state of the believers, the hypocrites, and the disbelievers in the preceding verses, this verse turns toward a fundamental, general, and comprehensive discussion regarding the emergence of religion, its objectives, and its various stages.

First, It states: "Mankind was [in the beginning] a single community" (كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً). [^1] In those days, no conflict existed among them; human life and society were simple, natures (fitrah) were untouched, and the motives of passion, desire, discord, and strife among them were negligible. They worshipped Allah according to the command of their innate nature and performed their simple duties before Him (this was the first stage of human life), which likely filled the interval between the time of Adam and Noah.

Subsequently, human life took on a social form, and it had to be so, for man was created for perfection, and his perfection is only secured within the heart of society (and this was the second stage of human life).

However, with the emergence of society, differences and conflicts arose—both in terms of faith and belief, and in terms of action and the determination of the rights and entitlements of every individual and group in society. At this point, humanity became thirsty for the laws, teachings, and guidance of the Prophets to end their disputes in various aspects (this was the third stage).

"Thereupon Allah raised Prophets as bearers of glad tidings and as warners" (فَبَعَثَ اللَّهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَ مُنْذِرِينَ).

(And this was the fourth stage.) At this time, through the warnings of the Prophets and by turning their attention toward the Origin and the Resurrection—and the other world where they would receive the reward or punishment for their deeds—humans became prepared to receive Divine decrees and sound laws that could end disputes and ensure the health of society and the well-being of individuals. Therefore, It says: "And He sent down with them the Scripture with the truth that it might judge between the people concerning that in which they differed" (وَ أَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ).

In this way, faith in the Prophets and adherence to their teachings and the Heavenly Books poured water on the fire of disputes and extinguished it (and this was the fifth stage).

This state continued for a time, but gradually, satanic whisperings and the surging waves of carnal desires took their toll among a group. Through incorrect interpretations of the Prophets' teachings and the Heavenly Books, and by adapting them to their own heart's desires, they raised the flag of discord once again. However, this discord differed from the previous ones. The source of the earlier disputes was ignorance and lack of awareness, which were removed by the mission of the Prophets and the revelation of the Heavenly Books. In contrast, the source of the later disputes was oppression, stubbornness, and conscious deviation from the path of truth—in a word, "transgression" (baghy). Thus, in the continuation of this verse, It says:

"And none differed concerning it except those who had been given it, after clear signs had come to them, out of animosity [and transgression] among themselves" (وَ مَا اخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ).

(And this was the sixth stage.) Here, people were divided into two groups: the true believers who were submissive to the truth—they returned to the Heavenly Books and the teachings of the Prophets to end the new disputes and reached the truth. Therefore, It says: "Then Allah guided those who believed to the truth of that in which they differed, by His permission" (while the faithless, oppressive, and selfish individuals remained in misguidance and discord) (فَهَدَى اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لِمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ مِنَ الْحَقِّ بِإِذْنِهِ).

(And this was the seventh stage.) At the end of the verse, It says: "And Allah guides whom He wills (and deems worthy) to a straight path" (وَ اللَّهُ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ).

This is an indication that the Divine Will, which is intertwined with His Wisdom, is not arbitrary or without account, and is free from any undue discrimination.

All those who possess a pure intention and a spirit of submission to the truth are included in His guidance. Their ideological errors are corrected, they are granted special insights, their success in finding the right path is increased, they are kept away from the disputes and brawls of the faithless world-worshippers, and He grants them peace of soul, certainty of heart, and health of body and spirit.

[^1]: "Ummah" refers to any group among whom there is some kind of bond or unity, whether religious unity or in terms of time and place (Mufradat al-Raghib). Further discussion regarding the meaning of "Ummah" appears in relevant verses.

Key Points

1- Religion and Society

From the above verse, the reality is clearly derived that religion and human society are, in truth, inseparable. No society can have a sound life without religion and faith in God and the Resurrection.

Human laws, in addition to often being a cause of division and scattering among nations, do not originate from an internal guarantee of execution—namely, faith in God; they only create an extrinsic responsibility and cannot fully end disputes and conflicts. Human experiments in these last few centuries have well proven this reality; the so-called civilized but faithless world commits atrocities and sins that were never seen in underdeveloped societies.

Furthermore, the logic of Islam regarding the non-separation of religion from politics—meaning the administration of Islamic society—also becomes clear.

2- The Beginning of the Emergence of Sharia

From the above verse, it also implicitly becomes clear that the beginning of the emergence of religion and faith in the true sense of the word was the same time as the emergence of human society in its true sense. Therefore, it is not surprising that the first Ulul-'Azm Prophet (Arch-Prophet) and possessor of a Book, law, and Sharia was Prophet Noah (peace be upon him), not Prophet Adam.

3- The Middle East: The Center of the Emergence of Great Religions

From the above verse, one can also find the answer to the question: why did all the great Divine religions arise from the Middle East region? (Islam, Christianity, Judaism, the religion of Abraham, etc.) Because, as history testifies, the cradle of human civilization began to move in this region of the world, and the first great civilizations arose from here. Given the strong relationship between religion and civilization and the dire need of civilized societies for religion to prevent destructive disputes and conflicts, it becomes clear that religions had to arise from here.
