And if we see that Islam arose from an underdeveloped environment like the Mecca and Medina of that day, it was because this region was situated on the path of several great civilizations of that time: the Persian civilization and the remnants of the Babylonian civilization to the northeast of the Arabian Peninsula, the Roman civilization to the north, the ancient Egyptian civilization to the northwest, and the Yemenite civilization to the south.

In fact, the center of the emergence of Islam is the center of a circle around which all the important civilizations of that time were situated (reflect on this).

4- Ending Disputes: One of the Most Important Goals of Religion

Divine religions pursue many goals, including the refinement of human souls and bringing man to the station of proximity to Allah; however, certainly one of the most important goals has been the removal of disputes.

For races, ethnicities, languages, and geographical regions have always been factors of separation for human societies. That which can serve as a connecting link to bind all the children of Adam—from every race, language, ethnicity, and geographical region—together is Divine religion, which breaks down all these borders and gathers all humans under one banner, an example of which can be observed in the politico-religious rituals of the Hajj.

And if we see that some religions have become a cause of dispute and conflict, it is due to their being mixed with superstitions and blind prejudices; otherwise, untouched Heavenly religions are considered a factor of unity everywhere.

5- An Argument for the Infallibility of the Prophets

"Allamah Tabataba'i" in Al-Mizan, after dividing the infallibility ('Ismah) of the Prophets into three branches: 1- Infallibility from error when receiving revelation, 2- Infallibility from error in propagating the message, 3- Infallibility from sin and that which causes the violation of the sanctity of servitude—states: The verse under discussion is evidence for infallibility from error in the reception of revelation and the propagation of the message. This is because the goal of their mission was to give glad tidings and warnings to the people and to clarify the truth in belief and action and thereby guide them; certainly, this goal is impossible without infallibility in receiving revelation and propagating the message.

The third branch of infallibility can also be derived from the verse, for if an error occurs in the propagation of the message, it would itself be a factor for dispute. And if a lack of harmony between the actions and words of the proclaimers of revelation occurs through disobedience, that too is a factor for dispute. Therefore, from the above verse, indications of infallibility in all three sections can be utilized. [^1]

[^1]: Adapted from Al-Mizan, Vol. 2, p. 134 (under verse 213 of Surah Al-Baqarah).

Ayah

أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِكُم مَّثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلِكُم ۖ مَّسَّتْهُمُ ٱلْبَأْسَآءُ وَٱلضَّرَّآءُ وَزُلْزِلُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَقُولَ ٱلرَّسُولُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ مَتَىٰ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ نَصْرَ ٱللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ (٢١٤)
Do you suppose that you will enter paradise though there has not yet come to you the like of [what befell] those who went before you? Stress and distress befell them and they were convulsed until the apostle and the faithful who were with him said, ‘When will Allah’s help [come]?’ Behold! Allah’s help is indeed near! (214)

Occasion of Revelation

Some commentators have stated: When fear, dread, and severity overcame the Muslims during the Battle of the Trench (Ahzab) and they were under siege, this verse was revealed, inviting them to patience and steadfastness and promising them help and victory. It has also been said that when the Muslims were defeated in the Battle of Uhud, Abdullah ibn Ubayy said to them: "How long will you give yourselves up to be killed? If Muhammad were a Prophet, Allah would not have subjected his companions to captivity and slaughter." At this moment, the above verse was revealed. [^1]

Commentary

You Shall Not Enter Paradise Without Being Tested!

It appears from the above verse that a group of believers imagined that the primary factor for entering Paradise was merely the profession of faith in God, and that following this, they should not have to endure any discomfort or suffering; they thought that without exerting effort and struggle, Allah would set all affairs right and destroy the enemies.

In response to this incorrect thinking, the Quran points to the perpetual Sunnah (tradition) of Allah, stating: "Or do you think that you will enter Paradise while such [trial] has not yet come to you as came to those who passed on before you?" (أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَنْ تَدْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ وَ لَمَّا يَأْتِكُمْ مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ).

"They were touched by poverty and hardship and were shaken until [even their] messenger and those who believed with him said, 'When is the help of Allah?'" (مَسَّتْهُمُ الْبَأْساءُ وَ الضَّرَّاءُ وَ زُلْزِلُوا حَتَّى يَقُولَ الرَّسُولُ وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ مَتى‌ نَصْرُ اللَّهِ).

And because they exerted the utmost of their steadfastness in the face of these events and clung to the handle of Divine grace, it was said to them: "Unquestionably, the help of Allah is near." (أَلا إِنَّ نَصْرَ اللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ).

"Ba’sa" (البأساء) is derived from the root "Ba’s" (بأس). According to Mu'jam Maqayis al-Lughah, it originally means severity and the like, and it refers to any kind of suffering, torment, and discomfort. Brave individuals who resist intensely on the battlefield are called "Ba’is" or "Dhu al-Ba’s."

And "Darra" (الضراء), according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, is the opposite of "Sarra" (what causes joy and benefit). Therefore, it encompasses any kind of loss that affects a human being regarding life, property, reputation, and the like.

The phrase "مَتى‌ نَصْرُ اللَّهِ" (When will the help of Allah come?), which was uttered by Prophets and believers in times of extreme severity, does not mean doubt or uncertainty regarding this matter, nor is it an objection or criticism. Rather, it was posed as a plea and an expression of anticipation. Consequently, following it, glad tidings were given to them by Allah that His help is near.

The possibility that the phrase "مَتى‌ نَصْرُ اللَّهِ" was uttered by a group of believers and the phrase "أَلا إِنَّ نَصْرَ اللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ" was uttered by the Prophet of God صلى الله عليه وآله—as mentioned by some commentators—seems very unlikely.

In any case, the above verse points to one of the Divine traditions that has been prevalent among all nations. It warns believers in all centuries and eras that to achieve victory, success, and to attain Heavenly blessings, they must welcome difficulties and make sacrifices. In reality, these difficulties are a test that nurtures the believers and distinguishes those with true faith from those who merely pretend to believe.

The expression "الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ" (those who passed before you) tells the Muslims: You are not the only ones who have been afflicted with various difficulties from enemies in the straits of life; rather, previous nations were also afflicted with these same hardships and severities to the point that sometimes the knife reached the bone and their cry for help was raised.

Fundamentally, the secret of human perfection and progress lies in this: individuals and nations must be placed in the harsh furnaces of events to become tempered like hardened steel, so that their inner talents may flourish and their faith in Allah may grow stronger. Meanwhile, the worthy, resilient, and faithful individuals are distinguished from the unworthy, and their ranks are separated. We conclude this discussion with a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Khabbab ibn al-Aratt, who was one of the true Mujahideen of early Islam, says: "I complained to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله about the harassment of the polytheists. He said: 'Nations before you were tortured with various calamities, but this never turned them away from their religion, to the point that a saw would be placed on the head of some of them and they would be split into two pieces... By Allah, the program of Allah's religion will be completed and will reach victory until a rider will travel the distance between Sana'a and Hadramaut (and the terrifying deserts of Arabia) fearing nothing but Allah... but you are in a hurry.'" [^1]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1, p. 308. [^2]: Al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 1, p. 243 and Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 6, p. 20.

Ayah

يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ مَاذَا يُنفِقُونَ ۖ قُلْ مَآ أَنفَقْتُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ فَلِلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۗ وَمَا تَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمٌ (٢١٥)
They ask you as to what they should spend. Say, ‘Let whatever wealth you spend be for the parents, relatives, orphans, the needy, and the traveller.’ Allah indeed knows whatever good you do. (215)

Occasion of Revelation

"Amr ibn al-Jamuh" was an elderly and wealthy man. He said to the Prophet: "From what should I give charity, and to whom?"

The above verse was revealed and provided him with the answer. [^1]

Commentary

From what should one spend?

In the Holy Quran, there are numerous verses—especially in Surah Al-Baqarah—regarding spending (Infaq) and giving in the way of Allah, and great rewards and virtues have been mentioned for this act. This very matter caused people to ask the Prophet about the details of this deed, wanting to know what things they should spend and upon whom they should spend them.

Therefore, in the verse under discussion, He says: "They ask you what they should spend" (يَسْئَلُونَكَ ما ذا يُنْفِقُونَ‌).

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 309; Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 2, p. 91; Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 6, p. 23.

Then He adds: "Say: Whatever good (and any kind of beneficial material or spiritual capital) you spend should be for parents, relatives, orphans, the needy, and the wayfarer" (قُلْ ما أَنْفَقْتُمْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلِلْوالِدَيْنِ وَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ وَ الْيَتامى‌ وَ الْمَساكِينِ وَ ابْنِ السَّبِيلِ‌).

Certainly, the mention of these five groups is by way of providing clear examples; otherwise, it is not limited to them. Thus, the answer to them is, in reality, that both the objects from which they spend have a wide scope, as do the recipients of that spending.

Regarding the first part, by mentioning the word "Khayr" (good), which encompasses every type of useful deed, property, and capital, a complete and comprehensive answer is given to their question. It even includes spiritual matters such as knowledge, although the most prominent instance in the context of spending is wealth.

Regarding the second part, while the scope of spending is broad, priorities have also been stated. Certainly, parents and then needy relatives have priority in this matter, followed by orphans, then the needy, and even those who are not inherently poor but have become needy due to an incident, such as running out of funds during a journey.

At the end of the verse, He says: "And whatever good you do, indeed Allah is Aware of it" (وَ ما تَفْعَلُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ بِهِ عَلِيمٌ‌).

There is no need to show off or inform people of your work; how much better it is to keep your spending hidden for greater sincerity, for He who grants the reward is aware of everything, and He in whose hand is the recompense holds the account of all.

The phrase "وَ ما تَفْعَلُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ" (whatever good you do) has a broad meaning that includes all righteous deeds, not just spending in the way of Allah; for every good deed, Allah knows it, sees it, and grants a good reward for it.

Key Point: The Harmony of Question and Answer in the Verse

Some have imagined that the questioners in this verse asked about the objects that should be spent, but the answer was given regarding the expenditures and the people who are the recipients of spending, and that this is because knowing the recipient was more important. However, this is a mistake, because the Quran both answered their question and clarified the instances of spending. This is among the techniques of eloquence: to provide the answer to the question while also pointing to another important matter that was needed.

In any case, the phrase "ما أَنْفَقْتُمْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ" (whatever good you spend) says:

Spending can be from any good subject, and it includes all goodness, whether it be from wealth or services, from material subjects or spiritual ones.

Furthermore, the expression "Khayr" (good), used in an absolute sense, shows that wealth and riches are not inherently bad; rather, they are among the best means of good, provided they are utilized for goodness.

Also, the expression "Khayr" might point to the fact that spending must be free from any kind of reminder of favor (mann), injury (adha), or actions that damage the dignity of the recipients, such that it can be referred to as "absolute good."

Ayah

كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْقِتَالُ وَهُوَ كُرْهٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ وَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تَكْرَهُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ وَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تُحِبُّوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَهُوَ شَرٌّ لَّكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٢١٦)
Warfare has been prescribed for you, though it is repulsive to you. Yet it may be that you dislike something, which is good for you, and it may be that you love something, which is bad for you, and Allah knows and you do not know. (216)

Commentary

Sacrificing Life and Wealth

The previous verse primarily dealt with the spending of wealth, and in this verse, the discussion turns to the spending of lives in the way of Allah; both of these stand side by side in the field of sacrifice.

It states: "Fighting (against the enemy) has been ordained for you, though it is hateful to you" (كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْقِتالُ وَ هُوَ كُرْهٌ لَكُمْ).

The expression "written" (كُتِبَ) refers to the certainty and decisiveness of this Divine command.

Although "Kurh" (كره) has an infinitive meaning, here it carries the meaning of the past participle, i.e., "hateful" or "disliked." The fact that war is disliked and unpleasant—even if it is against the enemy and in the way of Allah—is a natural matter for ordinary human beings. This is because war involves the loss of wealth and lives, as well as various injuries and hardships. Of course, for those who are lovers of martyrdom in the way of Truth and those who possess a high level of gnosis (Ma'rifah), fighting the enemies of Truth is a sweet nectar that they pursue like thirsty travelers; certainly, their case is separate from that of the masses, especially at the dawn of Islam.

Then, it points to a general law and a fundamental principle that governs the creative (Takwini) and legislative (Tashri'i) laws of Allah, stating: "It may be that you dislike a thing while it is good for you and a source of prosperity and happiness" (وَ عَسى‌ أَنْ تَكْرَهُوا شَيْئاً وَ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ‌).

Conversely, withdrawing from war and seeking comfort might be pleasing to you, while in reality, it is not so: "And it may be that you love a thing while it is evil for you" (وَ عَسى‌ أَنْ تُحِبُّوا شَيْئاً وَ هُوَ شَرٌّ لَكُمْ‌).

And at the end, He states: "And Allah knows, and you do not know" (وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَ أَنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌).

The Lord of the universe declares with this decisive tone that human beings should not allow their own judgment to rule over matters concerning their destiny, for their knowledge is limited and insignificant from every perspective. Their knowns compared to their unknowns are like a drop against the sea. Just as in the creative laws of Allah, they are not aware of the secrets of the creation of all things and sometimes consider something useless while the progress of science reveals its important benefits, similarly, in legislative laws, they do not know many of the benefits (Masalih) and harms (Mafasid). Therefore, they might dislike something while their happiness lies within it, or be pleased with something while their misery lies within it.

Given their limited knowledge compared to the infinite knowledge of Allah, they must not frown upon Divine rulings. They must know for certain that if the Compassionate and Merciful Allah has legislated Jihad, Zakat, Fasting, and Hajj, it is all for their own benefit.

Attention to this reality nurtures the spirit of discipline and submission to Divine laws within a person, elevating their perception and vision beyond limited environments and linking them to the Unlimited—that is, the infinite knowledge of Allah.

Key Points

1- How can Jihad be unpleasant?

A question may arise here: how is this consistent with the innate (Fitri) nature of the principles of Divine rulings? If Jihad or other similar matters are innate, how can they be unpleasant to human nature?

In response to this question, one must note that innate matters manifest in a human being when they are accompanied by recognition (knowledge). For example, a human being innately seeks profit and opposes loss, provided they recognize the instance of profit and loss. However, if they fall into error in identifying it and perceive a beneficial matter as harmful, their nature will certainly be misled due to this mistake, and they will become averse to that beneficial thing. The reverse is also true.

Regarding Jihad, superficial individuals who only see the wounding, killing, and hardships of war may dislike it. However, far-sighted individuals who know that human honor, greatness, glory, and freedom lie in sacrifice and Jihad will certainly welcome it with open arms. Just as people ignorant of bitter and foul-tasting medicines are repulsed by them due to superficiality, once they realize that their health and salvation lie therein, they accept it with heart and soul.

2- A General Law

What is mentioned in the above verse is not limited to the issue of Jihad and war with enemies; rather, it unveils a general and universal law. It makes all the bitterness and hardships of obeying Allah's command easy and pleasant for a person. This is because, according to "And Allah knows, and you do not know," one realizes that Allah—Who is aware of everything and is Compassionate and Merciful toward His servants—has seen benefits in each of His commands that are the source of the servants' salvation and happiness. In this way, believing servants view all these commands like healing medicines and accept them with heart and soul.

Ayahs

يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلشَّهْرِ ٱلْحَرَامِ قِتَالٍ فِيهِ ۖ قُلْ قِتَالٌ فِيهِ كَبِيرٌ ۖ وَصَدٌّ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَكُفْرٌۢ بِهِۦ وَٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَإِخْرَاجُ أَهْلِهِۦ مِنْهُ أَكْبَرُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱلْفِتْنَةُ أَكْبَرُ مِنَ ٱلْقَتْلِ ۗ وَلَا يَزَالُونَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَرُدُّوكُمْ عَن دِينِكُمْ إِنِ ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن يَرْتَدِدْ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِۦ فَيَمُتْ وَهُوَ كَافِرٌ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢١٧)
They ask you concerning warfare in the holy month. Say, ‘It is an outrageous thing to fight in it, but to keep [people] from Allah’s way, and to be unfaithful to Him, and [to keep people from] the Holy Mosque, and to expel its people from it are more outrageous with Allah. And persecution is graver than killing. They will not cease fighting you until they turn you away from your religion, if they can. And whoever of you turns away from his religion and dies faithless—they are the ones whose works have failed in this world and the Hereafter. They shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (217)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَرْجُونَ رَحْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٢١٨)
Indeed those who are faithful and those who have migrated and waged jihad in the way of Allah—it is they who expect Allah’s mercy, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (218)

Circumstances of Revelation

It has been narrated that this verse was revealed concerning the "Sariyah of Abdullah ibn Jahsh." [^1] The event was as follows: Before the Battle of Badr, the Prophet of Islam called for Abdullah ibn Jahsh, gave him a letter, and sent eight of the Muhajireen with him. He commanded him not to open the letter until he had traveled for two days, and then to act according to its instructions. After two days of travel, he opened the letter and found the following: "Once you have opened this letter, proceed until you reach 'Nakhlah' (a land between Mecca and Ta'if); there, monitor the movements of the Quraysh and report the situation to us."

Abdullah related the matter to his companions and added: "The Prophet has forbidden me from forcing any of you on this path; therefore, whoever is prepared for martyrdom should come with me, and the others may return." They all set out with him. When they reached Nakhlah, they encountered a caravan of the Quraysh in which Amr ibn al-Hadrami was present. Since it was the last day of Rajab (one of the sacred months), they consulted one another regarding attacking them.

Some said: "If we leave them today, they will enter the sanctuary (Haram) and we will no longer be able to confront them." Ultimately, they bravely attacked them, killed Amr

[^1]: "Sariyah" refers to those Islamic battles in which the Prophet himself did not participate.

Some have said: A "Sariyah" consists of a group from the army numbering between 5 to 300 people.

It should be noted that "Sariyah" is derived from "Sari," which means something exquisite and precious; since the soldiers chosen for such missions are among the elite, they are called by this name.

Al-Mutarrizi says: "Sariyah" is derived from "Sura," which means traveling at night, because they usually move secretly.

Ibn Hajar, in his book "Al-Multaqatat," accepts this view and says: "Sariyah" is a group of the army that travels by night.

ibn al-Hadrami, and brought the caravan along with two prisoners to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. The Prophet said to them: "I did not command you to fight in the sacred months," and he did not interfere with the spoils or the prisoners. The warriors became distressed, and the Muslims began to reproach them. The polytheists also began to taunt, saying that Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله had declared fighting, bloodshed, and taking captives lawful in the sacred months. At this moment, the first verse was revealed. After this verse was revealed, Abdullah ibn Jahsh and his companions expressed that they had strived on this path to attain the reward of Jihad and asked the Prophet whether they would receive the reward of mujahideen or not. Then the second verse (Indeed, those who have believed and those who have emigrated...) was revealed.

Commentary

Fighting in the Sacred Months

As is evident from the circumstances of revelation, and as the tone of the verse briefly testifies, the first verse seeks to answer certain questions regarding Jihad and its exceptions.

It says: "They ask you about fighting in the sacred month" (يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الشَّهْرِ الْحَرامِ قِتالٍ فِيهِ).

Then it adds: "Say, 'Fighting therein is a great (sin)'" (قُلْ قِتالٌ فِيهِ كَبِيرٌ).

In this way, it decisively confirms the tradition that had existed among the Arabs since ancient times and the eras of previous Prophets regarding the prohibition of fighting in the sacred months (Rajab, Dhu al-Qi'dah, Dhu al-Hijjah, and Muharram).

Then it states: It is not the case that this law has no exceptions; a corrupt and mischief-making group should not be allowed to commit every kind of oppression, corruption, and sin under the umbrella of this law. It is true that Jihad in a sacred month is a grave matter, "but averting [people] from the way of Allah and disbelief in Him and [preventing access to] al-Masjid al-Haram and the expulsion of its people therefrom are greater [evil] in the sight of Allah" (وَ صَدٌّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَ كُفْرٌ بِهِ وَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ وَ إِخْراجُ أَهْلِهِ مِنْهُ أَكْبَرُ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ). [^1] Then it adds: "And fitnah (creating turmoil and diverting people from the religion of God) is greater than killing" (وَ الْفِتْنَةُ أَكْبَرُ مِنَ الْقَتْلِ).

This is because killing is a crime against a person's body, while fitnah is a crime against a person's soul, spirit, and faith. [^2] It then continues that Muslims should not be influenced by the misleading propaganda of the polytheists, because "they will not cease to fight you until they turn you back from your religion if they are able" (and in reality, they are satisfied with nothing less than this) (وَ لا يَزالُونَ يُقاتِلُونَكُمْ حَتَّى يَرُدُّوكُمْ عَنْ دِينِكُمْ إِنِ اسْتَطاعُوا).

Therefore, stand firm against them and pay no heed to their whisperings regarding the sacred month or otherwise. Then, it gives a serious warning to the Muslims regarding turning back from the religion of Allah, saying: "And whoever among you reverts from his religion and dies while he is a disbeliever—for those, their deeds have become worthless in this world and the Hereafter, and those are the companions of the Fire, they will abide therein eternally" (وَ مَنْ يَرْتَدِدْ مِنْكُمْ عَنْ دِينِهِ فَيَمُتْ وَ هُوَ كافِرٌ فَأُولئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمالُهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ وَ أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ).

What punishment could be more severe and terrifying than for all of a person's good deeds to be destroyed, benefiting him neither in this world nor in the Hereafter, and for him to be afflicted with eternal Divine punishment?

[^1]: According to the late Tabarsi in "Majma' al-Bayan" and Qurtubi in the commentary "Al-Jami'," Sadd (averting) is the subject (mubtada), and Kufr (disbelief) and Ikhraj ahlihi (expulsion of its people) are conjoined to it, while Akbar (greater) is the predicate (khabar).

[^2]: We have discussed the meaning of "Fitnah" in detail under verse 191 of this same Surah.

It is clear that good deeds have blessings and effects in both this world and the Hereafter; apostates are deprived of all these effects. Furthermore, apostasy causes all the effects of faith to be removed: their spouses are separated from them, and their property is inherited by their survivors.

In the next verse, it points to the opposite of this group and says: "Indeed, those who have believed and those who have emigrated and fought in the cause of Allah (and remained steadfast in their faith)—those expect the mercy of Allah, and Allah is Forgiving and Merciful" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ الَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا وَ جاهَدُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أُولئِكَ يَرْجُونَ رَحْمَتَ اللَّهِ وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ). Yes, this group, in the light of these three great acts (faith, emigration, and Jihad), even if they commit mistakes (as occurred in the story of Abdullah ibn Jahsh in the circumstances of revelation), may be subject to Divine grace and forgiveness. [^1]

Key Point: The Issue of "Ihabt" (Nullification) and "Takfir" (Expiation)

"Habt" (حبط), according to Raghib in "Al-Mufradat," originally means when an animal eats so much that its belly swells. Since this state causes the food to spoil and become ineffective, the word is used to mean becoming void and useless. Therefore, in "Mu'jam Maqayis al-Lughah," its meaning is mentioned as "nullity" (butlan). For this reason, in verse 16 of Surah Hud, it is mentioned alongside "void" (batil), where He says:

[^1]: We have discussed "Innate and National Apostates" (Murtad Fitri and Milli) under verse 106 of Surah An-Nahl, and a discussion will also follow in this second volume under verse 89 of Surah Al-Imran.

Those are the ones for whom there is nothing in the Hereafter but the Fire, and lost (habita) is what they did therein and void (batil) is what they used to do. (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ إِلَّا النَّارُ وَ حَبِطَ ما صَنَعُوا فِيها وَ باطِلٌ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ). As for "Ihabt" in the terminology of theologians and scholars of creed, it refers to the reward of previous good deeds being destroyed due to sins. Its opposite is "Takfir," which means the removal of the punishment and effects of previous sins because of future good deeds.

There is a debate among scholars of creed as to whether Ihabt and Takfir are correct regarding the reward of righteous deeds and the punishment of sins. According to the late Allamah Majlisi (may he rest in peace), the famous view among Imami theologians is the invalidity of Ihabt and Takfir. They simply say: reward is conditional upon a person leaving this world with faith, and punishment is conditional upon not leaving this world with Islam and repentance at the time of death. However, the Mu'tazilite scholars, looking at the outward appearance of some verses and narrations, believe in the validity of Ihabt and Takfir. [^1] Khwaja Nasir al-Din al-Tusi, in the book "Tajrid al-I'tiqad," considered Ihabt to be invalid and argued for this using both reason and tradition. His rational argument is that Ihabt entails injustice (for someone who had fewer rewards and more sins would, after Ihabt, be like someone who did no good at all, which would be a form of injustice toward him). As for the traditional evidence, the Quran explicitly says: So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it. [^2] (فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْراً يَرَهُ وَ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ). [^3]

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 5, p. 332.

[^2]: Surah Az-Zalzalah, verses 7-8.

[^3]: Tajrid al-I'tiqad, p. 327, Mustafawi edition.

Among the Mu'tazilite scholars, Abu Hashim combined Ihabt and Takfir and created the concept of "Muwazanah" (balancing), meaning that sin and reward are weighed against each other and subtracted from one another.

However, the truth is that Ihabt and Takfir are possible matters, and no injustice results from them; verses and narrations explicitly point to them. It seems that the opposition of the deniers boils down to a kind of "verbal dispute."

To explain: Sometimes a person works for many years and accumulates wealth with great hardship, but through one act of mismanagement or a whim, he loses it all—meaning his previous "merits" are "nullified" (habt).

Or conversely, he may have committed many mistakes and suffered great losses, but through one wise and calculated action, he compensates for them all; this is a type of "Takfir" (Takfir is a kind of covering and compensating). In spiritual matters, this same principle holds true.

Ayahs

۞ يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْخَمْرِ وَٱلْمَيْسِرِ ۖ قُلْ فِيهِمَآ إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنَـٰفِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَإِثْمُهُمَآ أَكْبَرُ مِن نَّفْعِهِمَا ۗ وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ مَاذَا يُنفِقُونَ قُلِ ٱلْعَفْوَ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٢١٩)
They ask you concerning wine and gambling. Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them, and some profits for the people, but their sinfulness outweighs their profit.’ And they ask you as to what they should spend. Say, ‘All that is surplus.’ Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may reflect (219)
فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۗ وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ ۖ قُلْ إِصْلَاحٌ لَّهُمْ خَيْرٌ ۖ وَإِن تُخَالِطُوهُمْ فَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ ٱلْمُفْسِدَ مِنَ ٱلْمُصْلِحِ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَأَعْنَتَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٢٢٠)
about the world and the Hereafter. And they ask you concerning the orphans. Say, ‘It is better to set right their affairs, and if you intermingle with them, they are of course your brothers: Allah knows those who cause corruption from those who set things right, and had Allah wished He would have put you to hardship.’ Indeed Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (220)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation for the first verse, it is said that a group of the Prophet's companions came to him and said: "Explain to us the ruling on wine and gambling, which deprive one of reason and waste wealth!" Then the first verse was revealed and provided them with the answer.

As for the occasion of revelation for the second verse, it is narrated in Tafsir al-Qummi from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) and in Majma' al-Bayan from Ibn Abbas as follows:

When the verse "وَ لا تَقْرَبُوا مالَ الْيَتِيمِ إِلَّا بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ‌" ("And do not approach the orphan's property except in a way that is best" [1]) and the verse "إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوالَ الْيَتامى‌ ظُلْماً إِنَّما يَأْكُلُونَ فِي بُطُونِهِمْ ناراً وَ سَيَصْلَوْنَ سَعِيراً" ("Indeed, those who unjustly consume the property of orphans only consume fire into their bellies, and they will burn in a blazing Fire" [2]) were revealed—prohibiting approaching the wealth and assets of orphans unless it benefits them, and forbidding the consumption of their property—people who had an orphan in their home distanced themselves from their guardianship and left them to their own devices. Some even expelled them from their homes, and those who did not expel them created a situation in the house that was no better than expulsion; they would not mix the orphan's food (prepared from the orphan's own wealth) with their own food. They would even cook for them separately, and after the orphan ate their specific meal in a corner of the room, if any was left over, they would store it for the orphan to eat next time, and if it spoiled, they would throw it away.

All of this was done to avoid the responsibility of consuming the orphan's wealth. This practice brought many difficulties for both the guardians and the orphans. Following this situation, they came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and asked about this manner of conduct. In response to them, this verse was revealed.

[1]: Surah Al-Isra, Verse 34. [2]: Surah An-Nisa, Verse 10.

Commentary

Answering Four Questions

The first verse begins with two questions regarding "wine" and "gambling." It says: "They ask you about wine and gambling" (يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَ الْمَيْسِرِ).

The word "Khamr" (wine) in its literal sense, according to Raghib, originally means to cover something; hence, the head-covering used by women is called "Khimar."

In Mu'jam Maqayis al-Lughah, a single root is mentioned for "Khamr" which denotes covering, mixing, and secret blending. Since wine covers the human intellect, it is called Khamr, as it causes intoxication, and intoxication casts a veil over the intellect, preventing a person from distinguishing between good and evil.

In Sharia terminology, "Khamr" does not only mean grape wine, but rather any intoxicating liquid, whether derived from grapes, raisins, dates, or anything else, even though linguistically there are specific names for various types of alcoholic beverages.

"Maysir" (gambling) is derived from the root "Yusr," which means ease and facility. Since gambling is considered by some people as an easy means to attain wealth and fortune, it is called Maysir.

Then, in response, He says: "Say, 'In them is great sin and [yet, some] benefit for people. But their sin is greater than their benefit'" (قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَ مَنافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَ إِثْمُهُما أَكْبَرُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِما).

Considering that the pre-Islamic Arabian society was heavily addicted to wine and gambling, the ruling for the prohibition of these two was revealed gradually and in several stages. If a degree of softness and leniency is observed in the tone of the verse, it is for this reason.

In this verse, the benefits and harms of these two are compared, and the superiority of their harms and heavy sin over their benefits is explicitly stated. Certainly, the material benefits that might occasionally be obtained through the sale of wine or gambling, or the imaginary benefits gained from the sedation of intoxication and the temporary forgetfulness of worries and sorrows, are very insignificant compared to the extraordinary moral, social, and health harms of these two.

Therefore, no rational person would submit to such extensive harm for the sake of that small benefit.

The word "Ithm" (sin), according to Mu'jam Maqayis al-Lughah, originally means slowness and falling behind. Since sins hold a person back from attaining good things, this term is applied to them. In fact, in some Quranic verses, no meaning other than this (slowness and delay) is appropriate, such as: "وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ أَخَذَتْهُ الْعِزَّةُ بِالْإِثْمِ‌" [1], "And when it is said to him, 'Fear Allah,' pride in the sin takes hold of him"—meaning his pride and illusory status make him slow in reaching piety.

In any case, Ithm refers to any action or thing that creates a state in the soul and intellect that prevents a person from reaching goodness and perfections. Therefore, the existence of "Ithm Kabir" (great sin) in wine and gambling is evidence of the negative impact of these two on attaining piety and spiritual and human perfections, the details of which will come in the "Key Points."

The third question raised in the above verse is about spending (Infaq). It says: "And they ask you what they should spend" (وَ يَسْئَلُونَكَ ما ذا يُنْفِقُونَ‌).

"Say, 'The excess [beyond needs]'" (قُلِ الْعَفْوَ).

[1]: Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 206.

In the commentary Ad-Durr al-Manthur, regarding the occasion of revelation for this part of the verse, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that a group of the companions of the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) asked: "Regarding your command to spend in the way of Allah, we do not know how much of our wealth we should spend. Should we give all of it to the needy in the way of Allah, or a portion of it?" [1] In response to them, the above verse was revealed, instructing them to observe "Afw" in their spending. Now let us see what "Afw" means here.

"Afw" originally—according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat—means the intention to take something, or something that is easy to take.

Since this meaning is very broad, it has been applied to various instances, including forgiveness and pardon, erasing an effect, the middle ground or mean of something, the surplus amount of something, and the best part of wealth. Apparently, the first two meanings are not compatible with the context of the verse, and it seems that one of the last three meanings is intended. That is, observe the middle ground in spending, or spend the surplus amount beyond your needs, or when spending, do not go for the worthless parts of your wealth; rather, spend from the best parts which are easy for you to take for yourselves due to their quality.

In Islamic narrations, the above verse has also been interpreted with this meaning. In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read that he said: "Al-Afw is the middle ground." [2] And in the commentary of Ali ibn Ibrahim, we read: "Neither stinginess nor extravagance." [3] And in Majma' al-Bayan, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام): "Afw is what is in excess of the year's food." [1]

Another possibility in the interpretation of this verse (though we have not seen it in the words of any other commentators) is that "Afw" carries its primary meaning, which is forgiveness and overlooking the lapses of others. According to this meaning, the interpretation of the verse would be: "Say, the best spending is the spending of forgiveness and pardon."

Considering the social conditions of the pre-Islamic Arabs and the location of the Quran's revelation, especially Mecca and Medina, which were at the peak of enmity, malice, and lack of forgiveness, this possibility is not far-fetched. Especially since the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself was the perfect example of this, as shown by his declaration of general amnesty for the polytheists of Mecca, who were the most stubborn and cold-hearted enemies of Islam. There is no obstacle to them asking about spending wealth, while their intense need for the "spending of forgiveness" causes the Quran to state what is more necessary in response. This is one of the aspects of eloquence (Fasahah and Balaghah) where the speaker leaves the direct answer to the questioner's query to address something more important.

There is no contradiction among these interpretations, and it is possible that all of them are encompassed within the concept of the verse.

Finally, at the end of the verse, He says: "Thus Allah makes clear to you the verses [of revelation] that you might give thought" (كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمُ الْآياتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَفَكَّرُونَ‌).

Immediately in the next verse, He identifies the primary focus of thought and reflection: "In this world and the Hereafter" (فِي الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ). Yes, all actions in material and spiritual life must be accompanied by thought and reflection. From this expression, two points become clear:

First, although a human is duty-bound to submit to Allah and His Prophet, this obedience is not blind obedience; rather, it is an obedience mixed with awareness. One must become aware of the secrets of divine rulings, not only regarding the prohibition of wine and gambling but in all fields, even if only in general, and perform them with correct understanding.

The meaning of this is not that obedience to divine rulings is conditional upon understanding their philosophy, but rather that parallel to this obedience, one must strive to become aware of the secrets and the spirit of Allah's laws.

Second: Thought must be applied in all fields—for the needs of the body and soul, the spirit and the physical form—because this world and the Hereafter are interconnected, and the ruin of one affects the other. Fundamentally, thinking about only one of these two in isolation cannot provide a human with a correct depiction of the reality of this universe, because each of these two is a part of this universe—the world being the smaller part and the Hereafter the greater part. Those who think only about one of these two do not have a correct understanding of the universe.

Then He addresses the fourth question and its answer, saying: "And they ask you about orphans" (وَ يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْيَتامى‌).

"Say, 'Improvement for them is best'" (قُلْ إِصْلاحٌ لَهُمْ خَيْرٌ).

"And if you mix your affairs with theirs, they are your brothers" (وَ إِنْ تُخالِطُوهُمْ فَإِخْوانُكُمْ‌) [1]. In this way, the Quran reminds Muslims that shunning the responsibility of guardianship over orphans and leaving them to themselves is not right. It is better to accept their guardianship, organize their affairs, and improve their situation. If their lives and wealth are mixed with yours, there is no problem, provided your intention is improvement and you treat them like a brother.

Then He adds: "And Allah knows the corrupter from the improver" (وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ الْمُفْسِدَ مِنَ الْمُصْلِحِ‌). Yes, He is aware of all your intentions, and He distinguishes those who intend to exploit the orphans' wealth—by mixing it with their own to squander it—from the truly sincere and kind-hearted.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "And if Allah had willed, He could have put you in difficulty (by ordering you to keep their wealth entirely separate while still being responsible for them, but Allah never does such a thing) for Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise" (وَ لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ لَأَعْنَتَكُمْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ‌). [1]

[1]: The conditional sentence here has an omitted part like "there is no harm in it" or "that is for you," and the expression "they are your brothers" serves as the reason for it. [1]: "A'natakum" is from the root "Anat," which originally means falling into a fearful matter. According to Mu'jam Maqayis al-Lughah, it originally means any kind of hardship.

Key Points

1- The Relationship Between the Four Rulings Above

As you have observed, four questions in the two verses above regarding the issues of "wine," "gambling," "spending," and "orphans" have appeared along with their answers. The mention of these four questions and answers together might be because people were truly involved with and troubled by these four issues; hence, they asked about these four subjects repeatedly (note that yas'alunaka is a present-tense verb indicating continuity).

There is also the possibility that all four cases are somehow related to financial matters: wine and gambling are causes of the destruction of wealth, spending is the cause of its flourishing, and the guardianship of orphans can be either beneficial or destructive.

Furthermore: spending has a public and universal aspect as well as an otherworldly and reformative aspect; wine and gambling have private, material, and corruptive aspects; and improving the affairs of orphans possesses both public and private aspects. Thus, they are examples of "thinking about this world and the Hereafter."

From here, the relationship between Khamr and Maysir (wine and gambling) also becomes clear, as both are causes of the destruction of wealth, the corruption of society, and various diseases of the human body and soul.

2- The Harms of Alcoholic Beverages

A- The Effect of Alcohol on Lifespan: One famous Western scientist states that for every 51 deaths among alcohol-addicted youths aged 21 to 23, there are not even 10 deaths among non-addicted youths. Another famous scientist has proven that twenty-year-olds who are expected to live fifty years do not live more than 35 years due to drinking alcohol. Based on experiments conducted by life insurance companies, it has been proven that the lifespan of alcohol addicts is 25 to 30 percent shorter than others. Statistics show that the average lifespan of alcohol addicts is around 35 to 50 years, whereas the average lifespan with the observance of health principles is 60 years and above.

B- The Effect of Alcohol on Offspring: A person who is drunk during conception transmits 35 percent of the symptoms of acute alcoholism to their child. If both the man and woman are drunk, 100 percent of the acute symptoms appear in the child. To better understand the effect of alcohol on children, we provide some statistics here: Children born prematurely: 45% from alcoholic parents, 31% from alcoholic mothers, and 17% from alcoholic fathers. Children unable to survive at birth: 6% from alcoholic fathers and 45% from alcoholic mothers. Children who were short in stature: 75% from alcoholic parents and 45% from alcoholic mothers. Children lacking sufficient intellectual and spiritual strength: 75% from both alcoholic mothers and fathers.

C- The Effect of Alcohol on Morality: In an alcoholic person, family affection and love for wife and children weaken, to the extent that it has been repeatedly seen that fathers have killed their own children with their own hands.

D- Social Harms of Alcohol: According to statistics prepared by the Institute of Forensic Medicine in the city of "Nyon" in 1961, the social crimes of alcoholics are as follows: Perpetrators of general murders: 50%. Assault and battery due to drinking alcohol: 77.8%. Thefts related to alcoholics: 88.5%. Sexual crimes related to alcoholics: 88.8%. These statistics show that the overwhelming majority of major crimes and offenses occur in a state of intoxication.

E- Economic Harms of Alcoholic Beverages: A famous psychiatrist says: Unfortunately, governments calculate the profits and tax revenues from wine, but they have not calculated the other massive budgets spent on repairing the evils of wine. If governments were to calculate the increase in mental illnesses in society, the losses of a degraded society, the waste of precious time, traffic accidents due to drunkenness, the corruption of pure generations, laziness, recklessness, unemployment, the backwardness of culture, the efforts and troubles of the police, orphanages for the care of children of alcoholics, hospitals, the judicial system for their crimes, and prisons for alcoholic criminals, and other losses resulting from drinking, they would realize that the income obtained as duties and taxes on wine is nothing compared to the aforementioned losses. Furthermore, the tragic results of consuming alcoholic beverages cannot be measured only in dollars and money, because the death of loved ones, the breaking up of families, shattered dreams, and the loss of human intellectual brains are in no way comparable to money.

In short, the harms of alcohol are so great that, according to one scientist, if governments guaranteed to close half of the taverns, it could be guaranteed that we would no longer need half of the hospitals and asylums.

From what has been said, the meaning of the verse under discussion becomes very clear: if there is any profit for mankind in the trade of alcoholic beverages, or if, for example, a few moments of unawareness and forgetting sorrows is considered a benefit, its harm is many degrees greater, wider, and longer-lasting, such that the two are not comparable.

3- The Evil Effects of Gambling

Few people can be found who are unaware of the various harms of gambling. For further explanation, we briefly mention some of its evil and home-destroying consequences:

A- Gambling as the Greatest Factor of Excitement: All psychologists and scientists of psychology believe that psychological excitements are the main cause of many diseases. For example, the depletion of vitamins, stomach ulcers, insanity, mild and acute neuropsychiatric diseases, and the like are in many cases caused by excitement, and gambling is the greatest factor in the emergence of excitement. To the extent that an American scientist says: Every year in this country, two thousand people die solely due to the excitement of gambling, and on average, the heart of a "poker player" beats more than a hundred times per minute. Gambling sometimes causes heart attacks and strokes and will certainly be a factor in premature aging.

Furthermore, according to scientists, a person engaged in gambling is not only subject to spiritual convulsions, but all the systems of his body are in an extraordinary state; that is, the heart rate increases, sugar substances are released into his blood, disturbances occur in the secretions of internal glands, the face turns pale, he suffers from loss of appetite, and after the gambling ends, following a war of nerves and a state of crisis, he goes to sleep, often resorting to alcohol and other narcotics to soothe the nerves and create calm in the body, in which case the harms resulting from those must also be added to the direct harms of gambling.

From the words of other scientists, we read: The gambler is a sick person who constantly needs psychological care; one must only try to make him understand that a psychological void drives him toward this abnormal act, so that he may seek his own treatment.

B- The Relationship of Gambling with Crimes: One of the world's largest statistical institutions has proven that: 30% of crimes have a direct relationship with gambling, and it is also considered a factor in the occurrence of 70% of other crimes.

C- Economic Harms of Gambling: Throughout the year, millions, or rather billions, of the wealth of the world's people are destroyed in this way, besides the many hours of human energy wasted in this path, which even robs the vitality for continuous work in other hours. For example, it is stated in reports: In the city of "Monte Carlo," which is one of the famous gambling centers in the world, one person lost 4 million Tomans of his wealth in 19 hours of gambling. When the doors of the casino closed, he went straight to the forest and shattered his brain with a bullet, ending his life. The reporter adds that the forests of Monte Carlo have many times witnessed the suicide of these "all-out losers."

D- Social Harms of Gambling: Many gamblers, because they sometimes win and may pocket thousands of Tomans of others' capital in one hour, are unwilling to submit to productive and economic work. As a result, the wheels of production and economy become crippled to that same extent. If we look closely, we see that all gamblers and their families are a burden on society, utilizing its hard-earned fruits without providing the slightest benefit to this society. Sometimes, when they lose in gambling, they resort to theft to compensate for it.

In short, the harms resulting from gambling are to such an extent that even many non-Muslim countries have declared it legally prohibited, even if they practically engage in it on a large scale. For example, England in 1853, America in 1855, the Soviet Union in 1854, and Germany in 1873 declared gambling prohibited.

At the end of this discussion, it seems interesting to point out that according to statistics prepared by some researchers, pickpocketing is 90%, moral corruption 10%, assault and battery 40%, sexual crimes 15%, divorce 30%, and suicide 5% caused by gambling.

If we want to provide a comprehensive definition for gambling, we must say: Gambling means sacrificing wealth and honor to obtain the wealth of others through deception and trickery, and sometimes under the guise of recreation, while achieving neither.

Up to this point, the irreparable harms of "Khamr and Maysir" (wine and gambling) have been noted. It is necessary to pay attention to another point: why did Allah, while censuring and prohibiting wine-drinking, mention its benefits, when we know that its benefits are very insignificant compared to its harms?

The reason might be that, firstly, in the age of ignorance (as in our age), the trade of wine and profiting from gambling were very common, and if this subject had not been mentioned, some narrow-minded people might have thought the issue was examined one-sidedly.

Furthermore, human thoughts always revolve around the axis of profit and loss, and to save them from the clutches of great moral evils, one must use this very logic.

Additionally, the verse under discussion practically answers the claims of some doctors who consider alcoholic beverages useful in fighting certain diseases, stating that such social benefits are never comparable to the harms resulting from them. That is, if it has a positive effect on one disease, it may be the source of much more dangerous diseases. The fact that it is mentioned in some narrations that Allah has not placed healing in alcoholic beverages perhaps refers to this very reality.

4- Moderation in the Matter of Spending

Although spending (Infaq) is one of the most important issues emphasized by Islam and the Quran, it nevertheless does not allow it to be reckless or excessive to the point that the life of the spender falls into disorder. The above verse, according to some interpretations, refers to this very meaning. It can also point to the fact that some individuals, to shirk this important Islamic duty, often bring up their own needs; the Quran says: many of you have surpluses and extras in your lives; at least choose from those and spend.

5- Thought in Everything

The phrase "that you might give thought in this world and the Hereafter" teaches an important lesson to Muslims: that they should perform no action, whether in material or spiritual life, without thought and reflection. Even the clarification of divine verses for the servants is to stimulate thoughts and move toward reflection. How wretched are the people whose religious works are not based on thought and reflection, nor are their worldly affairs.

Ayah

وَلَا تَنكِحُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكَـٰتِ حَتَّىٰ يُؤْمِنَّ ۚ وَلَأَمَةٌ مُّؤْمِنَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِّن مُّشْرِكَةٍ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ ۗ وَلَا تُنكِحُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ حَتَّىٰ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ ۚ وَلَعَبْدٌ مُّؤْمِنٌ خَيْرٌ مِّن مُّشْرِكٍ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكُمْ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلْجَنَّةِ وَٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۖ وَيُبَيِّنُ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٢١)
Do not marry idolatresses until they embrace faith. A faithful slave girl is better than an idolatress, though she should impress you. And do not marry [your daughters] to idolaters until they embrace faith. A faithful slave is better than an idolater, though he should impress you. Those invite [others] to the Fire, but Allah invites to paradise and pardon, by His will, and He clarifies His signs for the people so that they may take admonition. (221)

Occasion of Revelation

A person named "Marthad," who was a brave man, was commissioned by the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to go from Medina to Mecca and bring back a group of Muslims who were there. He entered Mecca with the intention of carrying out the command of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله. There, he encountered a beautiful woman named "Anaq" whom he had known during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah). She invited him to sin as she had in the past, but Marthad, who had since become a Muslim, did not submit to her desire. The woman then requested marriage. Marthad said: "This matter is subject to the permission of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله." After completing his mission, he returned to Medina and informed the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of the situation. This verse was revealed, stating that polytheistic and idol-worshipping women are not suitable for marriage with Muslim men.

Commentary

Marriage with Polytheists is Forbidden

According to the occasion of revelation mentioned above, this verse is actually a response to another question regarding marriage with polytheists. It says: "And do not marry polytheistic women until they believe" (وَ لا تَنْكِحُوا الْمُشْرِكاتِ حَتَّى يُؤْمِنَ‌).

Then, in a comparison, it adds: "A believing slave girl is better than a free polytheistic woman, even though her beauty might please you" (وَ لَأَمَةٌ مُؤْمِنَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِنْ مُشْرِكَةٍ وَ لَوْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ‌).

It is true that marrying slave girls (especially those who possess neither much beauty nor wealth) is not considered attractive or desirable in social custom, particularly when contrasted with a beautiful or wealthy polytheistic woman. However, the value of faith tips the scales of comparison in favor of the slave girls. This is because the purpose of marriage is not merely sexual gratification; a woman is a partner in a man's life and the nurturer of his children, forming half of his personality. Given this, how can polytheism and its dire consequences be exchanged for outward beauty and a bit of wealth?

The verse then addresses another part of this ruling, stating: "And do not give [your daughters] in marriage to polytheistic men until they believe (even if you are forced to marry them to believing slaves, for) a believing slave is better than a free polytheistic man, even though (his wealth, status, and beauty) might please you" (وَ لا تُنْكِحُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَتَّى يُؤْمِنُوا وَ لَعَبْدٌ مُؤْمِنٌ خَيْرٌ مِنْ مُشْرِكٍ وَ لَوْ أَعْجَبَكُمْ‌).

Therefore, just as Muslim men are forbidden from marrying polytheistic and idol-worshipping women, the marriage of polytheistic men to Muslim women is also prohibited. Even believing slaves have preference and priority over them, and are superior and more worthy than handsome, wealthy, and seemingly prestigious disbelieving men. In fact, the matter in this part of the ruling is stricter and more critical, because the influence of a husband over a wife is usually greater than the influence of a wife over a husband.

At the end of the verse, it states the reason for this divine ruling to stimulate reflection, saying: "Those (the polytheists) invite to the Fire, while Allah (and the believers who obey His command) invites to Paradise and forgiveness by His permission" (أُولئِكَ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى النَّارِ وَ اللَّهُ يَدْعُوا إِلَى الْجَنَّةِ وَ الْمَغْفِرَةِ بِإِذْنِهِ‌).

Then it adds: "And He makes His verses clear to the people that perhaps they may remember" (وَ يُبَيِّنُ آياتِهِ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ‌).

Key Points

1- The Philosophy Behind the Prohibition of Marriage with Polytheists

As we have seen, the above verse explains the philosophy of this ruling in a short sentence. If we break it down, it is as follows: Marriage is the primary foundation for procreation, the upbringing and education of children, and the expansion of society. The educational environment of the family is exceptionally influential in the destiny of children—on one hand, through the definitive effects of heredity, and on the other, through the definitive effects of upbringing in infancy. This is because infants, after birth, are mostly raised in the arms of their parents and remain under their supervision during the years when they are most impressionable.

Thirdly, polytheism is the root of all kinds of deviations and is, in reality, a consuming fire both in this world and the hereafter. Therefore, the Quran does not allow Muslims to throw themselves or their children into this fire. Furthermore, if polytheists, who are strangers to Islam, find their way into Muslim homes through marriage, the Islamic society will suffer from chaos and internal enemies. However, this remains the case as long as they persist in their polytheism; the path is open to them—they can believe, join the ranks of Muslims, and become "compatible" (kufu') in the matter of marriage.

Additionally, the word "Nikah" in linguistics refers both to sexual intercourse and the marriage contract. Here, it refers to the marriage contract, although "Raghib" in "Al-Mufradat" says: "Nikah" originally means the contract, and was later used metaphorically for sexual intercourse.

2- Who are the Polytheists (Mushrikin)?

The term "Mushrik" in the Quran is mostly applied to idol-worshippers. However, some commentators believe that "Mushrik" includes other disbelievers such as Jews, Christians, and Magians (and generally the People of the Book), because each of these groups attributed a partner to Allah. The Christians believed in the Trinity (three gods), the Magians believed in dual gods—Ahura Mazda and Ahriman (dualism)—and the Jews considered Ezra ('Uzayr) to be the son of God. However, although these beliefs lead to polytheism, considering that in numerous verses the polytheists are placed in contrast to the People of the Book, and given that Jews, Christians, and Magians originally rely on true prophethood and a Heavenly Book, it becomes clear that the Quran's intent by "Mushrik" is specifically the idol-worshipper.

The famous Hadith narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, where he said in his final instructions: "Expel the polytheists from the Arabian Peninsula," supports this claim, as it is certain that the People of the Book were not expelled from the Arabian Peninsula; rather, they lived under the protection of Islam as a religious minority by paying the Jizyah, according to the Quran's instructions.

3- This Verse is Not Abrogated

Some commentators have said that the ruling in the above verse has been abrogated, and its abrogator is the verse: "And [lawful in marriage are] chaste women from among those who were given the Scripture" [1], which permits marriage with women of the People of the Book.

This notion arose from the assumption that the verse under discussion prohibited marriage with all disbelievers; therefore, Verse 5 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, which permits marriage with disbelievers from the People of the Book, would be an abrogator of this ruling (or a specification of it). However, based on what was said in the commentary of the above verse, it becomes clear that this verse only concerns marriage with idol-worshippers, not disbelievers from the People of the Book like Jews and Christians. (Of course, regarding marriage with the People of the Book, there are also indications in the verse and the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام showing that it refers only to temporary marriage).

4- Family Formation Must Be with Care and Study

Some contemporary commentators have pointed out a subtle point here: the verse under discussion and the 21 verses that follow it explain the rulings related to family formation in various dimensions. In these verses, twelve rulings are stated in this regard: 1- The ruling on marriage with polytheists, 2- The prohibition of intercourse during menstruation, 3- The ruling on oaths as a prelude to the issue of Ila' (Ila' refers to someone swearing not to have intercourse with his wife), 4- The ruling on Ila' followed by divorce, 5- The waiting period (Iddah) for divorced women, 6- The number of divorces, 7- Retaining a wife with kindness or releasing her with kindness, 8- The ruling on breastfeeding infants, 9- The waiting period for a woman whose husband has died, 10- Proposing to a woman before her waiting period ends, 11- The dowry (Mahr) for women divorced before consummation, 12- The ruling on Mut'ah (giving a gift) to a woman after the husband's death or divorce. These rulings are intertwined with moral reminders and expressions showing that the issue of family formation is a form of worship of the Lord and must be accompanied by thought and reflection. [2]

[1]: Surah Al-Ma'idah, Verse 5. [2]: Tafsir Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 1, pp. 344-346.

Ayahs

وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْمَحِيضِ ۖ قُلْ هُوَ أَذًى فَٱعْتَزِلُوا۟ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فِى ٱلْمَحِيضِ ۖ وَلَا تَقْرَبُوهُنَّ حَتَّىٰ يَطْهُرْنَ ۖ فَإِذَا تَطَهَّرْنَ فَأْتُوهُنَّ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَمَرَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلتَّوَّٰبِينَ وَيُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ (٢٢٢)
They ask you concerning [intercourse during] menses. Say, ‘It is hurtful.’ So keep away from wives during the menses, and do not approach them till they are clean. And when they become clean, go into them as Allah has commanded you. Indeed Allah loves the penitent and He loves those who keep clean. (222)
نِسَآؤُكُمْ حَرْثٌ لَّكُمْ فَأْتُوا۟ حَرْثَكُمْ أَنَّىٰ شِئْتُمْ ۖ وَقَدِّمُوا۟ لِأَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُم مُّلَـٰقُوهُ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢٢٣)
Your women are a tillage for you, so come to your tillage whenever you like, and send ahead for your souls, and be Godwary, and know that you will encounter Him; and give good news to the faithful. (223)

Occasion of Revelation

Women undergo menstruation every month for a minimum of three days and a maximum of ten days. This refers to blood that exits a woman's womb with specific characteristics mentioned in books of jurisprudence (Fiqh). In such a state, the woman is called "Ha'idh" (menstruating) and that blood is called "Hayd" (menses). The current religions of the Jews and Christians hold contradictory laws regarding men's interaction with such women, which creates a sense of inquiry for everyone.

A group of Jews says that socializing with such women is absolutely forbidden, even if it is in the form of eating at the same table or living in one room. For example, they say a man must not sit where a menstruating woman has sat; if he does, he must wash his clothes, otherwise, they are impure (Najis). Furthermore, if he sleeps in her bed, he must wash his clothes and body. In short, they consider a woman during this period an impure being that must be avoided.

In contrast to this group are the Christians, who say: there is no difference at all between a woman's state of menstruation and non-menstruation; all forms of socializing, even sexual intercourse with them, are permissible! The Arab polytheists, especially those living in Medina, had more or less become accustomed to the temperament of the Jews and treated menstruating women like the Jews, separating from them during their monthly period. This very difference in religious practice and these inexcusable extremes of excess and negligence caused some Muslims to ask the Prophet ص about this matter. In response to them, this verse was revealed.

Commentary

The Ruling on Women During the Monthly Period

In the first verse, we encounter another question, which concerns the monthly period of women. It says: "They ask you about (the blood of) menstruation. Say: It is a harmful thing" (وَيَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْمَحِيضِ قُلْ هُوَ أَذىً).

And immediately it adds: "Since it is so, keep away from women during menstruation, and do not have sexual intercourse with them until they are clean" (فَاعْتَزِلُوا النِّساءَ فِي الْمَحِيضِ وَ لا تَقْرَبُوهُنَّ حَتَّى يَطْهُرْنَ).

"But when they have purified themselves, then go into them from where Allah has ordained for you, for Allah loves those who turn to Him in repentance and loves those who keep themselves pure" (فَإِذا تَطَهَّرْنَ فَأْتُوهُنَّ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَمَرَكُمُ اللَّهُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ التَّوَّابِينَ وَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ).

"Mahid" (محيض) is a mim-infinitived noun meaning the monthly period. In Mu'jam Maqayis al-Lughah, it is stated that this word originally meant the exiting of red water from a tree called Samurah (and was subsequently applied to the monthly period of women). However, in the commentary of Fakhr Razi, it is mentioned that Hayd originally means a flood; hence, when a flood flows, it is said Hada al-Sayl. A pool is also called Hawd for this reason, as water flows toward it.

Conversely, from the words of Raghib in Al-Mufradat, it is understood that this word originally meant the blood of the period itself (and was then applied to other meanings).

In any case, what is meant here is that very blood which the Quran introduces as Adha (something contaminated or harmful). In reality, this phrase explains the philosophy behind the ruling to avoid sexual intercourse with women during menstruation, which follows in the next sentence. For intercourse in such a state, besides being repulsive, brings about many harms that modern medicine has also proven. These include the possibility of sterility in men and women, creating a favorable environment for the growth of microbes of venereal diseases (such as syphilis and gonorrhea), inflammation of the female genital organs, the entry of contaminated blood into the male genital organ, and other issues mentioned in medical books. Therefore, doctors declare sexual intercourse with such women forbidden.

The origin of menstrual blood is related to the congestion and engorgement of the uterine vessels, followed by the shedding of its mucosa and the flow of existing blood. The secretion of said blood is initially irregular and colorless, but it soon becomes red and regular, and toward the end, it once again becomes pale and irregular [1]. Fundamentally, the blood discharged during the monthly period is the blood that accumulates every month in the internal vessels of the uterus to nourish a potential fetus. We know that a woman's uterus produces one ovum every month, and concurrently, the internal vessels of the uterus become filled with blood as a state of readiness to nourish the embryo. If, at the time the ovum enters the uterus, sperm (the male seed) is present there, it forms an embryo, and the blood in the uterine vessels is spent on its nourishment. Otherwise, due to the shedding of the uterine mucosa and the rupturing of the vessel walls, the existing blood exits, and this is the very blood of menstruation. From here, another reason for the prohibition of sexual intercourse in this state is obtained, because the woman's uterus, at the time of discharging this blood, has no natural readiness to accept the seed and thus suffers harm from it.

The phrase "Yathurna" (يَطْهُرْنَ), according to many commentators, means the women becoming clean from menstrual blood. However, the phrase "Fa-idha Tatahharna" (فَإِذا تَطَهَّرْنَ) has been taken by many to mean performing the ritual bath (Ghusl). Therefore, according to the first phrase, sexual intercourse is permissible upon becoming clean from blood even if she has not performed Ghusl, while according to the second phrase, it is not permissible until she performs Ghusl [2]. Thus, the verse is not devoid of ambiguity. However, considering that the second phrase is an explanation of the first and its result—hence it is joined with the Fa of derivation—it seems that Tatahharna also means becoming clean from blood. Therefore, with the cessation of the period, intercourse is permitted, especially since at the beginning of the verse there was no mention of the obligation of Ghusl. This is the same opinion that great jurists have also decreed in Fiqh: that after becoming clean from blood, even before Ghusl, sexual intercourse is permissible, though undoubtedly, it is better to be after performing Ghusl.

The phrase "Min haythu amarakumullah" (مِنْ حَيْثُ أَمَرَكُمُ اللَّهُ - from where Allah has commanded you) can be an emphasis on the previous sentence, meaning: have sexual intercourse only in the state of women's purity and not otherwise. It is also possible to derive a broader and more general meaning from it: that even after becoming clean, intercourse must be within the framework of Allah's command. This command can be the Lord's creative (Takwini) command or His legislative (Tashri'i) command. For Allah, for the preservation of the human species, has placed a specific attraction between the two opposite sexes toward one another, and for this reason, sexual intercourse has a specific pleasure for both parties. However, it is certain that the ultimate goal was the preservation of the lineage, and this attraction and pleasure are a prelude to it. Therefore, sexual pleasure must only be placed in the path of preserving the lineage, and for this reason, masturbation, sodomy, and the like are a type of deviation from this creative command and are forbidden.

It is also possible that the legislative command is intended, meaning: after women become clean from their monthly period, you must consider the aspects of Halal and Haram in the religious law.

Some have also said: the meaning of this phrase is the prohibition of sexual intercourse with wives through other than the usual way. However, considering that there was no mention of this matter in the preceding verses, this interpretation does not seem appropriate [1].

In the second verse, a beautiful allusion is made to the ultimate goal of sexual intercourse, stating: "Your wives are a tilth for you" (نِساؤُكُمْ حَرْثٌ لَكُمْ).

"So go to your tilth when/how you will" (فَأْتُوا حَرْثَكُمْ أَنَّى شِئْتُمْ).

Here, women are likened to a field (Mazar'ah). This comparison might seem heavy to some—why Islam has used such an expression regarding half of the human race—while a subtle point is hidden in this comparison. In reality, the Quran wants to show the necessity of woman's existence in human society; that woman is not a means for extinguishing the lust and hedonism of men, but rather a means for preserving the life of the human species. This statement serves as a warning to those who look upon the female sex as a plaything or a means for indulgence.

"Harth" (حرث) is an infinitive meaning sowing, and it is sometimes applied to the field itself.

"Anna" (أَنَّى) is one of the conditional nouns and is often used in the sense of "Mata" (when), which refers to time; in this case, it is called the temporal Anna. Sometimes it also means place, like what appears in verse 37 of Surah Al-Imran: "Zakariya said: O Maryam! From where (Anna) did this (heavenly food) come to you? She said: It is from Allah."

If Anna in the above verse is temporal, it expresses the temporal expansion of the issue of sexual intercourse, meaning you are permitted at any hour of the night or day. If it is locational, it is an expansion in place and the manner of various types of intercourse.

The possibility also exists that it refers to both aspects, and in this way, the two spouses can benefit from sexual pleasure in any manner and at any time and place (except what is forbidden in the religious law).

Then, in the continuation of the verse, it adds: "And send forth (good deeds) for yourselves" (وَ قَدِّمُوا لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ) by performing righteous deeds and raising righteous children.

This indicates that the ultimate goal of sexual intercourse is not pleasure and gratification, but rather this subject should be used for the creation and upbringing of worthy children, sending them forth as a spiritual treasure for the tomorrow of Resurrection. This statement warns that in choosing a spouse, one must observe principles that lead to this important result, namely the training of righteous children and a worthy human generation.

In a Hadith from the Prophet of Islam ص, we read that he said: "When a human being dies, his hope is cut off except from three things: ongoing charity (Sadaqah Jariyah), knowledge from which benefit is derived, and a righteous child who prays for him" [1]. In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "No reward follows a man after his death except three traits: an ongoing charity that he established during his life which continues after his death, a sunnah of guidance that he established which is acted upon after his death, and a righteous child who seeks forgiveness for him" [2]. This same theme has been reported in numerous other narrations, and in some narrations, six subjects are mentioned, the first of which is a righteous child [3]. In this way, righteous children are placed alongside scientific works, the authorship of guiding books, and the establishment of charitable buildings such as mosques, hospitals, and libraries.

At the end of the verse, He commands piety (Taqwa) and says: "And fear Allah, and know that you will meet Him, and give glad tidings to the believers"—the tidings of Allah's mercy, happiness, and salvation under the shadow of piety (وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُمْ مُلاقُوهُ وَ بَشِّرِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

Since the issue of sexual intercourse is an important matter and deals with the most attractive of human instincts, Allah, in these final sentences, has invited the believers to be careful in this matter and to avoid any sin or deviation, warning them to know that all will hasten to meet the Lord, and the only way of salvation is faith and piety under the shadow of faith.

Key Point: Islam's Just Command Regarding Women's Monthly Period

Previous nations held various beliefs regarding women during their monthly period: the Jews were extremely strict and during these days would completely separate from women in everything—in eating, drinking, sitting together, and bedding. In the current Torah, severe laws are seen in this regard [1]. Conversely, the Christians did not consider any limitation or prohibition for themselves in interacting with women during these days. As for the Arab idolaters, no specific command or tradition was found among them in this field, but the inhabitants of Medina and its surroundings had adopted some of the Jewish customs in this regard and were strict in socializing with women during menstruation, while other Arabs were not so. Some even perhaps considered sexual intercourse in this state desirable, believing that if a child were granted to them, he would be very bloodthirsty, and this was among the prominent and desirable traits among the bloodthirsty Bedouin Arabs [1].

2- The mention of purity (Taharah) and repentance (Tawbah) alongside each other in the above verses may indicate that purity relates to outward cleanliness, while repentance refers to inward cleanliness.

The possibility also exists that purity here means not being contaminated by sin; that is, Allah loves both those who have not been contaminated by sin and those who repent after contamination and join the ranks of the pure.

Furthermore, the reference to the issue of repentance here may be directed at the fact that some, due to the pressure of the sexual instinct, could not restrain themselves and became contaminated by sin contrary to Allah's command, then became regretful and distressed by their action. So that they do not see the way of return closed to them and do not despair of the Divine mercy, He shows them the path of repentance [2].
