[1]: Adapted from I'jaz al-Quran, pages 55 and 56. [2]: The second is the implication of a condition, and the first is the implication of an ultimate limit. [1]: Note that "Haythu" (حيث) has appeared as both an adverb of place and time, and here it can refer to the time when intercourse is permitted, i.e., the time of purity. [1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1, page 321. [2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 6, page 294, Hadith No. 4. [3]: Ibid, page 293, Hadith No. 1. And every thing that she lieth upon in her separation shall be unclean: every thing also that she sitteth upon shall be unclean. And whosoever toucheth her bed shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even..." and other laws of this kind. In the book Anis al-A'lam, Vol. 2, pages 106 and 107, a detailed explanation with the citation of documents is also provided in this regard. Ayahs

وَلَا تَجْعَلُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عُرْضَةً لِّأَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ أَن تَبَرُّوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ وَتُصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٢٤)
Do not make Allah an obstacle, through your oaths, to being pious and Godwary, and to bringing about concord between people. And Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (224)
لَّا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِٱللَّغْوِ فِىٓ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا كَسَبَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ (٢٢٥)
Allah will not take you to task for what is unconsidered in your oaths, but He will take you to task for what your hearts have incurred, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-forbearing. (225)

Occasion of Revelation

A dispute arose between the son-in-law and the daughter of one of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) companions named "Abdullah ibn Rawahah." He swore an oath that he would not intervene in any way to reconcile them and would not take a single step in this regard. The above verse was revealed, declaring such oaths prohibited and baseless.

Commentary

Do Not Swear Oaths as Much as You Can

As we read in the occasion of revelation, the two verses above address the misuse of the issue of oaths. They serve as a preamble to the discussion in the upcoming verses regarding Ila' and oaths taken to abstain from sexual relations with one's wife.

In the first verse, He says: "Do not make Allah an excuse in your oaths for [avoiding] doing good, being righteous, and making peace among people; and [know that] Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing."

He hears your words and is aware of your intentions (وَ لا تَجْعَلُوا اللَّهَ عُرْضَةً لِأَيْمانِكُمْ أَنْ تبرُّوا وَ تَتَّقُوا وَ تُصْلِحُوا بَيْنَ النَّاسِ وَ اللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ) [1]. "Ayman" is the plural of "yamin," meaning an oath. "Urdah" (on the weight of ghurfah) refers to something being exposed or set up as a target/obstacle. For example, goods brought to the market for sale and put up for transaction are called urdah. Sometimes, obstacles are also referred to as urdah because they stand in front of a person and in their path.

Some have also said: The meaning is that you should not swear oaths even for good deeds, whether small or large, and do not belittle the name of Allah. Thus, swearing an oath is an undesirable act except in cases where an important goal is at stake. This subject is also found in many hadiths; for instance, in a hadith, Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) says:

"Never swear by Allah, whether you are truthful or lying, for Allah, the Sublime, says: 'Do not make Allah an excuse in your oaths...'" [2]. Several other hadiths have been narrated in this regard [3]. In this case, its connection to the occasion of revelation is that swearing oaths for good deeds is not a commendable act, let alone someone swearing an oath to abandon good deeds.

[1]: According to this interpretation, the word "la" (not) is implied, and it was originally "li-alla tabarru" (so that you do not do good). This meaning is fully consistent with the occasion of revelation. There is also the possibility that urdah means an "obstacle," meaning: do not make swearing by the name of Allah an obstacle to performing good deeds and making peace among people (in fact, the implication becomes: do not make Allah, by reason of your oaths, a barrier to doing good and being righteous). However, the first justification seems more appropriate.

[2]: Al-Kafi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 218, Hadith 833; and Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 16, p. 116, Hadith 5.

[3]: Same source, Hadiths 832 and 834; and Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 16, p. 115 onwards.

In the next verse, to complete the point that an oath must not prevent a good deed, He says:

"Allah will not call you to account for what is unintentional in your oaths" (لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللَّهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ).

"But He will call you to account for what your hearts have earned (and the oaths you take out of will and choice), and Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Forbearing" (وَ لكِنْ يُؤاخِذُكُمْ بِما كَسَبَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ).

In this verse, Allah refers to two types of oaths. The first type is "idle" (laghw) oaths, which have no effect and should not be heeded. These are the oaths that people use as a figure of speech without attention and have become accustomed to, saying "No, by Allah..." or "Yes, by Allah..." in every matter. These are called idle oaths.

This is because laghw (idle/vain) in linguistics refers to all actions and words that do not have a specific goal or do not stem from will and decision.

Therefore, oaths that a person swears in a state of anger (provided the anger causes them to lose their normal state) are considered idle oaths. According to the above verse, Allah does not call one to account for such oaths, and they should not be acted upon (although a person should train themselves to set aside such oaths as well). In any case, these types of oaths are not binding, and breaking them does not require an expiation (kaffarah), because they are not based on will and decision.

The phrase "وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ" (And Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Forbearing) could be an allusion to this meaning.

The second type consists of oaths taken out of will and decision, and in the expression of the Quran, the human heart "earns" them. This type of oath is valid and must be adhered to. Violating it is both a sin and requires expiation, except in cases that will be mentioned later. This is the same thing referred to in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 89, as "بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ" (for the oaths which you have tied/ratified), meaning oaths you have made firm through your will.

Key Point: Invalid Oaths

Swearing an oath is not a good deed from the perspective of Islam, but at the same time, it is not forbidden (haram). If it is done for important educational, social, or reformative goals, it may become obligatory (wajib) or recommended (mustahabb). However, there is a series of oaths that are completely invalid in Islam, including:

  1. Oaths made by other than the name of Allah. Even swearing by the name of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Imams of Guidance (عليهم السلام) is not binding; meaning, if someone swears by other than the name of Allah, they are not required to fulfill it, and breaking it carries no expiation.

  2. Oaths made to perform a forbidden (haram) or disliked (makruh) act, or to abandon an obligatory (wajib) or recommended (mustahabb) act, are also invalid. For example, if someone swears an oath not to pay their debt, or to sever ties with their relatives, or to refrain from making peace between people—as is frequently seen when some people, due to a bad experience in a reconciliation effort, swear never to pursue such work again. Such oaths should not be heeded, even if they are in the name of Allah. One of the interpretations of "لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللَّهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ" is precisely this. However, oaths made in the name of Allah whose subject is a good deed, or at least a permissible (mubah) act, must be fulfilled, and if someone violates them, they must pay expiation.

The expiation for it is feeding ten poor persons, or clothing ten needy persons, or freeing a slave (this meaning is mentioned in verse 89 of Surah Al-Ma'idah). Since slavery no longer exists today, one must perform one of the first two options.

Ayahs

لِّلَّذِينَ يُؤْلُونَ مِن نِّسَآئِهِمْ تَرَبُّصُ أَرْبَعَةِ أَشْهُرٍ ۖ فَإِن فَآءُو فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٢٢٦)
For those who forswear their wives shall be a waiting [period]of four months. And if they recant, Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (226)
وَإِنْ عَزَمُوا۟ ٱلطَّلَـٰقَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٢٧)
But if they resolve on divorce, Allah is indeed all-hearing, all-knowing. (227)

Commentary

Combating an Ugly Pre-Islamic Custom

During the era of Jahiliyyah (Ignorance), women held no value or status in Arabian society. Consequently, there were several abhorrent methods used to separate from a wife or to place her under extreme pressure. One of these was "Ila’" [^1]—meaning to take an oath to abstain from marital relations. Whenever a man grew resentful of his wife, he would swear an oath not to sleep with her. Through this inhumane method, he would place his wife in a severe predicament; he would neither formally divorce her so she could freely choose another husband, nor would he be willing to reconcile and maintain a proper life with her after this oath. Of course, the men themselves were usually not under pressure, as they often had multiple wives.

[^1]: "Ila’" is derived from the root "alw," which means a display of power and determination. Since an oath is an example of this, the term is applied to it.

The verses under discussion combat this erroneous tradition and explain the method for dissolving such an oath. It says: "For those who take an oath to abstain from their wives (Ila’), there is a waiting period of four months" (لِلَّذِينَ يُؤْلُونَ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ تَرَبُّصُ أَرْبَعَةِ أَشْهُرٍ).

This four-month grace period is intended for them to clarify their status with their wives and to rescue the woman from this state of uncertainty.

Then it adds: "If they return (within this period), then Allah is surely All-Forgiving, All-Merciful" (فَإِنْ فاؤُ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Indeed, Allah forgives his past actions in this matter as well as the breaking of the oath—although the expiation (kaffarah), as we shall mention, remains in effect.

"And if they resolve on divorce (that too is permissible under its specific conditions), then Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing" (وَ إِنْ عَزَمُوا الطَّلاقَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ).

Whenever a man chooses neither of these two paths—neither returning to a healthy marital life nor releasing the woman through divorce—the Sharia judge intervenes. He may imprison the man and treat him strictly so that after the four months have passed, he is forced to choose one of the two paths and bring the woman out of her state of limbo.

In this way, although Islam did not completely abolish the ruling of "Ila’" (swearing to abstain from sexual intercourse), it eliminated its evil effects. It does not permit anyone to leave his wife stranded through this method. If we see that a period of four months has been set as a deadline, it is not because marital rights can be nullified for that duration; rather, it is because sexual intercourse is a religious obligation at least once every four months (provided that the woman does not fall into sin due to the length of time; therefore, in the case of young women where there is a fear of falling into sin, it is necessary for this interval to be shorter).

Key Points

1. Ila’ is an Exceptional Ruling

In the preceding verses, the discussion was about vain and ineffective oaths. We stated that any speech intended for a wrong act is considered a vain and futile oath, and breaking it carries no penalty. According to this rule, an oath to abandon a marital duty should technically have no effect at all. However, in Islam, an expiation (kaffarah) has been assigned for it [^1] (the same expiation for breaking an oath mentioned in the previous discussion). This is, in fact, a punishment for stubborn men so that they do not resort to this unchivalrous method to nullify a woman's rights and do not repeat such an act.

2. Comparison Between Islamic Law and the Western World

In the West and their own "modern" traditions of ignorance, something similar to Ila’ exists, which they call "physical separation."

To explain: Since divorce did not exist among Christians, one of the ways approved after the French Revolution for the separation of a man and woman who were unwilling to live together was "physical separation." This meant that the man and woman would temporarily separate and live in different houses (the duty of maintenance by the man and obedience by the woman would be waived, but the marriage bond remained). Despite this, neither the man could take another wife nor could the woman marry another husband. The duration of this separation could last up to three years, after which they were forced to either end the separation or live together [^1]. Although the Western world allows this separation for up to three years, Islam was not willing to let this disorganized state continue for more than four months—a duration that is permissible even without an oath. The man must clarify his status after this period, and if he refuses, the Islamic government can place him under severe pressure to settle the matter once and for all.

3. Divine Attributes at the End of Each Verse

It is noteworthy that in many verses of the Quran, attributes of Allah conclude the discussions. These attributes always have a direct relationship with the content of the verse; their selection is never without a close connection.

For instance, in the verses under discussion, when speaking of Ila’ and the decision to break this sinful oath, the verse ends with the phrase "All-Forgiving, All-Merciful" (غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ). This indicates that this correct move causes the sinful past to be covered by Divine forgiveness and mercy. Conversely, when the decision for divorce is mentioned, the attributes "All-Hearing, All-Knowing" (سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ) are emphasized, meaning Allah hears your words and is aware of the motives behind the divorce and separation, and He will recompense you accordingly.

[^1]: If the man has intercourse before the four months, the expiation is certain and agreed upon. If it is after four months, this ruling is famous among jurists, although some have denied the expiation in this case.

[^1]: Women's Rights in Islam and Europe

Ayah

وَٱلْمُطَلَّقَـٰتُ يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنفُسِهِنَّ ثَلَـٰثَةَ قُرُوٓءٍ ۚ وَلَا يَحِلُّ لَهُنَّ أَن يَكْتُمْنَ مَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ فِىٓ أَرْحَامِهِنَّ إِن كُنَّ يُؤْمِنَّ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۚ وَبُعُولَتُهُنَّ أَحَقُّ بِرَدِّهِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ إِنْ أَرَادُوٓا۟ إِصْلَـٰحًا ۚ وَلَهُنَّ مِثْلُ ٱلَّذِى عَلَيْهِنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ وَلِلرِّجَالِ عَلَيْهِنَّ دَرَجَةٌ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٢٢٨)
Divorced women shall wait by themselves for three periods of purity [after menses], and it is not lawful for them to conceal what Allah has created in their wombs if they believe in Allah and the Last Day; and their husbands have a greater right to restore them during this [duration], if they desire reconcilement. The wives have rights similar to the obligations upon them, in accordance with honourable norms; and men have a degree above them, and Allah is all-mighty and all-wise. (228)

Commentary

Iddah or the Sanctuary of Marriage?

In the previous verse, the discussion was about divorce, and in this section, the rulings of divorce or matters related to it are explained, totaling five rulings:

First, regarding the Iddah (waiting period), He says: "Divorced women shall wait by themselves for three menstrual cycles" (وَ الْمُطَلَّقاتُ يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنْفُسِهِنَّ ثَلاثَةَ قُرُوءٍ).

"Quru’" (قروء) is the plural of "Qur’" (قرء), which is used to mean both the menstrual period and the state of purity from it. However, these two meanings can be unified into one general concept: the transition from one state to another. Raghib in Al-Mufradat believes that "Qur’" is actually a term for entering from the state of menstruation into purity; since both titles are involved, it is sometimes applied to the state of menstruation and sometimes to purity. It is also understood from some narrations and many lexicons that "Qur’" means "collection," and since during a woman's state of purity, the menstrual blood collects within her being, this word is applied to purity. In any case, numerous narrations explicitly state that the meaning of "three quru’"—which is the limit of the Iddah—is three periods of a woman becoming pure from menstrual blood. [^1] Since divorce must take place during a period of purity in which the husband has not had sexual intercourse with her, this initial purity counts as one time. When she subsequently menstruates twice and becomes pure, as soon as the third purity ends and the moment menstruation begins, the Iddah is complete, and her remarriage becomes permissible at that very moment. Furthermore, in addition to these narrations, this reality can be inferred from the verse itself because:

Firstly, "Qur’" has two plurals: Quru’ and Aqra’. Some have specified that when "Qur’" means purity, its plural is Quru’, and when it means menstruation, its plural is Aqra’. Therefore, Quru’ in the above verse refers to the days of a woman's purity, not the days of menstruation. [^2] Secondly, as mentioned above, "Qur’" originally means "collecting," and collecting is more compatible with the state of Tuhr (purity), because in this state, blood gradually collects in the womb, whereas during menstruation, it flows out and disperses. [^3]

The second ruling is that "it is not lawful for them to conceal what Allah has created in their wombs, if they believe in Allah and the Last Day" (وَ لا يَحِلُّ لَهُنَّ أَنْ يَكْتُمْنَ ما خَلَقَ اللَّهُ فِي أَرْحامِهِنَّ إِنْ كُنَّ يُؤْمِنَّ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ).

It is noteworthy that the matter of the beginning and end of the Iddah days—which usually only the woman herself knows and not others—has been entrusted to her, and her word is considered the legal proof. Thus, Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says in the commentary of the above verse: "Allah has delegated three things to women: menstruation, purity, and pregnancy." [^1] This meaning can also be broadly inferred from the verse itself, as it says it is not permissible for a woman to conceal what Allah has created in her womb and speak contrary to the truth, meaning her word is accepted.

The phrase "what Allah has created in their wombs," according to a group of commentators, can have two meanings: "child" and "menstruation," because Allah has created both in the woman's womb. That is, she must not hide her pregnancy and claim she is menstruating to shorten the Iddah period (since the Iddah of a pregnant woman is until delivery), nor should she speak falsely regarding menstruation, whether concerning its start or its end. Deriving both meanings from the above expression does not seem far-fetched.

The third ruling derived from the verse is that the husband has the right of return (Ruju') during the Iddah of a revocable divorce (Talaq Raj'i). It says: "And their husbands have a better right to take them back (and resume shared life) during this period (over others), if they desire reconciliation" (وَ بُعُولَتُهنَّ أَحَقُّ بِرَدِّهِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ إِنْ أَرادُوا إِصْلاحاً). [^2]

In fact, while a woman is in the Iddah of a revocable divorce, the husband can resume the marital life without any formalities; this is achieved by any word or action intended as a return. However, with the phrase "if they desire reconciliation," the verse expresses the reality that the goal of the return must be reform and harmony, not like the era of Jahiliyyah (Ignorance) where men abused this right to pressure women and keep them in a state between having a husband and being divorced. This right exists only if he truly regrets his action and seriously intends to resume family life, and his goal is not to cause harm or leave the woman in limbo.

Additionally, from the fact that the issue of return is mentioned at the end of the verse, it is inferred that the ruling of observing Iddah at the beginning of the verse also pertains to this group of women. In other words, the verse speaks generally about revocable divorce; therefore, there is no obstacle to some types of divorce having no Iddah at all.

Then, it proceeds to state the fourth ruling, saying: "And for women are rights similar to the obligations upon them, according to what is fair, but men have a degree over them" (وَ لَهُنَّ مِثْلُ الَّذِي عَلَيْهِنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَ لِلرِّجالِ عَلَيْهِنَّ دَرَجَةٌ).

According to the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, this sentence is among the most wondrous, beautiful, and comprehensive words, containing many benefits. [^1] In fact, it expands the discussion to more important issues beyond divorce and Iddah, referring to the entire set of marital rights for men and women. It says: "Just as rights have been established for men over women, women also have rights over men which they are obligated to observe," because in Islam, a right is never one-sided; it is always reciprocal.

The word "Ma'ruf" (معروف), which means a good, recognized, reasonable, and logical deed, is repeated twelve times in this series of verses (from the verse under discussion until verse 241) as a warning to men and women never to abuse their rights, but rather to strive to strengthen the marital bond and seek Allah's pleasure by respecting each other's mutual rights.

The phrase "but men have a degree over them" is, in truth, a completion of what was previously said about the mutual rights of men and women. Its meaning is that the issue of justice between man and woman does not mean they are equal in everything and must walk step-by-step in every matter. Is it truly necessary for the two to be equal in everything?

Given the extensive differences between the physical and psychological powers of men and women, the answer to this question becomes clear. The female gender was created to perform duties different from the male, and for this reason, she possesses different emotions. The law of creation has placed the sensitive duty of motherhood and the nurturing of strong generations upon her; therefore, she has been given a greater share of affection and emotions. Meanwhile, according to this law, harsher and heavier social duties are placed upon the male gender, and a greater share of intellectual reasoning is allocated to him. Therefore, if we wish to implement justice, some social duties that require more thought, resistance, and endurance of hardships must be placed on men, and duties that require more affection and emotion on women. For this reason, the management of the family is placed on the man, and its deputy-management on the woman. In any case, this does not prevent women from undertaking tasks and duties in society that suit their physical and spiritual structure, and performing other sensitive duties alongside their role as mothers.

Furthermore, this difference does not prevent a group of women from being more advanced than many men in terms of spiritual stations, knowledge, and piety.

The insistence of some intellectuals to portray these two genders as equal in everything is an insistence that never aligns with realities, and various scientific studies deny it. Even in societies where the slogan of equality in all aspects fills the air, something else is seen in practice. For example, the political and military management of all human societies—except in exceptional cases—is entirely in the hands of men. Even in Western societies where the primary slogan is equality, this reality is clearly visible.

In any case, laws such as the right of divorce, or return during Iddah, or judgeship being in the hands of men (except in specific cases where the woman or the Sharia judge is given the right of divorce) stem from this very point and are a direct result of this reality.

Some commentators have said that the phrase "men have a degree over them" only refers to the issue of return during the Iddah of divorce. [^1] However, it is clear that this interpretation does not align with the apparent meaning of the verse, because before it, a general law regarding women's rights and the observation of justice was stated in the form of "And for women are rights similar to the obligations upon them, according to what is fair," and then the phrase under discussion follows it as another general law.

Finally, at the end of the verse, we read: "And Allah is Mighty, Wise" (وَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ).

This is, in fact, an answer to those who raise objections in this regard, and an indication that Divine wisdom and planning necessitate that everyone in society performs the duties that the law of creation has determined for them, which are harmonious with their physical and spiritual structure. Allah's wisdom necessitates that in exchange for the duties placed upon women, certain rights are established to maintain a balance between duty and right.

Key Points

1- Iddah: A Means for Peace and Return

Sometimes, due to various factors, the psychological state reaches a point where a minor disagreement or a small quarrel ignites the sense of revenge so fiercely that it extinguishes the light of reason and conscience. Family separations often occur in such states. However, it frequently happens that after a short time has passed since the conflict, the man and woman come to their senses and regret it, especially as they see that by the disintegration of the family home, they will face various hardships.

This is where the verse under discussion says: women must observe Iddah for a period and wait so that these fleeting waves pass and the dark clouds of conflict and enmity disperse from the sky of their lives. Especially with the instruction Islam has given regarding the woman not leaving the house during the Iddah period, better reflection is stimulated in her, and it is completely effective in improving her relationship with her husband. Thus, in Surah At-Talaq, verse 1, we read: "Do not turn them out of their houses... you know not, it may be that Allah will afterward bring some new thing to pass," meaning perhaps Allah will bring about a resolution and peace will occur. Often, remembering the warm and sweet moments before the divorce is enough to bring back lost affection and sincerity and strengthen the weakened light of love.

2- Iddah: A Means of Preserving Lineage

Another philosophy of Iddah is to clarify the woman's status regarding pregnancy. It is true that seeing one menstruation is usually proof of the absence of pregnancy, but it has sometimes been seen that a woman experiences menstruation at the beginning of pregnancy while being pregnant. Therefore, to fully observe this matter, it is commanded that the woman see menstruation and become pure three times so that the absence of pregnancy from the former husband is definitively clarified before she can remarry. Of course, Iddah has other benefits that will be mentioned in their proper place.

3- Right and Duty are Inseparable

Here, the Quran points to a fundamental principle: wherever a "duty" exists, a "right" is also established alongside it. That is, duty is never separate from right. For example, parents have duties toward their children, and this very fact causes them to have rights over them. Or a judge is obligated to exert maximum effort to spread and generalize justice; in return, many rights are established for him. This subject is even true regarding Prophets and their nations.

In the verse under discussion, this reality is pointed out: to the same extent that women have duties, rights have also been established for them. From the equality of these "rights" with those "duties," the "implementation of justice" regarding them is realized. The reverse is also true: if a right is established for someone, they will have duties in return. Therefore, we cannot find anyone who has a right in a matter without having a duty placed upon their shoulders.

4- The Painful History of Women Throughout History

The history of women is an eventful and painful journey that is considered one of the most important topics in modern "sociology." Generally, the life of women can be divided into two periods: first, the prehistoric era, of which we have no accurate information today regarding the status of women, and perhaps in that era, they enjoyed more natural rights.

With the beginning of human history, the second period arrived. In this period, in some societies, the woman was recognized as a non-independent personality in all economic, political, and social rights, and this situation continued in some countries until recent centuries. This way of thinking about women is even seen in the so-called progressive French Civil Code. As an example, a few articles regarding the financial relations of spouses are mentioned:

"From Articles 215 and 217, it is understood that a married woman cannot perform any legal act without the permission and signature of her husband, and any transaction for her requires the husband's permission" (unless the husband wants to abuse his power and refuses permission without a justified reason).

"According to Article 1242, the husband has the right to alone make any disposition he wishes in the joint property of the man and woman, and the woman's permission is not necessary" (with the proviso that for any transaction exceeding the limits of administration, the woman's consent and signature are not required).

And even higher than this: "In Article 1428, the right to manage all of the woman's exclusive property is also assigned to the man" (with the proviso that in any transaction exceeding the limits of administration, the woman's consent and signature are also necessary). [^1]

In the environment where Islam emerged, namely the Hejaz, before the appearance of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, women were treated as dependent, non-independent humans. Their behavior closely resembled semi-savage humans because they exploited women in a scandalous and shameful manner. In their environment, a woman was so lacking in will and choice that she was sometimes put up for hire for the livelihood of her owner. Deprivation of civilization and affliction with poverty had trapped them in a strange violence, leading them to commit the famous crime of "Wa'd" (burying female infants alive).

5- A New Stage in the Life of Women

With the emergence of Islam and its special teachings, the life of women entered a new stage that was far removed from the previous two stages. In this period, she became independent and enjoyed all individual, social, and human rights. The foundation of Islamic teachings regarding women is what we read in the verses under discussion: "And for women are rights similar to the obligations upon them, according to what is fair." This means that just as a woman has heavy duties in society, she also possesses significant rights. Islam considers the woman, like the man, to possess a complete human soul, will, and choice, and sees her on the path of perfection, which is the goal of creation. Therefore, it places both in the same rank and addresses them with calls like "O mankind" and "O you who believe," making educational, moral, and scientific programs necessary for both. With verses such as "And whoever does righteous deeds, whether male or female, while being a believer—those will enter Paradise," [^1] the promise of enjoying complete happiness is given to both genders. And with verses like "Whoever does righteousness, whether male or female, while he is a believer—We will surely cause him to live a good life, and We will surely give them their reward [in the Hereafter] according to the best of what they used to do," [^2] it says that both man and woman can reach spiritual and material perfection by following Islamic programs and divine duties and step into a "Tayyib" (pure) life that is entirely happiness and light.

Islam considers the woman, like the man, to be independent and free in every sense. The Quran, with verses such as "Every soul, for what it has earned, will be retained," [^1] or "Whoever does righteousness—it is for his [own] soul; and whoever does evil [does it] against it," [^2] expresses this freedom for all individuals, whether male or female. Therefore, in penal programs as well, we see in verses like "The woman and the man guilty of adultery or fornication—flog each of them with a hundred stripes," [^3] and the like, it sentences both to the same punishment.

On the other hand, since independence is a prerequisite for will and choice, Islam brings this independence into all economic rights. It considers various types of financial relations permissible for women and regards her as the owner of her own income and capital. In Surah An-Nisa, verse 32, we read: "For men is a share of what they have earned, and for women is a share of what they have earned." Considering the word "Iktisab" (earning), which—unlike "Kasb"—is for acquiring wealth whose result belongs to the person who acquired it, [^4] and also considering the general rule: "People have authority over their wealth," it is understood how Islam respected the economic independence of women and made no difference between man and woman.

In summary, the woman in Islam is considered a fundamental pillar of society and should never be treated as a being lacking will, dependent, and in need of a guardian.

6- Equality or Justice?

The only matter that must be noted (and to which Islam has paid special attention), but which some deny based on a series of extreme and uncalculated emotions, is the issue of the psychological and physical differences between men and women and the difference in their duties.

No matter how much we deny it, we cannot deny the reality that there is a great difference between these two genders both physically and psychologically, the mention of which in various books spares us from repeating them. The summary of them all is that since the woman is the base of existence and the emergence of humans, and the growth of young ones takes place in her lap, just as she is physically created suitable for carrying, nurturing, and educating future generations, she also possesses a greater share of affection and emotions psychologically.

Given these extensive differences, can it be said that man and woman must walk together in all affairs and be one hundred percent equal in all tasks? Is it not true that one must advocate for justice in society? Is justice anything other than everyone performing their duty and benefiting from their existential gifts and advantages?

Therefore, is it not contrary to justice to involve women in tasks that are outside their psychological and physical suitability? This is where we see that Islam, while advocating for justice, has prioritized the man in some social tasks that require more harshness or precision, such as the guardianship of the family home, and has assigned the role of deputy to the woman.

A "home" and a "society" each need a manager, and the issue of management in its final stage must terminate in one person; otherwise, conflict and chaos will prevail. In this situation, is it better for the man to be nominated for this task or the woman? All calculations free from prejudice say that the physical structure of the man necessitates that the management of the family be placed on him and the woman be his "deputy."

Although a group insists on ignoring these realities, the state of life even in today's world, and even in countries that have given women complete freedom and equality, shows that in practice, the matter is exactly as stated above, even if the opposite is said in speech!

Ayah

ٱلطَّلَـٰقُ مَرَّتَانِ ۖ فَإِمْسَاكٌۢ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ تَسْرِيحٌۢ بِإِحْسَـٰنٍ ۗ وَلَا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَن تَأْخُذُوا۟ مِمَّآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ شَيْـًٔا إِلَّآ أَن يَخَافَآ أَلَّا يُقِيمَا حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا يُقِيمَا حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَا فِيمَا ٱفْتَدَتْ بِهِۦ ۗ تِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا تَعْتَدُوهَا ۚ وَمَن يَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٢٢٩)
[Revocable] divorce may be only twice; then [let there be] either an honourable retention, or a kindly release. It is not lawful for you to take back anything from what you have given them, unless the couple fear that they may not maintain Allah’s bounds; so if you fear they would not maintain Allah’s bounds, there is no sin upon them in what she may give to secure her own release. These are Allah’s bounds, so do not transgress them, and whoever transgresses the bounds of Allah—it is they who are the wrongdoers. (229)

Circumstances of Revelation

A woman came to one of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives and complained about her husband, stating that he constantly divorced her and then took her back solely to cause her harm and distress. In the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), it was customary that a man had the right to divorce his wife a thousand times and return to her, with no limit imposed. When this complaint reached the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), the above verse was revealed, setting the limit of divorce at three times. [1]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 329 (This circumstance of revelation is also mentioned under the discussed verse in Tafsir al-Kabir, Qurtubi, and Ruh al-Ma'ani).

Commentary

Either a Reasonable Married Life or a Graceful Separation!

In the commentary of the previous verse, we reached the point where the laws of "Iddah" (waiting period) and "Ruju'" (recourse/return) were established to reform the family situation and prevent separation and division. However, some new Muslims, following the customs of the Age of Ignorance, began to abuse these laws. To put their wives under pressure, they would repeatedly divorce them and then return to them just before the waiting period ended, thereby placing the woman in a severe predicament.

The above verse was revealed to prevent this ugly and unchivalrous act, stating: "Divorce (referring to the divorce that allows for return) is twice" (الطَّلاقُ مَرَّتانِ).

It then adds: "After each of these two times, he must either retain his wife in an honorable manner and reconcile, or release her with kindness and separate from her forever" (فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا يُقِيما حُدُودَ اللَّهِ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِما فِيمَا افْتَدَتْ بِهِ).

Therefore, the third divorce allows no recourse or return. Once the cycle of conflict, divorce, and subsequent peace and return has occurred twice, the matter must be settled decisively. In other words, if during these two times, the lost affection and sincerity return, he can live with his wife through peace and harmony; otherwise, if he divorces her a third time, he no longer has the right of recourse to her except under the conditions that will appear in the following verse.

It should be noted that "Imsak" means retention, and "Tasrih" means releasing. The phrase "releasing with kindness" (تَسْرِيحٌ بِإِحْسانٍ) following the phrase "Divorce is twice" refers to the third divorce, which separates the two while observing the standards of justice and morality (numerous narrations state that "releasing with kindness" refers to the third divorce). [1] Thus, the meaning of separating coupled with "Ihsan" (kindness/excellence) is that he should pay the woman her rights, and after the separation, he should not cause her any harm or loss, nor speak inappropriately behind her back, nor make people cynical toward her, nor deprive her of the possibility of remarriage.

Therefore, just as retaining a wife and reconciling must be accompanied by "Ma'ruf" (honor/goodness) and sincerity, separation must also be coupled with "Ihsan."

Hence, the verse continues: "And it is not lawful for you to take back anything of what you have given them" (وَ لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَأْخُذُوا مِمَّا آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ شَيْئاً).

Consequently, the husband cannot, at the time of separation, take back what he gave the woman as a dowry (Mahr), and this is an instance of separation based on "Ihsan" (In Surah An-Nisa, verses 20 and 21, this ruling is explained in more detail, which will be discussed later).

Some commentators consider the concept of this phrase broader than just the "Mahr" and have said that he should also not take back other things he has gifted her. [2] It is interesting to note that regarding return and reconciliation, the term "Ma'ruf" (that which is not disliked by custom) is used, but regarding separation, the term "Ihsan" is used, which is something higher than "Ma'ruf," so as to compensate the woman for the bitterness and sting of separation through this means. [3] The verse then proceeds to mention the issue of "Khul'" divorce, stating: only in one scenario is there no objection to taking back the dowry, and that is when the woman has no desire to continue the married life, and "the two spouses fear that by continuing the married life they will not maintain the limits of Allah" (إِلَّا أَنْ يَخافا أَلَّا يُقِيما حُدُودَ اللَّهِ).

It then adds: "If you fear that they will not observe the limits of Allah, then there is no sin upon either of them if she gives a ransom (compensation)" and obtains a divorce (فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا يُقِيما حُدُودَ اللَّهِ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِما فِيمَا افْتَدَتْ بِهِ).

In reality, here the source of the separation is the woman, and she must pay the compensation for this act and allow the man—who is willing to live with her—to choose another spouse with that same dowry.

It is noteworthy that the pronoun in the phrase "they will not maintain" (أَلَّا يُقِيما) is in the dual form, referring to the two spouses, while in the phrase "If you fear" (فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ), it is in the plural address. This difference may point to the necessity of the supervision of religious authorities over such divorces, or it may indicate that the determination of the impossibility of continuing the marriage while observing Allah's limits is not left solely to the husband and wife.

For it often happens that due to anger, they consider small matters as reasons for the impossibility of continuing the marriage. Rather, this issue must be established from the perspective of general custom, the masses, and those who know the couple, in which case the "Khul'" divorce is permitted.

At the end of the verse, referring to all the rulings mentioned therein, it says:

"These are the limits of Allah, so do not transgress them. And whoever transgresses the limits of Allah—it is they who are the wrongdoers" (تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ فَلا تَعْتَدُوها وَ مَنْ يَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَ اللَّهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ).

[1]: Tafsir al-Ayyashi, Vol. 1, p. 116. [2]: Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 6, p. 99. [3]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 2, p. 234 (under the verse).

Key Points

1. The Necessity of Multiple Divorce Sessions

From the phrase "Divorce is twice," it is understood that two or three divorces cannot take place in a single session; they must occur in multiple sessions. This is especially because the multiplicity of divorce is intended to provide more opportunity for recourse, so that perhaps after the first conflict, peace and harmony might be restored.

If reconciliation does not occur in the first stage, it may in the second; however, the occurrence of several divorces in one instance closes this path entirely and separates them forever, effectively making the multiplicity of divorce meaningless.

This ruling is accepted by the Shia, but there is a difference of opinion among the Sunnis, although most of them believe that three divorces in one session are valid.

However, the author of Tafsir al-Manar narrates from Musnad Ahmad ibn Hanbal and Sahih Muslim (two authentic Sunni books): "This ruling—that three divorces in one session are counted as no more than one divorce—was agreed upon by all the companions from the time of the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) until two years into the caliphate of Umar. But from that time, the second caliph ruled that three divorces occur in one session!"

2. The Grand Mufti of the Sunnis and the Shia View on Divorce

Although it is well-known that the second caliph ruled that three divorces in one session are permissible, the issue is not unanimously agreed upon by the Sunnis. Among those who, contrary to other Sunni scholars, chose the Shia view on this matter was the former head of Al-Azhar University and the Grand Mufti of the Sunni world, Sheikh Mahmoud Shaltout. He writes:

"Since long ago, when I began studying and comparing schools of thought at the Faculty of Oriental Studies, it has often happened that I referred to the various views and theories of the schools on certain issues. When I found the arguments of the Shia to be firm and solid, I yielded to them and chose the Shia view." He then cites several examples, one of which is this very issue of the multiplicity of divorce. Regarding this, he writes:

"Three divorces in one session and with one phrase are considered three divorces according to the four Sunni schools, but according to the Imami Shia belief, it is counted as no more than one divorce. Since, in truth, from the perspective of the law (and the apparent meaning of the Quranic verses), the Shia view is the truth, the Sunni theory has lost its value as a fatwa." [1]

3. The Limits of Allah

In this verse and many other verses of the Holy Quran, a beautiful expression regarding divine laws is observed: the term "Hudud" (limits/boundaries). Accordingly, sin and opposition to these laws are considered a transgression of the limits and boundaries. In reality, among the actions that man performs, there are a series of "forbidden zones" where entry is extremely dangerous. Divine laws and rulings define these zones and are like signs placed in such areas. Therefore, in verse 187 of Surah Al-Baqarah, we see that even approaching these boundaries is forbidden: "These are the limits of Allah, so do not approach them" (تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ فَلا تَقْرَبُوها), because proximity to these boundaries places a person on the edge of a precipice. Furthermore, in narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), we read that they forbade doubtful matters, saying: "This act is like approaching the boundary, and perhaps with one moment of negligence, a person who has approached the boundary might step to the other side and fall into destruction."

[1]: Risalat al-Islam, No. 1, Year 11, p. 108, as quoted in the footnotes of Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 2, p. 271.

Ayah

فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا تَحِلُّ لَهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدُ حَتَّىٰ تَنكِحَ زَوْجًا غَيْرَهُۥ ۗ فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَآ أَن يَتَرَاجَعَآ إِن ظَنَّآ أَن يُقِيمَا حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ يُبَيِّنُهَا لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٣٠)
And if he divorces her, she will not be lawful for him thereafter until she marries a husband other than him, and if he divorces her, there is no sin upon them to remarry if they think that they can maintain Allah’s bounds. These are Allah’s bounds, which He clarifies for a people who have knowledge. (230)

Occasion of Revelation

It is narrated in a hadith that a woman came to the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "I was the wife of my cousin, Rifa'ah. He divorced me three times. After him, I married a man named Abd al-Rahman ibn Zubayr. Coincidentally, he also divorced me without any sexual intercourse occurring between us during this time. Can I return to my first husband?"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied: "No, you can only do so if you have had sexual intercourse with the second husband." At this point, the above verse was revealed. [^1]

Commentary

Conditional Separation!

In the previous verse, the discussion concerned two divorces, after which the two spouses must either choose the path of affection and peace or separate from one another.

This verse, in reality, acts as a proviso appended to the previous ruling. It states: "And if he has divorced her (for the third time after two divorces and revocations), then she is not lawful to him afterward until she has married another husband (and had sexual intercourse with him. In this case, if the second husband) divorces her, there is no blame upon the two of them and they may return to each other (and the woman may marry her first husband again), provided they feel that they will keep the limits of Allah" (فَإِنْ طَلَّقَها فَلا تَحِلُّ لَهُ مِنْ بَعْدُ حَتَّى تَنْكِحَ زَوْجاً غَيْرَهُ فَإِنْ طَلَّقَها فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهما أَنْ يَتَراجَعا إِنْ ظَنَّا أَنْ يُقِيما حُدُودَ اللَّهِ).

And at the end, it emphasizes: "These are the limits of Allah, which He makes clear to a people who know" (وَ تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ يُبَيِّنُها لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ).

From the narrations reached to us from the great leaders of Islam, it is understood that: Firstly, the marriage with the second person must be a permanent marriage, and following the execution of the marriage contract, the marital act (intercourse) must also take place. These two conditions can also be broadly inferred from the verse itself. As for the marriage being permanent, the phrase "فَإِنْ طَلَّقَها" (if he divorces her) testifies to this, because divorce is only conceivable in a permanent marriage. As for the performance of the marital act, it can be inferred from the phrase "حَتَّى تَنْكِحَ زَوْجاً غَيْرَهُ" (until she marries a husband other than him). According to some Arabic linguists, when it is said nakaha fulan fulanah, it means to conclude a marriage contract, but whenever it is said nakaha zawjatahu, it means to perform sexual intercourse (since the premise is that she is already his wife; therefore, using the term nikah regarding a wife can mean nothing other than sexual intercourse). [^1] Furthermore, an absolute term is typically understood to refer to its most common instance, and usually, a marriage contract is followed by intercourse. Beyond all this, as will be mentioned later, this ruling has a philosophy that is not achieved merely by reciting the marriage formula.

Key Point: The Muhallil as a Deterrent Against Divorce

It is common among jurists to call the second husband in such cases a muhallil (the legalizer), because he causes the woman to become lawful (of course, after divorce and the waiting period) to the first husband. It appears that the intention of the Divine Legislator was to prevent successive divorces through this ruling.

To explain: Just as marriage is a vital and necessary matter, divorce also becomes a necessity under certain conditions. Therefore, Islam—unlike distorted Christianity—has permitted divorce. However, since the disintegration of families causes irreparable harm to the individual and society, Islam has limited divorce as much as possible using various factors and has legislated laws that reduce divorce to a minimum.

The requirement of remarriage or the muhallil, which appears in the above verse after three divorces, is considered one of those factors. This is because the formal marriage of the woman to another man after three divorces—especially with the condition that sexual intercourse must take place—is a great barrier to the continuation of divorces and "divorce-games."

It warns the person who wants to initiate a third divorce that the way back might be closed to him forever, because the path of return passes through a permanent marriage with another man. The second husband might not divorce her, and even if he does, this process can wound the man's conscience and emotions; therefore, he will not resort to such an action unless forced.

In reality, the subject of the muhallil—or in other words, the permanent remarriage of the woman to another husband—is an obstacle in the path of hedonistic and deceitful men, so they do not make the woman a plaything of their whims and do not use the law of divorce and revocation limitlessly. At the same time, the path of return is not completely closed.

The conditions set for this marriage, such as it being permanent, indicate that the goal of the new marriage was not merely to pave the way for the woman to reunite with the first husband. This is because the second husband might not be willing to divorce, and it is not a temporary marriage that would end on its own. Therefore, this law cannot be easily abused.

Considering what was mentioned above, it can be said: The goal was that after three divorces, the man and woman should separate through another marriage so that each may pursue the life they desire, and the sacred matter of marriage does not fall prey to the satanic inclinations of the first husband. Yet, if she also separates from the second husband, the path of return is not closed to them, and their marriage becomes lawful again; hence, the name muhallil is given to the second husband.

From what has been said, it becomes clear that the discussion is about a real and serious marriage. If someone does not intend a permanent marriage from the beginning and merely creates a facade to achieve the title of muhallil, such a marriage is void and will have no effect in making the woman lawful to the first husband. The famous hadith narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in many books of commentary:

"Allah has cursed the muhallil and the one for whom the muhallil acts" [^1]

may refer to these superficial and fabricated marriages.

Some have also said that if he enters into a serious permanent marriage but his intention is to pave the way for the woman's return to the first husband, his marriage is void and the woman does not become lawful to the first husband. Others have said that if his intention was a serious marriage, even if his ultimate goal was to open the way for the first husband, that marriage is valid, though it is disliked (makruh), provided that such a matter is not mentioned as a condition of the contract.

From here, it becomes clear that the outcry of some biased and uninformed individuals—who have attacked this issue without knowledge of its conditions and characteristics, weaving words out of personal malice toward Islamic sanctities or ignorance of its laws—has the least value. It is only evidence of their ignorance and animosity toward Islam. Otherwise, this divine ruling, with the conditions mentioned, is a factor for deterring repeated divorces, ending the autocracy of some men, and organizing the system of marriage and matrimony.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 330 (summarized). This occasion of revelation is also mentioned in Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Qurtubi, and Maraghi. [^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 6, p. 104. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 331. This hadith is also narrated by Qurtubi, Al-Manar, and Maraghi under the verse in question.

Ayah

وَإِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَبَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ سَرِّحُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ ۚ وَلَا تُمْسِكُوهُنَّ ضِرَارًا لِّتَعْتَدُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَقَدْ ظَلَمَ نَفْسَهُۥ ۚ وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ هُزُوًا ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَمَآ أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَٱلْحِكْمَةِ يَعِظُكُم بِهِۦ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٢٣١)
When you divorce women and they complete their term [of waiting], then either retain them honourably or release them honourably, and do not retain them maliciously in order that you may transgress; and whoever does that certainly wrongs himself. Do not take the signs of Allah in derision, and remember Allah’s blessing upon you, and what He has sent down to you of the Book and wisdom, to advise you therewith. Be wary of Allah and know that Allah has knowledge of all things. (231)

Commentary

Further Restrictions on Divorce

Following the previous verses, this verse also points to other restrictions regarding the matter of divorce to prevent the violation of women's rights.

At the beginning, it says: "And when you divorce women and they have reached [the end of] their term, (you may reconcile with them) either retain them according to acceptable terms or release them according to acceptable terms" (وَ إِذا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّساءَ فَبَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ بِمعرُوفٍ أَوْ سَرِّحُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ‌).

Either sincerely decide to continue the marital life, or if you do not find the circumstances favorable, separate from one another with kindness, not with conflict, contention, harassment, or seeking revenge.

Then, referring to the opposite concept, He says: "And do not keep them to intend harm, to transgress [against them]" (وَ لا تُمْسِكُوهُنَّ ضِراراً لِتَعْتَدُوا).

This sentence is, in fact, an explanation of the word "Ma'ruf" (acceptable/kindness), because in the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), returning to the marriage was sometimes used as a means of seeking revenge; therefore, He says with a decisive tone: "You must never harbor such a thought in your head."

"For whoever does that has certainly wronged himself" (وَ مَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ فَقَدْ ظَلَمَ نَفْسَهُ‌).

Thus, these incorrect actions are not only an injustice against the woman, but rather an injustice and oppression you have committed against yourselves because:

Firstly: A return (ruju') that is intended for the purpose of violating rights and causing harassment can offer no tranquility, and the environment of marital life becomes a burning hell for both parties.

Secondly: From the perspective of Islam, man and woman are members of one body in the system of creation; therefore, trampling upon the rights of the woman will be a transgression and injustice against oneself.

Thirdly: By committing this injustice and oppression, men are actually inviting Divine punishment, and what injustice against oneself could be greater than this?

Then He warns everyone, saying: "And do not take the verses of Allah in jest" (وَ لا تَتَّخِذُوا آياتِ اللَّهِ هُزُواً).

This expression could also refer to the wrongful practices of the Age of Ignorance, the remnants of which remained in people's thoughts.

It is mentioned in a Hadith that during the Age of Ignorance, some men, when they would divorce, would say: "Our intention was play and a joke," and they would say the same when they freed a slave or took a woman in marriage.

The above verse was revealed and warned them, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Whoever divorces a woman, or frees a slave, or marries a woman, or gives one in marriage, and then claims he was playing or joking, it will not be accepted from him and it shall be regarded as serious." [^1] There is also the possibility that the verse refers to those who create "legal loopholes" (Kolah-e Shar'i) for their wrongful acts and use outward appearances as a pretext. The Quran considers this a form of mocking the Divine verses, including this very issue of marriage, divorce, and returning during the waiting period (Iddah) with the intention of seeking revenge and harassing the woman while pretending to exercise one's legal right.

Therefore, one must not make the Divine verses a plaything or a toy by ignoring the spirit of Divine laws and clinging to dry appearances and soulless forms, for the sin of this act is more severe and its punishment more painful.

Then He adds: "And remember the favor of Allah upon you and what He has revealed to you of the Book and wisdom by which He instructs you" (وَ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ ما أَنْزَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَ الْحِكْمَةِ يَعِظُكُمْ بِهِ‌).

"And fear Allah and know that Allah is Knowing of all things" (وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيمٌ‌).

These warnings are so that, firstly, they realize that Allah has liberated them from the superstitions and ugly customs of the Age of Ignorance regarding marriage, divorce, and other matters, and has guided them toward the life-giving decrees of Islam; they should appreciate this and fulfill their obligations. Secondly, regarding the rights of women, they should not abuse their position and should know that Allah is aware even of their intentions. [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 2, p. 964—a similar meaning with slight differences is mentioned in Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 2, p. 179. [^2]: Therefore, the phrase "وَ ما أَنْزَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَ الْحِكْمَةِ" (and what He has revealed to you of the Book and wisdom) might be an "Atf-e Bayan" (explanatory conjunction) for "نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ‌" (the favor of Allah), or it could be of the type of "conjoining the specific to the general." In this case, "Ni'mat Allah" has a broad meaning that encompasses all Divine blessings, including the blessing of love and affection that Allah has created between two spouses.

Ayah

وَإِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَبَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَلَا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ أَن يَنكِحْنَ أَزْوَٰجَهُنَّ إِذَا تَرَٰضَوْا۟ بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ يُوعَظُ بِهِۦ مَن كَانَ مِنكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۗ ذَٰلِكُمْ أَزْكَىٰ لَكُمْ وَأَطْهَرُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٣٢)
When you divorce women and they complete their term, do not hinder them from [re]marrying their husbands, when they honourably reach mutual consent. Herewith are advised those of you who believe in Allah and the Last Day. That will be more decent and purer for you, and Allah knows and you do not know. (232)

Circumstances of Revelation

One of the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) named "Ma'qil ibn Yasar" had a sister named "Jamla" who had been divorced by her husband, Asim ibn Adi. After her waiting period (iddah) ended, she wished to marry her husband again, but her brother prevented this. The above verse was revealed, forbidding him from opposing such a marriage.

It has also been said that the verse was revealed when "Jabir ibn Abd Allah" opposed the remarriage of his cousin to her former husband. [^1] Perhaps in the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), such a right was granted to most close relatives to interfere in the marriage affairs of their female kinsfolk. There is no doubt that according to Shia jurisprudence, a brother or a cousin has no guardianship (wilayah) over his sister or female cousin, and the above verse seeks to negate such unauthorized interferences. Rather, as we shall see, a broader ruling is derived from this verse even regarding legal guardians (awliya), such that even a father or grandfather—let alone other relatives or strangers—has no right to oppose such marriages.

Commentary

Breaking Another Chain of Women's Captivity

As previously mentioned, during the Age of Ignorance, women were in the chains of men's captivity. Without any regard for their will or desire, they were forced to organize their lives according to the whims of autocratic men.

Among these issues was the choice of a spouse; no importance was given to the woman's wish or desire. Even if a woman married with the guardian's permission and then separated from her husband, her subsequent reunion with the first husband depended on the will of the men in the family. It often happened that although the husband and wife desired to return to each other after separation, the male relatives would prevent it based on superstitions and vain imaginings.

The Quran explicitly condemns this practice, stating: "And when you divorce women and they have reached their term, do not prevent them from marrying their (former) husbands if they agree among themselves in a lawful manner" (وَ إِذا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّساءَ فَبَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَلا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ أَنْ يَنْكِحْنَ أَزْواجَهُنَّ إِذا تَراضَوْا بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ).

This applies if the addressees in this verse are the guardians and male relatives. However, there is also a possibility that the addressee is the first husband; meaning:

When you divorce a woman, do not interfere with her remarriage to other husbands. This is because some stubborn individuals, both in the past and today, exhibit sensitivity toward their former wife marrying another man after the divorce, which is nothing but a remnant of Ignorant thought. [^1] Furthermore, in the previous verse, "reaching the term" (bulugh al-ajal) meant reaching the final days of the waiting period, whereas in the verse under discussion, by the context of remarriage, it refers to the complete end of the waiting period. [^2] Therefore, it is inferred from the verse that "previously married" women (thayyib) have no need to obtain the consent of guardians for their remarriage; even their opposition is ineffective.

Then, continuing the verse, He warns once more and says: "This is an instruction by which whoever among you believes in Allah and the Last Day is exhorted" (ذلِكَ يُوعَظُ بِهِ مَنْ كانَ مِنْكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ).

And again, for further emphasis, He says: "That is more virtuous for you and purer (for your families), and Allah knows and you do not know" (ذلِكُمْ أَزْكى‌ لَكُمْ وَ أَطْهَرُ وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَ أَنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ).

This part of the verse essentially says: These rulings are all stated for your benefit; however, only those can benefit from them who possess the capital of faith in the Origin and the Resurrection and can control their desires.

In other words, this sentence says: The result of acting upon these instructions returns one hundred percent to yourselves, but due to limited knowledge, you may not be aware of the philosophy behind these rulings. However, Allah, who is aware of their secrets, has ordained these laws to preserve the purity and cleanliness of your families.

It is noteworthy that acting upon these instructions is introduced as both a cause for "purification" (tazkiyah) and "purity" (taharah) (أَزْكى‌ لَكُمْ وَ أَطْهَرُ).

That is, it both removes the pollutions that afflict families due to wrongdoings and is a source of growth, perfection, goodness, and blessing. (It should not be forgotten that tazkiyah is originally derived from zakat, meaning growth).

Some commentators consider the phrase "أَزْكى‌ لَكُمْ" (more virtuous/purer for growth) to refer to the rewards obtained by acting on these instructions, and the phrase "أطهر" (purer) to refer to being cleansed from sins.

It is obvious that incidents occur where two spouses, despite all the affection they have for one another, separate under certain influences. Later, when they see the deadly effects of separation with their own eyes, they regret it and decide to return. Strictness and prejudice against their return deal a heavy blow to both and may even become a cause for their deviation and pollution. If there are children involved—as there often are—they will face a very painful fate, and those who prevent their reconciliation are responsible for these grim consequences.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 332. Many other commentators such as al-Qurtubi, Tafsir al-Kabir, Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, and Fi Zilal al-Quran have narrated both circumstances of revelation or one of them under the verse in question.

[^1]: Some have preferred the second interpretation because it aligns with the preceding verses where the addressees are generally the husbands (but this has the drawback that the expression "their husbands" (azwajuhunna) in relation to future husbands is a metaphorical expression; furthermore, it does not align with the circumstances of revelation of the verse).

[^2]: In technical terms, in the previous verse, the "limit" (ghayah) was part of the "limited" (mughayya), while here it is outside of it.

Ayah

۞ وَٱلْوَٰلِدَٰتُ يُرْضِعْنَ أَوْلَـٰدَهُنَّ حَوْلَيْنِ كَامِلَيْنِ ۖ لِمَنْ أَرَادَ أَن يُتِمَّ ٱلرَّضَاعَةَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱلْمَوْلُودِ لَهُۥ رِزْقُهُنَّ وَكِسْوَتُهُنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ لَا تُكَلَّفُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۚ لَا تُضَآرَّ وَٰلِدَةٌۢ بِوَلَدِهَا وَلَا مَوْلُودٌ لَّهُۥ بِوَلَدِهِۦ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱلْوَارِثِ مِثْلُ ذَٰلِكَ ۗ فَإِنْ أَرَادَا فِصَالًا عَن تَرَاضٍ مِّنْهُمَا وَتَشَاوُرٍ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَا ۗ وَإِنْ أَرَدتُّمْ أَن تَسْتَرْضِعُوٓا۟ أَوْلَـٰدَكُمْ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذَا سَلَّمْتُم مَّآ ءَاتَيْتُم بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٢٣٣)
Mothers shall suckle their children for two full years—that for such as desire to complete the suckling—and on the father shall be their maintenance and clothing, in accordance with honourable norms. No soul is to be tasked except according to its capacity: neither the mother shall be made to suffer harm on her child’s account, nor the father on account of his child, and on the [father’s] heir devolve [duties and rights] similar to that. And if the couple desire to wean with mutual consent and consultation, there will be no sin upon them. And if you want to have your children wet-nursed, there will be no sin upon you so long as you pay what you give in accordance with honourable norms, and be wary of Allah and know that Allah watches what you do. (233)

Commentary

Seven Instructions Regarding the Nursing of Infants

This verse, which is in fact a continuation of the discussions related to marriage and matrimonial issues, addresses an important matter: the issue of "Radā‘" (nursing). In very brief and concise yet profound and instructive terms, it explains the details of this matter.

1- First, it states: "Mothers shall suckle their children for two whole years" (وَ الْوالِداتُ يُرْضِعْنَ أَوْلادَهُنَّ حَوْلَيْنِ كامِلَيْنِ‌).

"Wālidāt" is the plural of "Wālidah," which in the Arabic language means mother. However, "Umm" has a broader meaning, sometimes referring to the mother or the maternal grandmother, and sometimes to the root and foundation of anything.

In this part of the verse, the right to nurse during the two years of infancy is given to the mother. It is she who can care for her child during this period; in legal terms, the right of custody (Hadhānah) during this time belongs to the mother. Although the guardianship (Wilāyah) over minor children is entrusted to the father, because the physical and spiritual nourishment of the infant during this period is inseparably linked to the mother's milk and affection, this right is granted to the mother. Furthermore, the mother's emotions must also be respected, as she cannot bear to see her arms empty of her child during such sensitive moments or remain indifferent to the infant's condition. Therefore, establishing the right of custody, care, and nursing for the mother is a kind of bilateral right intended to consider the well-being of both the child and the mother. The expression "their children" (awlāduhunna) is a subtle allusion to this point.

Although the appearance of this sentence is absolute and includes both divorced and non-divorced women, the subsequent sentences show that this verse specifically considers divorced women. Even though other mothers also possess such a right, in the absence of separation and divorce, it has no practical effect [on legal disputes].

2- It then adds: "This is for him who desires to complete the nursing period" (لِمَنْ أَرادَ أَنْ يُتِمَّ الرَّضاعَةَ).

This means that the duration of nursing the child does not necessarily have to be two years. Two years is for one who wishes to complete the nursing, but mothers have the right to reduce this period based on the infant's condition and the preservation of its health.

In traditions reached to us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, the complete period of nursing is introduced as two years, and the minimum as twenty-one months. [^1] It is not unlikely that this meaning is derived by combining the above verse with the verse: "and his bearing and weaning is thirty months" (وَ حَمْلُهُ وَ فِصالُهُ ثَلاثُونَ شَهْراً) [^2]. We know that the pregnancy period is usually nine months; if we subtract that from thirty months, twenty-one months remain, which would be the standard nursing period. In fact, considering that what appears in Surah al-Ahqaf is also not an absolute obligation, mothers have the right—considering the best interest and health of the infant—to reduce the nursing period even to less than twenty-one months.

3- The mother's living expenses in terms of food and clothing during the nursing period are the responsibility of the infant's father, so that the mother can nurse the child with a peaceful mind. Therefore, the verse continues: "And upon him to whom the child is born (the father) is their provision and their clothing according to what is fair" (وَ عَلَى الْمَوْلُودِ لَهُ رِزْقُهُنَّ وَ كِسْوَتُهُنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ‌).

Here, the expression "the one to whom the child is born" (al-mawlūdi lahu) instead of "father" (ab or wālid) is noteworthy. It is as if it seeks to mobilize the father's emotions in the path of fulfilling this duty; meaning, if the expense of the child and its mother at this time is placed upon the man, it is because the child is his and the fruit of his heart, not a stranger.

The description "according to what is fair" (bil-ma‘rūf) shows that fathers, regarding the mother's clothing and food, must consider what is appropriate, customary, and suitable for her status—neither being stingy nor extravagant.

To explain further, He says: "No soul shall be burdened beyond its capacity" (لا تُكَلَّفُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا وُسْعَها).

Therefore, every father has a duty according to his ability. Some have considered this sentence as the rationale for the original ruling, and others as an explanation of the previous ruling (and both lead to the same result).

4- Then, it proceeds to state another important ruling, saying: "Neither shall a mother be made to suffer on account of her child, nor shall he to whom the child is born" on account of his child (لا تُضَارَّ والِدَةٌ بِوَلَدِها وَ لا مَوْلُودٌ لَهُ بِوَلَدِهِ‌).

That is, neither of the two has the right to make the child's fate a bargaining chip for their own disputes, nor to inflict harm upon the infant's body or soul.

Men must not trample upon the mothers' right of custody and care by taking the children from them during the nursing period, which would harm the child. Similarly, mothers must not shirk this responsibility or refuse to nurse the child under various pretexts, nor deprive the father of seeing his child.

Another possibility suggested in the interpretation of the verse is that it means the father cannot deprive the woman of her marital rights for fear of pregnancy and subsequent harm to the nursing infant, nor can the mother withhold this right from the husband for the same reason.

However, the first interpretation is more consistent with the literal meaning of the verse. [^1] The use of the terms "her child" and "his child" is also to encourage fathers and mothers to care for the nursing infants. Additionally, it shows that the infant belongs to both, contrary to the customs of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) where the child was considered to belong only to the father and no share was recognized for the mother.

5- It then addresses another ruling related to after the father's death, saying: "And upon the heir is the like of that" (وَ عَلَى الْوارِثِ مِثْلُ ذلِكَ‌).

Meaning: they must provide for the mother's needs during the period she nurses the child. Here, some other possibilities have been suggested in the interpretation of the verse which seem weak.

6- In the continuation of the verse, the issue of weaning the child is mentioned, leaving the choice to the father and mother. Although a time for nursing the child was determined in the previous sentences, the father and mother, considering the child's physical and spiritual condition and by mutual agreement, can wean the child at any appropriate time. It says: "If they both desire weaning by mutual consent and counsel, there is no sin upon them" (فَإِنْ أَرادا فِصالًا عَنْ تَراضٍ مِنْهُما وَ تَشاوُرٍ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِما).

In fact, the father and mother must consider the child's best interests and, through mutual thinking and agreement—or in the Quran's terms, "mutual consent and counsel"—set a plan for weaning the child. In this task, they should avoid conflict, quarreling, pursuing their own interests, and trampling upon the child's interests.

7- Sometimes it happens that the mother refuses her right regarding nursing and custody, or a genuine obstacle arises for her. In this case, a solution must be devised. Therefore, the verse continues: "And if you wish to have your children nursed by a wet-nurse, there is no sin upon you, provided you pay what you have given according to what is fair" (وَ إِنْ أَرَدْتُمْ أَنْ تَسْتَرْضِعُوا أَوْلادَكُمْ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذا سَلَّمْتُمْ ما آتَيْتُمْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ‌).

Regarding the interpretation of the phrase "provided you pay what you have given according to what is fair," various views have been expressed by commentators. A group has accepted the above interpretation: that choosing a wet-nurse instead of the mother, after mutual consent, is permissible provided this does not lead to the loss of the mother's rights regarding the past; rather, her right for the period she did nurse should be paid according to custom.

While some have considered it referring to the wet-nurse's right, saying her fee must be paid according to custom. Some have also said this phrase refers to the agreement of the father and mother in the matter of choosing a wet-nurse, thus becoming an emphasis on the previous sentence. However, this interpretation seems weak, and the first and second interpretations are more correct; the late Tabarsi preferred the first interpretation. [^1] At the end of the verse, He warns everyone: "And fear Allah, and know that Allah is Seer of what you do" (وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ).

Lest the conflict between man and woman ignite a spirit of revenge in them, endangering each other's fate or that of the innocent children. Everyone must know that Allah is closely watching their actions.

These precise and calculated rulings, along with the warnings accompanying them, clearly show to what extent Islam values the rights of children as well as mothers, and commands the observance of maximum justice in this regard. Indeed, Islam—contrary to what exists in the world of oppressors where the rights of the weak are always trampled—has given the utmost importance to preserving their rights.

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 15, p. 177 (Chapter on the minimum and maximum duration of nursing), Hadith 2 and 5. In some of these traditions, it is stated that whatever is less than 21 months is an injustice to the infant. [^2]: Surah al-Ahqaf, Verse 15. [^1]: According to the first interpretation, the verb "la tudharra" is in the active voice, and according to the second interpretation, it is in the passive voice, although the pronunciation is the same in both—take note. [^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 336.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ يُتَوَفَّوْنَ مِنكُمْ وَيَذَرُونَ أَزْوَٰجًا يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنفُسِهِنَّ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ وَعَشْرًا ۖ فَإِذَا بَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيمَا فَعَلْنَ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (٢٣٤)
As for those of you who die leaving wives, they shall wait by themselves four months and ten days, and when they complete their term, there will be no sin upon you in respect of what they may do with themselves in accordance with honourable norms. And Allah is well aware of what you do. (234)
