وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيمَا عَرَّضْتُم بِهِۦ مِنْ خِطْبَةِ ٱلنِّسَآءِ أَوْ أَكْنَنتُمْ فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ عَلِمَ ٱللَّهُ أَنَّكُمْ سَتَذْكُرُونَهُنَّ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تُوَاعِدُوهُنَّ سِرًّا إِلَّآ أَن تَقُولُوا۟ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا ۚ وَلَا تَعْزِمُوا۟ عُقْدَةَ ٱلنِّكَاحِ حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ أَجَلَهُۥ ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ فَٱحْذَرُوهُ ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ (٢٣٥)
There is no sin upon you in what you may hint in proposing to [recently widowed] women, or what you may secretly cherish within your hearts. Allah knows that you will be thinking of them, but do not make troth with them secretly, unless you say honourable words, and do not resolve on a marriage tie until the prescribed term is complete. Know that Allah knows what is in your hearts, so beware of Him; and know that Allah is all-forgiving, all-forbearing. (235)

Commentary

Superstitions That Oppressed Women!

One of the fundamental issues and challenges for women is marriage after the death of a husband. Since an immediate marriage to another man following a husband's death is inconsistent with affection, friendship, maintaining respect for the former husband, and ensuring the womb is free from the seed of the previous spouse—and furthermore, because it wounds the emotions of the deceased's relatives—the above verse stipulates that the remarriage of women is conditional upon observing a waiting period ('iddah) of four months and ten days. Respecting the sanctity of marital life even after the death of a spouse is an innate human inclination; hence, various tribes have always had different customs and traditions for this purpose. However, they sometimes went to such extremes in these customs that they practically placed women in a deadlock and captivity, and at times committed the most criminal acts against them. For example: some tribes would burn the woman after her husband's death, or some would bury her with the man. Some deprived the woman of remarriage forever, forcing her into seclusion. In some tribes, women were required to spend a period beside the husband's grave under a black, filthy tent, wearing tattered and dirty clothes, away from any adornment, jewelry, or even washing, spending their days and nights in this state. [^1]

The above verse strikes a line of cancellation through all these superstitions and crimes, allowing widowed women to proceed with marriage after observing the waiting period and maintaining the sanctity of the past marriage. It says: "And those of you who die and leave wives behind, they shall wait by themselves for four months and ten days. And when they have reached their term, there is no sin upon

[^1]: Islam and Human Beliefs and Opinions, page 617.

you for what they do for themselves in a fitting manner" and marry the man of their choice. (وَ الَّذِينَ يُتَوَفَّوْنَ مِنْكُمْ وَ يَذَرُونَ أَزْواجاً يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنْفُسِهِنَّ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ وَ عَشْراً فَإِذا بَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيما فَعَلْنَ فِي أَنْفُسِهِنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ).

And since sometimes the guardians and relatives of the woman interfere unnecessarily in her affairs or consider their own interests in her future marriage, at the end of the verse, Allah warns everyone and says: "And Allah is Aware of whatever you do," and He will bring everyone to the recompense of their good and evil deeds (وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ).

The phrase "there is no sin upon you for what they do for themselves in a fitting manner," considering that the addressees are the male relatives, indicates that they seemingly considered it a sin for themselves to leave the woman free after the husband's death; conversely, they viewed restriction and harshness as their duty. This verse clearly states: leave them free, and there is no sin upon you (furthermore, it is inferred from this expression that the guardianship of the father and grandfather is also terminated here). However, it also reminds the women not to abuse their freedom and to proceed in a fitting manner (bil-ma'ruf) for choosing a new husband.

According to narrations reached us from the leaders of Islam, women are obliged to maintain a form of mourning during this period; that is, they must absolutely not wear makeup and should remain simple. Indeed, the philosophy of observing such a waiting period necessitates this.

Islam rescued women from the superstitious customs of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) to such an extent that some thought they could marry even within this short period of 'iddah. One such woman, who thought this way, came to the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله one day seeking permission for remarriage. She asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Do you permit me to apply kohl and adorn myself?"

The Prophet said: "You women are strange creatures! Before Islam, you spent the waiting period

after a death in the harshest conditions, sometimes until the end of your lives, while you did not even allow yourselves the right to wash. Now that Islam, for the sake of the family's sanctity and respecting the right of marriage, has commanded you to live simply for a short period, you cannot endure it." [^1] It is noteworthy that in Islamic laws regarding the 'iddah, it is explicitly stated that even if there is no possibility of the woman being pregnant, women whose husbands have died must still observe the waiting period.

Also, for this reason, the start of the 'iddah is not the moment of the husband's death, but rather when the news of the death reaches the woman, even if it is months later. This itself shows that the legislation of this ruling is, above all, for the sake of maintaining respect and the sanctity of marriage, although the issue of the woman's potential pregnancy was certainly considered in this law.

The next verse refers to one of the important rulings for women who are in the waiting period (in connection with the discussion on the 'iddah of death that passed). It says: "There is no sin upon you if you make a hint of a marriage proposal (to women who are in the 'iddah of death), or conceal it in your hearts. Allah knew that you would remember them, but do not make a promise to them secretly, except that you speak a fitting word (by hinting)" (وَ لا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيما عَرَّضْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْ خِطْبَةِ النِّساءِ أَوْ أَكْنَنْتُمْ فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ عَلِمَ اللَّهُ أَنَّكُمْ سَتَذْكُرُونَهُنَّ وَ لكِنْ لا تُواعِدُوهُنَّ سِرًّا إِلَّا أَنْ تَقُولُوا قَوْلًا مَعْرُوفاً).

This instruction is actually so that the sanctity of the previous marriage is preserved, while widowed women are not deprived of the right to determine their future destiny—an instruction that is both just and coupled with maintaining mutual respect.

In fact, it is a natural matter that upon the death of a husband, a woman thinks about her future

[^1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 2, page 422.

destiny, and men might also—due to the easier conditions widowed women have—think about marrying them. On the other hand, the sanctity of the previous marriage must also be preserved. What was mentioned above is a calculated instruction in which all these issues have been observed.

The phrase "but do not make a promise to them secretly" implies that in addition to the necessity of refraining from an explicit proposal, one should not meet such women in secret during the 'iddah period and propose explicitly, unless the conversation is such that it is compatible with social etiquette and the matter of the husband's death—meaning it occurs under a veil and through hints.

The expression "‘arradtum" (عَرَّضْتُمْ) from the root "ta'rid" (تعريض), according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, refers to speech that can carry two meanings: truth and falsehood, or apparent and hidden.

And according to the noble commentator, the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, ta'rid is the opposite of tasrih (explicit statement). It is originally derived from "‘ard" (عرض), which means the side or edge of something. [^1] In Islamic narrations, examples have been mentioned in the interpretation of this verse for proposing in a veiled manner, or what the Quran calls "fitting speech" (qawlan ma'rufan). For instance, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "Fitting speech is, for example, that a man says to the woman he is interested in: 'I am indeed inclined toward you and I honor women, so do not precede me regarding yourself' (meaning, do not make a decision without me)." [^2] This same meaning or similar to it has appeared in the words of many jurists.

A noteworthy point is that although the above verse is placed after the verse regarding the 'iddah of death, jurists have specified that the above ruling is not exclusive to the 'iddah of death but includes others as well.

The late Sahib al-Hada'iq, the famous jurist and traditionist, says: "Our companions have specified that ta'rid and hinting at a proposal regarding a woman who is in a revocable divorce ('iddah raj'iyyah) is forbidden. However, regarding a woman in an irrevocable divorce (mutallaqah ghayr raj'iyyah), it is permissible both from her husband and from others, but explicit proposal is not permissible for either..."

As for the irrevocable waiting period ('iddah ba'in), ta'rid is permissible from the husband and others, but explicit proposal is permissible only from the husband, not others—for more details on this subject, please study the books of jurisprudence, especially the continuation of the words of Sahib al-Hada'iq. [^1]

Then, in the continuation of the verse, He says: "(But in any case) do not resolve on the tie of marriage until the waiting period reaches its end" (وَ لا تَعْزِمُوا عُقْدَةَ النِّكاحِ حَتَّى يَبْلُغَ الْكِتابُ أَجَلَهُ).

And certainly, if someone concludes a marriage contract during the 'iddah, it is void; rather, if he does this knowingly, it causes that woman to become forbidden to him forever.

Following that, He says: "And know that Allah knows what is in your hearts, so beware of Him. And know that Allah is Forgiving, Forbearing" and He does not hasten in punishing His servants (وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ ما فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ فَاحْذَرُوهُ وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ).

In this way, Allah is aware of all the intentions and deeds of His servants and does not punish the violators quickly. "La ta'zimu" (لا تعزموا) is from the root "‘azm" (عزم) meaning intention, and when He says: "do not resolve on the tie of marriage," it is actually a prohibition against performing the marriage contract in an emphasized manner—meaning do not even intend such an act during the time of the 'iddah.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, page 338. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, page 232, Hadith 905. [^1]: Al-Hada'iq, Vol. 24, page 90.

Ayahs

لَّا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِن طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ مَا لَمْ تَمَسُّوهُنَّ أَوْ تَفْرِضُوا۟ لَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً ۚ وَمَتِّعُوهُنَّ عَلَى ٱلْمُوسِعِ قَدَرُهُۥ وَعَلَى ٱلْمُقْتِرِ قَدَرُهُۥ مَتَـٰعًۢا بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۖ حَقًّا عَلَى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٢٣٦)
There is no sin upon you if you divorce women while you have not yet touched them or settled a dowry for them. Yet provide for them—the well-off according to his capacity, and the poorly-off according to his capacity—with a sustenance that is honourable, an obligation on the virtuous. (236)
وَإِن طَلَّقْتُمُوهُنَّ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَمَسُّوهُنَّ وَقَدْ فَرَضْتُمْ لَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً فَنِصْفُ مَا فَرَضْتُمْ إِلَّآ أَن يَعْفُونَ أَوْ يَعْفُوَا۟ ٱلَّذِى بِيَدِهِۦ عُقْدَةُ ٱلنِّكَاحِ ۚ وَأَن تَعْفُوٓا۟ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ ۚ وَلَا تَنسَوُا۟ ٱلْفَضْلَ بَيْنَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٢٣٧)
And if you divorce them before you touch them, and you have already settled a dowry for them, then [pay them] half of what you have settled, unless they forgo it, or someone in whose hand is the marriage tie forgoes it. And to forgo is nearer to Godwariness; so do not forget graciousness among yourselves. Indeed Allah watches what you do. (237)

Commentary

How to Pay the Dowry (Mahr)

Continuing the discussion on the rulings of divorce, these two verses outline further regulations.

First, it states: "There is no sin upon you if you divorce women before you have touched them (had sexual intercourse) or settled a dowry for them" (لَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِنْ طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ مَا لَمْ تَمَسُّوهُنَّ أَوْ تَفْرِضُوا لَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً). [^1] This applies to cases where the man, or both the man and woman, realize after the marriage contract (Nikah) but before the consummation of the marriage that they cannot live together for certain reasons. In such a situation, it is better for them to separate through divorce at this stage, as matters become more difficult in later stages.

In any case, this expression is a response to those who imagined that divorce before sexual intercourse or before the settlement of the dowry was not valid. The Quran states: such a divorce carries no sin and is valid (and perhaps prevents further corruption).

Some have also interpreted "Junah" (sin/blame) here to mean the "dowry" itself which weighs upon the husband's shoulders; meaning, upon divorce before intercourse and settlement of the dowry, no dowry is incumbent upon you. Although some commentators [^2] have provided lengthy explanations regarding this interpretation, using the word "Junah" to mean "dowry" is not linguistically familiar.

Others have suggested that the meaning of the above sentence is: divorcing women before intercourse is permissible in all states (whether they are in their menstrual period or not), whereas after intercourse, she must certainly be in a state of purity (Tuhr) in which no intercourse has occurred. ^3 This interpretation seems very unlikely, as it does not align with the phrase "أَوْ تَفْرِضُوا لَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً" (or settled for them a dowry).

Then, it proceeds to state another ruling in this regard, saying: "In such a state, you must provide for them (with a suitable gift)" (وَ مَتِّعُوهُنَّ).

Therefore, if no dowry has been settled and no intercourse has taken place, the husband must

[^1]: "Mass" (touching) linguistically means making contact, and here it is a metaphor for sexual intercourse. "Faridah" means obligatory, and here it refers to the dowry (Mahr). [^2]: Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 6, p. 137.

provide a gift suitable to the woman's status after the divorce. However, in giving this gift, the husband's financial capability must also be considered. Thus, the continuation of the verse says: "The wealthy according to his means and the poor according to his means; a provision in a fair manner, a duty upon the doers of good" (عَلَى الْمُوسِعِ قَدَرُهُ وَ عَلَى الْمُقْتِرِ قَدَرُهُ مَتَاعاً بِالْمَعْرُوفِ حَقًّا عَلَى الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

"Musi‘" means wealthy, and "Muqtir" means poor (derived from the root 'qatar', which also implies stinginess and narrow-mindedness), as in: (وَ كَانَ الْإِنْسَانُ قَتُوراً). [^1] Therefore, the wealthy must pay according to their status and the poor according to their ability, and the woman's status is also taken into account in this regard.

The phrase "مَتَاعاً بِالْمَعْرُوفِ" (provision in a fair manner) can refer to all of this: a gift given appropriately, free from extravagance or stinginess, and suitable for both the giver and the receiver.

Since this gift has a significant effect in preventing feelings of revenge and releasing the woman from the complexes that might arise due to the breaking of the marital bond, the above verse links it to the spirit of benevolence and excellence, saying: حَقًّا عَلَى الْمُحْسِنِينَ [^2] "This is a duty upon the doers of good." That is, it should be mixed with the spirit of kindness and peace.

It goes without saying that referring to them as "doers of good" (Muhsinin) is not because the aforementioned ruling is non-obligatory, but rather to stimulate the benevolent feelings of individuals in fulfilling this duty; otherwise, as mentioned, this ruling is obligatory.

Another interesting point derived from the verse is that the Quran refers to the gift

[^1]: Surah Al-Isra, Verse 100. [^2]: "Haqqan" can be an adjective for "Mata'an", or a circumstantial adverb (Hal), or an absolute object (Maful Mutlaq) for a hidden verb. "Mata'an" is also an absolute object for the phrase "وَ مَتِّعُوهُنَّ".

that the man must pay the woman as "Mata‘". "Mata‘" linguistically refers to things that a human benefits from and enjoys, and it is often applied to things other than money and cash, because money cannot be used directly; rather, it must be converted into goods (Mata‘). For this reason, the Quran refers to the gift as "Mata‘".

This subject has a specific psychological effect, for it often happens that a gift of usable items such as food, clothing, and the like—even if low in price—leaves an impression on the soul that would never be achieved if it were converted into money. Therefore, in the narrations that have reached us in this regard, we see that the Immaculate Imams (peace be upon them) often mentioned examples of gifts such as clothes, food items, or agricultural land.

Furthermore, it is clearly understood from the verse that in a permanent marriage, it is not necessary to settle the dowry beforehand, and the parties can agree upon it after the contract. [^1] It is also understood that if divorce occurs before the settlement of the dowry and sexual intercourse, the dowry will not be obligatory, and the aforementioned "gift" replaces the "dowry".

It should be noted that time and place are effective in determining the amount of the "suitable gift."

In the next verse, the discussion turns to women for whom a dowry has been settled, but who are separated before intercourse and the wedding ceremony. It says: "And if you divorce them before you have touched them (and intercourse has occurred) while you have already settled a dowry for them, then half of what you settled is due to them"

[^1]: Of course, if they do not settle a dowry in a permanent marriage, the dowry is not waived; rather, "Mahr al-Mithl" (a dowry equivalent to women of her status) becomes applicable, unless she is divorced before sexual intercourse, in which case only the gift mentioned above becomes obligatory.

(وَ إِنْ طَلَّقْتُمُوهُنَّ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَمَسُّوهُنَّ وَ قَدْ فَرَضْتُمْ لَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً فَنِصْفُ مَا فَرَضْتُمْ).

This is the legal ruling of the matter, which gives the woman the right to receive half of the entire dowry without any reduction, even though no intercourse has taken place.

However, it then moves toward moral and emotional aspects, saying: "Unless they forgo their right" (or if they are minors or incompetent, their guardian—that is) "the one in whose hand is the marriage tie—forgoes it" (إِلَّا أَنْ يَعْفُونَ أَوْ يَعْفُوَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ عُقْدَةُ النِّكَاحِ).

It is clear that the guardian can only waive the right of a minor if the minor's best interest necessitates it.

Therefore, the ruling to pay half the dowry is independent of the issue of forgiveness and forgoing.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that the meaning of "الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ عُقْدَةُ النِّكَاحِ" (the one in whose hand is the marriage tie) is the guardian of the minor or the incompetent person, for he is the one who has the right to permit the marriage. However, some commentators have imagined that it refers to the husband; meaning, if the husband had already paid the full dowry (as was common among many Arabs), he has the right to take back half of it unless he forgives and waives it.

But a careful look at the verse shows that the first interpretation is correct, because the discourse in the verse is addressed to husbands; for this reason, it addresses them directly saying:

"And if you divorce them" (وَ إِنْ طَلَّقْتُمُوهُنَّ), while the phrase "أَوْ يَعْفُوَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ عُقْدَةُ النِّكَاحِ" is mentioned in the third person (absent) form, and it is not appropriate for it to refer to the husbands.

Yes, in the following sentence it says: "And that you forgo (and pay the full dowry) is nearer to righteousness (Taqwa). And do not forget graciousness and favor among yourselves, for

Allah is Seer of what you do" (وَ أَنْ تَعْفُوا أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوَى وَ لَا تَنْسَوُا الْفَضْلَ بَيْنَكُمْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ).

Undoubtedly, the addressees in this sentence are the husbands, and consequently, the previous sentence speaks of the forgoing by guardians, while this sentence speaks of the forgoing by husbands.

And the phrase "وَ لَا تَنْسَوُا الْفَضْلَ بَيْنَكُمْ" (And do not forget graciousness among yourselves) is an address to all Muslims not to forget the spirit of forgiveness and nobility in all such cases.

The narrations that have reached us from the Ma'sum Imams (peace be upon them) also interpret the verse in this manner, and Shi'a commentators, considering the content of the verse and the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), have also chosen this view, stating that this expression refers to the guardians of the wife. Of course, cases arise where being stubborn about taking half the dowry—especially before the wedding—might wound the feelings of the husband and his relatives, leading them to seek revenge, and it might put the woman's dignity and reputation at risk. It is here that sometimes the father, to protect his daughter's interests, finds it necessary to waive her right.

The phrase "وَ أَنْ تَعْفُوا أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوَى" (And that you forgo is nearer to righteousness) describes the duty of men toward their divorced wives: if they have paid the full dowry, they should not take anything back, and if they have not paid it, they should pay it all and waive the half that is their right. For it is certain that a girl or woman who is separated from her husband after the contract or before the wedding suffers a severe blow and faces social and psychological problems. Undoubtedly, the husband's forgoing and paying the full dowry acts, to some extent, as a balm for these wounds.

The tone of the entire verse emphasizes the fundamental principles of "Ma'ruf" (fairness) and "Ihsan" (excellence) in these matters, so that even divorce and separation are not mixed with conflict, strife, and the provocation of the spirit of

revenge, but rather are based on nobility, excellence, forgiveness, and forgoing. For if a man and woman cannot live together and separate for certain reasons, there is no reason for enmity and hostility to prevail between them.

Ayahs

حَـٰفِظُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلصَّلَوَٰتِ وَٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ٱلْوُسْطَىٰ وَقُومُوا۟ لِلَّهِ قَـٰنِتِينَ (٢٣٨)
Be watchful of your prayers, and [especially] the middle prayer, and stand in obedience to Allah; (238)
فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ فَرِجَالًا أَوْ رُكْبَانًا ۖ فَإِذَآ أَمِنتُمْ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَمَا عَلَّمَكُم مَّا لَمْ تَكُونُوا۟ تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٣٩)
and should you fear [a danger], then [pray] on foot or mounted, and when you are safe remember Allah, as He taught you what you did not know. (239)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of hypocrites had used the intense heat as an excuse to create division within the ranks of the Muslims and did not participate in the congregational prayer. Following them, some of the believers also refrained from attending the congregation, causing the number of participants to decrease. The Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was distressed by this, to the extent that he threatened them with severe punishment. Thus, it is narrated in a hadith from Zayd ibn Thabit that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله used to perform the (Zuhr) prayer in congregation during the extraordinary heat of the summer midday, and this prayer was the most difficult for the companions and followers. At times, there were no more than one or two rows behind the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. At this point, he said: "I have resolved to burn down the houses of those who do not participate in our prayer." Then the above verse was revealed, emphasizing the importance of the Zuhr prayer (in congregation) [1]. This intensification shows that the issue of non-participation was not merely due to the heat, but rather a group wanted to use this excuse to weaken Islam and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and create a rift in the ranks of the Muslims, against whom the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله took a stand with such a stern tone.

Commentary

The Importance of Prayer, Especially the "Middle" Prayer

Since prayer is the most effective connection between man and God, and if performed under the correct conditions, it overflows the heart with the love and affection of Allah, and in its light, man can better purify himself from the pollution of sin, great emphasis has been placed upon it in the verses of the Quran. Among these, in the first verse above, He says: "Maintain with care the [obligatory] prayers and [in particular] the middle prayer" (حافِظُوا عَلَى الصَّلَواتِ وَ الصَّلاةِ الْوُسْطى‌).

"And stand before Allah, devoutly obedient" (وَ قُومُوا لِلَّهِ قانِتِينَ‌), with humility, submissiveness, and complete attention.

Lest heat, cold, worldly preoccupations, or attending to wealth, spouse, and children distract you from this important matter.

Regarding what is meant by "al-Salat al-Wusta" (the middle prayer), commentators have mentioned many interpretations. In Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, six views are cited; in Tafsir Fakhr Razi, seven views; in Tafsir Qurtubi, ten views; and in Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, thirteen views are narrated.

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 342. The same content is mentioned in Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur under the mentioned verse (as cited in Al-Mizan).

Some have considered it the Zuhr prayer, some the Asr prayer, some the Maghrib prayer, some the Isha prayer, some the Fajr prayer, some the Friday prayer, and some the night prayer or specifically the Witr prayer. A justification has been mentioned for each of these views. However, with the various clues at hand, it is clear that the intended meaning is the Zuhr prayer. This is because, in addition to the fact that the Zuhr prayer is performed in the middle of the day, the occasion of revelation also bears witness to this, and numerous narrations cited from the Infallibles عليهم السلام emphasize it [1]. The emphasis on this prayer was because, due to the heat of the summer midday or the intense preoccupations of business and trade, people gave it less importance. The above verse emphasizes the importance of the middle prayer and the necessity of guarding it.

"Qanitin" is derived from the root "Qunut," which has two meanings: 1. Following and obeying, 2. Humility, submissiveness, and modesty. It is not unlikely that in the above verse, it carries both meanings, as we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the phrase "وَ قُومُوا لِلَّهِ قانِتِينَ‌": "It means that you should perform the prayer with humility and attention toward Allah."

And in another hadith, he says: "It means stand up out of obedience" [2].

In the next verse, it emphasizes that in the most difficult conditions, even on the battlefield, prayer must not be forgotten. However, in such a situation, many conditions of prayer, such as facing the Qibla and performing Ruku' and Sujud in the conventional manner, are waived. Therefore, He says: "And if you fear (due to war or another danger), then [pray] on foot or riding" and perform the Ruku' and Sujud by gesturing (فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ فَرِجالًا أَوْ رُكْباناً).

"But when you are secure, then remember Allah [in prayer] as He has taught you that which you did not [previously] know," and perform the prayer in this state in the normal way with all its etiquettes and conditions (فَإِذا أَمِنْتُمْ فَاذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ كَما عَلَّمَكُمْ ما لَمْ تَكُونُوا تَعْلَمُونَ‌).

It is clear that the gratitude for this divine teaching, which taught humans the manner of praying in states of security and fear, is to act according to it.

"Rijal" here is the plural of "Rajil," meaning pedestrian (on foot), and "Rukban" is the plural of "Rakib," meaning rider. The meaning is that during fear of an enemy attack, you can perform the prayer while you are riding or walking, in a state of movement and activity.

In a hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, it is narrated that in some battles, he ordered the prayer to be performed during combat with Tasbih, Takbir, and "La ilaha illa Allah" [1]. Also, in another hadith, we read: ان النبى صلى الله عليه وآله صلى يوم الاحزاب ايماء "The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله prayed by gesturing on the Day of Ahzab" [2]. Furthermore, it is narrated from Imam al-Kadhim عليه السلام that in response to the question: if a person is trapped by a predatory animal and the time for prayer arrives, and out of fear of that predator he cannot move, how should he pray? He replied: "He should pray in whatever state he is, even if his back is toward the Qibla, and perform Ruku' and Sujud by gesturing while standing" [3].

[1]: The famous view among Shi'a jurists is that it refers to the Zuhr prayer; rather, consensus (Ijma') has been claimed upon it, and several authentic narrations cited in the book Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 3, p. 14, Chapter 5, testify to this meaning. A rare and weak view says it refers to the Asr prayer (Sunni jurists mostly favor the Asr prayer) and they have argued using narrations that do not have significant chains of transmission and which the companions disregarded (for more detail, study the books of jurisprudence). [2]: Refer to Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and Al-Mizan under the verse in question. [1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 239, Hadith 949. [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse in question. [3]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 5, p. 483 (Chapter 3, Hadith 3, summarized and paraphrased). Other hadiths on this subject have also been recorded with this meaning.

This prayer is the "Prayer of Fear" (Salat al-Khawf), which jurists have discussed in detail in books of jurisprudence. Therefore, the above verse emphasizes that guarding the prayers is not only for times of security; rather, prayer must be performed in all states so that the bond between the servants and the Creator of the world remains established. Certainly, through this, a point of reliance and hope is created for man, which will make him victorious in overcoming difficulties.

Key Point: The Role of Prayer in Strengthening Morale

Some might imagine that such insistence and emphasis regarding prayer is considered a kind of hardship and might distract man from the grave duties he has for self-defense in such moments.

However, this is a great mistake. Usually, in these states, man needs the strengthening of morale more than anything else. If fear, terror, and weakness of spirit overcome him, his defeat will be almost certain. What action better than prayer and connection with the God whose command is effective throughout the entire universe and for whom everything is easy and simple before His will, can strengthen the morale of mujahid soldiers or those facing danger! Beyond this, many evidences are seen in the struggles of the early Muslims. In the reports concerning the fourth war of the Muslims with the Zionists, which took place in Ramadan 1393 AH, we read that the attention of the soldiers of Islam to prayer and the foundations of Islam had an extraordinary effect on their spiritual strengthening and victory over the enemy.

In any case, the importance and impact of prayer are more than can be contained in this brief summary. Undoubtedly, if prayer is performed with all its etiquettes, especially with the presence of heart which is its soul, it has an extraordinarily positive impact on the individual and society. It can solve many problems, deliver society from many vices, and be a helper and supporter for man in difficult and complex events [1].

[1]: For more explanation regarding the "Benefits of Prayer," refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 16, p. 289 (under verse 45 of Surah al-Ankabut).

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ يُتَوَفَّوْنَ مِنكُمْ وَيَذَرُونَ أَزْوَٰجًا وَصِيَّةً لِّأَزْوَٰجِهِم مَّتَـٰعًا إِلَى ٱلْحَوْلِ غَيْرَ إِخْرَاجٍ ۚ فَإِنْ خَرَجْنَ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِى مَا فَعَلْنَ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِنَّ مِن مَّعْرُوفٍ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٢٤٠)
Those of you who die leaving wives shall bequeath for their wives providing for a year, without turning them out; but if they leave, there is no sin upon you in respect of what they may do with themselves in accordance with honourable norms. And Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (240)
وَلِلْمُطَلَّقَـٰتِ مَتَـٰعٌۢ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۖ حَقًّا عَلَى ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٢٤١)
For the divorced women there shall be a provision, in accordance with honourable norms—an obligation on the Godwary. (241)
كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٢٤٢)
Thus does Allah clarify His signs to you so that you may exercise your reason. (242)

Commentary

Another Part of the Rulings on Divorce

In these verses, the discourse returns once again to the subject of marriage, divorce, and related matters. First, it speaks regarding husbands who are on the threshold of death and leave behind wives, stating: "And those of you who die—meaning those who are on the verge of death—and leave wives behind, should make a will for their wives for a year’s maintenance without turning them out" from the house; they should remain in the husband's house and their living expenses should be paid (وَ الَّذِينَ يُتَوَفَّوْنَ مِنْكُمْ وَ يَذَرُونَ أَزْواجاً وَصِيَّةً لِأَزْواجِهِمْ مَتاعاً إِلَى الْحَوْلِ غَيْرَ إِخْراجٍ‌).

Of course, this applies if they do not leave the husband's house of their own accord, "but if they leave (they have no right to expenses or housing, yet) there is no blame upon you for what they do with themselves in a lawful manner (such as choosing a new husband after the completion of the iddah)" (فَإِنْ خَرَجْنَ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي ما فَعَلْنَ فِي أَنْفُسِهِنَّ مِنْ مَعْرُوفٍ‌).

At the end of the verse, as if to comfort such women so they do not worry about their future, it says: Allah is capable of opening another path for them after the loss of their former husband, and if a calamity has befallen them, there was certainly a wisdom in it, for "Allah is Almighty, All-Wise" (وَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ‌).

If He closes one door out of His wisdom, He will open another by His grace, and there is no cause for concern.

Based on what has been stated above, it becomes clear that the word "yutawaffawn" (die) here does not mean the state of having already died, but rather, given the context of mentioning a "will," it means being on the threshold of death.

The phrase "but if they leave, there is no blame upon you for what they do with themselves in a lawful manner," according to the above commentary, is evidence that providing living expenses for up to one year is a right of the wife upon the husband's heirs. Whenever the woman chooses not to stay in the husband's house and not to utilize the maintenance, no one bears responsibility toward her, and likewise, if she wishes to take steps toward a new marriage, there is no obstacle.

However, some have mentioned another interpretation for this phrase: that if she waits for the duration of one year and after that leaves the husband's house and marries, there is no objection.

According to the second interpretation, observing the iddah (waiting period) for a duration of one year is mandatory for the woman, whereas according to the first interpretation, it is not. In other words, continuing the iddah for up to one year is a "right" according to the first interpretation and a "ruling" (obligation) according to the second. However, the literal appearance of the verse is more consistent with the first interpretation, as the final clause appears to be an exception to the preceding rule.

Key Point: Is This Verse Abrogated?

Many commentators believe that this verse has been abrogated by verse 234 of this same Surah, which appeared previously and established the iddah for death as four months and ten days. The fact that that verse precedes this one in the Quranic order and arrangement is not evidence that it was revealed earlier, for we know that the arrangement of verses in a Surah is not according to the chronological order of revelation; rather, sometimes verses revealed later were placed at the beginning of a Surah, and verses revealed earlier were placed toward the end, and this was done due to the thematic relevance of the verses and by the command of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

They have also said that the right to one year's maintenance existed before the revelation of the verses of inheritance, and after inheritance was established for the wife, this right was abolished. Therefore, the above verse is abrogated from two aspects: the duration of the iddah and the matter of maintenance.

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan states: All scholars agree that this verse is abrogated. He then narrates a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that (in the Age of Ignorance) when a man passed away, they would provide the wife's maintenance from the husband's property for one year, after which she would leave without any inheritance. Later, the verse of the "one-fourth and one-eighth" (regarding the wife's inheritance) abrogated this verse.

Therefore, the wife's maintenance during the iddah period must come from her inheritance. He also narrates from the Imam عليه السلام that he said: The verse regarding keeping the iddah for four months and ten days, as well as the verse of inheritance, abrogated this verse. [^1] In any case, it is understood from the words of the great scholars that the iddah for death in the Age of Ignorance was one year, and they held superstitious and arduous customs for the woman during this period. Islam initially abolished those superstitious customs but maintained the iddah of death at one year, then later changed it to four months and ten days, only prohibiting the woman from wearing various ornaments and beautification during this period.

From the statements of Fakhr al-Razi, it is understood that the common view among Sunni commentators is also that the above verse was abrogated by the verses of inheritance and the iddah of four months and ten days. [^2] However, if it were not for the consensus of the scholars and the numerous narrations in this regard, it could be said that there is no contradiction between these verses: the iddah of four months and ten days is a Divine decree, but keeping the iddah for up to one year and staying in the husband's house while utilizing his maintenance is a "right." That is, the woman is given the right that if she wishes, she may stay in her deceased husband's house for up to one year, and her living expenses be paid throughout this period according to the husband's will; and if she does not wish to, she may leave the husband's house after four months and ten days or proceed to marry, and at that point, her maintenance from the former husband's property would naturally cease.

However, given the numerous narrations transmitted through the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام and the fame of the ruling of abrogation or the consensus of scholars upon it, accepting such an interpretation is not possible, even if it might align with the literal appearance of the verses.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 345, under the verse in question. [^2]: Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 6, p. 158.

In the following verse, another ruling on divorce is addressed, stating: "And for divorced women is a reasonable provision—a duty upon the righteous" which is paid by the husband (وَ لِلْمُطَلَّقاتِ مَتاعٌ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ حَقًّا عَلَى الْمُتَّقِينَ‌).

Although the literal appearance of the verse includes all divorced women, by the context of verse 236 which preceded it, this ruling applies to women for whom no dower (mahr) was specified at the time of the marriage contract and who are divorced before consummation. In reality, it is an emphasis on the aforementioned ruling so that it is not neglected.

The possibility also exists that the said ruling includes all divorced women, except that in the case mentioned above it is obligatory, and in other cases it is recommended. In any case, it is one of the profoundly humane instructions introduced in Islam, which has a positive effect in preventing the vengefulness and animosity resulting from divorce.

Some have also said that paying a reasonable gift is obligatory for all divorced women and is a matter separate from the dower. However, it seems that among the Shi'a scholars—as understood from the words of the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan—no one holds this view (the late author of Jawahir al-Kalam also explicitly states that the said gift is not obligatory except in that specific case, and this matter is a point of consensus). [^1] The possibility has also been raised that it refers to maintenance (nafaqah), which is a very weak possibility.

In any case, according to narrations narrated from the Infallible Imams عليه السلام, this gift is paid after the end of the iddah and complete separation, not during the iddah of a revocable divorce. In other words, it is a "parting gift," not a means for returning. [^2]

[^1]: Jawahir al-Kalam, Vol. 31, p. 58. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 240, Hadith 956 and 957.

In the final verse under discussion, which is the last verse related to the issue of divorce, He says: "Thus Allah makes clear His signs to you that you may reflect" (كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ آياتِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

It is obvious that the purpose of reflection and intellect (ta'aqqul) is for it to be the starting point for action; otherwise, mere thought regarding the rulings will yield no result.

From studying Islamic verses and narrations, it is gathered that "intellect" (aql) is often used in cases where "perception" and understanding are blended with "emotions and feelings" and followed by action. For example, if the Quran, in many discussions of theology, describes examples of the wondrous system of this world and then says We explain these signs "that you may reflect" (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ‌), the intention is not merely to store information about the system of nature in one's brain. For natural sciences, if they do not affect the center of the heart and emotions and have no impact on creating love, friendship, and acquaintance with the Creator of the world, they will have no connection to matters of monotheism and theology.

The same applies to information that has a practical aspect; it is called "intellect" only when it is accompanied by "action." In Tafsir al-Mizan [^1], we read that ta'aqqul is used in a context where, following perception and understanding, a person enters the stage of action. Verses such as: (وَ قالُوا لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ) "And they will say, 'If only we had listened or used our intellect, we would not be among the companions of the Blaze'" [^2] or the verse (أَ فَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَتَكُونَ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ يَعْقِلُونَ بِها) "Have they not traveled through the land and have hearts by which to understand?" [^3] are evidence of this statement.

For if the criminals on the Day of Resurrection wish for intellect in the world, they mean an intellect that is blended with action. Or if Allah says people should travel and understand things by observing and studying the conditions of the world, the intention is an understanding followed by changing one's path and stepping onto the straight path.

In other words, if thought and reflection are deep-rooted, it is impossible for their effects not to appear in action. How is it possible for a person to firmly believe a food is poisoned and yet eat it, or to have a definitive belief in the effectiveness of a medicine for treating a dangerous disease and not take it?

[^1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 2, pp. 249-250. [^2]: Surah Al-Mulk, Verse 10. [^3]: Surah Al-Hajj, Verse 46.

Ayah

۞ أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ وَهُمْ أُلُوفٌ حَذَرَ ٱلْمَوْتِ فَقَالَ لَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مُوتُوا۟ ثُمَّ أَحْيَـٰهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٢٤٣)
Have you not regarded those who left their homes in thousands, apprehensive of death, whereupon Allah said to them, ‘Die,’ then He revived them? Indeed Allah is gracious to mankind, but most people do not give thanks. (243)

Circumstances of Revelation

In one of the cities of "Syria" (Sham), the plague broke out, and with a strange and staggering speed, people were dying one after another. In the meantime, a large number of people left that environment and land in the hope that they might escape the clutches of death. Since they felt a sense of power and independence after fleeing their environment and escaping death—ignoring the Divine Will and fixing their eyes only on natural causes—they became arrogant. Consequently, the Lord caused them to perish by that same disease in that very desert.

It is understood from some narrations that the arrival of the aforementioned disease in that land was originally a punishment. This was because their leader and guide asked them to prepare themselves for struggle and to leave the city, but they refused to go to the battlefield under the pretext that the plague existed in the war environment. The Lord afflicted them with the very thing they feared and used as an excuse for fleeing; the plague spread among them. They emptied their houses and fled to escape the plague, but they all perished in the desert. A long time passed after this event, and Ezekiel [^1], who was one of the prophets of the Children of Israel, passed by that place and asked God to bring them back to life. Allah answered his prayer, and they returned to life.

Commentary

How did they die and how were they revived?

This verse, as mentioned in the circumstances of revelation, is a concise yet instructive reference to the extraordinary story of a former people among whom a terrifying contagious disease appeared. Thousands of people fled from that region. It says: "Have you not considered those who left their homes in many thousands, fearing death?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ وَ هُمْ أُلُوفٌ حَذَرَ الْمَوْتِ).

It is certain that the phrase "Have you not seen" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ) here means "Do you not know," because in Arabic literature, whenever they want to fully visualize a matter and present it as an obvious fact, they address the listener with the phrase "Have you not seen." Although the addressee in this sentence is the Prophet, in reality, it refers to all individuals.

Although the above verse does not mention a specific number and only uses the word "aluf" (أُلُوفٌ), which means thousands, some narrations mention their number as ten thousand, and some as seventy or eighty thousand [^2]. Then, referring to their ultimate fate, it says: "Allah said to them: 'Die!'" and they died by the same disease they had used as an excuse (فَقالَ لَهُمُ اللَّهُ مُوتُوا).

[^1]: According to some narrations, "Ezekiel" was the third leader of the Children of Israel after Moses عليه السلام. [^2]: Refer to the commentaries of Majma' al-Bayan, Qurtubi, and Ruh al-Bayan under the verse in question.

"Then He restored them to life" so that the story of their lives would be a lesson for others (ثُمَّ أَحْياهُمْ).

It is clear that the meaning of "Die" (مُوتُوا) is not a verbal command, but rather the creative command (amr takwini) of Allah that governs the entire existence and the world of life. That is, Allah provided the causes of their death, and they all perished quickly. This command is similar to the command mentioned in verse 82 of Surah Ya-Sin: "His command is only, when He intends a thing, that He says to it, 'Be,' and it is!" (إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ).

The phrase "Then He restored them to life" (ثُمَّ أَحْياهُمْ) refers to the revival of that population after death, which, as we read in the circumstances of revelation, took place through the prayer of the Prophet Ezekiel. Since their return to life was one of the clear Divine blessings (both for themselves and as a lesson for the people), the end of the verse states: "Indeed, Allah is full of bounty to the people, but most of the people do not show gratitude" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَشْكُرُونَ).

Not only this group, but all human beings are subject to His favors, graces, and blessings.

Key Points

1- Was this event a historical incident or an allegory?

Is what is mentioned in the above story a real historical event that the Quran refers to concisely and is detailed in narrations, or is it a type of parable used to visualize intellectual truths in sensory forms? Since the aforementioned story has extraordinary aspects and is difficult for some commentators to digest, they have denied the occurrence of such an event and considered the verse to be merely a "parable." They argue it describes the state of a population that falters in the struggle against the enemy and consequently suffers defeat, but then learns a lesson, wakes up, restarts the movement and struggle, and finally becomes victorious.

According to this interpretation, the word "Die" (مُوتُوا) mentioned in the verse is a metaphor for defeat following lethargy and stagnation, and the phrase "He restored them to life" (أَحْياهُمْ) refers to awareness, awakening, and the subsequent victory.

According to this interpretation, the narrations that have been transmitted in this regard, describing it as a historical event, are considered fabricated or "Isra'iliyyat" (Judeo-Christian interpolations). However, it must be said that although deriving the theme of "defeat" and "victory" following "lethargy" and "awakening" from the verse is an interesting topic, it cannot be denied that the literal meaning of the verse is the narration of a historical event and not just a parable. The verse recounts the state of a group of predecessors who died following their flight from a terrifying event and then Allah revived them. If the extraordinary nature of an event were a reason to justify and reinterpret it metaphorically, then one would have to do the same with all the miracles of the prophets.

In short, if such justifications and interpretations are introduced into the Quran, one could, in addition to denying the miracles of the prophets, deny most of the historical discussions of the Quran and consider them as "allegories" or, in modern terms, "symbolic." For example, one could consider the story of "Cain and Abel" as a parable for the struggle of justice and truth-seeking against cruelty and hard-heartedness. In that case, all the historical discussions of the Quran would lose their value.

Furthermore, with such an interpretation, one cannot ignore all the narrations that have been transmitted regarding the commentary of the verse, because some of them are narrated in authentic texts, and attributing fabrication or being "Isra'iliyyat" to them is very improper.

2- A Lesson to be Learned

As mentioned in the circumstances of revelation, the above verse refers to a group of the Children of Israel who resorted to making excuses to escape the responsibility of Jihad. Allah afflicted them with the plague, which wiped them all out quickly and lightning-fast, in a way that no dangerous enemy on the battlefield could. This means: do not imagine that by fleeing from responsibility and resorting to various excuses you can remain safe; do not imagine that you can resist the power of the Lord. God can, with a very small enemy like the microbe of plague or cholera—which cannot even be seen with the eye—mow you down so that no trace of you remains.

3- The Issue of "Raj'ah" (Return) and Returning to the World

Another point worthy of attention here is the possibility of "Raj'ah" (the Return), which is clearly derived from this verse.

To explain: in the history of the past, we find cases where individuals returned to this world after death, such as the story of a group of the Children of Israel who went to Mount Tur with Moses عليه السلام, mentioned in verses 55 and 56 of Surah Al-Baqarah; the story of Ezra (Uzair) or Jeremiah mentioned in verse 259 of this same Surah; and also the incident referred to in the verse under discussion.

Therefore, there is no obstacle to this same issue being repeated in the future.

The famous Shiite scholar, the late "Saduq," also argued using this verse for the possibility of the issue of Raj'ah, saying: "One of our beliefs is the belief in Raj'ah (that a group of people from the past will return to life once again in this very world)."

This verse can also serve as evidence for the issue of the Resurrection (Ma'ad) and the revival of the dead on the Day of Judgment.

Ayahs

وَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٤٤)
Fight in the way of Allah, and know that Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (244)
مَّن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يُقْرِضُ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا فَيُضَـٰعِفَهُۥ لَهُۥٓ أَضْعَافًا كَثِيرَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَقْبِضُ وَيَبْصُۜطُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٢٤٥)
Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan that He may multiply it for him severalfold? Allah tightens and expands [the means of life], and to Him you shall be brought back. (245)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation for the second verse, it has been narrated that: One day, the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Whoever gives a charity shall have twice its likeness in Paradise." Abu al-Dahdah al-Ansari said, "O Messenger of Allah, I have two gardens; if I give one of them as charity, will I have twice its likeness in Paradise?" He replied, "Yes." He asked, "Will Umm al-Dahdah be with me as well?" He replied, "Yes." He asked, "Will my children be with me too?" He replied, "Yes." Then, he gave the better of his two gardens as charity to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Consequently, the above verse was revealed, and it multiplied his charity for him two million times, and this is the meaning of أَضْعافاً كَثِيرَةً (many multiples).

Abu al-Dahdah returned and saw his wife, Umm al-Dahdah, and the children in the garden he had given as charity. He stood at the gate of the garden, not wishing to enter, and called out to his wife, saying: "I have given this garden as charity and have purchased twice its likeness in Paradise, and you and the children will be with me." His wife replied, "Blessed is what you have sold and what you have bought!" Then they all exited the garden and surrendered it to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "How many date palms in Paradise have their branches hanging down for Abu al-Dahdah!" [^1]

Commentary

Jihad with Life and Wealth

From here, the verses related to Jihad begin, followed by a story on the same subject regarding previous nations. Considering the account mentioned in the previous verse about a group of the Children of Israel who fled from Jihad under the pretext of a plague and eventually perished by that very plague, the connection between these verses and the preceding ones becomes clear.

First, He says: "Fight in the way of Allah, and know that Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing." He hears your words and is aware of your inner motives and intentions regarding the matter of Jihad (وَ قاتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ).

Then He adds: "Who is it that will lend to Allah a goodly loan (and spend from the wealth He has bestowed in the way of Jihad and in the way of supporting the needy) so that He may multiply it for him many times over?" (مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يُقْرِضُ اللَّهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً فَيُضاعِفَهُ لَهُ أَضْعافاً كَثِيرَةً).

Therefore, lending to Allah means "spending in the way of Allah" (Infaq fi sabilillah). According to some commentators, it refers specifically to spending in the way of Jihad, because at that time, providing for the various costs of Jihad rested upon the shoulders of the Muslim fighters. Meanwhile, others believe it encompasses every kind of spending, even though it follows the verse of Jihad [^1]. However, the second interpretation is more consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse, especially since it also includes the first meaning. Fundamentally, spending in the way of Allah, helping the needy, and supporting the deprived serve the same purpose as Jihad, as both are sources of independence and honor for the Islamic society.

"Ad'af" is the plural of "di'f" (rhyming with shi'r), meaning to double or multiply something. Given that this word appears in the plural form in the verse and is emphasized by the word "kathirah" (many), and additionally, the verb "yuda'ifu" conveys more emphasis than "yuda'ifu" (as linguists have stated) [^2], it is understood from the sum of these points that Allah has ordained a very abundant reward for those who spend. Spending in the way of Allah is like a potent seed sown in prepared soil and irrigated by successive rains, yielding tens and sometimes hundreds of times the result (as will be mentioned in verse 261 of this same Surah).

At the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah withholds and extends (the provision of His servants), and to Him you shall be returned" (وَ اللَّهُ يَقْبِضُ وَ يَبْصُطُ وَ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ).

This is an indication that you should not imagine that spending and giving will decrease your wealth, for the expansion and restriction of your provision is in the hand of Allah. He has the power to place at your disposal far more than what was spent in exchange for it. Rather, considering the connection and interconnectedness of the individuals in a society, that very spent wealth actually returns to you.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 349. [^1]: Refer to Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 6, p. 166. [^2]: Raghib in al-Mufradat under the root d-'-f says: Some have said: da'aftu is more eloquent/emphatic than da'aftuhu.

This is regarding this world; as for the Hereafter, do not forget that you shall all return to Him, and your great rewards are there.

Key Point: Why the Expression "Loan"?

In several verses of the Holy Quran (including the verse above), spending in the way of Allah is expressed as a "loan" and "lending to the Lord." This demonstrates, on one hand, the ultimate grace of Allah toward His servants and, on the other hand, the extreme importance of the issue of spending. This is despite the fact that He is the true Owner of the entire universe, and humans only act as representatives of Allah in managing a small portion of it, as we read in Surah al-Hadid, verse 7: "آمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ وَ أَنْفِقُوا مِمَّا جَعَلَكُمْ مُسْتَخْلَفِينَ فِيهِ" (Believe in Allah and His Messenger and spend out of that in which He has made you successors). Yet, despite this, He turns and requests a loan from His servant—and a loan with such a vast profit at that! "Behold the generosity and grace of the Lord!"

It is mentioned in Nahj al-Balagha that Imam Ali عليه السلام says: "He has asked you for a loan while the treasures of the heavens and the earth belong to Him, and He is the Self-Sufficient, the Praiseworthy. He only intended to test you as to which of you is best in conduct." [^1]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, final part of Sermon 183.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلْمَلَإِ مِنۢ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مُوسَىٰٓ إِذْ قَالُوا۟ لِنَبِىٍّ لَّهُمُ ٱبْعَثْ لَنَا مَلِكًا نُّقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ هَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِن كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْقِتَالُ أَلَّا تُقَـٰتِلُوا۟ ۖ قَالُوا۟ وَمَا لَنَآ أَلَّا نُقَـٰتِلَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَقَدْ أُخْرِجْنَا مِن دِيَـٰرِنَا وَأَبْنَآئِنَا ۖ فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقِتَالُ تَوَلَّوْا۟ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنْهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢٤٦)
Have you not regarded the elite of the Israelites after Moses, when they said to their prophet, ‘Appoint for us a king that we may fight in the way of Allah.’ He said, ‘May it not be that you will not fight if fighting were prescribed for you?’ They said, ‘Why should we not fight in the way of Allah, when we have been expelled from our homes and [separated from] our children?’ So when fighting was prescribed for them, they turned back except a few of them, and Allah knows well the wrongdoers. (246)
وَقَالَ لَهُمْ نَبِيُّهُمْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ بَعَثَ لَكُمْ طَالُوتَ مَلِكًا ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ عَلَيْنَا وَنَحْنُ أَحَقُّ بِٱلْمُلْكِ مِنْهُ وَلَمْ يُؤْتَ سَعَةً مِّنَ ٱلْمَالِ ۚ قَالَ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰهُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَزَادَهُۥ بَسْطَةً فِى ٱلْعِلْمِ وَٱلْجِسْمِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يُؤْتِى مُلْكَهُۥ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٤٧)
Their prophet said to them, ‘Allah has appointed Saul as king for you.’ They said, ‘How can he have kingship over us, when we have a greater right to kingship than him, as he has not been given ample wealth?’ He said, ‘Indeed Allah has chosen him over you, and enhanced him vastly in knowledge and physique, and Allah gives His kingdom to whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing.’ (247)
وَقَالَ لَهُمْ نَبِيُّهُمْ إِنَّ ءَايَةَ مُلْكِهِۦٓ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمُ ٱلتَّابُوتُ فِيهِ سَكِينَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَبَقِيَّةٌ مِّمَّا تَرَكَ ءَالُ مُوسَىٰ وَءَالُ هَـٰرُونَ تَحْمِلُهُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٢٤٨)
Their prophet said to them, ‘Indeed the sign of his kingship shall be that the Ark will come to you, bearing tranquillity from your Lord and the relics left behind by the House of Moses and the House of Aaron, borne by the angels. There is indeed a sign in that for you, should you be faithful.’ (248)
فَلَمَّا فَصَلَ طَالُوتُ بِٱلْجُنُودِ قَالَ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُبْتَلِيكُم بِنَهَرٍ فَمَن شَرِبَ مِنْهُ فَلَيْسَ مِنِّى وَمَن لَّمْ يَطْعَمْهُ فَإِنَّهُۥ مِنِّىٓ إِلَّا مَنِ ٱغْتَرَفَ غُرْفَةًۢ بِيَدِهِۦ ۚ فَشَرِبُوا۟ مِنْهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنْهُمْ ۚ فَلَمَّا جَاوَزَهُۥ هُوَ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ قَالُوا۟ لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا ٱلْيَوْمَ بِجَالُوتَ وَجُنُودِهِۦ ۚ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُم مُّلَـٰقُوا۟ ٱللَّهِ كَم مِّن فِئَةٍ قَلِيلَةٍ غَلَبَتْ فِئَةً كَثِيرَةًۢ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (٢٤٩)
As Saul set out with the troops, he said, ‘Allah will test you with a stream: anyone who drinks from it will not belong to me, but those who do not drink from it will belong to me, barring someone who draws a scoop with his hand.’ But they drank from it, [all] except a few of them. So when he crossed it along with the faithful who were with him, they said, ‘We have no strength today against Goliath and his troops.’ Those who were certain that they would encounter Allah said, ‘How many a small party has overcome a larger party by Allah’s will! And Allah is with the patient.’ (249)
وَلَمَّا بَرَزُوا۟ لِجَالُوتَ وَجُنُودِهِۦ قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنَا صَبْرًا وَثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا وَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢٥٠)
So when they marched out for [encounter with] Goliath and his troops, they said, ‘Our Lord, pour patience upon us, make our feet steady, and assist us against the faithless lot.’ (250)
فَهَزَمُوهُم بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ وَقَتَلَ دَاوُۥدُ جَالُوتَ وَءَاتَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُلْكَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَهُۥ مِمَّا يَشَآءُ ۗ وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلنَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّفَسَدَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٥١)
Thus they routed them with Allah’s will, and David killed Goliath, and Allah gave him kingdom and wisdom and taught him whatever He liked. Were it not for Allah’s repelling the people by means of one another, the earth would surely have been corrupted; but Allah is gracious to the world’s people. (251)
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ نَتْلُوهَا عَلَيْكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ وَإِنَّكَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٢٥٢)
These are the signs of Allah, which We recite for you in truth, and you are indeed one of the apostles. (252)

Commentary

A Lesson-Bearing Passage from the History of the Children of Israel

Before we proceed to the commentary of these verses, it is necessary to point to a segment of the history of the Children of Israel to which these verses refer:

The Jewish people, who had become weak and powerless under the domination of the Pharaohs, were saved from that deplorable state through the wise leadership of Moses عليه السلام and attained power and greatness.

By the blessing of this Prophet, Allah bestowed upon them many favors, including the Ark of the Covenant—the history and contents of which we will soon discuss.

By carrying this Ark at the front of their army, the Jewish people gained a kind of reassurance and spiritual strength. This power and greatness continued for some time after Moses عليه السلام, but these very victories and blessings gradually led to their arrogance, and they succumbed to lawbreaking. Eventually, they were defeated by the Philistines and lost their power and influence along with the Ark of the Covenant. Following this, they became so scattered and divided that they lacked the power to defend themselves against the smallest of enemies, to the point that their enemies drove a large group of them out of their land and even took their children into captivity.

This situation continued for years until Allah raised a Prophet named "Samuel" (Ashmu’il) for their salvation and guidance. They, too, having grown weary of the oppression and tyranny of their enemies and seeking a refuge, gathered around him and asked him to choose a leader and commander for them so that they might all fight the enemy with one heart and one mind under his command and guidance to regain their lost honor.

Samuel, who was well-acquainted with their temperament and lack of resolve, replied: "I fear that if the command for Jihad is issued, you will disobey the order of your commander and leader and shirk from facing and fighting the enemy."

They said: "How could we possibly refuse the command of the leader and withhold from performing our duty when the enemy has driven us out of our homeland, occupied our territories, and taken our children captive!" Samuel, seeing that the people had come to the physician having diagnosed their own ailment and seemingly understood the secret of their backwardness, turned to the threshold of Allah and presented the people's request to His presence. It was revealed to him: "I have chosen 'Saul' (Talut) as king over them."

Samuel said: "O Allah, I have not yet seen Saul and do not know him."

The revelation came: "We shall send him to you; when he comes to you, entrust the command of the army to him and hand over the banner of Jihad to him."

Who is Saul (Talut)?

Saul was a tall, robust, and well-proportioned man with firm and powerful nerves. In terms of spiritual faculties, he was very clever, knowledgeable, and resourceful. Some believe the reason he was named "Talut" was due to his tall stature (tul). Despite all this, he was not famous and lived with his father in a village on the banks of a river, herding his father's livestock and farming.

One day, some of the livestock went missing in the wilderness. Saul, accompanied by one of his friends, went searching for them around the river. This continued for several days until they reached near the city of "Zuph" (Suf).

His friend said: "We have now reached the land of Zuph, the city of the Prophet Samuel. Let us go to him; perhaps in the light of revelation and the brilliance of his judgment, we may find the way to our lost property." When they entered the city, they encountered Samuel. As soon as the eyes of Samuel and Saul met, a bond of familiarity was established between their hearts.

Samuel recognized Saul at that very moment and knew that this young man was the one appointed by Allah to lead the people. When Saul explained his story to Samuel, the Prophet said: "As for the livestock, they are currently on the way to the village toward your father's garden; do not worry about them. But I invite you to a matter far greater than that: Allah has commissioned you to save the Children of Israel." Saul was initially surprised by this proposal and then accepted it with pleasure. Samuel said to his people: "Allah has chosen Saul as your commander; it is necessary for all of you to follow him and prepare yourselves for Jihad against the enemy."

The Children of Israel, who considered certain privileges of lineage and wealth necessary for a commander and head of the army and saw neither in Saul, fell into deep astonishment at this appointment. According to them, he was neither from the house of Levi, who had a history of prophethood, nor from the house of Joseph or Judah, who had a history of governance; rather, he was from the obscure house of "Benjamin" and was financially destitute. Therefore, they said by way of protest: "How can he rule over us when we are more deserving than him?!" Samuel, seeing them in grave error, said: "Allah has appointed him as commander over you, and the merit for command and leadership lies in physical strength and spiritual power, both of which Saul possesses in sufficient measure, and in this regard, he is superior to you." But they demanded a sign that would prove this selection was from Allah.

Samuel said: "The sign of it is that the Ark (the Ark of the Covenant), which is one of the important relics of the Prophets of the Children of Israel and has been a source of encouragement and reassurance for you in wars, will return to you while a group of angels carries it." It was not long before the Ark of the Covenant appeared before them. Upon seeing this sign, they accepted Saul's command.

Saul Takes the Reins of the Country!

Saul took charge of the army and, in a short time, proved his competence and merit in managing the affairs of the state and commanding the troops. He then called them to fight an enemy that had endangered everything they possessed. He emphasized to them: "Only those should march with me whose entire thoughts are on Jihad; those who have a half-finished building or an incomplete transaction and the like should not participate in this battle." Soon, a seemingly large and powerful multitude gathered and moved toward the enemy.

Due to marching under the sun, they all became thirsty. To test and purify them by Allah's command, Saul said: "Soon you will reach a river on your path; Allah will test you by it. Those who drink from it and satisfy their thirst are not of me, and those who do not drink except a small amount are of me!" As soon as their eyes fell upon the river, they were overjoyed and rushed to it, drinking their fill. Only a small number remained faithful to the covenant.

Saul realized that his army consisted of a majority that was weak-willed and unfaithful to their word, and a minority of believers. Therefore, he left behind the undisciplined and disobedient majority and set out from the city with that small group of believers toward the field of Jihad.

Saul's small army, terrified by their few numbers, said to him: "We have no power today against this mighty army." But those who had firm faith in the Resurrection and whose hearts were overflowing with the love of Allah did not fear the size and strength of the enemy's army or their own small numbers. With total courage, they said to Saul: "Command whatever you deem right; we will be with you everywhere and, by Allah's will, we will fight them with this small number. How often a small group has overcome a large group by the will of the Lord, and Allah is with the steadfast."

Saul prepared for battle with that small but faithful and mujahid group. They prayed to Allah for patience and victory. As soon as the fire of war was ignited, "Goliath" (Jalut) emerged from his army and challenged a champion between the two ranks. His terrifying voice made hearts tremble, and no one dared to face him. In the midst of this, a youth named "David" (Dawud)—who perhaps due to his young age had not even come to the field to fight, but was commissioned by his father to help his older brothers who were in the ranks of the warriors—was nevertheless very agile and skilled. With the sling he had in his hand, he threw one or two stones so skillfully that they struck Goliath right in the forehead and head. He fell to the ground and was killed amidst the horror and astonishment of his troops. With the death of Goliath, a strange fear and dread seized his soldiers, and they finally fled from the ranks of Saul's army, and the Children of Israel were victorious. [1]

[1]: Adapted from Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Durr al-Manthur, and Qisas al-Qur'an (summarized).

Now we return to the commentary of the verses

In the first verse, addressing the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He says: "Have you not seen the assembly of the nobles of the Children of Israel after Moses عليه السلام when they said to a Prophet of theirs: 'Appoint for us a king so that (under his command) we may fight in the way of Allah?'" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الْمَلَإِ مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ مِنْ بَعْدِ مُوسى‌ إِذْ قالُوا لِنَبِيٍّ لَهُمُ ابْعَثْ لَنا مَلِكاً نُقاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ‌).

The word "Mala'" (assembly/nobles) linguistically refers to things or persons that fill the eye and arouse the wonder of the observer. For this reason, a large crowd that holds a single opinion or belief is called Mala', and the nobles and elders of any people or nation are also called Mala', because either of these two groups fills the eye of the observer due to their specific quantity or quality.

As previously mentioned, this verse refers to a large group of the Children of Israel who unanimously requested a commander and leader from their Prophet so that they could fight under his command against Goliath, who had endangered their entire religious, social, and economic standing.

It is noteworthy that they wanted to fight to repel the aggression of the enemy who had driven them out of their land, yet they called it "in the way of Allah" (fi sabilillah). From this expression, it becomes clear that whatever contributes to the freedom and salvation of humans from captivity and the removal of oppression is considered "in the way of Allah." Furthermore, the said battle also had a religious and spiritual dimension.

Some have mentioned the name of this Prophet as "Simeon," some as "Samuel," and some as "Joshua," but the most famous among commentators is Samuel, whose Arabic equivalent is Isma'il, as narrated in a tradition from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام. [1] In any case, their Prophet, who was concerned about their condition and did not see them as steadfast in their covenant, said to them: "If fighting is prescribed for you, perhaps you will (disobey and) not fight in the way of Allah" (قالَ هَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْقِتالُ أَلَّا تُقاتِلُوا).

They replied: "How could we not fight in the way of Allah when we have been driven out of our homes and from our children?" Our cities have been occupied by the enemy and our children taken captive (قالُوا وَ ما لَنا أَلَّا نُقاتِلَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَ قَدْ أُخْرِجْنا مِنْ دِيارِنا وَ أَبْنائِنا).

In this way, they declared loyalty to their covenant. Yet, despite all this, neither the name of Allah and His command, nor the preservation of their independence and existence, nor the freedom of their children, could prevent their breach of covenant. Therefore, we read in the continuation of this verse: "But when fighting was prescribed for them, they turned away, except for a few of them, and Allah is aware of the wrongdoers" and He knows them and will punish them (فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَليهمُ الْقِتالُ تَوَلَّوْا إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِنْهُمْ وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِالظَّالِمِينَ‌).

Some commentators have recorded the number of those who remained faithful as 313, the same as the faithful soldiers of Islam in the Battle of Badr. 2

In any case, their Prophet, according to the duty he had, responded to their request and chose Saul by Allah's command to be their ruler and said to them: "Allah has raised Saul for you as a king" (وَ قالَ لَهُمْ نَبِيُّهُمْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ قَدْ بَعَثَ لَكُمْ طالُوتَ مَلِكاً).

According to this verse, the selection of Saul as the ruler and commander of the army of the Children of Israel was from Allah. Perhaps the phrase "has raised" (qad ba'atha) refers to what was mentioned in the description of this story—that unexpected events led Saul to the city of that Prophet عليه السلام and his assembly, and this divine selection took place. Additionally, from the term "king" (malikan), it appears that Saul was not only the commander of the army but also the ruler of the country. [3] From here, the opposition began; a group said: "How can he have kingship over us when we are more deserving of kingship than him, and he has not been given much wealth?" (قالُوا أَنَّى يَكُونُ لَهُ الْمُلْكُ عَلَيْنا وَ نَحْنُ أَحَقُّ بِالْمُلْكِ مِنْهُ وَ لَمْ يُؤْتَ سَعَةً مِنَ الْمالِ‌).

This was the first objection and breach of covenant that the Children of Israel made against that Prophet عليه السلام. Even though he had explicitly stated that the selection of Saul was from Allah, they were in fact objecting to Allah's choice, claiming they were more deserving because they possessed two necessary conditions for leadership: noble lineage and abundant wealth. As previously mentioned in the story, Saul was a young man from an obscure tribe of the Children of Israel and, financially, a simple farmer.

But the Quran recounts the crushing response that the Prophet gave to the misguided ones of the Children of Israel: "He said: 'Indeed, Allah has chosen him over you and has increased him abundantly in knowledge and stature'" (قالَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفاهُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ زادَهُ بَسْطَةً فِي الْعِلْمِ وَ الْجِسْمِ‌).

The reason you see that Allah has chosen him and made him your ruler is because he is rich in terms of intelligence, wisdom, and knowledge, and in terms of physical strength, he is strong and powerful.

That is to say, firstly, this is the selection of the All-Wise Allah, and secondly, you are gravely mistaken and have forgotten the essential conditions of leadership. Noble lineage and wealth are no merit for leadership because both are conventional and external matters, whereas knowledge and physical strength are two real and intrinsic merits that have a profound impact on the issue of leadership. A leader must, through his knowledge, diagnose the interests of the society he heads and, through his power, implement them in a timely manner; he must draw a correct plan for fighting the enemy with his knowledge and resourcefulness and execute it with his physical strength.

The expression "abundance" (bastah - expansion) indicates that the existential breadth of a human is in the light of knowledge and power; the more these increase, the more the human's being expands.

Here, the expansion of knowledge is prioritized over the expansion of physical strength, because the first condition is knowledge and awareness.

Furthermore, from this expression, it is understood that Imamate and leadership are a divine selection, and it is He who recognizes merits. If He sees this merit in the children of a Prophet, He places the Imamate there, and if He sees it elsewhere, He places it there. This is exactly what Shia scholars believe in and defend.

Then He adds: "And Allah grants His sovereignty to whom He wills, and Allah is All-Encompassing (in His bounty), All-Knowing (of the merit and worthiness of individuals)" (وَ اللَّهُ يُؤْتِي مُلْكَهُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ اللَّهُ واسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ‌).

This sentence may refer to a third condition for leadership, which is the provision of various means and facilities by Allah. For it is possible for a leader to be fully capable in terms of knowledge and power but be placed in conditions and a context that has no readiness for the advancement of his goals; surely such a leader will not achieve a brilliant victory.
