The Quran says: Allah grants divine governance to whom He wills, meaning He provides the necessary conditions and means for him (this statement may be a continuation of the Prophet's words or the words of Allah in the Quran).

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 350. 2: Ruh al-Ma'ani and Tafsir al-Kabir under the verses in question. [3]: The author of "Al-Kashshaf" considers "Talut" a non-Arabic name, like Goliath and David, while some have said it is an Arabic name derived from the root tul (length), referring to his tall stature (Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 6, p. 172).

The next verse shows that the Children of Israel, even with the explicit statement of their Prophet Samuel, still did not feel certain about Saul's mission from Allah and demanded a sign and proof from him. "And their Prophet said to them: 'Indeed, the sign of his kingship is that the Ark will come to you, in which is reassurance from your Lord and a remnant of what the family of Moses and the family of Aaron left behind, carried by the angels. Indeed, in that is a sign for you, if you are believers'" (وَ قالَ لَهُمْ نَبِيُّهُمْ إِنَّ آيَةَ مُلْكِهِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُمُ التَّابُوتُ فِيهِ سَكِينَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَ بَقِيَّةٌ مِمَّا تَرَكَ آلُ مُوسى‌ وَ آلُ هارُونَ تَحْمِلُهُ الْمَلائِكَةُ إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

Key Points

1- What was the Tabut or the Ark of the Covenant?

"Tabut" linguistically means a chest made of wood, and the reason we see the box used for transporting corpses called a tabut (coffin) is for this reason. However, it should be noted that the original meaning of tabut is not exclusive to the dead but includes any kind of wooden chest.

Regarding what the Ark of the Children of Israel—or in other words, the "Ark of the Covenant"—was, who made it, and what its contents consisted of, there is much discussion in our traditions and commentaries as well as in the books of the "Old Testament" (Torah). The clearest account is what appears in the Hadiths of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام and the statements of some commentators like Ibn Abbas, which is that: the "Tabut" was the very chest in which Moses' mother placed him and cast him into the river. When it was taken from the river by Pharaoh's agents and Moses was removed from it, it continued to be kept in Pharaoh's establishment and later fell into the hands of the Children of Israel. The Children of Israel respected this memorable chest and sought blessings from it.

In the final days of his life, Moses placed the sacred tablets on which Allah's laws were written, along with his armor and other relics, into it and entrusted it to his successor, "Joshua son of Nun." Thus, the importance of this chest increased in the eyes of the Children of Israel, and so they would take it with them in the wars that occurred between them and their enemies. It had a specific psychological and spiritual effect on them. It is said that as long as this memorable chest with its sacred contents was among them, they lived with honor. But gradually their religious foundations weakened, and enemies overcame them and took that chest from them. However, Samuel, according to the verses under discussion, promised them that the Ark of the Covenant would soon return to them as a sign of the truth of his words.

From the phrase "in which is reassurance from your Lord and a remnant of what the family of Moses and the family of Aaron left behind," it is understood that, firstly, the Ark of the Covenant had contents that gave peace to the population of the Children of Israel and had a spiritual influence and psychological effect on them in various events (fihi sakinatun min rabbikum). Secondly, a portion of the relics of the family of Moses and the family of Aaron was also later added to its contents.

It should be noted that "Sakinah" is from the root sukun, meaning peace, and what is meant by it here is peace of heart and soul.

Samuel reminded the Children of Israel that the Ark of the Covenant would return to your midst once again and you would find your lost peace. In truth, the chest—which, in addition to its spiritual and historical aspect, was something higher than a flag or a slogan for the Children of Israel, and whose existence they considered a sign of their independence and existence, and upon seeing it, they would remember the renewal of their former era of greatness—was returning to them. Naturally, this was considered a great glad tiding for the Children of Israel.

2- What is meant by the angels carrying it (تَحْمِلُهُ الْمَلائِكَةُ)?

How did the angels bring the Ark of the Covenant? In response to this question, commentators have also said much. The clearest is what is found in histories: when the "Ark of the Covenant" fell into the hands of the idolaters of Palestine and they took it to their temple, they were subsequently afflicted with many troubles. Some said these were all effects of the "Ark of the Covenant," so they decided to send it out of their city and land. Since no one was willing to take it out, they were forced to tie it to two cows and let them loose in the wilderness. Coincidentally, this event occurred exactly at the time of Saul's appointment as commander of the Children of Israel. Allah's angels were commissioned to drive these two animals toward the city of Samuel. When the Children of Israel saw the "Ark of the Covenant" in their midst, they took it as a sign and evidence from Allah of Saul's mission.

Therefore, although outwardly those two cows brought it to the city, in reality, this work was done by divine angels. For this reason, the carrying of the Ark is attributed to the angels.

Fundamentally, "angel" (farishtah and malak) in the Quran and traditions has a broad meaning which, in addition to rational spiritual beings, also includes a series of mysterious forces of this world.

From what has been said, it is well-utilized that with these miraculous signs, the issue of Saul's divine leadership and command was proven. However, as understood from the phrase "if you are believers," the weak-in-faith individuals still did not submit to the truth, and the continuation of this story will reveal this reality.

Finally, they submitted to the leadership and command of Saul, and he mobilized many armies and set out. It was here that the Children of Israel faced a strange test. It is better to hear this account from the tongue of the Quran in the continuation of these verses; He says:

"When Saul set out with the soldiers, he said: 'Indeed, Allah will test you with a river. So whoever drinks from it is not of me, and whoever does not taste it is indeed of me, except one who takes [from it] in the hollow of his hand'" (فَلَمَّا فَصَلَ طالُوتُ بِالْجُنُودِ قالَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ مُبْتَلِيكُمْ بِنَهَرٍ فَمَنْ شَرِبَ مِنْهُ فَلَيْسَ مِنِّي وَ مَنْ لَمْ يَطْعَمْهُ فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي إِلَّا مَنِ اغْتَرَفَ غُرْفَةً بِيَدِهِ‌). [1] Here, Saul's soldiers faced a great test, which was the issue of intense resistance against thirst. Such a test was necessary for Saul's army—especially given the bad track record the Children of Israel had in some wars—because the victory of any group depends on the amount of discipline, power of faith, steadfastness against the enemy, and obedience to the orders of the leader and commander.

Saul wanted to know to what extent the mass of the army would obey his command, especially since they had accepted his leadership with doubt, hesitation, and even protest. Although they had outwardly submitted, perhaps doubt and hesitation still ruled their hearts. For this reason, he was commanded to test them to clarify whether these soldiers, who wanted to stand against the fire-breathing sword of the enemy, had the ability to endure a bit of thirst or not?

But the majority of them—as we previously read in the description of the story and as briefly mentioned in the verse under discussion—did not emerge healthy from the crucible of this test, as the Quran says: "So they drank from it, except a few of them" (فَشَرِبُوا مِنْهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِنْهُمْ‌).

In this way, the "second purification" in Saul's army took place, for the "first purification" was what he had said at the time of announcing general mobilization: that those who have trade or a half-finished building and the like should not come with me.

Then He adds: "When he (Saul) and those who believed with him (and emerged healthy from the crucible of the test) had crossed it, they said: 'Today we have no power against Goliath and his soldiers'" (فَلَمَّا جاوَزَهُ هُوَ وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ قالُوا لا طاقَةَ لَنَا الْيَوْمَ بِجالُوتَ وَ جُنُودِهِ‌).

This sentence clearly shows that only that small group which emerged healthy from the test of thirst moved with him. But even they, when they thought that they would soon be facing the massive and powerful army of Goliath, cried out about their few numbers. This was the third stage of the test, because only an even smaller group from this small group declared full readiness and loyalty, as the Quran says in the continuation of this verse: "Those who were certain that they would meet Allah (and had faith in the Day of Resurrection and divine promises) said: 'How many a small group has overcome a large group by Allah's permission, and Allah is with the patient (and steadfast)'" (قالَ الَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُلاقُوا اللَّهِ كَمْ مِنْ فِئَةٍ قَلِيلَةٍ غَلَبَتْ فِئَةً كَثِيرَةً بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ وَ اللَّهُ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ‌). 2 This sentence clearly shows that those who had firm faith in the Day of Resurrection warned the rest that one should not look at the "quantity" of the population, but rather consider the "quality," for it has often happened that a population small in terms of numbers but high in quality—in terms of faith, will, determination, and reliance on divine favors—has overcome massive populations by Allah's permission.

It should be noted that "yadhunnun" (they think/assume) here means "ya'lamun" (they know); that is, those who had certainty in the Resurrection and knew the divine promises regarding the reward of the true mujahideen (for dhann is used in many cases to mean certainty, and even if we take it to mean "assumption," it would still not be inappropriate, for the concept of the verse would be that even an assumption of the Resurrection, let alone knowledge and certainty, makes a person determined toward divine goals, just as the assumption of profit in important works like agriculture, trade, and industry makes their owners determined).

Regarding why the Day of Judgment is called the Day of Meeting the Lord, we discussed it sufficiently under verse 46 of this same Surah.

[1]: "Fasal" originally means cutting and severing, and here it means separation (separating from the city and homeland). "Junud" is the plural of "jund," which originally means land that has large, piled-up stones; then it was applied to anything dense and striking, and usually a massive army is called "jund." The expression "lam yat'amhu" (did not taste it) instead of "did not drink it" indicates that they should not drink more than one handful of the water of that river and should not even put it in their mouths or taste it.

In the next verse, the issue of the confrontation of the two armies is raised; He says: "And when they went forth to face Goliath and his soldiers, they said: 'Our Lord! Pour down upon us patience and constancy, and make our feet firm, and help us against the disbelieving people'" (وَ لَمَّا بَرَزُوا لِجالُوتَ وَ جُنُودِهِ قالُوا رَبَّنا أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنا صَبْراً وَ ثَبِّتْ أَقْدامَنا وَ انْصُرْنا عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْكافِرِينَ‌).

"Barazu" is from the root buruz, meaning appearance. Since when a person is ready and appears in the battlefield, he has appearance and manifestation, this act is called combat or biraz.

This verse says: when Saul and his army reached a place where the powerful army of Goliath became visible and appeared, and they lined up against that massive power, they raised their hands in prayer and asked three things from Allah. First, patience, endurance, and steadfastness to the utmost degree; therefore, they used the expression "pour down upon us patience" (afrigh 'alayna sabran), which is from the root ifragh, meaning to pour water or another fluid from a vessel such that the vessel becomes completely empty. The indefinite form of "sabran" (patience) is also an emphasis on this point.

The reliance on the Lordship of the Lord (Rabbana), the expression ifragh which means emptying the cup, the expression 'ala (upon) which indicates descent from above, and the expression sabran which in such cases denotes greatness—each contains a point that makes the concept of this prayer completely deep and rich.

Their second request from Allah was: "make our feet firm" so that they are not uprooted and we do not flee. In truth, the first prayer had an internal and inner aspect, and this prayer has an external and outer aspect, and certainly, firmness of feet is among the results of the spirit of steadfastness and patience.

Their third request was: "help us and grant us victory over this disbelieving people," which in fact constitutes the main goal and is the final result of patience, steadfastness, and firmness of feet.

Certainly, Allah will not leave such servants alone, even if their number is small and the enemy's number is large. Therefore, in the next verse, He says: "So they defeated them by Allah's permission" (فَهَزَمُوهُمْ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ‌).

"And David (the young, strong, and brave youth who was in Saul's army) killed Goliath" (وَ قَتَلَ داوُدُ جالُوتَ‌).

Here, the details of how that tyrannical king was killed at the hands of David—the youth and newcomer to war—are not described, but as mentioned in the description of the story, with the sling he had in his hand, he threw one or two stones so skillfully that they struck Goliath right in the forehead and head, sank into it, and he let out a cry and fell. Fear and horror seized his entire army, and they quickly fled. It was as if Allah wanted to show His power here: how a king of such greatness and a massive army is brought down by a youth newly arrived on the field, and that too with a seemingly worthless weapon.

Then He adds: "And Allah gave him kingship and wisdom and taught him of what He willed" (وَ آتاهُ اللَّهُ الْمُلْكَ وَ الْحِكْمَةَ وَ عَلَّمَهُ مِمَّا يَشاءُ).

The pronoun in these two sentences refers to David, who was in fact the victor of this war.

Although it is not explicitly stated in this verse that this David is the same David, the great Prophet of the Children of Israel and father of Solomon, the phrase "Allah gave him kingship and wisdom and taught him of what He willed" shows that he reached the station of prophethood, because such expressions are usually about the Prophets of Allah, especially since a similar expression appears in verse 20 of Surah Sad regarding David: "And We strengthened his kingdom and gave him wisdom and decisive speech."

From the traditions narrated in the commentary of this verse, it is also clearly understood that he is the same David, the Prophet of the Children of Israel.

This expression may refer to the science of statecraft, the making of armor and war equipment, and the like, which David عليه السلام needed in his very great government, for Allah provides the necessary preparations for whoever He entrusts with a position.

And at the end of the verse, He points to a general law, saying: "And if it were not for Allah's repelling some people by means of others, the earth would have been corrupted, but Allah is full of bounty to the worlds" (وَ لَوْ لا دَفْعُ اللَّهِ النَّاسَ بَعْضَهُمْ بِبَعْضٍ لَفَسَدَتِ الْأَرْضُ وَ لكِنَّ اللَّهَ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ‌).

Allah is Kind and Merciful toward the worlds in that He prevents corruption from becoming universal and all-encompassing on the earth.

It is true that the Sunnah of the Lord has been established such that in this world, the principle of freedom of will and choice governs, and humans are free to choose the path of good or evil. But when the rebellion of tyrants puts the world at risk of general corruption and ruin, Allah raises and helps a group of His servants to stop their rebellion and crush their demonic state, and this is one of the favors of the Lord upon His servants.

A similar meaning also appears in verse 40 of Surah Al-Hajj; He says: "And if it were not for Allah's repelling some people by means of others, there would have been demolished monasteries, churches, synagogues, and mosques..."

And these verses are a glad tiding for the believers that in times when they are under intense pressure from taghuts and tyrants, they should expect divine help and victory.

Does this verse refer to the issue of the "Struggle for Existence"?

A question arises here: does this verse refer to the issue of the struggle for existence, which is one of the four principles of Darwin's theory on the evolution of species? A theory that says there must always be war and struggle among mankind, and if there isn't, stagnation, lethargy, and corruption will encompass everyone, and the human race will regress. But constant struggle and war cause the strong to remain and the weak to be trampled and perish in this system, and in this way, natural selection takes place.

Answer

This interpretation is possible only if we completely sever the relationship of the verse from what precedes it and even ignore the verse similar to it in Surah Al-Hajj. But with attention to them, it becomes clear that the verse under discussion refers to the struggle against rebellious and oppressive individuals; if Allah did not stop them through various means, the entire earth would be drawn into corruption. Therefore, it does not sanctify war as a general principle in human life.

Furthermore, what is called the law of the "struggle for existence"—a legacy of Darwin's four principles in the evolution of species—is not only not a proven scientific law but is a refuted hypothesis. Even proponents of the evolution of species today do not rely on the principle of the struggle for existence at all and consider the evolution of living beings to be related to the issue of "mutation." [1] Beyond all this, even if we were to consider the hypothesis of the struggle for existence a scientific principle, it should only be used regarding the life of animals; human life can never be built upon it, because human evolution is in the light of "cooperation for existence," not the struggle for existence! It seems that the generalization of the struggle for existence hypothesis to the human world is a type of colonialist thinking that some capitalist sociologists have resorted to in order to justify the bloody and hateful wars of their governments, wanting to introduce war and conflict as a natural law and a means for the evolution and progress of human societies, thereby putting a scientific cover over their crimes. Those who have unknowingly fallen under the influence of their anti-human thoughts and applied the above verse to it have certainly strayed from Quranic teachings, for the Quran explicitly says: "O you who have believed, enter into peace completely" (Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 208). It is surprising that some Islamic commentators, like the author of "Al-Manar" and also "Al-Maraghi" in his commentary, have been so influenced by this hypothesis that they have imagined it to be one of the divine Sunnahs and interpreted the above verse by it, imagining that this hypothesis is among the innovations of the Quran and not the inventions of Darwin! But as we said, neither is the above verse referring to it, nor does this hypothesis have any basis; rather, the principle governing human relations is cooperation for existence, not the struggle for existence!

The last verse says: "These are the verses of Allah which We recite to you in truth, and indeed, you are of the messengers" (تِلْكَ آياتُ اللَّهِ نَتْلُوها عَلَيْكَ بِالْحَقِّ وَ إِنَّكَ لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ‌).

This verse refers to the multiple stories that appeared in the previous verses regarding the Children of Israel and says: each one is a sign of the power and greatness of the Lord (and pure from any superstition or legend) and has been revealed by Allah to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and this itself is one of the signs of the truth of his speech and prophethood.

[1]: For further explanation, refer to the book "The Latest Hypothesis of Evolution."

Ayah

۞ تِلْكَ ٱلرُّسُلُ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۘ مِّنْهُم مَّن كَلَّمَ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَرَفَعَ بَعْضَهُمْ دَرَجَـٰتٍ ۚ وَءَاتَيْنَا عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَأَيَّدْنَـٰهُ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ ۗ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا ٱقْتَتَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ وَلَـٰكِنِ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ ءَامَنَ وَمِنْهُم مَّن كَفَرَ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا ٱقْتَتَلُوا۟ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ مَا يُرِيدُ (٢٥٣)
These are the apostles, some of whom We gave an advantage over others: of them are those to whom Allah spoke and some of them He raised in rank, and We gave Jesus, son of Mary, manifest proofs and strengthened him with the Holy Spirit. Had Allah wished, those who succeeded them would not have fought one another after the manifest proofs had come to them. But they differed. So there were among them those who had faith and there were among them those who were faithless, and had Allah wished, they would not have fought one another; but Allah does whatever He desires. (253)

Commentary

The Role of Prophets in Human Life

This verse refers to the degrees of the Prophets and their ranks, as well as a glimpse of their mission within human society. First, It states: "Those messengers—some of them We caused to exceed others" (تِلْكَ الرُّسُلُ فَضَّلْنا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ‌).

"تلك" (Those) is a demonstrative pronoun for the distant, and we know that this expression is sometimes used to show respect for the status and position of individuals, indicating that the station of the Prophets is so high that it is, as it were, beyond reach.

There is discussion among commentators regarding whether "the messengers" (الرسل) refers to all divine Prophets and messengers, or specifically to those messengers mentioned or whose stories were alluded to in the previous verses of this Surah—such as Abraham, Moses, Jesus, David, and Samuel—or all messengers whose names were in the Quran at the time this verse was revealed.

However, it seems more likely that it refers to all of God's Prophets, because the word "الرسل" is a "plural defined by the definite article (al-)" which denotes generality.

The expression "We caused some of them to exceed others" (فَضَّلْنا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ‌) clearly shows that although all divine Prophets were equal in terms of prophethood and messengership, they were not equal in rank. This is because the scope of their missions differed, and the extent of their sacrifices also varied.

Then, addressing the specific characteristics of some of them, It says: "Among them were those to whom Allah spoke" (مِنْهُمْ مَنْ كَلَّمَ اللَّهُ‌).

It is clear that this refers to Prophet Moses عليه السلام, who became famous as Kalim-Allah (the one to whom Allah spoke). In verse 164 of Surah An-Nisa, regarding him, It says: وَ كَلَّمَ اللَّهُ مُوسى‌ تَكْلِيماً, "And Allah spoke to Moses with [direct] speech."

The possibility that this refers to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله—referring to the words spoken to him on the Night of Ascension (Mi'raj), or referring to divine revelation which is called "speech" in verse 51 of Surah Ash-Shura: "And it is not for any human being that Allah should speak to him except by revelation..."—seems very unlikely. This is because revelation applies to all Prophets and does not align with the expression "among them" (منهم), where the "min" is partitive (indicating a specific group).

Then It adds: "and some of them He raised in degrees" (وَ رَفَعَ بَعْضَهُمْ دَرَجاتٍ‌).

Given that the difference in the degrees of Prophets was mentioned at the beginning of this verse, it is possible that this repetition refers to a specific individual or individuals, the perfect example of whom is the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. His religion was the most complete and the last of all religions, and one whose mission is to bring the most perfect of faiths must himself be superior to all. This is especially true since in verse 41 of Surah An-Nisa, the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله is considered the witness over all Prophets on the Day of Resurrection, while every other Prophet is a witness over his own nation: "So how [will it be] when We bring from every nation a witness and we bring you, [O Muhammad], against these [people] as a witness?"

Another piece of evidence for this is that the preceding phrase referred to the virtue of Moses عليه السلام, and the following phrase explicitly mentions the status of the Messiah عليه السلام. The flow of the discussion necessitates that this middle phrase also refers to the position of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, as these three Prophets are each the leader of one of the world's great religions. It is not surprising if the name of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله is placed between the two, for is it not true that his religion is the "middle way" among religions, where everything is implemented in a balanced manner? As the Quran says: "And thus we have made you a middle nation." [1] However, it is also said that the subsequent phrases of this verse indicate that "He raised some of them in degrees" refers to some of the earlier Prophets like Abraham and his likes, because It says in the past tense: "And if Allah had willed, those [generations] after them would not have fought each other..."

Then, moving to the distinction of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام, It says: "And We gave Jesus, the son of Mary, clear proofs and supported him with the Pure Spirit" (وَ آتَيْنا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ الْبَيِّناتِ وَ أَيَّدْناهُ بِرُوحِ الْقُدُسِ‌).

"Clear proofs" refers to miracles such as healing incurable diseases, raising the dead, and sublime religious knowledge.

"The Pure Spirit" (Ruh al-Qudus) refers to the messenger of God's revelation, namely Gabriel, or a specific mysterious spiritual power that exists—with variations—in the "Friends of Allah" (Awliya-Allah). This was discussed in detail in the commentary on verse 87 of Surah Al-Baqarah. The reason support by the Pure Spirit is mentioned here regarding the Messiah عليه السلام is that he had a greater share of it compared to many other Prophets.

Continuing the verse, It points to the condition of the nations and their differences after the Prophets, saying: "And if Allah had willed, those [generations] after them would not have fought each other after clear proofs had come to them" (وَ لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ مَا اقْتَتَلَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ‌).

That is, if Allah had willed, He had the power to forcibly prevent them from fighting and contending. However, the divine Sunnah (way) has always been to leave people free to choose their path. But they abused their freedom "and they differed" (وَ لكِنِ اخْتَلَفُوا).

"So some of them believed and some of them disbelieved" (فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ آمَنَ وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ كَفَرَ).

Certainly, this difference originated from the people themselves and their whims and desires; otherwise, there was no difference among the divine Prophets, as they all pursued a single goal.

Once again, It emphasizes: it was easy for Allah to prevent their differences by way of compulsion, for "if Allah had willed, they would not have fought each other, but Allah does what He intends" (وَ لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ مَا اقْتَتَلُوا وَ لكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ ما يُرِيدُ). (And His will is according to wisdom and in harmony with the purpose of human creation, which is freedom of will and being a free agent).

Undoubtedly, a group derives a negative result from this freedom, but overall, the existence of freedom is one of the most important pillars of human perfection, for forced perfection is not considered perfection.

Furthermore, from this verse—in which the issue of compulsion is mentioned twice—the falsehood of the belief in fatalism (jabr) becomes clearly evident. It proves that Allah has left humans free; a group accepts faith and a group accepts disbelief.

Key Point

The Source of Conflict Among Followers of Religions

Some Western writers have criticized religions and sects, claiming they have caused division and hypocrisy among mankind and that much blood has been shed in this path, as history remembers many religious wars. In this way, they have sought to condemn religions and consider them the source of war and strife.

In response to this claim, it should be noted that

Firstly—as indicated in the above verse—the differences were not actually between the true and sincere followers of religions, but rather between the "followers" and the "opponents" of religion. If we observe that wars and conflicts occurred even among followers of different religions, it was not because of their religious teachings, but rather due to the distortion of religions, undue prejudices, and the mixing of heavenly religions with superstitions.

Secondly—today, religion (or at least the influence of religion) has been removed from a portion of human societies, yet we see that wars have expanded in a more horrific manner. They continue with greater dimensions and intensity in various parts of the world. Are these because of religion? Or is it that the rebellious spirit of a group of humans is the real source of these wars? It simply manifests one day in the garb of religion, another day in the garb of political and economic schools of thought, and other days in other forms. Therefore, religion is not the guilty party here; it is these rebellious individuals who are guilty, igniting the fires of war under various pretexts.

Thirdly—heavenly religions, "especially Islam," due to their anti-racist and anti-nationalist nature, caused many racial, geographical, and tribal boundaries to be dismantled. The wars originating from them naturally disappeared. In this way, a portion of wars was eliminated from human history in the light of religion. Additionally, the spirit of peace, friendship, morality, and human emotions—which have been the focus of all heavenly religions—has had and continues to have a profound effect on reducing the hostilities and hatreds of various peoples.

Fourthly—one of the missions of heavenly religions was to liberate the deprived and suffering classes. For this reason, wars broke out between the Prophets and their followers against oppressors and exploiters like the Pharaohs and Nimrods.

These wars, which were in fact considered jihads for human liberation, were not only not a flaw for religions but were counted as a point of strength. The conflicts that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله had with the Arab polytheists and the usurers of Mecca on one hand, and with the Caesars and Chosroes on the other, were all of this kind.

[1]: Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 143.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ يَوْمٌ لَّا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَلَا خُلَّةٌ وَلَا شَفَـٰعَةٌ ۗ وَٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٢٥٤)
O you who have faith! Spend out of what We have provided you before there comes a day on which there will be no bargaining, neither friendship, nor intercession. And the faithless—they are the wrongdoers. (254)

Commentary

Spending in the Way of Allah—One of the Most Important Means of Salvation on the Day of Resurrection!

Following the previous verses which discussed parts of the history of past nations, their struggles (Jihad), Divine governance, and the disputes they had after their prophets, this verse addresses the Muslims. It points toward one of the duties that leads to the unity of society and the strengthening of the government, defensive capabilities, and Jihad, stating: "O you who have believed! Spend from that which We have provided for you" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْفِقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقْناكُمْ‌).

Although the phrase "from that which We have provided for you" (مِمَّا رَزَقْناكُمْ‌) has a broad meaning that encompasses both obligatory and recommended financial spending, as well as spiritual spending such as knowledge and other matters, it is not unlikely—given the warning at the end of the verse—that the intended meaning is obligatory spending, namely Zakat and the like. Furthermore, it is obligatory spending that strengthens the foundation of the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) and the government. Additionally, the use of the expression "from" (مما) implies that obligatory spending always comprises a portion of one's wealth, not all of it.

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan prefers the generality of the verse regarding both obligatory and recommended spending. He believes the end of the verse does not contain a threat but rather informs of the terrifying events of the Day of Resurrection [1]. However, considering the final sentence of the verse which labels the disbelievers as the wrongdoers, it serves as an indication that abandoning spending is a form of disbelief and wrongdoing, which is typically only conceivable regarding obligatory spending.

It then adds: Spend today while you have the ability "before there comes a Day in which there is no exchange, no friendship, and no intercession" (مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ يَوْمٌ لا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَ لا خُلَّةٌ وَ لا شَفاعَةٌ). [2] This is an indication that none of the material means of salvation that exist in this world will be present there. You cannot engage in any transaction or trade to purchase happiness and salvation from punishment for yourselves, nor will the material friendships of this world—which you acquire with your capital—benefit you there, for they too are preoccupied with their own deeds and cannot attend to others. Nor will intercession benefit you there, because Divine intercession occurs in the shadow of Divine-oriented deeds that make a person worthy of intercession, which you have not performed. As for material intercessions that can be obtained with wealth and status in this world, they will not exist there.

Therefore, by abandoning spending, hoarding wealth, and being miserly toward others, you have closed all the doors of salvation upon yourselves.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "And the disbelievers—they are the wrongdoers" (وَ الْكافِرُونَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ‌).

This is an indication that those who abandon spending and Zakat wrong both themselves and others.

In this sentence, the Quran intends to clarify this reality:

Firstly, the disbelievers wrong themselves because by abandoning obligatory spending and other religious and human duties, they deprive themselves of the greatest happiness; it is their own deeds that will seize them in that world, and Allah has not done any injustice to them.

Secondly, disbelieving individuals also wrong their society. Fundamentally, disbelief is the source of callousness, hard-heartedness, materialism, and world-worship, all of which are the primary sources of injustice and oppression.

It is also necessary to remind that "disbelief" (Kufr) in this verse—by the context of it being placed after the command to spend—is in the sense of disobedience, sin, and violation of Allah's command. The use of "Kufr" in this sense has many precedents in the Quran and Islamic Hadiths.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 360.

[^2]: "Khullah" (خُلَّةٌ) is derived from the root "Khallal" (خلل), meaning the gap between two things. Since friendship and affection permeate the depths of a human's being and soul, filling the gaps, this word is used to mean deep friendship.

Ayah

ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ ۚ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَىْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّا بِمَا شَآءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۖ وَلَا يَـُٔودُهُۥ حِفْظُهُمَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٢٥٥)
Allah—there is no god except Him—is the Living One, the All-sustainer. Neither drowsiness befalls Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that may intercede with Him except with His permission? He knows what is before them and what is behind them, and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes. His seat embraces the heavens and the earth and He is not wearied by their preservation, and He is the All-exalted, the All-supreme. (255)

Introduction: "Ayat al-Kursi," One of the Most Important Verses of the Quran

Regarding the importance and virtue of this verse, it is sufficient to note what has been narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. He asked Ubayy ibn Ka'b: "Which verse is the most superior verse in the Book of Allah?" He replied: "اللَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ الْحَيُّ الْقَيُّومُ." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله tapped him on the chest and said: "May knowledge be pleasant for you! By the One in whose hand is the soul of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, this verse has two tongues and two lips that glorify and sanctify God at the base of the Divine Throne."

In another hadith from Ali عليه السلام, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "سيد القرآن البقرة و سيد البقرة آية الكرسى يا على ان فيها لخمسين كلمة فى كل كلمة خمسون بركة" "The choice part of the Quran is Surah al-Baqarah, and the choice part of al-Baqarah is Ayat al-Kursi. In it are fifty words, and in every word are fifty blessings."

In another hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, it is stated: "Whoever recites Ayat al-Kursi once, Allah will turn away from him one thousand unpleasant things of the world and one thousand unpleasant things of the Hereafter; the easiest unpleasantness of the world being poverty, and the easiest unpleasantness of the Hereafter being the punishment of the grave." [^1] Narrations regarding the virtue of this noble verse are very numerous and have been cited in the books of both Shi'a and Sunni scholars. We conclude this discussion with two more hadiths from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله. He said: "اعطيت آية الكرسى من كنز تحت العرش و لم يؤتها نبى كان قبلى‌" "I was given Ayat al-Kursi from a treasure beneath the Throne, and it was not given to any prophet before me." [^2] In another hadith, it is mentioned that two brothers came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "We are going to Sham for trade; teach us what to say (to remain safe from the evil of wrongdoers)." He said: "When you reach a lodging and have prayed the Isha prayer, and when one of you goes to bed, recite the Tasbih of Fatima Zahra عليه السلام and then recite Ayat al-Kursi: "فانه محفوظ من كل شى‌ء حتى يصبح‌" 'For surely he will be protected from everything until morning.'" Then, in the continuation of this hadith, it is mentioned that at one of the lodgings, thieves intended to attack them, but no matter how hard they tried, they did not succeed. [^1] Certainly, all this importance given to Ayat al-Kursi is due to its significant and prominent content, which we will observe during its commentary.

Commentary

A Collection of His Attributes of Beauty and Majesty

It begins first with the Sacred Essence of Allah and the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid), His Beautiful Names, and His Attributes. It says: "Allah! There is no deity except Him" (اللَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ).

"Allah" is the specific name of God and refers to the Essence that encompasses all attributes of perfection, majesty, and beauty. He is the Originator of the universe; for this reason, no deity is worthy of worship except Him. Since the meaning of "Allah" implies oneness, the phrase "لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ" (there is no deity except Him) serves as an emphasis on it.

Then it adds: "The Ever-Living, the Self-Subsisting" (الْحَيُّ الْقَيُّومُ‌).

"Hayy" (Living) is derived from "Hayat" (life). This word, like any other intensive adjective (Sifah Mushabbahah), denotes permanence. It is obvious that life in Allah is true life, because His life is identical to His Essence, unlike living beings in the world of creation whose life is accidental; thus, they are alive at times and then they die. But this is not so for Allah, as we read in verse 58 of Surah al-Furqan: "وَ تَوَكَّلْ عَلَى الْحَيِّ الَّذِي لا يَمُوتُ‌" (And rely upon the Ever-Living who does not die). This is from one perspective. From another perspective, perfect life is a life that is not mixed with death. Therefore, true life is His life, which continues from eternity to eternity. However, human life—especially in this world, which is mixed with death—cannot be true life. Thus, we read in verse 64 of Surah al-Ankabut: "وَ ما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَ لَعِبٌ وَ إِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيوانُ‌" (And this worldly life is nothing but diversion and amusement, and the home of the Hereafter—that is [true] life). Based on these two aspects, true life belongs exclusively to Allah.

What is the Meaning of Allah Being "Alive"?

In common expressions, a living being refers to an entity that possesses growth, nutrition, reproduction, absorption, excretion, and perhaps sensation and movement. However, one must note that short-sighted individuals might assume life in the case of Allah to be like this, even though we know He possesses none of these attributes. This is the same analogy that leads humans into error regarding the knowledge of God, as they compare God's attributes with their own.

However, life in the broad and real sense of the word consists of "Knowledge" and "Power." Therefore, a Being that possesses infinite knowledge and power has perfect life. Allah's life is the sum of His knowledge and power. In reality, it is through knowledge and power that a living being is distinguished from a non-living one. As for growth, movement, nutrition, and reproduction, these are characteristics of beings that are deficient and limited, possessing needs they fulfill through nutrition, reproduction, and movement. But for Him who has no deficiency, these matters are not applicable.

As for "Qayyum," it is an intensive form derived from "Qiyam" (standing/sustaining). For this reason, it is applied to a Being whose existence is self-subsisting (Qiyam bi-dhat), and the existence of all beings is sustained by Him. Furthermore, He is the sustainer of the management of the affairs of the creatures.

It is clear that "Qiyam" means standing, and in everyday conversation, it refers to a specific posture, such as showing a person in a vertical state on the ground. Since this meaning is inconceivable regarding Allah, who is sanctified from body and physical attributes, it means the performance of the work of creation, management, and preservation. This is because when a person wants to perform a task, he stands up. Yes, He is the One who created all beings in the universe and has taken upon Himself their management, preservation, training, and development, and He is constantly and without any pause sustaining these affairs.

From this explanation, it becomes clear that "Qayyum" is, in fact, the root and foundation of all Divine Attributes of Action (Attributes of Action refer to those attributes that describe the relationship between God and the beings of the world), such as Creator, Provider, Giver of Life, Guide, and the like. He is the One who provides, He is the One who gives life, He is the One who causes death, and He is the One who guides. Therefore, the attributes of Creator, Provider, Guide, Giver of Life, and Bringer of Death are all gathered within the description of "Qayyum."

From here, it becomes clear that those who have limited its meaning to sustaining the matter of creation, or only the matter of providing sustenance and the like, have in fact pointed to one of the instances of "Qiyam," whereas its concept is broad and encompasses all of them. As we said, it means the One who is self-subsisting and others are sustained by Him and are in need of Him.

In reality, "Hayy" encompasses all Divine attributes such as Knowledge, Power, Hearing, and Seeing, and "Qayyum" expresses the need of all beings for Him. Therefore, it has been said that these two together are the Greatest Name (Ism al-A'zam) of Allah.

Then, in the continuation of the verse, it adds: "Neither drowsiness nor sleep overtakes Him" (لا تَأْخُذُهُ سِنَةٌ وَ لا نَوْمٌ‌), and He does not become heedless of the management of the world for even a moment.

"Sinah," according to many commentators, is derived from "wasan" and refers to the specific lethargy that occurs at the onset of sleep, or in other words, light sleep. "Nawm" means sleep, which is a state where the majority of a person's senses cease to function. In fact, "sinah" is a sleep that affects the eyes, but when it becomes deeper and affects the heart, it is called "nawm."

The sentence "لا تَأْخُذُهُ سِنَةٌ وَ لا نَوْمٌ‌" is an emphasis on Allah being "Hayy" and "Qayyum," because the absolute perfect sustaining of the affairs of the universe is possible only if there is not even a moment of heedlessness. Therefore, anything that is incompatible with the principle of Allah's "Qayyumiyyah" is automatically negated from His Holy Presence, even the weakest factor that might cause lethargy in His work, such as light sleep, which does not exist in His Pure Essence.

As for why "sinah" is mentioned before "nawm," even though usually the stronger state is mentioned first and then the weaker one is referred to, it is because, in terms of natural order, first "sinah" (light sleep) occurs and then it becomes deeper and turns into "nawm."

In any case, this sentence points to the fact that the grace and favor of Allah's management is constant and is not interrupted for a moment, and He is not like servants who become heedless due to light or heavy sleep or other factors.

Furthermore, the expression "لا تَأْخُذُهُ‌" (does not take/overtake Him) regarding sleep is a beautiful expression that visualizes how sleep dominates a person. It is as if sleep is a powerful-clawed being that captures and imprisons a person in its grip, and the helplessness of the strongest humans when they are restless against it is completely palpable. [^1] Then it points to the absolute ownership of Allah, saying: "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth" (لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

This is, in fact, the fifth description among the Divine attributes mentioned in this verse, as before it, there was a reference to Monotheism, His being Ever-Living and Self-Subsisting, and the fact that sleep does not overcome His Pure Essence.

In reality, this ownership is the result of that very "Qayyumiyyah," because when the sustaining of the affairs of the world and their management, as well as creation, belong specifically to His Essence, the ownership of everything also belongs to Him.

Therefore, what man possesses and utilizes is not his true property; it is merely a trust placed in his hands for a few days under conditions determined by the True Owner, and he has the right to dispose of them. In this way, ordinary owners are duty-bound to fully observe the conditions set by the True Owner; otherwise, their ownership is void and their disposals are unauthorized. These conditions are the very laws that Allah has determined for financial and economic matters.

It goes without saying that paying attention to this attribute—that everything belongs to God—has an important educational effect on humans. When they know that what they have is not their own and has been entrusted to them for a few days as a loan or trust, this belief certainly prevents a person from encroaching on the rights of others, exploitation, colonialism, hoarding, greed, stinginess, and avarice. [^2]

In the sixth description, He says: "Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission?" (مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يَشْفَعُ عِنْدَهُ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ‌).

This is, in fact, a response to the vain claim of the idolaters who said: "We worship these (idols) so that they may intercede for us in the presence of God," as stated in verse 3 of Surah az-Zumar: "ما نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونا إِلَى اللَّهِ زُلْفى‌" (We do not worship them except that they may bring us closer to Allah in station).

In fact, with a rhetorical question, it says that no one can intercede in His presence without God's command. This sentence is also an emphasis on Allah's "Qayyumiyyah" and His absolute ownership over all beings in the world. That is, if you see some who intercede in the presence of God (such as prophets and saints), it is not evidence that they own anything or have independent influence; rather, God has granted them this station of intercession. Therefore, their intercession, because it is by God's command, is considered another sign of His "Qayyumiyyah" and ownership.

Key Point: What is Intercession?

Regarding intercession, we have discussed it in detail in the first volume of the commentary under verse 48 of Surah al-Baqarah; therefore, we will suffice here with a brief reference.

"Intercession" (Shafa'ah) consists of a strong being helping a weaker being so that it can easily and successfully pass through the stages of its growth. Of course, this word (intercession) is usually used regarding intercession for sinners, but the concept of intercession in a broader sense includes all factors, motives, and causes in the universe. For example, earth, water, air, and sunlight are four factors that "intercede" for and guide a plant seed in reaching the stage of a tree or a complete plant. If we take intercession in the above verse in this broad sense, the result is that the existence of various factors and causes in the universe never limits Allah's absolute ownership nor diminishes anything from it, because the influence of all these causes is by His command and is, in fact, a sign of His "Qayyumiyyah" and ownership.

However, some imagine that the title "intercession" in religious concepts is similar to a kind of groundless social recommendation or so-called "nepotism" (parti-bazi). Its concept would be that individuals can sin as much as they can, and when they are polluted from head to toe, they cling to the hem of an intercessor and say: "At that moment when people reach for an intercessor, we have our hands on the hem of the children of Fatima!" But neither these critics have understood the logic of religion regarding the issue of intercession, nor that group of bold and reckless sinners. As mentioned above, "intercession" performed by the special servants of God is like "existential intercession" (shafa'ah takwiniyyah) carried out by natural factors. That is, just as if there is no factor of life or living cell within a plant seed, the shining of the sun for thousands of years or the blowing of breezes and the falling of life-giving raindrops will have no effect on its growth and development, the intercession of the saints of God is also ineffective for unworthy individuals; that is, fundamentally, they will have no intercession for such persons.

"Intercession" requires a kind of spiritual connection between the intercessor and the one being interceded for. In this way, one who hopes for intercession is duty-bound to establish a spiritual connection in this world with the person from whom he expects intercession. This connection will, in fact, be a kind of educational means for the one being interceded for, bringing him closer to the thoughts, actions, and school of the "intercessor," and as a result, he becomes worthy of intercession. Thus, intercession is a factor of education, not a means of nepotism or escaping responsibility. From here, it becomes clear that intercession does not change the will of the Lord regarding the sinner; rather, it is the sinner who, through a spiritual connection with the intercessor, undergoes a kind of perfection and development and reaches a boundary where he becomes worthy of God's forgiveness (pay close attention!).

With this reference, we return to the continuation of the commentary on the verse.

In the seventh description, He says: "He knows what is before them (the servants) and what is behind them" (يَعْلَمُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ ما خَلْفَهُمْ‌), and He is aware of their past and future.

In fact, this sentence is a proof for what was mentioned in the previous sentence regarding intercession. That is, Allah is aware of the past and future of the intercessors and knows what is hidden even from themselves. Therefore, they cannot present any new information regarding those for whom they wish to intercede before God to draw His attention to the ones being interceded for.

To explain: In ordinary intercessions, the intercessor enters through one of two ways: either he provides information regarding the merit and worthiness of the one being interceded for to that great person and asks him to reconsider his judgment, or he describes the relationship of the one being interceded for with the intercessor so that, for the sake of the affection that great person has for the intercessor, he might change his judgment. It is clear that each of these two subjects is contingent upon the intercessor having information that does not exist with the person in whose presence he is interceding. But if He has complete scientific encompassment over everything and everyone, no one can intercede for anyone in His presence [independently], because He knows both the merits of individuals and their connections with one another. Therefore, intercession is valid only with His permission.

All this is if the pronoun in "ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ ما خَلْفَهُمْ‌" refers to the intercessors or the ones being interceded for. However, the possibility is also given that the pronoun refers to all rational beings in the heavens and the earth, which was included in the sentence "لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ‌." This would be an emphasis on the perfect power of the Lord over everything and the inability of others; for he who is unaware of his own past and future and has no knowledge of the unseen of the heavens and the earth has very limited power. Conversely, He who is aware of everything in every age and time, in the past and future, has infinite power. For this reason, every action—even intercession—must take place by His command.

Thus, both meanings can be combined.

Regarding what is meant by "ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ ما خَلْفَهُمْ‌," commentators have given several possibilities. Some have said "ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ‌" refers to the affairs of the world which are before man, and "وَ ما خَلْفَهُمْ‌" means the affairs of the Hereafter which are behind man [in terms of sequence]. Some have interpreted it the opposite way.

Some have also considered it a reference to man's appointed time (ajal), or his good and evil deeds, or matters they know and do not know.

However, by referring to the verses of the Quran, it is understood that these two expressions are used in some cases regarding place, such as verse 17 of Surah al-A'raf, which quotes Satan saying: "لَآتِيَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ وَ عَنْ أَيْمانِهِمْ وَ عَنْ شَمائِلِهِمْ‌" (I will come to them from before them and from behind them and on their right and on their left). It is clear that here these two expressions refer to place, and therefore right and left have also been added.

And sometimes it means before and after in time, such as what is mentioned in verse 170 of Surah Al-Imran: "وَ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِالَّذِينَ لَمْ يَلْحَقُوا بِهِمْ مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ‌" (The martyrs in the way of Allah receive good tidings about those who are still behind them and have not yet joined them). It is clear that here it refers to time.

But in the verse under discussion, it may refer to a comprehensive meaning that encompasses both. That is, Allah knows all that was and is in the past and future, as well as what is before people and they are aware of it, and what is behind them and is covered and hidden from them. He knows all and is aware of all. In this way, the expanse of time and place is all clear in the presence of His knowledge; thus, every action—even intercession—must be by His permission.

In the eighth description, He says: "And they encompass nothing of His knowledge except what He wills" (وَ لا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَيْ‌ءٍ مِنْ عِلْمِهِ إِلَّا بِما شاءَ). He has placed only a small portion of knowledge, which He deemed beneficial, at the disposal of others. [^1] In this way, the limited knowledge and science of others is a reflection of His infinite knowledge.

This sentence is also, in fact, an emphasis on the previous sentence regarding the limited nature of the intercessors' knowledge compared to the Lord's knowledge, because they do not encompass Allah's known things, and they become aware only of the amount He wills.

From this sentence, two other points are also derived: First, that no one possesses knowledge of himself, and all human sciences and knowledge are from God.

It is He who gradually removes the veil from the wondrous secrets of the world of creation and places new truths at the disposal of humans, expanding their knowledge.

Second: Allah may place some of the hidden sciences and secrets of the unseen (ghayb) at the disposal of those He wills. Therefore, it is a response to those who imagine that knowledge of the unseen is impossible for humans, and it is also an interpretation for the verses that negate knowledge of the unseen from humans; that is, man inherently knows nothing of the secrets of the unseen except for the amount God wills and teaches him (further and more detailed explanation on this subject will come, God willing, under the verses related to the knowledge of the unseen, especially verse 26 of Surah al-Jinn).

The expression "لا يُحِيطُونَ‌" (they do not encompass) is also a subtle reference to the reality of knowledge, which is a kind of encompassment.

In the ninth and tenth descriptions, He says: "His Kursi (Throne/Authority) extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not" (وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ لا يَؤُدُهُ حِفْظُهُما).

And in the eleventh and twelfth descriptions, He says: "And He is the Most High, the Most Great" (وَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ‌).

Key Points

1- What is Meant by Arsh and Kursi?

"Kursi," in terms of its linguistic root, is derived from "kurs" (rhyming with 'urth'), which means foundation and basis. Sometimes it is also applied to anything that is joined and composed. For this reason, short thrones are called "kursi," and its opposite is "Arsh," which means something "roofed," the roof itself, or a high-based throne.

Since a master or teacher sits on a "kursi" during teaching and instruction, the word "kursi" is sometimes a metaphor for knowledge. Given that the "kursi" is under the control, influence, and mastery of a person, it is sometimes used as a metaphor for "government," "power," and sovereignty over a region.

In the above verse, we read that Allah's Kursi encompasses all the heavens and the earth. "Kursi" here can have several meanings:

A: The Region of the Realm of Government

  • Meaning that Allah governs all the heavens and the earth, and His region of influence encompasses everywhere. In this way, Allah's Kursi is the collection of the material world, including the earth, stars, galaxies, and nebulae.

Naturally, the "Arsh" (Throne), according to this meaning, must be a stage higher and more exalted than the material world (as we said "Arsh" linguistically means roof, canopy, and high-based thrones, unlike "kursi"). In this case, the meaning of "Arsh" would be the world of spirits, angels, and the metaphysical world. Of course, this is if "Arsh" and "Kursi" are mentioned in contrast to each other, where one means the "world of matter and nature" and the other means the "metaphysical world." However, as will come under verse 54 of Surah al-A'raf, "Arsh" has other meanings as well, and especially if it is not mentioned in contrast to "Kursi," it may mean the entirety of the universe.

B: The Region of the Influence of Knowledge

  • Meaning that Allah's knowledge encompasses all the heavens and the earth, and nothing is outside the realm of His knowledge's influence, because as we said, "Kursi" is sometimes a metaphor for knowledge. In several narrations, this meaning has been emphasized. Among them, Hafs ibn Ghiyath narrates from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he asked him: What is meant by "وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ"? He replied: "It means His knowledge." [^1]

C: An Entity Vaster than All the Heavens and the Earth

  • Which encompasses them from every side. In this way, the meaning of the verse becomes: Allah's Kursi has encompassed all the heavens and the earth and surrounded them.

In a hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, this interpretation is narrated where he says: "الكرسى محيط بالسماوات و الارض و ما بينهما و ما تحت الثرى‌" "The Kursi encompasses the heavens and the earth and what is between them and what is beneath the depths of the earth." [^1]

It is even understood from some narrations that the Kursi is many times vaster than the heavens and the earth, such that the collection of them compared to the Kursi is like a ring placed in the middle of a desert. Among these, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "ما السماوات و الارض عند الكرسى الا كحلقة خاتم فى فلاة و ما الكرسى عند العرش الا كحلقة فى فلاة" [^2] (The heavens and the earth compared to the Kursi are but like a signet ring in the middle of a desert, and the Kursi compared to the Arsh is but like a ring in the middle of a desert). Of course, the first and second meanings are completely understandable and clear, but the third meaning is something that human science and knowledge have not yet been able to unveil. This is because the existence of such a world that encompasses the heavens and the earth and is many times vaster than our world has not yet been proven through conventional scientific methods. At the same time, there is no evidence to negate it; rather, all scientists admit that the vastness of the heavens and the earth in our view increases day by day with the advancement of astronomical study tools, and no one can claim that the vastness of the universe is only as much as today's science has succeeded in discovering. Rather, in all probability, there are countless other worlds that are outside the range of our current instruments.

It should not be left unsaid that the three interpretations above are not at all incompatible with each other. The sentence "وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ‌" can refer to the influence of absolute government and the power of the Lord in the heavens and the earth, as well as His scientific influence, and also a world vaster than this world that encompasses the heavens and the earth. In any case, this sentence completes the preceding sentences of the verse regarding the vastness of the Lord's knowledge.

The result is that the government and power of the Lord have filled all the heavens and the earth, and the Kursi of His knowledge encompasses all these worlds, and nothing is outside the realm of His government and scientific influence.

In the sentence "وَ لا يَؤُدُهُ حِفْظُهُما"—"ya'uduhu" is originally from the root "awd" (rhyming with 'qawl') meaning weight and heaviness—it means that the preservation of the heavens and the earth entails no heaviness or difficulty for Allah. This is because He is not like His creatures and servants whose power is limited and who sometimes become tired and incapable of maintaining something. His power is infinite, and for an infinite power, fundamentally, heaviness and lightness, difficulty and ease, have no meaning. These concepts all apply to limited powers! From what we have said above, it becomes clear that the pronoun in "ya'uduhu" refers to Allah. The previous sentences of the verse and the subsequent sentence also testify to this meaning, as their pronouns all return to Allah. Therefore, the possibility of the pronoun returning to the Kursi—in the sense that "the preservation of the heavens and the earth is not heavy or burdensome for the Kursi"—seems very weak.

The sentence "وَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ‌" is, in fact, a proof for the previous sentences. That is, the God who is higher and more exalted than any peer or partner and any kind of deficiency, flaw, or shortcoming, and the God who is Great and Magnificent and infinite—no task is difficult for Him, and He never becomes tired, incapable, heedless, or unaware of the administration and management of the universe, and His knowledge encompasses everything.

2- Is Ayat al-Kursi Only This One Verse?

Is Ayat al-Kursi only this one verse mentioned above, which starts from "اللَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ" and ends at "وَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ‌," or are the two following verses also part of it? Thus, if in a place like the prayer for the night of burial (Salat Laylat al-Dafn) it is commanded to recite Ayat al-Kursi, must all three verses be recited?

There are indications at hand showing that it is only that one verse:

1- All the narrations that have been mentioned regarding its virtue and refer to it as "Ayat al-Kursi" (The Verse of the Kursi) show that it is no more than one verse.

2- The expression "Kursi" occurs only in that first verse, and the naming as "Ayat al-Kursi" is also related to this verse.

3- In some hadiths, this meaning is explicitly stated, such as a hadith narrated in the Amali of the Shaykh from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, who, while explaining the virtue of Ayat al-Kursi, started from "اللَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ" until "وَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ‌."

4- In Mustadrak Safinat al-Bihar, it quotes from Majma': "و آية الكرسى معروفة و هى الى قوله‌وَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ‌" "Ayat al-Kursi is well-known, and it is until His words: 'وَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ‌'." [^1]

5- In a hadith from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, we read that the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Whoever recites four verses from the beginning of Surah al-Baqarah, Ayat al-Kursi and two verses after it, and three verses from the end of al-Baqarah, will never see anything unpleasant in himself or his property, Satan will not approach him, and he will not forget the Quran." [^2] From this expression as well, it is understood that Ayat al-Kursi is one verse.

6- In some narrations, it is stated that Ayat al-Kursi is fifty words and in every word are fifty blessings. [^1] Counting the words of the verse also shows that until "وَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ‌" it is fifty words.

Yes, in some narrations, it is commanded to recite until "هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ‌" without the title "Ayat al-Kursi" being the subject.

In any case, what is understood from the above indications is that Ayat al-Kursi is no more than one verse.

3- The Reason for the Importance of Ayat al-Kursi

The extraordinary importance of Ayat al-Kursi is due to the fact that it encompasses a collection of Islamic knowledge and Divine attributes, including attributes of Essence and Action, especially the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) in its various dimensions. These attributes, which amount to twelve sections, each of which can be relevant to one of the educational issues of man, are worthy of careful consideration. According to Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, each of these attributes negates one of the false sects (and in this way, twelve false and incorrect thoughts are corrected by it). [^2]

Ayah

لَآ إِكْرَاهَ فِى ٱلدِّينِ ۖ قَد تَّبَيَّنَ ٱلرُّشْدُ مِنَ ٱلْغَىِّ ۚ فَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِٱلطَّـٰغُوتِ وَيُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدِ ٱسْتَمْسَكَ بِٱلْعُرْوَةِ ٱلْوُثْقَىٰ لَا ٱنفِصَامَ لَهَا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٥٦)
There is no compulsion in religion: rectitude has become distinct from error. So one who disavows fake deities and has faith in Allah has held fast to the firmest handle for which there is no breaking; and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (256)

Circumstances of Revelation

The renowned Islamic commentator, al-Tabarsi, narrates in Majma' al-Bayan regarding the circumstances of this verse's revelation: A man from the people of Medina named "Abu Husayn" had two sons. Some merchants who imported goods to Medina met these two boys and invited them to the creed and religion of Christianity. They were deeply influenced and entered this faith, and upon the merchants' return, they departed with them toward Levant (Sham).

Abu Husayn was deeply distressed by this event. He informed the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and asked him to bring them back to their original religion, questioning whether he could force them to return to his faith.

The above verse was revealed, expressing this reality: "There is no compulsion or coercion in [accepting] religion."

In the commentary Al-Manar, it is narrated that Abu Husayn wanted to force his two children back to Islam. They came to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) to complain. Abu Husayn said to the Prophet: "How can I allow myself to watch my children enter the Fire while I stand by as a witness?" The verse under discussion was revealed for this very purpose.

Commentary

Religion is Not Compulsory

Ayat al-Kursi was, in fact, a collection of Tawhid (Monotheism) and the attributes of God's Beauty and Majesty, which form the foundation of religion. Since it is provable through rational reasoning at every stage and requires no force or coercion, He says in this verse: "There is no compulsion in religion, [for] the right way has become distinct from error" (لَا إِكْرَاهَ فِي الدِّينِ قَد تَّبَيَّنَ الرُّشْدُ مِنَ الْغَيِّ).

"Rushd" (Right Way) linguistically refers to finding the path and reaching reality, as opposed to "Ghayy" (Error), which means deviating from the truth and distancing oneself from reality.

Since religion and faith deal with the soul and mind of people, and its foundation and bedrock are built upon faith and certainty, it inevitably has no path other than logic and reasoning. The phrase "There is no compulsion in religion" is, in fact, an allusion to this very point.

Furthermore, as understood from the circumstances of revelation, some uninformed individuals wanted the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) to act like tyrannical rulers and use force and pressure to change people's beliefs (even if only outwardly). The above verse explicitly answered them that religion and creed are not things to be propagated through coercion and force, especially since, in the light of clear proofs and manifest miracles, the path of truth has been distinguished from falsehood, leaving no need for such measures.

This verse is a crushing response to those who imagine that Islam, in some instances, had an imposed and compulsory aspect and advanced through force, the sword, and military power.

Where Islam does not permit a father to pressure his own child to change his belief, the duty toward others is clear. If such a thing were permissible, it would have been necessary to grant this permission to a father regarding his child before anyone else; yet, such a right was not given to him.

From this, it becomes clear that this verse does not pertain only to the People of the Book, as some commentators have imagined, nor has the ruling of the verse ever been abrogated, as others have claimed. Rather, it is an eternal ruling consistent with the logic of reason.

Then, as a conclusion to the previous statement, it adds: "So whoever disbelieves in Taghut (idols, Satan, and rebellious humans) and believes in Allah has grasped the firmest handhold which will never break" (فَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِالطَّاغُوتِ وَيُؤْمِن بِاللَّهِ فَقَدِ اسْتَمْسَكَ بِالْعُرْوَةِ الْوُثْقَىٰ لَا انفِصَامَ لَهَا).

"Taghut" is an intensive form derived from the root "Tughyan," meaning transgression and exceeding limits. It is applied to anything that causes one to exceed the limits. Therefore, devils, idols, tyrannical and arrogant rulers, any deity other than the Lord, and any path that leads to other than the Truth are all "Taghut" (it should be noted that this word is used for both singular and plural).

In this sentence, the Quran says: Whoever disbelieves in "Taghut," turns away from it, and believes in Allah has grasped a firm handle that will never be severed.

Regarding what is meant by "Taghut" in the verse, commentators have offered many views. Some have interpreted it as Satan, some as soothsayers, and others as sorcerers. However, it seems that the intention is all of these, and even broader. That is, the same general concept derived from the word "Taghut," which encompasses every rebellious being and every deviant and incorrect creed or path.

The verse is, in truth, a proof for the preceding sentences—that there is no need for coercion in religion. For religion invites toward Allah, who is the source of every good, blessing, and happiness, while others invite toward destruction, deviation, and corruption. In any case, grasping the mantle of faith in Allah is like grasping a firm handle of salvation that can never possibly break.

And at the end, He says: "And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing" (وَاللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ).

This is an allusion to the fact that the matter of disbelief and faith is not something that can be achieved through mere pretense. For Allah hears the words of everyone, whether spoken openly or in private and secret meetings, and He is also aware of what is hidden in the hearts and consciences. Therefore, the disbelief or faith of everyone is clear to Him.

This sentence is, in reality, an encouragement for true believers and a threat to the hypocrites who misuse the outward cloak of Islam.

Key Point: Religion Cannot Be Imposed

Fundamentally, Islam and any true religion cannot be imposed for two reasons:

A) After all those clear proofs, logical reasoning, and manifest miracles, there is no need for this. Those resort to force and imposition who lack logic, not Islam with its clear logic and powerful arguments.

B) Fundamentally, religion, which stems and draws its essence from a series of heart-felt beliefs, cannot be imposed. Force, the sword, and military power can affect our physical actions and movements, but not our thoughts and beliefs.

From what has been said, the answer to the poisonous propaganda of the enemies of Islam, including the Church, becomes clear. For no statement more explicit than "There is no compulsion in religion," which appears in the text of the Quran, can be found in this regard.

Of course, they resort to Islamic struggles and wars for the sake of distortion. However, a study of Islamic wars clearly reveals that a portion of these wars was defensive, and another portion, which had the aspect of initial Jihad, was not for conquest or forcing individuals into the religion of Islam. Rather, it was to topple erroneous and oppressive systems and to allow people the freedom to study religion and social lifestyles freely.

A vivid witness to this statement is that it is frequently seen in Islamic history that when Muslims conquered cities, they gave followers of other religions the same freedom as Muslims.

If a small tax called "Jizyah" was collected from them, it was for the sake of providing security and the costs of the forces protecting that security, because their lives, property, and honor were protected under the shelter of Islam, and they even performed their religious ceremonies freely.

All those who are familiar with Islamic history know this truth, and even Christians who have written books about Islam have confessed to this. For example, in the book The Civilization of the Arabs, we read: "The behavior of Muslims toward other populations was so mild that their religious leaders were permitted to hold their own religious assemblies."

And it is narrated in some histories: A group of Christians who had come to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) for reports and inquiries performed their religious worship ceremonies freely in the Prophet's Mosque in Medina.

Fundamentally, Islam resorted to military power in three cases:

  1. In the case of erasing the traces of polytheism and idolatry. Because from the perspective of Islam, idolatry is not a religion or creed, but rather a deviation, a disease, and a superstition. A group should never be allowed to proceed on a path that is one hundred percent wrong and superstitious and be led to a fall. Therefore, Islam invited idolaters toward monotheism through propagation, and where they resisted, it resorted to force, smashed the idol temples, and prevented any manifestation in the name of idols and idolatry so that this spiritual and intellectual disease would be completely uprooted. Verses regarding fighting the polytheists, such as verse 193 of Surah Al-Baqarah: "And fight them until there is no more Fitnah (polytheism)..." refer to this very subject. Therefore, there is no contradiction or conflict between the verse under discussion and these verses such that one would speak of abrogation.

  2. Against those who plot the destruction of and attack on Muslims, the command for "Defensive Jihad" and resorting to military power has been given. Perhaps most of the Islamic wars during the time of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) were of this kind; as examples, one can name the battles of Uhud, Ahzab, Hunayn, Mu'tah, and Tabuk.

  3. To gain freedom in propagation. Because every creed has the right to introduce itself freely and logically. If some prevent this, it can obtain this right by resorting to force.

Ayah

ٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَوْلِيَآؤُهُمُ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتُ يُخْرِجُونَهُم مِّنَ ٱلنُّورِ إِلَى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٥٧)
Allah is the wali of the faithful: He brings them out of darkness into light. As for the faithless, their awliya are the fake deities, who drive them out of light into darkness. They shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they will remain in it [forever]. (257)

Commentary

The Light of Faith and the Darkness of Disbelief

Following the reference in the previous verse regarding the matter of faith and disbelief, and the clarity of truth from falsehood and the right path from the deviant one, here He specifies the status of believers and disbelievers in terms of their guide and leader, stating: "Allah is the Wali (Protector and Guardian) of those who believe" (اللَّهُ وَلِيُّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا).

And in the light of this guardianship and leadership, "He brings them out from darknesses into the light" (يُخْرِجُهُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ).

The word "Wali," as will be discussed in detail later under the verse: "Verily, your Wali is Allah and His Messenger..." [1], originally means "closeness and lack of separation." For this reason, a guardian, educator, and leader is called a "Wali," and the term is also applied to intimate friends and companions. However, it is clear that in the verse under discussion, it carries the first meaning, and thus He says:

[1]: Surah Al-Ma'idah, Verse 55.

"Allah, who is the Wali of the believers, guides them from darknesses into the light."

It might be argued that guiding believers from darknesses to light is "attaining what is already attained" (redundant).
