However, considering the hierarchy of guidance and faith, it becomes clear that this is not the case. Believers, in their journey of guidance and proximity to Allah, are intensely in need of Divine guidance at every stage, and they require His guidance at every step and in every action and program—exactly like what we say day and night in our prayers: "Guide us to the straight path" (اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ), meaning "O Allah, continuously guide us to the straight path."

Then He adds: "But those who disbelieve, their guardians are Taghut (idols, Satan, and tyrannical or deviant individuals) who bring them out from the light into darknesses" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَوْلِياؤُهُمُ الطَّاغُوتُ يُخْرِجُونَهُمْ مِنَ النُّورِ إِلَى الظُّلُماتِ).

"For this reason, they are the dwellers of the Fire, and they will abide therein forever" (أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ).

Key Points

  1. The comparison of "faith" and "disbelief" to "light" and "darkness" is the most eloquent comparison imaginable in this regard. Light is the source of life and all vital blessings and effects; it is the fountainhead of growth, development, perfection, and movement. Light is calming, reassuring, enlightening, and revealing, whereas darkness is the symbol of silence and death, sleep and ignorance, deviation and terror. Faith and disbelief are exactly like this.

  2. Another point is that in this verse and similar verses in the Holy Quran, "darkness" is brought in the plural form (ظلمات - darknesses), while "light" is brought in the singular form (نور). This is an indication that in the path of truth, there is no scattering or duality; rather, it is entirely unity and oneness.

The path of truth is like a straight line drawn between two points, which is always one, and multiplicity in it is impossible.

However, falsehood and disbelief are the center of all kinds of differences and fragmentation. Even the people of falsehood are not harmonious in their falsehood and do not have unity of purpose. It is exactly like the deviant lines drawn between two points, the number of which on both sides of the "straight line" is countless and unlimited.

Some have also suggested the possibility that the ranks of falsehood are more numerous compared to the people of truth.

  1. It might be said that disbelievers have no light to be brought out from; however, considering that the light of faith exists in the primordial nature (Fitrah) of everyone, this expression seems perfectly correct.

  2. It goes without saying that Allah does not forcibly bring believers out of darknesses (sin, ignorance, vice, and distance from truth) into light, nor does He forcibly take disbelievers out of their monotheistic nature; rather, it is their own actions that necessitate such a destiny from the Lord.

Ayah

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِى حَآجَّ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ فِى رَبِّهِۦٓ أَنْ ءَاتَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُلْكَ إِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ رَبِّىَ ٱلَّذِى يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ قَالَ أَنَا۠ أُحْىِۦ وَأُمِيتُ ۖ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْتِى بِٱلشَّمْسِ مِنَ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ فَأْتِ بِهَا مِنَ ٱلْمَغْرِبِ فَبُهِتَ ٱلَّذِى كَفَرَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢٥٨)
Have you not regarded him who argued with Abraham about his Lord, [only] because Allah had given him kingdom? When Abraham said, ‘My Lord is He who gives life and brings death,’ he replied, ‘I [too] give life and bring death.’ Abraham said, ‘Indeed Allah brings the sun from the east; now you bring it from the west.’ Thereat the faithless one was dumbfounded. And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (258)

Commentary

Abraham’s Dispute with the Tyrant of the Time!

Following the previous verse, which spoke of the guidance of the believers under the light of the Lord’s guardianship and the straying of the disbelievers due to following Taghut (false deities), Allah mentions several examples. One of the clearest examples is found in the verse under discussion: the story of the dialogue and dispute between Abraham, the hero of iconoclasm, and the tyrant of his time, "Nimrod." He says: "Have you not considered (are you not aware of) him who argued with Abraham about his Lord?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِي حَاجَّ إِبْراهِيمَ فِي رَبِّهِ).

In a single phrase, He points to the primary motive for this dispute, saying: "Because Allah had given him kingship." Due to his lack of inner capacity, he became intoxicated by the wine of pride and arrogance (أَنْ آتاهُ اللَّهُ الْمُلْكَ).

How many people are there who, in normal circumstances, are moderate, compliant, faithful, and alert, but when they attain wealth, status, or influence, they consign everything to oblivion and trample upon the most sacred values.

The verse continues: When he asked Abraham (peace be upon him), "Who is your Lord to whom you invite?" "Abraham said: My Lord is He who gives life and causes death" (إِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ رَبِّيَ الَّذِي يُحْيِي وَ يُمِيتُ).

In truth, he presented the greatest masterpiece of creation—the law of life and death—as a clear sign of the Lord's knowledge and power.

However, the tyrant Nimrod took the path of deception and sophistry to mislead the people and his entourage, and "said: I [too] give life and cause death," claiming the law of life and death was in his hands (قالَ أَنَا أُحْيِي وَ أُمِيتُ).

To prove this false claim, according to a well-known narration, he resorted to a trick. He ordered two prisoners to be brought forth; he commanded that one be released and the other be executed. Then, turning to Abraham and those present, he said: "Did you see how life and death are in my hands?"

To neutralize this conspiracy, Abraham (peace be upon him) resorted to another argument that the enemy could not manipulate through fallacies before the simple-minded. "Abraham said:

Indeed, Allah brings the sun from the East (the eastern horizon); so (if you speak the truth that you are the ruler of the universe), bring it from the West" (قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْتِي بِالشَّمْسِ مِنَ الْمَشْرِقِ فَأْتِ بِها مِنَ الْمَغْرِبِ).

It was at this point that "the disbeliever was utterly confounded and left speechless" (فَبُهِتَ الَّذِي كَفَرَ).

Indeed, "Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ).

Thus, that man, intoxicated and arrogant with his sovereignty and status, became silent, bewildered, and helpless, unable to utter a word against the living logic of Abraham (peace be upon him). This is the best way to silence such stubborn individuals.

While it is certain that the issue of life and death is, in some respects, more significant than the rising and setting of the sun and serves as a more eloquent testimony to the Lord's knowledge and power—which is why Abraham argued with it first—and if there were thoughtful and enlightened individuals in that gathering, they would naturally have been convinced by that first proof. Everyone knows that the act of releasing or killing a prisoner has no relation to the issue of natural, real life and death. However, for those who lacked sufficient understanding and might have been influenced by the sophistry of that deceitful tyrant, he turned to the second argument. He raised the issue of the sun's rising and setting so that the truth would become clear to both groups [1]. How well Abraham (peace be upon him) acted by first bringing up the matter of life and death so that the tyrant would claim partnership with the Lord in managing the world, and then mentioning the rising and setting of the sun, where he was left completely powerless.

Furthermore, from the phrase "وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ" (And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people), it becomes clear that although guidance and misguidance are in Allah's hands, their prerequisites are provided by the servants themselves. Oppression, tyranny, and sin act like dark, thick clouds that cast a shadow over the mirror of the heart, preventing it from perceiving realities.

[1]: The fact that the second argument begins with "fa" (so/then) seemingly indicates that the second argument is not a dismissal of the first, but rather something added to it.

Key Points

  1. Who faced Abraham in this session and disputed with him? The Quran does not explicitly mention his name, but only states: أَنْ آتاهُ اللَّهُ الْمُلْكَ, meaning that because of the arrogance stemming from his autocratic rule, he rose to dispute with Abraham. However, in a narration from Ali (peace be upon him) cited in the commentary Al-Durr al-Manthur, as well as in historical records, his name was "Nimrod son of Canaan."

  2. Although the timing of this discussion is not specified in the verse, evidence suggests that this event occurred after Abraham’s iconoclasm and his rescue from the fire. It is certain that before Abraham was cast into the fire, there was no opportunity for such dialogues; fundamentally, the idolaters did not grant him the right to such a debate. They viewed Abraham as a criminal and sinner who must be punished as soon as possible for his actions and his uprising against the sacred gods. At that time, they only questioned his motive for breaking the idols and then, with extreme anger and violence, issued the order to burn him. But when he was miraculously saved from the fire, he was able to, so to speak, enter Nimrod's presence and engage in debate with him.

  3. The verse clearly indicates that Nimrod was never seeking the truth in this discussion; rather, he wanted to impose his false words. Perhaps the use of the word "hajja" (disputed) is for this very purpose, as this word is often used in such contexts.

  4. The verse clearly shows that the tyrant of that time claimed divinity, not only in the sense that he should be worshipped, but he also presented himself as the creator of the universe. That is, he considered himself both the object of worship and the Creator. This is not surprising; when people prostrate before stone and wood and, in addition to worshipping them, consider them influential and partners in the affairs of the world, an opportunity arises for a "deceptive tyrant" to exploit the people's simplicity, invite them to himself, and present himself as an idol to be both worshipped and acknowledged as creator.

  5. The History of Idolatry: It is difficult to pinpoint a specific historical start date for idolatry. Rather, from the earliest times of which we are aware today, this subject has existed among human beings with degraded and low-level thoughts. In reality, idolatry is a type of distortion of the belief in God—the same belief that is part of human nature (fitrah). Since this nature has always been in man, its distortion among degraded individuals has also always existed. Therefore, it can be said that the history of idolatry is almost concurrent with the history of the emergence of man.

To explain: By virtue of his nature and creation, man has been attentive to a supernatural power. This nature was reinforced by clear arguments from the system of existence, which indicates the existence of an All-Knowing and All-Powerful Origin. Through these two paths (nature and reason), man has always been more or less acquainted with that Origin of existence. However, just as the feeling of hunger in a child will lead them to reach for things like soil if not guided in time and provided with healthy food—gradually becoming accustomed to it and losing their health—man, if not guided in the path of nature and reason regarding the search for God, will turn toward fabricated gods and various idols, bowing before them and attributing divine qualities to them.

On one hand, it needs no reminder that narrow-minded and foolish individuals try to see everything in a sensory mold; their thought has fundamentally not stepped beyond the realm of the perceptible. For this reason, worshipping an unseen God is difficult and burdensome for them, and they desire to cast their God into a sensory mold. When this ignorance and lack of awareness are mixed with the "nature of God-worship," it manifests as idolatry and a sensory god.

On the other hand, it is said that ancient peoples, out of a special respect for prophets and religious elders, built memorial statues of them after their death. The spirit of hero-worship found in weak and shallow-minded individuals compelled them to attribute extraordinary status and influence to those elders and subsequently to their statues, elevating them to the level of divinity. This was another source of idolatry.

Another source of idolatry was that a series of entities that were sources of blessings and benefits in human life—such as the moon, sun, fire, and water—attracted man's attention. They would bow down before these sources as a sign of gratitude without broadening their intellectual horizon to see the First Cause and the Creator of the world beyond them. These great acts of respect took the form of idolatry over time.

Of course, the root of all forms of idolatry is one thing: the intellectual degradation, ignorance, and lack of correct guidance of man in matters of seeking and knowing God, which can be well prevented through the education and guidance of the Prophets.

Ayah

أَوْ كَٱلَّذِى مَرَّ عَلَىٰ قَرْيَةٍ وَهِىَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا قَالَ أَنَّىٰ يُحْىِۦ هَـٰذِهِ ٱللَّهُ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا ۖ فَأَمَاتَهُ ٱللَّهُ مِا۟ئَةَ عَامٍ ثُمَّ بَعَثَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتَ ۖ قَالَ لَبِثْتُ يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ ۖ قَالَ بَل لَّبِثْتَ مِا۟ئَةَ عَامٍ فَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰ طَعَامِكَ وَشَرَابِكَ لَمْ يَتَسَنَّهْ ۖ وَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰ حِمَارِكَ وَلِنَجْعَلَكَ ءَايَةً لِّلنَّاسِ ۖ وَٱنظُرْ إِلَى ٱلْعِظَامِ كَيْفَ نُنشِزُهَا ثُمَّ نَكْسُوهَا لَحْمًا ۚ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُۥ قَالَ أَعْلَمُ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢٥٩)
Or him who came upon a township as it lay fallen on its trellises. He said, ‘How will Allah revive this after its death?!’ So Allah made him die for a hundred years, then He resurrected him. He said, ‘How long did you remain?’ Said he, ‘I have remained a day or part of a day.’ He said, ‘No, you have remained a hundred years. Now look at your food and drink which have not rotted! Then look at your ass! [This was done] that We may make you a sign for mankind. And now look at its bones, how We raise them up and clothe them with flesh!’ When it became evident to him, he said, ‘I know that Allah has power over all things.’ (259)

Commentary

The Wondrous Story of "Uzair"

This verse, mentioned as a conjunction to the previous verse, narrates another story of one of the former Prophets, which contains vivid evidence regarding the issues of the Resurrection. The connection between these two verses may be because the previous verse discussed Monotheism (Tawhid) and the recognition of God, while this verse and the one following it (Verse 260) discuss the Resurrection and life after death.

Some commentators have also suggested that at the end of Ayat al-Kursi, there is mention of Divine guidance; then, in the verses that follow, it points to one of the methods of guidance, which is the presentation of intellectual proofs (the story of Ibrahim عليه السلام and Nimrod). In the verse under discussion and the subsequent verse, sensory evidence is utilized, presenting the matter through the lens of witnessing (observation). [^1] In any case, it is appropriate to first briefly review the summary of the story in this verse as recorded in Islamic histories, and then proceed to the commentary of the verse.

The verse refers to the story of someone who, during his journey while riding a mount and carrying some food and drink, passed by a settlement that had collapsed and been destroyed in a terrifying manner, with the corpses and decayed bones of its inhabitants visible. When he saw this horrifying scene, he said: "How will Allah bring these dead to life?"

Of course, this statement was not out of denial or doubt, but rather out of wonder. The evidence present in the verse indicates that he was one of the Prophets عليه السلام, as we read at the end of the verse that Allah spoke to him, and narrations also confirm this fact, as we will mention later.

At that moment, Allah took his soul and brought him back to life a hundred years later, asking him: "How long have you been in this wilderness?" He, imagining that he had not tarried there for more than a short while, immediately replied: "A day or less!" He was addressed: "Nay, you have been here for a hundred years. Now look at your food and drink and see how, over this period, by the command of Allah, no change has occurred in them. But so that you may know that a hundred years have passed since your death, look at your mount and see how it has disintegrated and scattered, subjected to the ordinary laws of nature, and death has pulled it apart. Then look and see how We gather its scattered parts and bring it to life." When he saw this scene, he said: "I know that Allah is Powerful over all things," meaning: "Now my heart is at peace, and the issue of the resurrection of the dead has taken a sensory form for me."

Regarding which of the Prophets he was, various possibilities have been suggested. Some consider him to be "Jeremiah" and others "Khidr," but the most famous and well-known view is that he was "Uzair" (Ezra), and this is confirmed in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. [^1] Similarly, regarding where this settlement was, some have considered it to be Jerusalem, which was destroyed and crushed by the attacks of Nebuchadnezzar, but this possibility seems unlikely...

Now we return to the commentary of the verse.

First, He says: "Or like the one who passed by a township while it had fallen down upon its roofs, (and the corpses and bones of its people were scattered everywhere, he said out of wonder to himself:) 'How will Allah bring this to life after its death?'" (أَوْ كَالَّذِي مَرَّ عَلى‌ قَرْيَةٍ وَ هِيَ خاوِيَةٌ عَلى‌ عُرُوشِها قالَ أَنَّى يُحْيِي هذِهِ اللَّهُ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها).

"Urush" is the plural of "Arsh," which here means "roof." "Khawiyah" originally means "empty" and is used here as a metaphor for being destroyed, because inhabited houses are usually occupied, and houses that become empty are either due to destruction or will gradually be destroyed because they are empty. Therefore, the phrase "وَ هِيَ خاوِيَةٌ عَلى‌ عُرُوشِها" means that their houses had been destroyed in such a way that first their roofs had collapsed and then the walls had fallen upon them. This type of destruction is complete and total, because during destruction, usually the roof is destroyed first while the walls stand for a while, then they tumble over the ruins of the roof.

Some have specified that the concept of "Arsh" differs from "Saqf" (ceiling/roof); Saqf refers only to the upper part of a building, while Arsh consists of a roof along with its supports.

From the context, it is understood that no one was with that Prophet in this event, and when his eyes fell upon the disintegrated bones of the people of the town, he pointed to them and asked himself this question: "How will Allah bring these to life after their death?"

Continuing the verse, He says: "So Allah caused him to die for a hundred years, then He raised him. He said: 'How long have you tarried?' He said: 'I have tarried a day or part of a day.' He said: '(No) rather, you have tarried a hundred years.'" (فَأَماتَهُ اللَّهُ مِائَةَ عامٍ ثُمَّ بَعَثَهُ قالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتَ قالَ لَبِثْتُ يَوْماً أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ قالَ بَلْ لَبِثْتَ مِائَةَ عامٍ‌).

Most commentators have understood from this sentence that Allah took the soul of that Prophet and brought him back to life after a hundred years, and the word "amātahu," which comes from the root mawt meaning death, conveys this very concept.

However, some so-called "intellectual" commentators, such as the author of Al-Manar and also Al-Maraghi, insist on interpreting it as a type of sleep and loss of sensation and movement, which modern scientists call "hibernation" or "torpor," where a living being falls into a deep sleep for a long time, but the flames of life are not extinguished within it. He then adds: "What has happened so far regarding these long sleeps has not exceeded a few years; therefore, reaching a hundred years is extraordinary, but it is certain that when this matter is possible for a few years, it will also be possible for a hundred years, and what is necessary in accepting extraordinary matters is that the act be possible and not an intellectual impossibility." [^1] But apparently, there is no evidence in the verse for this claim; rather, the literal meaning of the verse is that the aforementioned Prophet passed away and resumed life after a hundred years. Of course, such death and life is a completely extraordinary matter, but in any case, it is not impossible, and on the other hand, extraordinary events in the Quran are not limited to this case such that we would need to justify or interpret it away.

Indeed, if the purpose of mentioning long sleeps of several years—or for example, what is seen in many animals as "winter sleep" where they sleep throughout the winter and wake up when the weather warms, or for example, the issue of natural freezing of some animals or artificial freezing of other living beings by humans for a long period without dying—is to show that the issue of death and life after a hundred years is considered a possible matter, then it seems to be a good point. The meaning of this statement would be: "The God who can put living beings into a long sleep or a state of freezing for hundreds of years and then wake them and return them to their original state is capable of bringing the dead to life after death."

Fundamentally, by accepting the principle of the Resurrection and the reviving of the dead, as well as accepting the extraordinary events and miracles of the Prophets, there is no reason for this insistence to interpret all the verses of the Quran into a series of ordinary and natural matters and to commit all sorts of violations of the literal meaning of the verses, because this work is neither correct nor necessary! As some commentators have said, it is as if we have forgotten that in the beginning, we were inanimate beings, then Allah brought us to life; what is there to prevent such death and life from being repeated?

The phrase "لَبِثْتُ يَوْماً أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ‌" shows that the Prophet's death and his revival occurred at two different hours of the day; for example, his death was before noon and his revival was in the afternoon, and therefore he was in doubt whether a full day and night had passed or only a few hours of a day. For this reason, he said with hesitation, "a day or part of a day," but he was soon addressed that no, rather you have remained here for a hundred years.

Then, so that the Prophet would gain greater certainty in this matter, he was commanded to look at the food and drink he had with him, as well as his mount; the former had remained completely healthy while the latter had entirely disintegrated, so that he might observe both the passage of time and the power of Allah to preserve whatever He wills. He says: He was told, "So now look at your food and your drink (which you had with you, see that with the passing of years) it has not changed." (فَانْظُرْ إِلى‌ طَعامِكَ وَ شَرابِكَ لَمْ يَتَسَنَّهْ‌). [^1] "Lam yatasannah" is from the root "sanah" meaning "year," meaning a year has not passed over it, but here it is a metaphor for the absence of change and not spoiling. That is, look at them, for despite the passage of all those years, it is as if no year or time has passed over them and no change has occurred in them. This points to the fact that the God who can preserve your food and drink—which normally should spoil quickly—in its original state, it is not difficult for Him to bring the dead to life. For the continuation of the life of such perishable food, whose lifespan is usually very short, over this long period is no simpler than reviving the dead. [^2] What this food and drink were is not reflected in the verse; some have mentioned his food as figs and his drink as a type of fruit juice, and we know that both spoil very quickly, and their preservation over a long period is a significant matter. Some have also mentioned them as grapes and milk.

Then He adds: "But look at your donkey (how it has disintegrated, so that you may gain certainty in life after death) and that We may make you a sign for the people," We shall bring it to life (وَ انْظُرْ إِلى‌ حِمارِكَ وَ لِنَجْعَلَكَ آيَةً لِلنَّاسِ‌).

Of course, the Quran says nothing more than this about the Prophet's mount, but it is understood from the subsequent sentences that his mount had completely disintegrated with the passage of time and only decayed bones remained of it; otherwise, there would have been no sign of the passage of a hundred years. This itself is wondrous: that an animal, which has the possibility of a relatively long life, should thus disintegrate, while fruit and fruit juice or milk, which should spoil rapidly, undergo not the slightest change in taste or smell. This is the ultimate demonstration of Allah's power in the matter of life and death.

In any case, in completing this very issue, He adds: "Look at the bones (that remain from your mount) and see how We raise them and join them together and clothe them with flesh." (وَ انْظُرْ إِلَى الْعِظامِ كَيْفَ نُنْشِزُها ثُمَّ نَكْسُوها لَحْماً).

"Nunshizuha" is from the root "nushūz" meaning to rise and become high, and here it means lifting them from the ground and joining them to one another. That is, look at how We join them together and clothe them with flesh and bring them to life.

The fact that some commentators have suggested that the bones referred to are the Prophet's own bones seems very strange and unlikely, because these conversations take place after his revival. Similarly, the possibility of referring to the decayed bones of the inhabitants of that town [^1] is unlikely, because before this sentence, the discussion was about the donkey and the mount, not the people of the town.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "When (everything) became clear to him by observing these clear signs, he said: 'I know that Allah is Powerful over all things.'" (فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ قالَ أَعْلَمُ أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

Meaning: "My knowledge and awareness became complete and reached the stage of witnessing." It is noteworthy that he did not say "Now I have come to know" (like the statement of Zulaikha regarding Yusuf: "الْآنَ حَصْحَصَ الْحَقُ‌": "Now the truth has become manifest" [^2]), but rather he said: "I know," meaning "I confess to my knowledge and awareness."

[^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, under the verse discussed. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 370. [^1]: Tafsir al-Manar and al-Maraghi (under the verse discussed). [^1]: Regarding whether "lam yatasannah" is derived from the root "sanah," many commentators such as Tabarsi, Fakhr Razi, Qurtubi, Bursawi in Ruh al-Bayan, and Abu al-Futuh Razi are in agreement. Raghib in al-Mufradat has pointed to this meaning under the root "sanah," although he interpreted it differently under the root "sann." [^2]: It should be noted that if the phrase "lam yatasannah" is from the root "sanah," the hā is original; if it is from the root "sann" or "sanw," the hā is for silence (hā al-sakt). In any case, the verb is mentioned in the singular form, while the hidden pronoun within it refers to "food and drink" and should have been dual. However, since the reference here is to the category of food and drink, they are all considered as one thing and mentioned in the singular form. [^1]: Al-Kashshaf, Vol. 1, p. 307. [^2]: Surah Yusuf, Verse 51.

Ayah

وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ رَبِّ أَرِنِى كَيْفَ تُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۖ قَالَ أَوَلَمْ تُؤْمِن ۖ قَالَ بَلَىٰ وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَطْمَئِنَّ قَلْبِى ۖ قَالَ فَخُذْ أَرْبَعَةً مِّنَ ٱلطَّيْرِ فَصُرْهُنَّ إِلَيْكَ ثُمَّ ٱجْعَلْ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ جَبَلٍ مِّنْهُنَّ جُزْءًا ثُمَّ ٱدْعُهُنَّ يَأْتِينَكَ سَعْيًا ۚ وَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٢٦٠)
And when Abraham said, ‘My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead,’ He said, ‘Do you not believe?’ He said, ‘Yes indeed, but in order that my heart may be at rest.’ He said, ‘Catch four of the birds. Then cut them into pieces, and place a part of them on every mountain, then call them; they will come to you hastening. And know that Allah is all-mighty and all-wise.’ (260)

Commentary

Another Scene of Resurrection in This World

Following the story of Uzair regarding the issue of Resurrection, another story about Ibrahim (peace be upon him) is presented here to complete that discussion.

Most commentators and historians have narrated the following story under this verse:

One day, Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was passing by a sea and saw a carcass lying by the shore, with part of it in the water and part on land. Sea creatures and land animals from both sides were preying upon it, sometimes even fighting each other over it. Seeing this scene prompted Ibrahim to think about a matter that everyone wishes to understand in detail: the quality of how the dead are brought back to life after death. He thought that if a similar event were to happen to a human corpse and its body became part of the bodies of other living creatures, how would the Resurrection—which must occur with this same physical body—take place? Ibrahim (peace be upon him) said: "My Lord! Show me how You give life to the dead."

Allah said: "Do you not believe?"

He replied: "I do believe, but [I ask] so that my heart may be at ease."

Allah commanded him to take four birds, slaughter them, mix their flesh together, then divide them into several parts and place each part on a mountain. Then, he was to call them so he could witness the scene of Resurrection. He did so and, to his utmost amazement, saw the parts of the birds gathering from various points, coming to him, and resuming life! In contrast to this well-known narration, a commentator named "Abu Muslim" expressed another view, which the famous commentator Fakhr al-Razi included in his book. Since Abu Muslim's theory, despite being contrary to the views of other commentators, has been supported by a contemporary commentator (the author of Tafsir al-Manar), we shall mention it.

He says: The verse does not indicate in any way that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) killed the birds and they were then brought to life by Allah's command. Rather, the verse presents an analogy to clarify the issue of Resurrection, meaning: "O Ibrahim! Take four birds and tame them to yourself, such that whenever you call them, they come to you, even if you place each one on a mountain. Just as this task is easy for you, the matter of reviving the dead and gathering their scattered parts from different parts of the world is just as simple for Allah!" Therefore, the purpose of the command Allah gave to Ibrahim regarding the four birds was not for him to actually perform such an act, but merely to provide an example and a comparison. It is just like someone who wants to tell another that he can perform a certain task with ease and speed, saying: "You take a sip of water, and I will have this done," meaning it is that simple, not that the person is actually required to drink a sip of water!

Proponents of the second theory argue based on the phrase "فَصُرْهُنَّ إِلَيْكَ" (fa-surhunna ilayk), stating that when this verb is made transitive with the word "إلى" (ila), it means "to incline and make familiar." Thus, the meaning of the sentence becomes "make the said birds familiar with yourself." Furthermore, the pronouns in "صرهن" (surhunna), "منهن" (minhunna), and "ادعهن" (id'uhunna) all refer back to the birds. This would be correct if we accept the second interpretation, because according to the first interpretation, some of these pronouns refer to the birds themselves and some to their parts, which does not seem appropriate.

Of course, the "Answer" to these arguments will be given during the commentary of the verse. However, what is necessary to point out here is that the verse clearly conveys the fact that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) requested a sensory observation of the scene of Resurrection to bring peace to his heart. It is obvious that mentioning an analogy or comparison neither visualizes a scene nor brings peace of mind. In reality, Ibrahim believed in the Resurrection through reason and logic, but he wanted to perceive it through sensation and intuition as well.

Now we return to the commentary of the verse to make this truth clearer. First, He says: "And [recall] when Ibrahim said, 'My Lord, show me how You give life to the dead.'" (وَ إِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ رَبِّ أَرِنِي كَيْفَ تُحْيِ الْمَوْتى‌).

From the phrase "أَرِنِي كَيْفَ ..." ("Show me how..."), it is clearly understood that he wanted to strengthen his faith through vision and intuition, specifically regarding the how of the Resurrection, not its essence. This is why we read in previous verses that he explicitly said to Nimrod: "My Lord is He who gives life and causes death."

For this reason, in the continuation of this dialogue, when Allah "said, 'Have you not believed?'" (قالَ أَ وَ لَمْ تُؤْمِنْ‌).

He replied in response: "Yes [I have believed], but [I ask] so that my heart may be at ease." (قالَ بَلى‌ وَ لكِنْ لِيَطْمَئِنَّ قَلْبِي‌).

It is as if Allah wanted Ibrahim's request not to be perceived by people as a wavering of faith; therefore, he was asked "Have you not believed?" so that he could provide this explanation and no misunderstanding would arise for anyone.

Additionally, it is inferred from this sentence that scientific and logical reasoning may bring certainty, but it might not bring peace of mind, because reasoning satisfies the human intellect but often does not penetrate the depths of the heart and emotions. (Just as logic tells a person that a dead body can do nothing, yet some people are afraid of the dead, especially at night and in solitude, and cannot stay beside a corpse, because the aforementioned logic has not penetrated the depths of their being. However, those who constantly deal with the dead and are engaged in washing, shrouding, and burying them never have such fear).

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 2, p. 304

In any case, what satisfies both the intellect and the heart is visual intuition, and this is an important subject that should be explained further in its proper place! The expression of "tranquility" and "peace" shows that human thoughts, before reaching the stage of intuition, are constantly in motion, wandering, and fluctuating, but once they reach the stage of intuition, they become calm and established.

Here, Ibrahim is commanded to take an extraordinary action to reach his goal, as the Quran describes in the continuation of this verse. Allah said:

"Then take four birds and (after slaughtering them) cut them into pieces (and mix them), then place a part of them on each mountain; then call them—they will come to you in haste." (قالَ فَخُذْ أَرْبَعَةً مِنَ الطَّيْرِ فَصُرْهُنَّ إِلَيْكَ ثُمَّ اجْعَلْ عَلى‌ كُلِّ جَبَلٍ مِنْهُنَّ جُزْءاً ثُمَّ ادْعُهُنَّ يَأْتِينَكَ سَعْياً).

See this "and know that Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise." (وَ اعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ‌).

He both knows all the particles of the bodies of the dead perfectly and has the power to gather them.

The word "صرهن" (surhunna) is originally derived from the root sawr (rhyming with ghawr), which means "to cut," "to incline," and "to cry out." Among these three meanings, the first one is intended here, meaning: take four birds, slaughter them, cut them into pieces, and mix them together.

This is because the goal was for him to witness with his own eyes an example of the Resurrection and the reviving of the dead when the parts of their bodies have disintegrated, every particle has fallen into a corner, and has mixed with other particles from other bodies.

Those who have taken the phrase "فَصُرْهُنَّ إِلَيْكَ‌" to mean taming and inclining the birds have seemingly overlooked the concept of the word "جزءا" (juz'an - a part) as well as the primary objective of this act.

Ibrahim (peace be upon him) did this and called them. At that moment, the scattered parts of each bird separated, gathered, joined together, and resumed life. This event showed Ibrahim (peace be upon him) that this same scene, on a much larger scale, will take place at the Resurrection.

Some have tried to infer from the word "سعيا" (sa'yan) that the birds did not fly after being revived but ran toward Ibrahim on their feet, because sa'y in the Arabic language usually means "walking fast." It is also narrated from "Khalil ibn Ahmad," the famous Arabic linguist, that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was walking when the birds came toward him (meaning sa'yan is a state for Ibrahim, not the birds). [1] However, the context indicates that sa'yan here is a metaphor for rapid flight.

Key Points

1. A Supernatural Event:

Undoubtedly, this event that occurred regarding the birds was a completely supernatural matter, just as the process of the Day of Judgment and Resurrection is supernatural. We know that Allah is the Ruler over the laws of nature, not subject to them; therefore, performing such supernatural acts is not a complex matter for Him. As we previously mentioned, the insistence of some modernist commentators to abandon the well-known interpretation here and claim that absolutely no slaughtering or cutting occurred—but rather that the birds were to be tamed and inclined while alive and then called—is a very weak and flimsy argument. it bears no proportion to the issue of Resurrection, nor to the story of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) witnessing the scene by the sea and subsequently requesting to witness the scene of Resurrection.

It is noteworthy that, according to "Fakhr al-Razi," all Muslim commentators are in agreement regarding the well-known interpretation except for Abu Muslim, who denied it. [2]

2. Four Different Birds:

There is no doubt that the four aforementioned birds were of four different types, because otherwise, Ibrahim's goal—which was the return of the parts of each bird to its original body—would not have been fulfilled. According to some well-known narrations, these four birds were the "peacock," the "rooster," the "pigeon," and the "crow," which differ greatly from one another in various aspects. Some consider them symbols of different human temperaments and traits: the peacock as a symbol of vanity, beauty, and arrogance; the rooster as a symbol of intense sexual desires; the pigeon as a symbol of play and amusement; and the crow as a symbol of far-reaching desires!

3. The Number of Mountains:

The number of mountains upon which Ibrahim placed the parts of the birds is not explicitly mentioned in the Quran, but in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), they are identified as ten. For this reason, we read in narrations: if someone leaves a will that a "portion" (juz') of their wealth be spent on a certain cause without specifying the amount, giving one-tenth is sufficient. [3]

4. When Did This Event Occur?

Did it happen while Ibrahim was in Babylon or after his arrival in Sham (the Levant)? It appears to have been after his arrival in Sham, because the land of Babylon has no mountains.

5. Physical Resurrection:

Most of the verses in the Holy Quran regarding the Resurrection are explanations and descriptions of "Physical Resurrection." Fundamentally, those familiar with the verses of Resurrection in the Quran know that Resurrection is not presented in the Quran except in the form of "Physical Resurrection," meaning that at the time of Resurrection, both this "body" returns and the "soul and spirit." Therefore, it is expressed in the Quran as "giving life to the dead" (ihya al-mawta), and if Resurrection were only spiritual, "giving life" would have no meaning at all.

The verse under discussion also explicitly raises the subject of the return of the scattered parts of this very body, an example of which Ibrahim (peace be upon him) saw with his own eyes.

6. The Doubt of the Eater and the Eaten:

From the explanation we previously mentioned regarding the motivation for Ibrahim's request to witness the scene of reviving the dead (the story of the animal carcass falling by the sea and being eaten by sea and land animals), it is inferred that Ibrahim's primary focus in this request was: how can the body of an animal that has become part of the bodies of other animals return to its original form? This is what we refer to in the science of theology (Kalam) as the "Doubt of the Eater and the Eaten" (shubhat al-akil wa al-ma'kul).

To explain: In the Resurrection, Allah returns the human with this same material body; in other words, both the human body and the human soul return.

Now this "Question" arises: if a human body turns to dust and, through the roots of trees, becomes part of a plant or fruit, and another human eats it and it becomes part of their body—or, for example, if during years of famine, a human feeds on the flesh of another human's body—at the time of Resurrection, which of the two bodies will the consumed parts belong to? If they belong to the first body, the second body will be incomplete, and if, conversely, they remain part of the second body, the first will be incomplete or destroyed.

Answer

Philosophers and theologians have provided various answers to this ancient objection, all of which need not be discussed here.

Some scholars, unable to find a convincing answer, have interpreted and allegorized the verses related to Physical Resurrection, limiting human personality to the soul and its spiritual attributes. However, human personality is not solely dependent on the soul, nor are the verses related to Physical Resurrection such that they can be allegorized; rather, as we said, they are completely explicit in this meaning.

Some have also posited a type of seemingly physical resurrection that does not differ much from spiritual resurrection. However, there is a clearer path here based on the texts of the verses, which is also completely compatible with modern sciences, and its explanation requires several premises:

  1. We know that the parts of the human body change many times from childhood until death. Even brain cells, although they do not increase or decrease in number, still change in terms of their components, because they "feed" on one hand and "dissolve" on the other, which leads to their complete replacement over time. In short, in a period of less than ten years, almost none of the previous particles of the human body remain.

However, it must be noted that the previous particles, when they are on the verge of death, pass all their properties and effects to the new and fresh cells. For this reason, the physical characteristics of a human—from color, shape, and appearance to other physical qualities—remain constant over time. This is only because of the transfer of traits to the new cells (observe carefully).

Therefore, the final parts of every human body that turn to dust after death possess the set of traits acquired throughout life and are a living history of the physical journey of the human throughout their entire life!

  1. It is true that the human soul forms the basis of human personality, but it must be noted that the "soul" develops and evolves alongside the "body," and both have a mutual influence on each other. Therefore, just as no two bodies are identical in every respect, no two souls will be identical in every respect.

For this reason, no soul can have full and extensive activity without the body with which it developed and evolved. Thus, in the Resurrection, the same previous body must return so that the soul, by joining it, can resume its activity at a higher stage and benefit from the results of the deeds it performed.

  1. Every single particle of the human body contains all of their physical characteristics. That is, if we could truly cultivate any of the body's cells to become a complete human, that human would possess all the personal traits taken from the individual from whom this part was derived (observe carefully).

Was there more than one cell on the first day? That single "sperm cell" contained all their traits and gradually, through division, turned into two cells, and two cells into four, and in this way, all the cells of the human body came into existence. Therefore, every cell of the human body is a branch of the first cell, which, if cultivated like it, would produce a human similar to it in every respect, possessing its exact traits.

Now, considering the three aforementioned premises, we address the original objection: The verses of the Quran explicitly state: the final particles present in the human body at the time of death return to that same body on the Day of Resurrection. [4] Therefore, if another human fed on them, these parts leave their body and return to the body of the original owner. The only thing here is that the second body will presumably be incomplete, but it must be said that it does not actually become incomplete; rather, it becomes smaller. This is because the parts of that body were scattered throughout the second body, and when they are taken from it, the total second body becomes thinner and smaller in the same proportion. For example, a sixty-kilogram human might lose forty kilograms of their body weight that belonged to another, and only a small body the size of a child would remain of them.

But can this issue create a problem? Certainly not, because this small body contains all the traits of the second person without any deficiency, and at the time of Resurrection, it develops just like a child who is small and then grows large, and is resurrected as a complete human. This type of evolution and development at the time of Resurrection has no intellectual or traditional (naqli) problem.

Is this development at the time of Resurrection instantaneous or gradual? It is not clear to us, but we know this much: whichever it is, it produces no problem, and in both cases, the issue is resolved.

Only one question remains here: what if the entire body of a human is composed of the parts of another?

The answer to this question is also clear: such a thing is fundamentally impossible, because the issue of the "Eater and the Eaten" is predicated on a first body existing, feeding on another body, and developing. Given this, it is impossible for all the particles of the first body to be formed from the second body; we must assume a body existed beforehand to eat from another body. Therefore, the other body will certainly become a part of it, not the whole of it (observe carefully).

Based on what we have said, it becomes clear that the issue of Physical Resurrection with this same body produces no problem whatsoever, and we have no need to allegorize the verses that explicitly prove this matter.

[1]: Al-Bahr al-Muhit, Vol. 2, p. 300, under the verse discussed. [2]: Tafsir al-Kabir, Vol. 7, p. 41. [3]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 278. [4]: Refer to the verses that say: "People are raised from their graves."

Ayah

مَّثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ حَبَّةٍ أَنۢبَتَتْ سَبْعَ سَنَابِلَ فِى كُلِّ سُنۢبُلَةٍ مِّا۟ئَةُ حَبَّةٍ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُضَـٰعِفُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٦١)
The parable of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is that of a grain which grows seven ears, in every ear a hundred grains. Allah enhances severalfold whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. (261)

Commentary

The Beginning of the Verses on Spending—Spending is the Means of Human Growth!

The issue of spending (infaq) is one of the most important matters emphasized by Islam, and the Holy Quran has placed great stress upon it. The above verse is the first in a series of verses in Surah Al-Baqarah that speak about spending. Perhaps their mention immediately following the verses related to the Resurrection (Ma'ad) is because one of the most significant means of salvation on the Day of Judgment is spending and generosity in the way of Allah.

Some have also suggested that these verses are linked to the verses of Jihad and spending in the way of Jihad, which appeared earlier in this same Surah before the verses concerning the Resurrection and Monotheism (Tawhid).

First, It states: "The likeness of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is as the likeness of a grain that sprouts seven ears" (مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ يُنْفِقُونَ أَمْوالَهُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ حَبَّةٍ أَنْبَتَتْ سَبْعَ سَنابِلَ).

"And in every ear a hundred grains," such that a total of seven hundred grains arise from a single grain (فِي كُلِّ سُنْبُلَةٍ مِائَةُ حَبَّةٍ).

Furthermore, their reward is not limited to this; rather: "Allah multiplies it for whom He wills (and for those in whom He sees merit in their spending regarding intention, sincerity, quality, and quantity), twofold or several times over" (وَ اللَّهُ يُضاعِفُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ).

And such a vast reward from Allah is not surprising, "for He is All-Encompassing (in mercy and power) and All-Knowing (of all things)" (وَ اللَّهُ واسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ).

Some commentators have said that the "spending" intended in the above verse refers only to spending in the way of Jihad, as this verse is actually an emphasis on what appeared in previous verses before the stories of Uzair, Ibrahim, and Talut. However, the truth is that the concept of the verse is broad, and even its connection to previous verses cannot be a reason for restricting this verse and the subsequent ones. This is because "in the way of Allah" (fi sabilillah) has a wide meaning that encompasses every virtuous expenditure. Additionally, the upcoming verses show that the discussion of spending is pursued here independently, and Islamic narrations also point toward the generalization of the verse's meaning. [^1]

A noteworthy point is that in this verse, those who spend in the way of Allah are likened to a prolific grain sown in fertile soil, whereas normally their action should be likened to this grain, not the individuals themselves. Therefore, many commentators have suggested that there is an omitted word (muqaddar) in the verse, such as the word "charity" (sadaqat) before "those who," or the word "sower" (badhir) before "a grain," and the like.

However, there is no evidence for any omission or estimation in the verse. Likening the individuals who spend to prolific grains is a beautiful and profound metaphor. It is as if the Quran wants to say: the action of every human being is a reflection of their existence, and to whatever extent the action expands, the human existence has, in reality, expanded.

In other words, the Quran does not separate man's action from his existence and considers both as different forms of a single reality. Therefore, there is no omission or estimation in the verse; it indicates that righteous people achieve spiritual growth and development in the light of their good deeds. These individuals are like fruitful seeds that spread roots and branches in every direction, bringing everything under their wing.

In short: in every simile, in addition to the tool of comparison, three things are necessary: the "subject" (mushabbah), the "object of comparison" (mushabbah bihi), and the "point of resemblance" (wajh al-shabah). Here, the "subject" is the person who spends, the "object of comparison" is the prolific seeds, and the "point of resemblance" is growth and development. We believe that the person who spends achieves extraordinary spiritual and social growth in the light of their action, and there is no need for any linguistic estimation.

A similar meaning appears in verse 265 of this same Surah.

There is also a discussion among commentators regarding the expression "sprouts seven ears, in every ear a hundred grains," which refers to a grain from which seven hundred or more grains are obtained. Is this a hypothetical comparison that has no external existence (since in the case of wheat grains, seven hundred grains have never arisen from one), or does it refer to grains like millet? Interestingly, a few years ago, which was a year of heavy rainfall, news was published in the press that in some southern cities, in certain fields, very tall wheat stalks with many ears were seen, where in some cases, about four thousand grains of wheat were counted on a single plant. This itself shows that the above comparison is a completely realistic one, not imaginary.

The word yuda'ifu is derived from the root di'f, meaning double or several times over. Given what was mentioned above—that grains are found yielding several thousand grains—this expression is also a realistic description.

Key Point: Spending is the Most Important Way to Solve the Problem of Class Disparity

One of the great social problems that humanity has always suffered from, and still faces despite all the industrial and material progress achieved, is the problem of class disparity. This means poverty, misery, and destitution on one side, and the accumulation of wealth on the other. Some amass so much wealth that they cannot keep track of it, while others suffer from poverty and deprivation to the extent that providing the basic necessities of life, such as food, shelter, and simple clothing, is impossible for them.

It is obvious that a society built partly on luxury and wealth and largely on poverty and hunger cannot endure and will never reach true happiness. In such a society, anxiety, distress, pessimism, and ultimately enmity and war are inevitable.

Although this disparity existed in human societies in the past, it must be said that unfortunately, in our time, this class gap has become far wider and more dangerous. On one hand, the doors of human aid and cooperation in the true sense have been closed to the people, while usury (riba)—which is one of the major causes of class disparity—is open to them in various forms. The emergence of Communism and the like, and the horrific bloodshed and wars, both small and large, that occurred in the last century and still continue in corners of the world—often stemming from economic roots and being a reaction to the deprivation of the majority of human societies—bear witness to this reality.

While scientists and economic schools of the world have thought of remedies to solve this great social problem and each has chosen a path—Communism through the abolition of private property, and Capitalism through heavy taxation and the establishment of public welfare institutions (which resemble formalities more than a solution to class disparity)—they believed they were fighting it. But the truth is that neither has been able to take an effective step in this path, because solving this problem is impossible with the spirit of materialism that governs the world.

By reflecting on the verses of the Holy Quran, it becomes clear that one of the goals of Islam is to eliminate the unjust differences that arise between the rich and weak classes due to social injustices, and to raise the standard of living for those who cannot meet their life's needs without the help of others, so they may have at least the basic necessities of life. Islam has considered a vast program to achieve this goal: the absolute prohibition of usury, the obligation of paying Islamic taxes such as Zakat, Khums, and Sadaqat, and the encouragement of spending (Infaq), endowments (Waqf), interest-free loans (Qard al-Hasan), and various financial aids form part of this program. Most importantly, it is the revival of the spirit of faith and human brotherhood among Muslims.

[^1]: The late "Tabarsi" in "Majma' al-Bayan," after explaining the breadth of the verse's concept, says: "And it is narrated from Abu Abdillah عليه السلام," the same has been narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام—Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 374.

Ayah

ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ لَا يُتْبِعُونَ مَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ مَنًّا وَلَآ أَذًى ۙ لَّهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٢٦٢)
Those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah and then do not follow up what they have spent with reproaches and affronts, they shall have their reward near their Lord, and they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (262)

Commentary

What Kind of Spending is Valuable?

In the previous verse, the importance of spending in the way of Allah was stated in general terms, but in the verse under discussion, some of its conditions are mentioned. (Furthermore, it is clearly understood from the expressions in this verse that it does not only refer to spending for Jihad).

It says: "Those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah and then do not follow up what they have spent with reminders [of generosity] or injury, their reward is with their Lord" (الَّذِينَ يُنْفِقُونَ أَمْوالَهُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ ثُمَّ لا يُتْبِعُونَ ما أَنْفَقُوا مَنًّا وَ لا أَذىً لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ‌). [^1] "Additionally, there is no fear upon them, nor shall they grieve" (وَ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ‌).

It is clearly understood from this verse that spending in the way of Allah is accepted in the presence of the Lord only if it is not followed by reminders of favor (Mann) or anything that causes

[^1]: "Mann" (منت) literally means a heavy weight; subsequently, it came to mean bestowing an important favor. If this is practical in nature, it is a very good thing (and Allah's favors are of this kind), but if it is verbal and involves boasting or reminding someone of the favor, it is very bad, and this is the meaning intended in the above verse.

injury and distress to the needy.

Therefore, those who bestow wealth in the way of Allah but follow it with reminders of favor or an act that causes injury and distress, in reality, destroy their reward and recompense with this unseemly act.

What draws the most attention in this verse is that the Quran does not limit human life's capital to material assets; rather, it also takes psychological and social capital into account.

One who gives something to another and then places a burden of obligation (Mann) upon him, or breaks his heart with injury, has in reality given him nothing. For if he gave him a material asset, he has also taken a psychological asset from him, and often those humiliations and spiritual breakages are far greater than the wealth bestowed upon him.

Thus, if such individuals receive no reward or recompense, it is completely natural and just; rather, it can be said that in many cases, such people are debtors rather than creditors, because a human being's dignity is far superior and higher than wealth and property.

Another point is that "reminding of favor" and "injury" are mentioned in the verse with the word "Thumma" (ثم), which is usually used for a gap between two events (known as tarakhi). Therefore, the meaning of the verse becomes: those who spend and later do not remind the recipient of the favor or cause injury, their reward is preserved with their Lord.

This indicates that the Quran's intent is not only that the act of spending should be polite, respectful, and free from reminders of favor at the time of giving, but also that in later times, one must not place a burden of obligation on the recipient by recalling it. This demonstrates the ultimate precision of Islam regarding sincere humanitarian services.

It should be noted that the reminder of favor and injury that causes the rejection of spending is not exclusive to the destitute; rather, in public and social works such as Jihad in the way of Allah and public welfare projects that require the expenditure of wealth, observing this principle is also necessary.

The phrase "their reward is with their Lord" (لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ‌) assures the spenders that their reward is preserved with the Lord so that they may step onto this path with peace of mind. For whatever is with Allah is neither in danger of destruction nor reduction; rather, the expression "their Lord" (ربهم) may imply that Allah nurtures [their deeds] and increases them.

The phrase "there is no fear upon them, nor shall they grieve" (وَ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ‌) indicates that they will have no anxiety of any kind. For "fear" (Khawf), as previously mentioned, relates to future matters, while "grief" (Huzn) relates to past matters. Therefore, considering that the reward of the spenders is preserved in the presence of Allah, they neither fear their future on the Day of Resurrection, nor do they allow grief into their hearts for what they have bestowed in the way of Allah.

Some have also said they have no fear of poverty, malice, stinginess, or being cheated, nor any grief over what they have spent.

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"Whoever bestows a kindness upon a believer and then injures him with words or reminds him of the favor, he has certainly nullified his charity." [^1] But those who have not done so harbor no fear of their spending being nullified. Islam is so precise in this regard that some early scholars said: "Whenever you spend on someone and you know that your greeting him will be difficult and heavy for him, reminding him of the memory of the gift, then do not greet him." [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 253, Hadith 1.

[^2]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Vol. 2, p. 364.

Ayah

۞ قَوْلٌ مَّعْرُوفٌ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِّن صَدَقَةٍ يَتْبَعُهَآ أَذًى ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَنِىٌّ حَلِيمٌ (٢٦٣)
An honourable reply [in response to the needy] and forgiving [their annoyance] is better than a charity followed by affront. Allah is all-sufficient, most forbearing. (263)

Commentary

Kind Treatment is Better than Charity Followed by Injury

This verse is, in fact, a completion of the previous verse regarding the abandonment of reminders of generosity and injury during charity. It states: "Kind speech (toward those in need) and forgiveness (of their harshness) are better than charity followed by injury" (قَوْلٌ مَعْرُوفٌ وَ مَغْفِرَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِنْ صَدَقَةٍ يَتْبَعُها أَذىً).

Know also that whatever you spend in the way of Allah, you are actually storing for your own salvation, "and Allah is Self-Sufficient (unaffected by it) and Forbearing (toward your harshness and ingratitude)" (وَ اللَّهُ غَنِيٌّ حَلِيمٌ).

Key Points

1- The above verse clarifies the logic of Islam regarding the social values of individuals and the dignity of people. It considers the actions of those who strive to preserve these human assets—benefiting the needy with kind words and necessary guidance while never disclosing their secrets—to be superior and higher than the charity of selfish and narrow-minded individuals who, in exchange for a small amount of help, inflict a thousand types of verbal stings upon honorable people and shatter their character. In reality, as we have mentioned, such individuals cause more harm than good; if they provide some capital, they also blow away other [human] capitals.

From what we have said above, it becomes clear that "Kind Speech" (قَوْلٌ مَعْرُوفٌ) has a broad meaning and includes every type of good word, consolation, comfort, and guidance.

Some have also said that it refers to "enjoining what is right" [1], but this possibility does not seem appropriate.

"Forgiveness" (مَغْفِرَةٌ) means "pardon and overlooking" in the face of the harshness of those in need.

Those whose cup of patience has overflowed due to the onslaught of hardships and who sometimes, without any internal desire, utter harsh words.

These individuals, in reality, wish to take revenge in this way against an unjust society that has not given them their due rights. The least compensation that society and well-to-do individuals can offer for their deprivation is to listen to their words—which are sparks from their internal fire—with tolerance and to extinguish them with gentleness.

It is obvious that enduring their harshness and forgiving their offensive behavior reduces their complexes; hence, the importance of this Islamic instruction becomes clearer.

Some have taken the word "Maghfirah" here in its original root meaning, which is "covering and concealing," and have considered this word an allusion to veiling the secrets of honorable needy people.

However, this interpretation does not contradict what we said above, because if "Maghfirah" is interpreted in its broad sense, it will encompass both "pardon and forgiveness" and "covering and concealing" the secrets of the needy.

In the commentary "Majma' al-Bayan," it is narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

"When a petitioner asks, do not interrupt his request until he finishes it, then respond to him with dignity and gentleness, either with a small gift or a beautiful refusal; for there may come to you one who is neither human nor jinn, watching how you act regarding what Allah Almighty has bestowed upon you."

In this Hadith, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله clarifies a part of the etiquette of charity, saying:

"When a needy person asks something of you, do not cut off his speech until he explains his full purpose, then answer him with dignity, etiquette, and gentleness; either provide him with something within your power or turn him away in a proper manner, for it is possible that the questioner is an angel commissioned to test you to see how you act in the face of the blessings Allah has granted you." [1]

2- The short phrases usually mentioned at the end of verses, which describe attributes of Allah, are certainly related to the content of those verses. With this point in mind, the purpose of the phrase "And Allah is Self-Sufficient, Forbearing" (وَ اللَّهُ غَنِيٌّ حَلِيمٌ) seems to be that: since man is naturally rebellious and considers himself self-sufficient upon reaching status and wealth, and this state sometimes causes his aggression and foul language toward the deprived and the poor, He says: "Self-Sufficient by Essence" is Allah. In reality, it is He who is independent of everything, and the self-sufficiency of man is actually nothing more than a mirage and should not cause his pride, rebellion, and indifference toward the poor. Furthermore, Allah is Forbearing toward the ingratitude of people; the people of faith should also be like this.

It is also possible that the mentioned phrase refers to the fact that Allah is independent of your charities, and whatever you do is for your own benefit; therefore, you have no claim of favor over anyone. Additionally, He is Forbearing toward your harshness and does not hasten in punishment so that you may wake up and reform yourselves.

[^1]: Narrated as an opinion in Tafsir al-Bahr al-Muhit, Vol. 2, p. 307. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1-2, p. 375.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُبْطِلُوا۟ صَدَقَـٰتِكُم بِٱلْمَنِّ وَٱلْأَذَىٰ كَٱلَّذِى يُنفِقُ مَالَهُۥ رِئَآءَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَا يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۖ فَمَثَلُهُۥ كَمَثَلِ صَفْوَانٍ عَلَيْهِ تُرَابٌ فَأَصَابَهُۥ وَابِلٌ فَتَرَكَهُۥ صَلْدًا ۖ لَّا يَقْدِرُونَ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ مِّمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢٦٤)
O you who have faith! Do not render your charities void by reproaches and affronts, like those who spend their wealth to be seen by people and have no faith in Allah and the Last Day. Their parable is that of a rock covered with soil: a downpour strikes it, leaving it bare. They have no power over anything of what they have earned, and Allah does not guide the faithless lot. (264)
وَمَثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ وَتَثْبِيتًا مِّنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ كَمَثَلِ جَنَّةٍۭ بِرَبْوَةٍ أَصَابَهَا وَابِلٌ فَـَٔاتَتْ أُكُلَهَا ضِعْفَيْنِ فَإِن لَّمْ يُصِبْهَا وَابِلٌ فَطَلٌّ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٢٦٥)
The parable of those who spend their wealth seeking Allah’s pleasure and to confirm themselves [in their faith], is that of a garden on a hillside: the downpour strikes it, whereupon it brings forth its fruit twofold; and if it is not a downpour that strikes it, then a shower, and Allah watches what you do. (265)

Commentary

Two Interesting Examples Regarding the Motives of Charity

In the two verses above, reference is first made to the fact that the believers must not render their charities void and ineffective through reminders of generosity (reproach) and injury.

Then, it presents two interesting parables: one for charities mixed with reproach, injury, hypocrisy, and ostentation, and another for charities that spring from the roots of sincerity and religious and human emotions.

It says: "O you who have believed, do not invalidate your charities with reminders [of generosity] or injury" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تُبْطِلُوا صَدَقَاتِكُمْ بِالْمَنِّ وَالْأَذَىٰ).

Then, it likens this act to charities accompanied by hypocrisy and ostentation, stating: "as does one who spends his wealth [only] to be seen by the people and does not believe in Allah and the Last Day" (كَالَّذِي يُنْفِقُ مَالَهُ رِئَاءَ النَّاسِ وَلَا يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ).

And then He adds: "[His example] is like that of a [large] smooth stone upon which is [a thin layer of] dust and is hit by a heavy rain [which washes away the dust and seeds] and leaves it bare. They are unable [to keep] anything of what they have earned" (فَمَثَلُهُ كَمَثَلِ صَفْوَانٍ عَلَيْهِ تُرَابٌ فَأَصَابَهُ وَابِلٌ فَتَرَكَهُ صَلْدًا لَا يَقْدِرُونَ عَلَىٰ شَيْءٍ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا). [^1] What a subtle, eloquent, and expressive description! Imagine a solid piece of stone covered by a thin layer of soil, with viable seeds scattered in that soil, exposed to the open air and sunlight. Then, a heavy, blessed rain falls upon it. Although all the means for growth and development are present here, everything is destroyed due to one deficiency. This rain does nothing but wash away that thin layer along with the seeds, scattering them and revealing the hard, impenetrable stone upon which no plant can grow, with its harsh appearance. This is because the seeds were sown in an unsuitable place; it had a polished exterior but a harsh and impenetrable interior, covered only by a thin layer of soil, whereas the cultivation of plants and trees requires deep soil ready to accept roots, store water, and nourish the plant.

Such are hypocritical acts and charities mixed with reproach and injury, which originate from hard and callous hearts; their owners derive no benefit from them, and all their efforts go to waste.

At the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah does not guide the disbelieving people" (وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكَافِرِينَ).

This indicates that Allah withdraws the grace of guidance from them because they have chosen, by their own feet, the path of disbelief, hypocrisy, reproach, and injury; such people are not worthy of guidance. In this way, hypocritical charities and those mixed with reproach and injury are all placed in the same category.

[^1]: "Safwan" (صفوان) means a large, smooth piece of stone that nothing penetrates and nothing stays upon. "Wabil" (وابل) means heavy rain with large drops; hence, it is also applied to difficult, heavy, and calamitous tasks. "Salda" (صلداً) also means a smooth stone upon which nothing grows.

In the next verse, He presents another beautiful example for the opposite of this group—those who spend in the way of Allah based on faith and sincerity. He says: "And the example of those who spend their wealth seeking the pleasure of Allah and for the certainty [of the high human virtues] within their souls is like a garden on a high ground which is hit by heavy rain [and because of its high location, it benefits sufficiently from open air and sunlight, growing such that] it yields its fruit twofold" (وَمَثَلُ الَّذِينَ يُنْفِقُونَ أَمْوَالَهُمُ ابْتِغَاءَ مَرْضَاتِ اللَّهِ وَتَثْبِيتًا مِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ كَمَثَلِ جَنَّةٍ بِرَبْوَةٍ أَصَابَهَا وَابِلٌ فَآتَتْ أُكُلَهَا ضِعْفَيْنِ).

Then He adds: "And if it is not hit by heavy rain, then a drizzle [or dew] is sufficient" and it still yields fruit and remains lush and fresh (فَإِنْ لَمْ يُصِبْهَا وَابِلٌ فَطَلٌّ). [^1] And at the end, He says: "And Allah is Seeing of what you do" (وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ).

He knows whether the charity has a divine motive or is hypocritical, whether it is mixed with reproach and injury or with love and respect.

Key Points

  1. From the phrase "do not invalidate your charities with reminders [of generosity] or injury," it is understood that certain actions can destroy the results of good deeds. This is the issue of Ibtal (nullification) or Ahbat (forfeiture), the explanation of which was provided under the commentary of verse 217 of this same Surah.

  2. Likening a hypocritical act to a piece of stone covered by a thin layer of soil is very expressive. This is because hypocrites cover their harsh and fruitless inner selves with a facade of benevolence and righteousness, performing acts that have no stable roots in their being. However, the events of life soon pull back the curtain and reveal their true nature.

[^1]: "Tall" (طل) means a fine rain that sometimes falls on plants like a mist, and it is also sometimes used to mean dew.

  1. The phrase "seeking the pleasure of Allah and for the certainty within their souls" (ابْتِغَاءَ مَرْضَاتِ اللَّهِ وَتَثْبِيتًا مِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ) explains the motives for correct and divine charity, which are two things: "seeking Allah's pleasure" and "strengthening the spirit of faith and creating tranquility in the heart and soul." This phrase says: true spenders are those who act only for the sake of Allah's pleasure, to cultivate human virtues and establish these traits within their souls, and also to end the anxiety and distress that arises in their conscience due to the sense of responsibility toward the deprived (therefore, "min" in the verse would mean "fi" [within]).

  2. The phrase "And Allah is Seeing of what you do," mentioned at the end of the second verse, is a warning to all who wish to perform a good deed to be careful not to allow the slightest contamination in terms of intention or method, for Allah is fully observant of their actions.

Ayah

أَيَوَدُّ أَحَدُكُمْ أَن تَكُونَ لَهُۥ جَنَّةٌ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَابٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ لَهُۥ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ وَأَصَابَهُ ٱلْكِبَرُ وَلَهُۥ ذُرِّيَّةٌ ضُعَفَآءُ فَأَصَابَهَآ إِعْصَارٌ فِيهِ نَارٌ فَٱحْتَرَقَتْ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٢٦٦)
Would any of you like to have a garden of palm trees and vines, with streams running in it, with all kinds of fruit for him therein, and old age were to strike him while he has weakly offspring; whereupon a fiery hurricane were to hit it, whereat it lies burnt? Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may reflect. (266)

Commentary

Another Striking Example of Charity Tainted by Hypocrisy and Reproach

In this verse, another eloquent parable is presented regarding the issue of charity mixed with hypocrisy, reproach, and injury, illustrating how these reprehensible acts destroy its effects. It says: "Would any of you wish to have a garden of palm trees and vines, with rivers flowing beneath it, and containing all kinds of fruits for him—while he is stricken with old age and has weak (young) children—then a whirlwind containing fire strikes it and it is consumed by flames and burnt?" (أَ يَوَدُّ أَحَدُكُمْ أَنْ تَكُونَ لَهُ جَنَّةٌ مِنْ نَخِيلٍ وَ أَعْنابٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ لَهُ فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ الثَّمَراتِ وَ أَصابَهُ الْكِبَرُ وَ لَهُ ذُرِّيَّةٌ ضُعَفاءُ فَأَصابَها إِعْصارٌ فِيهِ نارٌ فَاحْتَرَقَتْ).

This is a very beautiful depiction of the state of such individuals who, through hypocrisy, reproach, and injury, draw a line of cancellation over their charity. It visualizes an elderly man surrounded by small, young children, whose only means of securing their present and future life is a lush, green garden filled with palm trees, grapes, and other fruits—trees that are constantly passed by flowing water, requiring no labor for irrigation. Suddenly, a fiery whirlwind blows and turns it into ashes. What a deadly regret and sorrow such a person feels! The state of those who perform good deeds and then destroy them with hypocrisy, reproach, and injury is exactly like this. They have endured great toil, yet on the day they need its results, they find it all turned to ashes because the fiery whirlwind of hypocrisy, reproach, and injury has burnt it away.

At the end of the verse, following this eloquent and expressive parable, He says: "Thus Allah makes clear His signs to you, that perhaps you may reflect" and distinguish the path of truth from falsehood (كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمُ الْآياتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَفَكَّرُونَ).

Indeed, the source of human misfortunes—especially foolish acts like reproach and hypocrisy, whose benefit is negligible and whose harm is swift and immense—is the abandonment of thought and reflection, and Allah invites everyone to engage in it.

Key Points

  1. These successive parables, each more expressive and subtle than the last, are all related to agricultural matters. This is because they were instructive not only for the people of Medina—where these verses were revealed and who were an agricultural people—but also for all people of the world, as agricultural matters constitute a part of their lives in any case.

  2. From the phrase "وَ أَصابَهُ الْكِبَرُ وَ لَهُ ذُرِّيَّةٌ ضُعَفاءُ" (the owner of that garden being old and having small, weak children), it is understood that charity and spending in the way of Allah and helping the needy is like a lush garden from whose fruits both the individual himself and his children benefit. Conversely, hypocrisy, reproach, and injury cause deprivation for both himself and future generations who should have benefited from the fruits and blessings of his good deeds. This itself is evidence that future generations share in the results of the good deeds of past generations. Socially, this is also true, as the popularity and trust that fathers gain in public opinion due to good deeds will be a great asset for their children.

  3. The phrase "إِعْصارٌ فِيهِ نارٌ" (a whirlwind in which there is fire) may refer to whirlwinds caused by "Samum" winds—scorching and drying winds—or a whirlwind that passes over a heap of fire and, as is typical for whirlwinds to carry whatever they find in their path, lifts it from the ground and scatters it elsewhere. It may also refer to a whirlwind accompanied by a lightning strike that hits a spot and turns everything into ashes. In any case, it refers to rapid and absolute destruction. [1]

[1]: "I'sar" (إِعْصار) literally means a whirlwind that forms when winds blow from two opposite directions, taking a vertical shape with one end touching the ground and the other in the sky, sometimes carrying many objects with it.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنفِقُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا كَسَبْتُمْ وَمِمَّآ أَخْرَجْنَا لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَلَا تَيَمَّمُوا۟ ٱلْخَبِيثَ مِنْهُ تُنفِقُونَ وَلَسْتُم بِـَٔاخِذِيهِ إِلَّآ أَن تُغْمِضُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ (٢٦٧)
O you who have faith! Spend of the good things you have earned [through trade and the like] and of what We bring forth for you from the earth, and do not be of the mind to give the bad part of it, for you yourselves would not take it, unless you ignore. And know that Allah is all-sufficient, all-laudable. (267)

Occasion of Revelation
