It has been narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that this verse was revealed concerning a group of people who had accumulated wealth through usury (riba) during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) and were spending from it in the way of Allah. Allah forbade them from this action and commanded them to spend from pure and lawful (halal) wealth in His way.

In the commentary Majma' al-Bayan, after citing this hadith, it is narrated from Ali عليه السلام that he said: "This verse was revealed concerning those who, when giving charity, would mix dry, thin, and low-quality dates with good dates and then spend them. They were commanded to refrain from this practice."

These two occasions of revelation are in no way contradictory; it is possible that the verse was revealed regarding both groups—one referring to spiritual purity (lawfulness) and the other to material and outward quality.

However, it should be noted that according to Verse 275 of Surah Al-Baqarah, those who had accumulated wealth through usury during the Age of Ignorance and ceased doing so after the verse was revealed were not considered to have possessed that past wealth unlawfully; meaning, the law did not apply retroactively. Nevertheless, it is certain that such wealth, while technically permissible, differed from other wealth and was, in reality, similar to wealth acquired through discouraged (makruh) means.

Commentary

From what wealth should one spend?

In response to the previous verse, the fruits of spending (infaq), the qualities of those who spend, and the actions that might taint this humane and God-pleasing deed and destroy its reward were explained. In this verse—which is the sixth in this series—the discussion turns to the nature of the wealth that should be spent.

First, He says: "O you who have believed! Spend from the good things which you have earned (through trade) and from that which We have brought forth for you from the earth (from underground resources, minerals, agriculture, farming, and orchards)" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْفِقُوا مِنْ طَيِّبَاتِ مَا كَسَبْتُمْ وَمِمَّا أَخْرَجْنَا لَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ).

The phrase "مَا كَسَبْتُمْ" (what you have earned) refers to commercial income, and the phrase "مِمَّا أَخْرَجْنَا لَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ" (from what We have brought forth for you from the earth) refers to all types of agricultural and farming income, as well as underground minerals. Therefore, it encompasses all types of income, because the source of all wealth that man possesses is the earth and its various resources; even industries and livestock farming derive their essence from the earth.

This expression also subtly points to the fact that We have placed these resources at your disposal; therefore, you should not hesitate to spend a portion of the "good things" and the "prime choice" of it in the way of Allah.

Then, for further emphasis, He adds: "And do not aim toward the defective therefrom to spend [from that] while you would not take it [yourselves] except with closed eyes (with disdain and reluctance)" (وَلَا تَيَمَّمُوا الْخَبِيثَ مِنْهُ تُنْفِقُونَ وَلَسْتُمْ بِآخِذِيهِ إِلَّا أَنْ تُغْمِضُوا فِيهِ). [^1] Since some people have the habit of always spending from worthless property and that which is nearly obsolete or unusable for themselves—and such spending, besides being of little benefit to the needy, is a form of insult and humiliation toward them and does not contribute to the spiritual training and cultivation of the human soul—this sentence explicitly forbids people from this act and accompanies it with a subtle reason: You yourselves are not willing to accept such goods except with reluctance and necessity; why then, regarding your Muslim brothers, and even higher, regarding God in whose way you spend and from whom everything you have originates, do you settle for this?

In reality, the verse points to a delicate point: that in spending for the sake of Allah, one side is the needy believers and the other side is Allah. Thus, if lowly and worthless goods are chosen, on one hand, it is a humiliation toward the needy—who, despite their poverty, may hold a high rank in faith and humanity and whose spirits may be grieved—and on the other hand, it is a lack of etiquette toward the Sublime Station of the Lord.

The phrase "لَا تَيَمَّمُوا" (do not aim/intend) may indicate that if, among the wealth you spend, something of low quality is included unintentionally, it is not subject to this warning. The point is that you should not deliberately set out to do such a thing.

The term "طَيِّبَاتِ" (good/pure things) includes both outward purity—meaning value for consumption—and spiritual purity—meaning wealth that is not doubtful or forbidden. This is because believers are reluctant to accept any of these, and the phrase "إِلَّا أَنْ تُغْمِضُوا فِيهِ" (except that you close your eyes to it) covers all of them. The view of some commentators who restricted it to only one of these two does not seem correct.

A similar verse appears in Surah Al-Imran, Verse 92, where He says: "Never will you attain the good [reward] until you spend [in the way of Allah] from that which you love" (لَنْ تَنَالُوا الْبِرَّ حَتَّىٰ تُنْفِقُوا مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ). Of course, that verse relies more on the spiritual effects of spending.

At the end of the verse, He says: "And know that Allah is Free of need and Praiseworthy" (وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ غَنِيٌّ حَمِيدٌ).

This means that not only does He have no need for your spending and is Self-Sufficient in every respect, but He is the one who has placed all blessings at your disposal, and therefore He is Hamid (Praiseworthy) and deserving of all praise.

Some have suggested that "حميد" here might be in the sense of the active participle (the One who praises) rather than the passive (the One who is praised); meaning that while He is independent of your spending, He praises you for the pure wealth that you spend.

Key Point

There is no doubt that spending in the way of Allah is for the purpose of seeking proximity to His Pure Essence. When people wish to seek proximity to kings and great personalities, they bring their best possessions as gifts and presents, even though those are weak humans like themselves. How then is it possible for a human to seek proximity to the Great God, to whom the entire universe belongs, using worthless and obsolete goods? The fact that we see in the obligatory Zakat and even in the sacrificial animal (Qurbani) that one must not use an inferior type also falls within this same context. In any case, this Quranic culture must be revived among all Muslims: to choose the best for spending.

[^1]: "Tayamum" originally means to intend or aim for something; here it is used in that same sense. The well-known "Tayamum" (dry ablution) is called so because a person intends to use the pure soil of the earth, as the Quran says: "فَتَيَمَّمُوا صَعِيداً طَيِّباً" (Surah An-Nisa, 43).

Ayah

ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ يَعِدُكُمُ ٱلْفَقْرَ وَيَأْمُرُكُم بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَعِدُكُم مَّغْفِرَةً مِّنْهُ وَفَضْلًا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٦٨)
Satan frightens you of poverty and prompts you to [commit] indecent acts. But Allah promises you His forgiveness and bounty, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. (268)

Commentary

Combating the Obstacles to Spending

In continuation of the verses regarding spending (infaq), the text here addresses one of its major obstacles: the satanic whispers concerning spending. These whispers frighten man with the prospect of poverty and destitution, especially if he spends good and useful portions of his wealth. Often, these satanic whispers prevent recommended spending in the way of Allah and even obligatory spending such as Zakat and Khums.

In this regard, He says: "Satan threatens you with poverty when you spend" (الشَّيْطانُ يَعِدُكُمُ الْفَقْرَ).

He says: Do not forget to secure the future of yourself and your children; look toward tomorrow from today; what is permissible for yourself is not permissible for others; and similar misleading whispers. Furthermore, "he commands you to commit indecency and sin" (وَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ بِالْفَحْشاءِ).

"Fahsha" means every ugly and highly loathsome act. Here, in accordance with the context of the discussion, it refers to stinginess and the abandonment of spending, which in many cases is a type of disobedience and sin (although the word fahsha is sometimes used to mean the sin of unchastity, we know it does not have much relevance here). Some commentators have even explicitly stated that the Arabs call a stingy person "fahish." [^1] It is also possible that fahsha here means choosing unusable goods for spending. It has also been said that it refers to any sin, because through the fear of poverty and destitution, Satan compels man to acquire wealth through various illegitimate means.

The expression of Satan "commanding" refers to those very whispers of his. Fundamentally, the source of every negative, deterrent, and short-sighted thought is submission to satanic whispers. Conversely, the source of every positive, constructive thought imbued with magnanimity is Divine inspiration and the pure, God-given primordial nature (fitrah).

In explaining this statement, it must be said: at first glance, spending and bestowing wealth is nothing but "diminishing" wealth, and this is the very short-sighted satanic view. However, with precision and a broad vision, we see that spending is the guarantor of the survival of society and the consolidation of social justice; it is the cause of reducing class disparity and achieving collective and public progress. It is certain that with the progress of society, the individuals living within that society will also enjoy welfare and comfort, and this is the very realistic Divine view.

In this way, the Quran draws the attention of Muslims to the fact that if spending seemingly reduces something from you, in reality, it adds things to your capital, both spiritually and materially.

In today's world, where the results and effects of class differences and the squandering of wealth due to the disruption of the balance of wealth distribution are clearly visible, understanding the meaning of the above verse is not very difficult.

Furthermore, it is inferred from the verse that a type of connection exists between "abandoning spending" and "fahsha." Of course, if fahsha means stinginess, its connection is from the aspect that abandoning spending and financial gifts gradually creates the trait of "stinginess"—which is among the worst traits—within man. If we take fahsha to mean absolute sin or sexual sin, its connection with the abandonment of spending is still hidden from no one, for the source of many sins, unchastity, and self-prostitution is poverty and destitution. In addition to all this, spending has a series of spiritual effects and blessings that cannot be denied.

Then He adds: "Allah promises you forgiveness from Himself and bounty" (وَ اللَّهُ يَعِدُكُمْ مَغْفِرَةً مِنْهُ وَ فَضْلًا).

In the commentary Majma' al-Bayan, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that: "When spending, two things are from Allah and two things are from Satan. Those from Allah are 'forgiveness of sins' and 'expansion and increase of wealth,' and those from Satan are the promise of poverty and destitution and the command to indecency."

Therefore, "forgiveness" (maghfirah) refers to the pardoning of sins, and "bounty" (fadl)—as narrated from Ibn Abbas—refers to the increase of capital in the light of spending.

It is noteworthy that it is narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, who said: "When you fall into hardship and destitution, trade with Allah by means of spending" (spend so that you may be saved from destitution). [^1] At the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah is All-Encompassing, All-Knowing" (وَ اللَّهُ واسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ).

[^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 1, p. 431 (under the verse being discussed). [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 258.

This is an allusion to the reality that since Allah possesses vast power and infinite knowledge, He is able to fulfill His promise. Therefore, one should be heartened by His promise, not the promise of the "deceitful" and "weak" Satan who drags man toward sin. Since he is unaware of the future and possesses no power, his promise will be nothing but misguidance and the encouragement of ignorance.

Ayah

يُؤْتِى ٱلْحِكْمَةَ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُؤْتَ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ فَقَدْ أُوتِىَ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا ۗ وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّآ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (٢٦٩)
He grants wisdom to whomever He wishes, and he, who is given wisdom, is certainly given an abundant good, and none takes admonition except those who possess intellect. (269)

Commentary

The Greatest of Divine Blessings

Considering what was mentioned in the previous verse—that during the act of charity, satanic whispers regarding poverty and Divine attractions regarding forgiveness and grace pull a person in different directions—the verse under discussion speaks of wisdom, gnosis, and knowledge. This is because only wisdom can distinguish between these two pulls of the Divine and the Satanic, leading man toward the valley of forgiveness and grace while delivering him from the misleading whispers of the fear of poverty.

In other words, due to their purity and struggle against the self (jihad al-nafs), Allah grants certain individuals a type of knowledge and insight that allows them to perceive the effects and benefits of Divine obedience—including charity and its vital role in society—and to distinguish it from satanic whispers.

He says: "He grants wisdom to whom He wills (and deems worthy)" (يُؤْتِي الْحِكْمَةَ مَنْ يَشاءُ).

In the commentary of "wisdom" (hikmah), many meanings have been mentioned, including: "gnosis and recognition of the secrets of the universe," "awareness of the realities of the Quran," "attaining the truth in terms of speech and action," "gnosis and recognition of God," and "that Divine light which separates satanic whispers from Divine inspirations."

It appears that wisdom has a broad meaning that encompasses all these matters, even prophethood, which some have listed as one of its meanings, as it is a form of knowledge and awareness. Originally, it is derived from the root h-k-m (meaning to prevent or restrain); since knowledge, insight, and prudence restrain man from wrong actions, they are called hikmah.

It is evident that the phrase "whom He wills" (مَنْ يَشاءُ) does not mean that Allah grants wisdom and knowledge to this or that person without any cause. Rather, Allah's will and providence are everywhere intertwined with the merits of individuals; that is, He quenches the thirst of whoever He finds worthy from this pure, life-giving spring. Then He says:

"And whoever is granted wisdom has certainly been granted much good" (وَ مَنْ يُؤْتَ الْحِكْمَةَ فَقَدْ أُوتِيَ خَيْراً كَثِيراً).

As a certain sage said: "O Lord! To whom You gave intellect, what did You not give? And to whom You did not give intellect, what did You give?"

It is noteworthy that while the Giver of wisdom is Allah, His name is not explicitly mentioned in this part of the sentence; it simply says: "Whoever is granted wisdom has been granted much good."

This expression perhaps suggests that knowledge and wisdom are inherently good; regardless of where they come from or through whom they are received, there is no difference in their goodness.

It is also noteworthy that in this sentence He says: whoever is granted knowledge and wisdom has been granted "much good" (khayran kathira), not "absolute good." This is because absolute good and salvation do not lie in knowledge alone; rather, knowledge is only one of its major factors. At the end of the verse, He says: "And none will remember except those of understanding" (وَ ما يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّا أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ).

"Remembrance" (tadhakkur) means "recalling" and "preserving sciences and knowledge" within the soul. And "al-albab" is the plural of "lubb," meaning "core" or "marrow." Since the core of anything is its best and most essential part, the "intellect" and "reason" are called "lubb."

This sentence states that only the possessors of intellect and reason preserve these truths, recall them, and benefit from them. Although all individuals (except the insane) possess an intellect, not all are called "ulul-albab." Rather, it refers to those who employ their intellect and reason, and in the light of this brilliant lamp, find the path of life and salvation.

We conclude this discussion with the words of an Islamic scholar (who likely heard this from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله): "Sometimes Allah intends to punish and penalize the people of the earth, but when He hears a teacher teaching wisdom to children, He averts the punishment from them because of this act." [1]

[1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 2, p. 1138.

Ayahs

وَمَآ أَنفَقْتُم مِّن نَّفَقَةٍ أَوْ نَذَرْتُم مِّن نَّذْرٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُهُۥ ۗ وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍ (٢٧٠)
Allah indeed knows whatever charity you may give, or vows that you may vow, and the wrongdoers have no helpers. (270)
إِن تُبْدُوا۟ ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ فَنِعِمَّا هِىَ ۖ وَإِن تُخْفُوهَا وَتُؤْتُوهَا ٱلْفُقَرَآءَ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۚ وَيُكَفِّرُ عَنكُم مِّن سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (٢٧١)
If you disclose your charities, that is well, but if you hide them and give them to the poor, that is better for you, and it will atone for some of your misdeeds, and Allah is well aware of what you do. (271)

Commentary

The Manner of Spending

Following the previous verses which discussed spending and giving in the way of Allah, choosing good quality wealth for this purpose, and accompanying it with proper ethics and sincerity, these two verses speak about the manner of spending and Allah’s knowledge regarding it.

In the first verse, He says: "Whatever you spend or whatever vows you make (in this regard), Allah surely knows it" (وَ ما أَنْفَقْتُمْ مِنْ نَفَقَةٍ أَوْ نَذَرْتُمْ مِنْ نَذْرٍ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُهُ‌).

Whether it is little or much, good or bad, acquired through lawful means or unlawful, accompanied by sincerity or tainted with hypocrisy, followed by reminders of generosity and injury or free from them, whether it is from the wealth that Allah has commanded to be spent or something you have made obligatory upon yourselves through a vow—whatever the case may be, Allah is aware of all its details and will grant its reward or punishment, for good or evil, accordingly.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "And for the wrongdoers there are no helpers" (وَ ما لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ أَنْصارٍ).

"Wrongdoers" (ظالمان) here refers to the miserly hoarders of wealth, the hypocritical spenders, and those who reproach and annoy people; Allah shall not help them, and their spending will not be a helper for them in this world or the Hereafter.

It may also refer to those who wronged the deprived and the destitute by withholding spending, thereby harming them, the society, and themselves.

Or it could refer to those who did not utilize their spending in its proper place, for "wrong" (ظلم) in its broad sense means performing any action in other than its rightful place. Since there is no contradiction between these three meanings, it is possible that all three are encompassed within the concept of the verse.

Indeed, they have no friend or helper in this world, nor any intercessor on the Day of Resurrection; and this is the characteristic of wrongdoing and oppression, in whatever face or form it appears.

Furthermore, it is inferred from this verse that making a vow (nadhr) is legitimate and must be fulfilled. This was among the practices that existed before Islam, and Islam has validated it.

In the second verse, the discussion concerns the manner of spending in terms of being open or hidden. He says: "If you disclose your charities, it is well; but if you hide them and give them to the poor, it is better for you" (إِنْ تُبْدُوا الصَّدَقاتِ فَنِعِمَّا هِيَ وَ إِنْ‌ تُخْفُوها وَ تُؤْتُوهَا الْفُقَراءَ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ‌).

"And He will atone for some of your sins (and you will be forgiven in the light of this act), and Allah is Well-Aware of what you do" (وَ يُكَفِّرُ عَنْكُمْ مِنْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ).

Key Points

1- There is no doubt that both public spending in the way of Allah and its concealment have beneficial effects. When a person spends his wealth openly in the way of Allah, if the spending is obligatory, then besides the fact that people are encouraged toward such righteous deeds, it also removes any suspicion from the person that he has failed to perform his obligatory duty.

If the spending is recommended (mustahabb), it is in fact a form of practical propagation that encourages people toward good deeds, supporting the deprived, and performing social and public welfare works.

And if the spending is done secretly and away from the eyes of the people, it is certainly less prone to hypocrisy and showing off, and there will be greater sincerity in it. Especially regarding aid to the deprived, their dignity is better preserved. Therefore, the above verse says: "Each of the two actions is good and proper in its own place."

Some commentators have said that this instruction applies only to recommended spending, and that obligatory spending such as Zakat and the like is always better to be open and public.

However, it is certain that neither of these two instructions (disclosing or hiding spending) is universal or absolute; rather, cases vary. In some instances where the encouraging effect is greater and it does not damage sincerity, it is better to disclose it. In cases where there are dignified individuals whose honor requires that the spending be done secretly, and there is fear of hypocrisy and lack of sincerity, hiding it will be better.

In some hadiths, it is explicitly stated that it is better for obligatory spending to be disclosed, while recommended spending is better performed secretly.

It is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "Separate the obligatory Zakat from your wealth openly and spend it openly, but as for spending other than Zakat, if you give it secretly, it is better." [^1] These hadiths do not contradict what we mentioned above, because performing obligatory duties is less likely to be mixed with hypocrisy, as it is a duty that everyone in an Islamic environment is compelled to perform, much like a definitive tax that everyone must pay. Therefore, disclosing it is better. But recommended spending, since it is not mandatory, might have its sincerity of intention compromised by disclosure; thus, its concealment is more appropriate.

2- From the phrase "وَ يُكَفِّرُ عَنْكُمْ مِنْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ" (And He will atone for some of your sins), it is understood that spending in the way of Allah has a profound effect on the forgiveness of sins, for after the command to spend, He says in this phrase: "And He covers your sins."

Of course, the meaning of this statement is not that every sin will be forgiven due to a small act of spending; rather, considering the word "min" (مِنْ) which is usually used for "partitive" (tab'id) purposes, it is understood that spending covers a portion of sins. It is clear that this portion depends on the "amount of spending" and the "degree of sincerity."

Regarding the fact that spending causes forgiveness, many narrations have been recorded through the channels of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام and the Sunnis. Among them, it is stated in a hadith: "Secret charity extinguishes the wrath of the Lord, and just as water extinguishes fire, it removes the sin of man." [^1] It is also stated in a narration: "There are seven whom Allah will place under the shade of His grace on the day when there is no shade but His: a just leader, a youth who grows up in the worship of the Lord, a person whose heart is attached to the mosques until he returns to them, two people who love each other for the sake of Allah—meeting for His sake and parting for His sake, a man whom a beautiful woman of high status invites to sin and he says 'I fear Allah,' a person who gives charity secretly such that his right hand does not know what his left hand has spent, and a person who remembers Allah in solitude and tears flow from the corners of his eyes." [^2]

3- Additionally, the meaning of the phrase "وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ" (And Allah is Well-Aware of what you do) is that Allah knows what you spend, whether it is open or hidden, and He is also aware of your intentions—for what purpose and goal you disclose or hide the spending.

In any case, what is effective in spending is a pure intention and sincerity in action; furthermore, the knowledge or ignorance of the people has no effect. What is important is the knowledge of Allah, for it is He who gives the reward for human actions and is aware of the hidden and the manifest.

[^1]: "The obligatory Zakat is taken out openly and paid openly, and other than Zakat, if you give it secretly, it is better." - Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 384, quoting from Ali ibn Ibrahim, Vol. 1, p. 93.

[^1]: "Secret charity extinguishes the wrath of the Lord and extinguishes the sin just as water extinguishes fire!" - Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 385.

[^2]: "Seven whom Allah will shade in His shade on the day when there is no shade but His: the just Imam, the youth who grew up in the worship of Allah Almighty, a man whose heart is attached to the mosques until he returns to them, two men who loved each other for Allah's sake—meeting upon it and parting upon it, a man whom a woman of status and beauty invited and he said 'I fear Allah Almighty,' a man who gave charity and hid it until his right hand did not know what his left hand spent, and a man who remembered Allah in solitude and his eyes overflowed." - Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1 & 2, p. 385.

Ayah

۞ لَّيْسَ عَلَيْكَ هُدَىٰهُمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلِأَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ وَمَا تُنفِقُونَ إِلَّا ٱبْتِغَآءَ وَجْهِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تُظْلَمُونَ (٢٧٢)
It is not up to you to guide them; rather, it is Allah who guides whomever He wishes. Whatever wealth you spend, it is for your own benefit, as you do not spend but to seek Allah’s pleasure, and whatever wealth you spend will be repaid to you in full and you will not be wronged. (272)

Circumstances of Revelation

In the commentary Majma' al-Bayan, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that: The Muslims were reluctant to spend (charity) on non-Muslims; thus, the above verse was revealed, granting them permission to do so in times of necessity.

Another circumstance of revelation has been narrated for the above verse which is not unlike the first: A Muslim woman named "Asma" was in the service of the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله during the journey of Umrat al-Qada'. Her mother and grandmother came to her seeking assistance. Since the two were polytheists and idol-worshippers, Asma refused to help them, saying: "I must seek permission from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله because you do not follow my religion." She then went to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and asked for permission, and the verse under discussion was revealed.

Commentary

Spending and Humanitarian Aid to Non-Muslims

In the preceding verses, the issue of spending and giving in the way of Allah was discussed in general terms. In this verse, the discussion pertains to the permissibility of spending on non-Muslims; meaning that Muslims should not withhold charity from the non-Muslim destitute for the purpose of putting them under pressure to choose Islam and be guided.

He says: "Their guidance (by compulsion) is not upon you" (لَيْسَ عَلَيْكَ هُداهُمْ).

Therefore, withholding charity from them to force them into Islam is not correct. Although this statement is addressed to the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it actually encompasses all Muslims.

He then adds: "But Allah guides whom He wills (and deems worthy)" (وَ لكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ).

After this reminder, He continues the discussion on the benefits of spending in the way of Allah and says: "Whatever good you spend is for yourselves" (وَ ما تُنْفِقُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلِأَنْفُسِكُمْ).

"And do not spend except for the sake of Allah" (وَ ما تُنْفِقُونَ إِلَّا ابْتِغاءَ وَجْهِ اللَّهِ).

This is the case if we take the declarative sentence "وَ ما تُنْفِقُونَ" to mean a prohibition; meaning your spending is only beneficial if it is done for the sake of Allah.

There is also the possibility that the sentence remains in its declarative sense; meaning: You Muslims do not spend except for the pleasure of Allah and to seek His satisfaction.

In the final phrase, as a further emphasis, He says: "Whatever good you spend will be repaid to you in full, and you shall not be wronged" (وَ ما تُنْفِقُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ وَ أَنْتُمْ لا تُظْلَمُونَ).

Meaning, do not imagine that you gain only a minor benefit from your spending; rather, everything you spend will be returned to you completely, and that too on a day when you are in desperate need of it. Therefore, be completely generous and open-hearted in your spending.

Furthermore, considering that the literal appearance of this phrase suggests that the very thing spent is returned to the person (not just its reward), the verse can serve as evidence for the "Embodiment of Deeds," which will be discussed in detail in its proper place [^1].

Key Points

1- The above verse states: Just as Divine gifts and His blessings in this world encompass all human beings (regardless of their beliefs and creeds), believers should also consider the condition of non-Muslims when giving recommended charity and fulfilling the needs of the destitute in times of necessity.

Of course, this is provided that spending on non-Muslims is for the sake of humanitarian aid and does not lead to the strengthening of disbelief or the advancement of the sinister plots of enemies; rather, it should make them aware of the humanitarian spirit of Islam.

2- Guidance has various types:

It is clear that the meaning of guidance not being obligatory upon the Prophet is not that he is not duty-bound to guide and preach to them, for preaching and guidance are the clearest and most fundamental programs of the Prophet. Rather, it means he is not duty-bound to put them under pressure or compel them toward guidance.

Is the intended meaning of this guidance "Creative Guidance" (Takwini) or "Legislative Guidance" (Tashri'i)? For guidance has several types:

A) Creative Guidance (Hidayat-e Takwini)

Creative guidance means that Allah has created a series of factors for progress and evolution in various beings of the world—including humans, other living creatures, and even inanimate objects—which lead them toward perfection.

[^1]: This issue will be discussed in detail under the commentary of Surah Al-Imran, verse 30, in this same volume.

The growth and development of the fetus in the mother's womb, the growth and progress of plant seeds in the heart of the earth, the movement of various planets of the solar system in their orbits, and the like, are different examples of creative guidance. This type of guidance belongs exclusively to Allah, and its means are natural and supernatural factors and causes. The Holy Quran says:

"Our Lord is He who gave each thing its form and then guided [it]" (رَبُّنَا الَّذِي أَعْطى‌ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى‌) [^1].

B) Legislative Guidance (Hidayat-e Tashri'i)

This guidance refers to directing individuals through education, training, beneficial laws, just government, advice, and exhortation. This type of guidance is carried out by prophets, Imams, righteous individuals, and compassionate mentors, and it is frequently mentioned in the Quran. The Holy Quran says:

"This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for those conscious of Allah" (ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ) [^2].

C) Guidance in the sense of providing means

This type of guidance, sometimes referred to as "Success" (Tawfiq), consists of placing the necessary means at the disposal of individuals so they may use them for progress by their own will and volition. For example, building schools, mosques, educational centers, preparing necessary programs and books, and training worthy preachers and teachers all fall under this category of guidance. In reality, this type of guidance is an intermediary between creative and legislative guidance. The Quran says:

"And those who strive for Us - We will surely guide them to Our ways" (وَ الَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا) [^3].

D) Guidance toward blessings and rewards

This guidance refers to making worthy individuals benefit from the results of their good deeds in the hereafter. This type of guidance is specific to people of faith and righteousness. The Holy Quran says:

"He will guide them and amend their condition" (سَيَهْدِيهِمْ وَ يُصْلِحُ بالَهُمْ). This sentence, which comes after mentioning the sacrifice of the martyrs in the way of Allah, says:

"Allah guides them and improves their state" [^1]. It is evident that this type of guidance relates only to their enjoyment of the beneficial results of their actions in the other world.

In reality, these four types of guidance are different stages of one "Truth," each following the other. First, the creative guidance of Allah reaches man, placing intellect, thought, and other powers at his disposal (Creative Guidance).

Then, the guidance and direction of the prophets begin, and they invite people to the path of truth (Guidance in the sense of preaching and instruction).

After that, upon entering the stage of action, the success (Tawfiq) of the Lord encompasses them, and the paths are made smooth for them; thus they traverse the third stage of guidance (Guidance in the sense of Tawfiq).

Finally, in the other world, they benefit from the results of their deeds (Guidance toward rewards).

Of these four types of guidance, one type (preaching and instruction) is among the definitive duties of the prophets and Imams. Another type, which is smoothing the path, is to a large extent part of the programs of the divine government of the prophets and Imams. However, the rest belong exclusively to the Essence of Allah.

[^1]: Surah Ta-Ha, verse 50. [^2]: Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 2. [^3]: Surah Al-Ankabut, verse 69. [^1]: Surah Muhammad, verse 5.

Therefore, wherever the Quran negates guidance from the Prophet, as in the above verse, it does not refer to the first two types.

Although in the phrase "But Allah guides whom He wills" (وَ لكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ), this matter is made contingent upon the will of Allah, these guidances of the Lord are certainly not without account or wisdom. That is, He does not guide one and deprive another without reason; rather, individuals must first establish their "worthiness" for guidance to benefit from it.

In any case, another truth can be derived from the above verse: if among the Muslims there are those who, after all this emphasis on avoiding ostentation, reminders of generosity, and injury, still taint their spending with these matters, do not be distressed. Your duty is only to explain the laws and provide a healthy social environment, and you are never duty-bound to compel them to these matters. It is clear that this interpretation does not contradict the previous one, and both can be derived from the verse.

3- The Effect of Spending on the Lives of the Spenders

The phrase "Whatever good you spend is for yourselves" (وَ ما تُنْفِقُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلِأَنْفُسِكُمْ) states that the benefits of spending return to you, thereby encouraging the spenders toward this humanitarian act. Certainly, when a person knows that the result of his work returns to him, he will be more interested in that work.

It might initially seem that the return of the benefits of spending to the spender refers only to the rewards and results in the hereafter.

While this meaning is correct, one should not imagine that the benefit of spending is only for the hereafter; rather, it is also to their benefit in this world, both from a "spiritual" and a "material" perspective. Spiritually, it nurtures the spirit of forgiveness, generosity, sacrifice, philanthropy, and brotherhood in the spender, and is in fact an effective means for his spiritual perfection and the cultivation of his personality.

As for the material perspective, "deprived and destitute individuals" in a society cause dangerous explosions—the same explosions that sometimes swallow the entire principle of ownership and consume and destroy all wealth.

Spending reduces the class gap and eliminates the dangers that face members of society from this path. Spending extinguishes the fire of anger and the burning flames of the deprived classes and removes the spirit of revenge and malice from them.

Therefore, spending is also to the benefit of the spenders in terms of "social security," "economic health," and "various material and spiritual aspects."

4- What does "Wajh Allah" mean?

"Wajh" literally means "face" and is sometimes used to mean "Essence." Therefore, "Wajh Allah" means the Essence of Allah. Spenders should have the Pure Essence of the Lord in view. Thus, the mention of the word Wajh in this verse and similar ones implies a kind of emphasis, because when it is said "for the Essence of Allah," its emphasis is greater than "for Allah"; meaning it must strictly be for Allah and no one else.

Additionally, the human face is usually the most noble external part of the body, as the important organs of sight and speech are located there. For this reason, when the word Wajh is used, it conveys nobility and importance. Here, it is used "metaphorically" regarding Allah, and in fact, a sense of respect and importance is understood from it. It is obvious that Allah is neither a body nor does He have a face.

Ayah

لِلْفُقَرَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أُحْصِرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ ضَرْبًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَحْسَبُهُمُ ٱلْجَاهِلُ أَغْنِيَآءَ مِنَ ٱلتَّعَفُّفِ تَعْرِفُهُم بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ لَا يَسْـَٔلُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ إِلْحَافًا ۗ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمٌ (٢٧٣)
[The charities are] for the poor who are straitened in the way of Allah, not capable of moving about in the land [for trade]. The unaware suppose them to be well-off because of their reserve. You recognize them by their mark; they do not ask the people importunately. And whatever wealth you may spend, Allah indeed knows it. (273)

Occasion of Revelation

It has been narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that: This verse was revealed concerning the "Ashab al-Suffah" (The People of the Bench). (The Ashab al-Suffah were about four hundred Muslims from Mecca and the outskirts of Medina who had neither homes in Medina nor relatives with whom they could stay. For this reason, they took up residence in the Prophet's Mosque and declared their readiness to participate in the fields of Jihad).

However, since their stay in the Mosque was not compatible with the sanctity of the Mosque, they were ordered to move to the Suffah (a large and spacious platform) located outside the Mosque. The above verse was revealed, instructing the people not to withhold any possible assistance from this group of their brothers, and they acted accordingly [^1]. Some commentators have explicitly stated that they were the guards and protectors of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله [^2].

Commentary

The Best Case for Spending

Continuing with the etiquette and rulings of spending (Infaq), this verse describes the best cases where spending should take place, namely those who possess the three characteristics mentioned in this verse. In describing their first attribute, He says: Your spending should specifically "be for the poor who are restricted in the way of Allah" (لِلْفُقَراءِ الَّذِينَ أُحْصِرُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ).

This refers to those who, due to being occupied with Jihad in the way of Allah, fighting the enemy, learning the arts of war, or acquiring other necessary sciences, have been prevented from striving for a livelihood and providing for their living expenses. A clear example of this was the Ashab al-Suffah during the era of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله [^3]. Then, for emphasis, He adds: "those who cannot travel" and acquire capital (لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ ضَرْباً فِي الْأَرْضِ).

The expression "striking the earth" (darban fil-ard) is used instead of "traveling" because travelers, especially those who travel on foot for important goals, constantly strike their feet upon the ground as they move forward.

Therefore, those who are able to provide for their lives must endure the hardship and toil of travel and not live off the labor of others, unless a more important task, such as Jihad in the way of Allah or learning obligatory sciences, prevents them from traveling.

In the second description of them, He says: "those whom the ignorant and uninformed person thinks are wealthy because of their self-restraint" (يَحْسَبُهُمُ الْجاهِلُ أَغْنِياءَ مِنَ التَّعَفُّفِ).

But this statement does not mean that these dignified needy people cannot be recognized; hence He adds: "You recognize them by their mark" (تَعْرِفُهُمْ بِسِيماهُمْ).

"Sima" (mark/sign) linguistically means a sign or indication [^1]. Its use in modern Persian to mean "face" or "countenance" is a newer meaning; otherwise, in its Arabic concept, such a meaning is not mentioned. In any case, the meaning is that although they do not speak of their condition, there are signs of internal suffering in their faces that are apparent to discerning individuals. Indeed, "the color of the face reveals the secret of the heart."

In the third description of them, He says: They are so noble that: "they never beg of people with importunity" (لا يَسْئَلُونَ النَّاسَ إِلْحافاً) [^2]. Fundamentally, they do not ask people for anything, let alone persist in asking. In other words, it is the habit of ordinary needy people to persist in asking, but they are not ordinary needy people.

Therefore, if the Quran says: "They do not ask with importunity," it does not mean that they ask without importunity; rather, it means they are not ordinary poor people who would ask, because their asking is usually accompanied by persistence and importunity. This is supported by the fact that He says: "You must recognize them by their mark," not by their asking; otherwise, the phrase "the ignorant thinks them wealthy because of their self-restraint" would have no meaning.

There is another possibility in the interpretation of this verse: that they never ask under normal circumstances, (and whenever extreme necessity forces them to disclose their state,) they never persist.

Some have also said the meaning is that they are persistent in refraining from asking. (However, this possibility is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse).

At the end of the verse, He again encourages everyone to spend from all kinds of good things, saying: "And whatever good you spend in the way of Allah, indeed Allah is aware of it" (وَ ما تُنْفِقُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ بِهِ عَلِيمٌ).

This sentence is to encourage those who spend, especially spending on individuals who possess self-respect and a high nature. Often in these cases, gifts are given under the guise of something other than charity, but in reality with the intention of Infaq, so that the recipient does not feel uncomfortable. Certainly, Allah is aware of these hidden intentions and will reward them in proportion to their intention and efforts.

Key Point: Asking Without Need is Forbidden!

One of the major sins is begging and asking or requesting from people without being in need. This act has been condemned in numerous narrations. In a hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"Charity is not permissible for the wealthy."

And in another hadith from him, it is stated:

"Whoever asks while he has that which suffices him, he is only increasing for himself the embers of Hell" [^1]. It is also mentioned in narrations that: "The testimony of one who begs in the palm of his hand is not accepted" [^2].

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan; Abu al-Futuh Razi; al-Bahr al-Muhit; Qurtubi; Ruh al-Ma'ani and other commentaries (with variations in wording). [^2]: Fi Zilal al-Quran, under the verse discussed. [^3]: "Hasr" means to imprison, prevent, or restrict, and here it refers to all matters that prevent a person from securing a livelihood. [^1]: Some have derived it from the root "wasm" and others from the root "sawm." [^2]: "Ilhaf" is from the same root as "lihaf" (quilt/cover), referring to that specific covering. Persistence in asking is called "ilhaf" because it covers the heart of the person or the opposite party. [^1]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 3, p. 50. [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 18, p. 281, Book of Testimonies, Chapter 35.

Ayah

ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ سِرًّا وَعَلَانِيَةً فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٢٧٤)
Those who give their wealth by night and day, secretly and openly, they shall have their reward near their Lord, and they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (274)

Occasion of Revelation

It has been mentioned in many Hadiths that this verse was revealed concerning Ali عليه السلام, for he possessed four Dirhams; he spent one Dirham at night, one Dirham during the day, one Dirham secretly, and one Dirham openly, and thus this verse was revealed [^1]. However, we know that the revelation of a verse regarding a specific instance does not limit its meaning, nor does it negate the inclusion of others under the same ruling.

Commentary

Charity in Every Form and Manner is Desirable

Once again, this verse speaks of another matter related to spending in the way of Allah, which is the various and diverse qualities of charity. It says: "Those who spend their wealth by night and by day, secretly and openly, they shall have their reward with their Lord" (الَّذِينَ يُنْفِقُونَ أَمْوالَهُمْ بِاللَّيْلِ وَ النَّهارِ سِرًّا وَ عَلانِيَةً فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ).

It goes without saying that the selection of these different methods is to observe the best conditions for charity. That is, those who give charity must consider moral and social aspects when spending during the night or day, secretly or openly. Where giving to the needy provides no reason for disclosure, they should keep it hidden so that both the dignity of the recipient is preserved and there is greater sincerity in the act. However, where other interests are involved—such as honoring religious symbols or encouraging and motivating others—and the charity does not have a personal aspect that would violate someone's respect (such as spending for Jihad, charitable buildings, and the like), and it does not conflict with sincerity, they should spend openly.

It is not unlikely that placing "night" before "day" and "secretly" before "openly" (in the verse under discussion) is an indication that keeping charity hidden is better unless there is a reason for disclosure, although charity must not be forgotten at any time or in any form.

Certainly, that which is with the Lord (especially with the emphasis on the attribute of Rububiyyah [Lordship], which oversees nurturing and perfection) will not be something small or of little value. It will be proportionate to the Graces and Favors of the Lord, encompassing both the blessings of this world and the virtues of the Hereafter, as well as proximity to Allah.

Then it adds: "There shall be no fear upon them, nor shall they grieve" (وَ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ).

We know that since man does not consider himself independent of wealth and property for the continuation and management of his life, he usually becomes sorrowful when he loses it and becomes anxious about his future, for he does not know what his situation will be. This very matter often prevents charity, except for those who, on one hand, have faith in Divine promises and, on the other hand, understand the social effects of charity.

Such individuals have no fear or dread of the future regarding spending in the way of Allah, and they do not grieve over losing a portion of their wealth. This is because they know that in exchange for what they have given up, they will benefit far more from the Grace of their Lord and from its individual, social, and moral blessings in this world and the next.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, pp. 290-291. The author of al-Durr al-Manthur has narrated it from Ibn Asakir, Tabarani, Abu Hatim, Ibn Jarir, and several others. According to some, this verse was revealed concerning Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام by the consensus of Shi'a scholars and the majority of Sunni scholars... Among the Sunni scholars: Wahidi, Tha'labi, Khwarizmi, Suddi, Kalbi, Zamakhshari, Tafi, Qushayri, Mawardi, Ibn Maghazili, Ibn Abi al-Hadid, and others have also narrated this Hadith. Refer to Tafsir al-Burhan and Tafsir Ithna Ashari under the verse in question.

Ayahs

ٱلَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ لَا يَقُومُونَ إِلَّا كَمَا يَقُومُ ٱلَّذِى يَتَخَبَّطُهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ مِنَ ٱلْمَسِّ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَا ٱلْبَيْعُ مِثْلُ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ ۗ وَأَحَلَّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْبَيْعَ وَحَرَّمَ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ ۚ فَمَن جَآءَهُۥ مَوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ فَٱنتَهَىٰ فَلَهُۥ مَا سَلَفَ وَأَمْرُهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَنْ عَادَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٧٥)
Those who exact usury will not stand but like one deranged by the Devil’s touch. That is because they say, ‘Trade is just like usury.’ While Allah has allowed trade and forbidden usury. Whoever relinquishes [usury] on receiving advice from his Lord shall keep [the gains of] what is past, and his matter will rest with Allah. As for those who resume, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]. (275)
يَمْحَقُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ وَيُرْبِى ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ كَفَّارٍ أَثِيمٍ (٢٧٦)
Allah brings usury to naught, but He makes charities flourish. Allah does not like any sinful ingrate. (276)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٢٧٧)
Indeed those who have faith, do righteous deeds, maintain the prayer and give the zakat, they shall have their reward near their Lord, and they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (277)

Commentary

Usury as the Opposite of Infining (Anfaq)

Following the discussion regarding spending in the way of Allah and bestowing wealth to support the needy, these verses speak of the issue of usury (Riba), which is exactly the opposite of spending (Anfaq). In reality, it completes the objective of the previous verses, because usury leads to an increase in poverty within society, the accumulation of wealth in the hands of a limited few, and the deprivation of the majority of social members. Spending causes the purification of the heart, the sanctity of souls, and the tranquility of society, whereas usury causes the emergence of miserliness, malice, hatred, and impurity.

These verses describe the prohibition of the ruling on usury with utmost severity, yet it is evident from their tone that there had been prior discussions regarding usury. Considering the chronological order of the revelation of the Surahs of the Quran, the matter is indeed so.

For in Surah Ar-Rum, which according to the order of revelation is the thirtieth Surah revealed in Mecca, usury is mentioned, and we do not encounter any mention of usury in any other "Meccan" Surah. However, in that Surah, the discourse on usury appears only in the form of moral advice, stating: Usury is not a pleasing act in the presence of the Lord: وَ ما آتَيْتُمْ مِنْ رِباً لِيَرْبُوَا فِي أَمْوالِ النَّاسِ فَلا يَرْبُوا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ [1]

[1]: Surah Ar-Rum, Verse 39.

"From the perspective of short-sighted individuals, wealth may increase through usury, but in the presence of Allah, nothing shall be added to it."

Subsequently, after the Migration (Hijrah), the discussion of usury appeared in three other Surahs among those revealed in Medina, which are, in order: Surah Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and An-Nisa. Although Surah Al-Baqarah was revealed before Al-Imran, it is not unlikely that specifically Verse 130 of Surah Al-Imran, which expresses the explicit ruling on the prohibition of usury, was revealed before Surah Al-Baqarah and the aforementioned verses.

In any case, this verse and other verses related to usury were revealed at a time when usury was prevalent with the utmost intensity in Mecca, Medina, and the Arabian Peninsula. It was one of the major factors of class division, the severe weakness of the working class, and the tyranny of the aristocracy. Therefore, the Quran's struggle against usury constitutes a significant part of the social struggles of Islam.

With this point in mind, we return to the commentary of the verse:

First, in a vivid and eloquent metaphor, it visualizes the state of usurers, stating: "Those who consume usury cannot stand [on the Day of Resurrection] except as one stands who is being beaten by Satan into insanity" and cannot maintain his balance, sometimes falling to the ground and sometimes rising (الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ الرِّبا لا يَقُومُونَ إِلَّا كَما يَقُومُ الَّذِي يَتَخَبَّطُهُ الشَّيْطانُ مِنَ الْمَسِ). [1] In this sentence, the usurer is likened to an epileptic or a diseased insane person who, while walking, is unable to maintain his balance and take proper steps.

Is the intention to depict their state in the Hereafter and upon entering the scene of Resurrection?

[1]: "Yatakhabbatuhu" is from the root "khabt," meaning the failure to maintain bodily balance while walking or rising.

Meaning, will they be resurrected in the form of the insane and the epileptic upon entering the field of gathering?

Most commentators have accepted this possibility.

However, some say the intention is to visualize their state in the life of this world, because their actions are like those of the insane. They lack proper social thinking and cannot even consider their own interests; issues such as cooperation, sympathy, human emotions, and altruism have no meaning for them. The worship of wealth has so blinded the eye of their intellect that they do not realize that exploiting the lower classes and plundering their hard-earned labor sows the seeds of enmity in their hearts, leading to social revolutions and explosions that jeopardize the foundation of ownership. In such a case, security and peace will not exist in such a society; therefore, he too cannot live in comfort. Thus, his conduct is the conduct of the insane.

But since the state of man in the other world is a manifestation of his deeds in this world, it is possible that the verse refers to both meanings. Yes, the usurers, whose standing in the world is irregular, irrational, and mixed with "insane wealth-accumulation," will also be resurrected like the insane in the other world.

Interestingly, in the narrations of the Infallibles عليهم السلام, both aspects have been mentioned. In a narration regarding the interpretation of the verse, we read that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said:

"آكل الربا لا يخرج من الدنيا حتى يتخبطه الشيطان"

Meaning, "The consumer of usury does not leave this world until he is afflicted with a type of insanity." [1] And in another narration, to visualize the state of the gluttonous usurers who only think of their own interests and whose wealth will become a burden for them, it is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "When I went on the Ascension (Mi'raj), I saw a group whose bellies were so large that no matter how hard they tried to stand and walk, it was not possible for them, and they repeatedly fell to the ground. I asked Gabriel: Who are these people and what is their crime?

He replied: These are the usurers." [2] The first Hadith reflects the state of human agitation in this world, and the second Hadith describes the states of usurers on the scene of Resurrection, and both relate to a single reality. Just as overeaters develop a repulsive and excessive obesity, the wealthy who become fat through usury also have an unhealthy economic life that is a burden upon them.

Question

A question arises here: Is the source of insanity and epilepsy from Satan, as mentioned in the verse above, even though we know that epilepsy and insanity are mental illnesses and often have known causes?

Answer

A group believes that the expression "touch of Satan" is a metaphor for mental illness and insanity, and this expression was common among the Arabs, not that Satan actually exerts an influence on the human soul. However, it is not at all unlikely that some satanic acts and irregular, incorrect deeds cause a type of satanic insanity; meaning, following those deeds, Satan influences the person and disrupts his mental balance. Furthermore, when satanic and incorrect deeds accumulate, their natural effect is the loss of the sense of correct discernment and the ability for logical thinking.

Then, it points to a corner of the usurers' logic, stating: "That is because they say: Trade is just like usury," and there is no difference between the two (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قالُوا إِنَّمَا الْبَيْعُ مِثْلُ الرِّبا). That is, both are types of exchange conducted with the consent of both parties.

But the Quran says in response to them: How can these two be the same "while Allah has permitted trade and has forbidden usury" (وَ أَحَلَّ اللَّهُ الْبَيْعَ وَ حَرَّمَ الرِّبا).

Certainly, this difference had a reason and a philosophy for which the All-Wise Allah issued such a ruling. The Quran has not provided further explanation on this, perhaps due to its clarity, because:

Firstly—In ordinary buying and selling, both parties are equally exposed to profit and loss; sometimes both profit, sometimes both lose, and sometimes one profits while the other loses. Whereas in "usurious transactions," the usurer never suffers a loss, and all potential losses weigh heavily on the opposite party. It is for this reason that usurious institutions become more extensive and capitalized day by day, and their volume of wealth constantly increases against the depletion of the weak classes.

Secondly—In ordinary trade and buying and selling, the parties take steps in the path of "production and consumption," whereas the usurer performs no positive action in this regard.

Thirdly—With the spread of usury, capital falls into unhealthy paths, and the foundations of the economy, which is the basis of society, are shaken, while correct trade causes the healthy circulation of wealth.

Fourthly—Usury is the source of enmities and class wars, whereas correct trade is not so and never drives society toward class division and the wars resulting from it.

Then, it opens the way for the repentant, stating: "So whoever receives an admonition from his Lord and desists (from usury), he shall have what has passed (before the ruling of prohibition), and his affair is with Allah," and Allah will forgive his past (فَمَنْ جاءَهُ مَوْعِظَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ فَانْتَهى‌ فَلَهُ ما سَلَفَ وَ أَمْرُهُ إِلَى اللَّهِ‌).

"But whoever returns (to it out of stubbornness and continues this sin), those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally" (وَ مَنْ عادَ فَأُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ‌). In the previous sentence, it was explicitly stated that this law, like any other law, does not include the past, and so to speak, it is not retroactive. For if laws were to include the time before their legislation, many problems would arise in people's lives; for this reason, laws are always implemented from the time they become official.

Of course, the meaning of this statement is not that if usurers had debts from individuals, they could take anything more than their capital from them after the revelation of the verse; rather, the intention is that the profits they took before the revelation of the verse have been made permissible for them.

The phrase "وَ أَمْرُهُ إِلَى اللَّهِ‌" (and his affair is with Allah), although its appearance suggests that the future of such individuals regarding pardon and punishment is not clear but rather depends on Divine grace, yet considering the sentence before it (فَلَهُ ما سَلَفَ‌), it becomes clear that the intention is that very pardon. It is as if the gravity of the sin of usury caused even the ruling of pardon for those who engaged in this act before the revelation of the verse not to be stated explicitly.

Other possibilities have also been suggested for the meaning of this phrase, which were omitted as they were contrary to the apparent meaning. [1] At the end of this verse, eternal punishment was mentioned, even though we know that such punishment is specific to disbelievers, not sinful believers. It is as if this expression indicates that usurers who insist on usury do not have proper faith, for they have risen in opposition to this definitive Divine law, opposition to which is like war with Allah; or that continuous and permanent usury causes them to leave the world without faith, and their end becomes dark and gloomy.

The possibility also exists that "Khulud" (eternity) here—like in Verse 93 of Surah An-Nisa, which mentions the ruling of eternal punishment and Khulud regarding the killers of innocent people—means a long-term punishment, not an everlasting and eternal one.

In the next verse, He makes a comparison between usury and spending in the way of Allah, stating: "Allah destroys usury and gives increase for charities" (يَمْحَقُ اللَّهُ الرِّبا وَ يُرْبِي الصَّدَقاتِ‌). Then He adds: "And Allah does not love any ungrateful sinner (who has forgotten all those blessings of spending and goes toward the scorching fire of usury)" (وَ اللَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ كَفَّارٍ أَثِيمٍ‌).

"Mahq" means gradual reduction and destruction, and "Riba" is gradual growth and increase. Since the usurer, by means of the wealth in his hand, collects the product of the labor of the working class and sometimes terminates their existence and life with this means, or at least sows the seeds of enmity and malice in their hearts such that they gradually become thirsty for the usurer's blood and place his life and property in danger.

The Quran says: "Allah drives usurious capital toward destruction." This gradual destruction that exists for usurious individuals also exists for a usurious society.

In contrast, those who step into society with human emotions and compassion, spending from the capital and property under their control and striving to meet the needs of the people, are met with public love and affection. Their capital is not only not in danger but, through public cooperation, achieves its natural growth. This is why the Quran says, "and He increases charities."

This ruling is the same for the individual and society. In a society where public needs are attended to, the intellectual and physical power of the working and laboring class, which constitutes the majority of society, is put to work, and following that, a correct economic system based on public cooperation and public benefit is established.

"Kaffar" (from the root "Kufur") is said of one who is very ungrateful and a denier of blessings, and "Athim" is one who commits many sins.

The above sentence says: Usurers not only fail to show gratitude for the blessing Allah has bestowed upon them by abandoning spending, interest-free loans, and using wealth for public needs, but they also make it a means for every kind of oppression, sin, and corruption. It is natural that Allah does not love such people.

And in the last verse under discussion, it speaks of the group of believers who are the exact opposite of the usurers, stating: "Indeed, those who believe and do righteous deeds and establish prayer and give zakah will have their reward with their Lord, and there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَ أَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ آتَوُا الزَّكاةَ لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ وَ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ‌).

In contrast to the ungrateful and sinful usurers, those who, in the light of faith, have abandoned selfishness and revived their innate emotions, and in addition to connecting with the Lord and establishing prayer, hasten to help and support the needy—thereby preventing the accumulation of wealth and the emergence of class differences and the thousand types of crimes that follow—will have their reward with their Lord and will benefit from the result of their good deed in both worlds.

Naturally, the factors of anxiety and apprehension no longer arise for this group. The danger that lay in the path of the parasitic capitalists and the curses that were subsequently showered upon them are not for this group.

And finally, they enjoy "complete tranquility," having no anxiety or grief, just as it appears at the end of the verse: "وَ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ‌".

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَذَرُوا۟ مَا بَقِىَ مِنَ ٱلرِّبَوٰٓا۟ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٢٧٨)
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and abandon [all claims to] what remains of usury, should you be faithful. (278)
فَإِن لَّمْ تَفْعَلُوا۟ فَأْذَنُوا۟ بِحَرْبٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۖ وَإِن تُبْتُمْ فَلَكُمْ رُءُوسُ أَمْوَٰلِكُمْ لَا تَظْلِمُونَ وَلَا تُظْلَمُونَ (٢٧٩)
And if you do not, then be informed of a war from Allah and His apostle. And if you repent, then you will have your principal, neither harming others, nor suffering harm. (279)
وَإِن كَانَ ذُو عُسْرَةٍ فَنَظِرَةٌ إِلَىٰ مَيْسَرَةٍ ۚ وَأَن تَصَدَّقُوا۟ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٨٠)
If [the debtor] is in straits, let there be a respite until the time of ease; and if you remit [the debt] as charity, it will be better for you, should you know. (280)
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ يَوْمًا تُرْجَعُونَ فِيهِ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ ثُمَّ تُوَفَّىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٢٨١)
And beware of a day in which you will be brought back to Allah. Then every soul shall be recompensed fully for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged. (281)

Circumstances of Revelation

It is mentioned in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim ^1 that after the verses concerning usury (riba) were revealed, a person named Khalid ibn Walid came to the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "Since my father had usurious dealings with the 'Thaqif tribe' and had not collected his claims, he bequeathed that I should receive a sum of the profits from his wealth that has not yet been paid. Is this action permissible for me?"

The above verses were revealed and strictly forbade the people from this act.

In another narration, it is stated that after the revelation of this verse, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

«ألا إن كل ربا من ربا الجاهلية موضوع وأول ربا اضعه ربا العباس بن عبد المطلب»

"Be aware that all usurious claims that people had against one another during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) must be forgotten [annulled], and the first usurious claim that I consign to oblivion is the claim of Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib." [^2] From this statement, it is clearly understood that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله drew a red line through the usurious claims of the Age of Ignorance, He began with His own relatives; if there were wealthy individuals among them like Abbas who, like other wealthy people of the Jahiliyyah, were tainted [with riba], the Prophet first annulled their claims.

It is also mentioned in narrations that after the revelation of these verses, the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله ordered the governor of Mecca to fight the Al-Mughirah—who were well-known usurers—if they did not cease their practice. [^3]

^1: Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim, Vol. 1, p. 93. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1-2, p. 392; and al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 2, p. 109, with slight variations. [^3]: al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 2, pp. 107-108.

Commentary

Usury is a Grave Sin

In the first verse, Allah addresses the believers and, to further emphasize the prohibition of usury, He says: "O you who have believed, fear Allah and give up what remains [due to you] of usury, if you should be believers." (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَذَرُوا مَا بَقِيَ مِنَ الرِّبَا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ).

It is interesting that the above verse both begins with faith in God and ends with faith. In reality, this is an emphasis on the fact that usury is incompatible with the spirit of faith; therefore, faith is realized for them only when they practice piety (taqwa) and abandon the remainder of usury—meaning the claims they have in this regard.

The intention is not that usurers are disbelievers (kafar), as the Kharijites imagined regarding major sins in general, but rather that it is inconsistent with firm and fruitful faith.

In the next verse, the tone of the discourse changes. After the exhortations in the previous verses, it confronts usurers with severity, warning that if they continue their practice, refuse to submit to truth and justice, and persist in sucking the blood of the deprived, the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله will be compelled to stop them by resorting to war. He says: "And if you do not, then be informed of a war [against you] from Allah and His Messenger." (فَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا فَأْذَنُوا بِحَرْبٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ).

This is the same war that is carried out according to the law: "fight against the one that oppresses until it returns to the ordinance of Allah" ^1 (فَقَاتِلُوا الَّتِي تَبْغِي حَتَّى تَفِيءَ إِلَى أَمْرِ اللَّهِ).

^1: Surah Al-Hujurat, Verse 9.

Thus, we read in a narration that when Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام heard that a usurer was consuming riba with utter audacity and calling it "laba" (colostrum or first milk),

He said: "If I gain power over him, I will put him to death." ^1 Of course, it is understood from this hadith that the ruling of execution applies to those who deny the prohibition of usury.

"Fa'dhanu" (فَأْذَنُوا) is from the root "idhn" (اذن). Whenever it is made transitive with the letter "lam," it means to give permission, and whenever it is made transitive with "ba," it means knowledge and awareness. Therefore, the meaning of "be informed of a war from Allah" (فَأْذَنُوا بِحَرْبٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ) is: "Be aware that Allah and His Messenger will fight you usurers." In reality, it is a declaration of war from Allah and the Prophet against this headstrong group.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 2, p. 376

Therefore, what is common in the words of some who say in the translation of the verse: "Declare war against Allah," is not correct. [^2] In any case, it is evident from the above verse that the Islamic government can stop usury by resorting to force. (Additionally, the use of "war" (harb) in the indefinite form indicates the magnitude and gravity of the war).

Then He adds: "But if you repent, you may have your principal—[thus] you do no wrong, nor are you wronged." (وَإِنْ تُبْتُمْ فَلَكُمْ رُؤُوسُ أَمْوَالِكُمْ لَا تَظْلِمُونَ وَلَا تُظْلَمُونَ).

This means that if you repent and dismantle the machinery of usury, you have the right to collect your original capital that is in the hands of the people (excluding the interest). This law is perfectly just because it both prevents you from oppressing others

^1: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 12, p. 429 (Chapter on the establishment of execution and disbelief for deeming usury permissible—Hadith 1). [^2]: The interpretation of "fa'dhanu" as "fa'lamu" (know/be aware) has been mentioned by most commentators such as Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Abu al-Futuh Razi, Fakhr Razi, Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani, and Allameh Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan, among others.

and prevents wrong from being inflicted upon you; in this case, you will be neither the oppressor nor the oppressed.

The phrase "you do no wrong, nor are you wronged" (لَا تَظْلِمُونَ وَلَا تُظْلَمُونَ), although mentioned regarding usurers, is in fact a broad and profound Islamic slogan which states: just as Muslims must refrain from committing oppression, they must also avoid submitting to oppression. Fundamentally, if there were no one to endure oppression, fewer oppressors would be found. If Muslims possess sufficient readiness to defend their rights, no one can oppress them. Before we tell the oppressor "do not oppress," we must tell the oppressed "do not submit to oppression."

In the next verse, He says: "And if [the debtor] is in hardship, then [let there be] postponement until [a time of] ease." (وَإِنْ كَانَ ذُو عُسْرَةٍ فَنَظِرَةٌ إِلَى مَيْسَرَةٍ). ^1 Here, He states one of the rights of debtors: if they are unable to pay even the principal of their debt (not the interest), not only should they not be charged doubled interest according to the custom of Jahiliyyah and placed under pressure, but they must also be given a respite to pay the principal debt. This is a general law regarding all debtors.

Even in Islamic laws, which are in fact an interpretation of the above verse, it is explicitly stated that one can never seize or take away a person's house or essential living equipment because of their debt; rather, creditors can only take their due from the surplus. This is a clear protection of the rights of the weak classes of society.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "And that you give [it as] charity is better for you, if you only knew." (وَأَنْ تَصَدَّقُوا خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ).

This is, in fact, a step beyond legal matters; it is a moral and humanitarian issue that completes the previous legal discussion. It tells the creditors: in such cases where debtors are in severe straits, if their debt is forgiven, it is better for you from every perspective. It transforms feelings of malice and revenge into love and sincerity, and encourages the weak members of society toward renewed activity whose results benefit everyone. In addition to these, it is considered charity (sadaqah) and spending in the way of Allah, which is a treasure for the Day of Judgment.

In the final verse under discussion, He concludes the issue of usury with a severe warning, saying: "And fear a Day when you will be returned to Allah." (وَاتَّقُوا يَوْماً تُرْجَعُونَ فِيهِ إِلَى اللَّهِ).

"Then every soul will be compensated for what it earned," (ثُمَّ تُوَفَّى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَا كَسَبَتْ).

"And they will not be wronged," (وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ) rather, whatever they see is the result of their own actions.

It is the custom of the Holy Quran that after explaining the intricacies of Islamic rulings and programs, in many cases, it provides a general, universal, and comprehensive reminder to emphasize and reinforce what was previously said, so that the preceding rulings and programs fully penetrate the mind and soul. Therefore, in this verse, He directs people's attention to the Resurrection and the punishment for the deeds of evildoers, warning them to be mindful that a Day lies ahead when all of man's actions will be given back to him without any deficiency. Everything that has been preserved in the archives of the universe will be handed over to him at once. It is then that he will be terrified by their grim consequences, but this harvest is something he himself sowed, and no one has

wronged him; rather, it is he who has permitted wrong against himself: "And they will not be wronged."

Additionally, this verse is another piece of evidence for the embodiment of human actions in the next world.

Key Points

  1. It is noteworthy that in the Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated through several channels that this verse is the last verse revealed to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. Considering its content, this does not seem unlikely at all. If Surah Al-Baqarah is not the last Surah revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it does not contradict this subject, for we know that sometimes verses revealed later were placed in earlier Surahs by the command of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

2. The Harms of Usury

A) Usury disrupts the economic balance in societies and concentrates wealth at one pole of society, because as a result, one group only gains profit while all economic losses fall upon another group. If we hear that the gap between the wealthy and poor countries of the world is increasing day by day, one factor is this very issue, followed by the outbreak of bloody wars.

B) Usury is a type of unhealthy economic exchange that weakens emotions and bonds and sows the seeds of hatred and enmity in hearts. In reality, usury is based on the principle that the usurer only sees the profit of his money and has no regard for the loss or hardship of the debtor.

It is here that the debtor realizes the usurer has made money a means of making him and others miserable.

C) It is true that the borrower submits to usury out of necessity, but he will never forget this injustice. It even reaches a point where he feels the pressure of the usurer's grip

on his throat as much as possible. At this moment, the entire being of the wretched debtor sends curses and imprecations upon the usurer; he becomes thirsty for his blood and sees with his own eyes that the existence and income attained at the cost of his life are poured into the usurer's pocket. In these critical conditions, dozens of horrific crimes occur; the debtor sometimes resorts to suicide, and sometimes, due to the intensity of distress, kills the creditor in a tragic manner. Sometimes it manifests as a social crisis, a general explosion, and a universal revolution.

This rupture of the bond of cooperation and collaboration is also clearly visible among nations and countries that are lenders and borrowers of usury. Nations that see their wealth poured into the pockets of another nation as interest look upon that nation with a specific grudge, hatred, and resentment. Even though they needed the loan, they wait for the day to show a suitable reaction.

This is why we say usury, from a moral perspective, leaves an exceptionally bad effect on the psyche of the borrower, who finds hatred for the lender in his heart, and it weakens the bond of social cooperation and collaboration between individuals and nations. ^1

D) In Islamic narrations, the bad moral effect of usury is pointed out in a short and meaningful sentence. In the book Wasa'il al-Shia, regarding the reason for the prohibition of usury, we read that Hisham ibn Salim said: Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said:

«إنما حرم الله عز وجل الربا لكيلا يمتنع الناس من اصطناع المعروف»

"Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, has only forbidden usury so that people do not refrain from doing good deeds." [^2] [^3]

^1: Refer to the book Usury or Economic Colonialism. [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 12, Chapters on Usury, Chapter 1, p. 422. [^3]: We have explained more about the harms of usury in the book Basic Outlines of Islamic Economics.

Ayah
