يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا تَدَايَنتُم بِدَيْنٍ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى فَٱكْتُبُوهُ ۚ وَلْيَكْتُب بَّيْنَكُمْ كَاتِبٌۢ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۚ وَلَا يَأْبَ كَاتِبٌ أَن يَكْتُبَ كَمَا عَلَّمَهُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ فَلْيَكْتُبْ وَلْيُمْلِلِ ٱلَّذِى عَلَيْهِ ٱلْحَقُّ وَلْيَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّهُۥ وَلَا يَبْخَسْ مِنْهُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ فَإِن كَانَ ٱلَّذِى عَلَيْهِ ٱلْحَقُّ سَفِيهًا أَوْ ضَعِيفًا أَوْ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُ أَن يُمِلَّ هُوَ فَلْيُمْلِلْ وَلِيُّهُۥ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۚ وَٱسْتَشْهِدُوا۟ شَهِيدَيْنِ مِن رِّجَالِكُمْ ۖ فَإِن لَّمْ يَكُونَا رَجُلَيْنِ فَرَجُلٌ وَٱمْرَأَتَانِ مِمَّن تَرْضَوْنَ مِنَ ٱلشُّهَدَآءِ أَن تَضِلَّ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا فَتُذَكِّرَ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا ٱلْأُخْرَىٰ ۚ وَلَا يَأْبَ ٱلشُّهَدَآءُ إِذَا مَا دُعُوا۟ ۚ وَلَا تَسْـَٔمُوٓا۟ أَن تَكْتُبُوهُ صَغِيرًا أَوْ كَبِيرًا إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَأَقْوَمُ لِلشَّهَـٰدَةِ وَأَدْنَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَرْتَابُوٓا۟ ۖ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَ تِجَـٰرَةً حَاضِرَةً تُدِيرُونَهَا بَيْنَكُمْ فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَلَّا تَكْتُبُوهَا ۗ وَأَشْهِدُوٓا۟ إِذَا تَبَايَعْتُمْ ۚ وَلَا يُضَآرَّ كَاتِبٌ وَلَا شَهِيدٌ ۚ وَإِن تَفْعَلُوا۟ فَإِنَّهُۥ فُسُوقٌۢ بِكُمْ ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ وَيُعَلِّمُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٢٨٢)
O you who have faith! When you contract a loan for a specified term, write it down. Let a writer write with honesty between you, and let not the writer refuse to write as Allah has taught him. So let him write, and let the one who incurs the debt dictate, and let him be wary of Allah, his Lord, and not diminish anything from it. But if the debtor be feeble-minded, or weak, or incapable of dictating himself, then let his guardian dictate with honesty, and take as witness two witnesses from your men, and if there are not two men, then a man and two women—from those whom you approve as witnesses—so that if one of the two defaults the other will remind her. The witnesses must not refuse when they are called, and do not consider it wearisome to write it down, whether it be a big or small sum, [as a loan lent] until its term. That is more just with Allah and more upright in respect to testimony, and the likeliest way to avoid doubt, unless it is an on-the-spot deal you transact between yourselves, in which case there is no sin upon you not to write it. Take witnesses when you make a deal, and let no harm be done to the writer or witness, and if you did that, it would be sinful of you. Be wary of Allah and Allah will teach you, and Allah has knowledge of all things. (282)

Commentary

Documenting Commercial Contracts in the Longest Verse of the Quran

After explaining the rulings related to spending in the way of Allah as well as the issue of usury, in this verse—which is the longest verse of the Quran—Allah explains precise rules and regulations for commercial and economic affairs so that capital may achieve its natural growth as much as possible and no deadlocks, disputes, or conflicts occur among the people.

In this verse, nineteen important instructions regarding financial transactions are stated in the following order: [^1]

1- In the first ruling, He says: "O you who have believed! When you contract a debt for a specified term (due to a loan or a transaction), write it down" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا تَدَايَنْتُمْ بِدَيْنٍ إِلَىٰ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى فَاكْتُبُوهُ).

Furthermore, from this expression, both the permissibility of debt and loans becomes clear, as well as the necessity of determining a term for loans.

It is noteworthy that in the verse, the word "dayn" (debt) is used, not "qard" (loan), because "qard" is only used for the exchange of two identical things—such as borrowing something to return its equivalent later—whereas "dayn" encompasses any kind of indebtedness, whether through borrowing or other transactions like leasing, settlement (sulh), or buying and selling where one party takes an obligation upon themselves. Therefore, the verse under discussion includes all general debts existing in transactions, such as advance payment (salaf) and credit sales (nasi'ah), while also including loans. The claim by some that it specifically refers to "salaf" transactions is entirely without evidence, although the occasion of revelation might have been a "salaf" sale.

2 and 3- Then, to ensure greater confidence and to keep the contract safe from potential interference by the parties, He adds: "And let a scribe write [it] between you in justice" (وَلْيَكْتُبْ بَيْنَكُمْ كَاتِبٌ بِالْعَدْلِ).

Therefore, this contract must be drafted by a third party, and that person must be just. Although the literal appearance of this sentence and the previous one suggests that writing such contracts is obligatory (wajib) because a command indicates obligation—and for this reason, some Sunni jurists consider it mandatory—the famous view among the great scholars of the Shi'a

[^1]: Of course, some rulings are also derived implicitly (not by direct denotation), which if added to the nineteen rulings above, would total twenty-one or more.

and Sunnis, due to other evidence, is that it is recommended (mustahabb). (Or that the command is of an advisory nature to prevent disputes and conflict). From the following verse which says: "And if one of you entrusts another, then let him who is entrusted deliver his trust [at its time]" (فَإِنْ أَمِنَ بَعْضُكُمْ بَعْضًا فَلْيُؤَدِّ الَّذِي اؤْتُمِنَ أَمَانَتَهُ), it is understood that this ruling applies where there is no complete trust and there is a possibility of disputes arising.

It is also worth noting that "justice" in the above phrase is a description of the act of writing, but from it, it is understood that the scribe must be just so that his writing is performed with justice.

4- "And let no scribe refuse to write as Allah has taught him. So let him write" (وَلَا يَأْبَ كَاتِبٌ أَنْ يَكْتُبَ كَمَا عَلَّمَهُ اللَّهُ ۚ فَلْيَكْتُبْ).

That is, in gratitude for this blessing that Allah has bestowed upon him, he should not shirk from writing the contract; rather, he should assist the parties of the transaction in this important matter (especially in environments like the one where the verse was revealed, where literate individuals were few).

The phrase "as Allah has taught him" (كَمَا عَلَّمَهُ اللَّهُ), according to the above interpretation, is for further emphasis and encouragement, but it is also possible that it refers to another ruling: observing the utmost honesty in writing, meaning he should draft the document precisely as Allah has taught him.

Does accepting the invitation to draft documents constitute an individual obligation (wajib 'ayni)? Certainly not, because it is waived if performed by others; for this reason, some jurists have ruled it a collective obligation (wajib kifa'i), but many have said that this act is also recommended and is considered a type of "cooperation in righteousness and piety" (ta'awun 'ala al-birr wa al-taqwa). Some evidence for its recommendatory nature may also be found in the subsequent sentences of this verse, but in any case,

as far as the order of Islamic society necessitates, this task is obligatory, and beyond that, it is recommended.

Can the scribe take a fee, and who bears the cost of ink, paper, and pen? Perhaps some have imagined that all these are the responsibility of the scribe and he has no right to a fee, but this is not correct. Taking a fee for such obligations is not problematic, and the costs belong to the one for whom the work is being done.

5- "And let the one who has the obligation (the debtor) dictate" (وَلْيُمْلِلِ الَّذِي عَلَيْهِ الْحَقُّ).

Certainly, one of the parties to the transaction must state the terms of the contract for the scribe to write, but which one? The verse says: the one who owes the right must dictate. Fundamentally, the primary signature on documents is always that of the debtor, and when it is done by his dictation, it prevents any form of denial. [^1]

6- "And let him fear Allah, his Lord, and not diminish anything from it" (وَلْيَتَّقِ اللَّهَ رَبَّهُ وَلَا يَبْخَسْ مِنْهُ شَيْئًا).

7- "But if the one who has the obligation is of limited understanding (safih) or weak (intellectually or insane) or unable to dictate himself (due to being mute), then let his guardian dictate in justice" (فَإِنْ كَانَ الَّذِي عَلَيْهِ الْحَقُّ سَفِيهًا أَوْ ضَعِيفًا أَوْ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُ أَنْ يُمِلَّ هُوَ فَلْيُمْلِلْ وَلِيُّهُ).

Therefore, in the case of three groups, the "guardian" (wali) must dictate: those who are "safih" (of limited understanding) and cannot distinguish their own loss and gain or manage their financial affairs

[^1]: "Wa-lyumlil" is originally from the root "millah" meaning religion and divine laws, and some have said it is from "malal" (boredom), and because repetition in dictation can be tedious, it is called so (sometimes appearing as "imla'" and sometimes as "imlal").

(even if they are not insane); those who are intellectually weak, such as young children, senile elderly, the mentally challenged, or the insane; and those who are unable to dictate, even if they are not mute.

From this sentence, other rulings are also implicitly understood, including the prohibition of financial disposals by the "safih" and the intellectually weak, as well as the permissibility of the guardian's intervention in such matters.

8- The "guardian" must also "observe justice" (بالعدل) in dictating and acknowledging the debt of those under his guardianship.

He should neither state more than their obligation nor act to their detriment.

9- Then He adds: "And bring to witness two witnesses" (وَاسْتَشْهِدُوا شَهِيدَيْنِ). [^1]

10 and 11- These two witnesses must be "from among your men" (مِنْ رِجَالِكُمْ).

Meaning they must be both adults and Muslims (the term "men" implies adulthood, and adding it to the pronoun "your" implies Islam, as the addressees here are the group of Muslims).

12- "And if there are not two men [available], then a man and two women" (فَإِنْ لَمْ يَكُونَا رَجُلَيْنِ فَرَجُلٌ وَامْرَأَتَانِ).

13- "From those whom you accept as witnesses" (مِمَّنْ تَرْضَوْنَ مِنَ الشُّهَدَاءِ).

From this phrase, the requirement of the witnesses being just, reliable, and trustworthy is derived, which has been extensively mentioned in Islamic narrations.

[^1]: Some have said: the difference between "shahid" and "shahid" is that "shahid" is said of one who is present at the event to be able to testify to it, and "shahid" is one who delivers the testimony—Kanz al-'Irfan, Vol. 2, p. 50.

Additionally, some have inferred from this expression that the witness must not be biased (such as someone who has specific interests in that dispute).

14- In the event that the witnesses consist of two men, each can testify independently; however, if it is one man and two women, those two women must testify together "so that if one of them errs, the other can remind her" (أَنْ تَضِلَّ إِحْدَاهُمَا فَتُذَكِّرَ إِحْدَاهُمَا الْأُخْرَىٰ).

Because women, due to strong emotions, might be influenced and might not follow the correct path during testimony due to forgetfulness or other factors; therefore, one reminds the other. Of course, this possibility exists for men as well, but to a lower and lesser degree.

15- Another ruling in this chapter is that "the witnesses should not refuse when they are called" (وَلَا يَأْبَ الشُّهَدَاءُ إِذَا مَا دُعُوا).

Therefore, bearing witness when invited for this purpose is obligatory.

The possibility has also been raised that it is necessary both to accept the invitation to bear witness (witnessing the event) and to deliver the testimony.

16- Whether the debt is small or large, it should be written down, because the health of economic relations intended by Islam requires that even in contracts related to small debts, one should not neglect writing a document. Thus, in the next sentence, He says: "And do not be weary to write it, whether it is small or large, for its [specified] term" (وَلَا تَسْأَمُوا أَنْ تَكْتُبُوهُ صَغِيرًا أَوْ كَبِيرًا إِلَىٰ أَجَلِهِ). [^1]

[^1]: Placing "small" before "large" is because people's indifference to small transactions either makes them not committed to writing them—which becomes a source of conflict—or people might think that writing small matters is a sign of stinginess, which the Quran negates by this means.

Then He adds: "That is more just in the sight of Allah and more upright for testimony and nearer to preventing doubt among you" (ذَٰلِكُمْ أَقْسَطُ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ وَأَقْوَمُ لِلشَّهَادَةِ وَأَدْنَىٰ أَلَّا تَرْتَابُوا).

In fact, this sentence refers to the philosophy of the above rulings regarding the writing of transaction documents. It says: drafting documents and precision in them, on one hand, guarantees the execution of justice; on the other hand, it strengthens and reassures the witnesses when delivering testimony; and on a third hand, it prevents the creation of pessimism among members of society.

This sentence clearly shows that drafted documents can be considered as evidence and proof by judges, although unfortunately, a group of jurists has not paid much attention to it.

17- Then He excludes one case from this ruling, saying: "Except when it is an immediate transaction which you conduct among yourselves, for [then] there is no blame upon you if you do not write it" (إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونَ تِجَارَةً حَاضِرَةً تُدِيرُونَهَا بَيْنَكُمْ فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَلَّا تَكْتُبُوهَا).

"Tijaratan hadiratan" means a cash transaction, and the phrase "tudirunaha baynakum" (which you conduct among yourselves) is an emphasis on the transaction being cash-based.

Furthermore, from the words "there is no blame upon you" (فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ), it is understood that even in the case of a cash transaction, if they draft a document, it is appropriate. This is because conflicts often arise even in cash transactions regarding the payment of the price, its amount, or issues related to options (khiyarat), which a written document would end.

18- In a cash transaction, although drafting a document and writing it is not necessary, taking witnesses for it is better, as it prevents potential future disputes. Therefore,

He says: "And take witnesses when you conclude a contract" (وَأَشْهِدُوا إِذَا تَبَايَعْتُمْ).

The possibility also exists that the intention is taking witnesses in all transactions, whether cash or credit. In any case, the jurists of the Shi'a and Sunnis—except for a small group—consider this instruction to be a recommended matter, not obligatory. In the next verse, there is also evidence for this.

And it is certain that it does not include very small daily transactions (such as buying bread, food, and the like).

19- In the final ruling mentioned in this verse, He says: "Let no scribe be harmed or any witness" (وَلَا يُضَارَّ كَاتِبٌ وَلَا شَهِيدٌ).

"For if you do so, indeed, it is disobedience in you" (فَإِنَّهُ فُسُوقٌ بِكُمْ).

In this way, the Quran gives immunity and security to scribes and witnesses, and emphatically asks the people not to harass these establishers of truth and justice.

From what we have said, it became clear that the phrase "wa la yudarra" is in the passive voice, meaning this group should not suffer harm, not that it is in the active voice meaning they should not distort or cause harm—as a group of commentators has mentioned—because this ruling appeared at the beginning of this very verse and there is no need for repetition.

And at the end of the verse, after mentioning all those rulings, He "invites the people to piety, fear of God, and obedience to Allah's command" (وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ).

And then He reminds them that "Allah teaches you what you need in your material and spiritual life" (وَيُعَلِّمُكُمُ اللَّهُ).

"And He is Aware of all the interests and harms of the people and ordains for them what is for their good and welfare" (وَاللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ).

Key Points

1- The precise rulings mentioned in this verse regarding the drafting of documents for transactions, including the mention of details at all stages in the longest verse of the Holy Quran, indicate the deep attention the Quran pays to the economic affairs of Muslims and the order of their work. This is especially significant considering that this Heavenly Book was revealed in a backward society where even the literacy to read and write was very scarce, and even the bringer of this Quran had not studied or attended school. This itself is proof of the greatness of the Quran on one hand, and the importance of the economic system of Muslims on the other.

"Ali ibn Ibrahim" says in his famous Tafsir: It is mentioned in a report that there are five hundred Islamic rulings in Surah Al-Baqarah, and fifteen specific rulings are contained in this verse. [^1] As we saw, the number of rulings in this verse reaches nineteen; in fact, if we consider its implicit rulings, the number will be more than this, to the extent that Fadil al-Miqdad in Kanz al-'Irfan derived twenty-one rulings plus numerous other branches from it. Therefore, the aforementioned statement that counted the rulings of this verse as fifteen is due to merging some of them into others.

2- The phrases "And fear Allah" (وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ) and "And Allah teaches you" (وَيُعَلِّمُكُمُ اللَّهُ), although they appear as independent sentences conjoined to one another, their placement side by side is a sign of the link between the two. Its meaning is that piety, God-fearing, and the worship of God have a

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qummi, Vol. 1, p. 94.

deep effect on awareness, insight, and the increase of science and knowledge. Yes, when the human heart is polished by piety, it reflects truths like a mirror. This meaning is entirely logical because ugly traits and impure deeds cast veils over human thought and do not allow one to see the face of truth as it is. When the veils are removed by piety, the face of truth becomes manifest.

Truth is a house fully adorned, But passion and desire have raised the dust. Do you not see that where dust has risen, The sight sees not, though the man be keen-eyed?

However, some ignorant Sufis have misused this meaning and taken it as a reason to abandon the study of formal sciences, whereas such a statement contradicts many verses of the Quran and established Islamic narrations.

The truth is that one part of the sciences must be learned through formal teaching and learning, and another part of divine sciences must be obtained through the purity of the heart and washing it with the water of gnosis and piety. This is the very light that Allah casts into the heart of whomever He wills and deems worthy:

"Knowledge is a light that Allah casts into the heart of whomever He wills."

Ayah

۞ وَإِن كُنتُمْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ وَلَمْ تَجِدُوا۟ كَاتِبًا فَرِهَـٰنٌ مَّقْبُوضَةٌ ۖ فَإِنْ أَمِنَ بَعْضُكُم بَعْضًا فَلْيُؤَدِّ ٱلَّذِى ٱؤْتُمِنَ أَمَـٰنَتَهُۥ وَلْيَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّهُۥ ۗ وَلَا تَكْتُمُوا۟ ٱلشَّهَـٰدَةَ ۚ وَمَن يَكْتُمْهَا فَإِنَّهُۥٓ ءَاثِمٌ قَلْبُهُۥ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ (٢٨٣)
If you are on a journey and cannot find a writer, then a retained pledge [shall suffice]. And if one of you entrusts [an asset] to another, let him who is trusted deliver his trust, and let him be wary of Allah, his Lord. And do not conceal your testimony; anyone who conceals it, his heart will indeed be sinful, and Allah knows well what you do. (283)

Commentary

Continuation of the Discussion on Organizing Commercial Documents

This verse, in fact, complements the previous verse by mentioning several other rulings regarding the issue of organizing commercial documents, which are as follows:

1- "And if you are on a journey and cannot find a scribe (to organize the transaction documents for you and write the contract), then let there be a pledge taken" (وَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ عَلى‌ سَفَرٍ وَ لَمْ تَجِدُوا كاتِباً فَرِهانٌ مَقْبُوضَةٌ).

Although at first glance the apparent meaning of the verse suggests that the legislation of the "law of mortgages/pledges" (rahn) is specific to travel, considering the phrase "and cannot find a scribe" (وَ لَمْ تَجِدُوا كاتِباً), it is clearly understood that the intent refers to cases where a person to organize the document cannot be found. Therefore, whenever it is difficult to access a scribe even in one's hometown, there is no obstacle to settling for a pledge. The goal is for transactions to be based on a firm foundation, whether certainty is achieved through organizing a document and taking witnesses, or through a mortgage and pledge.

In the commentaries of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, this reality has also been pointed out. For instance, it is mentioned in famous Hadith sources of both the Shi'a and the Sunnis that the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله placed His armor as a pledge with a non-Muslim person in Medina and took an amount as a loan from him. [^1] Additionally, it can be inferred from this that literacy in that environment was so scarce that often during journeys, there was not a single literate person in the entire caravan.

2- The pledge must definitely be "received" (possessed) and placed at the disposal of the creditor so that it may have the effect of providing security; hence He says: "a pledge taken into possession" (فَرِهانٌ مَقْبُوضَةٌ).

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام who said: "There is no pledge except that which is possessed," meaning a pledge does not exist unless the creditor takes delivery of it. [^2]

3- Then, as an exception to the above rulings, He says: "And if one of you entrusts another (he may transact with him without writing a document or a pledge and entrust his property to him), then let him who is entrusted deliver his trust (and his debt on time) and let him fear Allah, his Lord" (فَإِنْ أَمِنَ بَعْضُكُمْ بَعْضاً فَلْيُؤَدِّ الَّذِي اؤْتُمِنَ أَمانَتَهُ وَ لْيَتَّقِ اللَّهَ رَبَّهُ‌).

Indeed, just as the creditor has trusted him, he too must respect that trust and confidence, fulfilling the right without delay and not forgetting piety (Taqwa).

It is noteworthy that here, the creditor's claim is mentioned as a "trust" (amanah), in which betrayal is a great sin.

4- Then, addressing all people, He states a comprehensive command regarding testimony and says: "And do not conceal testimony, for whoever conceals it—his heart is indeed sinful" (وَ لا تَكْتُمُوا الشَّهادَةَ وَ مَنْ يَكْتُمْها فَإِنَّهُ آثِمٌ قَلْبُهُ‌).

Therefore, those who are aware of the rights of others are duty-bound not to conceal it when called upon to give testimony; rather, many believe that regarding the rights of people, one must give testimony even without being called.

It is clear that giving testimony is a collective obligation (Wajib Kifa'i); that is, if some undertake it such that the right is proven by it, the responsibility is lifted from others.

And since the concealment of testimony and the refusal to disclose it are performed by the heart and soul, He introduces it as a sin of the heart and says: "whoever does so, his heart is sinful." [^1] Again, at the end of the verse, for further emphasis and attention toward preserving trusts, fulfilling each other's rights, and not concealing testimony, He warns: "And Allah is All-Knowing of what you do" (وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ‌).

People might not know who is capable of giving testimony and who is not, and people might also not know who is the creditor and who is the debtor in cases where no documents or pledges exist, but Allah knows all of this and will recompense everyone according to their deeds.

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Vol. 2, p. 420; and Tafsir al-Maraghi, under the verse being discussed. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 301. [^1]: The meaning of "heart" (qalb) in Quranic verses is the soul and the intellect; for more explanation on this subject, see Volume 1 under verse 7 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Ayah

لِّلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَإِن تُبْدُوا۟ مَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُم بِهِ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَيَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢٨٤)
To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. Whether you disclose what is in your hearts or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it. Then He will forgive whomever He wishes and punish whomever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things. (284)

Commentary

Everything Belongs to Him

This verse, in reality, completes what was mentioned in the final sentence of the previous verse. It states: "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, and (for this reason) whether you reveal what is in your souls or conceal it, Allah will bring you to account for it" (لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ إِنْ تُبْدُوا ما فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحاسِبْكُمْ بِهِ اللَّهُ‌).

"Then He forgives whom He wills (and deems worthy) and punishes whom He wills (and sees as deserving)" (فَيَغْفِرُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَشاءُ).

That is to say, do not imagine that acts such as concealing testimony or other sins of the heart remain hidden from Him. For One who is the Sovereign over the entire universe, the earth, and the heavens, nothing shall be hidden from Him. Therefore, do not be surprised if it is said that Allah calculates and punishes even the hidden sins.

There is also the possibility that the above verse refers to all the rulings mentioned in various preceding verses, such as sincere charity versus charity mixed with hypocrisy, as well as reminders of generosity and injury, and also prayer, fasting, and other rulings and beliefs.

At the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah is Powerful over all things" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

He is both Aware of everything in this world and Capable of determining merit and worthiness, as well as punishing the transgressors.

Key Points

1- It is sometimes imagined that this verse contradicts numerous hadiths stating that the intention to sin is not a sin.

However, the answer to this is clear, because those hadiths pertain to sins that involve an external action for which the intention is a preliminary step—such as oppression, lying, and the usurpation of rights—not sins that are inherently internal and are considered acts of the heart (such as polytheism, hypocrisy, and the concealment of testimony).

There is another interpretation for this verse as well: that a single action may be performed in different ways. For example, charity is sometimes for the sake of Allah and sometimes for hypocrisy and fame-seeking. The verse says: whether you reveal your intention or conceal it, Allah is aware of it and will reward or punish you accordingly. In fact, it refers to the same theme as the narration:

لا عمل الا بنية

"There is no action except by intention." [1]

2- It is clear that when He says: He forgives whom He wills and punishes whom He wills, this "willing" is not without reason. Rather, His forgiveness has a reason, and that reason is the worthiness of pardon in the person being forgiven; the same applies to the withholding of forgiveness and the infliction of punishment.

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 1, p. 33.

Ayah

ءَامَنَ ٱلرَّسُولُ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ۚ كُلٌّ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتِهِۦ وَكُتُبِهِۦ وَرُسُلِهِۦ لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّن رُّسُلِهِۦ ۚ وَقَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا ۖ غُفْرَانَكَ رَبَّنَا وَإِلَيْكَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٢٨٥)
The Apostle and the faithful have faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord. Each [of them] has faith in Allah, His angels, His scriptures and His apostles. [They declare,] ‘We make no distinction between any of His apostles.’ And they say, ‘We hear and obey. Our Lord, forgive us, and toward You is the return.’ (285)

Commentary

The Way and Manner of Faith

Surah Al-Baqarah began by explaining a portion of Islamic knowledge and true beliefs, and it concludes with the same theme found in the above verse and the verse following it; thus, its beginning and end are harmonious.

Some commentators have also mentioned a context of revelation (Sha'n al-Nuzul) for this verse: when the previous verse was revealed—stating that whether you hide what is in your hearts or reveal it, Allah will call you to account for it—a group of the companions became fearful (saying that none of us is free from internal whispers and passing thoughts of the heart, and they expressed this to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله). Then, the above verse was revealed, teaching them the way and manner of faith, supplication to the threshold of Allah, obedience, and submission. [^1]

[^1]: Adapted from Al-Bahr al-Muhit, Vol. 2, p. 363; the same meaning is mentioned in Tafsir Ithna Ashari and Qurtubi under the verse in question.

First, He says: "The Messenger has believed in what was revealed to him from his Lord" (آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ رَبِّهِ‌).

This is one of the distinctions of the Divine Prophets: they generally possessed a decisive faith in their own path and school of thought and had no hesitation whatsoever in their belief. Before anyone else, they themselves were believers, and more than anyone else, they possessed steadfastness and perseverance.

In verse 158 of Surah Al-A'raf, this subject is counted as one of the special attributes of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: فَآمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ النَّبِيِّ الْأُمِّيِّ الَّذِي يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ وَ كَلِماتِهِ‌:

"So believe in Allah and His Messenger, the unlettered Prophet, who believes in Allah and His words."

Then it adds: "And the believers [as well] all believe in Allah, His angels, His books, and His messengers, [saying], 'We make no distinction between any of His messengers'" and we believe in all of them (وَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ كُلٌّ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَ مَلائِكَتِهِ وَ كُتُبِهِ وَ رُسُلِهِ لا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِنْ رُسُلِهِ‌). [^1] Indeed, the believers—unlike "those who wish to separate between Allah and His messengers and say, 'We believe in some and disbelieve in others'" (وَ يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُفَرِّقُوا بَيْنَ اللَّهِ وَ رُسُلِهِ وَ يَقُولُونَ نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَ نَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ‌ [^2])—make no distinction between the Divine messengers; they consider them all to be from Allah and respect them all.

It is clear that this subject does not contradict the abrogation of previous religions by subsequent ones, because as previously mentioned, the teachings of the Prophets are like the teachings of different educational stages—from primary and middle school to high school and university. Although

[^1]: The phrase "وَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ ..." can be an independent sentence (as interpreted above), or it can be conjoined to "the Messenger," and there is not much difference, though the first meaning is more appropriate.

[^2]: Surah An-Nisa, Verse 150.

their principles are one, they are implemented at different levels, and upon ascending to a higher stage, the previous programs are set aside, while the respect for all of them remains preserved.

Then it adds: In addition to this firm and comprehensive faith, the believers, in the position of action, "said: 'We hear and we obey. [We seek] Your forgiveness, our Lord, and to You is the [final] destination'" (وَ قالُوا سَمِعْنا وَ أَطَعْنا غُفْرانَكَ رَبَّنا وَ إِلَيْكَ الْمَصِيرُ).

"We hear" (Sami'na) has been used in some instances to mean "we understood and we testified," one example of which is this very verse; meaning, we accepted the call of Your Prophets with our entire being and entered the state of obedience and following.

But O Allah! Ultimately, we are human, and sometimes instincts and desires overcome us and we fall into error; we expect forgiveness from You, and we know that the end of our affair is toward You. [^1] In this way, faith in the Origin (Mabda'), the Resurrection (Ma'ad), and the Divine messengers is accompanied and harmonized with practical commitment to all Divine commands.

[^1]: Many commentators have said that there is an omitted verb in the final phrase, and the implied meaning was "We ask You" or "We desire Your forgiveness."

Ayah

لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۚ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْهَا مَا ٱكْتَسَبَتْ ۗ رَبَّنَا لَا تُؤَاخِذْنَآ إِن نَّسِينَآ أَوْ أَخْطَأْنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا وَلَا تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْنَآ إِصْرًا كَمَا حَمَلْتَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا وَلَا تُحَمِّلْنَا مَا لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا بِهِۦ ۖ وَٱعْفُ عَنَّا وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَٱرْحَمْنَآ ۚ أَنتَ مَوْلَىٰنَا فَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢٨٦)
Allah does not task any soul beyond its capacity. Whatever [good] it earns is to its own benefit, and whatever [evil] it incurs is to its own harm. ‘Our Lord! Take us not to task if we forget or make mistakes! Our Lord! Do not place upon us a burden as You placed on those who were before us! Our Lord! Do not lay upon us what we have no strength to bear! Excuse us, forgive us, and be merciful to us! You are our Master, so help us against the faithless lot!’ (286)

Commentary

Several Important Requests

As mentioned in the commentary of the previous verse, these two verses refer to those who became anxious upon hearing the statement that if you hide something in your hearts or reveal it, Allah will bring you to account for it and recompense you accordingly. They said: "None of us is free from whispers and passing thoughts of the heart."

This verse states: "Allah does not charge a soul except [with that within] its capacity" (لا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها).

"Wus’" (وسع) linguistically means ease and power. Therefore, the verse confirms this intellectual reality: that divine duties and obligations are never beyond the level of the power and ability of individuals. Thus, it must be said that all rulings are interpreted and restricted by this very verse, being limited to cases within human power. It is obvious that a Wise and Just Legislator cannot enact a law other than this. Furthermore, the above sentence once again confirms the truth that Sharia rulings are never separated from intellectual rulings and the commands of reason and wisdom; these two proceed side by side at all stages.

Then it adds: "It will have [the consequence of] what [good] it has gained, and it will bear [the consequence of] what [evil] it has earned" (لَها ما كَسَبَتْ وَ عَلَيْها مَا اكْتَسَبَتْ).

Yes, everyone reaps the harvest of their own good and bad deeds and will face their results and consequences in this world and the next.

With this statement, the above verse directs people toward their own responsibility and the consequences of their actions, and it draws a line of cancellation through the myths of fatalism (jabr), luck, fortune, and other such superstitions that individuals have devised to exonerate themselves.

It is noteworthy that in the noble verse, regarding good deeds, "kasabat" (کسبت) is used, while for bad deeds, "iktasabat" (اكتسبت) is used. Perhaps the difference in expression is because "kasb" is used for matters that a person performs with internal inclination and without affectation, aligning with their primordial nature (fitrah). In contrast, "iktasab" is its opposite—meaning actions that go against human nature and essence. This itself indicates that good deeds are in harmony with human nature, while evil deeds are inherently contrary to it.

"Raghib" in "Al-Mufradat" has mentioned another point regarding the difference between these two expressions which is also worthy of consideration. He states that "kasb" is specific to actions whose benefit is not limited to the person themselves but also encompasses others (like charitable acts whose results do not only include the doer but may also involve their relatives, kin, and friends). Meanwhile, "iktasab" is used in cases where the effect of the action only affects the person themselves, and this applies to sin. (Of course, it should be noted that these differences apply when "kasb" and "iktasab" are placed in contrast to one another).

Following these two fundamental principles (obligation is according to capacity—and everyone is responsible for their own actions), the verse expresses seven requests from the Lord on the tongue of the believers, which is in fact a teaching for everyone on what to say and what to ask for.

First, it says: "Our Lord, do not impose blame upon us if we have forgotten or erred" (رَبَّنا لا تُؤاخِذْنا إِنْ نَسِينا أَوْ أَخْطَأْنا).

Because they know they are responsible for their actions, they call upon Allah with a special supplication as "Rabb" (Lord)—the One who has had and continues to have a special grace in their nurturing—and they say: Life, in any case, is not devoid of forgetfulness, error, and mistake; we strive not to pursue intentional sin, but forgive us for our errors and slips.

A discussion arises here: Is it even possible for the Lord to punish someone for a slip originating from forgetfulness or lack of attention, such that there remains a basis for this request?

In response to this question, it must be said: Sometimes forgetfulness is the result of a person's own negligence. It is certain that such forgetfulness does not absolve a person of responsibility. As stated in the Quran: "So taste [punishment] because you forgot the meeting of this, your Day" (فَذُوقُوا بِما نَسِيتُمْ لِقاءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هذا). [^1] Therefore, forgetfulness born of negligence is punishable.

Another point to consider is that "nisyan" (forgetfulness) and "khata" (error) have a clear difference from one another.

"Khata" is usually applied to actions that occur out of heedlessness and lack of attention, such as someone firing an arrow while hunting and it hitting a person without intent, wounding them. But "nisyan" is used where a person pursues an action with attention but has forgotten the specifics of the event, such as punishing an innocent person thinking they are the culprit because they forgot the characteristics of the actual culprit.

Then, expressing their second request, it says: "Our Lord, and lay not upon us a burden like that which You laid upon those before us" (رَبَّنا وَ لا تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْنا إِصْراً كَما حَمَلْتَهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِنا).

"Isr" (إصر) originally means holding back and imprisoning, and it is applied to any heavy task that prevents a person from activity. It is also applied to covenants and pledges that restrict a person. For this reason, punishment and penalty are also sometimes called "isr." In this sentence, the believers request Allah to exempt them from heavy obligations that sometimes cause individuals to deviate from obeying the Lord. This is the same thing narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the instructions of Islam:

"بعثت بالحنيفية السمحة السهلة" [^2] "I have been sent with a primordial religion that is easy and simple for everyone to practice."

Here, a question may be asked: If the ease of the Sharia and religion is good, why was it not so for previous nations? In response, it must be said: As understood from the verses of the Quran, arduous obligations for previous nations were not part of the original Sharia, but were rather placed as a penalty and punishment following acts of disobedience. Just as the Children of Israel, due to successive acts of disobedience, were deprived of eating certain lawful meats (Surah Al-An'am, Verse 146 and Surah An-Nisa, Verse 160).

In the third request, they say: "Our Lord, and burden us not with that which we have no ability to bear" (رَبَّنا وَ لا تُحَمِّلْنا ما لا طاقَةَ لَنا بِهِ).

This sentence may refer to grueling trials or heavy punishments in this world and the hereafter, or both. Perhaps the use of "la tahmil" (لا تحمل) in the previous sentence and "la tuhammilna" (لا تُحَمِّلْنا - with shaddah) in this sentence is for this reason, as the first expression refers to difficult matters and the second refers to unbearable matters.

In the fourth, fifth, and sixth requests, they say: "And pardon us; and forgive us; and have mercy upon us" (وَ اعْفُ عَنَّا وَ اغْفِرْ لَنا وَ ارْحَمْنا).

"Afw" (عفو) linguistically means erasing the traces of something, and it often means erasing the traces of sin, which includes both its natural effects and its punishment.

While "maghfirah" (مغفرة) only means covering the sin.

Therefore, the believers ask Allah both to cover their sins and to remove their consequential and existential effects from their souls and spirits, and to lift the punishment from them. Then they ask Him for His vast mercy, which encompasses all things, to include them.

Finally, in the seventh and last request, they say: "You are our protector, so give us victory over the disbelieving people" (أَنْتَ مَوْلانا فَانْصُرْنا عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْكافِرِينَ).

In this way, their requests encompass this world and the hereafter, individual and social victories, and divine pardon, forgiveness, and mercy. This is a very comprehensive request.

Key Points

  1. Since these two verses contain a summary of the entire Surah Al-Baqarah and teach us the spirit of submission to the Creator of the world, this point is noted: if believers ask Allah to overlook their slips and grant them victory over various enemies, they must implement the program of "we hear and we obey" (سمعنا و اطعنا) and say: "We have accepted the call of the callers with heart and soul and have set out to follow it." They must not cease any effort or struggle in this path and then ask Allah for victory over obstacles and enemies.

The repetition of Allah's name as "Rabbana" (Our Lord)—the One who has a special grace in their nurturing—completes this reality.

Therefore, the leaders of Islam, within numerous hadiths, have encouraged Muslims to recite these two verses and have stated various rewards for them. If the tongue and heart are harmonized in reciting these verses—and it is not merely words but becomes a life program—reciting these two verses can link the center of the heart with the Creator of the world, purify the soul and spirit, and become a factor for movement and activity.

  1. It is clearly understood from this verse that "obligation beyond capacity" (taklif ma la yutaq) does not exist, neither in Islam nor in other religions, and the principle is freedom of will. For it says: everyone is a hostage to their own good and bad deeds; whatever good they do is for themselves, and whatever evil they do is to their own detriment. The request for pardon, forgiveness, and mercy is also evidence for this claim.

This matter is consistent with the logic of reason and the issue of [rational] beauty and ugliness, for the Wise Allah would never do such a thing. This itself is a proof for the negation of fatalism (jabr). How could Allah compel servants to sin and simultaneously forbid sin?

However, arduous and difficult obligations are not impossible, such as the arduous obligations that existed for the Children of Israel, which were also born of their own actions and a punishment for their stubbornness.

End of Surah Al-Baqarah May 6, 1993 (16/2/1372)
